title: Career Readiness for Applied and Technical Professions
description: This course will allow students to synthesize career and academic opportunities related to their technical coursework into viable career pathways. Exploration of career interests and values will be accomplished through selected assessments, applied learning projects, and online resources. Identification of defined academic and career pathways are curated through evaluation, decision-making, and defining short and long-term career goals. Since the course is foundational for the degree completion program in Applied and Interdisciplinary Studies, it is assumed that a student will already have completed 30 credit hours or an associate’s degree completed.

title: Undergraduate Topics in Applied and Interdisciplinary Studies
description: Selected undergraduate topics in applied and interdisciplinary studies.

title: Undergraduate Special Topics in Applied and Interdisciplinary Studies
description: Special Topics in Applied and Interdisciplinary Studies.

title: Internship in Applied and Interdisciplinary Studies
description: This internship course provides students in interdisciplinary fields with hands-on experience in a professional setting. Students are responsible for finding their own internship site(s) prior to enrolling in the course. The course includes reflections and discussions where students can share their experiences and insights by integrating their academic knowledge with practical application.

title: Applied Studies Seminar
description: This applied studies seminar is designed to bridge the gap between undergraduate academic experiences and the professional world. Students will connect their research, writing, and exploration with real-world issues in their intended career fields. The course also focuses on expanding the critical skills necessary for new employees, with topics ranging from professional communication to navigating employment challenges in an evolving landscape.

title: Interdisciplinary Process
description:
The overall goal of this course is for students to develop an understanding of and practice 
interdisciplinary and multidisciplinary thinking, problem-solving, and team-based leadership, using models that allow interdisciplinary and cross-function teams to work together and generate imaginative and creative solutions to complex challenges and problems.

title: Emotional Intelligence
description:
This course focuses on cultivating core emotional intelligence (EI) competencies, self-awareness, self-regulation, motivation, empathy, and social skills, within professional and organizational contexts. Students will develop the interpersonal and reflective skills necessary for effective teamwork, ethical leadership, and client engagement. Graduate 
students will engage in advanced application through research, analysis, and instructional design.

title: Adaptability and Innovation in the Workplace
description: This course prepares students to thrive in rapidly changing work environments by developing their adaptability, resilience, and innovative thinking. Students will engage in reflective, analytical, and project-based learning to identify, evaluate, and apply practices of adaptability and innovation in individual and organizational contexts.

title: Undergraduate Advanced Topics in Applied and Interdisciplinary Studies
description: Advanced undergraduate topics in Applied and Interdisciplinary Studies.

title: Integrative Professional Portfolio
description:
The Integrative Professional Portfolio course is an experience for students in applied and
interdisciplinary programs at K-State Olathe. Students integrate coursework, applied learning, and professional development into a curated digital portfolio of skills in context. Through critical reflection, artifact selection, and a professional presentation, students will demonstrate readiness for career advancement or further study.

title: Special Topics in Applied and Interdisciplinary Studies
description: Special topics in Applied and Interdisciplinary Studies.

title: Emotional Intelligence
description: This course focuses on cultivating core emotional intelligence (EI) competencies, self-awareness, self-regulation, motivation, empathy, and social skills, within professional and organizational contexts. Students will develop the interpersonal and reflective skills necessary for effective teamwork, ethical leadership, and client engagement. Graduate students will engage in advanced application through research, analysis, and instructional design.

title: Adaptability and Innovation in the Workplace
description: This course prepares students to thrive in rapidly changing work environments by developing their adaptability, resilience, and innovative thinking. Students will engage in reflective, analytical, and project-based learning to identify, evaluate, and apply practices of adaptability and innovation in individual and organizational contexts.

title: Topics in Applied and Interdisciplinary Studies
description: Selected topics in applied and interdisciplinary studies.

title: U.S. Animal Politics and Policymaking
description: The goal of this course is for students to become familiar with basic concepts of politics and policymaking related to animals in the United States. The course will examine the role of interest groups, elected representatives, and government agencies in the creation and implementation of policies involving companion animals, wildlife, farm animals, and animals used in research.

title: Integrative Professional Portfolio
description:
The Integrative Professional Portfolio course is an experience for students in applied and
interdisciplinary programs at K-State Olathe. Students integrate coursework, applied learning, and professional development into a curated digital portfolio of skills in context. Through critical reflection, artifact selection, and a professional presentation, students will demonstrate readiness for career advancement or further study.

title: Interdisciplinary Process
description: The overall goal of this course is for students to develop an understanding of and practice in interdisciplinary and multidisciplinary thinking, problem solving, and team based leadership, using models that allow interdisciplinary and cross-function teams to work together and to generate imaginative and creative solutions to complex challenges and problems.

title: Regulatory Aspects of Drug and Vaccine Development in the Animal Health Industry
description: This course explores the topic of regulations associated with animal health product development and manufacturing. Topics for discussion will include an overview of the regulatory affairs process in the U.S. and other countries, drug and vaccine classifications and the approval process, GCP/GLP guidelines, drug and vaccine efficacy and safety testing, human and environmental safety issues, and future challenges and current industry needs.

title: Strategies in Preclinical and Clinical Research for Regulatory Affairs in Animal Health
description: In this course, students will review the history of regulations as well as gain an understanding of the integration of science into successful product approval outcomes. Students will learn how to achieve target claims for developing new products while ensuring safety. Students will also be introduced to the planning, design, and conduct of preclinical and clinical studies. The topics covered include protocol design and the evaluation and assessment of regulatory submissions. The course will consider practical issues in regulatory affairs. Students may participate in case study exercises.

title: Strategies in the Stewardship of Licensed Pharmaceuticals and Biologics in Animal Health
description: In this course, students will learn about the role of regulatory affairs in the stewardship of animal health products. The topics covered include expected activities primarily post product approval, compliance, strategies for supply management, product life cycle management, risk management, and the role of industry in policy development. Communication with various stakeholders, including regulatory agencies, research and development, manufacturing, and marketing and sales are covered. The course will consider practical issues in regulatory affairs. Students may participate in case study exercises.

title: Regulatory Development of Animal Pharmaceuticals - FDA
description: In this course, students will focus on the regulatory strategies that support product development and maintenance regulated by the Food and Drug Administration (FDA) Center for Veterinary Medicine (CVM). This includes product development strategies based on clinical and nonclinical data, key manufacturing and supply chain regulations, and post approval product life cycle management, including pharmacovigilance. Variances between food animal and non-food animal targets in developing regulatory strategies will be reviewed. Special topics, current topics and FDA enforcement actions will be covered.

title: Regulatory Development of Animal Pesticides - EPA
description: In this course, students will focus on the regulatory strategies for the development of products deemed to be regulated by the Environmental Protection Agency (EPA).  This course will focus on product development strategies, manufacturing and supply chain regulations, and post marketing product life cycle management, including pharmacovigilance.  Environmental laws and risk assessment will also be covered.

title: Regulatory Development of Animal Biologicals and Diagnostics - USDA
description: In this course, students will focus on the  structure and detailed approach for developing animal biologicals and diagnostics regulated by the United States Department of Agriculture (USDA).  Students will gain solid understanding of the critical pathway to successful product development, manufacturing, compliance, and product life cycle management, including post marketing surveillance.  The course also addresses adapting new technology to emerging and re-emerging diseases.

title: Special Topics: Regulatory Affairs in Animal Health
description: This course will be used as the final culminating experience in the Animal Health Regulatory Affairs (AHRA) graduate certificate program, where it will require the completion of a final project that integrates learning following the completion of program courses.

title: Vaccinology
description: This multidisciplinary course is designed to provide the student with an understanding of vaccine development (including technology) from conceptualization through development, testing, and utilization. This course will specifically cover topics on the principles of pathogenesis of infectious diseases, immune protection, and eliciting protective immunity by vaccination; application of traditional and new technologies to vaccine development; principles of pre-clinical development, manufacturing, and clinical evaluation of a vaccine; importance of the regulatory process to vaccine development; and utilization of epidemiology to measure vaccine need and effectiveness.

title: Capstone Experience I
description: This course provides students the opportunity to synthesize and integrate knowledge in its application to professional practice. It is designed for students who intend to work in an applied professional setting where they are expected to critically apply existing knowledge and methods to solve problems. Students will complete a project proposal on a topic of interest and related assignments, in consultation with the instructor.

title: Capstone Experience II
description: This course provides students the opportunity to synthesize and integrate knowledge in its application to professional practice. It is designed for students who intend to work in an applied professional setting where they are expected to critically apply existing knowledge and methods to solve problems. Students will produce written reports and oral presentations on their project of focus.

title: Introduction to Systems and Data in Animal Health
description: The field of Animal Health data analytics is comprised of the application of data systems, data computing, and data programming. This course is focused on data systems and data acquisition within animal health systems.    			This is an interdisciplinary course for students interested in data insights and digital technologies in animal health that explores life cycle and data collection systems encountered in Animal Health for both production and companion species, including the realities of data acquisition, data types, and analytics that may exist within those systems.

title: Research Strategies to Drive Development of Animal Health Products
description: Develop a working knowledge of the product development pipeline and continuous discovery techniques using both research and market insights as drivers for innovation in the Animal Health industry.

title: Applied Analytics in Animal Health
description: The field of Animal Health data analytics is comprised of the application of data systems, data computing, and data programming. This course will cover all three areas of data systems, data computing, and data programming in an applied format that is specific to the animal health industry.   This is an applied course for students interested in developing skills in analytics and data science as they apply to animal health. Students will be expected to assess relevant outcomes in animal health systems while deducing proper methods for data preparation and manipulation, extrapolating understanding of data analytics to select  the appropriate analysis approach, performing supervised and unsupervised machine learning, and effectively communicating  both analysis and interpretation of outcomes as they apply to the field of animal health. Students will be asked to use Python and other software systems. Students will formulate reasons why working in interdisciplinary teams benefits analytic projects in animal health.

title: Special Topics: Data Analytics and Insights in Animal Health
description: This course is the final component of the Data Analytics and Insights in Animal Health Graduate Certificate program. Students will demonstrate core certificate objectives by completing a project proposing a new data analytic and insight technology for the animal health industry. Students will describe the necessary components for project execution, requirements to develop the analytic tool or technology, and how the analytic tool or technology will be used in animal health.

title: Introduction to Computing for Animal Health
description: The field of Animal Health data analytics is comprised of applications of data systems, data computing, and data programming. The focus of this course is on data computing and programming as applied to animal health data.   The purpose of this course is to introduce students to the application of essential data-driven approaches to tackle the challenges facing animal health. The use of computational approaches is considered to be more valuable in helping scientists study specific hypotheses in animal health. The Introduction to Computing for Animal Health course will focus on intuitive computational methods to address animal health questions with interdisciplinary philosophy. Students will understand the analysis workflows and computational methods that unravel and translate complicated data from animal health into a comprehensive demonstration.

title: Assessing Effectiveness of Animal Therapeutics and Vaccines
description: This interdisciplinary course is designed for students working with data derived from animal health systems to expand their knowledge and understanding of the pathophysiology of common animal diseases. This course will familiarize students with animal disease processes and basic principles behind the use of clinical laboratory assays, including their use in assessing efficacy of drugs or vaccines. Laboratory data from animal studies will be used to apply principles discussed during the course.

title: Zoonotic Pathogens in the Food Chain
description: This interdisciplinary course is designed for students to expand their knowledge of how modern food production systems contribute to the risk of zoonotic diseases, and where mitigation strategies need to be focused. Through case study discussions, students will examine attributes of microorganisms that allow potential contamination of food sources, as well as how new trends in animal production, such as organic livestock farming and raw milk consumption, influence safety in the food chain.

title: Seminar in Applied and Interdisciplinary Studies
description: Student presentations and discussion of current topics and recent findings in applied and interdisciplinary studies.

title: Problems in Applied and Interdisciplinary Studies
description: Opportunity for advanced independent study of a specific problem or technique in applied and interdisciplinary studies. Topics selected jointly by student and instructor.

title: Advanced Topics in Applied and Interdisciplinary Studies
description: Focus on advanced topics in applied and interdisciplinary studies.

title: Research in Applied and Interdisciplinary Studies
description: Research with a focus on applied science and interdisciplinary studies.

title: Large Fixed-Wing Operations Flight Lab
description: In this course, students will learn to fly a larger than small UAS. This will require students to fly within a regulatory environment outside of 14 CFR Part 107. Course delivery will focus on safety and crew resource management. Emphasis is on experiential learning.

title: Introduction to Advanced Air Mobility Systems
description: Students will be introduced to the Urban Air Mobility and Uncrewed Traffic Management ecosystem. Additional learning areas will include an overview of regulations, airspace structures, enabling technologies and subsystems, and principles of systems engineering.

title: System Architectures in Advanced Air Mobility Systems
description: Using a Systems Engineering approach, students will be introduced to the major technical components of Advanced Air Mobility frameworks and critical subsystems such as Autonomous Systems, Guidance and Navigation, Cyber-Physical Systems, Sensory Platforms, Ground Systems, and Uncrewed Traffic Management Systems. Other emerging topics will be included.

title: Aerospace Communication Systems
description: Students will be introduced to radio systems, air-to-air, air-to-ground communications, and an overview of current approaches to line-of-sight and beyond-line-of-sight techniques. Additional learning areas include Airspace integration UAS/UAM/ATM/ATC Technologies, BLOS and Satellite Data Link Connectivity, Communications system design, Review of models and techniques (e.g. Uplink/Downlink Model, Noise, SNR), Antennas design and propagation, and Cyber-attack challenges and mitigations.

title: Guidance and Navigation Systems
description: Students will develop an understanding of classical and advanced guidance and navigation theories to critically select design guidance and navigation systems.  Topics include corridor planning, uncrewed guidance, conventional and advanced guidance approaches, such as proportional navigation, geometric guidance, optimal guidance, GNSS (Global Navigation Satellite Systems), terrain-based navigation, SLAM (Simultaneous Localization and Mapping), cooperative guidance and collision avoidance.

title: Electromagnetic Threats and Mitigation Design for Advanced Air Mobility Systems
description: Fundamentals of Electromagnetic Environmental Effects (E3) for AAM systems and mitigation using analytical and software tools. Emphasis will be placed on understanding and hazard mitigation of electromagnetic (EM) energy coupling processes through various transfer paths to onboard receptor devices. E3 sources to be studied include lightning, high-intensity radiated fields (HIRF), portable electronic devices (PED), and precipitation static (P-static).

title: Introduction to Managerial Accounting
description: Introduces the concepts associated with managerial accounting including cost accounting, job order costing, activity-based costing, cost-volume profit, and incremental analysis.  Introduction to budgetary planning, variance analysis, and data analysis is included.

title: Introduction to Financial Accounting
description: Introduces the concepts associated with the accounting cycle, the revenue cycle, the expenditure cycle (including business investments), and the financing cycle.  Included is introduction to financial statements and data analysis.

title: Intermediate Accounting and Analytics I
description: Enhances the understanding of the conceptual framework, the accounting information system, the financial statements, revenue recognition, and current assets. These topics will be enhanced using analytics.

title: Taxation I
description: Fundamental concepts of income determination in federal and state income tax regulations; examination of the impact of tax regulation on business and personal financial planning and decision making.

title: Accounting Information Systems
description: This course describes how information flows through an enterprise, the sources and nature of documents, and the controls necessary to insure the accuracy and reliability of information. The course will also incorporate information technology controls, current computer systems, data analytics, and software applications into the course.

title: Cost Accounting
description: Identifying relevant accounting data and organizing, summarizing, and analyzing that data into information useful for planning and budgeting, decision making, controlling, and evaluating functions of management.

title: Intermediate Accounting and Analytics II
description: Enhances the understanding of long-term assets, current liabilities, long-term liabilities, and stockholders' equity concepts. These topics will be enhanced using analytics.

title: Auditing I
description: An introduction to the environment of auditing and the objectives and techniques of both financial and operational auditing.

title: Financial Statement Analysis
description: This course will develop a student's skills and knowledge in the analysis and interpretation of corporate financial statements and their related footnotes.  The class will encourage the student to think logically and creatively about the strengths and weaknesses of accounting information available to investors and shareholders.  The main course objective is for the student to be capable of doing an in-depth analysis of a company's financial and operating performance from several financial statements.

title: Accounting Analytics
description:
This course will develop in-depth knowledge of the use of Microsoft Excel and other technology to analyze complex accounting information.  The course will focus on using large volume data sets to simulate real world analytical problem solving.
.

title: Law For Accountants
description: An intensive study of an accountant's professional responsibilities to the public and the profession and the knowledge of the legal implications of business transactions, particularly as they relate to accounting and auditing.

title: Fraud and Forensics
description: This is a full semester course focused on the tools needed to conduct a fraud-sensitive audit or to conduct a fraud investigation.  As a part of this course, the student will learn numerous techniques used by fraudsters to deceive their victims as they misappropriate resources. In addition, the student will learn how to investigate accusations or suspicions of fraud from the initial gathering of evidence to the ultimate resolution of the investigation which could include criminal and/or civil lawsuits. Such understandings are important to the practicing auditor. Given the recent scandals in financial reporting, the auditor needs to be alert to signals of fraud in order to discharge his or her responsibilities to the profession, client, and public.

title: International and Multijurisdictional Taxation
description: Explores the fundamental principles underpinning the US tax law with regard to multijurisdictional activity (primarily international, some interstate).

title: Accounting Internship
description: Provides a full semester of practical accounting experience prior to entering graduate accounting program.

title: Intermediate Accounting and Analytics III
description: Builds on the second intermediate class. Enhances the understanding of investments, accounting for income taxes, pensions and post-retirement benefits, leases, accounting changes (including errors), the statement of cash flows, and disclosures.  These topics will be enhanced using analytics.

title: Accounting Research and Communication
description: Use of the sources of authoritative guidance in resolving complex, professionally oriented problems that include auditing, financial reporting, and tax reporting. Students analyze numerous unstructured cases and present their conclusion with written reports and oral presentations.

title: Foundations of Accounting
description: A study of business transactions, including identification of relevant economic events and how those events are recorded in financial statements, and a study of managerial accounting information for decision making, planning, and control.

title: Advanced Financial Reporting
description: An examination of the reporting requirements of large (often multinational) corporations, e.g., foreign currency translation, interim and segment reporting, and business combinations.

title: Professional Ethics in Accounting
description: Examination of ethical issues in industry and public accounting, including auditing standards, code of professional conduct, fraud, and internal controls.

title: Fraud and Forensics
description: This is a full semester course focused on the tools needed to conduct a fraud-sensitive audit or to conduct a fraud investigation.  As a part of this course, the student will learn numerous techniques used by fraudsters to deceive their victims as they misappropriate resources. In addition, the student will learn how to investigate accusations or suspicions of fraud from the initial gathering of evidence to the ultimate resolution of the investigation which could include criminal and/or civil lawsuits. Such understandings are important to the practicing auditor. Given the recent scandals in financial reporting, the auditor needs to be alert to signals of fraud in order to discharge his or her responsibilities to the profession, client, and public.

title: Advanced Auditing
description: An in-depth exposure to authoritative auditing pronouncements and specialized topics, e.g., statistical methods, EDP auditing, internal auditing, operational auditing, and audit management.

title: Corporate Taxation
description: A study of the federal taxation of corporations with emphasis on tax planning.

title: Partnership Taxation
description: Intensive study of the federal taxation of partnerships.

title: Business Consulting and Advanced Managerial Accounting
description: This class develops skills in consulting and advising businesses using advanced managerial accounting techniques and practices

title: Introduction to Academic Accounting Research
description: An introduction to academic accounting research, with an emphasis on educational/pedagogical research; practitioner research and basic research (archival, behavioral, and qualitative).

title: Advanced Accounting Theory
description: A critical examination of accounting theory with emphasis upon information economics, agency theory, and capital market information.

title: Advanced Topics in Taxation
description: Intensive examination of selected topics in taxation.

title: Design of Accounting and Business Information Processes
description: This course focuses on understanding and designing contemporary business and information processes in organizations, providing an introduction to database systems, project management and information systems controls and technology.

title: International Accounting
description: An examination of comparative international accounting systems, efforts to harmonize accounting standards internationally, problems of international financial analysis, accounting and tax issues of particular relevance to U.S. multinational enterprises.

title: International and Multijurisdictional Taxation
description: To explore the fundamental principles underpinning the US tax law with regard to multijurisdictional activity (international, interstate).  At the conclusion of the course, students should be able to identify, analyze and evaluate the following: The theoretical basis of the U.S. approach to international taxation; the U.S. tax treatment of the foreign activity of U.S. citizens, residents and domestic corporations; the U.S. tax treatment of foreigners engaged in business or investment activity in the U.S.; the key issues surrounding state and local taxation.

title: Merger and Acquisition Taxation
description: A study of the federal taxation of corporations, with an emphasis on tax planning related to mergers and acquisition.

title: Accounting Research and Communication
description: This is a team taught course that focuses on developing skills at the higher levels of Bloom's Taxonomy (analysis, synthesis and evaluation) to conduct technical research and communicate the results of that research in both oral and written forms.

title: Financial Statement Analysis and Business Valuation
description: This course is about the analysis of financial information for making decisions to invest in businesses.  The methods of fundamental analysis will be examined in detail and applied in cases and projects involving listed companies.  Topics include models of shareholder value, a comparison of accrual accounting and discounted cash flow approaches to valuation, the analysis of profitability, growth and value generation in a firm, diagnosing accounting quality, forecasting earnings and cash flows, pro-forma analysis for strategy and planning, and the determination of price/earnings (P/E) and market-to-book (P/B) ratios.

title: Accounting Analytics
description: This course will develop in-depth knowledge of the use of Microsoft Excel and other technology to analyze complex accounting information.  The course will focus on using large volume data sets to simulate real world analytical problem solving.

title: Accounting for Not-for-Profit Entities
description: An introduction to the source of authoritative guidance, rules and regulations that govern current reporting to external entities by not-for-profit entities.

title: Management Accounting and Business Problem Solving
description: A study of managerial accounting information for decision making, planning, and control.

title: Problems in Accounting
description: An in-depth study of specified topics in accounting.

title: Enterprise Information Systems Assurance
description: This course focuses on types of assurance available in an enterprise-wide information systems environment and methods for providing assurance from the view of management, external auditors and consultants. Assurance topics discussed include assurances related to systems security, fraud, business and information process integrity, and operational efficiency. Focus is on designing systems to increase the level of inherent assurance as well as auditability. This course also includes a service learning component.

title: Seminars in Professional Accounting
description: Reviews content and skills for professional certification.

title: Accounting Internship
description: This course provides an opportunity for students to experience firsthand the work performed by typical accountants on a day to day basis.

title: Accounting International Experience
description: Students will experience a faculty supervised travel abroad experience that will expose them to international issues related to accounting and general business.

title: Composites I
description: Introduction to composite materials used in a broad range of industries with an emphasis on aero structure. Topics include defining a composite, understanding the materials, safety aspects, and shop layouts that are used to produce composite parts. An overview of the construction methods, understanding how to inspect components for damage, and procedures to perform the actual repair used in industry today. Students develop an understanding of how to design/source industrial composite fasteners and install into the composite structure and the science of coatings for the protection of both the composite materials as well as metallic components adjacent to composites. The labs support the lectures with projects designed to give the student hands on application.

title: Introduction to Composite and Nonmetallic Structures
description: Introduces composite materials used in the design and production of legacy and modern aircraft. Topics include material characteristics, fabrication, and inspection; repair of non-metallic primary and secondary structures and materials using manufacturers' instructions. Materials include fabric, wood, fiberglass, plastics, honeycomb, and others. Students practice the selection, installation, application of protective coatings, and removal of special fasteners used in these structures. Laboratory exercises emphasize the fabrication and inspection of modern composite structures.

title: Composites II
description: Continued study of the mechanical properties of several composite materials along with strengths and limitations. Topics include gelcoats for tooling and part production, layup of semi complex composite parts using wet layup and vacuum bagging techniques, simple mold development, construction, and repair. Additionally, students are introduced to compression composite molding compounds and tooling required for production, and composite facility layout and outfitting. The lab will support the lectures with projects designed to give the student the hands on application.

title: Composites III
description: Continued study of the highly complex composite parts and associated molding technologies used to produce these parts. Topics include the construction of resin infusion parts and mold construction, resusable vacuum bagging materials, application of preimpregnated materials and tooling designed to withstand oven cure cycles, discussion of advanced materials and techniques new to the industry. The lab will support the lectures with projects designed to give the student the hands on application in a relative environment.

title: Composites Capstone
description: Capstone for students completing the Composite Repair Certificate. This is an observed independent study course. The topic is chosen by the student and the instructor. The instructor is to facilitate the student's acquisition of materials, facilities, and additional resources needed to complete the project. Periodic updates will be provided to share status, progress, and needs throughout the project. The student will present the finished project to peers, instructors, and sponsors.

title: Introduction to Addiction
description: Analyzes one’s knowledge, beliefs, and attitudes concerning addiction, within the context of professional addiction counseling values and ethics. Examines evidence-based treatment strategies of addiction prevention, treatment, recovery, and relapse. Discusses the impact of addiction on the individual, family, and society.

title: Addiction Pharmacology
description: Evaluate drug use in modern society. Understand the impact of commonly-used substances on the body. Review pharmacological treatment approaches to withdrawal, treatment, recovery, and relapse prevention.

title: Professional, Ethical, and Legal Issues in Counseling
description: Explores professional, legal, and ethical issues facing addiction counselors or those in similar helping professional roles. Topics include client rights and responsibilities, confidentiality, and other legal considerations. Follows a discussion of general principles of ethics and major principles of professional conduct.

title: Holistic Recovery and Addiction Services Coordination
description: Introduces students to holistic recovery and the roles administrators, counselors, and case managers play in assisting clients in achieving their treatment and recovery goals. Explores life challenges that affect a person's ability to enter recovery and maintain it. Examines advocacy skills, coordination of services across multiple systems, and referral activities used to connect clients with community resources.

title: Methods of Individual Counseling
description: Provides comprehensive examination of substance use treatment and rehabilitation, and includes a thorough examination of the treatment models of addiction, recovery, and relapse. Equips students with the theoretical basis of treatment and ability to clinically assess individuals with substance-related problems and provide evidence-based interventions.

title: Practicum in Addiction Counseling
description: Integrates and applies knowledge and skills from coursework to addiction counseling settings for a practicum field experience consisting of 400 clock-hours. The practicum experience will consist of clinical activities that integrate didactic learning supporting the diagnosis and treatment of substance use disorders. Students will be under the supervision of a LAC or LMAC.

title: Professional Seminar
description: Integrates academic learning, values exploration and skills acquisition in order to make a successful transition from student to professional.  Extensive classroom sharing and discussion will enable students placed in one addiction counseling setting to learn the various facets of the vast array of placement experiences of their classmates.

title: Methods of Group Counseling
description: Provides the theoretical framework for effective practice with groups. Facilitates the development of group leaders who possess an understanding of group dynamics and effective leadership skills. Provides students with opportunities to acquire the knowledge base, values, and practice skills necessary for evidence-based work with multicultural populations. Explores methods to establish group goals and treatment outcomes. Defines stages of group development and the challenges and tasks associated with each stage. Identifies value and ethical dilemmas that are common in group practice.

title: Addiction, Family and Continuity
description: Explores family, social, and community systems in relation to substance use disorder problems. Identifies addictive and intergenerational patterns within families and evaluates family, parenting, and couples counseling methods in relation to various ethnicities and cultures. Reviews relational clinical models including developmental, systemic, solution-focused, and narrative approaches and develops an understanding of the relationship between family factors and recovery.

title: Co-Occurring Disorders and Addictions Treatment
description: Explores co-occurring disorders and treatment needs of those who have a psychiatric or mental health disorder in combination with a substance abuse disorder. Examines assessment and treatment of persons with co-occurring disorders, along with evidence-based strategies for managing risks associated with treating individuals who have co-occurring disorders.

title: Aerospace Studies Lab
description: Leadership Laboratory (LLAB) is a dynamic and integrated grouping of leadership developmental activities designed to meet the needs and expectations of prospective Department of the Air Force second lieutenants and complement the AFROTC academic program. It is a student planned, organized, and executed practicum conducted under the supervision of the Detachment Commander and Operations Flight Commander.  Objectives are proficiency-based or characterized as task knowledge or performance. It is required to teach all lesson objectives and track the proficiency level for each student using the AFROTC-approved LLAB Objectives Tracker.  LLAB objectives may be presented throughout the academic year in any order. Proficiency levels defined for each objective are based on the minimum skill level required for successful completion.

title: The Foundation of the United States Air Force - Heritage and Values I
The Foundations of the United States Air Force-Heritage and Values II
description: A continuation for AERO 110.  Represents the second semester of a two semester class. ""Heritage and Values

title: Team and Leadership Fundamentals II
description: A continuation for AERO 210.  Represents the second semester of a two semester class. ""Leadership Foundation

title: AFROTC Field Training
description: Provides cadets with a 13-day program designed to evaluate military leadership and discipline, determine potential for entry into the Professional Officer Course (POC), and stratification amongst their peers.

title: Officer Leadership Studies 3B
description: A continuation for AERO 310.  Represents the second semester of a two semester class. ""Leading People

title: Briefing For Air Force Commissioned Service
Regional Studies and Defense Policy
description: Designed for college seniors and provides them the foundation to understand their role as military officers and how they are directly tied to our National Security Strategy. It is an overview of the complex social and political issues facing the military profession and requires a measure of sophistication commensurate with the senior college level.

title: Aerospace Studies/Civil Military Relationships
description: A continuation for AERO 410.  Represents the second semester of a two semester class.  Designed for college seniors and provides them the foundation to understand their role as military officers and how they are directly tied to our National Security Strategy. It is an overview of the complex social and political issues facing the military profession and requires a measure of sophistication commensurate with the senior college level.

title: Briefing For Air Force Commissioned Service
Problems in Aerospace Studies
description: Work offered in any of the AFROTC general or professional courses for students out of phase for graduation; material covered in a basic or advanced course.

title: African Study Abroad
description: The study abroad educational trip will provide students the opportunity to apply their knowledge of an African language and to learn first hand about various African societies and cultures, farming systems, environment, health and other issues related to their host country.

title: Seminar l
description: Economics of Poverty and Wealth Creation in Africa (social science component). Explores opportunities for breaking the cycle of poverty across the continent using strategic wealth creation techniques that overcome poverty. Focusing on the agriculture and food system, the environment and natural resources, and the health of the African people.

title: Seminar ll
description: African Development. Introduction to current political and economic conditions facing Africa from historical, social, economic, political, and human context. Solutions to the current problems confronting the African continent are analyzed.

title: Agricultural Communications Orientation
description: Orientation to the agricultural communications and journalism industry, on-campus resources, and college-level skill building.

title: Introduction to Agricultural Communications
description: Introduction to agricultural communications programs, activities, careers, and trends. This course will cover audience analysis across media platforms, Associated Press writing style, and understanding target audiences.

title: Layout and Design Principles
description: This course uses leading industry-adopted software to develop printed communications pieces to the point of being ""printer ready"", addressing design concepts and layout aspects critical to successfully printing a project.  Assignments will integrate software functionality and design concepts with print production technology requirements.

title: Agricultural Business Communications
description: Written communications as used in agricultural industries. A writing course emphasizing the tools and techniques of writing memos, letters, short and long reports, proposals, and presentations.

title: Topics in Agricultural and Natural Resources Communications
description: Examination of current topics in undergraduate agricultural communications.

title: Problems in Agricultural and Natural Resources Communications
description: Independent guided studies in any of the many fields associated with agricultural and natural resources communications.

title: Careers in Agricultural Communications
description: This course provides students with advanced knowledge regarding careers in agricultural communications. Students will learn about how to find, apply for, and manage a position in agricultural communications, including topics related to job search, professional development, workplace conflict, portfolio development, interview skills, writing in a professional environment, and salary negotiation. An emphasis in workplace ethics through case studies and service learning will be provided.

title: Communicating in the Agricultural Industry
description: The purpose of this course is to help students identify the influence of communication styles and theories related to issues in agriculture and natural resources. The course will build skill in oral and visual communication through formal and informal presentations and discussions.

title: Applied Editing in Agriculture and Natural Resources
description: Students gain hands-on experience editing written content for diverse audiences and platforms, with emphasis on Associated Press and other professional styles. Through real-world examples representing agriculture and natural resources topics, students sharpen their fact-checking and proofreading skills while also learning to critically evaluate and elevate written work.

title: Application of Layout and Printing Techniques in Agriculture and Natural Resources
description: This course advances students' understanding of how industry-adopted software and hardware are used to develop printed communications pieces, going from individual application functionality to application integration for developing and managing production projects.

title: Agricultural Student Magazine
description: Planning, interviewing, and preparing stories, headlines, layouts, advertising, and editing, for the Kansas State Agriculturist published by students in the College of Agriculture.

title: Topics in Agricultural Communications
description: Discussions of topics, studies, and activities important to agricultural communications.

title: Undergraduate Research in Agricultural Communications
description: This course uses qualitative, quantitative or mixed methods to conduct an agricultural and environmental communications research project. A faculty coordinator will assist students through the stages of conducting a research project and reporting its outcomes.

title: Video in Agriculture and Food
description: Theory of visual storytelling is combined with basic and intermediate video camera and non-linear editing instruction to produce TV documentaries, magazine-type stories, and personal videos for web and broadcast distribution.

title: Internship in Agricultural Communications
description: The intern works in a professional capacity in areas such as print journalism, electronic media, advertising, photography, and public relations. Student is supervised by a professional and a faculty member. One hour of credit for each four weeks of supervised work. Internship report and presentation required.

title: New Media Technology
description: This course focuses on the study and application of emerging "new media" technologies to effectively communicate information and market products and programs to general and targeted audiences. Students will study underlying communication theories such as Roger's Theory of Innovation Diffusion to provide a context of how society adopts and incorporates new technology.

title: Capstone Seminar in Agricultural Communications
description: A capstone experience and project for agricultural communications and journalism students. Includes analysis of trends and issues in the field and discussion of communications planning.

title: Crisis Communication
description: Focus is placed on the application of effective crisis communication practices and techniques.  Students will learn relevant interpersonal and organizational communication theory, assess/evaluate organizational risks, and develop a crisis communication plan. Students will partner with a community business to create and deliver a comprehensive crisis plan.

title: Advanced Multimedia Production in Agriculture and Food
description: Provides an understanding and application of advanced digital media production techniques. This is a ""practicum-based"" course focused on creating multimedia products for educational purposes. Students will create short-format, multi-media pieces on both agricultural research and activities conducted at Kansas State University and the Kansas agriculture industry.

title: Study Experience in Agricultural Communications and Journalism
description: Seminar and travel course to prepare students before and after the experience and for students to analyze, critique and report their experiences associated with study tours and short courses.

title: Environmental Communication
description: Combines theoretical discussions with practical experience regarding communication about environmental issues and provides introduction to natural and applied science topics related to the communications plan.

title: Technical Communication in Agricultural Education and Communications
description: Audience identification, writing, editing, formatting and production of social science-based materials for publication. Communicating information relevant to human subject research in agriculture, natural resources, and life sciences to research peers.

title: Proposal Development in Agricultural Education and Communications
description: Assist graduate students in the preparation of their thesis or dissertation research proposal. Students will produce the first three chapters of their thesis by the end of the course.

title: Grantwriting in Agricultural Education and Communications
description: Experience of navigating the research grant writing process, covering the process from idea conception through planning, proposing, receiving, executing grant-funded projects. Independent grant proposal as a major assignment in this course.

title: Graphical Design and Theory in Agricultural Communications
description: Graphic design and software skills specific to industries in Agriculture, Food, and Life Sciences using industry-standard software to prepare text and graphics and package them for use in print production.

title: Utilizing Online Media in Agricultural Communications
description: Identify agricultural audiences, conduct analyses, and use results to evaluate and produce online media that utilizes design fundamentals, visual communication theories, and new media technology.

title: Topics in Agricultural Communications
description: Examination of current topics in agricultural communications.

title: Foundations of Agricultural Communications
description: Historical foundations of agricultural communications philosophical concepts, changes over time, industry today and the future. In-depth analysis and discussion of communication theories, application and current issues.

title: Scientific Communication
description: Written, visual, and oral communications for scientists. Attention is focused on literature reviews, scientific papers, graphics, poster presentations, and oral paper presentations. Grant applications, ethics, and communications with non-science audience are discussed.

title: Graduate Studies in Leadership Communication
description: This course examines the intersections of community-engaged scholarship and leadership through practices of relationship, story, strategy, and action. Students are introduced to resources and opportunities for pursuing public engagement, community-engaged scholarship, and successful program completion.

title: Seminar in Leadership Communication
description: This seminar provides doctoral students opportunities for collaboration and mentorship with peers, faculty, and visiting scholars, and exploration of career pathways in public engagement.

title: Communicating Ethical Issues in Agriculture
description: This course provides an introduction to communicating ethical theories in the context of agriculture. Ethical theory and current research are used to critique contemporary issues in agriculture.

title: New Media Technology
description: This course focuses on the study and application of emerging ""new media"" technologies to effectively communicate information and market products and programs to general and targeted audiences. Students will study underlying communication theories such as Roger's Theory of Innovation Diffusion to provide a context of how society adopts and incorporates new technology.

title: Diffusion of Innovations
description: This course introduces a structured approach for dealing with the organizational and human aspects of technology transition, including the key concepts of resistance and change management, organizational change, communications, and processes by which professional change agents influence the introductions, adoption, and diffusion of technological change.

title: Theory in Agricultural Communications
description: The study of major communication theories and theorists in the context of agricultural communications.

title: Approaches to Public/Community Engagement
description: The course will provide students a survey of key concepts and theoretical frameworks associated with deliberative democracy and public engagement. A specific focus will be given to how forms of community-engaged scholarship intersect course content in both theory and practice.

title: Knowledge Management in Agriculture and Natural Resources
description: Knowledge Management is a discipline that takes a comprehensive, systematic approach to the information assets of an organization by identifying, capturing, collecting, organizing, indexing, storing, integrating, retrieving and sharing them. This strategy turns an agricultural and natural organization's intellectual assets into greater productivity and increased competitiveness. It encourages collaboration for the sharing of expertise and knowledge of the best practices. This course examines knowledge management as it is emerging and being applied in agriculture and natural resources.

title: Master's Project
description: The project is designed for students to complete a practitioner-based scholarly project that integrates theory, research and application in relationship to a current need or issue approved by the student's supervisory committee. While research is included in this project, the focus will be on demonstrating an ability to apply the knowledge and skills learned within the context of a for-profit or not-for-profit enterprise, school, community, organization or policy process.

title: Master's Thesis
description: Students investigate an academic problem in their chosen area of interest and document this investigation in a thesis. The investigation integrates and applies knowledge from courses in the program to address unstructured problems. The thesis is a practice in applying scientific criteria on a problem or topic approved by the student's supervisory committee and also is practice in applying scientific methods.

title: Communication Theories and Engagement
description: Focusing on the intersections of communication theory and engagement, this course reviews the paradigmatic evolution of communication theories as they relate to culture, organizations, leadership, social influence, emerging media technologies and other pertinent areas. Additionally, students will be exposed to philosophical and theoretical work pertaining to community engaged research.

title: Public Problem-Solving
description: This course is a rigorous and applied exploration of the fundamentals of social science research, social sector evaluation, and knowledge mobilization.

title: Research in Leadership Communication
description: Doctoral research demonstrates the candidate's ability to conduct significant original research related to leadership and communication, to analyze the information obtained from the research, and to present the results in a dissertation format appropriate to the field.

title: Biological Materials And Machine Functions In Agriculture
Energy Use And Control In Agricultural Systems
Energy Use And Control In Agricultural Systems 2
Thermal Processing Operations In Food Engineering
Design Of Control Systems For Agricultural Machines & Proces
Principles Of Occupational Safety And Health Management
Agricultural Economics and Agribusiness Orientation
description: Introduction to agricultural economics and agribusiness programs, activities, resources, and careers. Required of all freshmen in agricultural economics or agribusiness at K-State.

title: Decision Tools for Agricultural Economics and Agribusiness
description: Development of foundational computer-based empirical skills that address economic and business issues in food and agriculture. Students will learn how to model information on an issue or problem using mathematical spreadsheets to find relevant answers for decision makers and stakeholders.

title: Agricultural Economics and Agribusiness
description: A course suggested for all students interested in the agricultural economy. A study of economic principles, with emphasis on their application to the solution of farm, agribusiness, and agricultural industry problems in relationship to other sectors of the United States economy and foreign countries.

title: Honors Agricultural Economics and Agribusiness
description: A course suggested for all honors students interested in the agricultural economy. A study of economic principles, with emphasis on their application to the solution of farm, agribusiness, natural resource and agricultural industry problems in relationship to other sectors of the United States economy and foreign countries. The course will include extensive discussion, writing, and computer assignments on application of economic principles to real-world problems and issues.

title: Small Business Operations
description: Opportunities in business ownership, principles governing the starting of a small enterprise; importance, status, problems, and management of a small business.

title: Grain and Livestock Marketing Systems
description: Survey of the institutions and mechanisms that facilitate and regulate the sale and marketing of grain and livestock commodities. Topics include the physical and informational flows in the commodity supply chains, cash pricing, commodity grades to improve market efficiency and the governmental regulations and agencies influencing commodity trading.

title: Farm and Ranch Management
description: Decision-making process, cost concepts, farm records and financial management, budgeting, time value of money, and introduction to whole farm/ranch planning.

title: Contemporary Issues in Global Food and Agricultural Systems
description: A survey of contemporary economic issues affecting world agricultural systems including population growth, trade, globalization, natural resource and environmental policy.

title: Food and Agribusiness Management
description: A study of marketing, production, risk, human resource management, and financial management in agribusiness firms. Particular attention is given to the application of economic principles to the management of marketing and farm supply firms.

title: The Global Agricultural Economy, Hunger And Poverty
Commodity Futures
description: This course is designed to introduce students to the purpose, operation, and use of commodity futures and options markets. The objectives are to: (1) understand why futures exchanges and commodity futures contracts exist; (2) understand and be able to forecast basis; (3) understand hedging and be able to design hedging strategies for various commodity producers and users; (4) understand both put and call options and their potential use in a commodity risk management program; and (5) understand the usefulness and shortcomings of fundamental and technical analysis.

title: Agricultural Economics and Agribusiness Seminar
description: Seminars of special interest will be offered upon sufficient demand in selected areas relating to agricultural economics and agribusiness or competitive teams qualifying for academic credit.

title: Agribusiness Internship
description: Approved and supervised work-study programs in various areas of agribusiness.

title: International Food and Agribusiness Study Tour
description: An experiential learning course to prepare students for and to immerse them in an international study tour of selected food and agribusiness industries.  The topics vary by the tour and the industries studied.

title: Agricultural Economics Global Learning Experience
description: An immersive, faculty-guided international course in which students apply disciplinary concepts through site visits, field activities, and cultural engagement. Emphasizes experiential learning and structured reflection within the context of the host country.

title: Cooperative Management
description: This course focuses on the unique aspects of the cooperative business model and its evolution over time. Students will utilize course material to gain a deeper understanding of the history and need for cooperatives by applying economic, finance, marketing, and management tools to cooperatives.

title: Returning to the Farm
description: This course is designed for students who are planning or would like to farm in the future. Students will develop a feasible plan tailored to their individual circumstances.

title: Farm and Ranch Income Tax Management
description: This course introduces unique income tax provisions that apply to commercial agricultural producers. Students will better understand the importance of accounting records, the income tax impact of farm management decisions, and tools available to manage income taxes. Emphasis will also be placed on the importance of considering income taxes when planning for transition of the family farm or ranch from one generation to the next.

title: Computer Applications In Agricultural Economics & Agribus
Production Economics
description: Application of economic principles to problems of agricultural production. Analysis of consumer demand for agricultural products and input and output decisions of the agricultural firm. AGEC 505 is a continuation of this course and they are intended to be taken in consecutive semesters.

title: Data Analysis and Optimization
description: Analysis of agricultural business and economic data and optimization for decision making.  Study applications of regression, time series analysis and forecasting to agricultural and economic data. Introduction to mathematical programming to model optimization of problems commonly encountered in agricultural economics.

title: Agricultural Market Structures
description: Theory and application of economic principles to marketing problems in agriculture. Pricing of agricultural output and productive services under various forms of economic organization and competition; regional specialization, location, and trade; determinants of economic change; evaluation of economic and consumer welfare.

title: Agricultural Finance
description: Analysis of capital investments, interpretation of financial statements, capital structure considerations for agricultural firms, and farm real estate pricing.

title: Food and Agribusiness Marketing
description: A broad view of marketing; food markets and consumption; marketing functions and institutions; prices, competition, and marketing costs; functional and organizational issues; food marketing regulations; commodity marketing.

title: Agricultural Law and Economics
description: The legal framework for decision making by farm firms, families, and individuals; liabilities, real and personal property, contracts, uniform commercial code, organization of farm firms, intergeneration property transfers, water law, fence law, federal and state regulatory power, insurance, income tax, and social security.

title: Economic Principles Of Agricultural Business Firms
Market Fundamentals and Futures/Options Trading
description: This is an experiential course in the trading of commodity futures and options. Attention is focused on the study of market price determination, the implications of market efficiency notions, and on actual trading of futures and options. Students invest in a commodity educational trading fund. Class approves recommendations by vote, orders are placed with a broker, and the class monitors open trades. The pool balance at the end of the semester is redistributed to students.

title: Export Marketing Of Agricultural And Food Products
Natural Resource and Environmental Economics
description: Emphasis on the application of demand, supply, and price concepts in the study of natural resource use, policies, and management. Interdependence between environmental quality and economic actions are examined through discussion of property rights, economic incentives, externalities, and economic components of environmental policies.

title: Agricultural Sales
description: Agricultural Sales will introduce students to the role of sales and sales management in the agricultural industry.

title: Agricultural Economics and Agribusiness Seminar
description: Seminars of special interest will be offered upon sufficient demand in selected areas relating to agricultural economics and agribusiness.

title: Undergraduate Research in Agricultural Economics
description: Mentored undergraduate research experience in agricultural economics and agribusiness.

title: Data Analytics for Agricultural Economics
description: Advanced collection and applied analysis of agricultural business and economics data for decision making.  Applied communication of results and implications.

title: Food Manufacturing, Distribution, and Retailing Economics
description: Advanced study of the economic and business aspects of the food industry from processing to distribution to retailing. Emerging trends in nutrition, food consumption, food safety and supply chain arrangements along with management and marketing issues in the food industry will be studied.

title: Agricultural Economics And Agribusiness Honors Problems
Farm Management Strategy
description: A study of management concepts, tools, and decision strategies applied to farm firms. Alternative measures of farm business performances, as well as planning and evaluation techniques for an uncertain environment, are examined.

title: Food and Agribusiness Management Strategy
description: This course integrates the risk, production, marketing, and financial management strategies of agribusiness firms. Special attention is given to the application of economic theory and quantitative analysis to business decision-making processes. In addition to case studies, a variety of analytical techniques will focus on both markets and firms involved in the production and marketing of food commodities.

title: Price Analysis and Forecasting
description: The analysis of selected agricultural prices; application of regression analysis to price analysis, the role of futures markets and market efficiency, optimal hedging strategies, commodity option pricing, and price forecasting.

title: Current Agricultural and Natural Resource Policy Issues
description: Current issues in agricultural and natural resource policy from divergent perspectives. Classroom discussion, debate, writing assignments, and student presentations. Current events are analyzed and synthesized from both economic and noneconomic perspectives. Topics may include environmental issues, international agricultural development, the politics of farm programs, and the relationship between technology, agriculture, and society.

title: Global Agricultural and Economic Development
description: This course covers contemporary challenges in global development, with an emphasis on agricultural issues. Students will analyze problems using economic models, and develop empirical skills by learning key data collection concepts, and working with survey data. Topics include poverty, technology adoption, agricultural policy, health, and program evaluation.

title: International Agricultural Trade
description: Applied economics of agricultural trade. Emphasis on why trade occurs, current agricultural trade patterns, the effects of agricultural policy on trade, and the institutions of trade.

title: Agribusiness Logistics
description: Planning for efficient use of transportation, storage and processing facilities in the handling of raw materials and products for agribusiness firms, controlling shipments and inventory in coordination with warehouse and handling operations, and scientific selection of routes, schedules, and equipment.

title: Agricultural Economics and Agribusiness Seminar
description: Seminars of special interest will be offered upon sufficient demand in selected areas relating to agricultural economics and agribusinesses.

title: Risk Management
description: Analytical concepts and quantitative tools to make better decisions in risky situations. Identifying various types of risk an operation faces, measuring the degree of risk, and exploring ways to manage risk. Includes portfolio analysis, futures and options, contracting, and insurance. Emphasis on computer applications. Topics are focused on agriculture but are applicable to other industries and household decisions.

title: Applied Agribusiness Economics
description: Applications of economic principles to problems of agricultural production and agribusinesses. Demand theory and economic forecasting will be used to examine changes in demand and business trends. Cost minimization and profit maximization will be used to examine firm and market issues.

title: Introduction to Computer Decision Tools for Agribusiness
description: Development of decision tools for agribusiness using spreadsheets. Topics include internet as a data source, budget development and analysis, financial calculations, regression, search techniques, and optimization.

title: Comparative Food and Agriculture Systems
description: This course provides a comparative analysis of the business and economic situation of the food and agriculture sector around the world. The course will cover the European Union, the Former Soviet Union, MERCORSUR, and Southeast Asia, Oceania and possibly other regions. The course will focus briefly on the historical development, the current situation, and the future outlook of the food and agriculture sector. Upon completion of this course, students will be able to evaluate the comparative advantage of the United States in the production of food and fiber.

title: Optimization Techniques For Agricultural Economics
description: Application of optimization techniques including linear programming and nonlinear programming for research and decision analysis in agricultural economics.

title: Agribusiness Financial Management
description: This course covers financial management, long-term investment analysis, the performance of alternative investments, and the theory of efficient markets. Topics covered include comparative financial analysis, short-term budgeting, and capital budgeting. Applications include agribusiness decision making and project analysis.

title: Agribusiness Risk Management
description: Various forms of risk inherent to firms operating in agricultural and food product markets are explored. Risk management techniques including diversification, insurance, forward contracting, hedging and options are examined through applied problems with the use of simulation. Emphasis is placed on managing risk associated with input prices, output prices, enterprise organization and financial instruments.

title: Applied Agribusiness Logistics
description: A study of agribusiness logistics and the fundamental concepts underpinning logistical decisions and their impact on a firm's sustainability. Topics include supply chain management, transportation and storage, contracting and out sourcing, strategic logistic planning, network analysis, and B2B e-commerce in food and agriculture. Focus will be on agribusiness logistics issues in grain and grain-based food supply chains and in the livestock and meat supply chains, including global logistics.

title: Sales and Marketing in the Global Animal Heath Industry
description: This course is designed to introduce students to marketing and sales in the complex world of animal health industry.  Understanding product life cycles; product development; forecasting; planning; positioning and branding; as well as promotion and compliance. Markets addressed include business to business food animal markets and companion animal consumer markets.

title: Seminar In Agricultural Economics Analysis
description: Seminar on methods of economic analysis.

title: Risk Management Fellows Seminar
description: This course serves as The Center for Risk Management Education and Research Student Fellows seminar course.  Students will conduct a risk management research project.

title: Applied Econometric Analysis
description: Use of econometrics to enhance agribusiness decision making is the focus. Concepts of simple and multiple regression and limited dependent variable models are presented. Econometric issues of functional form, relevant variables, predictive performance, and model diagnostics will be examined. Numerous applications of techniques to agribusiness problems are used.

title: Optimization Techniques For Agribusiness
description: This course is an introduction to optimization techniques designed to provide quantitative support for agribusiness decision making. Upon completion of this course, students will be able to construct and evaluate optimization models useful for evaluating linear problems, integer problems, and nonlinear program.

title: International Agribusiness and Policy Analysis
description: Policies affecting agribusiness firms will be examined. These topics may include U.S. agricultural policies, fiscal and monetary policies, international trade agreements, environmental issues, and current policies influencing agribusiness firms.

title: Economics of the Global Animal Heath Industry
description: Economic Issues in the Global Animal Health Industry (AGEC 780) provides a forum to study the economics and business challenges and opportunities confronting the animal health industry.  Policy issues in the various significant animal sectors are examined to provide tools to develop the appropriate responses to ensure sustainable superior performance .

title: Agricultural Marketing
description: The study of the demand for supply of agricultural commodities, alternative market structures, the dynamics of marketing institutions that affect market structures, governmental intervention in agricultural markets, futures markets in agriculture, and international agricultural commodities trade.

title: Price, Income and Trade Policies in Agriculture
description: This course examines the effects of agricultural policies on the welfare of society and the distribution of benefits and costs within the global market. Modern tools are developed to examine the causes and consequences of policies.

title: Advanced Farm Economics
description: A study of managerial techniques and processes applied to farm firms involved in production and marketing of agricultural products. This study includes managerial planning, evaluation, and control of farm businesses.

title: International Agricultural Development
description: To study the principles of economic development when applied to the development of the agricultural sector. This includes the examination of national and international strategies and policies that inhibit and/or encourage the development of the agricultural sectors in low income countries. Individual study is encouraged to meet student interest: (a) in understanding the process and including an appreciation of the interdependence between low and high income countries and (b) receiving an introduction to agricultural project analysis.

title: Farming Systems Approach To Sustainable Development
Production Economics II
description: Economic theories of choice under conditions of imperfect knowledge (i.e. under risk and uncertainty) and the application of these theories to production decisions.

title: Natural Resource Policy
description: The economic tolls of welfare analysis, non-market valuation, and dynamics are used to evaluate natural resource use, natural resource policies, and conflicts among users, conservationists, and preservationists.

title: International Markets and Agricultural Trade
description: International Markets and Agricultural Trade bridges the fundamental concepts of international trade with the practical analysis of trade policies. The course seeks to develop a solid foundation for future practitioners at industry or public institutions and for those who will undertake more in-depth empirical research involving trade issues.

title: Agribusiness Industry Structures
description: Applied industrial organization and competitive relationships in agriculture markets. Emphasis on the role of bargaining power, vertical coordination and integration, contracting, and other forms of vertical control. Product differentiation, branding and advertising of commodities and anti-trust laws.

title: Advanced Food and Agribusiness Management
description: The objective of the course is to demonstrate how economic concepts underscore strategic management principles. Applications to food and agribusiness firms are used to show how managers and firms function when the assumptions of perfect competition are relaxed. Students will complete a market analysis study as part of the course requirements.

title: Research Methods in Economics
description: A study of scientific methodology in economic research including the history of various debates regarding methodology in economics. The course also deals with problem definitions, formulation of hypotheses, listing of hypotheses, and presentation of research results.

title: Agricultural Demand and Commodity Marketing
description: A study of the demand for and supply of farm products, price information and markets, the causes of price variations and instability, the dynamic analysis of agricultural prices.

title: Economics of Agricultural Production
description: A study of agricultural production response to prices; methods of estimating supply response and price expectations; the effects of government and institutions on agricultural supply and the role of risk, technical change, and the number and size of farms on agricultural supply.

title: Advanced Resource and Environmental Economics
