description: Dynamics analysis of natural resource and environmental problems, including the optimal extraction of nonrenewable resources, optimal management of renewable resources, and the optimal regulation of stock pollutants.

title: Quantitative Topics in Agricultural Economics
description: A study of recent developments reported in the literature concerning quantitative methods of analysis in agricultural economics and economics. The study will include assigned projects to apply selected techniques of analysis.

title: Seminar in Agricultural Economics
description: Problems and current developments in agricultural economics.

title: Independent Study of Advanced Topics In Agricultural Economics
description: Advanced independent study of an agricultural economics topic based upon a student proposal approved by the student's supervisory committee

title: Orientation in Agricultural Education
description: Develop an understanding to the expectations of college and program responsibilities. A foundation of the high quality teaching and secondary agriculture programs. Active learning will include discussions, applications and a day long tour of education systems.

title: Foundations of Diversified Agriculture
description: Exploration of the program requirements, responsibilities, methodology, organization, current trends, and issues. Active involvement in discussions of the agriculture industry and design a program plan to meet career aspirations.

title: Equipment and Material Management
description: Provides a foundation for teaching and managing laboratories related to power, structural and technical systems.  Laboratory safety procedures and practices will be applied to the instructional setting. Includes material selection & management, project planning & design and facility management.

title: Topics in Agricultural Education
description: Examination of current topics in undergraduate agricultural communications.

title: Problems in Agricultural Education
description: Independent guided studies in any of the many fields associated with agricultural education.

title: Introduction to Agricultural Education
description: Introduction to the program responsibilities, methodology, organization, current trends and issues, and future direction of programs in agricultural education. Students will be actively involved in the discussion and application of course material both in the classroom and in early field experiences conducted as a part of this course.

title: Diversified Agriculture Foundations
description: Discussion and exploration of the program requirements, distance learning considerations, organization, current trends, and issues. Design a program plan to meet career aspirations in the agriculture industry.

title: Field Experience in Agricultural Education
description: A course for prospective teachers to help bridge the gap between classroom theory and student teaching. Emphasis will be on observation of and participation in school and community organizations and programs.

title: Leadership and Professional Development in Agricultural Education
description: This course provides an introduction to the leadership skills needed in the role of FFA advisor and professional agricultural educator including career, professional, and student leadership development and FFA chapter management.

title: Leadership Development in Agricultural Youth Organizations
description: Facilitate leadership development in youth through participation in FFA. Explores youth development stages, how to help youth grow their leadership skills through agriculture youth organizations and activities, and organizational structures.

title: Agricultural Project Management
description: Applied project management methodology, tools, and techniques for agricultural industries. Project organization, planning, execution, and communications; the project life cycle; risk analysis; and best practices in project management.

title: Undergraduate practicum in agricultural-related occupations
description: Related occupational or professional experiences in approved industry, school, Cooperative Extension Service, or similar agency setting under faculty supervision.

title: Topics in Agricultural Education
description: Examination of current topics in undergraduate agricultural education.

title: Undergraduate Research in Agricultural Education
description: This course uses qualitative, quantitative or mixed methods to conduct an agricultural education research project. A faculty supervisor will assist students through the stages of identifying, conducting and reporting the results of a social science research project connected to an agricultural education topic.

title: Methods of Teaching Agriculture in the Secondary and Middle Schools
description: Principles of teaching applied to content area instruction in the secondary and middle schools; motivation; organization of subject matter; lesson planning; evaluation and reporting; challenging the levels of ability; organization and management of the classroom; methodology and materials of the secondary schools.

title: Block II Lab:  Content Area Methods and Field Experience
description: This field-based experience provides the opportunity for students to plan, teach, and evaluate agricultural education lessons within a placement at the middle or secondary level. This practicum also requires students to incorporate reading methods and apply instructional technology in their instruction.

title: Educating the Public about Agriculture
description: Organization and preparation of materials and methods used in educating public audiences about agriculture through non-formal settings, businesses, and extension disciplines.

title: Planning Programs in Extension & Non-Formal Education
description: Principles and procedures used in planning and conducting Extension programs. Including workshops, traninings, and 4-H programing.

title: Senior Seminar in Agricultural Education
description: Discussion and application of Block II learning outcomes in the student to professional educator transition. Topics will include professional development, licensure, professional opportunities and the community of Agricultural and Extension Education.

title: Laboratory Instructional Techniques in Teaching AgriScience
description: Provides knowledge and skills to design, organize, and conduct programs in agriscience instruction in secondary agricultural education. Experiences in lab planning, safety and student management.

title: Principles and Philosophy in Agriculture and Career and Technical Education
description: Principles of career and technical education in Kansas and other states and countries; principles and philosophy underlying such education, relation of career and technical education to school objectives and community, state, and national needs.

title: Program Planning in Agricultural Education
description: The program development and planning process; development of guides for teaching and evaluating reimbursable secondary programs.

title: Inquiry-Based Curriculum in Agricultural STEM Biotechnology Education
description: Provides students with knowledge of inquiry-based techniques related to STEM and Biotechnology in the context of Agriculture. Students will experience activities, labs, and projects developed for inclusion in secondary agriculture classrooms. The learning environment will be grounded in specific agricultural content area, offered on a rotational basis (ex.comprehensive agriculture, animal systems, plant systems, ag power and technology, etc).

title: Exploration of Global Agricultural Education
description: Education Abroad for the exploration of the agriculture industry and agricultural education system in an international location.

title: Inquiry and Literacy in Agricultural STEM and Biotechnology
description: Knowledge of inquiry-based instruction in STEM and Biotechnology in the context of Agriculture. Literacy and reading techniques will be highlighted. Includes activities, labs, projects, and literacy developed for inclusion in secondary agriculture classrooms.

title: Extension Organization and Programs
description: Development and objectives of Cooperative Extension and other university adult education programs; with emphasis on programs and procedures.

title: Organization Problems in Teaching Agricultural Mechanics
description: Analysis of the agricultural mechanics course of study; needs and interests of students; learning difficulties; skills and technical knowledge required; correlation with agriculture; application of laws of learning to the teaching process; determination of objectives.

title: Principles of Teaching Adults in Extension
description: Methods and principles of adult teaching with emphasis on Cooperative Extension Service; application to various adult education programs.

title: Leadership Analysis Through Film
description: Builds upon the study of leadership theory by allowing students to analyze, reflect, synthesize, and apply leadership theories, models and concepts in the context of film. The course materials encourage students to reflect, synthesize, analyze, and apply the information learned from major leadership theories and apply them to various scenarios and situations demonstrated in selected films.

title: Practicum In Agriculture-Related Occupations
description: Related occupational or professional experiences in approved industry, school, Cooperative Extension Service, or similar agency setting under faculty supervision.

title: Methods of Technological Change in Agriculture
description: Structured approach for dealing with the organizational and human aspects of technology transition, including the key concepts of resistance and change management, organizational change, communications, and processes by which professional change agents influence the introductions, adoption, and diffusion of technological change.

title: Practicum in Extension Education
description: Related occupational or professional experiences in approved industry, school, Cooperative Extension Service, or similar agency setting under faculty supervision.

title: Teaching Diverse Learners in Agricultural Education
description: Practical application of learning theories as they apply to diverse learners. Specific instruction relevant to secondary agricultural instructors such as Supervised Agricultural Experience Projects through the lens of diverse students, global leadership and intercultural competence.

title: Assessing Learning in Agricultural Education
description: Develop an understanding and application of assessment relevant to agricultural education. Students develop, administer, and analyze formal, informal, and performance assessment techniques to monitor and evaluate student learning and guide modification.

title: Topics in Agricultural Education
description: Examination of current topics in agricultural education.

title: Agricultural Education Methods and Practicum for Teaching and Learning
description: Principles of teaching applied to agriculture education instruction in the secondary and middle schools including:  motivation, organization of subject matter, lesson/unit planning, assessment and reporting, organization and management of the classroom, and methodology and materials of the secondary and middle schools.

title: Agricultural Education Colloquium I
description: This course explores key concepts within the field of Agricultural Education. Focus will be on advancing the development of disciplinary content knowledge and pedagogical content knowledge.

title: Agricultural Education Colloquium II
description: This course explores additional key concepts within the field of Agricultural Education. Focus will be on advancing the development of disciplinary content knowledge and pedagogical content knowledge.

title: Agricultural Education Colloquium III
description: Class explores the content knowledge of the candidate in regards to the Agricultural Education content curriculum, identifies areas of strength and weakness in content knowledge, and provides enrichment and remediation to prepare the candidate for the internship experience.

title: Research Methods in Agricultural Education and Communications
description: This graduate-level course will focus on applying the principles, practices, and strategies for conducting research in the social sciences, particularly dealing with the interface of the human dimension in the agricultural and natural sciences.

title: Social Data Analysis in Communications and Agricultural Education
description: A course in measurement and analysis as related to social science research and evaluation. The course takes an applied approach to organizing data, analyzing data according to research and evaluation objectives and/or hypotheses, using descriptive and inferential statistics, and interpreting data. Students gain practical experience in data entry and using SPSS for calculating statistics through laboratory exercises.

title: History and Philosophy of Agricultural and Extension Education
description: The primary purpose of this course is to provide the student with an examined grounding in the seminal events and people in the history of agricultural and extension education (history), principle divisions of thought (philosophies), and decisions made (policy), resulting in the current state of affairs in agricultural and extension education. The convergence of these factors has a notable impact on the current challenges and future direction of agriculture and extension education.

title: Agricultural Education For Beginning Teachers
description: Securing and organizing information and planning teaching activities which will help the beginning vocational agriculture teacher.

title: Young Farmer & Adult Farmer Education in Agriculture
description: Organization, objectives, and procedures of conducting young farmer and adult farmer classes. Designed for teachers in service.

title: The History and Leadership of the Land Grant University
description: The course will provide a historical overview of the evolution and development of land-grant colleges/universities. It will reflect on the fundamental beliefs of those who conceptualized and implemented the land-grant college and university system. The course will enable students to examine early public mandates and subsequent successes of these special institutions of higher education, as well as evaluate significant education, research, and public service developments and new initiatives needed for land-grants to effectively serve society in the future. It is expected that students will gain an understanding of and benefits derived from the institutions that comprise the land-grant college and university family, as well as envision developments and opportunities that will mold the future of these special institutions that represent the land-grant legacy.

title: Survey Development in Agricultural Education and Communications
description: Provide the expertise required to design and conduct survey research. Instruments (scales/questionnaire) used in data collection processes and acquire the statistical skills necessary to develop and test these survey instruments, SPSS and Qualtrics.

title: Foundations and Theory of Agricultural Leadership
description: Organizational concepts of leadership; administrative styles and structures; leadership for boards, committees, governmental bodies, and review of societal and political processes.

title: International Agriculture and Extension Education
description: This course focuses on a broad range of topic areas in the field of extension education as it impacts agriculture and rural community development. The primary purpose is to further students' understanding about extension systems' influence on agricultural development and the interface of agriculture with rural communities throughout the world. Emphasis will be placed upon research, theory, history and conceptual models of extension for establishing a strong philosophical foundation.

title: Qualitative Design in Agricultural Education and Communications
description: Overview of philosophies, approaches toward, design, data collection, analysis and reporting of qualitative research.

title: Advanced Theory and Methods of Teaching Agriculture
description: This course will use contemporary and foundational theory and research on teaching and learning processes in the application and organization of instructional methods and techniques in formal and non-formal educational settings particularly in agriculture, food and natural resources disciplines.

title: Curriculum Development in Agriculture I
description: Curriculum problems; planning local programs in agriculture; developing facilities and plans for meeting current and advanced problems in the teaching of agriculture.

title: Field Studies in Agricultural Education
description: Planning, organizing, and coordinating the various phases of the local program of vocational education in agriculture.

title: Program Planning and Evaluation in Agricultural and Extension Education
description: This course is designed for students who are interested in the development and evaluation of agricultural and extension education programs. The course is designed to help students in the following areas: Acquire an understanding of program development from theory to practice; Strengthen their skills in planning, designing, implementing, evaluating, and accounting for educational programs of targeted audiences; and Provide application of program planning and evaluation concepts through experiential learning and class projects.

title: Management of Volunteers in Agricultural and Extension Education
description: This course is intended to prepare students to be effective managers of volunteer programs, or to challenge those students already engaged in those roles to improve on their existing skills. Theory will be emphasized in the course, only in so much as it is essential to be grounded in theory in order to apply it. Major topics of the course will include, but are not limited to: volunteer recruitment, training, evaluation and reward. This is an active course where students will be required to be active in and outside of class sessions.

title: Program Evaluation in Agricultural and Extension Education
description: Evaluation principles, models, and procedures used in developing and analyzing agricultural, vocational, technical, and extension education programs; role of comprehensive evaluation in needs assessments, program planning, program implementation, and the marketing of outcomes to major stake-holders; designs for evaluating agricultural and extension programs. Evaluation logic model is presented to identify and describe program inputs, activities, outputs, and outcomes.

title: Portfolio Development in Agricultural Education and Communications
description: The final semester of the MS in Ag Ed and Comm non-thesis/project option requires the completion of a portfolio. Completion, scoring and tracking will be completed through this course.

title: Master's Project
description: Students select and prepare a project designed to improve their professional practice. It may be the construction of a professional portfolio or project to represent the student's learning throughout the master's degree program.

title: Master's Thesis
description: A master's thesis presents the results of an original investigation or a problem or topic within the student's classroom or educational field approved by the candidate's supervisory committee.

title: Agronomy Orientation
description: Introduction to agronomy options, activities, resources, and careers.  Required of all freshmen in agronomy at K-State.

title: Special Topics in Agronomy
description: Discussion and lectures on selected topic areas related to Agronomy.

title: Crop Science
description: Principles underlying practices used in the culture of grain and forage crops. Crop classification, structure, growth, and improvement. Crop response to environmental factors, soils, and pests and associated management practices. A basic course for majors in agronomy and others interested in crop production.

title: Crop Science Laboratory
description: Hands-on application of principles underlying practices used in the culture of grain and forage crops. Crop classification, crop botany, crop development, crop management, and problem solving for majors in agronomy and others interested in crop production.

title: Spreadsheet Skills for Agronomy
description: An introduction to the use of spreadsheets in Agronomy. Topics include best practices for designing and modifying spreadsheets, functions and formulas, data visualization, and how spreadsheets can be used to guide agronomic decisions.

title: Plants And Soils For Crop Production
Soils Laboratory
description: Hands-on lab and field experience in fundamental chemical, physical, and biological properties of soils; significance of these properties to soil classification and mapping, soil-plant relationships, soil management, and the environment.

title: Range Management
description: Fundamental ecological principles of production, conservation, and use of grasslands. Application of these fundamental principles to management.

title: Agronomy International Study Tour
description: An experiential learning course to prepare students for and to immerse them in an international study tour of selected agronomic industries. The topics vary by the tour and the industries studied.

title: World Food Crops
description: An introduction to the production and consumption patterns of major global food crops. Examine relationship between staple crops and food security. Guest speakers from around the world will discuss important crops grown in their home country. Hands-on activities with edible plant parts and food products.

title: Weed Science
description: For those interested in crop production, crop protection, and agricultural education. Considers the origin of weeds, their relations to crops, and control systems emphasizing cultural, mechanical, biological, and chemical practices. Includes weed identification.

title: Environmental Quality
description: An examination and survey of topics in environmental quality. Includes classification of soil, air, and water pollutants and their interaction with the environment, including the human food chain. Discussion of remediation techniques, risk assessment, and environmental legislation.

title: Grain Grading
description: Procedures for grading grains, emphasizing soybeans, corn, wheat, and sorghum. Identification and evaluation of kernel damage and other conditions determining grades of these grains.

title: Plant and Seed Identification
description: Identification of crops and weeds by seed and vegetative characteristics. Analysis of seed samples for impurities.

title: Crop Growth and Development
description: Comparative growth and development of warm- and cool-season monocot and dicot crops. Environmental influences on growth and development processes and management techniques to minimize stresses.

title: Agricultural Biodiversity
description: An interactive introduction to the value, measurement, and management of biodiversity in agricultural areas. Discussions will consider both the “planned biodiversity” selected by managers (crops and livestock) and the “associated biodiversity” (all the other organisms existing in agroecosystems). The overarching goal is to recognize biodiversity as an integral component of sustainable agriculture. A semester-long term project is a key output of the course.

title: Nutrient Cycling and Fertilizer Management
description: Detailed information on nutrient cycling and management in soils and agroecosystems, including plant nutrition, nutrient interactions with soil, nutrient availability, and fertilizer management. Numerous soil biological, physical, and chemical properties on nutrient availability to crops will be emphasized.

title: Introduction to Grassland Monitoring and Assessment
description: Vegetation sampling techniques used in rangeland research and for grassland monitoring and assessment. Use of statistics for sampling, analysis, and presentation of data.

title: Introduction to Grassland Monitoring and Assessment Lab
description: A field laboratory course designed as a companion to AGRON 380. Focus on methods used for determination of biomass, basal cover, frequency, and density. Additional time will be spent on basic statistics, experimental design, and analyzing data sets.

title: Soil Analysis and Nutrient Recommendations
description: Detailed information on (1) techniques for soil sample collection, (2) chemical methods for routine soil analysis, (3) development of fertilizer recommendations, and (4) fertilizer characteristics. Laboratory exercises reinforce the theoretical principles presented in lectures.

title: Undergraduate Topics in Agronomy
description: Special topics in agronomy not completely treated in other courses.

title: Internship in Agronomy
description: Supervised professional experience in any agronomic area. One credit is equivalent to 120 hours of supervised and evaluated work experience. Cooperating employers and interns will complete a learning contract and set learning outcomes. Student presentation required upon completion of internship.

title: Soils Judging
description: Techniques employed in writing descriptions of soil morphology and in classifying soils for intercollegiate soils judging.

title: Forage Quiz Bowl Team
description: Prepare quiz bowl team members with review of forage history, soils and fertility, establishment, grazing management, plant physiology, poisons and toxicity, forage quality, hay and silage, and identification of forages and weeds.

title: Introduction to Environmental Soil Physics
description: A conceptual and quantitative introductory study of vadose zone soil physical properties and processes in relation to the main components of the hydrologic cycle and the soil surface energy balance.

title: Crops Team
description: Grain grading, seed and plant identification, and seed analysis. Studies lead to participation in intercollegiate crops contest.

title: NACTA Crops Team
description: Diverse agronomic content including crop production statistics, physiology, management, soils, environment, and current events. Components include general agronomic knowledge exam, math and problem solving, plant and seed identification, and lab practicum. Studies lead to participation in North American Colleges and Teachers of Agriculture crops contest.

title: Range Grasses
description: Field and laboratory study of range and pasture plants, with special emphasis on grasses and their distinguishing characteristics.

title: Introduction to Range Management Planning
description: Inventory and analysis of rangeland resources and development of detailed management plan. Emphasizes range management principles and practices useful in maximizing production from rangelands.

title: Emerging Topics in Agronomy
description: This course will provide a platform for in-depth discussion of emerging topics in the agronomic field. Topics that have immediate relevance in crop production, plant breeding, range science, soil fertility, soil management, and soil and water quality are discussed. Experts in fields outside of agronomy will often be guest facilitators in this course.

title: Pre-Travel Seminar
description: Prepares students for short-term international study through academic, cultural, and logistical orientation. Content includes destination-specific history and culture, discipline-relevant topics, learning objectives, and reflective communication connected to the international experience.

title: Forage Management and Utilization
description: Production and utilization of forage crops. Development of forage programs for livestock production, including pasture and stored forages.

title: Forage Management and Utilization Laboratory
description: Identification of forage species, techniques for estimating forage quality, forage physiology, and field trips.

title: Undergraduate Research in Agronomy
description: Independent research experience guided by faculty in crop science, weed science, range science, or soil science.

title: Problems in Agronomy
description: Independent study may be chosen in any area of agronomy: genetics, crop improvement, forages, ecology, weed control, plant physiology, crop production, soils, fertility, genesis, or conservation. Study conducted under the guidance of a faculty member, with topic jointly selected by student and instructor. Course format and number of credit hours determined by the instructor.

title: Agronomy Capstone Experience
description: A culminating learning experience to apply the knowledge gained by the student in the undergraduate curriculum, to apply the skills of problem-solving and critical thinking, and to develop the ability to work in teams and participate in civil discourses. Current issues in Agronomy will be discussed and a team project will be completed.

title: Soil and Environmental Chemistry
description: A study of inorganic and organic chemistry of soils with a detailed examination of the solid, liquid, and gaseous phases. Includes discussions of mineral solubility, electrochemical and adsorption phenomena, acidity, salinity, and fertility. Emphasis is placed on the biogeochemical cycling of plant nutrients and important soil contaminants.

title: Biotechnology
description: The use of biotechnology and molecular genetic approaches in plant and animal sciences. Emphasis is on the use of molecular techniques for plant and animal improvement.

title: Topics in Agronomy
description: Discussion and lectures on important topics in the field of Agronomy.

title: Applied Crop Physiology
description: Study of cultivated crop botany, whole plant and canopy physiology, impact of abiotic (water, temperature, and plant nutrition) and biotic stresses (weeds, insects, and diseases), crop seed physiology, and global climate change effects on crops.

title: Applications of Nutrient Management
description: Principles for developing plant nutrient management programs in the Great Plains. Topics include assessing crop nutrient needs, making fertilizer recommendations, applying application technology and products to enhance nutrient use efficiency, using sensing technology to assess in-season nutrient needs, quality control functions, and the use of waste products as nutrient sources.

title: Crop Improvement and Biotechnology
description: Techniques in basic plant breeding and biotechnology used to genetically improve crops and procedures to increase, distribute, and maintain breeding stocks and varieties.

title: Soil and Water Conservation
description: Principles, prediction, and effects of soil erosion from water and wind. Conservation practices for erosion control and promoting soil, water, and air quality. History of soil and water conservation and the Dust Bowl. Past and present government agencies and policies for soil and water conservation.

title: Cropping Systems
description: Principles for developing and managing cropping systems in the Great Plains for the efficient use of natural resources, primarily water. Emphasis on dryland cropping systems, management of crop rotations, and the plant/soil/environment interaction. Includes the efficient use of natural resources via an understanding of yield limiting factors, impact of crop rotations and cultural practices, climate, plant growth, and development.

title: Bioenergy Feedstock Production
description: Overview of production and characteristics of cultivated crops, perennial grasses, and woody species as feedstocks for bioenergy. Fundamentals of plant growth factors, culture, harvest and storage, quality and improvement, and introduction to environmental impact, modeling, and resource utilization. Knowledge of Microsoft-compatible word processing and spreadsheet programs including graphing is required.

title: Soil Microbiology
description: The nature and function of soil microorganisms in the soil ecosystem. The role of soil microbial activity to soil organic matter, mineral transformations, plant nutrition, and environmental quality.

title: Soil Microbiology Laboratory
description: Discuss and perform laboratory procedures for enumerating soil microorganisms, quantifying soil biological activities, and nutrient cycling.

title: Integrated Weed Management
description: The development of weed management systems based on diagnosis of weed and crop problems, understanding crop-weed associations and interactions with the environment, and integrating weed control tactics such as chemical, biological, cultural, and mechanical practices into recommendations.

title: Crop/Weed Science Team
description: Review of weed identification, weed control applications, agronomic math and problem solving, herbicide symptomology, and diagnosis of field problems. Studies prepare undergraduate and graduate student team members to participate in a regional or national contest held during the summer.

title: Site Specific Agriculture
description: Introduction to spatial analysis and management of agricultural and environmental resources using Geographic Information Systems (GIS) technology. Emphasis on collecting, displaying, and analyzing spatial or georeferenced soil, crop, or other land surface data.

title: Grassland Monitoring and Assessment
description: Vegetation sampling techniques used in rangeland research and for grassland monitoring and assessment. Use of statistics for sampling, analysis, and presentation of data.

title: Grassland Monitoring and Assessment Lab
description: A field laboratory course designed as a companion to AGRON 660. Labs will consist of methods used for determination of biomass, basal cover, frequency, and density. Additional time will be spent on basic statistics, experimental design, and use of the microcomputer in analyzing data sets.

title: Rangeland Watershed Management
description: Study of the management of physical/biological settings and processes along with human activities on water and watersheds considering preventative and restorative strategies in a natural resource rangeland setting. Web-based distance AG*IDEA course.

title: Soil Genesis and Classification
description: Factors and processes of soil formation, classification of soils according to soil taxonomy, and use of soil survey information. Practical field experience describing soil properties. Required field trips.

title: Plant Genetics
description: Concepts and application of basic genetic principles in higher plants.

title: Range Ecology
description: Application of ecological principles to range ecosystem management. Study of plant-soil-animal interactions with rangelands, and discussion of plant succession, environmental influences, and ecological concepts.

title: Grassland Fire Ecology
description: The course is designed to describe the ecological effects of fire on grassland ecosystems. It also provides insight into the history of fires, the people who use them and why, the parts of a a fire, how fires behave in relation to fuel and weather, and the conducting and safety of prescribed burns.

title: Tropical Soil Management
description: Characteristics of agriculture in tropical conditions with a focus on the environment and soils. Includes discussion of soil management systems in tropical agriculture that use conventional methods as well as methods that emphasize sustainability including no-till, crop rotation, and cover crops.

title: Climate Change and Agriculture
description: Impact of climate and climate change on agricultural systems and soil and water resources at global and regional scales. Discussion on adaptation and mitigation strategies including economics and policies that affect agriculture.

title: Agricultural Meteorology
description: Fundamental concepts of meteorology and climatology and their applications to agriculture. Use of weather data to study meteorological variables and to estimate relevant parameters and processes, such as crop growth, development, yield, evapotranspiration, and soil water budget.

title: Nutritional Improvement of Crops
description: Discussion of current genetic and breeding efforts to improve the nutritional value of food crops, focusing on vitamins, minerals, amino acids, fatty acids, and phytonutrients. Impact of nutritionally-improved crops in food security.

title: Introduction to Plant Resistance to Pests
description: Basic concepts of the biology, ecology, genetics and breeding for pest resistance in plants.

title: Environmental Soil Physics
description: A conceptual and quantitative introductory study of vadose zone soil physical properties and processes in relation to the main components of the hydrologic cycle and the soil surface energy balance.

title: Advanced Nutrient Cycling and Fertilizer Management
description: Detailed information on nutrient cycling and management in soils and agroecosystems, including plant nutrition, nutrient interactions with soil, nutrient availability, and fertilizer management. Numerous soil biological, physical, and chemical properties on nutrient availability to crops will be emphasized. Students will independently assess nutrient availability in soils and develop nutrient management plans for production systems.

title: Ecology of Invasive Species
description: Ecological principles and their application to invasive species. Discussion of population, community and ecosystem level characteristics affecting a wide variety of invasive plant and animal species. Discussions will include current global consequences and governmental policies/programs designed to limit the spread of invasives.

title: Range Management Planning
description: Inventory and analysis of rangeland resources and development of detailed management plan. Emphasizes range management principles and practices useful in maximizing production from rangelands.

title: Advanced Problems in Agronomy
description:
Independent study of a specific problem in agronomy under the guidance
of a member of the department's graduate faculty. For non-thesis or
non-dissertation studies. Topic is selected jointly by student and
instructor. Course format and number of credit hours are determined by
the instructor.

title: Soil Physics
description: A study of the transport of water, heat, gases, and solutes in soil. Examples are presented that related to both agricultural and engineering land uses. Emphasis is given to understanding how soil physical properties and soil management practices influence transport processes.

title: Plant-Water Relations
description: Properties of water, terminology in plant and soil water relations, environmental aspects of plant-water relations, soils as a water reservoir, water as a plant component, water movement through the plant, special aspects of transpiration, development and significance of internal water deficits, drought resistance mechanisms, water consumption by crop plants.

title: Principles of Forage Quality
description: The course provides an indepth study of the chemical characteristics of forage components and the interactions with ruminant physiology and digestion that influence forage feeding value and the laboratory procedures used to evaluate forages for grazing livestock. Students should have knowledge of the basic principles of chemistry, ruminant nutrition, and plant physiology so that they can develop an understanding of the chemical characteristics of forages and how they affect the value of forages to grazing livestock.

title: Herbicide Interactions
description: Herbicide fate in plants and soils. Physiological and biochemical processes in plants and soils as affected by herbicides. Molecular biochemistry of herbicide resistance that have developed in the field. Biotechnologically derived herbicide-resistant crops.

title: Advanced Weed Ecology
description: A study of advanced weed ecology topics including weed/crop interference, weed growth and development, herbicide resistance, biological control, spatial and temporal population dynamics, and ecological approaches to weed management.

title: Soil And Plant Analysis
description: Theories and procedures for the chemical analysis of soils and plant materials. Applications of analysis in soil fertility evaluations and in research work are discussed.

title: Quantitative Genetics In Relation To Plant Breeding
Grassland Plant Identification
description: Study of plants that have ecological and/or agricultural importance in the Great Plains.  Emphasis on plant identification, grassland ecosystems, and plant forage value, palatability, and utilization by both domestic livestock and wildlife.  Cultural and historical uses of grassland.

title: Nutrient Sources, Uptake and Cycling
description: An examination of the behavior of plant nutrients in soils emphasizing nutrient sources, the interaction of nutrient with soils, and the role of plant root as nutrient sinks. Includes processes used in the formulation of chemical fertilizers and the assessment of available nutrients in non-commercial plant nutrient sources.

title: Crop Physiology
description: Principles of nitrogen metabolism, mineral nutrition, photosynthesis, growth substances, and hardiness applied to crop production.

title: Advanced Forage Management
description: Examines the integrated management of forages, livestock, soil, water, pests, and the environment.

title: Reproducible Research and Scientific Computing
description: Develop skills to handle large datasets, generate reproducible science, and develop creative data analysis and interpretation skills. Focus will be on understanding programming logic by developing and writing computing codes for common tasks and concepts related to environmental science.

title: Applied Plant Breeding
description: This course considers in detail the mechanics of an applied plant breeding program for agronomic crops.

title: Breeding Self-Pollinated Species Of Field Crops
Advanced Site-Specific Agriculture
description: Spatial analysis and management of agricultural and environmental resources using Geographic Information Systems technology and agricultural software. Emphasis is on collecting, displaying, and analyzing spatial or georeferenced soil, crop, or other land surface data to develop prescriptions for variable rate input applications. Students will work on a special project throughout the semester linked to precision agriculture tools and technologies.

title: Grazing Ecology and Management
description: This course discusses the ecological principles of domesticated livestock grazing and their application to manage grazing lands.  Theoretical and applied models of plant/animal interactions will be presented.  Grazing systems and their management of ecosystem services will be presented as balance between production and conservation outcomes.

title: Agricultural Simulation Modeling
description: Techniques for developing and testing computer simulation models for research, management, and design applications in agriculture.

title: Master's Report
description: Preparation of a written report either of research or of problem work on a topic in the major field.

title: Master's Research
description: Research on a problem which may extend throughout the year and furnish data for a master's thesis.

title: Micrometeorology
description: A comprehensive analysis of interactions between living organisms and their physical environment. Emphasis is placed on characterizing the transport of heat, water, and carbon within the soil-plant-atmosphere continuum. Includes discussions on aerodynamic transfer, surface energy balances, evapotranspiration, trace gas fluxes, and basic micrometeorology. The potential impact of climatic change on biosphere productivity will be considered.

title: International Agronomy Experience for Graduate Students
description: Students will apply knowledge gained in their graduate curriculum focused on international crops, soils, and agronomic systems.  Topics vary by offering depending on the agronomic systems studied and locations visited.

title: Advanced Soil Chemistry
description: Application of physical chemistry to soils; cation and anion equilibria, ion activities, sorption, redox reactions, and other physiochemical phenomena in soils.

title: Advanced Soil Physics
description: An advanced study of the transport of water, heat and solutes in soil. The theory of unsaturated water flow, coupled heat and water flow, and the convection and dispersion of reactive solutes will be studied in detail. Spatial variability of soil physical properties will be discussed, solute transport will be presented.

title: Advanced Topics in Agronomy
description: Discussion and lectures on important papers and contributions in the field of Agronomy.

title: Advanced Soil Genesis and Classification
description: An advanced study of processes of soil formation and systems of soil classification including soil taxonomy.

title: Quantitative Genetics
description: Deduction of the consequences of Mendelian inheritance for the simultaneous segregation of multiple genes in populations. Topics include single/multi-locus models, sources of variation, resemblance between relatives, linkage/association mapping, genomic prediction, parameter estimation, response to selection, and inbreeding/heterosis.

title: Soil Mineralogy
description: Theory and application of methods for analyzing minerals in soil environments, including X-ray, electron optical, thin section, and wet chemical techniques.

title: Advanced Crop Ecology
description: Principles of growth and development of crops in relation to the environment.

title: Soil Microbial Ecology
description: Theories and concepts of the ecology and function of microorganisms in the soil environment. Discussions will include factors regulating microbial activity, the flow of energy (carbon), and nitrogen transformations as they relate to plant productivity and environmental quality.

title: Predictive Modeling for Plant Breeding
description: Introduction to machine learning methods applied to agriculture and plant breeding including core concepts in predictive modeling and best practices for reproducible computational research. Conceptual understanding of methods and hands-on practice with real datasets are emphasized.

title: Advanced Plant Breeding I
description: Application of quantitative genetics in plant breeding, heritability, mating designs, selection techniques and theory, marker assisted selection, population improvement, breeding autopolyploid crops.

title: Genomic Analysis for Crop Improvement
description: Concepts and applications of genomic analysis in crop improvement, including crop evolution, and origin, germplasm conservation and utilization, population structure and genetic distances, genomic-wide association and linkage studies.

title: Phd Research
description: Research on a problem which may extend throughout the year and furnish data for a doctoral dissertation.

title: Ag Pathway: Agricultural Technology and Mechanical Systems
description: For completers of the Kansas Career Cluster Pathway in Agriculture, Food, and Natural Resources Career Cluster, Agricultural Technology and Mechanical Systems

title: Independent Study in Agricultural Technical Education
description: Student-specific projects or independent guided studies in agricultural technical education or applied studies.

title: Topics in Agricultural Technical Education
description: Topics of special interest. Will be offered upon sufficient demand in selected areas of agriculture that are technical or applied in nature.

title: Research in Animal Science and Industry
description: Research in Animal Science and Industry

title: Advanced Technical Mathematics
description: This course provides an in-depth study of essential mathematical concepts required to address multiple scenarios within engineering technology, with a focus on Plane Trigonometry, introductory Calculus, and Physics principles.

title: Applied Mechanics I
description: Static force equilibrium, free-body force analysis, friction, structural force analysis, centroids and center of gravity, area moment of inertia, concepts of stress and strain. Strength of materials. Axial, torsional, bending and combined stress and strain analysis. Considerations of stress concentration, fatigue, and impact conditions. Applied fluid mechanics, mass conservation and energy conservation equations.

title: Electric Circuits and Power Technology
description: DC and AC circuits, series and parallel connections. Electrical properties of Conductors. Ohm’s law. Resistive loads and non-linear loads. Power sources. AC power generation and distribution, single phase and 3-phase, active, reactive and apparent powers, motors.

title: Semiconductor Electronics and Digital Logic
description: Studies of basic semiconductor theory. Principles, characteristics and applications of semiconductor devices such as various types of diodes, transistors, and thyristors. Small scale integrated circuits. Logic gates. Number system and Boolean algebra.

title: Electronics Principles and Practices
description: In this course, we will study DC, AC circuits, Ohm’s law, series and parallel connections, impedance of RLC circuits; electrical properties of materials; semiconductor devices such as various types of diodes, transistors, and thyristors; small scale integrated circuits; logic gates; control systems and devices; number system and Boolean algebra.

title: Technology Project Management
description: This course covers the following subjects: 1) principles of engineering decision making process, financial aspects of technology projects, including principal and compound interest calculations, 2) uniform annual cost, rate of return, depreciation, equipment replacement, and competing projects, 3) technology project planning, including aggregate planning and master production schedule, inventory control, material and capacity planning, and shop floor control as well as 4) technical document preparation and reporting.

title: Tillage, Planting, And Crop Harvesting Systems
Introduction To Food Engineering Technology Lab
Soil Erosion And Sediment Pollution Control
Private Water Supply And Waste Management
Introduction To American Ethnic Studies
description: This course introduces students to the major concepts related to ethnicity and to some of the major American ethnic groups.

title: Race, Film, and American Stories
description: Introduces students to how race functions in movies, books, and more. It will further examine how movies and books activate and reflect change. Students will analyze artistic expression from one or more American Ethnic traditions, including film and literature, and possibly also music, comics, TV, and other texts.

title: Intercultural Competence in Institutions
description: Prepares students to understand and appreciate America's race relations and its diverse histories. Emphasis on implementing cultural competency in various social settings.

title: African American Perspectives
description: A critical examination of African American experience in the context of U.S. society and institutions. The analysis will consider the interrelationships between and among various African American groups and ""mainstream"" America, and their corresponding nationalities and regions of origin.

title: American Indian Perspectives
description: A critical examination of American Indian experience in the context of U.S. society and institutions. The analysis will consider the interrelationships between and among various American Indians and ""mainstream"" America, American Indian nations and/or tribes.

title: Latina/o Perspectives
description: A critical examination of U.S. Latina/o experience in the context of American society and institutions.  The analysis will consider the interrelationships between and among various Latina/o groups, ""mainstream"" America, and their corresponding nationalities and regions of origins.

title: Asian American Perspectives
description: A critical examination of Asian American experience in the context of U.S. society and institutions. The analysis will consider the interrelationships between and among various Asian American groups, ""mainstream"" America, and their corresponding nationalities and regions of origin.

title: Politics of Women of Color
description: Explores the coalitional politics of women of color from its earliest formation as the ""Third World Women's Alliance"" in the 1960s to its manifestation in present-day movements like ""Incite! Women of Color Against Violence.""

title: Comparative Ethnic Studies
description: Provides a comparative framework for understanding both the specificities within and the differences between the situations of racially marginalized groups in the U.S. and beyond.

title: Independent Reading and Research in American Ethnic Studies
description: Guided reading and research on a specific topic of student interest, leading to preparation of a research paper or creative work.

title: Transformative Thought
description: Engages a range of advanced theories and critical concepts in American Ethnic Studies, including the intersections of race, ethnicity, gender, class, indigeneity, (dis)ability, citizenship, and sexuality.

title: Senior Research Project in American Ethnic Studies
description: Guided research in American ethnic studies. Students prepare a research paper on a relevant subject of their choice. Each student is responsible for arranging to work with a member of the American ethnic studies faculty.

title: Recitation Leadership in American Ethnic Studies
description: Integrative review of concepts in American ethnic studies under faculty supervision. Preparation for leading discussions, workshops and reviews in American ethnic studies. Students attend two lecture sessions per week concurrent with AMETH 160, one additional seminar session focused on planning and preparation for recitations, and are responsible for leading discussions in one or more recitation sections in AMETH 160 per week.

title: Multicultural Psychology
description: To introduce and familiarize students with the concept of multicultural.

title: Topics in American Ethnic Studies
description: Selected topics of special interest in American Ethnic Studies.

title: Independent Reading and Research in American Ethnic Studies
description: Advanced guided reading and research on a specific topic of student interest, leading to preparation of a research paper or creative work.

title: Advanced Topics in American Ethnic Studies
description: Provides students the opportunity to investigate topics in American Ethnic Studies that enable greater depth and complexity of understanding of field's methodological approaches and specializations.

title: Introduction to Aviation Regulations and Aircraft Drawings
description: An introduction to the role and organizational structure of the Federal Aviation Administration and its responsibilities in aircraft certification and continued airworthiness. Emphasizes the privileges and limitations of certificated maintenance personnel and the application of Federal Aviation Regulations in aviation maintenance activities. Topics include basic weight and balance principles, maintenance record entries, interpretation of graphs and charts, and the use of FAA and manufacturer maintenance publications such as specifications, data sheets, manuals, Airworthiness Directives, and Advisory material. Students develop the ability to read, interpret, and apply aircraft drawings and technical documentation commonly used in aviation maintenance.

title: Aviation Math and Physics
description: A study of the mathematics and physics principles required of aircraft maintenance technicians in accordance with FAR Part 147 and FAA Aviation Mechanic Airman Certification Standards. Emphasizes algebra, ratios and proportions, unit conversion, force, motion, heat transfer, fluid dynamics, aerodynamics, aircraft structures, and theory of flight. Students apply quantitative reasoning to aviation maintenance, aircraft performance, and weight and balance calculations using industry accepted methods.

title: Concepts of Aircraft Materials and Tooling Standards
description: A foundational course in aircraft materials, hardware, inspection programs, and tooling standards used in aviation maintenance. Emphasizes identification and application of ferrous and non-ferrous materials, heat treatment processes, structural load considerations, aircraft hardware and fastening systems, precision measurement, nondestructive testing methods, corrosion identification and treatment, approved cleaning procedures, aircraft registration markings, and fabrication and inspection of rigid and flexible fluid lines. Students apply accepted maintenance practices and regulatory standards to determine component serviceability and support airworthiness.

title: Basic Aircraft Electricity and Ground Operations
description: A basic concept of DC/AC circuits, with basic laws relating to the following: measuring voltage, current, resistance, continuity and leakage; relationship of voltage, current and resistance in electrical circuits; reading and interpretation of electrical circuit diagrams; electrical devices and inspection and servicing of batteries. Introduction to digital numbering systems and digital logic functions. Accurate use of graphs and charts to determine critical performance values, and the servicing, starting, ground operations, and security of aircraft.

title: Application of General Aviation Maintenance Concepts
description: This laboratory course provides hands on experience in fundamental aviation maintenance tasks aligned with concepts from AMP 101–104 and FAA industry standards. Students develop practical skills in aircraft weighing and balancing, precision measurement, nondestructive testing, aircraft movement and securing procedures, ground operations, servicing and fueling, and basic engine ground run operations. Emphasis is placed on shop safety, flight line safety, and adherence to approved maintenance practices and professional standards.

title: Introduction to AC Materials and Tooling Standards
description: Introduces foundational skills and technical competencies related to aircraft material properties and fabrication processes. Emphasizes shop safety, organization, and risk management in maintenance environments; identification and application of aircraft structural materials and hardware; fluid lines and fittings; hand tool selection and torque practices; precision measurement and dimensional inspection techniques; cleaning and corrosion control; welding procedures; nondestructive testing methods; and foundational airframe inspection techniques in accordance with FAA standards.

title: Aircraft Regulations, Compliance and Operations
description: A comprehensive examination of the Federal Aviation Administration regulatory framework governing aircraft certification, continued airworthiness, and maintenance operations in general, business, and commercial aviation environments. Emphasizes the privileges, limitations, and responsibilities of certificated maintenance personnel and the application of Title 14 CFR to maintenance decision making. Topics include aircraft weight and balance analysis, preparation of compliant maintenance record entries, interpretation of performance charts and technical publications, aircraft servicing and ground operations, and the integration of human factors and risk awareness into regulatory compliance and maintenance practices.

title: Basic Aircraft Electricity
description: Comprehensive study of aircraft electrical and electronic fundamentals aligned with FAA Airman Certification Standards under Fundamentals of Electricity and Electronics. Emphasizes DC and AC circuit theory, electrical laws, magnetism, capacitance, inductance, electrical measurement, aircraft batteries, protective devices, semiconductors, digital logic, and interpretation of aircraft electrical schematics. Students perform measurements, troubleshooting, inspection, and servicing procedures using approved maintenance data and industry-standard practices.

title: Aircraft Science
description: Introduces the mathematics and physics principles required for aviation maintenance technicians in accordance with FAR Part 147 and FAA industry standards. Emphasizes algebra, geometry, ratios, proportions, unit conversion, force, motion, heat transfer, fluid dynamics, simple machines, aerodynamics, and the theory of flight. Students apply quantitative reasoning and physical principles to aircraft maintenance and performance scenarios using industry accepted methods and technical data.

title: Aircraft Drawings
description: Introduces interpretation and application of aircraft technical drawings used in aviation maintenance. Emphasizes reading and analyzing assembly, detail, and installation drawings; identification of lines, symbols, dimensions, tolerances, revisions, and bills of materials; and extraction of technical data necessary to perform maintenance in accordance with FAA approved documentation. Includes foundational computer aided drafting concepts.

title: Aircraft Metallic Structures
description: A study of aircraft metallic structures and materials used in the design, construction, and repair of airframes, including fuselages, wings, and control surfaces. Emphasizes structural properties, load paths, stress considerations, sheet metal fabrication techniques, fastener installation, welding practices, titanium structures, and damage evaluation. Students develop practical skills in aluminum sheet metal fabrication and structural repair in accordance with FAA approved data, applicable regulations, and manufacturer recommended procedures consistent with Aviation Mechanic Airman Certification Standards.

title: Aircraft Environmental and Fire Protection Systems
description: A comprehensive study of aircraft environmental control and fire protection systems for both airframe and propulsion installations. Emphasizes system design, operation, inspection, servicing, and troubleshooting of pressurization, heating and cooling, supplemental oxygen, ice and rain control, fire detection and extinguishing, and waste and water systems. Students apply manufacturer technical data and FAA standards to evaluate system performance, determine serviceability, and perform maintenance procedures in accordance with Aviation Mechanic Airman Certification Standards.

title: Aircraft Electrical Systems
description: A comprehensive, task intensive study of aircraft and engine electrical systems including DC and AC power generation, distribution, circuit protection, ignition, and starting systems. Emphasizes inspection, fabrication, installation, troubleshooting, repair, and system level fault isolation using manufacturer technical data and FAA standards. Students apply structured diagnostic methods and perform complex electrical maintenance tasks to ensure system reliability and continued airworthiness.

title: Aircraft Landing Gear, Fluid Power and Fuel Systems
description: A comprehensive study of hydraulic, pneumatic, landing gear, and aircraft fuel systems used in airframe applications. Emphasizes system design, operation, inspection, servicing, and troubleshooting of fluid power components, landing gear assemblies, braking and anti-skid systems, and aircraft fuel storage, distribution, and indicating systems. Students perform maintenance tasks in accordance with FAA approved technical data and manufacturer procedures to ensure system reliability and continued airworthiness.

title: Aircraft Avionics and Instrument Systems
description: A comprehensive study of aircraft flight instrument, navigation and communication systems, including mechanical, electrical, and electronic indication equipment. Emphasizes system operation, inspection, installation, testing, troubleshooting, and repair of pitot static systems, flight instruments, autopilot systems, communication and navigation equipment, radar and transponder systems, flight management systems, and associated antenna installations. Students apply manufacturer technical data and FAA standards to evaluate system performance and ensure continued airworthiness.

title: Rotary and Fixed Wing Aircraft Design and Assembly
description: Advanced study of aircraft-level airframe inspection, flight control systems, and rotorcraft fundamentals aligned with FAA Airman Certification Standards for Airframe subject areas AM.II.D and AM.II.N. Emphasizes comprehensive airframe inspection, rigging and adjustment of flight control systems, rotor system design and balance, aircraft jacking and ground handling procedures, and aircraft-level weight and balance application. Students evaluate inspection findings, determine aircraft airworthiness, perform functional checks, and document maintenance actions in accordance with approved maintenance data and regulatory standards.

title: Aviation Maintenance Airframe Capstone
description: A comprehensive review and integration course designed to prepare students for FAA General and Airframe certification examinations and entry level employment in airframe maintenance environments. Emphasizes applied knowledge review, scenario based troubleshooting, oral and practical task simulation, regulatory compliance, maintenance documentation, and professional workplace readiness. Students perform integrated maintenance tasks aligned with the Aviation Mechanic Airman Certification Standards and develop the communication, safety, and decision making skills required for industry employment.

title: Powerplant Fundamentals
description: Introductory study of aircraft powerplant theory and operation with emphasis on reciprocating and gas turbine engine fundamentals. Topics include thermodynamic principles, engine operating cycles, and performance calculations. The course also includes propeller aerodynamics, constant-speed and controllable-pitch operation, propeller inspection, removal and installation procedures, governor adjustment, and operational checks. Emphasis is placed on foundational powerplant knowledge, performance interpretation, and approved maintenance practices.

title: Reciprocating Powerplant Technology
description: Advanced study of reciprocating aircraft powerplant systems with emphasis on inspection, maintenance, and overhaul procedures. Topics include cylinder assemblies, crankcase components, valve trains, fuel and induction systems, ignition systems, lubrication systems, cooling systems, exhaust systems, and engine installation. Students perform disassembly, dimensional inspection, reassembly, operational testing, and diagnostic evaluation of reciprocating engine systems in accordance with approved maintenance data and industry standards.

title: Gas Turbine Powerplant Technology
description: Advanced study of gas turbine aircraft powerplant systems with emphasis on inspection, maintenance, and overhaul procedures. Topics include compressor and turbine sections, combustion systems, fuel control systems, lubrication systems, starting and ignition systems, bleed air systems, and engine installation. Students perform module-level disassembly, inspection, reassembly, operational testing, and diagnostic evaluation of turbine engine systems in accordance with approved maintenance data and industry standards.

title: Aviation Maintenance Powerplant Capstone
description: A capstone course designed to prepare students for industry-required credentials with emphasis on aviation maintenance knowledge, regulatory application, and professional readiness. The course is comprehensive in nature and integrates subject matter from across the aviation maintenance curriculum in alignment with applicable certification standards. Emphasis is placed on synthesis of technical knowledge, maintenance decision-making, and transition to entry-level employment.

title: Introduction to Cultural Anthropology
description: Introduction to ethnology and ethnography; analysis and comparison of technological, social, and religious characteristics of cultural systems.

title: A General Education Introduction to Cultural Anthropology
description: Introduction to ethnology and ethnography; analysis and comparison of technological, social, and religious characteristics of cultural systems.

title: Introduction to Cultural Anthropology Honors
description: Introduction to basic ethnology and ethnography; technological, social, and religious characteristics of cultural systems; discussion and independent study.

title: Introduction to Linguistic Anthropology
description: Language as a part of human behavior: its origins, uses and abuses, and ways of defining reality. Basic descriptive and ethnosemantic skills used by anthropologists to learn languages in the field.

title: Introduction to Archaeology
description: Brief introduction to the field of anthropological archeology.  General survey of world prehistory revealing major cultural changes from the development of early foraging societies through the rise of agricultural and complex communities.

title: Introduction to Biological Anthropology
description: Provides knowledge regarding the scope of Biological Anthropology, and develop an understanding of: 1) evolutionary theory and evolutionary processes, 2) patterns of adaptation to the environment in primates, and 3) human evolution, human adaptation and human variation. Key concepts/perspectives will include: evolution, selection, adaptation genetics, population genetics, as well as variation and variability.

title: Initiation to Anthropology
description: Identify and apply the core elements of the anthropological perspective while learning professional and academic writing and presentation skills.

title: Cultures of South Asia
description: Provides an interdisciplinary exploration of the disparate cultures and communities of the South Asian region.  We will examine thematic issues of colonization, religion, nationalism, modernity, sexuality, gender, globalization and social stratification in their various South Asian contexts.

title: Environmental Anthropology: Living with Change in the Anthropocene
description: Explores anthropological and human science approaches to the complex causes and consequences of environmental change, including climate change, mass species extinction, industrial pollution, and deforestation.  Students learn about economic, cultural, and political drivers of environmental degradation and discuss solutions for a more sustainable future from the perspective of social and cultural theory.

title: Museums, Cultures, and Collaborations
description: Critically examine the cultures and histories of museums and collections in the United States and elsewhere by exploring topics such as: the complexity of representing others; museums, colonization, and power; museum ethics; collaborative museum work with, and by, indigenous communities and curators; the global art and artifact market and growing repatriation movements, etc. Students will get a chance to visit museums, as well as to engage with the ethnography collections housed by the Anthropology program at K-State.

title: Introduction to the World's Religions
description: Survey of religious beliefs, practices, and experiences from around the world. Emphasis is on religious practices and experiences in different cultural contexts.

title: Introduction to Medical Anthropology & Global Health
description: Explores disease, illness, and medical systems from a cross-cultural perspective.  Includes the impacts of culture, gender, wealth inequality and other structural conditions on health and well-being.

title: Engaged Anthropology
description: Applies anthropology to real-world problems, politics, and advocacy in various fields including business, technology, engineering, health, agriculture, conservation, human rights, and international development. Explores anthropological engagement with various social, political, and environmental movements and organizations. Emphasis on ethics in research and practice.

title: Topics in Cultural Anthropology
description: Exploration of problems in cultural anthropology for both majors and non-majors.

title: Food and Culture
description: An examination of heritage cuisines, nutrition, and diet as a part of daily life, special holidays, and ritual events, as well as how subsistence, taste, and culinary practices have transformed over time and across borders. Anthropological examples from various subcultures will help students consider what the global flow of people, food commodities, and recipes means for our communities, our identities, our palates, and our bodies.

title: Topics in Linguistic Anthropology
description: Exploration of problems in linguistic anthropology for both majors and non-majors.

title: Topics for Educators
description: Examination of selected topics in archaeology, cultural, or linguistic anthropology designed to address education standards in the social sciences.

title: Special Studies in Anthropology
description: Explores topics in anthropology through guided field trips (study abroad or domestic excursion).

title: Topics in Cultural Heritage and Museum Studies
description: Anthropological exploration of issues related to cultural heritage institutions including museum management, collections care, interpretation, and public engagement.

title: Museum Collections Management
description: Overview of the management and care of tangible objects that form the foundation of museums and other cultural heritage institutions. Introduces policies and procedures essential to ethical collecting and care, particularly preventive conservation.

title: Anthropology of Disability
description: Focuses on the cultural developments and transformations of disability over time. The course addresses different periods throughout the world to understand how past and contemporary societies see disability and bodily configurations. Case studies may be drawn from a mixture of ethnohistory, archaeology, cultural anthropology, and biological anthropology.

title: Archaeological Myths and Mysteries
description: Introduces evidence-based thinking and the scientific method in archaeology through evaluation of ""mysteries"" of our human past.  Explores social factors affecting interpretation of past cultures.  Case studies include who ""discovered"" the Americas, ancient alien intervention, Atlantis, myth of the mound builders, and more.

title: Exploring Kansas Archaeology
description: Exploration of the archaeological record of past Native people of Kansas and the Central Plains and their diverse lifeways.

title: Topics in Archaeology
description: Exploration of problems in archaeology for both majors and non-majors.

title: Archaeology of Death and Burial
description: Examines a wide range of topics related to death in prehistory, across culture and through time, and look at the ways that archaeologists use cutting edge technologies to study the dead in the past. The ethics, politics, and legality of excavating and analyzing prehistoric burials and other ritual sites will also be addressed.

title: Introduction to Human Evolution
description: Survey of the human fossil record, including an introduction to evolutionary theory and a discussion of the place that humans occupy among the other primates.

title: Plagues:  the Co-Evolutionary History of Humans and Pathogens
description: The course will explore the proximate (mechanistic/physiological) and ultimate (evolutionary) causes of disease from a biocultural and historical perspective.

title: Fundamentals of Forensic Anthropology
description: Provide students with basic knowledge of human skeletal anatomy, as well as the principles guiding the recovery and analysis of human remains, and the bioanthropological methods applied in their identification, within a legal context. Will rely on case study examples to familiarize students with the steps and analyses involved in the process of forensic recovery and identification of human remains. The use of case studies will facilitate learning by providing an engaging way to introduce students to Forensic Anthropology.

title: Topics in Physical Anthropology
description: Exploration of problems in physical anthropology for both majors and non-majors.

title: Honors Seminar in Anthropology
description: Readings and discussion of selected topics.

title: Intro to the Civilizations of South Asia II
Honors Project
description: Open only to Arts & Sciences students who are active members of the University Honors Program.

title: Proficiency Development
description: Integrative review of anthropological concepts and skills under faculty supervision. For single students or groups of students. Not applicable to major field requirements.

title: South Asian Civilizations
description: Interdisciplinary survey of the development of civilizations in India, Pakistan, Sri Lanka, Bangladesh, Nepal, Bhutan, and Afghanistan, including geography, philosophy, social, economic, political institution, and historical movements.

title: Central America:  Its Peoples And Problems
Cultures of Africa
description: Family life, subsistence patterns, exchange systems, languages, religions, and development of the peoples of Africa.

title: Kinship and Marriage in Cross-Cultural Perspective
Immigrant America
description: Discussion of post-1965 immigration to the United States with a focus on Asian and Latino newcomers. Immigrant adaptation, economic strategies and the reinterpretation of cultural identity. Implications for American society.

title: Narrative Medicine
description: Narrative medicine is the use of narrative competency in clinical and other healing contexts to enhance patient, family, or community healing and well-being. Use of well-established research and practice in medical anthropology, the broader medical humanities, and cutting-edge clinical scholarship to help students gain narrative competency. Students will learn why narrative and meaning are critical to healing, how to become aware of patient narratives, and how to practice attention to the stories of others. Students will also learn foundational interpretive skills grounded in close reading and discourse analysis.

title: Creativity and Culture: The Anthropology of Art
description: Explores creativity, aesthetics, and meaning in various visual art subcultures in the U.S. and around the world. Attention is paid to the intersection of art and contemporary social issues including structural racism.

title: Ethnomusicology
description: Music, dance, and other performance subcultures from the United States and around the world will be explored in depth. Emphasis is placed on understanding music and dance styles in their cultural context, including how they change in the era of globalization. The class also explores how social identity is expressed through music-making and other performance cultures.

title: Topics in Cultural Anthropology
description: Intensive exploration of themes and topics in cultural anthropology.

title: Language and Culture
description: Study of language and dialect as aspects of social and ethnic group identities. Emphasis on analysis of conversational style in diverse cultural settings. Research project to be determined according to student interests.

title: Topics in Linguistic Anthropology
