description: Intensive exploration of problems in linguistic anthropology for both major and non-majors with adequate background.

title: Topics in Cultural Heritage and Museum Studies
description: Anthropological exploration of issues related to cultural heritage institutions including museum management, collections care, interpretation, and public engagement.

title: Archaeological Fact or Fiction: applied Critical Thinking
description: Explores and develops practical critical thinking and research skills using archaeological approaches and case studies. Examines impact of interpretation of the human past on past and present populations.

title: Archaeological Field Methods
description: Provides hands-on training in archaeological field methods (i.e., data collection and documentation through archaeological survey and excavation) as part of a research project.

title: Archaeological Laboratory Methods
description: Hands-on instruction in and application of professional principles of processing, analyzing, and interpreting artifacts and related archeological materials.

title: Applied Archeology
description: Training in professional and ethical practice in cultural resource management and public archaeology.

title: North American Archaeology
description: The prehistoric of native cultures of North America explored through the archaeological record from the peopling of the continent, to the spread of agriculture and village life, up to contact period.

title: Topics in Archaeology
description: Intensive exploration of archaeological problems for both major and non-majors with adequate background.

title: Topics in Physical Anthropology
description: Intensive exploration of problems in physical anthropology for both major and non-majors with adequate background.

title: Anthropological Theory
description: Exploration of the major theoretical approaches in the principal branches of anthropology.

title: North American Indians
description: Description and comparison of native cultures of Canada and the United States; culture contact and change among surviving groups.

title: South American Indians
description: A survey of the nature and variability of the original cultures of Latin America. Analysis of sample cultures, stressing economic, social, political, and religious structures.

title: Ethnohistory
description: Introduction to ethnohistory as an anthropological research method.

title: Gender, Power and International Development
description: Examination of various models of development and their impact on various roles of woman and men in various cultures. Emphasis upon Africa, Asia, and Latin America. Comparisons of public, service, and economics sectors, including agriculture, marketing, and industry. Examination of policy issues.

title: Race and Culture
description: The biological meaning of race; the interrelationships of biological and cultural traits in human evolution; processes of racial formation of man; methods of classifying human races; cultural inheritance; the distinction of race, culture, personality, and intelligence; a review of modern racism; race as an evolutionary episode.

title: Religion in Culture
description: The nature of religion in different cultural systems.

title: Internship in Applied Anthropology
description: Supervised field experience of at least three weeks full time or 150 hours part time with an organization or institution in the application of anthropological approaches to problem solving and working in a professional setting. Emphasis is on anthropological skills in relation to the objectives and operations of an institution.

title: Internship in Museology
description: Practical professional museum experience of at least three weeks full time or 150 hours part time in the processing of collections, conservation, cataloging, archive and library maintenance, and/or planning and preparation of exhibits.

title: Independent Reading And Research In Anthropology
description: Guided reading and research on a specific anthropological topic of student interest, leading to preparation of a research paper.

title: Andean Archaeology
description: Examines the economic, social, and political development of the region, including the Tawantinsuya (Inca Empire). Covers the adoption of agriculture; the origin of the state; power; and the role of religion, cosmology, ideology, and art.

title: European Archeology
description: Survey of human prehistory in Europe from the earliest Paleolithic hominins to evolve in Europe, expansion of Homo sapiens  and associated cultural  developments, the spread of agriculture, diversity of Bronze Age adaptations, to the development of Celtic societies. Studies of archaeological and related evidence are investigated to interpret the dynamics of past ways of living.

title: Forensic Anthropology
description: Anthropological survey of the predominantly biological areas of forensic science, their methods and techniques, as they pertain to the application of that science to the purpose of the law. Particular emphasis will be given to perspectives about the science itself, its application to anthropology, and the unique ways in which that science may be used by law.

title: Primatology
description: Survey of the primate order including considerations of evolution, morphology, and behavior. Particular emphasis will be given to developing perspectives about the origin and evolution of hominids in the context of the primate order.

title: Human Growth and Development
description: Provides an anthropological examination of the process of growth and development in humans that emphasizes both the biological, evolutionary, and cultural aspects that have shaped them through time. Emphasis is given to the evolution of the life cycle, as well as the social and environmental conditions that affect human growth.

title: Osteology
description: Detailed study of human skeleton, with special attention to health and demographic conditions in prehistoric cultures and the evaluation of physical characteristics and genetic relationships of prehistoric populations. Laboratory demonstration and exercise in working with skeletal material for analysis of sex, age, stature, and race. Complete metric and nonmetric analysis with consideration given to paleodemography, paleopathology in situ analysis and excavation, and preservation.

title: Forensic Medicine And The Investigation Of Death
Bioarchaeology
description: Explores how archaeologists and bioanthropologists approach the study of death, mortuary practices and skeletal remains, to reconstruct past lives and understand the associated behavior.

title: Seminar in Osteology
description: Analysis of human and nonhuman skeletal remains including age, sex, stature, race, anomalities, pathologies, trauma, metric and nonmetric traits, cause of death, and time since death. This course allows greater breadth and depth of osteological analysis than either ANTH 694 or ANTH 695, and allows for more concentration on individual methods and techniques and case studies.

title: Paleoanthropology
description: Human origins and evolution as indicated by fossil evidence; interpretation of man-apes, Pithecanthropus, Neanderthal, Cro-Magnon, and other major fossil groups within the context of evolutionary theory, primate comparisons, and cultural evolution.

title: Writing Cultures: Ethnographic Methods
description: An introduction to qualitative field methods and research ethics in cultural anthropology. Students will examine issues of truth, representation, and reflexivity in ethnographic writing.

title: Research Methods in Digital Ethnography
description: A hands-on exploration of the uses of digital technology for ethnographic research and representation.

title: Field Methods in Linguistics
description: Techniques of collecting and analyzing linguistic data in the field. Work with language consultants in class, on languages such as Swahili.

title: Introduction to the Civilizations of South Asia I
description: Introduction to the Civilizations of South Asia I

title: Introduction to Pharmacology of Farm Animals
Topics in the Human Animal Bond
Introduction to Pharmacology for Health Professionals
description: Introduction to Pharmacology for Health Professionals is designed to provide an introduction to the principles of pharmacology and how they apply to health professionals.

title: Cardiorespiratory Physiology
description: An examination of the structure and function of the respiratory system and the manner in which oxygen passes from the atmosphere to its site of utilization in the mitochondria. Exercise, disease, and environmental stresses will form the basis for examining the capacity, plasticity, and limitations to respiratory function.

title: Cardiovascular Physiology
description: Study of the structure and function of the cardiovascular system as it pertains to health and disease. Topics include the control of blood pressure, vascular volume, and blood flow during orthostasis, aging and disease.

title: Histology for Health Professions
description: A brief introduction to the microscopic structure of the cells and tissues of a mammalian body. The class format will be a lecture followed by a laboratory session. The lectures will introduce the day's material, which will then be developed through laboratory sessions that include using light microscopy to read and interpret micrographs, drawing, making glass slides.

title: Stem Cells and Comparative Biomedicine
description: Characteristics of major categories of stem cells. Applicable or potential clinical uses, including their utilization in tissue engineering or targeted delivery of therapeutics.

title: Introduction to Cell Physiology
description: An introduction to cellular physiology. The objective of this course is to introduce students to the principles of cellular physiology and prepare them to apply the knowledge of cellular physiology to other medical disciplines such as Pharmacology.

title: Systems Physiology
description: An introduction to human physiology.  The physiology of each body system will be introduced and important variations in medically relevant species will be compared.

title: Bioinstrumentation Laboratory
description: Practical experience with and evaluation of laboratory and clinical techniques related to electrodes, transducers, computer-based data acquisition and analysis.

title: Respiratory Function In Health And Disease
Readings in Physiology
description: This course is designed to introduce students to primary literature in systems or cell physiology.  By the end of this course, students will be able to read and understand the physiology literature and will be prepared to use the physiology literature in future education and careers.

title: Problems in Anatomy and Physiology
description: Introduction of undergraduate and graduate students to research involving various anatomical and physiological systems.

title: Topics in Exercise Physiology
description: Intensive study of a selected topic in Exercise Physiology involving either great in-depth study, or application of theory presented in a related course.

title: Seminar for OYM Students
description: This seminar course is designed for students enrolled in the One Year Master's in Biomedical Sciences program.  Students will be exposed to current research in biomedicine and will have the opportunity to present.

title: Seminar
description: Designed primarily for graduate and senior students enrolled for graduate credit in physiology. Each student is required to give a report on some subject related to physiology. The course is intended to stimulate interest in research and evaluation of data.

title: Veterinary Physiology I
description: Function of the animal body at the cellular level, including nerve and muscle function. Basic pathophysiological mechanisms and intermediary metabolism will be emphasized and correlated with clinical topics.

title: Cell Physiology
description: Functions of the animal body are described with a primary focus at the cellular and molecular level with correlations to clinical topics. Course content builds on knowledge of chemistry, biochemistry, physics and biology. Basic physiological and pathophysiological mechanisms are examined in the context of the cell environment, intermediary metabolism and cell signaling.

title: Gross Anatomy I
description: Gross dissection of the dog with comparative aspects of the cat.

title: Integrated Physiological Systems 1
description: Microstructure and function of the cardiovascular, endocrine, respiratory, and renal systems of domestic animals with emphasis on physiologic control mechanisms, interrelationships of body systems, and criteria for evaluating animal health. Microanatomy laboratory is restricted to DVM students.  Graduate students should enroll in 5 credits unless they have made arrangements with the course instructor.

title: Introduction to Veterinary Medicine and Biomedical Research: Concepts and Values
description: This course is intended to provide first-year students an opportunity to experience the depth of veterinary/biomedical research required to advance the practice of veterinary medicine, and advance the field of medicine in general.  The students will examine the challenges of conducting 1) foundational and translation research (genetic/genomic and mechanistic research), 2) foundational and applications to clinical research 3) population and observational research (epidemiology, prospective clinical trials, retrospective studies), 4) pharmacologic research (the impact of pharmacology on the biomedical sciences).  The students will examine relevant connections, consider conceptual relationships, and critically evaluate integrative issues germane to both the practice of and the innovative progress of veterinary medicine.  Faculty members in the Departments of A&P, DM/P, and CS, will present topical overviews, based on their professional experiences. This will be designed to connect and integrate fundamental principles and relationships between research and clinical application.  These presentations provide examples of the fundamental requirements for clinical therapies and also provide examples of how innovative science leads to new therapies for long standing clinical disease.  Additionally, students will learn the tools required to search relevant topic to their career paths within the medical literature.  Finally, drawing on the multidisciplinary literature presented by faculty, students will explore the challenges in conducting genetic/genomic research, using genomic information, implementing research results to clinical practice, as well as addressing the social, legal, and professional policies that are emerging from new research. Students will be expected to do reading and to complete online worksheets and present their findings/opinions in class. Class time will be devoted to presentations and to discussion of readings and related current events.

title: Microscopic Anatomy I
description: Origin, development, and microscopic structure and appearance of the cells and tissues of the animal body.

title: Gross Anatomy II
description: Gross dissection of the horse and ruminant with comparative aspects of the pig, laboratory animals, and the chicken.

title: Integrated Physiological Systems 2
description: Microstructure and function of the reproductive and digestive systems of domestic animals with emphasis on physiologic control mechanisms, interrelationships of body systems, and criteria for evaluating animal health. Microanatomy laboratory is restricted to DVM students.  Graduate students should enroll in 3 credits unless they have made arrangements with the course instructor.

title: Veterinary Physiology II
description: Function of the cardiovascular, endocrine, respiratory, renal, digestive, and reproductive systems of domestic animals with emphasis on physiologic control mechanisms, interrelationships of body systems, and criteria for evaluating animal health.

title: Advanced Muscle Physiology
description: Course will examine current topics in muscle physiology.  Format to include discussion of scientific papers, oral presentations, and debate of controversial topics.

title: Physiology of Oxygen Transport
description: This course is designed to promote critical reading of the literature, particularly with respect to the understanding of exercise physiology and cardiorespiratory, vascular and muscle energetics responses.  Activities will include presenting papers, debate and discussion regarding all aspects of science and scientific philosophy.

title: Special Anatomy
description: The gross and/or microscopic study of any system (or systems) of any domestic animal.

title: Advanced Cardiovascular Physiology
description: Discussion and critical examination of cardiovascular control mechanisms, with specific emphasis on regulation of blood pressure during severe challenges such as exercise, heat stress, and upright posture.

title: Electricity in Biological Systems
description: This course provides students in the life and/or physical sciences with foundational understanding of electrical concepts that contribute to life processes spanning the molecular, cellular, systems, and ultimately organismal levels. Historical and cultural discussions of bioelectric phenomena introduce essential concepts for further consideration through critical reading of primary literature.

title: Toxins in the Biol Systems
description: An advanced toxicology course concerned with the cellular land subcellular effects of various groups of toxins on the intact animal organism.

title: Current Topics in Toxicology
description: An advanced toxicology course providing in-depth examination of toxicological area of current relevance to and/or controversy on mammalian health.

title: Problems in Toxicology
description: Individual investigations into the interactions of chemical compounds with living systems.

title: Animal Care and Welfare
description: This course will describe the veterinarian's role in protecting the health and welfare of animals. Students will be introduced to the major philosophical positions of animal use. The evolution of animal welfare science will be discussed and students will learn how to assess the welfare of companion, laboratory, zoo and food animals. Common causes of reduced animal welfare and ethical dilemmas will be covered.

title: RNA in Physiology, Biotechnology and Medicine
description: The objective of this course is to immerse students in a condensed study of RNA chemistry, their molecular anatomy and roles in cellular physiology, and how this is being exploited in research, biotechnology and medicine.

title: Anatomy l
description: Part 1 of the comparative anatomy sequence (AP 838, AP 842).  An introduction to comparative anatomy with a focus on human clinical anatomy.

title: Biomedical Methods Laboratory
description: An introduction to the theory and practice of advanced biomedical laboratory techniques.

title: Anatomy ll
description: An introduction to comparative human and domestic animal anatomy with a focus on clinical relevance.

title: Veterinary Pharmacology I
description: The basic principles of pharmacology, the interactions of drugs and living systems including fundamental principles of pharmacokinetics, pharmacodynamics, receptor-coupling, and mechanisms of action. A systems-based approach to drug classes will be emphasized.

title: Pharmacology II
description: The basic principles of pharmacology, the relationship of drug actions and interactions on physiological principles and pathophysiological conditions. Pharmacokinetics, pharmacodynamics, and a systems-based approach to drug classes will be emphasized.

title: Stem Cell Techniques
description: Cellular and molecular techniques and techniques on tissue culture. Lecture and laboratory hours to be determined.

title: Observing and Analyzing Animal Behavior
description: This course will guide students on how to design, conduct, and analyze animal behavior experiments. The course will emphasize the development of an ethogram and the proper way to score animal behavior, including counts, duration, latencies, and bouts. Students will develop their own ethogram to score and analyze the behaviors of a focal animal and present the data as a written project.

title: Advanced Physiology Review for Residents 1
description: This course is the first in a two semester intensive review of veterinary physiology and is meant to prepare residents for successful completion of board exams.

title: Advanced Physiology Review for Residents 2
description: This course is the second in a two semester intensive review of veterinary physiology and is meant to prepare residents for successful completion of board exams.

title: Ultrastructural Interpretation of the Nervous System
description: Study of the fine structure of neurons, axons, synapses, neuroglia and choroid plexus, the interconnections among neurons, the location of specific tracers and antibodies which define synaptic terminals, and a survey of methodologies used with transmission electron microscopy.

title: Advanced Cardiovascular Physiology
description: Comprehensive overview of cardiovascular physiology in domestic animals with special emphasis on the dog and horse, including current research.

title: Toxicology
description: Effects of harmful substances on the animal body. Emphasis placed on toxicologic principles and management of the poisoned patient.

title: Physiologically Based Pharmacokinetic Modeling
description: This modeling course is designed for life science graduate students with an interest in computational toxicology and pharmacology. Development of physiologically based pharmacokinetic (PBPK) models for environmental chemicals, drugs, and nanomaterials in laboratory animals, food-producing animals, companion animals, and/or humans will be introduced. Applications of PBPK models in food safety, toxicology, risk assessment, and pharmaceutical industry will be discussed. This course will cover theory, application software, experimental design, model development, optimization, validation, evaluation, and extrapolation. The expected outcome is that the student will have PBPK modeling knowledge and skills to develop a PBPK model with some mentoring from an experienced PBPK modeler. Format will include both weekly lectures and hands-on computer lab exercises.

title: Clinical Pharmacokinetics
description: An overview of pharmacokinetics with emphasis on practical implications for the clinician, including bioavailability, bioequivalence, residues in food of animal origin, dosage forms and regimens, therapeutic drug monitoring, drug interactions, interspecies difference, and the effect of disease on the pharmacokinetics of drugs. Background in physiology and statistics strongly recommended.

title: Mechanisms of Drug Action
description: Discussion of pharmacologic mechanisms at the molecular and cellular level, including receptors, second messengers, and pharmacokinetics. Specialized areas of pharmacology such as neuropharmacology and drug design will be discussed. Areas of current research interest will be emphasized.

title: Problems in Pharmacology
description: Individual investigations of the interactions of pharmaceutical compounds with living systems.

title: Beef Nutritional Health and Feeding Management
Diary Nutritional Health and Feeding Management
Introduction to Nanomedicine
description: This course is a brief introduction to nanomaterials and nanomedicine with a focus on clinical applications of nanotechnology. The course presents basic principles of nanotechnology; current clinical applications of nanomedicine; future horizons in nanomedicine.

title: Comparative Embryology
description: An overview of development anatomy as it relates to clinically important developmental defects and/or normal structure and function of common domestic animals.

title: Equine Exercise Physiology
description: Comprehensive overview of the physiology of exercise in the horse with comparison to other species. Emphasis will be on cardiovascular, respiratory, and musculoskeletal systems, including current research.

title: Introduction to Responsible Conduct of Biomedical Research
description: This course will focus on providing graduate/professional students an introduction to the regulations, practices, ethical considerations, and professional interactions that define responsible conduct of biomedical research. Investigator responsibilities associated with initiating and establishing a research program, conducting experimental studies, analyzing and reporting data, publishing in peer-reviewed journals, considerations for submitting grant applications, and understanding compliance issues and regulations will be emphasized. Students will learn through reading journal articles and discussion of pertinent topics to identify and consider issues that are germane to the biomedical research environment.

title: Problems in Animal Welfare
description: This course will expand on the role of veterinarians in the field of animal welfare. Students will be introduced to the major philosophical positions of animal use. The evolution of animal welfare science will be discussed and students will learn how to assess the welfare of companion, laboratory, zoo and food animals. Contemporary issues in animal welfare will also be explored.

title: Research
description: For graduate students in the field of anatomy or physiology working toward the M.S. Degree.

title: Physiology and Pharmacology of the Hormones
Advanced Physiology
description: The principles and techniques in the investigation of bioelectrical phenomena in relation to: (a) the physiology of the digestive organs, (b) myophysiology, (c) endocrinology, and (d) neurophysiology. Advanced physiological experiments will be conducted to provide an understanding of the applications of electronic equipment.

title: Problems in Physiology
description: Special problem-involving techniques utilized in studying the function of various organ systems of the body.

title: Research in Physiology
description: For graduate students working toward the Ph.D. Degree.

title: Finite Element and Other Approximate Methods in Mechanics
Arabic I
description: Introduction to the structure of modern Arabic. Essentials of grammar, speaking, reading, and writing.

title: Arabic III
description: Further development of four skills of listening, speaking, reading, and writing; with emphasis on building and applying basic language knowledge in communicative tasks; exercises in oral interactions and interactions involving text.

title: Arabic V
description: Further development of language and culture skills.

title: Arabic Vl
description: A continuation of Arabic V, with further development of language and culture skills.

title: Science,Technology and Architecture
description: An exploration of the interrelationships between architecture and various sciences including the technological applications of selected scientific theories.

title: Fundamentals of Architectural Technology
description: Instruction in the fundamentals of architectural technology with emphasis on the concepts and principles necessary for developing an integrated approach to building design.

title: History of Contemporary Architecture
description: A critical exploration of key ideas, practices, and works that have shaped the global history of architecture in the 20th and 21st centuries.

title: Basic Problems in Architectural Design
description: Study of specific architectural design problems under the direction of a member of the architecture faculty.

title: Digital Representation
description: This course introduces various techniques and theoretical contexts of digital representation in architecture, including two-and three-dimensional parametric drawing and modeling, and vector and raster graphics. Course is structured around weekly topical lectures and readings, introductory technical tutorials and individual practical application.

title: Digital Architecture I
description: This course will introduce students to digital management concepts, basic architectural digital representation, 3-dimensional computer modeling, raster, and vector graphics with an emphasis on conceptual development.

title: Architecture Through the Ages
description: An introductory survey of the history of architecture worldwide from its prehistoric beginnings up to the present day.

title: Appreciation of Architecture
description: An analysis of the evolution of architectural styles to determine the relation of architectural expression to the needs of society.

title: Architectural Design Studio I
description: Instruction in architectural design focusing on the application of elements and principles of form and space in design. Instruction in the use of techniques for visually representing design ideas.

title: Architectural Design Studio II
description: Instruction in architectural design focusing on the synthesis of basic social, functional, technical, and aesthetic factors in design. Continued instruction in techniques for visually representing design ideas.

title: Introduction to Architectural Theory
description: An introduction to the ideas and literature that have driven architectural discourse and informed practice through various eras.

title: Structural Systems in Architecture I
description: Introduction to structure as a building system.  Overview of contemporary structural systems and their components, including wood, timber, steel, concrete, masonry, and hybrid structures.  Discussion of building code requirements for structure, general guidelines for building structures, including appropriate application, and methods for schematic estimation of structural member sizing.  Basic understanding of how gravity and lateral forces are propagated through a structural frame from load to building foundation.

title: Structural Systems in Architecture
description: Introduction to statics; force analysis and the study of forces in equilibrium; principles of statics as applied to the study of simple elemental structures; the origin, the nature, and the action of loads on structural systems. Instruction in the use of statics in the preliminary stages of building design. For ARCH and IAPD students.

title: Digital Architecture II
description: This course will introduce students to methods for digital 3-dimensional visualization, including rendering, animation, compositing and analysis.

title: Accelerated Architectural Design Studio I
description: An introduction to the principles, processes and vocabularies of architectural design; basic architectural concerns (space/light/form and human factors); and techniques for visually representing design ideas (sketching/diagramming/instrument-aided drawings and model making).

title: Architectural Design Studio III
description: Relation of structures to their environment; client and community restraint; development of building programs; synthesis of functional, technical, and aesthetic considerations in the design of structures for human use.

title: Architectural Design Studio IV
description: Relation of structures to their environment; client and community restraint; development of building programs; synthesis of functional, technical, and aesthetic considerations in the design of structures for human use.

title: Environmental Systems in Architecture I
description: Instruction in environmental and building control systems of architecture, including bioclimatic and ecological design principles. Emphasis on integrated system design approach, with a focus on passive and natural strategies. Contemporary and developing approaches are explored.

title: Building Construction Systems In Architecture I
description: A lecture course that develops an understanding of how materials and systems assembly reinforce and extend the intentions of the designer as well as an understanding of the strategies and techniques for integration and coordination of the building components.  For ARCH and IAPD students.

title: Building Construction Systems in Architecture II
description: This course develops a more sophisticated level of understanding of how materials and systems assembly reinforce and extend the intentions of the designer. Students will develop their ability to both analyze and employ building construction techniques and assemblies as a design generator in preparation for Architectural Design Studio V.

title: Structural Systems in Architecture II
description: Instruction in the quantitative analysis of structural systems, focusing on design loads, material strengths, structural behavior, and system sizing.

title: Structural Systems in Architecture
description: Instruction in strength of materials focusing on the behavior of building materials under loading; their ability to resist deformation and failure. Instruction in sizing simple structural elements.  For ARCH and IAPD students.

title: Structural Systems in Architecture III
description: Instruction in the design of building structures as whole systems. Instruction in the principles of structural subsystem design; emphasis on the overall structural behavior and subsystems integrity required to achieve a variety of building forms.  Instruction in strategies for the use of approximation in the manipulation of key quantitative properties of whole systems and major subsystems in building design.

title: Structural Systems in Architecture IV
description: Instruction in the design of building structures as whole systems; overall-to-specific systems behavior and manipulative design of major subsystems. Emphasis on the design of subsystems and subsystem components as they are affected by structural material. Instruction in specialized issues associated with the analysis and design of high rise and long-span building structure, including foundation, constructive, and economic factors which affect building design.

title: Problems in Architectural Presentation
description: Study of various methods of graphically representing architectural problems to develop professional office techniques.

title: Academic Internship Preparation
description: Preparation for an academic internship in the field of architecture.

title: Academic Internship
description: Off-campus work study program with an approved professional sponsor.

title: Academic Internship Seminar
description: Seminar on practice-based topics in architecture.

title: Architectural Internship, Part C
description: A continuation of the approved off-campus internship of ARCH 505 and ARCH 506. Preparation of internship journals, employer profiles, and a final internship analysis paper based on the entire 30-week internship experience.

title: Environmental Systems in Architecture II
description: Instruction in criteria for selection and application of natural and mechanical environmental control systems in architecture. Focus on the integration of thermal, illumination, sanitary, movement, and acoustical systems within the building and the natural environment. Contemporary and developing approaches are explored.

title: Environmental Systems in Architecture III
description: Criteria for selection and application of natural and mechanical environmental control systems in architecture. Focus on the integration of thermal, illumination, sanitary, movement, and acoustical systems with the building fabric and the natural environment. Contemporary and developing approaches are explored.

title: Problems in Architectural Design
description: Study of specific design problems under the direct supervision of a member of the architecture faculty.

title: Topics in History of the Designed Environment
Architectural Design Studio V
description: Design and development of an architectural project beyond schematic design.

title: Architectural Design Studio VI
description: Design and development of architectural projects on topics proposed by faculty. On or off-campus studio. Department and College approval required for off-campus studios, whether in a domestic or international studies program or university.

title: Architectural Programming
description: An introductory course surveying the basic philosophies and methodologies for architectural programming; emphasis on the comparative evaluation of different strategies and their integration within the process of design.

title: Study Abroad Orientation
description: Preparation for participation in study abroad with a focus on specific information pertaining to the design professions and the analysis of the built and natural environment.

title: History of American Architecture and Allied Design I
History of American Architecture and Allied Design II
Topics in History of the Designed Environment
description: Seminar on topics involving specific periods or subjects in the history of the built environment.

title: Environmental Aesthetics
description: Problems involving aesthetics in areas related to student's major field.

title: Topics in Environment and Behavior
description: Seminar on topics in human behavior as it relates to physical environments.

title: Project Programming
description: The development of a program for ARCH 707, Architectural Design VIII, under direction of a faculty member.

title: Architectural Design Studio VII
description: Integration of the physiological, psychological, and sociological parameters in the design of environments. Analysis, programming, and planning problems, increased complexity of function and space definition systems. Relating environmental technology to total design.

title: Architectural Design Studio VIII
description: Development of the student's project programmed in ARCH 705, under the direction of a faculty committee. Project must demonstrate a high level of achievement in systematic and comprehensive thinking, application of resources, and communication of total process.

title: Topics in Architectural Design Methods
description: Intensive review of selected design methodologies, including systematic and computer-based approaches to problem definition and project design; emphasis upon the comparative evaluation of problem-solving strategies within the architectural design process.

title: Topics in Architecture Seminar
description: Seminars that investigate a wide range of topics central to the discipline of architecture.

title: Topics in Environmental Systems in Architecture
description: A concentrated study of a particular subject related to the environmental systems of the built environment.

title: Environment and Behavior
description: Investigates the relationship between human behavior and design of the physical environment. Includes identification of psychological, social, cultural concepts which influence and are influenced by the built environment. Emphasizes applying this knowledge in design.

title: Architectural Research Methods
description: An introductory course surveying the basic philosophies and methodologies of science and research as they apply to the field of architecture.

title: Environmental Design And The Aging Process
Topics in Building Construction Systems in Architecture
description: Advanced study of the relationship of conceptual and/or technological factors of building construction to architecture.

title: Writing Intensive Architecture Seminar
description: A writing intensive seminar shall be an Architecture Seminar where over half of the course grade is based on written assignments (including papers, assignments, quizzes and exams).

title: Topics in Structural Systems in Architecture
description: Study of the relationship of conceptual and/or technological factors of structure to architectural design.

title: Foundations of Practice
description: This course examines the foundational principles of architectural practice in the United States. It equips students with the essential knowledge and understanding of the instruments of service for delivering a building project from concept to realization and positions them to think critically about each step in the process.

title: Advanced Problems in Architecture
description: Study of specific architectural problems under the direct supervision of a member of the architecture faculty.

title: History of American Architecture and Allied Design 1
History of American Architecture and Allied Design 2
Practicum
description: Practical application of studies related to a student's area focus.

title: Topics in History of the Designed Environment
description: Seminar on topics involving specific periods or subjects in the history of the built environment.

title: Environmental Aesthetics
description: Problems involving aesthetics in areas related to student's major field.

title: Topics in Environment and Behavior
description: Seminar on topics in human perceptions, reactions, and behavior as they relate to physical environments.

title: Comprehensive Design Preparation
description: Scholarly and design-based research, intended to deeply inform architectural decision-making, culminating in a program for an architectural project to be undertaken in ARCH 808.

title: Topics in Architectural Design Methods
description: Intensive review of selected design methodologies, including systematic and computer-based approaches to problem definition and project design; emphasis upon the comparative evaluation of problem-solving strategies within the architectural design process. May be repeated for credit with different topics.

title: Topics in Architecture
description: Seminar on topics in architecture with readings, class discussion, student presentations, research papers or projects.

title: Topics in Environmental Systems in Architecture
description: A concentrated study of a particular subject related to the environmental systems of the built environment.

title: Architectural Design Studio VII
description: Advanced design and development of architectural projects on topics proposed by faculty.

title: Architectural Design Studio VIII
description: Advanced design and development of an architectural project using the program developed in ARCH 805. Students must demonstrate the ability to achieve a comprehensive, integrated design from site- to detail-scale.

title: Environment and Behavior
description: Investigates the relationship between human behavior and design of the physical environment. Includes identification of psychological, social, cultural factors which influence and are influenced by the built environment. Emphasizes applying this knowledge in design.

title: Advanced Architectural Studies
description: Studies related to the area of focus of a M.S. Arch. student in the non-thesis option. Preparation of a non-thesis proposal and a non-thesis project which may include a design project, synthesis paper, portfolio of written work, series of case studies, or other project. May be taken by M. Arch. student with departmental permission only.

title: Topics in Building Construction Systems in Architecture
description: Advanced study of the relationship of conceptual and/or technological factors of building construction to architecture.

title: Topics in Structural Systems in Architecture II
description: Study of the relationship of conceptual and/or technological factors of structure to architectural design.

title: Professional Practice: Professional Responsibility
description: Interdisciplinary course addresses the connections between allied design professions and the larger community.  It helps students to understand and appreciate their professional legal and ethical responsibilities.

title: Professional Practice: Office Practices
description: Interdisciplinary course addresses practice-based issues concerning professional relationships in the design and delivery process, management of projects, communication practices, and fiscal concerns.

title: Professional Practice: Discipline-Specific Topics
description: Covers discipline-specific concerns, focusing on issues specific to architecture, interior architecture and product design, landscape architecture or regional and community planning.

title: Advanced Problems in Architectural Design
description: Study of specific architectural design problems under the direct supervision of a member of the architecture faculty.

title: History of American Architecture and Allied Design 1
History of American Architecture and Allied Design 2
Thesis
description: Study in architecture and related fields leading to thesis.

title: ARE New Student Workshop
description: Familiarize entering Architectural Engineering students with the nature of the architectural, engineering, and construction industry with an emphasis on terminology and professionalism.  Information related to architectural engineering, department policies, student organizations, department faculty, and career opportunities will be introduced.

title: ARE Career Readiness Workshop
description: Continued familiarization with the architectural, engineering, and construction industry with a stronger emphasis on terminology and professionalism, the fundamentals of engineering exam, and an emphasis on post-graduation preparation.

title: Architectural Engineering Seminar
description: Presentation of professional problems and practices by students, faculty, and professionals associated with the career of architectural engineering.

title: Architectural Engineering Orientation
description: Introduction to architectural engineering. Promotion of interaction and connection with the department as well as a network of academic and professional networks through the use of learning communities. Familiarize entering students with the nature of the architectural, engineering, and construction industry with an emphasis on terminology and professionalism.

title: Introduction to Building Systems
description: An introduction to building systems including: structural, electrical, mechanical, and plumbing. An emphasis will be made on the importance of these systems to the success of building function and operation. The course will not only expose the students to each system but the criticalness of integration between the systems as well as the architecture and construction of the facility.

title: Introduction to AutoCAD
description: An introduction to the basics of AutoCAD.

title: Introduction to Building Information Modeling
description: Introduction to building information modeling as a technology tool related to the engineering and construction industry.

title: Plan Reading & Systems Communications
description: The integration of architectural, structural, mechanical, electrical and plumbing building systems, the impact of codes/standards on design, plan reading, and drawing creation.

title: ARE Professional Practice
description: General business, management and contractual procedures in professional practice. Personal development of skills needed to enter and be accepted in industry today.

title: Undergraduate Research Experience
description: Open to students pursuing undergraduate research projects.

title: Honors Research in Architectural Engineering
description: Individual research problem selected with approval of faculty advisor. Open to students in the college of engineering honors program. A report is presented orally and in writing during the last semester.

title: Computer Aided Architectural Engineering
description: An introduction to the use of computer software for the design and integration of building systems. The course is intended to complement and enhance the content of the concurrent system courses (Mechanical II, Electrical II, and Structures II). The computer software used will represent the standard practices of the building construction industry.

title: Acoustic Systems
description: Hearing and the ear, sound generation, acoustical correction, noise reduction, and sound transmission all as integral parts of architectural design.

title: Power Systems
description: An introduction and study of the basic concepts and components of building electrical distribution systems, including circuit design.

title: Mechanical Systems I
description: An introduction to building HVAC and plumbing system terminology, principles, and practices of design and layout specifically for applications of human comfort and safety.

title: Structural Steel Design
description: Introduction to design of steel structures. Theoretical and practical bases for proportioning members, their connections, and layout specifically for applications of buildings.

title: Heating, Ventilating and Air Conditioning Systems
description: An introduction to building HVAC terminology, principles, and practices of design and layout specifically for applications of human comfort and safety.

title: Lighting Systems
description: The study and application of luminaires, lighting systems design and control, circuit and utilization component design, and design of building electrical systems distribution and service equipment.

title: Mechanical Systems II
description: The study of building HVAC system selection and application including load calculations, space coordination, system design and layout, and scheduling of equipment and materials.

title: Reinforced Concrete Design
description: Introduction to design of reinforced concrete structures. Theoretical and practical bases for proportioning members, layout specifically for applications of buildings and characteristic as a construction material.

title: Plumbing Systems
description: An introduction to building plumbing system terminology, principles, and practices of design and layout specifically for applications of human comfort and safety.

title: Systems Integration and Design
description: An analysis of structural, mechanical, electrical and plumbing systems design processes, budgeting and constructability that supports the Conceptual Design and Schematic Design phases of building projects.

title: Problems in Architectural Engineering
description: A study of specific design problems under the direct supervision of a member of the architectural engineering faculty.

title: Introduction to LEED
description: Introduction to green building design and construction principles and practices based on the Leadership in Energy and Environmental Design (LEED) Green Building Rating System of the United States Green Building Council (USGBC).

title: ARE Capstone
description: In a studio fashion, student working individually or as part of a small team will prepare and present a design project of appropriate scope and complexity with emphasis on structural or mechanical/electrical/plumbing building systems.

title: Building Energy Codes and Standards
description: Study of the background, importance, impact, and application of the energy codes to the mechanical and electrical systems design process.

title: Energy Modeling Lab
description: Study of current building energy sources and trends, basic engineering economics applied to building energy use, energy calculations for building systems, and software-based whole building energy simulation using eQUEST.

title: Problems in Architectural Engineering
description: Participation in student design competition teams under the direct supervision of a member of the architectural engineering faculty.

title: Topics in Architectural Engineering
description: A study of specific design problems in architectural engineering.

title: Building Loads and Computer Aided Structural Analysis and Design
description: Fundamentals of building structural systems and design with the emphasis on building loads and load paths. Review of International Building Code's structural loading requirements, applications, and determination of design loads by ASCE 7 - Minimum Design Loads and Associated Criteria for Buildings and Other Structures.  Introduction of building design and analysis software basics. Students will learn how to generate building structural models, and conduct analysis, design, and simulation using software applications.

title: Timber Structures
description: Analysis and design of timber structures including dimension lumber, glu-lam members, and engineered wood products.

title: Advanced Steel Design
description: Structural design computations for beams, girders, columns and beam-columns. Design of connections (bolted & welded). Structural working drawings (plan, elevation and connection details). Overview of failure mechanisms and design procedures for plate girders. AISC requirements for prevention of various failure mechanisms.

title: Cold-Formed Steel Design
description: Principles of behavior, design, fabrication, and construction of cold-formed steel structures.

title: Masonry Structural Design
description: Introduction to masonry materials, specifications, testing and construction methods. The design of unreinforced and reinforced masonry structures according to applicable building codes.

title: Building Seismic Design
description: Continuation of ARE 553 and ARE 563 with special emphasis on seismic design as applied to a complete structure.

title: Advanced Lighting Design
description: Design and application of lighting systems for commercial buildings, using current industry practices and computer-assisted analysis.

title: Advanced Mechanical Hydronic Systems Design
description: Design and application of mechanical systems for buildings, focusing on hydronic and steam systems, expanding on the material and topics presented in prior HVAC courses.

title: Advanced Mechanical Air Systems Design
description: Design and application of mechanical systems for buildings, focusing on air systems, expanding on the material and topics presented in prior HVAC courses.

title: Advanced Electrical Systems Design
description: Design and application of various electrical distribution system for commercial buildings. The course uses the National Electrical Code in conjunction with current industry practices.

title: Advanced Plumbing Design
description: Design and application of domestic water and waste systems, storm water systems, fuel gas systems, medical gas systems and fire protections systems.

title: Building Communication Systems
description: Detailed design and analysis of special electrical systems for buildings including, fire alarm, and communication systems.

title: Communications And Energy Management Systems Design
Problems in Architectural Engineering
description: A study of a specific problem under the direct supervision of a member of the architectural engineering faculty.

title: Master's Report
description: Topics selected with approval of major professor and department head.

title: Master's Thesis
description: Topics selected with approval of major professor and department head.

title: Ceramics for Non-majors
description: A foundational survey in ceramic processes covering sculptural and vessel-based making as well as nontraditional practices in ceramic art. Students learn the basic origins of clay, its physical properties, and working characteristics. Surface expression is emphasized through the use of colored slips and glazes and atypical applications.

title: Jewelry Design and Processes for Non-majors
description: Students without a background in art will be introduced to basic forming and construction techniques and design principles for jewelry and small-scale metal objects.

title: Digital Photography for Non-majors
description: Introduction to digital photography including the use of DSLR camera, Adobe Lightroom, and Adobe Photoshop. This course is designed for non-Majors

title: AandD: Career and Creative Industry Seminar
description: Introduction to art concentrations and professions.

title: Introduction to Modern and Contemporary Art for Non-Majors
description: A beginner's guide to understanding and appreciating movements in modern art from Realism, Impressionism, Post-Impressionism, Fauvism, Cubism, De Stijl, Constructivism, Dada, and Surrealism to Abstract Expressionism, Pop Art, Minimalism, and Conceptual Art, as well as art from the postmodern period   Neo-Expressionism, Neo-Abstraction, Neo-Conceptualism, Grafitti Art, etc.   to the present.

title: Photography, Civil Rights, Art and the Environment
description: Surveys the role(s) photography plays in society focusing on the US civil rights movement and built/natural environment. Examines examples of journalistic and artistic practices in the medium of photography that both look back, question and critique the present with regard to these topics.

title: A and D: Mixed Media Process and Exploration Lab
description: A studio-based experience designed for students interested in exploring visual arts and design through practical, experimental methods. Focusing on various techniques, materials, and inter-university collaborations, offering students an opportunity to discover their artistic interests while connecting with the academic and creative community

title: Drawing I
description: Fundamental principles of drawing skills based on observation through the introduction of basic materials and techniques.

title: A and D: Community and the Environment Experience
description: A foundational art experience that delves into the crossroads of art, design, and social and environmental matters. Students will harness diverse media to express their views on topics like community identity, cultural diversity, environmental justice, and urban planning. Through collaborative projects, including research and fieldwork, students engage with community partners, making this course relevant for all majors interested in applying visual literacy and artistic sensibility to real-world issues.

title: Survey of Western Art History I
description: Historical development of art from pre-history through the Middle Ages.

title: Survey of Western Art History II
description: Historical development of art from the Renaissance to the nineteenth century.

title: Survey of Non-Western Art History
description: An introductory survey of the art of Africa, Asia, Oceania, and the Americas from prehistory to the present.

title: A and D: Visual Representation and Fabrication
description: Explore the foundations of 2D and 3D design in 'AandD: Visual Representation and Fabrication,' developing skills in creating, analyzing, and manipulating visual forms across different media, including drawing, design, and 3D modeling. Fosters visual literacy, creativity, and an understanding of scale, composition, and cultural contexts, making it suitable for students of all majors seeking to enhance their creative sensibility.

title: Art and Insects
description: Provides a framework for examining arts and insects in which students consider evolution, genetics, behavior, and biodiversity, while also learning aesthetic interpretation, analysis of color and shape, perception, and the role of insect representations in the arts and humanities.

title: Visual Communication Design Fundamentals
description: An introduction to Visual Communication Design, focusing on design principles, concept development, and basic tools used in the profession.

title: Visual Communication Design I: Typography and Design Principles
description: Studio design experience introducing the formal and semiotic aspects of typographic systems as applied in design. Students implement the elements and principles of visual language to communicate through typography. Topics include characteristics, classifications, tools, and methods for communicating with typography as content, form, and image. Concepts, skills, process activities, and methods introduced are applicable across all areas of design.

title: Special Studies in Art
description: Specialized workshops or seminars conducted in studio, art therapy, art education, or art history.

title: Human Form and Composition
description: Building stylization and expressive image making of the human form with experimental methods: use of color, mono-print, mixed media. A connected and sustained studio time available during intersession only, providing students a working rhythm without interruption from other course work.

title: Visual Communication Design Techniques I
description: A hands-on experience intended to introduce non-majors to the visual and critical thinking skills that Visual Communication Designers use when creating print-based communication using text and image.

title: Digital Illustration
description: Introductory survey of digital drawing and illustration techniques whereby students are introduced to digital illustration software, illustration generes, methods, and materials.

title: Introduction to Museum Studies
description: Fundamentals of museum work including specific museum functions, role of professional personnel, and proper care and handling of art works.

title: Drawing: Expanded Media
description: Extension of Introduction of Drawing, observational drawing enhanced by composition, expanded materials and techniques.

title: Fundamentals of Fiberart and Materials Studies
description: Introduction to fiberart processes and innovative material as an art form in methods such as small scale tapestry weaving; fabric dye techniques; dye discharge techniques; fabric pigment applications of painting, stamping, printing; and non-loom basketry coiling or felting.

title: Digital Techniques in Visual Art
description: Survey of the most frequently used programs in the studio arts and equipment necessary for successful navigation on the digital arena.

title: Introduction to Digital and Expanded Media
description: Explores digital and expanded media and technology for artistic production including use of computers for graphics, sound, electronics, time-based art practice, installation, and spacial practices, interactive/performance, and programming. Students will apply these skills by relating them to contemporary issues and ideas within the expanded field of artistic production.

title: Introduction to Printmaking Techniques
description: A foundational survey of printmaking techniques for image development and creative expression. Individual and collaborative print based studio work in intaglio (etching), lithography, relief (woodcut), stencil (screen print), digital, or monoprint printmaking processes. Emphasis on exploration and experimentation through the handmade print and the graphic aesthetic.

title: Introduction to Oil Painting
description: Technical aspects of oil painting materials, including preparation of grounds and use of limited color palettes.

title: Drawing III
description: Continuation of Drawing II, emphasizing exploration in mixed media.

title: Introduction to 3D Modeling for Jewelry, Ceramics and Design
description: Techniques in the design of 3D objects with an emphasis on jewelry and ceramics employing various media.

title: Introduction to the Ceramics Spectrum
description: Introduces the spectrum of ceramics processes and techniques including hand building, slip casting, traditional glaze applications, and image transfer techniques.

title: Introduction to Metals and Jewelry
description: Students will be introduced to basic forming and construction techniques and design processes for jewelry and small-scale metal objects.

title: Introduction to Sound Art
description: Includes digital, electronic, environmental, and acoustic sound in the visual arts context. Sound as component of a studio practice; fundamentals of recording, digital sound editing, electronics, time-based structures, and innovative presentation methods.

title: Visual Communication Design III: Storytelling in Design
description: Organize complex systems of text and image material.  May address  photographic, sequential, or time-based media.

title: Minor in Art - Capstone
description: Students enroll in tis during their final semseter as an art minor

title: A and D: Undergraduate Research Studio
description: Delves into contemporary art, exploring it as a form of research and cultivating critical thinking skills. Students will analyze individual artists and art movements in the context of contemporary art theory, all while gaining insights into professional practice and development strategies. Combines readings, lectures, discussions, and hands-on studio sessions, complemented by online resources for staying updated on the latest developments in the field

title: Visual Communication Design Topics
description: Specialized Visual Communication Design course on varying topics.

title: B.F.A Exhibition Or Portfolio Presentation
description: Course builds an awareness of professional practices and emphasizes the production and exhibition of a final portfolio of the student's art work. Development of evaluative skills involved in the resolution and presentation of a body of individual work.

title: Undergraduate Art Studio Assistant in Faculty Research
description: Students will assist faculty with creative research. Students will gain invaluable firsthand skills and insight of professional art practices. Research activities include but are not limited to production of artwork, preparation of art making materials, exhibition and curatorial research, and professional practice.

title: Visual Communication Design IV: Design and Context
description: Explores issues related to the creation of visual design systems in the media and contexts within which they may be deployed.

title: Independent Study/Ceramics
description: Hands-on experience in studio workflow and project management.

title: Independent Study/Crafts
description: Work in crafts after competency has been achieved. Personal development is emphasized.

title: Independent Study/Drawing
description: Work in drawing after competency has been achieved. Personal development is emphasized.

title: Independent Study/Graphic Design
description: Work in graphic design after competency has been achieved. Personal development is emphasized.

title: Independent Study/Metalsmithing and Jewelry
description: Work in metalsmithing and jewelry after competency has been achieved. Personal development is emphasized.

title: Independent Study/Painting
description: Work in painting after competency has been achieved. Personal development is emphasized.

title: Independent Study/Printmaking
description: Work in printmaking after competency has been achieved. Personal development is emphasized.

title: Independent Study/Water Color
description: Work in water color after competency has been achieved. Personal development is emphasized.

title: Independent Study in Photography
description: Advanced course in photography with an emphasis on developing personal expression using photographic media, under the mentorship of the instructor.

title: Independent Study/Research Computer Art and Design
description: This course is intended to provide students an opportunity to focus on a specific visual project/problem that will be solved using computers to focus on as the primary tool/medium.

title: Visual Communication Design Techniques II
description: Develops an intermediate level practical understanding of the issues that Visual Communication designers face when they seek to combine words, ideas, and images into a more meaningful whole. Students will be exploring how the manipulation of the arrangement of text and image can effect visual organization, interest, and meaning. Focuses on visual conception and composition rather than software application, and is not Intended for students majoring in Visual Communication Design.

title: Advanced Techniques in Jewelry and Metal Design
description: Introduction and exploration of advanced techniques in jewelry and metal design, such as chasing, stone setting, enameling, various metal forming, and direct casting of 3d printed resin models.

title: Digital and Expanded Media Special Topics
description: Specialized studio art experience exploring technology and experimental media in the context of contemporary fine art production. Topics may include community art practice, systems art, ecology-based art, interactive media, code-based art and locative media.

title: Color Printmaking Workshop: Creative Process and Investigation
description: Individual and collaborative studio work in intaglio (etching), lithography, relief printing (woodcut), screen printing, or monoprint printmaking processes. Color theory, experimental and practical techniques provide experience in multicolor and intermedia graphic production. Emphasis on multi-color intermedia print development through creative process and material investigation.

title: Color Printmaking Workshop: Experimental Techniques and Vision
description: Individual and collaborative studio work in intaglio (etching), lithography, relief printing (woodcut), screen printing, or monoprint printmaking processes. Emphasis on multi-color intermedia print development through experimental techniques and artistic vision.

title: Ceramic Sculpture Studio
description: Sculptural ideas and methods in ceramics are explored through a variety of hand-building techniques. This course is designed for students majoring in other departments as well as art majors.

title: Twentieth Century Art History I: Post-Impressionism to Cubism
description: Origins and development of twentieth century western art from 1880 to 1914.

title: Twentieth Century Art History II: Dada to Surrealism
description: Origins and development of twentieth century western art from 1914 to 1950.

title: Painting Theme and Variation
description: Painting, with emphasis on subject and concept as the catalyst for development.  Painting’s dialogue will be explored through variations on theme, content, and subject in both historical and contemporary contexts.

title: Topics in Digital Photography
description: An intermediate level exploration of digital photography, students continue to develop their understanding of DSLR camera and software-based techniques to produce artwork. Students work on practical projects using digital imaging technologies such as DSLR capture, scanning, and professional inkjet printing, and research the use of such technologies by contemporary artists.

title: Wheel Thrown Ceramics Studio
description: Elements of intermediate functional ceramics and object design are explored with the pottery wheel, and intended for students majoring in other departments as well as art majors.

title: Topics in Analog Photography
description: An intermediate level exploration of photography, students continue to develop their analog photographic processes such as film developing, darkroom printing, and several historic photographic processes. Students work on practical projects using medium and large format film cameras, professional studio lighting equipment, and research the use of such technologies and processes by contemporary artists.

title: Painting Materials Application
description: Painting's dialogue will be explored through conventional and experimental materials and process in both historical and contemporary contexts. Emphasis on materials and process as synergists for subject, representation and content.

title: Web Art
description: Introduction to interactive web and network-based artistic practice. Topics covered: network art, interactive art, locative media, html, and code-based art.  Explores the history of web and network-based art practice.

title: Visual Communication Design Capstone I: Research
description: Development of a research trajectory to culminate in the planning and execution of the capstone project in the Spring semester.

title: Matrix Studio and Business Practices
description: Students are selected by portfolio review, design projects are completed to client specifications. Professional design management, ethics, setting up a business, client/designer relationships, and contractual options, billing practices, and operating procedures will be covered.

title: Visual Communication Design Capstone II: Project
description: Conduct research, planning, and execution of a capstone project in visual communication design.

title: Internships in Visual Art & Design
description: Emphasis on problem solving and related skills in visual art, art history, and graphic design in a professional setting.

title: Visual Communication Design BFA Portfolio and Professional Practices
description: Develops awareness of professional practices and helps prepare the students for professional life after graduation. Requires a final design portfolio and public presentation and/or exhibit.

title: Design for Digital Media II
description: Continues the exploration of designing for dynamic media including developing competencies in the areas of visual narrative, rhythm, space, animation, sound and video.

title: Drawing Methods – Thematic Approaches
description: Developing a thematic body of drawings.

title: Drawing Workshop
description: Research with emphasis on proposing a self-directed drawing project.

title: Graphic Design History/Theory/Criticism
description: Study of significant works from late nineteenth century to the present to provide understanding of the development and character of graphic design, artists, and designers.

title: Twentieth Century Art History III: Abstract Expressionism to Pop Art
description: Art movements beginning with abstract expressions and continuing through pop, op, minimal, and conceptual art movements up to 1980.

title: Twentieth Century Art History IV: Conceptual Art to Contemporary Art
description: The art movements of the 1980s beginning with photo-realism and continuing through pattern and decoration, new image art, neo-expressionisms, and neo-abstraction.

title: Greek Art History
description: The art of classical Greece, from its Aegean origins through the Hellenistic period.

title: Visual Art Administration & Leadership
description: Surveys facets of management in the visual arts as preparation for work in non-profit museums, galleries or commercial galleries, and working within communities generating projects of impermanence such as performance, social practice and generosity works, pop-up exhibitions, and on-line art projects.

title: Special Studies in Art
description: Specialized workshops or seminars conducted in studio, art therapy, art education, or art history.

title: Art History Research Seminar
description: Small collaborative research and writing workshop in art history. Students pursue their choice of topic in visual art and are introduced to tools and strategies for conducting research, analyzing peer-reviewed literature, formulating a thesis, and writing a final research paper for presentation.

title: Figure Drawing II
description: Continuation of Figure Drawing I, with emphasis on individual expression.

title: History of Italian Renaissance Art
description: Renaissance art of Italy from the thirteenth through the sixteenth centuries.

title: History of Northern Renaissance Art
description: Renaissance Art of Northern Europe from the fourteenth through the sixteenth century.

title: Advance Concepts in Digital Video
description: Introduction to digital video and experimental practices related to moving images and storytelling.

title: Baroque Art History
description: The development of the baroque period in northern and southern Europe, from its beginnings in the early seventeenth century to the rococo style of the eighteenth century.

title: Advanced Concepts in Digital and Expanded Media
description: Studio exploration of advanced concepts in Digital and Expanded Media artistic practice.

title: Advanced Studies in Photography
description: Advanced work in photography with an emphasis on personal development. Focuses on developing well-researched concrete body of work based on prior experience, and relevant new research in the history, and contemporary practices in art photography.

title: Advanced Study in Photography II
description: Advanced work in photography with an emphasis on personal development. Focuses on developing well-researched concrete body of work based on prior experience, and relevant new research in the history, and contemporary practices in art photography.

title: Foreign Studies in Art History
description: Participation in art history study abroad.

title: Foreign Studies in Visual Communication Design
description: Participation in Visual Communication study abroad.

title: Foreign Studies in Studio Art
description: Participation in studio art study abroad.

title: Contemporary Media Seminar
description: A review of trends in contemporary media and visual communication.

title: The Development of American Art
description: American art from the Colonial period to the beginnings of abstract expressionism in the early 1940s, with major emphasis on the late nineteenth and early twentieth century developments.

title: History of Modern Sculpture
description: Directions in sculpture since the time of Rodin.

title: Advanced Printmaking Workshop: Illustration and Experimentation
description: Advanced level individual and collaborative print based studio work in intaglio (etching), lithography, relief printing (woodcut), screen printing, digital, or monoprint printmaking processes. Emphasis on illustration of ideas and technical experimentation.

title: Advanced Printmaking Workshop: Imagination and Innovation
description: Advanced level individual and collaborative print based studio work in intaglio (etching), lithography, relief printing (woodcut), screen printing, digital or monoprint printmaking processes. Emphasis on imagination and technical innovations.

title: Nineteenth Century Art History
description: Painting, sculpture, and architecture of the late eighteenth and nineteenth centuries, with emphasis on the art of France.

title: Sculpture II
description: Emphasis on artistic development through exploratory experiences in the various media. Advanced wood working processes, mold making, foundry techniques and welding processes.

title: Art of Ancient Mesoamerica: Olmec to Aztec
description: An introduction to the art of Mesoamerica (central and southern Mexico, Guatemala, Belize, and parts of Honduras and El Salvador) from the early formative/preclassic period through the postclassic period. Principal cultures include the Olmec, the formative-period peoples of the basin of Mexico and Michoacán, the shaft-tomb cultures of West Mexico, Teotihuacán, the Maya, the Zapotec, the Mixtec, and the Aztec.

title: Art of Japan
description: A survey of the traditional arts of Japan – ceramics, metalworking, sculpture, painting, printmaking, garden design, textiles – from the prehistoric period to 1868, the beginning of the Meiji period and the modernization of Japan. Topics include art in the contexts of Shinto and Buddhism, samurai weaponry, the tea ceremony, and the ukiyo-e printmaking tradition.

title: Painting Topics
description: Individualized studio work in varied painting media directed toward identifying and developing a personal vision and mode of expression. Working primarily from the basis of subject, concept, and degree of representation as catalyst for form and material choice, a range of approaches will be explored within both contemporary and historical contexts. Emphasis for graduating seniors will be on producing a cohesive body of work in preparation for the BFA exhibition.

title: Advanced Painting Topics II
description: Continuation of ART 650. Individualized studio work in varied painting media for personal expression. Approaches explored within both contemporary and historical contexts. Seniors will contribute to their cohesive body of work in preparation of BFA exhibition.

title: Painting Methods
