description: Individualized studio work in varied painting media directed toward identifying and developing a personal vision and mode of expression. Working from the primary basis of methods, strategies, materials and process, painting's potential for interpretation of subject, representation, and content will be explored within both contemporary and historical contexts. Emphasis for graduating seniors will be on producing a cohesive body of work in preparation for the BFA exhibition.

title: Women in Art
description: The work of women artists from early Middle Ages to the twentieth century, with emphasis on the contemporary period.

title: Advanced Studio in Metals and Jewelry
description: Explore concepts and processes in contemporary metal art and jewelry field. Develop individual research projects through assignments, individual meetings, presentations, and group critiques. Students in capstone section will focus on creating a unique body of work for BFA show and portfolio.

title: Art Museum/Gallery Internship
description: Practical professional art museum or gallery experience in appropriate institutions of at least 120 hours full-time or 150 hours part-time. May include processing of collections, conservation, cataloging, archive and library maintenance, education and outreach, and/or planning and preparation of exhibits.

title: Advanced Sculpture
description: Conduct independent, individually-driven creative work for research, planning, and execution of a capstone project in sculptural practice for BFA Studio Art exhibition.

title: Advanced Painting Methods II
description: Individualized studio work in varied painting media directed toward identifying and developing a personal vision and mode of expression. Working from the primary basis of methods, strategies, materials and process, painting's potential for interpretation of subject, representation, an content will be explored within both contemporary and historical contexts. Emphasis for seniors will be on producing a cohesive body of work in preparation of BFA exhibition.

title: Southwestern Indian Arts and Culture
description: The development of southwestern Indian silversmithing, weaving, pottery, basketry, and painting from the prehistoric period through the twentieth century.

title: Art and Life of Michelangelo
description: In-depth analysis of the life and art of the Renaissance painter, sculptor, and architect, Michelangelo Buonarroti. Open to all students, this course provides the opportunity to learn more about Michelangelo and the art and history of Italy during the Renaissance.

title: Contemporary Ceramics Workshop
description: Contemporary ceramic practices are explored through concept-based projects using mixed media and ceramic techniques.

title: Ceramic Design Workshop
description: Explores both handmade and digital approaches to prototypes and production for functional ceramics.

title: History of Ceramics
description: History and development of ceramics; study of the use of pottery and other forms of ceramics from earliest known examples to present day.

title: Color Experiments, Theory and Application
description: Explores the interaction of color through a variety of exercises, both practical and experimental. Emphasis on visual perception and the relative use of color. Color's potential in illusionist structures and visual communication, color systems.

title: Topics in Contemporary Metals and Jewelry
description: Explores concepts and processes in contemporary metal art and jewelry.

title: Metals and Jewelry Workshop
description: Application of concepts and processes in contemporary metal art and jewelry through individual creative research and making.

title: Advanced Studies in Ceramic Studio
description: Explores ceramic material processes and characteristics through individual creative research projects. The capstone section is taken in conjunction with the BFA exhibition.

title: Ceramics Capstone
description: Creation of a cohesive body of work in ceramics around a selected theme. The capstone section is taken in conjunction with the BFA exhibition.

title: Techniques in Teaching Art
description: Lectures and class discussion of methods, consideration of suitable laboratory equipment, use of illustrative material, and preparation of courses of study.

title: Topics in Art History
description: Exploration of selected topics in art history.

title: Graduate Graphic Design/Visual Communications Studies
description: Advanced creative work with emphasis on technical and visual research.

title: Graduate Digital Media
description: Advanced creative work with emphasis on technical and visual research.

title: Professional Practices
description: Selected topics dealing with career preparation skills such as developing a professional and teaching portfolio, computer techniques for promoting one's art, establishing relationships with galleries/museums, and agencies, basic techniques of exhibition design and installation, techniques of critiquing works of art, etc.

title: Graduate Seminar
description: Emphasis upon the exchange of ideas and exploration of critical theory. Direct involvement in critiques, readings, discussions, and field trips. Each semester a different theme will guide a pertinent topic from within the field.

title: Graduate Sculpture Studies
description: Advanced creative work with emphasis on technical and visual research.

title: Graduate Drawing Studies
description: Advanced creative work with emphasis on technical and visual research.

title: Graduate Painting Studies
description: Advanced creative work with emphasis on technical and visual research.

title: Graduate Printmaking Studies
description: Advanced creative work with emphasis on technical and visual research.

title: Graduate Ceramics Studies
description: Advanced creative work with emphasis on technical and visual research.

title: Graduate Metalsmithing and Jewelry Studies
description: Advanced creative work with emphasis on technical and visual research.

title: Graduate Independent Study
description: Advanced individual work offered in studio areas of ceramics, graphic design, digital media, drawing, painting, printmaking, sculpture, and metalsmithing and jewelry.

title: Contemporary Art Theory and Criticism
description: Discussion of the principal theories influencing production and criticism of contemporary art.

title: Graduate Studies in Photography
description: Focus on the conceptual nature of the photographic medium. Students will be encouraged to produce work useful for their graduate thesis. Course readings will be on photo theory and image relationships.

title: Research in Art
description: Research which may form the basis for the master's of fine art thesis or report.

title: Concepts And Practices In Animal Science
Foundations in Animal Sciences & Industry
description: Introduction to the animal sciences & industry programs, activities, resources, faculty and career opportunities.

title: Principles of Animal Science
description: Basic principles which apply to animal agriculture; survey of the industry; types, purposes, and products of livestock; principles of breeding, selection, nutrition, lactation, reproduction, management, and marketing.

title: Livestock Lab
description: An introductory laboratory to expose students to breeding and market types of the primary production livestock species (beef cattle, swine, sheep, and meat goats), including a comparison of the live animal and animal products (meat and wool).

title: Companion Animal Lab
description: Introduction to the companion animal industry.

title: Anatomy and Physiology of Domestic Animals
description: An overview of anatomy and physiology in domestic animals. Emphasis will be placed on comparative anatomy across species.

title: Introduction to Research in Animal Science
History And Attitudes Of Animal Use
Domestic Animal Nutrition
description: An overview of major nutrients required by domestic animals and their inclusion in common ingredients. Basic feedstuff identification and introductory diet formulation is included.

title: Fundamentals of Nutrition
description: Elementary principles of comparative nutrition of farm animals.

title: Animal Sciences and Industry Problems
description: Independent guided studies in any of the many fields associated with animals and animal products.

title: Special Topics in Animal Science
description: Topics of special interest. Will be offered upon sufficient demand in selected areas relating to animal science.

title: Computer Applications for Animal Sciences and Industry
description: Applications of computer techniques and software to the solutions of problems in animal science and related food industries. Includes use of existing software packages for breakeven analysis, animal identification and health records, farm/ranch accounting, and electronic communication.

title: History And Attitudes Of Animal Use
Applied Microbiology For Meat And Poultry Processors
Poultry and Poultry Product Evaluation
description: Applied knowledge of physical and anatomical characteristics for evaluating poultry for egg and meat production. Evaluation of ready-to-cook poultry products as well as eggs on their exterior, interior, and broken-out appearance according to the latest USDA standards.

title: Livestock and Meat Evaluation
description: Evaluation of slaughter livestock and their carcasses as related to economically efficient production of red meat. Evaluation of breeding livestock on visual appraisal and performance records. A study of growth and the effects of nutrition, environment, and hormones on growth patterns. Breeds of livestock and performance programs will be studied.

title: Beginning Horse Evaluation
description: Evaluation of conformation and athletic performance of horses. The use of records in selection and the influence of heredity, environment, and training on conformation and performance is addressed.

title: Advanced Horse Evaluation
description: In-depth study of horse conformation and performance horse evaluation. Theories from various equine disciplines are covered with emphasis placed on interpreting horse industry standards, and abilities to communicate those ideals effectively.

title: The Horse As A Window To The World
Equine Enterprise Management
description: Emphasis will be placed on management issues affecting the successful development and operation of an equine facility. Topics covered include health care, diet formulation, equipment needs, facility design and management, and equine business considerations.

title: Beginning Horse Training and Management
description: Inherited and learned behavior of the horse. Development of methods to communicate with the horse. Emphasis on handling and safety techniques. Opportunities to observe and practice advanced training techniques used in saddling and riding.

title: Meat Science
description: An introduction to the red meat industry relating the fundamental properties of muscle structure, chemistry, and physiology to meat quality, composition, processing, nutritional value, and marketing. The laboratory will demonstrate the conversion of animals to meat and by-products, and meat processing technology.

title: Meat Animal Processing
description: A student participation course in processing live animals into meat and by-products. Interrelates all phases of modern slaughter and processing techniques, inspection and processing, and related operations.

title: Principles of Meat Evaluation
description: The use of subjective and objective standards to evaluate beef, lamb, and pork carcasses and wholesale cuts for both quality and yield of edible portion as they relate to value and consumer acceptance.

title: Wool Grading and Evaluation
description: A study of factors determining the commercial grades of wool and the desired fleece qualities of sheep, practice in judging and grading wool.

title: Principles of Livestock Selection
description: Origin, development, characteristics, and adaptation of different breeds of livestock, with special emphasis on the selection of market and breeding animals.

title: Intermediate Horse Training and Management
description: Application of training techniques used to start young horses under saddle. Emphasis is placed on developing groundwork skills needed to prepare horses for riding, including longeing, introduction to the saddle and bridle, and driving.

title: Dairy Cattle Judging
description: An introduction to the principles of evaluating dairy cattle on the basis of their physical characteristics. Interpretation of the official dairy cow unified score card. Training includes preparation and presentation of oral defense on one's placing of four cow classes.

title: Farm Animal Reproduction
description: Basic reproductive anatomy and physiology of cattle, horses, pigs, poultry, and sheep during the first half of the semester provides a solid basis for reproduction management topics which occupy the second half of the course.

title: Calving
description: Emphasis will be placed on various aspects of managing parturition in cattle. Focus on stages of parturition, dystocia, and calf care.

title: Foaling
description: Events associated with late gestation, parturition, and the early postpartum period in the horse. Emphasis will be placed on the important aspects of caring for and monitoring a pregnant/lactating mare and her newborn foal.

title: Lambing
description: Theoretical and practical experience in lambing. Management of the ewe and lambs at parturition, physiology of parturition, and common problems encountered by ewes and lambs during parturition.

title: Fundamentals of Milk Processing
description: A study of fundamentals of processing, quality assurance, inspection, and marketing of fluid milk and related products in a modern market milk enterprise.

title: Livestock Sales Management
description: Hands-on experience in the planning, promotion, and production of a purebred livestock sale.

title: Advanced Dairy Products Evaluation
description: Advanced training in the sensory analysis of dairy products, focusing on techniques for detecting and evaluating flavor, body and texture, and appearance. The course covers the scoring system used for dairy product judging and includes participation in collegiate dairy products evaluation contests.

title: Equine Health
description: Fundamentals of equine health care and practical experience with the procedures routinely performed by horse owners in the overall management of their horse's well-being. Topics covered include basic anatomy and physiology, recognizing and managing disease conditions and emergency situations, and preventative management strategies and treatment options for a variety of health-related issues.

title: Advanced Horse Training and Management
description: Students will further develop skills acquired in ASI 345. Students will learn how to utilize advanced training practices and applications to enhance the training and performance of horses.

title: Pre-Travel Seminar
description: Prepares students for short-term international study through academic, cultural, and logistical orientation. Content includes destination-specific history and culture, discipline-relevant topics, learning objectives, and reflective communication connected to the international experience.

title: Animal Sciences Global Learning Experience
description: An immersive, faculty-guided international course in which students apply disciplinary concepts through site visits, field activities, and cultural engagement. Emphasizes experiential learning and structured reflection within the context of the host country.

title: Form and Function in Livestock
description: A detailed study of animal form and type; influence of type related to function; special training in presenting orally the relative merits of beef cattle, sheep, swine, and horses.

title: Advanced Meat Evaluation
description: Advanced study in the evaluation of carcasses, wholesale cuts, and retail cuts of beef, lamb, and pork. Application of grade standards and specifications to beef, lamb, and pork carcasses and subprimal cuts.

title: Genetics
description: This course builds a foundation in essential genetic principles, starting with Mendelian inheritance and extending to molecular and population genetics.

title: Principles of Animal Breeding
description: Introduction to the basic principles of quantitative genetics and their applications to selection and mating in animal populations.

title: Equine Reproduction Management
description: Overview of the physiological mechanisms involved in equine reproduction and the scientific reasoning behind breeding management decisions. Recent advances in equine reproductive technology will be highlighted and relevant research will be incorporated into the presentations.

title: Equine Reproduction Management Lab
description: This course is the laboratory complement to ASI 504.  It includes demonstrations and hands-on exercises focused on learning equine breeding techniques. Topics will include mare and stallion handling, estrus detection, semen collection and processing, and artificial insemination.

title: Animal Breeding Principles
description: The genetic principles of evaluation, selection and mating systems used in beef, dairy, sheep, swine, poultry, horse and companion animal breeding. Intended for ASI majors and minors.

title: Applied Animal Breeding and Genetics
description: A combined animal breeding and genetics course featuring quantitative genetics and how the principles of genetic evaluation, natural and artificial selection, genomics, and mating systems are utilized for genetic improvement in livestock, horse, and companion animal species.  This course also includes an introduction to Mendelian, molecular, and population genetics and their applications in livestock breeding.

title: Bovine Reproductive Technologies
description: Reproductive technologies used in management of cattle including the physiology of the estrous cycle, embryo viability, and fetal development. Practical training in reproductive management technique.

title: Beef Science
description: A comprehensive course covering all phases of the beef cattle industry. Practical application of nutrition, breeding, physiology of reproduction, merchandising, risk management and related areas. Special emphasis on management systems of raising, growing and finishing beef cattle.

title: Companion and Laboratory Animal Management
description: General biology and management of pets in zoos, kennels and at home. Working safely with companion animals. Management of animals in kennels, shelters, research facilities and zoos. Regulatory issues of small animals used as companion pets and in laboratory research. Interactive discussion and written skills will be emphasized.

title: Horse Science
description: A study of the horse industry in the United States, breed profiles, anatomy and evaluation, nutrition, reproduction, growth and development, health, exercise physiology, facilities and equipment, business considerations.

title: Sheep and Meat Goat Science
description: Application of scientific management principles to the sheep and meat goat industries. Breeding, reproduction, nutrition, health, management and economic aspects as related to sheep and meat goat production.

title: Physiology of Domestic Animals
description: Advanced application of physiology within domesticated animals.

title: Introduction to Pharmacology of Farm Animals
description: The study of the basic principles of pharmacology as related to the proper and safe use of drugs and chemicals by the livestock industry.

title: Swine Science
description: Application of basic scientific principles to the economical production of pork. Recommendations are made in breeding, reproduction, nutrition, health, housing, marketing, and management of swine production units of varying sizes.

title: Principles of Animal Disease Control
description: A study of the factors that influence animal health and disease control for students majoring in agriculture and other fields.

title: Course-Based Undergraduate Research in Animal Sciences & Industry
description: Formal undergraduate research in the area of Animal Sciences & Industry conducted as part of a conventional course. Class consists of 1-hour lecture and two hours of laboratory per week

title: Undergraduate Research in Animal Sciences & Industry
description: Mentored undergraduate research experience in animal or food sciences.

title: Current Issues in Animal Science
description: This course reviews current issues in the animal science industry, with emphasis on understanding current scientific knowledge and challenges. It is intended to serve as a capstone course for students majoring in animal sciences and industry and should be taken in their last year.

title: Bioscience Internship in Animal Science
description: Supervised professional experience in bioscience realm of animal sciences. One credit hour per one month full-time supervised and evaluated work hours. Internship proposal, evaluation by supervisor, self-assessment, and presentation of internship required.

title: Animal Science Internship
description: Supervised professional experience in animal science related field. One credit hour per month full-time supervised and evaluated work hours. Internship proposal, evaluation by supervisor, self-assessment and presentation of internship required.

title: Applied Animal Biotechnology
description: The objective of this course is to provide insight into the use of large animals in areas of biotechnology.  The course will emphasize the use of livestock in food production as well as human medicine applications.  A two-hour lecture per week will include topics such as marker assisted selection of livestock, in-vitro fertilization, transgenics, cloning and stem cell technology. A two-hour lab per week will complement material from lectures including PCR, DNA isolation and oocyte collection.

title: Endocrinology and Lactation
description: Provides an overview of the endocrine systems that regulate mammalian lactation and other related physiological processes. Topics include structure and function of important endocrine glands and their hormones, receptors, target tissues and physiological mechanisms of action. Emphasis will be placed on whole animal hormonal interactions that regulate nutrient availability for production of milk in dairy cattle and other dairy species. Upon completion of the course students should be able to identify important endocrine organs and hormones and apply their understanding to problems in dairy production.

title: Equine Breeding and Genetics
description: Application of genetic and animal breeding principles to the horse.  Emphasis on inheritance of color and diseases, as well as selection and mating programs.

title: Instrumental Analysis Of Food & Agricultural Products
Processed Meat Operations
description: A course in processed meats, relating to the science, technology, and quality control of curing, smoking, and sausage manufacturing.

title: Beef Systems Management
description: Development of beef enterprise business plans and interacting with beef producers on topics related to cow-calf production, feedlot operations, seedstock operations, animal health, environmental management, and business management.

title: Dairy Cattle Management
description: Integration of biologic and economic aspects of dairy production with dairy farm organization, planning, operation and analysis. Field trips, dairy farm analysis and case studies will be used to supplement lecture material.

title: Dairy Cattle Management Laboratory
description: This course is the laboratory complement to ASI 621 Dairy Cattle Management. It includes field trips, dairy farm analysis and case studies.

title: Poultry Products Technology
description: Emphasis on the technical problems that exist between production and consumption during the processing and marketing of poultry meat and egg products. A study of the microbiology of shell eggs, meat and manufactured products and the basic principles of quality assurance. Food manufacturing and product development are discussed.

title: Poultry Management
description: A detailed study of the production and management practices involved in commercial poultry and game bird enterprises.

title: Identification and Data Management of Food Animals
description: Procedures and the conventions required to accomplish individual identification for farm animals. Principles of sound data collection and management. Principles of automatic information and data capture technologies for transforming data into information. Guest lectures from allied industry and livestock producers will illustrate various products, services and applications.

title: Behavior Of Domestic Animals
description: Students gain knowledge about the behavioral sciences' fields, historical perspectives, and scientific methodologies. Behavior is evaluated from a biological perspective (i.e. physiology, development, adaptation, and genetics). Students refine observational skills and identify methods for quantifying behavior and interpreting results to improve animal health, management, housing, training, and welfare.

title: Animal Growth and Development
description: The molecular and endocrine mechanisms of prenatal and postnatal growth and development of muscle, bone, and adipose tissue will be discussed. Historical and current scientific literature will be reviewed and utilized to reinforce the topics covered.

title: International Study Experience in Animal Science
description: Seminar and travel course designed to prepare students before the experience and for students to analyze, critique, and report their experiences of an international experience associated with study tours or short courses. Course will focus on appropriate oral and written documentation of the student learning objectives for course experience. The academic topics will vary with each international activity.

title: Animal Sciences and Industry Problems
description: Independent guided studies in any of the many fields associated with animals and animal products.

title: Special Topics in Animal Science
description: Seminars of special interest. Will be offered upon sufficient demand in selected areas relating to animal science.

title: Meat Selection and Utilization
description: Emphasis on meat cut identification, muscle and bone anatomy, grades, fabricated meat, institutional cuts, specification writing, processing, meat preparation and shrinkage costs.

title: Monogastric Nutrition
description: This advanced class provides a comparative summary of nutrition for domesticated monogastric animals, including digestive anatomy and the digestion, absoprtion, and metabolism of dietary energy (starch, fiber, lipids), proteins, vitamins, and minerals.

title: Avian Nutrition
description: Nutritional requirements of game birds, caged birds, exotics, and commercial poultry. Interactive discussion will be emphasized.

title: Companion Animal Nutrition
description: This course is intended to be taken in sequence following Monogastric Nutrition ASI 675. The course will cover the unique nature of nutrition for companion animals with an emphasis on the nutrition of dogs and cats. Details regarding dentition, digestion, metabolism and nutritional requirements will be covered. In addition, an overview of the nutrition of other minor companion species will be provided. Besides standard assessments methods students will be expected to review current research publications on the topic and provide written and oral presentations germane to the topic.

title: Equine Nutrition
description: Equine digestive anatomy and physiology. Nutrient requirements of the equine as they relate to growth, work, reproduction and lactation, as well as the relationship of nutrition to disease and environment. Practical management considerations and current equine nutrition research will be reviewed.

title: Swine Nutrition
description: A study of the nutrient requirements of swine for various stages of production. Discussion of the interrelationships among nutrition and other factors (environment, management, and health) that affect performance.

title: Ruminant Nutrition
description: Advanced study of nutritional management of different species of ruminant livestock. Topics covered include ruminal function, post-ruminal digestion and absorption, utilization of key nutrients, and discussion of select metabolic disorders.

title: Dairy Cattle Nutrition
description: Nutritional management of dairy calves, replacement heifers, dry and lactating dairy cows. Diet formulation, feeding systems and current concepts in dairy cattle nutrition.

title: Formulation of Livestock and Poultry Diets
description: Diet formulation for the major species of livestock and poultry. Major topics include hand formulation of diets: ingredient/nutrient cost comparisons; dry matter manipulation; computerized diet formulation; developing specifications for diets, supplements, basemixes and premixes; projecting animal performance; and feed labeling.

title: Grazing Livestock Nutrition
description: Nutritional management of grazing beef cattle. Nutrition of beef cows and stocker cattle maintained under grazing conditions. Major topics to be covered include nutrient requirements, forage intake, forage quality, and supplementation.

title: Nutrition of Feedlot Cattle
description: Nutritional management of growing and finishing beef cattle maintained under confined feeding conditions. Utilization of cereal grains and byproducts in the production of beef. Major topics include nutrient requirements, feed processing, growing-finishing systems, feed additives, metabolic disturbances, and nutrient management.

title: Stored Forage Systems for Ruminant Animals
description: A study of nutrient conservation in various stored forage systems including storage facilities and equipment, nutrient loss, aerobic deterioration, chemical and physical analysis. Discussions will also include the impact of stored forage systems on forage quality and the subsequent effects on digestion and utilization in ruminant animals.

title: Principles of Equine Exercise Physiology
description: Physiological systems reactions and adaption to exercise and training in the equine athlete.  Additional topics addressed will include biomechanics, injury prevention and rehabilitation.

title: Physiology of Reproduction in Farm Animals
description: The physiological aspects of reproduction in farm animals including endocrine inter-relationships controlling reproductive cycles and gamete production. Periodic demonstrations deal with specialized reproductive anatomy of farm animals, experimental techniques used in animal reproduction, and contemporary animal production practices.

title: Rapid Methods And Automation In Microbiology
Advanced Dairy Products Evaluation
description: Advanced training in the sensory analysis of dairy products, focusing on techniques for detecting and evaluating flavor, body and texture, and appearance. The course covers the scoring system used for dairy product judging and may include participation in collegiate dairy products evaluation contests.

title: Quantitative Genetics Applications of Matrix Algebra
description: The goal of this course is development of skills in matrix algebra to describe and solve problems in the agricultural and life sciences, with particular focus on quantitative genetics. The course is designed for students with no prior knowledge of matrix algebra, and whose formal training in mathematics may be somewhat limited. It will consider the vocabulary, concepts, application and, to a lesser extent, theory of matrix algebra that is relevant to graduate students in the agricultural and life sciences. Application exercises will consider ecological systems, genotypic transition matrices, selection indices, and the numerator relationship matrix. Application of methods such as least squares, and canonical transformation to solve problems in the biological sciences using matrix algebra will also be introduced.

title: Cybersheep: A Genetic Simulation Game
description: The goal of this course is for students to be able to make informed and effective decisions in a livestock breeding program. In order to accomplish this goal, the course will provide hands-on experience with selection and mating decisions, and their consequences through a genetic simulation. Genetic gains require time to achieve and there is very little opportunity for students to witness the consequences of breeding decisions in a course. The simulation is designed to offer an opportunity to see in real-time, the outcome of decision-making, and to experience the stochastic (chance) elements of a breeding program.

title: Advanced Animal Breeding
description: Application of genetic principles to livestock improvement, selection methods, mating systems, heritability estimates and methods of analyzing genetic data.

title: Meat Industry Technology
description: Apply concepts and information about meat composition, product safety and spoilage, quality; formulation, processing and evaluation of cured, pre-cooked, and sausage packaging, troubleshooting, and plant organization.

title: Meat Science and Muscle Biology
description: An in depth-overview of the conversion of muscle to meat as well as the impact of inherent characteristics of muscle on fresh meat quality. The fundamental concepts in the course will be discussed with an application to industry standards.

title: Graduate Internship in Animal Sciences and Industry
description: In-depth work-study experiences on beef cattle, sheep, dairy cattle, swine, horse or poultry production operations or in animal food products plants.

title: Gametes, Embryos, and Stem Cells in Farm Animals
description: A study of gametes, embryos, pregnancy, and stem cells in farm species including supporting information from laboratory species and humans. Emphasis will be on the regulation of stem cells, gametes, and embryos and on the conceptus-maternal interactions to establish and maintain pregnancy and program conceptus and postnatal development.

title: Topics In Meat Science And Muscle Biology
History and Perspectives in Animal Breeding and Genetics
description: The goal of this course is to provide students with a historical perspective of the discipline of Animal Breeding and Genetics and an appreciation for the contributions of several scientists that have significantly impacted the discipline. Weekly lectures will consist of pre-recorded interviews with scientists that have had an international impact in the field of animal breeding and genetics.

title: Primer to Quantitative Genetics
description: The goal of this course is to provide students with an introduction to the language and basic principles of quantitative genetics. Its purpose is to develop foundational knowledge for students entering a graduate program in animal breeding and genetics. Topics included will be the basic model for quantitative genetics (additive and non-additive genetic effects, including Mendelian sampling, and environmental effects), sources of variation, heritability, family resemblance and repeatability, selection response, and family selection. Expected values and concepts in applied statistics (e.g., linear regression) will also be considered.

title: Selection Index Theory and Application
description: The overall goal of this course is to increase your skills and knowledge related to the design of animal breeding programs. The focus will be on the application of index theory to the definition of breeding objectives in animal agriculture. The course will also introduce approaches for deriving economic weights, which are useful when predicting economic response to selection.

title: Economic Breeding Programs
description: The primary goal of this course is to provide a survey of methodology for developing an economic basis for multiple trait selection to improve the profitability of production.  Much of the material is abstracted from the scientific literature, from which a sampling of relevant references will be provided.  Therefore, an overarching goal of this course is to instill the capacity to critically analyze relevant literature as an aid to solving future problems.  Specific topics included are: a review of concepts relevant to selection index, an introduction to the concept of systems analysis, linear programming, and simulation with emphasis on economic values useful for selection index.

title: Heterosis and Crossbreeding Systems
description: The goal of this course is to provide students with an introduction to the principles of heterosis and mating systems utilizing crossbreeding. Students completing this course should be able to evaluate and compare various crossbreeding mating schemes through predicted performance of the potential progeny and overall system performance.

title: Application of Quantitative Genetics in R Software
description: Introduction and application of R software for basic and intermediate tasks in quantitative genetic analyses. The use of R as a high-level programming language and a gateway for more formal low-level languages.  No prior exposure to the language is necessary.

title: Fundamentals Of Meat Processing And Preparation
Rumen Metabolism
description: Metabolism, absorption, digestion, and passage of nutrients in the rumen; factors affecting the environment of the rumen; certain aspects of rumen function and dysfunction; techniques used in rumen research.

title: Stress Physiology of Livestock
description: Integrative physiology of livestock during environmental, management, and pathological stresses.

title: Nutritional Physiology
description: The course focuses on the structures and function of the gastrointestinal tract, with an emphasis on digestive physiology in the small intestine. Details of gastrointestinal tract secretion, regulation, digestion, and absorption of the major nutrient groups are emphasized with species comparisons.

title: Neuroendocrine Physiology
description: Neuroendocrine regulation of reproduction, growth, and metabolism, with an emphasis on neuroendocrinology of domestic livestock.

title: Molecular Reproductive Endocrinology
description: A study of molecular mechanisms that control gene expression in mammals, with an emphasis on genes and their products that control reproduction.

title: Ovarian Physiology
description: Endocrine and physiologic control of ovarian functions in domestic livestock.

title: Advanced Swine Nutrition
description: Advanced study of the feeding systems and practices and nutrient requirements for swine for various stages of production. An in-depth analysis of applied swine nutrition.

title: Techniques in Domestic Animal Behavior
description: A combined seminar and laboratory type course. Current and classical studies reported and discussed, relationships between behavior and other disciplines explored and methods of data collection examined. Small-scale demonstration experiments planned, executed and reported orally and/or in scientific written style.

title: Analytical Techniques In Animal Sciences And Industry
Methods In Radioisotope Use And Radioimmunoassys
Analytical Techniques--Sample Preparation and Beginning Analyses
description: Sample collection, processing and handling methodologies will be addressed as they pertain to research methods in the animal sciences. Basic laboratory techniques, sample collection, and analyses of moisture and nitrogen will be covered.

title: Analytical Techniques--Mineral Analyses
description: This course focuses on the analysis of minerals in common feedstuffs. This course will cover sample preparation and atomic absorption, emission, utraviolet/visible and fluorimetric spectrophotometric methods of analysis of feedstuffs and biological fluids.

title: Analytical Techniques--Carbohydrate and Lipid Analyses
description: This course covers the analysis of carbohydrate and lipid components of feedstuffs and biological materials using conventional as well as HPLC and gas chromatographic methods.

title: Analytical Techniques--Radioisotope Use
description: Study of radioisotope use in physiological applications of research in domestic animals including radioactive decay, detection methodology, and isotope dilution.

title: Analytical Techniques-Immunoassays
description: Study of measurement of biological substances and hormones utilizing enzyme-linked immunoassays (ELISA) and radioimmunoassays (RIA).

title: Analytical Techniques: Protein Analysis
description: The course will provide an overview of techniques commonly used for protein analysis in research using animal tissues. Topics will include protein extraction from tissues, determining protein concentration, types of gel electrophoresis for different protein sizes, Western blotting and proteomics.

title: Linear Models in Animal Breeding
description: Students completing this course will learn about linear models used in Animal Breeding. These models will be discussed in the context of the random variable that is to be predicted. Specifically, the course will cover animal models, sire/maternal grandsire models, and sire models. Models including a single record, repeated records, and models with both direct and maternal effects will be discussed.

title: Genetic Prediction
description: The goal of this course is to increase student understanding of best linear unbiased prediction and to develop skills in genetic prediction. A wide array of material will be covered with emphasis on real-world datasets designed to develop applied analytical skills relative in animal breeding. Topics will include data integrity diagnosis, contemporary grouping strategies, adjusting for known non-genetic effects, and use of software to perform genetic evaluations. Students will develop procedures for the utilization of various sources of information for the calculations of predictions of genetic merit in the form of estimated breeding values.

title: Applied Variance Component Estimation in Livestock Genetics
description: The goal of this course is to extend upon content covered in linear models and genetic prediction, with specific emphasis on estimation of (co)variance components and genetic parameters required to solve mixed models typical in livestock genetics. Upon successful completion of this module, students should have an applied knowledge of approaches used to estimate the G and R sub-matrices of the mixed model equations. Several tools will be used to demonstrate the models and approaches most commonly used in parameter estimation. Where appropriate, scientific literature that explains their implementation, and some attributes of the solutions obtained will be used.

title: Prediction and Control of Inbreeding in Breeding Programs
description: The goal of this course is for students to be introduced to, and to gain an understanding of, the concepts of inbreeding and genetic diversity, the impact of inbreeding on animal breeding and production populations, and of strategies to control and manage rates of inbreeding in animal breeding and production populations. The use of genomic data to assess and control inbreeding also will be discussed.

title: Marker Assisted and Gene-Assisted Selection
description: The goal of this module is to increase student understanding of potential methods for incorporating marker genetic information into selection decisions. Topics will include but are not limited to recombination, single-gene tests, molecular breeding values, suggested producer guidelines for use of the technologies, and incorporation of genomic information into genetic prediction procedures.

title: Introduction to Marker Association Analysis and QTL Detection
description: The goal of this course is to introduce the basic concepts of using genetic markers to identify QTL and of estimating marker-trait associations, and to expose students to applications of these methodologies. Materials will cover the basics of linkage and linkage disequilibrium, alternate designs or population structures for QTL mapping, and statistical methods for QTL detection, including QTL interval mapping and genome-wide association analyses. Properties, advantages, disadvantages, and requirements of alternate designs and analysis strategies will be discussed.

title: From Markers to Gene Function: Functional Change
description: This course takes the results from association analyses and learns how these markers translate into functional changes in the animal genome and in animal performance. Topics covered in the course include an introduction to the tools used to generate genomic data followed by introduction and application of key bioinformatics websites, databases to identify causative genetic variation, and develop gene pathways and networks.

title: MCMC Methods in Animal Breeding: A Primer
description: The goal of this course is to introduce computational techniques based on simulation that have become a staple in the field of animal breeding (and beyond) over the last 20 years. An overview of the most popular Monte Carlo methods will be provided to the students with an emphasis on hands on reproducible examples developed through the R software. Minimal exposure to the R programming language will be required while no previous exposure to Monte Carlo methods is required. While a few examples in the class will be set in a Bayesian framework, no previous exposure to Bayesian statistics is required

title: Applications in Swine Nutrition
description: Application of key principles of swine nutrition relating to nutrients, requirements and feeding programs for pigs of various stages of production.

title: Graduate Seminar in Animal Sciences and Industry
description: Discussion of research and technical problems in the discipline. Attendance required of all departmental graduate students. Maximum of two hours may be applied toward an advanced degree.

title: Masters Report
description: A written report of either research or problem work on a topic in the major field.

title: Master's Research In Animal Sciences and Industry
description: Research leading to the completion of a master's thesis.

title: Topics in Animal Science
description: Discussion and lectures on important areas and contributions in the field of animal science not covered in present courses.

title: Energy Utilization in Domestic Livestock
description: Comprehensive discussion of the development and application of energy systems used to guide livestock feeding, procedures used in energy experimentation, dietary/digestive/environmental factors that influence efficiency of energy utilization, and the efficiencies with which different energy substrates are used to support various maintenance and production functions. Emphasis will be placed upon ruminants.

title: Protein and Amino Acid Utilization in Domestic Livestock
description: Comprehensive discussion of protein and amino acids and their role in digestion, absorption, metabolism, protein synthesis, and degradation in livestock. Emphasis on techniques and interpretation of results from experiments designed to evaluate protein utilization and factors which influence amino acid metabolism in monogastrics and ruminants.

title: Vitamin and Mineral Nutrition of Domestic Livestock
description: A detailed examination of the vitamin and mineral nutrition of domestic livestock. Emphasis will be placed on current literature on the determination of vitamin and mineral requirements, practical considerations for vitamin and mineral supplementation in livestock feeding, and the potential for vitamin and mineral deficiency and toxicity in domestic livestock.

title: Rumen Microbiology
description: Three hours lecture a week dealing with the microorganisms of the rumen, their habitat, diversity, structure, interactions, and biochemical activities. Techniques for enumeration, isolation and identification of ruminal microorganisms.

title: Advanced Meat Science
description: Basic biochemical, physiological, and histological properties of muscle and related tissues; muscle contraction, rigor mortis, and muscle hydration; maturation; processing by thermal, dehydration, and cold sterilization techniques; meat flavor chemistry; meat research techniques.

title: Graduate Problem in Animal Sciences & Industry
description: In-depth study of a topic supervised by a member of the graduate faculty.

title: Seminar in Animal Sciences Research
description: Weekly evaluation of the scientific literature and the reasoning underlying the selection of research problems, the formulation and testing of hypotheses, and the evaluation and presentation of results.

title: Doctoral Research in Animal Sciences and Industry
description: Research leading to the completion of a Ph.D. Degree.

title: Intro to Food and Feed Manufacturing Employee Safety
description: This course introduces students to the fundamental principles of employee safety in food and feed manufacturing environments. Students will learn about workplace hazards, safety protocols, and regulatory requirements designed to protect workers from injury and illness. The course covers essential topics such as personal protective equipment (PPE), machine safety, chemical handling, and emergency procedures. By the end of the course, students will understand how to create and maintain a safe working environment in compliance with industry safety standards.

title: Emerging Technologies in Power, Structural, and Technical Systems
description: The continual evolution of the agricultural mechanics industry, necessitates the introduction of emerging practices and equipment to new teachers. The course includes demonstrations, hands-on activities, and field trips to be used in high school programs.

title: Agricultural Engine Systems
description: Theory, knowledge and skills necessary for the repair and maintenance of small gas engines. Special focus on the teaching of engine systems and management of laboratory instruction.

title: Agricultural Electrical Systems
description: Theory and practice of electrical wiring systems used in agricultural settings. Special focus on the teaching of electrical systems.

title: Agricultural Structures Education
description: Theory, knowledge and skills for construction/fabrication in agricultural structure projects. Emphasis on: laboratory safety, measurements, material selection, basic techniques, plumbing, and basic surveying.

title: Employee Safety in Grain Handling Facilities
description: This course focuses on employee safety in grain handling facilities, addressing the unique hazards associated with grain storage, transport, and processing. Students will learn about the risks of grain dust explosions, confined space entry, machine safety, and proper use of personal protective equipment (PPE). The course covers safety protocols, emergency procedures, and regulatory standards specific to grain handling operations. By the end of the course, students will be equipped to identify hazards and implement safety measures to ensure a safe working environment in grain facilities.

title: Safety Applications Practicum
description: This course offers a hands-on, applied learning experience focused on employee safety in grain handling and food and feed manufacturing facilities. Students will actively engage in safety assessments, hazard identification, and the implementation of safety protocols, including proper use of personal protective equipment (PPE), confined space entry procedures, and machine safeguarding. The course provides practical, real-world training in safety compliance, risk mitigation, and emergency response. By the end of the course, students will have gained valuable experience in applying safety practices to maintain safe working environments in high-risk industrial settings.

title: Agricultural Metal Processes Education
description: Provide students with the theory, knowledge and skills necessary for the construction/fabrication of metal projects. Emphasis will be placed on: laboratory safety, general laboratory measurements, metal identification/ characteristics, oxyacetylene welding and cutting, shielded metal arc welding (SMAW), gas metal arc welding (GMAW), gas tungsten arc welding (GTAW), plasma cutting, and project construction.

title: Agricultural and Occupational Safety Program Management
description: Addresses the development and administration of comprehensive safety, health, and environmental management programs in agriculture and industry.

title: Environmental Regulatory Compliance
description: Surveys requirements of air, land, and water regulations as they relate to agribusiness and industry. Focuses on practical applications in the work environment such as identification of pollution sources, evaluation strategies, engineering controls, permitting processes, and reporting.

title: Agricultural and Occupational Safety I
description: Examines traumatic injuries and their control in agricultural and industrial settings, with a focus on structural safety, walking and working surfaces, falls, electrical safety, and safety for livestock handlers.

title: Agricultural and Occupational Health I
description: Investigates chemical health hazards and their control in the context of agriculture and industry. Topics include hazardous gases, vapors, and aerosols; dermal hazards; pesticides; exposures to veterinary pharmaceuticals; methods of hazard assessment; and methods of control.

title: Agricultural and Occupational Safety II
description: Explores agricultural and industrial traumatic injuries involving machine hazards; materials handling and storage; confined spaces; fire protection and prevention; and pressurized vessels, fluids, and environments.

title: Agricultural and Occupational Health II
description: Analyzes occupational health hazards and controls in agricultural and industrial settings, with a focus on biological hazards, ergonomic stressors, and physical hazards (including noise, vibration, extreme temperatures, and radiation).

title: Topics in Agricultural and Occupational Safety and Health
description: Examination of current topics in agricultural and occupational safety and health.

title: Problems in Agricultural and Occupational Safety and Health
description: Independent guided studies in any of the many fields associated with agricultural and occupational safety and health.

title: Occupational and Agricultural Health
description: Addresses the identification, evaluation, and control of health hazards in occupational and agricultural settings. Topics include occupational toxicology, air contaminants, noise, radiation, thermal stress, ergonomics, biological hazards, emerging health risks, and health risks of special populations.

title: Occupational and Agricultural Injury Prevention
description: Explores traumatic injuries and their prevention in occupational and agricultural settings.  Topics include falls, workplace violence, animal handling, mobile equipment, fixed machines, work tools, electrical hazards, fires, and explosions.

title: Occupational and Agricultural Safety and Health Interventions
description: Examines the design and evaluation of behavioral interventions to prevent occupational and agricultural injuries and illnesses. Topics include theories of safety and health behavior; effective communication and persuasion strategies; planning, conducting and evaluating interventions; interventions for diverse populations; and intervention strategies in evolving occupational and societal contexts.

title: Agricultural and Occupational Safety Program Management
description: Addresses the development and administration of comprehensive safety, health, and environmental management programs in agriculture and industry.

title: Environmental Regulatory Compliance
description: Surveys requirements of air, land, and water regulations as they relate to agribusiness and industry. Focuses on practical applications in the work environment such as identification of pollution sources, evaluation strategies, engineering controls, permitting processes, and reporting.

title: Agricultural and Occupational Safety I
description: Examines traumatic injuries and their control in agricultural and industrial settings, with a focus on structural safety, walking and working surfaces, falls, electrical safety, and safety for livestock handlers.

title: Agricultural and Occupational Health I
description: Investigates chemical health hazards and their control in the context of agriculture and industry. Topics include hazardous gases, vapors, and aerosols; dermal hazards; pesticides; exposures to veterinary pharmaceuticals; methods of hazard assessment; and methods of control.

title: Agricultural and Occupational Safety II
description: Explores agricultural and industrial traumatic injuries involving machine hazards; materials handling and storage; confined spaces; fire protection and prevention; and pressurized vessels, fluids, and environments.

title: Agricultural and Occupational Health II
description: Analyzes occupational health hazards and controls in agricultural and industrial settings, with a focus on biological hazards, ergonomic stressors, and physical hazards (including noise, vibration, extreme temperatures, and radiation).

title: Introduction To Professions In The Apparel And Textile Ind
Principles Of Apparel Buying And Forecasting
Issues And Ethics In Apparel And Textile Marketing
Comfort And Performance Of Protective Clothing
Strategic Economic Analysis Of Apparel & Textile Industries
Chemical, Optical, And Spectroscopic Analysis Of Textiles
Professional Development Seminar In Clothing And Textiles
Introduction to Biological & Agricultural Engg & Tech
description: Introduction to discipline, department, university, and profession.  Exploration of career options.  Gain skills through application-oriented problem solving.

title: Engineered Systems and Technology in Agriculture
description: An introductory course designed for non-engineering majors. This course introduces engineering principles and problem solving associated with energy resources and power systems for agriculture, machinery systems, natural resources and environmental management, and food and fiber processing and storage.

title: Principles of Injury Prevention
description: This is an overview course that will cover a variety of safety and health topics focusing on injury prevention in the home, motor vehicle, public, and work environments. The enduring understanding that forms the basis for every discussion and activity in this course is all of us make decisions every day that impact our safety as well as the safety of those around us.

title: Introduction to Biomanufacturing Technology
description: History of biomanufacturing technology and its impact; overview of related industries including biopharmaceuticals, cellular agriculture, precision fermentation, and brewing; careers; fundamentals of biomanufacturing processes including cell types, gene technology, and unit operations.

title: Chemical Application Systems
description: Systems, components, operation practices, and safety procedures used in the chemical application industry.  Liquid and granular application systems and respective components will be studied along with procedures for equipment sizing and maintenance, minimizing drift, systems calibration, and safe handling, transporting, storage, disposal and spill clean-up of agrichemicals.

title: Chemical Application Systems Laboratory
description: Laboratory and hands-on activities on pumps, valves, nozzles, drift measurement, and application technology used in the home and garden, turf, private and commercial agricultural, aerial, and research industries.

title: Tillage, Planting, and Crop Harvesting Systems
Professional Practice for Agricultural Technology Managers
description: A preparatory course for ATM Capstone Project course to explore project ideas, build project teams, and chose a project to complete for the capstone project in the following semester. Topics include professionalism, project management, intellectual property, organizational behavior and ethics.

title: Occupational Safety
description: This is a survey course that identifies safety and health risks in industrial work environments. The enduring understanding that forms the basis for every discussion and activity in this course is as a manager or supervisor; you are responsible for the safety of all employees under you.

title: Cell Culture Technology in Biomanufacturing
description: Cell culture and fermentation technology in biomanufacturing including media optimization, cell types and growth, mass balance concepts, selection of bioreactor and operational modes for microbial fermentation and mammalian cell cultures.

title: Energy And Power In Biological Systems
Biorenewable Systems
description: Converting biorenewable resources into bioenergy and bio-based products. Biorenewable concepts as they relate to drivers of change, feedstock production, processes, products, co-products, economics, transportation/logistics, and marketing.

title: Introduction To Food Engineering Technology Lab
Separation Technologies for Biomanufacturing
description: Overview of separation technologies used in biomanufacturing facility including purification, principles of operations used for purification and polishing of active product ingredients such as proteins and peptides, common impurities and removal strategies, process integration, and risk management.

title: Regulatory Affairs for Biomanufacturing
description: Regulations and compliance for the biomanufacturing industry in the United States with a focus on biologics. Topics include regulatory framework, quality assurance procedures in a biomanufacturing facility (GLP and cGMP), preparation for regulatory inspection and facility audit.

title: Sensors and Controls for Agricultural and Biological System
description: Applications of instrumentation. Sensors used in agricultural machines and processes for measurement of voltage, force, torque, pressure, displacement, velocity, acceleration, flow, temperature, humidity, etc. Analog and digital signal conditioning and processing. Feedback controls concept. Computer interface.

title: Engines and Power Transfer
description: Principles, operation, and analysis of engine thermodynamics, fuel and combustion, engine components, engine cycles, engine power transfer and efficiencies, and exhaust gas emissions. Selection of bearings and design of belt, chain, gear, and shaft drives. Operation and performance analysis of hydraulic power components and systems, fluid mechanics, and hydraulic motors.

title: Internship in Agricultural Technology Management
description: Intern programs in agricultural technology management.

title: Undergraduate Research Experience
description: Undergraduate research experience for agricultural technology managment students.

title: Agricultural Building Systems
description: Environmental control systems for agricultural buildings; structural members and systems for agricultural buildings; farmstead planning concepts.

title: Emerging Topics in Agricultural Technology Management
description: Topics in the application of technical principles to agricultural technology management offered through collaborative distance education programs such as AG*IDEA.

title: Agricultural Technology Management Capstone Project
description: A team-oriented laboratory for Agricultural Technology Management students to complete an open-ended project related to the management of technology in agriculture.

title: Introduction to Food Engineering
description: Material and energy balances with application to food processing. Fluid flow and heat transfer in food processing. Thermodynamic properties and laws. Conc. enrollment in ATM 541 is urged.

title: Introduction to Food Engineering Laboratory Exercises
Processing and Storage of Grains
description: Principles of mass and energy balances, materials handling, along with the drying, storage, and bioprocessing of grains and biomass.

title: Precision Agriculture Technologies
description: Application of precision technologies for gathering information to assist decision making for production systems. Topics will include global positioning systems, vehicle guidance systems, crop yield monitors, variable rate control systems for applicators, and the communication among these devices.

title: Hydrology and Soil Erosion Management
description: Covers the water cycle and watershed hydrology, with an emphasis on precipitation, evapotranspiration, runoff, soil water movement, and aquifers; Soil erosion and water quality concerns; Engineered conservation practices to manage runoff, erosion, and water shortage issues; Site assessment techniques including surveying, mapping, watershed delineation, and data collection and analysis.

title: Mechanisms For Power Transfer And Material Handling
Problems in Agricultural Technology Management
description: Current and advanced problems in agricultural technology management.

title: Topics in Agricultural Technology Management
description: Topics for the understanding, management, or application of emerging technologies in agricultural systems. Topics announced when offered.

title: Agricultural Technology Management Capstone Project II
description: A continuation of ATM 536. Completion of a team-oriented capstone project, with emphasis on implementation, evaluation, documentation, and presentation of the project.

title: Water Management ahd Irrigation Systems
description: Management of water in crop production systems, crop water use, and irrigation scheduling. Fundamentals of water flow in pipe networks, pumping plants, and irrigation systems.

title: Water Management and Irrigation Systems Lab
description: Laboratory and hands-on activities on soil water balance characteristics, crop water use, water flow in pipe networks, pump hydraulics, sprinkler nozzles, drip irrigation systems, water filtration systems, and chemigation systems.

title: Watershed Assessment and Management
description: Overview of water legislation and policies (e.g. Clean Water Act, NPDES, TMDL). Principles of watershed pollutant sources, fate, and transport. Impacts of watershed protection and restoration strategies on water and environmental quality.

title: Private Water Supply And Waste Management
Topics in Agricultural Technology Management
description: The selection, operational theory, repair, maintenance, adjustment, and application of equipment and materials for agricultural systems.

title: Graduate Seminar in Agricultural Technology Management
Introduction to Aircraft Materials and Tooling Standards
description: Skills and techniques essential to understanding aircraft material properties and fabrication techniques. Emphasizes knowledge and practical experiences involving: shop safety, organization and human factors associated with shop practices, basic aircraft structural materials and hardware familiarization, fluid lines and fittings, hand tool selection and use, and aviation-specific dimensional inspection tools and techniques, aircraft hardware identification and applications, cleaning and corrosion control, aircraft metal selection an applications, and aircraft material inspection fundamentals.

title: Aviation Regulations, Compliance and Operations
description: A review of the role and organizational structure of the Federal Aviation Administration (FAA) as it relates to the certification and continued airworthiness of aircraft and its operation in general, business, and commercial aviation environments. Emphasis on the privileges and limitations of certificated personnel who maintain aircraft systems in the context of the FAA regulations. Includes practical applications of aircraft weight and balance, effective completion of maintenance record entries, accurate use of graphs and charts to determine critical performance values, and the servicing, staring, ground operations, and security of aircraft. Students demonstrate the ability to read, comprehend, and apply information contained in FAA and manufacturers' aircraft maintenance specifications, data sheets, manuals, publications, and related Federal Aviation Regulations, Airworthiness Directives, and Advisory material.

title: Intro to Aircraft Materials and Tooling Standards Laboratory
description: A laboratory course to supplement the material of AVM 101.

title: Basic Aircraft Electricity
description: A basic concept of DC/AC circuits, with basic laws relating to the following: measuring voltage, current, resistance, continuity and leakage; relationship of voltage, current and resistance in electrical circuits; reading and interpretation of electrical circuit diagrams; electrical devices and inspection and servicing of batteries. Introduction to digital numbering systems and digital logic functions.

title: Aircraft Welding
description: Theory and skill development in aircraft welding processes. Exercises in gas welding processes as applied to ferrous and nonferrous materials. Oxygen/acetylene, inert gas, and resistance welding processes are to be studied.

title: Aircraft Drawings
description: Students learn how to read, understand, and interpret aircraft drawings commonly found in the aviation maintenance industry.  Recognition and identification of drawing lines and symbols, and the meaning of these is covered.

title: Aircraft Standards
description: A survey of the organization of the Federal Aviation Administration. Emphasis will be placed on regulations, standards, specifications, procedures and the practice of using charts, graphs and drawings. Also included are the introduction to air transport maintenance procedures, weight and balance procedures, aircraft ground operations and the introduction to flight training to include several flights.

title: Aircraft Fluid Power
description: A study of basic fluid mechanics as it applies to practical applications in aircraft systems. Compressible and incompressible fluid systems will be studied.

title: Aircraft Science
description: This is a study of basic physics as required by aircraft technicians and defined by FAR 147. Use and understand the principles of simple machines, sound, fluid, and heat dynamics, basic aerodynamics, aircraft structures, and theory of flight.

title: Airframe Systems
description: A study of the airframe systems and components to include: pressurization, heating and cooling, and structural device.

title: Aviation Maintenance Fundamentals
description: This course is designed to permit the student to learn and practice those skills and techniques essential to the career development of the aviation maintenance technician. The subjects included are: shop safety, aircraft general familiarization, fluid lines and fittings, hand tools and measuring devices, aircraft hardware, cleaning and corrosion control, aircraft metals, inspection fundamentals, ground operation and servicing, and support equipment.

title: Airframe Struct/Repr
description: A study of materials commonly used in airframe structures and the associated study of making structural repairs according to recommended procedures. Skills in sheet metal are stressed.

title: Airframe Electrical Systems
description: An advanced study of DC/AC circuits law relating to circuit analysis and a detailed study of measuring instruments. Advanced study of relays, switches, alternators, and other devices encountered in circuit analysis, troubleshooting, and repair. Theory of operation and fault isolation on solid-state devices in aircraft circuitry. Inspection of aircraft electrical systems, including wire inspections and the types of repairs allowed, using appropriate technical manuals.

title: Aircraft Metallic Primary Structures
description: A study of aircraft metal primary structures and materials commonly used in the design and construction of airframes including fuselages, wings, and control surfaces. Students gain theoretical knowledge and practical experience associated with the structural properties of materials used in the manufacturing process as well as the methods used to fabricate and repair those structures. Students practice structural metal repairs according to FAA approved data and manufacturer's recommended procedures. Skills in aluminum sheet metal fabrication and repairs are stressed.

title: Aircraft Environmental and Fire Protection Systems
description: An in-depth study in small and large air vehicle environmental and fire detection and extinguishing systems. Emphasis on cabin pressurization and temperature climate control, supplemental oxygen, airframe and propulsion fire detection and extinguishing systems, crew visibility enhancement, and air toxicity detection. Students gain extensive practical experiences related to system design, operation, inspection, maintenance methodology, and fault analysis for both airframe and powerplant systems using manufacturer's technical instructions.

title: Aircraft Environmental and Fire Protection Systems Laboratory
description: A laboratory course to supplement the material of AVM 203.

title: Aircraft Landing Gear and Fluid Power Systems
description: A study of the design and application of compressible and incompressible fluid power systems used in both simple and complex aircraft flight control, auxiliary, emergency, and landing gear systems. Emphasis on landing gear and deceleration systems to include components, structures, operation, and fault analysis of shock struts, position and warning, single and multiple disk brakes, anti-skid, wheel assemblies, retraction and extension systems and methods of emergency landing gear extension. Topics include landing gear door operation and sequencing. Students practice inspection, servicing, repair and troubleshooting of key fluid power and landing gear systems necessary to ensure continued airworthiness of these systems.

title: Aircraft Electrical Systems
description: A review and advanced study of DC/AC circuits, and laws relating to circuit analysis and a detailed study of measuring instruments applied to aircraft. Study of relays, switches, alternators, and other devices encountered in circuit analysis, troubleshooting, and repair. Theory of operation and fault isolation on solid-state devices in aircraft circuitry. Inspection of aircraft electrical systems, including wire inspections and the types of repairs allowed using appropriate technical manuals. A study of battery, magneto high and low tension ignition systems, including turbine igniters. Also a study of powerplant starting ad charging systems and related components. Emphasis on effective fault diagnostics, repair, and timing of aircraft ignition systems.

title: Introduction to Aircraft Propulsion Theory, Design and Systems
description: A broad introductory study in the theory of operation of heat engines utilizing principles of the Otto and Brayton cycles found in aircraft reciprocating and gas turbine powerplants. Students gain a solid understanding of the basic principles of the construction, design, lubrication, cooling, and exhaust systems, including maintenance practices of reciprocating and gas turbine powerplants installed in aircraft. Thrust reverser operation of gas turbine engines is also included. The course has a practical component that includes the inspection, fault analysis, servicing and repair of key engines systems covered in the course.

title: Aircraft Propulsion Drive Systems
description: A detailed study of aircraft propulsion drive systems that convert engine power to thrust necessary for sustained flight. Drive systems include propellers, and rotor-blades found on small, medium, and large fixed, and rotary-wing aircraft. Emphasis on fixed and controllable-pitch propellers applications as well as rotor-blades, and rotor-hubs assemblies found on helicopters. Topics include theory of operation, dynamic and static balancing, vibration analysis, design characteristics, materials, and maintenance practices. Students practice inspection, servicing, balance, maintenance and repair of propeller and rotor systems.

title: Aircraft Finish & Fabrication
description: This course is designed to acquaint the student with the wood and fabric coverings and procedures used on aircraft, and methods used in preparation for and application of paint finishes to aircraft surfaces.

title: Navigational Aids and Communication Systems
description: A survey study of the aids to navigation and communications used in light and intermediate class aircraft. Operation and installation of the various types of equipment will be stressed.

title: Navigational Aids and Communication Systems for Avionics
Aircraft Inspection/Assm
description: A study of assembly and manufacturing procedures and inspection of aircraft components. This course also covers in detail annual and 100-hour inspections.

title: Advanced Reciprocating Powerplant Technology
description: A study in the theory, operation, construction, design, and maintenance of reciprocating powerplants installed in aircraft. Topics include the principles of the Otto cycle, reciprocating powerplant systems, and emerging trends in the design and application of reciprocating engines, including aviation diesel engines. Practical experiences include engine removal, installation, and troubleshooting to an airworthy standard, with an emphasis on disassembly and inspection of modern reciprocating engines found in single and twin-engine aircraft using visual, dimensional, and non-destructive techniques.

title: Aircraft Fuel Management and Metering Systems
description: A comprehensive study of airframe and propulsion engine fuel systems associated with fuel storage, management, transfer and metering as applied to both reciprocating and turbine powered aircraft. Lecture topics include systems operation, maintenance methods, procedures and safety precautions associated with aircraft fueling and defueling for over-the-wing and pressure fueling systems. Fuel quantity, pressure and temperature indicating and warning systems, fuel dump, and detail reciprocating and turbine engine fuel metering systems including carburetor overhaul, electronic engine fuel controls (EEC and FADEC) are covered. Students practice the inspection, check, service, troubleshooting and repair of the various fuel systems found on board the aircraft to a level that assures continued airworthiness and safety.

title: Rotary and Fixed Wing Aircraft Design and Assembly
description: A detailed study of the purpose of fixed-wing and rotary-wing aircraft designs and aerodynamic characteristics, their construction methods and assembly procedures. The course provides an insight critical maintenance tasks performed that impact the safe operation of the aircraft as well as exposure to the manufacture and assembly of aircraft. Students practice the rigging, assembly, jacking, weighing, and balancing of aircraft and flight control surfaces according to manufacturer's technical manuals; and making accurate record entries for tasks performed in accordance with FARs.

title: Aircraft Propellers
description: A study of the use, maintenance, and inspection of propellers and their related control systems.

title: Powerplant Fundamentals
description: A study of the principles of operation, design features, and operating characteristics of reciprocating aircraft engines. Includes overhaul inspection procedures on current horizontal opposed and radial engines.
