Diagnostic applications for medical images are emphasized along with safety and ethics issues that relate to the acquisition and management of biomedical image data.

title: Applications of Advanced Microscopy in BME
description: This course introduces the advanced applications of microscopy techniques including light, fluorescence, super-resolution, and electron microscopy. Students will gain a deep understanding of the interaction mechanisms of light with biological tissues, the working principles behind major microscopy technologies, and their applications in biomedical engineering.

title: Advanced Biomaterials
description: Structure-function relationships of biomaterials and biomaterial characterization will be covered. Materials for medical implants, tissue engineering, biosensing, imaging, and drug delivery will be addressed.

title: Advanced Tissue Engineering
description: This course introduces the field of tissue engineering with an eye toward improved human and animal health. Interdisciplinary topics include cells/tissue, biomaterials, organ scaffolds, transplantation, and clinical applications of tissue engineering. Students will explore application areas such as skin regeneration, cartilage repair, and organ transplantation.

title: Contemporary Molecular and Cellular Engineering
description: Investigates the molecules and mechanisms underlying cellular function from an engineering perspective, emphasizing physical, chemical, and quantitative approaches. Students analyze how molecular structure and energetics give rise to cellular behaviors such as gene expression, signaling, mechanics, and division, and they apply these principles to contemporary problems in medicine, biotechnology, bionanotechnology, synthetic biology, and tissue engineering. The course blends rigorous theory with critical reading of current papers and quantitative problem-solving.

title: Advanced Computer Vision with Biomedical Applications
description: This course provides a comprehensive introduction to classical and deep learning techniques in computer vision, with a focus on biomedical image analysis. Students will explore image preprocessing, segmentation, object detection, and motion analysis using Python and MATLAB. The course emphasizes hands-on learning, critical evaluation of published work, and project-based exploration of real-world biomedical datasets.

title: Topics in Biomedical Engineering
description: A course reserved for study of current topics in biomedical engineering. Topics announced when offered.

title: Graduate Seminar in Biomedical Engineering
description: Presentation and discussion of current advances and research in biomedical engineering. Required of all graduate students in biomedical engineering.

title: Master’s Report
description: Topics selected with approval of major professor and department head.

title: Master's Thesis
description: Topics selected with approval of major professor and department head.

title: Dissertation Research
description: Topics selected with approval of major professor and department head.

title: Introduction to Business
description: This course surveys the objectives, decisions, and activities within a business organization. Topics include a study of management responsibilities and controls, organizational structures, and marketing activities.

title: Financial Accounting
description: Study of business topics such as alternative forms of business organizations; typical business practices; legal instruments such as notes, bonds, and stocks; and financial statements and analysis. The main objective is to develop the ability to provide information to stockholders, creditors, and others who are outside an organization.

title: Managerial Accounting
description: This course outlines the use of internal accounting data by managers in directing the affairs of business and non-business organizations.

title: Supervisory Management
description: An analysis of the responsibilities and work environment of a supervisor, with an examination of skills, practices, and concepts helpful in developing effective relations with people in today's changing environment.

title: Intmed Acctg
description: This course addresses the commitment of management and the organization as a whole to the cultural changes necessary to implement quality improvements throughout the organization. Topics include quality organization and philosophy, quality audit and ISO 9000 series, integration of functional areas, team building, management principles, quality costs, and other associated interactive facets of Total Quality Management. The main concern is to provide the student with a working knowledge of conventional TQM tools.

title: Management with Information Technology
description: A comprehensive view of the role of information technology in satisfying organizations' information requirements. Problems and techniques concerning the management of responsive information systems with special attention to managers' use of systems outputs. Cases and hands-on exercises emphasizing the use of information systems in decision making, information gathering and organizing, use of modeling techniques, and presentation of information.

title: Foundations of Business Law
description: A study of law as it relates to business, including court procedures and systems, contracts, torts, agency and employment law, and business crimes.

title: Marketing Techniques and Applications
description: A general study of marketing principles which lead to the development of marketing strategy. A review of environmental influences and key analytical tools used in formulating marketing plans. Product or service design, distribution, pricing, and promotional programs.

title: Basic Project Management
description: This course focuses on applied project management methodology, tools, and techniques. Topics include project organization, planning, execution, and communications; the project life cycle; and risk analysis.

title: Transformational Leadership for Project Management
description: Study and application of leadership styles and common leader traits, skills, and behaviors. Introduces concepts of transformational leadership. Students learn to plan and lead strategic initiatives that represent significant change(s) within an organization.

title: Managerial and Project Economics
description: Economic analysis of problems as applied to managerial decision making. Students consider the economic viability of solutions in engineering and a variety of other kinds of projects common in technology-oriented businesses.

title: Applied Operations Management
description: Description and analysis of problems related to the output of goods and services, operations planning and control, and systems management.

title: Integrated Finance
description: Study of the basic principles of finance, including discounted cash flow analysis, risk-return tradeoff, asset pricing models, and financial and real asset valuation. Applications of these concepts to the firm's investment and financing decisions and performance analysis will be discussed.

title: Integrated Technology Management Capstone
description: A capstone course which integrates the functional areas of business, including management, marketing, finance, accounting, operations and production. The course uses business strategy simulation and case studies to apply acquired knowledge across various areas of concentration for successful business management.

title: Practices of Industrial and Labor Relations
description: Basic course in industrial and labor relations. Broad coverage of the institution of collective bargaining and its environment, the goals and operation of labor unions, the impact of unions on management, and labor relations law.

title: Strategic Human Resource Management
description: This course provides an overview of the human resource systems and processes needed to achieve organizational effectiveness and strategic success. All key functional areas including human resource planning, staffing, performance management, employee development, and compensation are addressed.

title: Personal Selling and Sales Management
description: Focuses on interpersonal communications between buyers and sellers, both oral and written. The mechanics and intricacies of personal sales presentations, which will be developed through practice.

title: Introduction to Computing
description: An overview of the history of computers and programming. Famous historical figures and their impact in modern computing. Introduction to major topics in computer science such as artificial intelligence, high performance computing, cryptography, big data, cybersecurity, robotics, and more.

title: Elements of Computer Programming
description: Brief experience with computer programming concepts such as variables, data types, functions, conditionals, iteration, and collections.

title: Web Page Development
description: The Internet, web browsers, and web-page-development technology: web-page design and implementation with Hypertext Markup Language (HTML), and CSS. Integration of program script into web pages. Introduction to graphics design, animation, and server utilization.

title: Elements of Artificial Intelligence
description: Hands-on introduction to Artificial Intelligence (AI) using Python. This course explores foundational topics like data handling, supervised and unsupervised learning, neural networks, natural language processing, computer vision, and the ethical implications of AI. Interactive labs and projects are used to develop real AI applications and reflect on their impact in society.

title: Privacy and Artificial Intelligence
description: Philosophical definitions of privacy, metrics (differential privacy, information leakage) and modern privacy tools as they apply, or apply to, AI. Hands-on applications to structured (tabular) and unstructured (images, videos) data. Timely real-world privacy problems faced by, and caused by, AI.

title: Artificial Intelligence and Cybersecurity
description:
Introduction to the rapidly evolving intersection of Artificial Intelligence and Cybersecurity. Students will explore how AI technologies—such as machine learning algorithms—can enhance system security, while also examining the vulnerabilities that adversaries exploit and the strategies used to defend against them.
Through real-world case studies, interactive labs, and hands-on activities, students will gain practical experience with both offensive and defensive techniques in AI security. The course also includes a forward-looking discussion on emerging research, including work being conducted at K-State.

title: Fundamental Computer Programming Concepts
description: Basic concepts in developing computer programs: program structure and syntax, primitive data types, variables, control flow, iteration, simple algorithms, debugging, and good software development practices. Introduction to object-oriented programming.

title: Foundational Topics in Applied Computer Science
description: Selected foundational topics in applied computer science. Specific course descriptions vary by topic.

title: Data Structures and Algorithms I
description: Exploration of data structures and related algorithms in computer programming. Basic concepts of complexity analysis. Object-oriented design concepts.

title: Data Structures and Algorithms II
description: Advanced data structures and related algorithms. Formal software development methods and software engineering fundamentals. Introduction to requirements analysis processes that provide the specification of algorithmic requirements.

title: Advanced Programming
description: Advanced programming techniques and projects. Concepts from simulation and modeling, media applications, secure design, information management, parallelism, and networking. Software development methodologies, processes, and design patterns. Practical experience with professional communication and collaboration.

title: Computer Systems Administration
description: Administration of the Windows and Linux operating systems, including installation, configuration, distributed file systems, firewalls and security, shell scripting, and user account management. Introduction to managing cloud computing resources.

title: Full Stack Web Development
description: Fundamental principles and best practices of web development, user interface design, web API design, advanced web interfaces, web development frameworks, single-page web applications, web standards and accessibility issues.

title: Database Essentials
description: Introduction to concepts and techniques in database management. Overview of relational databases, NoSQL databases, and related topics. Database programming and use of databases in applications. Theory and architecture of database management systems (DBMS).

title: Applied Data Science
description: An introduction to data science and discovery from data: Data wrangling, feature engineering, feature selection, statistical inference, correlations, principal component analysis, classification, regression, novelty detection, clustering, cross-validation, bootstrapping, class profiling, multidimensional scaling, association rules, visualization, data science and society.

title: Special Topics in Applied Computer Science
description: Selected topics in applied computer science.

title: Introduction to Computing for Educators
description: This course is a first step in preparing educators to teach computer science topics at the PreK-12 level. It provides content area knowledge including the historical context in which computing developed, an overview of the modern field of computing and its impacts on society. It also explores theories of computational thinking and learning, computer science education research, and the Kansas PreK-12 computer science standards.

title: Computer Education Programming Fundamentals
description: This course introduces PreK-12 educators to the ""Big ideas"" of programming - concept knowledge that is critical for developing and teaching programming skills. Students will learn to read simple program code, write short programs, and explore basic programming constructs such as conditional statements and iteration. Students will also explore basic programming concepts related to a particular discipline.

title: Computer Programming for Educators
description: The course introduces PreK-12 educators to computer programming using one of several programming languages. Interactive lessons and engaging projects reinforce new skills and concepts while exploring pedagogical strategies for teaching programming. This course covers the basic concepts of programming, from variables and control flow to functions, objects, and simple algorithms.

title: Data Structures and Algorithms for Educators I
description: This course provides PreK-12 educators the content knowledge necessary for teaching simple data structures such as sets, lists, stacks, queues, and maps. Students learn how to create data structures and the algorithms to work with them. Students are introduced to algorithm analysis to determine the efficiency of algorithms. It also addresses strategies for teaching these concepts to a diverse audience.

title: Data Structures and Algorithms for Educators II
description: This course provides PreK-12 educators the content knowledge necessary for teaching advanced data structures, such as trees, graphs, and heaps, along with pedagogical approaches for teaching the associated concepts and skills. Several new algorithms using these data structures are covered. Students also learn software development methods and software engineering fundamentals and use those skills to develop projects of increasing size and scope effectively.

title: Advanced Computing for Educators
description: This course helps PreK-12 educators gain experience writing programs using a variety of advanced programming techniques including object-oriented programming, inheritance, and events. It also introduces software development methodologies, processes, and design patterns, along with pedagogical strategies to teach them. It also emphasizes collaboration skills and tools used by professional programmers.

title: Topics in Computing for Educators
description: Topics in Computing Education for PreK-12 educators. Topics will be determined by faculty.

title: Indian Country Agriculture and Natural Resources
description: An introduction to the historical and contemporary issues related to natural resource management on Native American lands. Study will include a variety of ecological settings across Native American lands, along with an analysis of tribal sovereignty as it relates to land tenure and water rights. Arguments concerning natural resource conservation, preservation and extraction will be explored.

title: Youth Development in Native Communities
description: Focus on contemporary issues impacting native youth including: Demographics (Criminal justice, early parenting, poverty, education, suicide and morbidity), identity formation (risky behavior and achievement opportunities), health care (diabetes, obesity, fetal alcohol), prevention activities (4H, BGC, horse clubs, running strong), and influx of youth leadership (youth-elder connections).

title: Wellness in Native Communities:  Challenges and Opportunities
description: Healthcare issues challenging Native communities, and strategies and practices to address those challenges. Focus on the impact of the Indian Health System, other bureaucratic systems, and current consumer practices that impact healthcare for Native peoples and the ways tribes are working to create healthier communities and improve the lifestyle of Indian people.

title: Role of Tribal Colleges in Economic Development
description: Focus: the role of tribally-chartered colleges/universities in economic development within Native communities. Topics include the use of bonding/bridging social capital, the historical and contemporary case for tribally-chartered higher education, the economic impact of tribal colleges on their local economies, and opportunities/challenges of broad and diverse collaborative networks.

title: Ecological Economics
description: Synthesis across the notion of ""utility"" as represented in environmental/natural resource economics, as well as the notion of ""ecology"" in ecological economics. Focus will be on the influence the community and ecosystem have on one another, and the potential for complementarity between the two.

title: Impact Analysis
description: The basics of economics and fiscal impact analysis are explored, including the scenario construction, basics of input-output analysis, careful use of multipliers, estimations of local revenues and expenditures and discounting.

title: Local Economic Analysis
description: This course teaches economic base theory, a theory of regional economics, multipliers and how local economics are affected by external events. Basic methods for the analysis of a local economy are covered, including trend analysis, location quotients, shift share analysis and retail trade analysis.

title: Community Developer as Community Educator
description: A graduate level course on education strategies and tools for use in community development. This course will develop students' identities as community educators and provide knowledge on appropriate methodologies for working with adults in community settings as well as develop their creativity and critical thinking skills. Students will engage in peer-to-peer teaching and critical analysis of community education activities.

title: Participatory Action Research Methods (PAR)
description: A graduate level course to develop participatory action research knowledge and skills through real-world applications. PAR is a method of collecting information by community members in collaboration with a researcher that respects, places community central to, and reflects the experiences and culture of the people most directly impacted by the issue under consideration.

title: Leadership for Change
description: Course focus is on the role of leadership in community development and change, including situating leadership in the community development process, reviewing the effectiveness of different leadership styles, and relating leadership to community. Skills and processes that facilitate effective shared leadership, including facilitation, conflict resolution, use of participatory techniques, etc, are explained.

title: Grantwriting
description: The intricacies of grantsmanship provide the focus of this course. Topics covered will include identification of fund sources, procedures for proposal preparation, composition of grants, and the effects of organizational and personal linkages. Students will prepare a grant application based upon an RFP or to a continuous funding source.

title: Community Economic Development for the 21st Century
description: Study of the basic theories, concepts, and skills necessary to be a part of a productive community economic development team. Focus is on the importance of organizational values, mission and vision, as well as six approaches to development. The course will cover the five community functions, development strategies, action planning, and organizing for action.

title: Land Development Planning
description: An examination of the process of land development in the United States, and its impacts from the perspective of developers, financial institutions, community planners, and city administrators. Focus is on the understanding of the land development process in meeting community goals, and shaping land development to meet community expectations for the improvement of the community.

title: Policy and Politics of Coastal Areas
description: One half of the world's population lives on a coast and two-thirds of the world's largest cities are located on a coast. This course addresses the environmental, land use, and other critical issues affecting these areas worldwide.

title: Foundations of Community Development
description: This course is an introduction to the Community Development (CD) program, discipline, and profession.

title: Special Studies in Community Development
description: Independent study on special topics of interest in community development.

title: Community Engagement
description: This course is designed to help students add to their knowledge base and build their confidence in community engagement so that dialogue and deliberations leading to public decision making can be more productive and positive for everyone involved.

title: Applied Community Development
description: This course provides students the opportunity to apply knowledge to professional practice. Students will produce a written report on a project of focus, which they have worked on directly, relating their experience to concepts and skills learned through the community development curriculum. Projects must exist at the time of the course and may consist of activities via internships, coursework or capstones outside of the community development curriculum, or personal interests.

title: Community Leadership and Capacity Building
description: Explorations of the various approaches to leadership and leadership development, evaluation of leadership projects and the relationship of leadership to community capacity building. Topics include defining leadership and applying it to the workplace, and understanding the potential link between leadership and community capacity, as well as identifying strategies for leadership development in communities.

title: Organizing for Community Change
description: Examination of the role of society in community planning efforts. Focus on change within communities and the roles of government, planners, and citizens in reacting to or shaping change. Dimensions of social capital and the context of change and the implications of economic and demographic shifts on strategies and tactics for change will be explored.

title: Economic Development Strategies and Programs
description: Course explores theories of local economic development and addresses the development issues faced by communities in the 21st century. Students will understand and apply concepts from economic development planning, economic analysis, business development, human resource development, community-based development, and high-technology development.

title: Evaluations of Organizations and Programs
description: This course is an introduction to philosophy, techniques, and methodologies of organizational and program evaluation. Topics covered in the course include overview of program evaluation and theory, techniques to evaluate program processes and performance, evaluation designs, assessing program efficiency, models to diagnose organizations, and methods to assess organizational performance.

title: Government, Politics and Community Development
description: Introduction to classical and contemporary concepts of federalism, public administration, public policy, and politics with an emphasis on their bearing on community development activities and outcomes.

title: Housing and Development Programs
description: In this course, students review and evaluate historical and current housing issues, production, and financial systems. This includes consideration of racial, ethnic, income, and gender issues as they relate to the role of housing developments and programs in community development.

title: Community Analysis
description: An introduction to research methods relevant to community development. Topics include how to formulate and begin a research effort, methods of data collection and how conceptual frameworks are used to develop the questions and analyze data. Also included are strategies for reporting findings and applying findings in community action.

title: Immigrants in Communities
description: International migration has historically impacted rural and urban communities around the world. Taking a comparative approach, this course examines community-immigrant interactions and influences on community development and immigrant inclusion. Readings relate theories of immigrant and community change to case studies. Students will gather primary data to assess the capacity of communities to include new international immigrants.

title: Sustainable Communities
description: The management of natural capital as linked to other community-based actions around resource allocation and the impacts on quality of life. The literature on community-based natural resource management will be examined and alternative ways of valuing natural capital will be assessed. Contrasting theories of the role of natural capital in communities.

title: Nonprofit Management
description: Managing nonprofits including the role of nonprofit organizations in addressing various social problems. Topics include individual giving/volunteering, board and executive leadership, government and nonprofit relationship, ethics and accountability, and issues and challenges in nonprofit management.

title: Introduction to Native Community Development
description: This develops a basic understanding within the context of community development of the diversity of tribal structures and cultures and the unique history and jurisdictional considerations of these nations.

title: Principles and Strategies of Community Change
description: An analysis of the principles and practices of community change and development, using case studies and the students' communities of reference. the course will relate Community Development approaches to conceptual models from diverse disciplines. Conceptual models include conflict, neo-classical economic growth, participatory democracy, and others.

title: Building Native Communities and Economic Capacity
description: This course will focus on non-western approaches to helping native communities build their capacity. Students will learn to take a participatory, culture-centered, and strength-based approach to development.

title: Community and Regional Economic Analysis
description: The course introduces concepts of communities and regions, theories of economic growth, drivers of economic growth, the economic base of a community, sources of growth or decline in the community, roles of local government and institutions, analytical tools, and strategies for local economic development.

title: Community and Regional Economic Analysis II
description: This course will develop a substantive grounding in the theories and practice of measuring community economic dynamics plus build solid foundation skills for applied community economic analysis. Elements include basic descriptive tools of community economic analysis, such as economic base evaluation, industrial mix analysis, trade area assessment, and industrial and occupational composition analysis, input-output industrial accounting for economic impact analysis and the development of fiscal impact assessment techniques, and measures of public policy effectiveness.

title: Community and Natural Resource Management
description: An introduction to the breadth of consideration involved in community resource management. Included are theoretical frameworks, methodological investigation and applied practices to enhance the ability of community development professionals to work with their communities to plan, develop, and monitor the conversion and development of natural resources with multiple functions.

title: Topics in Community Development
description: Independent study of selected concepts and trends in community development. Students electing this course offering will be preparing a specialization paper as their final project.

title: Proposal Writing
description: Development of a master's report or thesis proposal.

title: Research in Community Development
description: Original research and advanced study in community development, and related fields for the master's report.

title: Engineering Assembly
description: This course provides an overview of the civil engineering profession through participation in the American Society of Civil Engineers (ASCE) student chapter. Students will engage with the professional community by attending presentations from civil engineering consulting firms, government agencies, and other organizations involved in civil works. These meetings offer insights into industry trends, project management, and career pathways. The course also emphasizes community engagement, with students earning assembly points through attendance at professional functions, participation in community service projects, or active membership in an engineering academic society or project club.

title: Introduction to Civil Engineering
description: Introduction to careers in civil engineering (construction, environmental geotechnical, structures, transportation, and water resources). Overview of CE educational requirements. History of the CE profession. Engineering ethics.

title: Civil Engineering Problem Solving I
description: Analysis of data and numerical calculations including presentation of results of common civil engineering problems using Excel.  Exposure to built-in functions, data presentation, solution techniques and macros will facilitate usage in other civil engineering classes.

title: Elementary Surveying Engineering
description: Coordinates, directions, distances, and elevation. transverses. boundary surveys. Leveling, national rectangular coordinate systems. property descriptions: public land subdivision and metes and bounds. Topographic surveys. surveying, planning, and estimating.

title: Introduction to Civil Engineering Materials
description: Properties and behavior of structural metals, timber, portland cement concrete, and bituminous concrete; standard specification and methods of test; inspection and control. Topics support ACI Field Testing Technician certification.

title: Civil Engineering Problem Solving
description: This course introduces the fundamental principles of data analysis and numerical calculations for solving common civil engineering problems. Students will learn to use professional engineering software packages to present results and interpret data. The curriculum covers key concepts like built-in functions, data visualization, and symbolic calculations, with a strong emphasis on structured programming techniques. This course aims to equip students with the computational skills necessary to succeed in future civil engineering courses.

title: Strength of Materials and Analysis
description: Study of behavior of materials subjected to tension, compression, shear, and bending; analysis of beams and columns.

title: Strength of Materials Laboratory
description: Tests to determine the physical properties of various structural materials. Analysis and interpretation of test data.

title: Statics
description: Composition and resolution of forces; equilibrium of force systems; application of general laws of statics to engineering problems, including use of vector algebra, friction and force analyses of simple structures, cables, and machine elements; center of gravity; moments of inertia.

title: Incompressible Fluid Mechanics
description: Fundamentals of incompressible fluid mechanics and includes the topics of fluid properties; hydrostatics; applications of conservation of mass; energy and momentum equations; dimensional analysis; pipe flow; channel flow; and flow about immersed objects.

title: Design of Civil Engineering Materials
description: Apply principles first introduced in CE 241 to design of traditional and specialized concretes (such as high-performance concrete, self-compacting concrete, low-CO2 concrete, internally-cured concrete, and RAP asphalt concrete); properties of concrete prepared using non-traditional cements; standardized testing to quantify long-term durability of concrete; planning experiments to minimize error variance and avoid bias.

title: Undergraduate Research Experience
description: Open to students pursuing undergraduate research projects.

title: Honors Research In Civil Engineering
description: Individual research problem selected with approval of faculty advisor. A report is presented orally and in writing during the last semester.

title: Project Economic Evaluation
description: This course provides an introduction to the economic evaluation of projects and the fundamental principles for comparing alternative projects. Students will learn core engineering economics concepts, including the time value of money, inflation, depreciation, and taxes. Through practical applications, students will develop the skills to make informed financial decisions in various engineering contexts.

title: Project Management
description: Fundamental concepts of project management. Construction planning and scheduling methodologies will be introduced.

title: Project Delivery
description: Fundamental concepts of project administration such as contracts, safety,  and delivery methods. Construction project delivery methods such as Design-Bid-Build (DBB), Design-Build (DB), etc. will be discussed in detail and students will be introduced to construction estimating.

title: Soil Mechanics
description: The course involves identification and classification of soils, hydraulic conductivity, compaction, ground water seepage, theory of consolidation, stress distribution in soil, theory of consolidation, soil compressibility and settlement, and shear strength.

title: Foundation Engineering
description: The course includes site investigation; in situ testing of soils; determining bearing capacity of shallow foundations, mat foundations, and deep foundations; calculation of lateral earth pressures; and design of earth retaining structures.

title: Statics and Dynamics
description: A shortened combined course in (1) statics, including a study of force systems, free-body diagrams, and problems in equilibrium, friction, centroids, and moments of inertia; and (2) dynamics, including a study of the kinematics and kinetics of particles and rigid bodies using the method of force-mass acceleration.

title: Mechanics of Materials
description: Elementary theories of stress and strain, behavior of materials, and applications of these theories and their generalizations to the study of stress distribution, deformation, and instability in the simple structural forms which occur most frequently in engineering practice.

title: Mechanics of Materials Laboratory
description: Determination of selected mechanical properties of several engineering materials, including iron-carbon alloys, aluminum alloys, concrete, wood, and plastics; relationship between structure and mechanical properties of these materials; elementary problems in experimental stress analysis and structural behavior; test procedures, instrumentation, and interpretation of results.

title: Introduction to Structural Analysis
description: Elastic analysis of determinate and indeterminate beams, frames, and trusses; construction of shear and moment diagrams and influence lines; calculation of deflections using conjugate beam and virtual work; solution of indeterminate structures by slope-deflection, moment distribution, and matrix stiffness method; with microcomputer applications.

title: Structural Engineering in Steel
description: Introduction to design of steel structures. Theoretical, experimental and practical bases for proportioning members and their connections.

title: Structural Engineering in Concrete
description: This course introduces the design concepts of reinforced concrete building components and their detailing. It includes design for flexure, shear, compression, anchorage, and serviceability using the latest American Concrete Institute (ACI) design requirements.

title: Water Resources Engineering
description: Introduction to: surface water hydrology, design of water handling structures, flood protection, pipe flow hydraulics, pump selection, and flow in soils.

title: Hydraulic Engineering
description: This course includes applications of the principles of fluid mechanics to control and use of water; reservoir, dam, and spillway design; enclosed conduit and open-channel design; hydraulic machinery and hydro-power development; principles of fluid measurement; laboratory-flow and velocity metering, hydraulic models, pipe losses, and open-channel flow.

title: Environmental Engineering Fundamentals
description: Basic physical, chemical, and biological concepts and the applications to the protection of the environment with emphasis on techniques used in water and wastewater treatment.

title: Water and Wastewater Engineering
description: In course includes application, analysis, and synthesis of foundational scientific principles and governing equations as they relate to municipal water and wastewater treatment. This is achieved through the study of unit operations and processes—adsorption, absorption, ion exchange, coagulation/flocculation, ion exchange, biological reaction, sedimentation, gas stripping—and their integration in municipal water and wastewater treatment process trains.

title: Introduction to Transportation Engineering
description: Introduction to elements of Transportation Engineering. Learn the roles of planning, operations, and policy for multi-modal transportation systems. Preliminary introduction to design, construction, and maintenance aspects of transportation. Explore the impacts of transportation systems on safety, access, and mobility.

title: Highway Engineering
description: Apply highway design principles first introduced in CE 571. Plan and propose multiple alternatives for a highway alignment.  Design new highway alignments and intersections. Consider earthwork and how it influences highway design.  Introduce pavement design as it applies to highway alignments and intersections.

title: Fundamentals of Public Transit Planning and Operation
Civil Engineering Project
description: This capstone course is designed for final-semester Civil Engineering majors. It provides students with the opportunity to apply and integrate knowledge and skills acquired throughout their elective coursework in a comprehensive, team-based design project. Students will work collaboratively to produce a complete civil engineering design, which includes preparing and presenting both a professional written design report and an oral presentation. The course emphasizes practical application, teamwork, and communication skills essential for a career in civil engineering.

title: Principles of Geoenvironmental Engineering
description: Introduction to soil formation, structure, flow and mass transport in soils, mass transfer principles, waste containment and isolation, and physical, chemical, and biological remediation principles.

title: Design of Groundwater Flow Systems
description: Introduction to fundamental, mathematical and physical concepts of groundwater flow; application of simple analytic models; introduction to field methods; application of computer modeling tools to address design with practical significance.

title: Unit Operations and Processes in Environmental Engineering
description: A laboratory study of various physical, chemical and biological operations and processes used in the professional practice of environmental engineering. Topics covered will be selected from reactor hydrodynamics, oxidation-reduction, coagulation-flocculation, chemical precipitation, ion exchange, adsorption processes, biological oxidation, anaerobic digestion and the activated-sludge process.

title: Economics of Design and Construction
description: Selection of alternative engineering design and construction solutions through study of unit cost determination, cost estimating, and financing procedures. Introduction to construction scheduling.

title: Selected Topics in Civil Engineering
description: Applications of Precast Concrete in Buildings, Precast Concrete Materials, Preliminary Analysis and Loading Path, Structural Loads, Design of Precast and Prestressed Concrete Components, Handling, Erection and Bracing, Precast Concrete Tolerances.

title: Responsibility in Engineering: Codes & Professionalism
description: Course designed to help engineers, scientists, and technical managers to: understand fundamental ""canons"" of professional societies, establish or revitalize personal ethical standards using these canons, realize when situations are ethical dilemmas, and to develop a process to solving dilemmas. Key activities are: interviewing peers, analyzing current environment for potential challenges, developing ethics workshops, and writing individual code of ethics.

title: Responsibility in Engineering:  Leadership & Diversity
description: Course designed to help engineers, scientists, and technical managers to: understand leadership approaches that promote high ethical conduct, and to understand the ethical value and challenges associated with a diverse workplace. Key activities are: interviewing peers; analyzing current environment for potential challenges; developing a workshop related to ethics, leadership and diversity; and writing individual code of ethics.

title: Engineering Photo Interpretation
description: Photo interpretation techniques, types of aerial photographic film and their uses; application in land use studies, land surveying, site selection, rainfall runoff and stream flow, location of construction materials, and in the determination of soil properties; other applications.

title: Designing With Geosynthetics
description: History of geosynthetics; overview of geosynthetic functions, applications and properties; relationship between testing and applications. Designing with geotextiles, geogrids, geonets, geomembranes, geosynthetic clay liners and geocomposites.

title: Advanced Soil Testing For Engineering Purposes
Seepage in Permeable Materials
description: Analysis of seepage; groundwater movement in slopes, embankments, dams, and earth-supporting structures; construction of flow nets; dewatering systems; filter and drain design.

title: Advanced Geotechnical Design
description: Advanced studies of soil investigations; design of retaining structures and reinforced earth walls, sheet piles, anchored bulkheads, underground conduits and tunnels; analysis and repair of failed structures.

title: Advanced Structural Analysis I
description: Plane truss and frame analysis by direct stiffness method; equivalent nodal forces; thermal and settlement effects; stiffness by energy methods; analysis of space frames and trusses; grid structures; advanced topics.

title: Civil Engineering Materials II
description: Advanced study of civil engineering materials including concrete, steel and bituminous concrete.

title: Advanced Steel Design
description: Plastic design of steel structures; stability problems in plastic design; design of complex steel structures.

title: Advanced Reinforced Concrete Design
description: Advanced methods of analysis and design of reinforced concrete members and structures.

title: Structural Dynamics
description: Introduction to fundamental concepts in structural dynamics; free and forced vibration of single and multiple degrees of freedom systems with and without damping, numerical methods for linear and non-linear dynamic analysis, and basics of the earthquake response and design of structures.

title: Hydraulics of Open Channels I
description: Properties of open-channel flow; types of open channels; conservation of mass, momentum, and energy; critical, uniform, and gradually varied flow; design of erodible channels; rapidly varied flow.

title: Advanced Hydrology
description: Review of basic principles; point and regional rainfall and flood frequency analyses; hydrologic and hydraulic flood routing; drainage and flood control facilities design; hydrologic modeling and simulation flood plain analysis and planning.

title: Environmental Engineering Seminar
description: Discussion of current advances in research and practice of environmental engineering.

title: Water Treatment Processes
description: Physical and chemical process principles and their application to water treatment plant design.

title: Wastewater Engineering: Biological Processes
description: Biological process principles and their application to the design of wastewater treatment plants.

title: Geoenvironmental Engineering Design
description: A team design project in geoenvironmental engineering focused on resolving interdisciplinary issues related to containment of pollutants and remediation of soil and groundwater.

title: Travel Demand Modeling
description: Historical development and current status of techniques used in urban transportation planning and travel demand forecasting; trip generation, trip distribution, mode choice, and traffic assignment.

title: Hot Mix Asphalt Mixture Design and Construction
Pavement Design
description: Methods of evaluating the load-carrying capacity of soil subgrade, subbase, and base courses; critical analysis of the methods of design for flexible and rigid pavements; methods of increasing the load-carrying capacity of highway and airport pavements.

title: Traffic Engineering
description: Traffic operations of roads, streets, and highways; traffic engineering studies; use of signs, signals, and pavement markings as traffic control devices; highway and intersection capacity, design and operations of traffic signals; current microcomputer models and applications.

title: Portland Cement Concrete Pavements
description: Portland cement concrete mix design and production. Paving operations. Saw and seal operations. Subgrade preparation. Base selection. Drainage selection, design and construction. Bonded and unbonded concrete overlays. Whitetopping and ultra-thin whitetopping. Concrete pavement restoration; quality assurance and quality control in concrete pavement construction.

title: Geotechnics and Advanced Pavement Design
description: Geotechnical aspects of mechanistic-empirical pavement design (MEPDG). Material characterization in MEPDG. Models relating mechanistic pavement responses to pavement performance. Applications of current mechanistic-empirical design methods.

title: Land Development for Civil Engineers and Planners
description: Engineering problems involved in site planning and design. The comprehensive planning process, zoning and subdivision regulations; design and location of streets and highways, water supply and sanitary facilities, drainage and public utilities.

title: Research in Civil Engineering
description: Original investigation or advanced study in some field related to the practice of civil engineering.

title: Advanced Mechanics of Materials and Applied Elasticity
description: Two- and three-dimensional deformation analysis , equilibrium, and elastic constitutive laws. Stress-strain transformations between coordinate systems. Advanced topics in bending, shearing, torsion and combined loads, with applications to engineering problems.

title: Numerical and Analytic Techniques for Engineers
description: Theory and application of linear algebra, differentiation, integration, optimization and complex variables to a broad range of engineering problems; introduction to solving partial differential equations using finite difference, finite element and boundary element methods.

title: Applied Geostatistics
description: Analysis of spatially-correlated data. Univariate, bivariate, and spatial description; global and point estimation; random function models; stationarity; intrinsic hypothesis; semivariogram; correlogram; ordinary and block kriging; cross validation; cross correlation and cokriging; experimental design.

title: Selected Topics in Civil Engineering
description: Study of intermediate level topics of interest in civil engineering. Topics announced when offered.

title: Shear Strength and Slope Stability of Soils
description: Advanced theories of soil strength; basic and advanced laboratory strength tests; analytical and numerical methods of slope stability analysis.

title: Engineering Properties of Cohesive Soils
description: Mineralogy and structures of clay minerals; fabric and bonding of the clay particles; compressibility and strength characteristics of clays; moisture effects, retention, and movement through clay.

title: Strength and Deformation of Geomaterials
description: Mathematical and physical modeling in geotechnical engineering. Advanced laboratory testing of soils. Plasticity, dilatancy, failure and critical state. Finite element applications: Laboratory strength tests and/or simple field problems.

title: Environmental Geotechnology
description: Soil/environment and soil/ pollutant interactions; pollutant effect on soil strength and behavior; design and performance of waste containment structures; clay liners, surface seals, and slurry walls; slope stability problems for landfills.

title: Computational Applications in Geo-Systems
description: Application of finite element method to multi-physics and pressure sensitive materials (soils, rocks, concrete and asphalt); formulation of 1D element; overview of elasticity (isotropic and anisotropic); theory of plasticity (Drucker-Prager and Cam Clay models); finite element application to stresses in the soil, shear strength, consolidation, shallow and deep foundations, retaining walls, mechanically stabilized earth and groundwater seepage.

title: Advanced Seepage and Settlement Analysis in Soils
description: Hydraulic conductivity; 2D steady state seepage analysis with application to dams and sheet piles; method of fragments; stress distribution in soil masses; consolidation and creep theories; advanced study of soil compressibility and settlement; application of numerical methods to seepage and consolidation problems.

title: Advanced Structural Analysis II
description: Nonlinear structural analysis using classical solutions; nonlinear geometry analysis by matrix methods for trusses and frames; nonlinear material analysis by matrix methods for trusses and frames; solution of nonlinear equilibrium equations; modeling structural problems by commercial software to predict ultimate capacity and nonlinear response.

title: Strengthening Design of Reinforced Concrete with Fiber Reinforced Polymers
description: The course introduces advanced aspects of the analysis and design of reinforced concrete beams and columns (3 weeks), it then covers the mechanical properties of FRP composites used in CE applications (2 weeks), it then covers the flexural strengthening analysis and design of reinforced concrete beams with FRP including the formulation for various failure modes (5 weeks), it them addresses the shear strengthening analysis and design of reinforced concrete beams with FRP (2 weeks), it then covers the strengthening and FRP wrapping of reinforced concrete columns with FRP (3 weeks) and finally it describes the practical methods of the proper installation and inspection of FRP (1 week).

title: Energy Methods and Applied Variational Principles
description: Theory and applications of virtual work, minimum potential, and variational principles using generalized coordinates, displacements, and forces to derive and solve advanced problems in structural, soil and hydrodynamic problems.

title: Structural Stability
description: Analysis of flexible members. Linear and nonlinear buckling of beams, frames, plates and complicated structural systems; post buckling behavior of steel structures.

title: Prestressed Concrete Design
description: Study of prestressing methods including strength and load-balancing approaches and their application to the analysis and design of beams, slabs, and axially loaded members. Flexural, shear, torsion, and anchorage-zone analysis. Study of deflection and time-dependent losses.

title: Analysis and Design of Folded Plate Structures
Analysis of Groundwater Flow
description: Principles of flow through porous media; applications of flow theory to well analysis and design; groundwater resource evaluation and regional groundwater systems analysis.

title: Advanced Civil Engineering Design Using Gis
description: Investigating the usage of Geographic Information Systems (GIS) technology to better solve engineering problems. Teams of students will use GIS to design solutions to various civil engineering related situations.

title: Environmental Engineering Chemistry
description: Chemical kinetics and equilibria, acid-base chemistry, complex formation, precipitation and dissolution processes, and applications to the analysis of environmental engineering problems.

title: Sustainable Resource Recovery Technologies
description: This course covers established as well as developing technologies for recovering valuable products from various biomass types (woody, herbaceous, energy crop residues, food wastes, municipal solid wastes, wastewater sludge, livestock manure, etc.)

title: Water Supply And Wastewater Collection Systems
Advanced Wastewater Treatment
description: Recent advances in municipal and industrial wastewater treatment. Wastewater microbiology, wastewater biochemistry, applications of biotechnology in wastewater engineering, recent advances in nutrient removal processes, wastewater treatment technologies, and water reclamation.

title: Advance Environmental Microbiology and Techniques
description: Provides a general introduction to the diverse roles of microorganisms in natural and artificial environments, and their engineering applications.  Topics including fundamentals of microbiology, microbial growth and metabolism, microbiology of engineered environmental systems, and laboratory techniques relevant to environmental microbiology are covered..

title: Chemicals in the Environment: Fate and Transport
description: Provides an understanding of processes that govern the behavior of both hazardous and naturally-occurring substances in the environment.  Mass transport (such as advection, dispersion, and diffusion), surface and groundwater hydrology, physical/chemical/biological degradation processes, and air pollution modeling are covered. Phase equilibrium and partitioning in environmental systems will be discussed as well as interphase mass transfer and modeling.

title: Transportation Safety
description: Importance of transportation safety, crash data collection, common crash databases, traffic safety studies, accident data analysis, identification of high crash locations, traffic control devices as related to safety, special population group safety, traffic conflict studies, accident reconstruction, statistical methods in crash data analysis and traffic calming.

title: Airport Design
description: Planning and design of a regional airport, including site selection in conformance with state and federal regulations; layout and design of runway system; size and layout of terminal buildings, landside facilities, parking lots, and circulation system.

title: Sustainable Transportation Asset Management
description: Recent advances in transportation asset management system. Inventory and condition data collection, life cycle analysis, prioritization, optimization, and implementation.

title: Traffic Flow Theory
description: Theory of traffic flow, traffic stream characteristics, car following models, shock wave analysis, queuing analysis, application of statistical methods to traffic engineering problems, traffic simulation.

title: Graduate Seminar in Civil Engineering
description: Discussion of current advances and research in civil engineering.

title: Master's Report
description: Topics selected with approval of major professor and department head.

title: Master's Thesis
description: Topics selected with approval of major professor and department head.

title: Advanced Topics in Civil Engineering
description: A course reserved for study of current topics in civil engineering.

title: Advanced Topics in Geotechnical Engineering
description: Advanced study of selected topics in geotechnical engineering.

title: Structural Dynamics
description: Analysis of structures subject to dynamic loadings. Optimization of structural systems to minimize earthquake hazards.

title: Theory of Plates and Shells
description: Equations and solutions of bending of thin plates of various edge conditions and shapes. Membrane and bending theory of shells of revolution. Nonlinear theory of plates and shells.

title: Advanced Topics in Structural Engineering
description: Advanced study of selected topics in structural engineering.

title: Physicochemical Processes
description: Advanced study of physical and chemical processes in the movement and removal of particulates and organics in natural and engineered systems.

title: Advanced Topics in Environmental and Water Resources Engg
description: Advanced study of selected topics in environmental and water resources engineering.

title: Advanced Topics in Transportation and Materials
description: Advanced study of selected topics in transportation engineering and civil engineering materials.

title: Dissertation Research
description: Topics selected with approval of major professor and department head.

title: Civil And Construction Engineering Technology Seminar
Materials Sampling and Testing
description: A course in the proper use of aggregates and concrete materials (Portland cement and asphalt) in construction. Sampling and testing methods conform with current testing standards.

title: Plane Surveying
description: A beginning course in the theory and practice of field measurements and notes for surveying. Emphasis is placed on accuracy and avoidance of common errors and mistakes.

title: Problem Solving with Computer Applications
description: In depth preparation with computer operating systems, spreadsheets, scheduling software and Visual Basic for construction applications.

title: Soils & Foundations
description: A course in the identification and classification of soils by the Unified method and the American Association of State Highway and Transportation Officials method. Routine field tests are covered and used in the laboratory.

title: Construction Safety
description: How to maintain safe working areas within unsafe working conditions. Particularly the recognition and prevention of unsanitary, dangerous, or hazardous conditions to workers in the construction industry. Instruction focuses on federal safety standards related to construction.

title: Construction Methods and Estimating
description: A study of the basic equipment needs, usage, costs, and quantity determinations for planning and estimating construction projects. Field trips through construction sites and visitations with inspectors assist in developing reporting procedures and inspection responsibilities.

title: Topics in Construction Engineering Technology
description: Provides an opportunity for faculty to present construction engineering technology topics.

title: Transportation Systems
description: A study of transportation systems with emphasis on traffic operations and control, planning, design, and drainage for highways, and urban roadways.

title: Structural Design
description: A course combining design of components of structures in steel and reinforced concrete. Basic stress calculations and design concepts are studied for use in either a simplified design, detailing, or inspection role.

title: Construction Materials
description: Study and analysis of construction materials, their properties, selection and use.

title: Route Location Surveying
description: A course in the geometric methods of horizontal and vertical curve alignment. In addition, transitional spirals are examined and calculated. The laboratory portion provides a grounding of these concepts in the field by actual calculation and staking of control for roads, streets, and various types of routes.

title: Mechanical And Electrical Systems
description: The study of the use of mechanical and electrical systems within constructed buildings. Students develop skills related to plumbing, heating, air conditioning, wiring, power, and lighting systems.

title: Mechanical Systems
description: The way mechanical systems, including plumbing, heating, ventilation, and air conditioning are used by contractors to construct a building.

title: Electrical Systems
description: The way electrical systems, including design and wiring, and power and lighting systems are used by contractors to construct a building.

title: Site Construction
description: Study of site construction problems and procedures, site survey and investigations, review of site plans, construction layouts, earthwork calculation, excavation/shoring methods, and computer applications.

title: Construction Techniques and Detailing
description: Study of construction methods and procedures in the assembly of building materials.

title: Parent-Child Interaction: Theory and Research
description: Developmental theories and empirical research concerning the reciprocal interactions between parents and their children focusing on the socialization of the child within the family.

title: Family Systems in Cultural Context
description: Helps students integrate an understanding of individuals, couples, and families within the context of culture. Helps participants to listen and respond in a thoughtful and sensitive manner to the individuals and families they serve professionally, especially those from diverse cultural backgrounds. Students will become more aware of their own cultural memberships, and develop a framework for appreciating the cultural context of the profession of family therapy.

title: Clinical Theory and Practice A
description: Introduction to the foundation of family therapy. A survey of the first half of major schools of family therapy.

title: Human Sexuality
description: Focus on implications of personal and familial aspects of human sexuality throughout the life cycle.

title: Sex Therapy
description: A focus on the framework and skills for delivering therapy to individuals and couples experiencing difficulties with their own sexuality or with sexual functioning in their relationships.

title: Pre-Practicum in Couple and Family Therapy
description: Introduction to master's program in couple and family therapy, including clinic policies and clinical observations. Restricted to students admitted to the couple and family therapy program.

title: Pre-Practicum in Couple and Family Therapy II
description: Preparation for screening into the clinical practicum in couple and family therapy and participation in clinic research program.

title: Systematic Treatment of Trauma Violence and Substance Abuse
description: Framework and skills for helping individuals and families experiencing domestic violence and/or substance abuse with emphasis on assessment and treatment.

title: Couples and Sex Therapy
description: Examination of couple processes and clinical approaches to couple intervention and treatment, including evidence based approaches. The course will include a specific focus on contextual and systemic dynamics affecting couple relationships as well as the treatment of couples experiencing difficulties with their own sexuality or with sexual functioning in their relationships.

title: Family Therapy
description: Discussion of the primary theories of Couple and Family Therapy which are used in the treatment of whole families who present themselves for therapy.

title: Individual and Family Assessment
description: Assessment of individual and family functioning within multiple contexts; including an emphasis on client focused progress monitoring.

title: Professional Studies in Family Therapy
description: Analysis of professional issues, techniques, and responsibilities associated with working effectively with families in a family therapy setting.

title: Systemic Assessment and Treatment of Psychopathology
description: Examination of the Diagnostic and Statistical Manual of Mental Disorders. Exploration of relational diagnoses and the interaction of individual disorders and relational functioning. Review of empirically supported treatments for individual and relational disorders.

title: Practicum in Couple and Family Therapy
description: Supervised experience in couple and family therapy. Designed for master's level students.

title: Clinical Intervention with Parents and Children
description: The course will examine the history of play therapy including the theories from which play therapy evolved, attachment theory, the fundamentals of play therapy, use of assessment with children and adolescents, sand tray play therapy, use of play therapy in the family context and special topics.

title: Clinical Project
description: Integration of clinical work and course work resulting in a final clinical project.

title: Readings in Couple and Family Therapy
description: Implications of research findings in preparation for professional work in counseling, teaching, and research in couple and family therapy.

title: Clinical Theory and Practice B
description: A survey of the second half of major schools of family therapy.

title: MS Research in Couple and Family Therapy
description: Individual research problems in couple and family therapy which may form the basis for the master's thesis or report.

title: Topics in Couple and Family Therapy
description: Examination of recent research, theory, and clinical practice related to couple and family therapy.

title: Advanced Research Methods in Human Sciences: SEM I
description: Advanced methodology course that provides foundational knowledge of quantitative methods in path analysis, confirmatory factor analysis, structural equation modeling, actor-partner interdependence modeling, testing mediation and moderation, and latent growth modeling to answer complex systemic research questions important to the discipline.

title: Advanced Research Methods in Human Sciences: SEM II
description: Advanced research methodology course that explores complex issues related to research in human sciences, using structural equation modeling for management of non-normal data and analysis of longitudinal, dyadic, and multiple-group data.

title: Specialization and Leadship
description: Seminar focusing on the development of specialization in research, teaching, and applied work.

title: Practicum in CFT Research
description: Student will work with a faculty member to complete a research project and submit it to a journal.

title: Advanced Research Family Methods
description: Analysis of a variety of family research methods.

title: Supervision of Couple and Family Therapy
description: Preparation of experienced couple and family therapists for supervision roles within educational, medical and agency settings.

title: PhD Practicum in Couple and Family Therapy
Practicum in Supervision in Couple and Family Therapy
description: Supervised experience in supervision of couple and family therapy.

title: Advanced Clinical Theory
description: Exploration of couple and family therapy clinical theories and the process of integrating these theories into a personalized model for utilization in both therapy and supervision.

title: Conjoint and Group Techniques in Family Counseling
description: Advanced theory in couple and family counseling with emphasis on group techniques.

title: Dissertation Proposal Seminar
description: Presentation and discussion of proposals for dissertation research in human development and family science.

title: Doctoral Research in Couple and Family Therapy
description: Doctoral Research in Couple and Family Therapy

title: Current Topics in Chemical Engineering
description: Recent advances in chemical engineering and the impact of the profession on society.

title: Chemical Process Analysis
description: An introduction to chemical engineering with emphasis on material and energy balances.

title: Basic Concepts in Materials Science and Engineering
description: Structure of materials with crystal structures of metals and their defects emphasized.  Two component phase diagrams, multiphase equilibrium, and the microstructures that result.

title: Fundamentals of Mechanical Properties
description: Overview of the mechanical properties of materials, especially metals, and the modification of these properties through changes in microstructure.  Effect of heat treatment on microstructure and mechanical properties.

title: Fundamentals of Electrical Properties
description: Overview of the electrical properties of materials including conductors, semiconductors, and dielectrics, and their basis in the band theory of solids.  Multiphase equilibrium and modification of properties through changes in microstructure.

title: Computational Techniques in Chemical Engineering
description: Use of computational techniques in chemical engineering.

title: Computational Techniques in Chemical Engineering II
description: Application and reinforcement of principles and concepts for problem-solving chemical engineering challenges using mathematical models and numerical methods. Computational tools and software packages covered in the course include Python and/or MatLab and the chemical process simulator Aspen Plus.

title: Biomaterials
description: This course provides an overview of interactions between materials and biological systems, techniques to assess biomaterial characteristics, and the role of biomaterial selection during the design of medical devices for select applications.

title: Undergraduate Research in Chemical Engineering
description: Open to students pursuing undergraduate research project.

title: Chemical Engineering Thermodynamics I
description: A study of the first and second laws of thermodynamics, real gases, heat of solution and reaction.

title: Chemical Engineering Thermodynamics II
description: A continuation of the study of the second law, thermodynamic analysis of processes, phase equilibrium, and chemical reaction equilibrium.

title: Transport Phenomena I
description: A unified treatment of the basic principles of momentum, energy, and mass transport.

title: Transport Phenomena II
description: Continuation of Transport Phenomena I with special emphasis on mass transfer.

title: Transport Phenomena Laboratory
description: Laboratory experiments in momentum, heat, and mass transfer.

title: Unit Operations Laboratory
description: Laboratory experiments on classical unit operations, e.g., distillation, absorption, extraction, and on chemical kinetics and process dynamics.

title: Chemical Reaction Engineering
description: Applied chemical kinetics and catalysis including the analysis and design of tubular, packed bed, stirred tank, and fluidized bed chemical reactors.

title: Separational Process Design
description: Development of the basic theory and design of separational processes such as distillation, gas absorption, liquid extraction, adsorption, and ion exchange.

title: Chemical Process Dynamics and Control
description: A study of the unsteady state behavior and control of chemical processes.

title: Health and Safety in Chemical Engineering
description: Fundamentals of chemical process safety, particularly as they apply to process design. Topics will include toxicology and industrial hygiene, fires and explosions, runaway reactions, relief systems, process hazards identification, risk assessment, and inherently safer design.

title: Chemical Engineering Systems Design I
description: Basic concepts of process economics and process optimization with application to the synthesis and design of chemical processing systems.

title: Chemical Engineering Systems Design II
description: The solution of comprehensive systems design problems.

title: Problems in Chemical Engineering or Materials Science
description: An introduction to chemical engineering research.

title: Honors Research in Chemical Engineering
description: Individual research problem selected with approval of faculty advisor.  Open to students in the College of Engineering honors program. A report is presented orally and in writing during the last semester.

title: Bioseparations
description: Engineering principles of unit operations such as cell lysis and extraction, precipitation, centrifugation, membrane filtration, and chromatography and analytical methods used in bioprocess development.

title: Nanotechnology in Chemical Engineering
description: This course has been taught as a topics course, and will now be given a specific course number for its future offerings.

title: Molecular Modeling
description: This course focuses on introducing basic concepts in chemical engineering and chemistry related to thermodynamics, Kinetics, Chemistry, Surface Science, Catalysis, Materials Science in the context of molecular-level modeling. Students will also gain hands-on experience in using common simulation tools and high-performance computing.

title: Microbial Biotechnology
description: Continued advancements in microbiology have enabled the ability to use microorganisms for engineering applications. Thus, microbial biotechnology is a rapidly expanding area that has potential to improve agriculture productivity, provide renewable sources of energy, and improve the health of plants, animals, and humans, among other applications. This course will highlight these research areas and will emphasize chemical engineering concepts found within these systems.

title: Protein Engineering and Applications
description: This course offers a comprehensive introduction to protein engineering, covering the fundamentals of protein chemistry, biology, and the principles of protein design. It also explores real-world applications of engineered proteins and their manufacturing processes.

title: AI Applications in Chemical and Bioengineering
description: Application of data-driven, symbolic, and hybrid AI for modeling, monitoring, control, design, and optimization of chemical and bioengineering systems.

title: Fundamentals of Conversion of Biorenewable Resources
description: An introduction to the conversion of biorenewable resources into biobased products and biofuels.

title: Life Cycle Assessment
description: Introduction to the life cycle assessment methodology for quantifying environmental impacts of products, processes, and systems. Topics include life cycle methodology, data inventory process and databases, life cycle impact modeling approaches and software, uncertainty quantification, and practical applications to demonstrate life cycle assessment as a tool for making decisions to improve sustainability of materials, products, and system

title: Hazardous Waste Engineering Seminar
description: Topics in hazardous materials management and control, waste reduction and minimization, hazardous substance tracking, and hazardous waste engineering.

title: Polymer Science and Engineering
description: An introduction to polymeric materials, including chemistry, structure and formation; physical states and transitions; and, basic physical and mechanical properties.

title: Processing of Materials For Solid State Devices
description: Structure, properties and processing of materials for solid state devices. Crystal growth, epitaxy, oxidation, diffusion, lithography, and etching as applied to device fabrication.

title: Environmental and Ecological Risk Assessment
description: Examination of processes and methodologies associated with human environmental and ecological risk assessments. Application of the methods learned to a project to gain experience in defining and quantifying uncertainty associated with human perturbation, management and restoration of environmental and ecological processes.

title: Sustainability Seminar
description: Topics in environmental sustainability, green engineering, life cycle analysis, sustainable development, and sustainability science.

title: Engineering Materials II
description: The structure and bonding in crystalline and amorphous materials; crystallography; thermodynamic stability in materials; equilibrium diagrams and the phase rule; rate theory and kinetics of solid-state transformations; mechanical behavior of engineering materials; dislocations; failure mechanisms.

title: Surface Phenomena
description: Principles and applications of interfacial phenomena, including capillarity, colloids, porosity, adsorption, and catalysis.

title: Biochemical Engineering
description: The analysis and design of biochemical processing systems with emphasis on fermentation kinetics, continuous fermentations, aeration, agitation, scale up, sterilization, and control.

title: Advanced Chemical Engineering Thermodynamics
description: Advanced topics in thermodynamics, with emphasis on a chemical and physical equilibria and the estimation of thermodynamic properties.

title: Chemical Engineering Analysis
description: The mathematical formulation of problems in chemical engineering using partial differential equations, vector and tensor notation. Solution of these problems by analytical and numerical techniques.

title: Air Quality Seminar
description: Topics in air quality including health effects, toxicology, measurement, characterization, modeling, management, and control.

title: Selected Topics in Materials Science
description: Areas of current interest in materials including solidification, transformations, solutions, dislocations, creep, fracture, failure analysis, and failure prevention.

title: Selected Topics in Biochemical Engineering
description: Subjects of current interest in the broadest sense of biochemical engineering.
