title: Computer Applications in Engineering and Construction
description: Fundamental applications of computer programming and software using engineering and construction problems, primarily advanced use of spreadsheets with macros, data tables, embedded graphics, and presentations.

title: Construction Materials
description: Study and analysis of construction materials, their properties, selection, and use.

title: Statics A
description: Composition and resolution of forces; equilibrium of force systems; application of the principles of statics to problems, including force analyses of simple structures.

title: Strength of Materials and Analysis
description: Study of behavior of materials subjected to tension, compression, shear, and bending; analysis of beams and columns.

title: Strength of Materials Lab
description: Tests to determine the physical properties of various structural materials. Analysis and interpretation of test data.

title: Introduction to Building Information Modeling
description: Introduction and application of building information modeling as a technology tool related to the management of the building process for projects in the engineering and construction industry.

title: Graphic Communications II
description: Study of construction methods and procedures in the assembly of building materials.

title: Construction Documents
description: A study of reading, understanding, and analysis of documents used in construction: building codes, accessibility standards, specifications, drawings, shop drawing submittals and other related documents.

title: Site Construction
description: Study of site construction problems and procedures, site survey and investigations, review of site plans, construction layouts, earthwork calculation, excavation/shoring methods, computer applications.

title: Construction Methods and Equipment
description: Operations, costs, productivity of construction equipment. Investments/life cycle costing of the equipment. Equipment selection criteria and analysis. Construction methods.

title: Undergraduate Research Experience
description: Open to students pursuing undergraduate research projects.

title: Honors Research in Construction Science
description: Individual research problem selected with approval of faculty advisor. A report is presented orally and in writing during the last semester.

title: Technology for Management of the Construction Process
description: Fundamentals and application of computer aided technology in the management of the construction process.

title: Steel and Timber Construction
description: Principles of design, fabrication, and erection of structural steel and timber structures in conformance with codes.

title: Concrete and Masonry Construction
description: Principles of design, fabrication, and erection of concrete and masonry structures.

title: Heating and Air Conditioning
description: Principles of designing, applying, and installing heating and air conditioning systems for buildings.

title: Electrical and Lighting
description: Basic design and construction of building electrical, lighting, and distribution systems with emphasis on the National Electrical Code and installation.

title: Water Supply and Plumbing
description: Principles and practices of the design and layout of plumbing and fire protection, and individual waste treatment systems for buildings including code requirements and estimating.

title: Ethics & Professional Standards
description: An application of the concepts of ethics as applied to the professional standards of the design and construction industry.

title: Problems in Construction Science
description: A study of specific design problems under the direct supervision of a member of the construction science faculty.

title: Heavy Construction Methods
description: Principles of asphalt, asphalt and concrete paving operations, concrete batch plant operations, heavy construction equipment and applications.

title: Construction Safety
description: Introduction to safety and safety programs, workers' compensation, OSHA organization and structure, safety policies and record keeping, safety standards. Emphasis will be on communication and job-site safety management. On-site safety inspections will be required within class presentation and written reports to be submitted.

title: Pre-Engineered Metal Buildings
description: Pre-engineered metal building systems: history, components, erection procedures and sequencing, delivery considerations, quality control, safety, and quantity surveying and plan reading.

title: Precast and Prestressed Concrete Construction Management
description: Basics of concrete, principles of precast and prestressed concrete, construction operations of transportation and commercial projects, plant manufacturing, site erection, and safety procedures, and business management of a precast manufacturing plant.

title: Tilt-up Concrete Structures in Construction Management
description: Basics of concrete, principles of tilt-up concrete, construction operations of tilt-up projects, planning procedures for job site erection, and safety procedures.

title: Building Systems Installation and Commissioning
description: Principles and methods of proper procedures for installing, commissioning and maintaining efficient performance of building envelope and support systems including mechanical and electrical systems.

title: Construction Estimating
description: Understanding estimating procedures, quantity surveying, specification reviews, pricing of an estimate, market analysis, subcontractor and supplier solicitation, and risk management, following the CSI format.

title: Construction Management
description: An introduction to the business of construction; study of legal considerations, contract documents, bonds and insurance. Evaluation of the characteristics of the construction firm, organization structure, and financial performance.

title: Preconstruction Services/Design Build
description: Application of Value Engineering to the construction process, Conceptual Estimating, Onscreen take-off quantity surveying, Design-Build. Other project delivery types and their impact on the preconstruction efforts including general contractor pricing, fees, contingencies, risk, and schedule impacts.

title: Topics In Construction Management
description: Topical material of importance in the management of construction such as marketing, ethics, personnel management, etc.

title: Construction Scheduling and Cost Control
description: Construction cost reporting and control. Construction planning, both long-term and short-interval, construction scheduling, monitoring, and controlling. Computer application.

title: Construction Financial Management
description: Principles and applications of financial management for the construction professional. Includes financial statements, cost control, cash flow, and risk management.

title: Current Trends in Construction
description: Broad overview of relevant topics in the construction industry in the areas of project management, field supervision, estimating, contracting, and various other special topics, taught by industry professionals who specialize in their area of expertise.

title: Construction Operations
description: Shop drawing and submittal processes, field and office practices, change orders, construction safety standards and practice, pre-construction planning, expediting, short-interval planning.

title: Mechanical Estimating
description: Principles and practices of mechanical and plumbing building systems estimating. Procedure for evaluating relative costs of different plumbing, fire protection, and HVAC systems.

title: Electrical Estimating
description: Principles and practices of electrical building systems estimating. Procedure for evaluating relative costs of different electrical and lighting systems.

title: Teaching Public Speaking I and Ia
Public Speaking 1A
description: Alternate to COMM 106. Principles and practice of message preparation, audience analysis, presentational skills, and speech criticism.

title: Public Speaking l
description: Principles and practice of message preparation, audience analysis, presentational skills, and speech criticism permitting greater practice in oral presentation.

title: Public Speaking 1A, Honors
description: Honors speech preparation and delivery; a survey of topics basic to rhetoric, communication, and linguistics.

title: Introduction to Human Communication
description: An introduction to the traditions, foundations and contexts of human communication that are studied and practiced in society.

title: Interpersonal Communication
description: Examination of the dynamics of face-to-face interpersonal interaction. Focus is on applying principles of relational communication.

title: Forensics, Debate, or Mock Trial Participation
description: Semester-long participation in K-State Debate, K-State Speech, or K-State Mock Trial.

title: Introduction to Trial Advocacy
description: A hands-on introduction to communication in the legal process, with a primary focus on basic techniques attorneys use to develop and present a persuasive case.

title: Business and Professional Speaking
description: Principles and practice of speaking in an organizational setting. Areas of emphasis will be oral reports, interviewing, interpersonal communication, and working in groups.

title: Theories in Human Communication
description: Survey of basic theories of human communication focusing on sending, receiving, and responding to messages face-to-face.

title: Advanced Public Speaking
description: Advanced principles and practices of speech composition, audience adaptation, and delivery.

title: Nonverbal Communication
description: Analysis of nonverbal communication in human interaction; theory and research in kinesics, proxemics, and paralinguistics.

title: Argumentation and Debate
description: Basic theories of argumentation with emphasis on the construction and criticism of well reasoned and supported positions.

title: Group Communication
description: Builds conceptual knowledge and practical skills in group communication, enabling students to be more effective group members, leaders, and organizers.

title: Professional Interviewing
description: Investigation of interviewing as it occurs in a variety of situations, including journalistic, diagnostic, persuasive, and managerial. Emphasis on developing practical skills in planning, managing interviews, and interpreting data in the professional context.

title: Rhetoric in Civic Life
description: Introduction of rhetorical theory and application of rhetorical concepts to personal experiences of and participation in civic life.

title: Communication & Technology
description: Focus on social and interpersonal topics, including how emerging media technologies are understood and the history of forms of communication technology via the Internet and mobile devices. Additional topics include diversity, political and civic engagement, intercultural communication, impression formation and management in online contexts, and relational maintenance.

title: Sophomore Honors Seminar
description: Open only to qualified students in the arts and sciences honors program.

title: Intercollegiate Forensics, Debate, or Mock Trial
description: Current practices, theories, and involvement for competitive intercollegiate forensics, debate, or mock trial activity.

title: Gender Communication
description: A study of gender as a variable in interpersonal, organizational, and public communication processes.

title: Organizational Communication
description: A survey of processes, practices, and theories of organizing and organizational communication to build conceptual knowledge and practical skill in communicating effectively in organizational contexts.

title: Coaching and Directing Speech Activities
description: Current practices in coaching curricular and extra-curricular speech activities with practical experience in the problems and procedures of directing a forensic program.

title: Freedom of Speech
description: A study of communication and legal principles pertaining to freedom of expression, and an examination of their implications for competing interests such as public order, national security, morality, civil rights, and fairness.

title: Criticism of Public Discourse
description: An examination of public influence based on study of historical and contemporary models of rhetorical criticism. The students' critical experiences will focus on a broad array of public discourse including political, social, and cultural messages.

title: The Rhetoric of the American Presidency
description: An examination of the American presidency from a rhetorical perspective, emphasizing the symbolic resources and duties of the office and those who hold it. Special attention paid to the public discourse of recent presidents during moments of national crisis.

title: Communication Research Methods
description: This course provides an overview of the concepts, methods, and tools by which communication research is designed and conducted.  Methods covered in this course include: focus groups, surveys, experiments, and content analysis.  By the end of this term, students should be able to critically consume communication research, understand the process of using social science to solve communication problems, and have experience in conducting your own research project.

title: Rhetoric of Social Movements
description: A study of the scope and functions of persuasive communication in contemporary social movements.

title: Political Communication
description: A study of political discourse. Attention is directed to theory that encompasses political discourse as it affects political behavior.

title: Collaborative Communication and Event Planning
description: Study of the role communication plays in collaborative interactions, including program/conference/event planning and civic commemorations. Application of organizational communication theory through planning, analysis, deconstruction, and critique of various collaborative events.

title: Theories of Human Communication Networks
description: Examines fundamental social network theories and concepts to understand how human communication networks and relational connections shape, constitute, and influence everyday life in organizational, political, relational, and civic contexts.

title: Special Studies in Human Discourse
description: A study of selected subjects in the analysis in human communication.

title: Advanced Trial Advocacy
description: Advanced study in theory and techniques of courtroom persuasion, emphasizing the complexities of case analysis, courtroom procedures, theme development, and the use of evidence.

title: Communication and Conflict
description: Theoretical and applied research in communication and conflictual situations.

title: Building Social and Cultural Connections
description: Examines beliefs, worldviews, and communication practices related to identity, diversity, and the building of communities of belonging. Develops skills in communicating beliefs and worldviews; listening;  engaging with different perspectives; and using communication processes and platforms to build inclusive and equitable communities.

title: Legal Communication
description: An examination of the vital role communication plays in the practice of law, with an emphasis on communication in the courtroom.

title: Intercultural Communication
description: A study of the relationship between language and culture and its impact on human communication. Examines how language and culture differ among people and how differences are handled through the process of communication.

title: Argumentation Theory
description: An advanced study of prominent argumentation theorists, with an in-depth examination of special topics concerning the philosophy, theory, and practice of argumentation.

title: Persuasion
description: The study of communication as persuasion; examination of contemporary approaches to persuasion.

title: Communication and Leadership
description: A study of the ways in which leadership in differing contexts is defined and exercised through communication. Constructs examined include: credibility, charisma, vision, power, myth, and public memory.

title: Negotiation and Communication
description: Develops conceptual knowledge and skill in negotiation in multiple contexts and examines the constitutive nature of communication in the negotiation process.

title: Relational Communication
description: A survey of theories and research on the role of communication in social and personal relationships. Emphasis is on observing and evaluating communication behaviors using real-life media examples.

title: Communication in Democracy
description: Examines the ways in which communication shapes democratic citizenship and participation in the public sphere. Builds communication skills that promote democratic citizenship and participation.

title: Senior Colloquium
description: A demonstration of the mastery of vocabulary, theory, and the ability to make practical applications of the study of rhetoric and communication will be required of all senior communication studies majors. Mastery will be demonstrated by completing a senior capstone project and presenting the result of that project to the assembled communication studies faculty and majors in a required colloquium.

title: Coaching and Mentoring
description: Develops language, skills, and perspectives on how to coach and mentor diverse stakeholders in personal and professional contexts. Explores coaching and mentoring as a communication process; applies successful coaching and mentoring communication across different contexts; and identifies the appropriateness of different approaches and requirements for stakeholder needs.

title: Internship in Communication Studies
description: Students will work for/within an organization during the course of a semester. Interns must complete a minimum of 100 work hours to earn course credit. The internship work experience is intended to help apply formal classroom education to "real World" work experience. Ideally internships are pre-professional and project based experiences allowing students the opportunity to make meaningful contributions to the organization. Each intern will complete a series of deliverables to the internship director as well as work under a site supervisor at the host organization. Prior to registration, the internship director must approve all internships.

title: Special Topics in Rhetoric and Communication
description: Intensive study of selected topics in communication and rhetoric.

title: Innovation Cultures
description: Examines histories, trends, and trajectories in technological innovation from a communication perspective. Develops familiarity with science and technology studies as a tool for evaluating and participating in innovation cultures.

title: Introduction to Communication Research Methods
description: Introduction to descriptive and experimental methodologies in communication, including conceptualization and operationalization of communication concepts, strategies of research design, and logic of inquiry.

title: Perspectives on Communication
description: Analysis of current perspectives on the communication process. Materials cover assumptions, principles, implications and selected research within each perspective.

title: Seminar in Persuasion
description: Survey and analysis of advanced theory and experimental studies in persuasion.

title: Theories and Research of Organizational Communication
description: Study of how organizations impact our lives through organizational communication.

title: Classical Rhetorical Theory
description: Study of rhetorical theory and criticism from early Greek to Roman times.

title: Contemporary Rhetorical Theory
description: Study of major European and American contributors to rhetorical theory in the twentieth century.

title: Rhetorical Criticism
description: Study of traditional and contemporary approaches to the analysis of public discourse.

title: Deliberation & Dialogue Theory
description: Explores the conceptual and theoretical underpinnings of the Dialogue, Deliberation, and Public Engagement field, including group theory, civic space, and deliberative democracy.

title: Problems in Communication Studies
description: Open to students in any communication studies area.

title: Teaching Public Speaking
description: Seminar for graduate teaching assistants in strategies, techniques, and materials for the introductory public speaking course. Includes current practices and research in communication education.

title: Instructional Communication
description: Study of theory and practice of communication in the classroom including both teacher and student communication. Topics include: integration of modes of communication, language choices, power, humor, communication strategies for instruction, and impact of communication on learning.

title: Research Writing in Communication Studies
description: A study of the problems of writing and rewriting the results of scholarly investigations in communication studies.

title: Graduate Studies in Leadership Communications
description: This course examines the intersections of community-engaged scholarship and leadership through practices of relationship, story, strategy, and action.  Students are introduced to resources and opportunities for pursuing public engagement, community-engaged scholarship, and successful program completion.

title: Seminar in Leadership Communication
description: This seminar provides doctoral students opportunities for collaboration and mentorship with peers, faculty, and visiting scholars, and exploration of career pathways in public engagement.

title: Small Group Communication
description: Review literature and develop research projects pertaining to the communication processes in small task groups. Topics include group communication processes, barriers to group communication, and style-specific theories of effective group communication.

title: Relational Communication
description: A survey of theories and research on the role of communication in social and personal relationships. Emphasis is on observing and evaluating communication behaviors using real-life media examples.

title: Computational Methods in Communication and Media Research
description: Advanced computational methods in communication and media research. Focus on collecting, analyzing, and interpreting digital data patterns, with applications in media, social media, and public discourse.

title: Experimental Research in Communication Studies
description: Descriptive and experimental methodologies pertinent to investigation in communication studies. Topics will include such techniques as content analysis, attitude scaling, and stylistic analysis.

title: Field Research In Communication Studies
description: Critical and interpretive methodologies pertinent to investigations in communication studies. Topics will include participant observation, unstructured interviewing, ethnography, and discourse analysis.

title: Competitive Forensic Theory
description: Theory and study of current research in competitive debate and individual events.

title: Language and Social Interaction
description: Study of the epistemological, social, and behavioral functions of language in communication. Examination of the processes by which language functions to construct one's worldview and guide individual action.

title: History of American Public Address
description: Study of American speakers, from the time of Jonathan Edwards to the present, including their education, major speeches, and contributions to the rhetorical history of the United States.

title: Political & Corporate Speech Writing
description: A study of the principles and practice of professional speechwriting with a special emphasis on the role of speechwriting in a democratic society.

title: Deliberation & Dialogue Process Models
description: Survey of and introduction to the variety of process models for Dialogue, Deliberation, and Public Engagement, including the following:  National Issues Forums, World Cafe, Sustained Dialogue, Appreciative Inquiry, 21st Century Town Hall.

title: Dialogue, Deliberation and Public Engagement: Core Skills and Strategies
description: Instruction in the assessment of situations and application of theoretical and conceptual frameworks for DDPE to real-world contexts and scenarios.

title: Dialogue, Deliberation and Public Engagement: Capstone Experience
description: Focuses on the individualized goals, objectives, and interests of the student, designed to integrate insights from the courses on theoretical foundations, process models, and conceptual frameworks for evaluation and design. Students will meet face-to-face with national leaders to present their research.

title: The Rhetoric of Social Movements
description: Study of the theory and criticism of social movement rhetoric. Topics may include the development of movement studies as a distinct genre of scholarship within the field of communication studies, questions and controversies in the rhetorical study of movements, and the rationale for protest tactics, from nonviolence to terrorism.

title: Leadership Communication
description: A study of the role that public discourse plays in attaining, enacting, and constraining leadership in a democratic society.

title: Practicum in Communication & Conflict
description: Supervised opportunity to apply theoretical information into a self-selected conflict situation in culturally diverse settings

title: Intercultural Communication
description: A study of the relationship between language and culture and its impact on human communication. Examines how language and culture differ among people and how differences are handled through the process of communication.

title: Communication and Technology
description: Explains, analyzes, and evaluates technological infrastructures in the background of social, economic, and political life. Using a keywords based approach, students test varying definitions of communication technology, seek to understand the relationship between technology and culture, examine the intended and unintended consequences of technology use on society, and evaluate future possibilities for our technological society.

title: Approaches to Public/Community Engagement
description: An advanced survey of key concepts and theoretical frameworks associated with deliberative democracy and public engagement. Focus on theoretical and practical community-engaged scholarship.

title: Political Communication
description: Overview of theory and research in the field of political communication. Introduces students to key concepts, research questions, and approaches in the field. Explores how political elites construct their messages and how citizens come to understand their roles in discourse. Topics covered: presidential communication, election campaign discourse, civic discourse and deliberation, and new media and civic/political engagement.

title: Research in Speech
description: A study of the relationship between language and culture and its impact on human communication.

title: Communication Theories and Engagement
description: Focus on the intersections of communication theory and engagement. Evolution of communication theories. Philosophy and theory pertaining to community-engaged research.

title: Social Science Research for Public Problem Solving
description: The study of how social science research and social sector evaluation can be applied to address public problems, including the identification of public problems and the critique of current evaluation methods, and applying concepts by developing an evaluation including quantitative and qualitative analysis in partnership with social sector partners.

title: Research in Leadership Communication
description: Doctoral research demonstrates the candidate's ability to conduct significant original research related to leadership and communication, to analyze the information obtained from the research, and to present the results in a dissertation format appropriate to the field.

title: University Honors Program
description: This course is for record keeping purposes to allow the University Honors Program and the College of Technology and Aviation to monitor and track students who are a part of the Honors Program.

title: Mastering Academic Conversations
description: Actively engage students in the process of becoming successful students, scholars, and citizens. Introduction to a successful university experience through participation in interactive seminar classes and co-curricular events. Events and topics include a broad survey of the humanities, examining the impact on culture, society, and the human condition.

title: The Humanities Through the Arts
description: A general introduction to the humanities, focusing on what they are and their basic importance. Painting, sculpture, architecture, literature, drama, music, dance, film, and photography will be explored. Emphasis will be on participation, involvement, guest speakers, tours, and appreciation.

title: Introduction to Cultural Studies
description: Introductory survey of core concepts in cultural studies and dimensions of culture, including development of the field and contemporary issues.

title: Introduction to University Honors Program
description: An overview of the University Honors Program including directions, goals and student requirements for completion of the program.

title: Introductory Industrial Internship
description: Introductory experiential learning program in an off-campus setting. Written documentation and oral presentation of project goals, experiences, and accomplishments.

title: Academic Success Lab
description: Introduction to and practice in academic study strategies. Topics may include critical thinking, time management, research and information skills, integrity, academic and career planning and goal setting

title: Problems in Arts, Sciences, and Business
description: Opportunity for advanced independent study in specific subject areas in Department of Arts, Sciences, and Business.

title: Six Sigma Analysis and Problem Solving I
description: Introduction to Six Sigma process and quality improvement methods and tools, including the Six Sigma problem-solving approach, voice of the customer, root cause and risk analysis, and process flow improvement. Emphasis on qualitative analysis and introductory statistical tools.

title: Six Sigma Analysis and Problem Solving II
description: Application of common statistical methods for Six Sigma improvement projects, including data acquisition, probability, data distributions, and statistically significant change.  Builds on the foundation from COT 301 Six Sigma Analysis and Problem Solving I.

title: Methods of Problem Solving for Integrated Professional Studies
description: Practice in applying creative problem solving methods to a selection of problems solicited from multiple disciplines. Analysis of student solutions will examine similarities and difference between disciplines with regard to strategic exploration, identifying and validating constraints, testing assumptions, defending choices and approaches, and making decisions. Cases are deliberately chosen such that conventional solutions are not immediately apparent, where procedural algorithms and instructional troubleshooting scripts are inadequate or unavailable, and for which exact solutions might not actually exist.

title: Salina International Experience
description: Seminar and travel course including faculty-led international field experience focused on Salina Campus students' area of interest, with travel followed by a final project. The course is designed to enhance students' global and cultural awareness while adding knowledge and skill in topical issues. In addition, students will be introduced to and immersed in historical components, both disciplinary and cultural.

title: Professional Conduct, Ethics, and Analysis
description: Examines the ethical foundations of professional conduct and decision-making in the workplace. Through reading and discussing ethical works, case studies, and contemporary ethical policies/procedures, students will develop the skills necessary for analysis, discourse, and ethical decision-making in a professional work setting.

title: Survey of Cryptography Development, Principles and Practice
description: A practical survey of the principles and practice of cryptography with respect to business / government / private applications and commercial computer security systems. Benefits of cryptography as a strategic and tactical tool for effective computer security in the commercial theater of operations. Addresses current topics such as personal privacy, cryptocurrency and blockchain. Students interact with selected cryptographic machines, codes, hand-techniques and computer products developed since the beginning of writing.

title: COTA Advanced Applied Learning Experience
description: This course engages students in an intentionally designed applied learning experience. Learners will actively engage in authentic experiences through a substantial investment of time and attention to foster deep learning. Collaboration, goal setting, self-reflection, and assessment of growth are required elements of the course. They will take leadership roles when working with teams to achieve established goals.

title: Advanced Industrial Internship
description: Advanced experiential learning program in an off-campus setting. Written documentation and oral presentation of project goals, experiences, and accomplishments.

title: Undergraduate Research Experience
description: Open to students pursuing undergraduate research projects.

title: Advanced Problems in Arts, Sciences, and Business
description: Opportunity for advanced independent study in specific subject areas in the Department of Arts, Sciences, and Business.

title: Radio Frequency Technology for Unmanned Systems
description: An introduction to the theory and design of electronic circuits for communications emphasizing the implementation and analysis of common radio-frequency (RF) building blocks. Topics include s-parameters, the Smith chart, component behavior, RF test equipment, computer simulation, filter design, impedance matching, amplifiers, oscillators, mixers, and demodulators.

title: Analytical and Computational Tools for Unmanned Systems
description: Ordinary differential equations, vector algebra, vector calculus, partial differential equations and the separation of variables technique for solving wave equations. Students also perform simulation and analysis using software tools including MATLAB and MATHCAD applied to Unmanned Systems.

title: Airport Law
description: A detailed study of how the U.S. regulatory and legal systems work in relation to airport management. This course emphasizes contract law related to the Federal Aviation Administration Airport covenants and restrictions, Federal Aviation Regulation compliance, and airport operator liability.

title: Airport Planning and Management
description: An overview of the Federal Aviation Regulation part 139 airport design standard and airport master planning process. Includes a study of the role of the airport in community development.

title: Aviation Management
description: Provides the Professional Master of Technology student with the opportunity to gain a thorough understanding of basic aviation industry management practices in use today combined with historical perspectives.  Includes case study analysis.

title: UAS Flight Operations Management
description: Best practices for managing complex operations of Unmanned Aircraft Systems (UAS) in the National Airspace System (NAS) including topics on: personnel and training, regulatory/policy considerations, airspace access, operating procedures, mission planning, system acquisitions, safety, customer relations, application-specific considerations, and data handling. Emphasis on the handling of unexpected delays and challenges to normal operations.

title: UAS Program Management
description: Best practices for top-level management of an Unmanned Aircraft Systems (UAS) program, including topics on: strategic planning, business model development, proposals, marketing, customer and government relations, personnel management, data handling and security, export control, regulatory environment, and operating procedures.

title: Processing Techniques for Low-Altitude Remotely Sensed Data
description: Students learn techniques for exploring remotely sensed data using geographic information systems and image processing software. Topics include manipulation of vector and raster data, management of spatial databases, image stitching, georeferencing, orthorectification, the use and interpretation of metadata and ""best practices"" in data management techniques.

title: Acquisition and Advanced Processing of LARS Data
description: Topics begun in COT 674 are further developed. Advanced topics include: multi-spectral data acquisition and processing, exploitation of full-motion video, interpretation of geospatial and remotely sensed data, geostatistical methods of data analysis, photogrammetric measurements, and an introduction to big data concepts.

title: Low-Altitude Remote-Sensing Product/Project Development
description: Building on the skills acquired in COT 674 and 675, students fly a low-altitude remote-sensing mission to obtain geospatial data to process, manipulate, and analyze as the means of developing a finished product in areas of interest to the student. Potential areas of investigation include: precision agriculture, environmental remediation, fire management, wildlife studies, or stewardship of managed ecosystems.

title: Conduct and Ethics for Cybersecurity and UAS Professionals
description: Explores the constitutional, legal, personal and public policy fundamentals associated with the field of cyber data collection, fusion, integration, and UAV surveillance.

title: Advanced Technical Communication
description: Intensive writing practice, applying rhetorical principles to a number of genres common to non-academic professions and workplaces, including oral presentations. Introduction to allied topics such as document design and editing, crafting technical presentations. Application of global information literacy and research methods, culminating in the preparation of a master's project proposal.

title: Applied Research Skills and Methods
description: Survey of qualitative and quantitative research methods; use of a range of tools to develop applied research skills focusing on literature reviews. Examines applied research concepts, methods, and skills to foster enlightened decision making in professional practice.

title: Project Management for Professionals
description: This course focuses on applied project management methodology, tools, and techniques. Topics include career aspects of project management; business factors affecting the project; project organization, planning, execution, and communications; the project life cycle; risk analysis; and best practices in project management.

title: Managerial Finances, Metrics, and Analytics
description: Provides an overview of an organization's financial statements, with an emphasis on the interaction between people in management positions and those statements, as well as an examination of the business investment decision-making process. Explores the use of metrics and analytics to measure and improve managerial performance.

title: Transformational Leadership for Technology
description: Study and application of leadership styles and common leader traits skills and behaviors needed in technology industries and dynamic environments where innovation is a key success factor. Includes application in virtual team environments, and introduces concepts of transformational leadership. This course enables students to build group vision, values, and commitment and to make connections between diverse organizational cultures, leadership styles, and business strategies that enable success in a rapidly changing technology world. Students develop lifelong learning networks and information sources that enable them to continue to grow as leaders.

title: Informatics and Technology Management
description: Provides theoretical and practical experience in using information technology to support organizational decision-making processes. Provides tools in areas such as statistics, research methods, data mining, and information technology to develop solutions tailored to business problems.

title: Advanced Aviation Safety Management
description: This course examines the development of safety and provides the student with an understanding of how safety management has become an important part of any company. Discussions emphasize Safety Management Systems and how this blueprint is becoming an integral part of most organizations. Various governmental and corporate programs will be discussed. Students will be exposed to various management tools and techniques to aid in systematically controlling risk and developing a safety culture mind-set.

title: Systems Project Management
description: This course will steer students through advanced project management that includes both techniques in application portfolio management (APM) and project portfolio management (PPM). Students will construct a Scope Statement, Statement of Work (SoW), and Work Breakdown Structures (WBS). Students practice Project Evaluation and Review Techniques (PERT) and learn the Critical Path Method (CPM). Lastly, they will examine and evaluate the project progression by applying Earned Value Management (EVM).

title: Application of Lean Six Sigma Methods
description: Six sigma and lean tools within an enterprise to improve product and process development, production operations, and service activities.

title: Reliability Centered Maintenance of Plant Equipment
description: This course emphasizes reliability modeling and assessment, reliability-centered maintenance, condition monitoring technologies, and computer tools.

title: Applied Electromagnetics for Unmanned Systems
description: Fundamentals of electromagnetic wave phenomena primarily using transmission line theory to study practical applications such as antennas, cables, and waveguides. Includes a treatment of EMI and related issues.

title: Aerospace Manufacturing and Materials
description: An introduction to aerospace manufacturing and materials processes that focus on understanding manufacturing applied to the production of   aerospace vehicles and associated materials.

title: Professional Master of Technology International Experience
description: Seminar and travel course including faculty-led international field experience focused on Professional Master of Technology students' area of interest, with travel followed by a final project. The course is designed to enhance students' global and cultural awareness while adding knowledge and skill in topical issues.  In addition, students will be introduced to and immersed in historical components, both disciplinary and cultural.

title: Material Properties of Bulk Solids
description: Covers material properties related to bulk solids handling such as particle density, bulk density, particle hardness, surface area, particle size and shape and its distribution, porosity and permeability. Additional material property topics include moisture content, temperatures, RH, explosiveness, cohesion and adhesion, angle of internal friction, wall friction, angle of repose, time consolidation, unconfined yield strength, shear strength and more.  Sampling and measurement techniques of materials are also included.

title: Storage and Flow of Bulk Solids
description: Covers topics related to storage and flow of bulk solids which include design of hopper , bin, chute, and feeders.  A study of solid flow problems such as cohesive arching, ratholing, bridging, and shear tester operational principles is included.

title: Pneumatic Conveying of Bulk Solids
description: Covers topics such as types and components of pneumatic conveying systems and its design principles. Trouble shooting, pipe wear and attrition, dust control are included.

title: Instrumentation and Safety of Bulk Solids Handling
description: Examines various instrumentation used in bulk solids handling operation such as measurement of airflow, pressure, temperature, level and measuring techniques. Different type valves, PLC control and safety are included.

title: Mechanical Conveying of Bulk Solids
description: Covers topics such as types and components of bucket elevator, screw conveyor, drag and belt conveying systems and their design principles. Different aspects of designing each component of these systems with examples and troubleshooting will be discussed.

title: UAS and High Altitude Platforms (HAPS) for Wireless Communications
description: An introductory course to HAPS technology applied in the civilian UAS sector. Covers technologies, communications, and protection of those computer communications from hostile intent. HAPS technology provides wireless narrowband and broadband telecommunications and broadcasting services to users using either aircraft (unmanned (UAS/UAV) or manned) or lighter-than-airships (LTA). Research indicates UAS to be the best alternative. These platforms are reusable and positioned at stratospheric altitudes, from approximately 12.4 miles to 31 miles (20 to 50 km).

title: Capstone in Systems Design and Dynamics
description: Students formulate research and execute a project to gain hands-on experience under expert guidance while integrating knowledge to solve complex problems. Students write a convincing proposal for a capstone experience, gather and analyze data, draw conclusions and present results. Teams of first and second-year students may form to work on real-world projects.

title: Problems in Cybernetics and Technology
description: Opportunity for advanced independent study of a specific topic in one of the following areas: aerospace, artificial intelligence, autonomous systems, data science, cyber systems, engineering, space systems, or other advanced technologies.  Topics selected jointly by student and the instructor.

title: Integrated Systems Thesis
description: This course must be taken in three 2 credit hour courses sequentially connected across three semesters.  With graduate faculty guidance, students will select and finalize a thesis chair and committee, submit the thesis proposal, develop a research roadmap, incorporate research proposal revisions, execute on approved research, make all necessary revisions to the thesis, produce and defend thesis.  The systems thesis framework will focus on a) applied industry research, b) research abstract, and c) publishable research paper.

title: Introduction to Criminology and Criminal Justice
description: An examination of crime and criminal justice. Considers the nature of crime and the academic discipline of criminology. Explores the major components in the American criminal justice system and how these reflect and are affected by norms and changes in our society. Special emphasis is placed on issues of gender, race/ethnicity, and class within crime and criminal justice.

title: Topics in Criminology
description: Supervised independent and/or interdisciplinary study projects for Criminology students.

title: Police and Society
description: Examines in detail the policing function in society and the role police play in the criminal justice process.

title: Criminological Perspectives
description: Addresses basic concepts, theories, and research methods used in the study of crime, with an emphasis on critical perspectives on crime as a social phenomenon. The course provides an overview of the nature and extent of major categories of crime as well as the integration of current issues dealing with crime.

title: Real World Criminology
description: Prepares students for careers in Criminology, including learning more about potential internship placements, occupation options in the field of criminology, graduate school, and job market preparation.

title: Criminal Law and Society
description: Examines the impact of the criminal law on US society, paying special attention to the state's use and justification of violence. Concerns the law as a source of violence and inequality in US society; students will demonstrate critical thinking and reasoning, analyzing the construction of laws and interpreting implications for various groups and segments of society.

title: Youth and Crime
description: Nature, extent, and causes of delinquency; characteristics of delinquents; means of prevention and treatment.

title: The War on Drugs
description: This course charts the history of the US war on drugs, placing it within the context of the war on terror, mass imprisonment and the problem of police violence. Studies how policies of law and crime impact various groups and social positions within the US. Compares US policies to others within a global context.

title: Gangs in American Society
description: Covers the historical development and current status of gangs in America. Focus on gang types/forms, criminal activities and sociological impact on communities. Also examines gang control mechanisms as well as classic and modern research conducted about gangs.

title: The Criminal Justice System and Family Violence
description: A critical examination of the incidence and prevalence of family violence in society, emphasizing how the criminal justice system responds to this social problem.

title: True Crime
description: Critically evaulates the public fascination with violent crime entertainment.

title: Prisons and Punishment
description: The historical development and current status of the correctional system. Major institutional components include jails, prisons, probation, parolee, and other forms of community corrections. Modern issues such as offender and victim rights and electronic monitoring are also covered.

title: Criminal Procedure and Investigations
description: Examines the academic and applied dimensions of criminal investigations and criminal procedure. The course is designed for students interested in learning about the processes and skills involved in evidence gathering, interviews and interrogations, case processing, and preparing cases for court for a variety of criminal offenses.

title: Contemporary Issues in Criminology
description: Analysis of a selected criminological topic of contemporary interest. Topics vary from semester to semester.

title: Cybercrime, Security, and Society
description: Critically examines the social and cultural issues surrounding technology, crime, deviance, and crime control, including hackers, digital activists, online fraudsters, cybersecurity agents, and other actors and subcultures from sociological and criminological perspectives.

title: Sex Crimes & Society
description: Provides critical analysis of background, laws, and practices regarding sex crimes in the US. Includes diverse reference points relative to perpetrators and victims/survivors of sex crimes; legal practices and challenges; and responses related to criminal justice and social justice perspectives.

title: Study of Serial Murder
description: The course critically examines the social construction of serial murder as a phenomenon that has long existed but only recently has been polarized as a concern of the criminal justice system and the public in general. The objective of the course is to synthesize historical and social scientific analysis of serial murder, assess public and media debates centered on both offenders and victims, and evaluate the portrayal of serial murder in contemporary literature and film.

title: Criminology Internship
description: Involves an internship with an approved organization related to criminology or criminal justice.  Includes practical experience, observational research, and a final report. Students will demonstrate an understanding of how social science methods and principles affect policies and practices in criminal justice and related organizations.

title: Field Experience in Criminology
description: Integrates field experience and everyday practices with relevant bodies of sociological and criminological theory and research. Students will develop analytical and interpretive skills to understand implications of criminal law and practices through direct field experience.

title: The Death Penalty
description: Studies data, public opinion, execution patterns and methods, and debates regarding capital punishment as public policy in the U.S. from early history to 21st century.  Examines selected cases and issues such as general deterrence, methods of execution, and constitutional challenges.

title: Criminal Minds
description: Examines interaction between social forces and physical experiences, such as cognitive impairment and/or abuse.  Critically reviews history of criminology and biological theories of crime; explores how remnants of these feature in current explanations of crime.

title: Anatomy of Mass Murder
description: Examines a specific kind of murder, killing of four or more people in a single incident. Examines the killers' pathways, the weapons, and especially the social context of mass murder.

title: Senior Thesis in Criminology
description: Independent research in criminology. Students conduct original research and produce a senior thesis in criminology.

title: Criminal Justice in Context
description: Examines various institutions of contemporary criminal justice operations, focusing primarily on the “three C’s”-cops, courts, and corrections-and adjacent subjects such as law. Emphasis will be given to foundational works that emphasize history, theory, policy, and empirical research.

title: Women and Crime
description: Nature and extent of criminal offending among women and women offenders' interactions with legal and criminal justice systems; women's victimization, including rape and intimate violence; women workers in the criminal justice system, specifically in law, policing, and prison work.

title: Systems Integration Of Small Animal Internal Medicine
Practicing Veterinary Medicine in a Multicultural Society
Veterinary Care and Management of South American Camelids
The Learning Curve of Equine Practice
Advanced Topics in Veterinary Soft Tissue Surgery
Advanced Concepts in Veterinary Clinical Anesthesia
Small Animal Clinical & Critical Care Nutrition
Production Medicine of Small Ruminants
description: Lectures and other exercises emphasizing production medicine of small ruminants.

title: Veterinary Diagnostic Imaging II
description: Students will receive advance training in radiologic interpretation and alternate imaging procedures. Emphasis will be on didactic and hands-on training in ultrasound imaging.

title: Incident Response
description: This introductory course provides students with the training and credentials needed to volunteer as a disaster responder.  In-person lectures classes will introduce students to disaster response as it relates to animals and the role of veterinarians on a local and state level.  Online modules within the Incident Command Systems (ICS) and National Field Response (NFR) will expose veterinary students to the veterinarian's role in ICS and the multiple agencies involved with disaster response on a national level.  Successful completion of the online modules aligns with training required to become certified for disaster response deployment.

title: Medicine and Surgery of South American Camelids
Introduction to Sustainable Beef Systems
description: This course provides the fundamentals of assessing sustainability in beef production systems through evaluation of aspects of sustainability pillars, people, planet and profit, in each sector of the beef value chain. The course focuses on principles of sustainable food systems, beef cattle production practices, systems thinking, and impacts of food choices.

title: Life-Long Learning Skills For Professional Students
Topics in Small Animal Internal Medicine II
description: Various topics in small animal internal medicine will be presented. One topic will be covered in a one-hour conference weekly. In-depth discussion of pathophysiology and recent advances in diagnostics and therapeutics.

title: Small Animal Endoscopy
description: Introduction to the fundamentals of small animal (canine and feline) endoscopy, including respiratory and gastrointestinal endoscopy, laparoscopy and anthroscopy. Thirteen lectures with 2 laboratories will be offered.

title: Advanced Topics in Small Animal Surgery
description: Current and foundational information on techniques, theory, and management in small animal surgery.

title: Wound Management and Reconstruction
description: Didactic lectures on the physiology of wound healing, advanced reconstructive techniques, and wound treatment products and technologies.

title: Applied Production Medicine
description: Advanced training in agricultural production medicine. Emphasis on answering production medicine problems through the appropriate design and interpretation of research models. Course is discussion-based and facilitated by a team of faculty members. Students will be expected to participate in weekly topic discussions.

title: Nephrology/Urology
description: Advanced studies about small animal nephrology/urology (various topics:  Pathophysiology, clinical signs, differential diagnoses, diagnostic approach, and treatment consideration).

title: Interpretation Of Radiologic Studies Of Body Systems
Advanced Small Animal Endocrinology
description: Advanced topics in small animal endocrinology. Emphasis on comparative aspects of endocrine gland disease and its clinical manifestations in dogs and cats. Primary literature, review articles, and advanced texts will be the principle sources of information.

title: Small Animal Clinical & Critical Care Nutrition
Advanced Clinical Gastroenterology
description: Advanced study of gastrointestinal structure and function with discussions of material presented applicable to the general ACVIM board examinations.  Assigned journal articles will cover literature references from sources on the ACVIM reading list, as well as general physiology taken from human literature.

title: Advanced Clinical Hepatology
description: Advanced study of hepatology with discussions of material presented applicable to the general ACVIM board examinations  Assigned journal articles will cover literature references from sources on the ACVIM reading list, as well as general physiology taken from human literature.

title: Res/Medicine
description: An attempted solution of some of the medical and parasitological problems confronting the practitioner of veterinary medicine.

title: Breeding Diseases
description: Advanced studies of the breeding diseases of domestic animals.

title: Interpretation of Radiologic Studies of Body Systems
description: Advanced discussions of radiologic interpretation, indications and interpretation of alternate imaging procedures (MRI, CT, nuclear medicine, ultrasound).

title: Systemic Medicine I
description: Study of the medical aspects of diseases of the urinary, nervous, and integumentary systems and special senses.

title: Systemic Medicine II
description: Study of the medical aspects of diseases of the cardiovascular, respiratory, musculoskeletal and endocrine systems.

title: Clinical Interpretation of Equine Diagnostic Images
description: Graduate veterinary students will meet one hour weekly to review clinical equine images and review pertinent literature.

title: Orthopedic Surgery
description: Fundamentals, theory, and practice concerning genetic, metabolic, infectious, neoplastic, and traumatic diseases of bones and joints.

title: Clinical Science Seminar
description: A seminar for all house officers and all Clinical Sciences graduate students.

title: Theriogenology
description: Consideration of prevention, diagnosis, and treatment of disease, and maintenance of health and productivity of the genital tract of domestic animals.  Three hours of lecture a week.

title: Respiratory Finction In Health And Disease
Artificial Intelligence in Veterinary Medicine
description: This elective course explores the impact of artificial intelligence on the field of medicine, with a focus on veterinary applications. Students will learn about the fundamentals of AI, machine learning, and data science, and how these technologies are used to improve diagnostics, treatment planning, patient monitoring, and overall patient care. The course will also address ethical considerations, regulatory frameworks, and the future of AI in healthcare. Through lectures, case studies, and hands-on projects, students will gain a comprehensive understanding of AI's role in advancing medical and veterinary practices.

title: Equine Diagnostic Imaging
description: This course is offered to students who have an interest and career focus in equine medicine, surgery and emergency care.  This course will build on the basic knowledge including imaging of the axial and appendicular skeleton, the thorax and abdomen.  Whilst not the primary focus of the elective course, there will be some introduction to additional, complementary diagnostic imaging modalities used to work up the equine patient including ultrasound, magnetic resonance imaging and computed tomography.

title: Diagnostic Methods in Feedlot Management
description: Practical experience in feedlot operation and bovine necropsy diagnosis consisting of 40 hours in bovine necropsy and 320 hours of an on-location practicum in a cattle feedlot.

title: Fundamentals Of Feedlot Health And Management
Exotic Animal And Wildlife Medicine Clinical Rotation
Clinical Science Problems
description: Advanced instruction in research topics and technologies, emphasizing various clinical disciplines.

title: Antimicrobial Resistance in Bacteria: Impact on Animal and Public Health
description: The course addresses issues of antimicrobial resistance (AMR) in bacteria in relation to antimicrobial use through an interdisciplinary approach that integrates animal, human and environmental health.

title: Research Methods
description: Discussion of research design, grantsmanship, practical statistics, manuscript preparation, and ethics.

title: Advanced Topics in Clinical Ophthalmology I
description: Designed to expose comparative ophthalmology residents to the source material associated with the ABVO certifying examination.  Assigned textbook reading covers various topics from the major required textbook reading list.  Meet with faculty to guide the resident's self-study, answer questions, and correct deficiencies.

title: Advanced Topics in Clinical Ophthalmology II
description: Designed to familiarize comparative ophthalmology residents with the basic format of the ABVO certifying examination. The course will include written and image recognition examinations that collectively assess the students' comprehensive knowledge of clinical ophthalmology, including medical and surgical case management. Basic vision science concepts associated with embryology, anatomy, physiology, and optics will also be reviewed. Meet to deliver mock examinations, including time to review the content, answer questions, and correct deficiencies.

title: MS Report in Clinical Sciences
description: A written report of either laboratory-based research, field-based research, or a review paper on a topic in the major field.

title: Research in Clinical Sciences
description: Individual research in any of the fields of Clinical Sciences.

title: Communication Sciences and Disorders
description: A survey of normal communication processes and communication disorders and an introduction to the fields of speech pathology and audiology that are responsible for the clinical management of these disorders.

title: Introduction to Phonetics
description: Basic information about speech sounds and their use in human languages, including physiological and perceptual characteristics of phonetics. Phonetic transcription of Standard American English will be emphasized.

title: Structure and Function of the Speech Mechanism
description: Anatomy of the structures involved in speech production. The course includes histology of the larynx and an overview of speech physiology.

title: Hearing Science
description: An introduction to hearing science concepts. Areas of focus include anatomy and physiology of the hearing mechanism, quantification of sound, sound generation, and sound transmission. Information on the role of the auditory system in the encoding and processing of simple and complex signals is also presented.

title: Developmental Psycholinguistics
description: Review of research and theory of early development of language comprehension and production, involving vocalization, phonology, morphology, syntax, semantics, and pragmatics. Includes discussion of the relationship between cognition and language, as well as other variables influencing language acquisition.

title: Language Assessment and Intervention I
description: The characteristics and nature of developmental language disorders in preschool-aged children, as well as general principles of language assessment and intervention are presented. Specific language assessment and intervention procedures for individuals between birth and five years old are reviewed.

title: Speech Sound Disorders
description: Theory, research, and principles of normal/abnormal phonetic and phonologic development, transcription of speech sound disorders assessment of speech sound disorders, and intervention for speech sound disorders.

title: Laboratory in Acoustic Phonetics
description: The study of speech perception and production through acoustic analysis. Laboratory experience in the use of computer-based speech analysis systems.

title: Clinical Procedures in Communication Disorders
description: Orientation to clinical practicum. Opportunities for clinical observation of speech, language, and hearing evaluation and treatment. Study of diagnostic tools, treatment materials, equipment, and clincial procedures.

title: Clinical Research in Communication Sciences and Disorders
description: Logic and methods of clinical research with emphasis on those most frequently used in speech-language pathology and audiology. Experience reading, planning, and evaluating research.

title: Hearing Assessment and Intervention
description: An introduction to audiology concepts and basic audiology testing procedures. Areas covered include disorders of the auditory system, testing procedures, and audiometric interpretation.

title: Hearing and Communication
description: The study of habilitation and rehabilitation techniques for individuals diagnosed with a hearing disorder. Specific areas of interest include hearing evaluation techniques, speech and language development for the hearing impaired, hearing aid amplification, cochlear implants, and central auditory processing.

title: Neurological Basis of Communication and Swallowing Disorders
description: Introduction to basic neuroanatomy and physiology as it relates to processes of human communication and swallowing.  Learn about the nervous system and how health conditions that affect neurological function contribute to activity limitations and participation restrictions. Provides foundational knowledge for students in communication sciences and disorders and health majors about individuals who have neurologically-based communication and swallowing disorders.

title: Undergraduate Topics in Communication Sciences and Disorders
description: Review of current topics in speech-language pathology and/or audiology.

title: Research Experience in Communication Sciences and Disorders
description: The course furthers understanding and application of research principles in communication sciences and disorders. Students participate in scholarly activities that apply to clinical populations in speech-language pathology.

title: Practicum in Speech-Language Pathology
description: Supervised practice in the use of the methods and materials of speech-language pathology.

title: Practicum in Audiology
description: Supervised practice in the use of equipment, materials, and methods of audiology.

title: Augmentative and Alternative Communication
description: This course examines the area of augmentative and alternative communication (AAC) for people across the lifespan. The etiologies and communicative needs of current and prospective users of AAC, as well as procedures for evaluation are addressed. The course includes strategies and procedures for implementing AAC systems for people who are permanently or temporarily severely communicatively impaired.

title: Stuttering
description: Research and theory concerning etiology, characteristics, assessment, and treatment of individuals with stuttering and related conditions.

title: Language Assessment and Intervention II
description: Theory and research concerning language disorders in school-aged children are presented. Specific language assessment and intervention methodologies for this population are reviewed. Dialectal and bilingual considerations for assessment and intervention are addressed.

title: Communication Impairments in Autism Spectrum Disorders
description: This course provides an overview of Autism Spectrum Disorders (ASD), including characteristics, diagnosis, and etiologies. Interventions used by speech-language pathologists for enhancing the communication of individuals with ASD across the lifespan will be presented, with a focus on using evidence-based practice guidelines to determine programs of treatment. Current developments and research findings related to assessment and intervention will be discussed.

title: Motor Speech Disorders
description: Examines motor speech disorders that result from neurologic disease, injury, and developmental conditions. Evidence-based practices concerning evaluation and planning of intervention are addressed for dysarthria and apraxia of speech.

title: Cleft Palate
description: An overview of communicative disorders related to cleft palate and other structurally-based conditions affecting velopharyngeal function. Research and practical knowledge related to the characteristics, assessment, and treatment of these communicative problems are addressed.

title: Voice Disorders
description: The etiologies and characteristics of voice disorders, as well as general principles of assessment, prevention, and treatment of these disorders, are presented. The role of the speech-language pathologist in rehabilitation planning following treatment for laryngeal cancer is also addressed.

title: Instrumental Measurement for Clinical Application
description: This course addresses instrumentation and measurement procedures for clinical application. Emphasis is placed on measurement tools that relate to subsystems of speaking.

title: Acquired Language and Cognitive Disorders
description: Study of acquired language and cognitive-communicative disorders in adults. Content addresses research and applied knowledge concerning etiologies, evaluation, and treatment of aphasia and communication deficits that result from right hemisphere damage, dementia, and traumatic brain injury.

title: Dysphagia
description: Study of normal and abnormal swallowing in children and adults. Procedures for assessment, diagnosis, and intervention are explored.

title: Externship in Speech-Language Pathology
description: Clinical practicum in off-campus sites, including supervised experience in evaluation and treatment of children and adults with communication disorders.

title: Graduate Topics in Communication Sciences and Disorders
description: Critical review of recent research related to measurement and modification of speech, hearing, or language deficits.

title: Professional Issues in Speech-Language Pathology
description: An overview of current professional issues in speech-language pathology are addressed. Primary topics include professional and ethical practices, scope of practice and service delivery, interprofessional education and interprofessional practice, diversity and cultural responsiveness, preparation for employment, and credentialing. Because this is a capstone course, the student must be within three semesters/terms of the graduate program.

title: Graduate Seminar in Communication Sciences and Disorders
description: Advanced course providing critical analysis of recent theory and research in a designated topic area.

title: MS Research in Communication Sciences and Disorders
description: Individual research problems in communication sciences and disorders which may form the basis for the master's thesis or report.

title: Introduction To Professions In The Apparel And Textile Ind
Apparel In Textile Marketing Field Experience
History Of Costume From 1780 To Present
Case Studies In Apparel And Textile Marketing
Strategic Planning In The Apparel And Textile Industry
Chemical And Optical Analysis Of Textiles
Consumers In The Apparel And Textile Market
Research Methods In Apparel And Textiles
Contemporary Topics In Clothing And Textiles
Seminar In Apparel, Textiles, Interior Design, & Housing
Professional Development Seminar In Clothing And Textiles
Research In Clothing, Textiles, And Interior Design
Study Tour in Clothing and Textiles
Case Studies in the Fashion Industry
Cyber Systems Seminar
description: CSDD Faculty and Senior-standing students will lead discussions and activities covering topics such as creative problem solving; empathy, ideation, prototyping, and testing as means to innovate discovery in diverse fields.

title: Computing Principles
description: Fundamental concepts of computer science and computational thinking. Topics include the use of abstraction, problem analysis, data representation, algorithms and programming. Students learn to use creative processes to develop computational artifacts. Student activities are designed to appeal to a broad audience, including those underrepresented in computing.

title: Principles of Interactive Digital Storytelling
description: This course explores the frontier of narrative innovation and uncovers the unique qualities and capabilities of immersive technologies such as virtual, augmented, and mixed reality in crafting captivating stories. Students will explore theoretical and practical dimensions of designing immersive narratives, learning how to leverage sensory engagement and spatial storytelling techniques to create compelling virtual environments. Students will have a well-rounded understanding of the principles underlying interactive and immersive storytelling and will be equipped with the skills to create profound and engaging narratives within these revolutionary digital landscapes.

title: Fundamentals of Design Thinking
description: Students learn how to use the design thinking process to solve problems creatively, collaboratively and empathetically. Additionally, the course equips students with the knowledge of the processes and techniques used to solve problems and innovate in the workplace.

title: Introduction to Database Systems
description: An introduction to properties and design principles of relational databases. Topics include database terms, E-R Modeling, relational table design and normalization, the relational algebra, Structured Query Language, and the database life cycle. Laboratory work includes the design and implementation of a database.

title: Interactive Media Development
description: In this course, students will learn to create visually rich interactive experiences. It is a course in programming graphics and media, but it is also a course on the relationship between ideas and code. Students will explore topics in math and physics by building programs that simulate and visualize processes in the natural world. Assignments will include major programming projects, such as building a virtual world inhabited by digital characters that display observable behaviors.

title: Programming I
description: Programming foundations and development of simple video games using a game engine. Topics include programming fundamentals, syntax and semantics, expressions, control statements, object-oriented programming, classes, functions, streams, game engine basics, and visual scripting concepts.

title: Hardware and Network Fundamentals
description: An introduction to computer systems with an emphasis on the internal workings of computer and network hardware. Hardware topics include data representation in binary, digital logic and the Von-Neumann architecture. Network topics include local-area and wide-area networks, topology, protocols and transmission media. Student activities include proper hardware configurations for various applications.

title: Applied Mathematics for Cyber Systems
description: This course builds on the foundations of algebra and trigonometry with a focus on mathematical topics used in computer graphics programming. Topics include digital data storage, numerical accuracy and stability, mathematical notation, formal logic, discrete math, sets, implementation of computational algorithms, proof theory, probability and statistics, numerical methods, an introduction to linear algebra with an emphasis on geometric transformations and the kinematics of serial-link systems, difference equations and Markov matrices, and an introduction to projective geometry.

title: Immersive COOP Studio I
description: Cooperative education is a work experience designed to supplement the educational process. Students will engage in on-campus activities designated as cooperative education, including relevant industrial experience, entrepreneurial activities, as well as faculty-supervised research and innovation opportunities.

title: 3D Animation and Asset Production for XR
description: This course provides an overview of 3D game asset production. Basic ideas learned within the first asset production course are also revisited within the 3D environments. Topics covered include modeling, texturing, skinning and animation. Emphasis is put on low polygon modeling techniques, best practices in game art production, and effective communication strategies between artists, programmers, and designers.

title: Foundations of Interactive Narratives
description: This course focuses on the major elements of narrative for interactive environments. Students in this course explore the basics of narrative in the context of interactive games and media, with examination of digital storytelling in games and interactive environments of several varieties. Branching narratives, hypertext, multi- and non-linear concepts are also explored with an emphasis on balancing immersive and interactive aspects of digital narrative.

title: Multi-platform Interactive Media Design
description: Interactive media applications are no longer restricted to personal computers. They can now be found on many distinct hardware platforms including mobile, tablet, wearable, and spatial computing devices. In this course, students will learn to design, prototype and develop media rich interactive experiences that can be deployed to a wide variety of hardware devices. Programming projects are required.

title: Interactive Game and Audio Design
