description: This course provides students with exposure to the design, creation and production of audio in interactive applications and computer games. Students will become familiar with the use of sound libraries, recording sounds in the studio and in the field, generating sound with synthesizers, and effects processing. Students will create sound designs for interactive media, integrating music, dialog, ambient sound, sound effects and interface sounds within interactive programs.

title: 3D Design and Modeling for XR
description: This course focuses on the digital composition of three-dimensional forms and the relationship to space in an XR environment. The course covers methods for 3D modeling and problem-solving. Students will build a toolkit around topics such as 3D design principles, materials, and building processes. Additionally, an awareness of idea development and design research will be explored throughout the course.

title: Programming II
description: Help students master basic concepts and become competent coders. Topics include object-oriented programming, pointers, inheritance, recursion, sorting and search algorithms, and advanced concepts such as overloading and memory management.  Covers leveraging artificial intelligence in a game engine and explores various project types such as simulation, animation, and gaming.

title: Computer Vision for Immersive Systems
description: Computer vision for immersive systems is a comprehensive course that explores how machines interpret visual data. This multidisciplinary subject combines elements of artificial intelligence, machine learning, image processing, and computational models to replicate human visual perception. Students delve into algorithms for object detection, facial recognition, and scene understanding, among others. Emphasizing both theoretical understanding and practical application, the course fosters innovation in areas like autonomous systems, robotics, extended and augmented reality.

title: Foundations of Game Engine Design and Development
description: This course introduces students to the evolution of game engine technology and its role in modern interactive media and real-time applications. Students will learn core game engine concepts, terminology, architecture, and contemporary integrations across industries while examining how engines power simulation, XR, and digital experiences. The course also covers fundamental physics principles—including motion, collision, forces, and rigid-body dynamics—used to create believable behavior in interactive environments.

title: User Experience (UX) Design for Immersive Systems
description: This course is an introduction to User Experience (UX) Design in the field of Digital Media and Immersive Systems. The course teaches and prepares students on how to use techniques of discovery and evaluation in the UX design process. Students will learn how to use digital toolkits to gather information about what users need within a UX framework and how to design and model interfaces based on UX Design best practices.

title: Cyber Security Operations
description:
Cyber security operations is an immersive course diving into the complex world of computer networks, digital security, information security (INFOSEC) and cyber threats. Covering topics from system risk assessments, threats, vulnerabilities, impact, effective countermeasures, and end-to-end communications
security (COMSEC); students learn to build robust security solutions, protect PII and information/data to national critical infrastructure, and combat cybercrime. Ideal for future cyber technology professionals, the course merges theory with hands-on labs, ensuring students gain crucial practical experience in this increasingly vital and rapidly evolving field of immersive cyber systems.

title: Data Structures and Algorithms for Cyber Systems
description: This course focuses upon the application of data structures and associated algorithms to the development of software applications. Topics include interfaces, design patterns, arrays, stacks, queues, lists, trees, hash tables, graphs, recursion, data structure search and traversals, and dynamic programming. The software design patterns related to graphical applications is emphasized. Programming assignments are a required part of this course.

title: Immersive COOP Studio II
description: Cooperative education is a work experience designed to supplement the educational process. Students will engage in on-campus activities designated as cooperative education, including relevant industrial experience, entrepreneurial activities, as well as faculty-supervised research and innovation opportunities.

title: Interactive Systems Design for Virtual Reality (VR)
description: This project-based course is an investigation of VR as an illustrative, imaging, and graphical generation medium. It develops foundational design skills in immersive media creation, editing, compositing, layout and visual design for production. Emphasis will be on the application of visual design organization methods and principles for virtual reality media.

title: Interactive Systems Design for Augmented Reality (AR)
description: Through project-based studies, this course explores perceptual understanding, including aesthetics, graphic form, structure, concept development, visual organization methods and interaction principles for AR media. Students will design immersive and dynamic AR solutions to communication problems for interactive projects. Students will focus on creating appropriate and usable immersive design systems through the successful application of design theory and best practices.

title: Human Computer Interactions for Cyber Systems
description: Explores psychology and computing convergence in the design of cyber systems for human use. Focuses users' various sensory, motor, and cognitive abilities to use technology. Discusses predictive modeling, heuristic evaluation, interpretive methods, experimental testing, information presentation, task analysis, prototyping and the iterative design cycle, user interface implementation, and interface evaluation.

title: Intelligent Spatial Computing Systems
description: Students will engage in the development and application of spatial computing and machine learning technologies. Additionally, students will create interactive digital media systems that intelligently perceive and interconnect with the physical world. The curriculum blends theoretical foundations with real-world project work.

title: Real-time Computing Systems
description: Introduces design and applications of real-time computing systems. Explores traditional hard real-time concepts, design, and implementation to ensure timing guarantees and safety. Develop a deeper understanding of real-time aspects of system components such as OS, memory, scheduling, communication, timing and fault management.

title: Software Engineering for Intelligent Cyber Systems
description: Project creation, from concept to final output, and provides insights into workflows, tools, and techniques. Discusses integrations that streamline workflows by providing a single platform for real-time management of projects, assets, tasks, and versions. Track the progress of assets through the pipeline, implementation and testing strategies, software principles, and metrics. Delves into more advanced topics like intelligent systems and case studies from industry professionals.

title: Senior Capstone I
description: Through formal studies and perceptual understanding, including aesthetics, graphic form, structure, concept development, visual organization methods and interaction principles, students will design immersive and dynamic solutions to communication problems for interactive projects. Students will focus on creating appropriate and usable immersive design systems through the successful application of design theory and best practices.

title: Senior Capstone II
description: This course is designed to engage students in a capstone production experience. The instructor will form interdisciplinary student teams that will design, plan, prototype, and implement immersive systems projects. Student groups are required to test their product with users and provide written feedback and analysis. Students will be evaluated on individual contributions and their team's final capstone project.

title: Special Topics in Modeling and Simulation
description: This course provides advanced study of selected topics in modeling and simulation relevant to cybersecurity, defense, training, and complex systems analysis. Content varies by offering and may include simulation frameworks, digital twins, agent-based modeling, virtual environments, and analytics-driven simulation. Students will apply theoretical concepts through hands-on projects and scenario-based analysis to address real-world challenges.

title: Introduction to Cybernetic Modeling and Simulation
description: Students will learn various cybernetic modeling and simulation methods to be applied within the context of complex integrated systems. Multiple simulation techniques and methodologies will be covered. This includes: Model Based Systems Engineering, Agent-Based Modeling, Discrete Event Simulation, and System Dynamics Simulation

title: Integrated Systems Architecture
description: Students are introduced to the principles and methods of Systems Architecture and  includes: the resolution of ambiguity to identify system goals and boundaries; the creative process of mapping form to function; and the analysis of complexity and methods of decomposition and re-integration. Both heuristic and formal methods are covered within the context of this course.

title: Cyber Systems and Risk Analysis
description: An introductory course in Cyber Systems history, elements, regulations, operations, use of geospatial data; automation, safety issues; detect and avoid systems; sensors and payloads, human factors, and future. Special attention to Cyber Security Risks, Threats, Impact, Vulnerabilities, and Countermeasures will be identified. The Ryan-Nichols Risk Assessment equations will be used for qualitative risk analysis of threats so identified.

title: Open Source Cyber Surveillance
description: This course questions the technical gaps, Intelligence Community (IC) assumptions, and important legal issues related to open source cyber surveillance with emphasis on Cyber Systems activities/deployment. Topics addressed include the responsible, legal, and ethical use of data and information gathered from the use of unmanned, semiautonomous systems, web data mining, social networks, and other modern technological systems.

title: Advanced Topics in Cyber Data Fusion
description: This course is scenario-based, applying cyber surveillance techniques and analysis of collected data to realistic, terrain-oriented problems. Topics include the digital soldier and sailor, 360-degree battlefield awareness and the use of semiautonomous and fully autonomous technologies. Risk Assessment and Cyber Security countermeasures are the "glue" to successful implementation of data fusion techniques. The Ryan-Nichols Risk Assessment equations and other methods will be used for qualitative risk analysis of identified Cyber Threats.

title: Risk Management for Cyber Operations
description: Cyber operations is focused on performing defensive and offensive security tasks to help assess organizational security postures of many government agencies.  In this course, students will explore risk management strategies in both Defensive Cyber Operations (DCO) and Offensive Cyber Operations (OCO), as well as the tasks performed by each.  Students will also be introduced to the Cyber Operations Cycle and its phases as well as the responsibilities of each member and their role within offensive and defensive teams.

title: Sense and Avoid Technologies in Aerospace Systems
description: An advanced course in Sense and Avoid (SAA) technologies for Aerospace Systems.  SAA is an extremely important concept and is the main obstacle for wider application of Aerospace Systems in non-segregated airspace related to traffic safety in civilian and military/ defense domains. This course presents the state-of-the-art research results from British, American, Australian and European universities, as well as corporate organizations such as Boeing and MITRE.

title: Research Methods, Design, and Analysis
description: Survey of qualitative, quantitative, and design science research methods; using a range of tools to develop applied research skills focusing on research planning, roadmap development, systematic review, experimental design, testing, journal publication, and patent creation.

title: Cybernetic Systems Design and Dynamics
description: Students survey and apply cybernetic techniques in analyzing and modeling complex systems. Requirements are derived in various domains and abstracted at conceptual, logical, and physical levels. Process, data, and state modeling are applied through industry projects that follow various systems development lifecycle.""

title: Advanced Data Analytics for Cybernetic Systems
description: This course prepares students to gather, describe, and analyze data, and use advanced statistical tools to make decisions on operations, risk management, finance, marketing, etc. Analysis is done targeting economic and financial decisions in complex systems that involve multiple partners. Topics include probability, statistics (hypothesis testing, regression), clustering, decision trees, and forecasting.

title: Cyber Defense Methods
description: The course explores defense-in-depth techniques of layering people, process and technology controls to secure the enterprise. Topics include information security law, ethics, security concepts and mechanisms; security technologies; authentication mechanisms; mandatory and discretionary controls; basic cryptography and its applications; digital forensics, biometrics database security, intrusion detection and prevention, anonymity and privacy issues for information systems.

title: Aerospace Cybersecurity Studio
description: The course provides a culminating experience for majors involving a substantive cybersecurity project that demonstrates a synthesis of learning accumulated in the major, including broadly comprehensive knowledge of the discipline and its methodologies. Students are required to synthesize, architect, design, and present applied cyber operations techniques within an industry context.

title: Design and Operations for Cyber Human Systems
description: This course will introduce students to the intelligent systems approach to designing, building, and operating complex cyber human systems; requirements, functional decomposition, systems architecting, analysis of alternatives, project life cycle modeling, cost analysis, and technical performance measurement.

title: Social Computing Dynamics and Cyber Human Systems
description: This course will orient students towards the Cyber Physical Social Systems (CPSS) paradigm.  Students will be engaged in CPSS thinking, system dynamics, urban policy analysis, and social computing applications to organizational trust and operations.  Additionally, students will be introduced to key principles of cyber systems, cybernetic systems theory, technological integrations, cyber marketing patterns, and technology adoption.

title: Interaction Design for Extended Reality Environments
description: Students in this course will work on the interaction design of dialogues for interactive extended reality environments. The course will explore the psychological, physiological, linguistic, and perceptual factors of immersive environments. Students will learn the advantages and disadvantages of various interaction techniques, command language syntaxes, and data presentations.

title: Spatial Computing for Virtual Environments
description: Covers tools and techniques for modeling, simulation, visualization and analysis of physical and virtual systems with an emphasis on multi-domain environments. Examples include airplanes, helicopters, trains, ships, cars, submarines, tanks, construction equipment, weapon systems, air traffic control, flight simulation, and gaming. Development of models towards immersive augmented and virtual reality simulations will also be explored.

title: Computer Geometry for Modeling
description: Covers numerical and computer representations of curves and surfaces. This includes but is not limited to: geometric data management, interpolation methods. graph-based models, boolean models, solid geometry modeling, cubic B-splines, Bezier curves and surfaces.  Additionally, industry applications towards robotics, computer graphics, and immersive systems development will be explored.

title: Artificial Intelligence for Modeling and Simulation
description: Fundamental concepts and techniques of modeling, simulating, visualizing, and analyzing complex real-world quantitative and qualitative systems of systems by using artificial intelligence, knowledge acquisition and representation, reasoning, planning, machine learning, expert systems, intelligent agents and multi-agent systems, and search strategies; emphasizes practical applications for creating intelligent simulations.

title: Special Topics in Cyber Systems
description: Offered on sufficient demand.  Advanced topics in the science, technology, or engineering of cyber systems.

title: Prototyping for Advanced Immersive Systems
description: Students will be given the opportunity to develop immersive experiences for high-end, experimental, and prototype equipment that is only available to students in this course. Students will learn how to work with complex immersive equipment systems and early access software in a collaborative online studio environment.

title: Czech for Travelers
description: Introduction to the Czech language with emphasis on everyday expressions and vocabulary. History, social life, politics and other cultural issues are also introduced.

title: Breathe, Move, Be
description: Through gentle movement, body awareness and breathing exercises, this course introduces students to the holistic well-being, movement efficiency and creative choice-making that are the result of somatic movement education. A wonderful foundation for any movement practice and excellent recuperation from the demands of academic life, this course is accessible to those with no prior movement training and prioritizes self-knowledge, acquired through the living body.

title: Yoga for Restoration
description: Improve balance, flexibility and body awareness through an exploration of Yoga postures and breathing exercises. Excellent cross-training and recuperation for performers and athletes, this course also serves anyone seeking to strengthen their body to calm their mind. Key alignment principles and standard modifications to increase or decrease difficulty will be introduced, as will be the basics of Yoga philosophy.

title: Contemporary Dance I
description: Introduction to principles of contemporary dance. Emphasis on correct body alignment, movement efficiency, and creative potential of the individual.

title: Ballet I
description: Introduction to basics of classical ballet training includes terminology, body positions, movement vocabulary, and principles of body alignment.

title: Jazz Dance I
description: A basic course in jazz technique and style, focusing on isolations, rhythmic articulation, and the control and release of energy.

title: Tap I
description: Designed for beginning dancers, this course develops skills, knowledge, vocabulary, and appreciation of tap dance as an art form through the presentation of fundamental tap techniques.

title: Anatomy For Dancers
description: Analysis of human skeletal structure. Application and implication for performance, teaching, and injury prevention.

title: Dance As an Art Form
description: Dance in its religious, social, and artistic forms. Film, slides, demonstrations, and lectures will trace the function of dance in society, the influence of society on dance, how dance relates to other art forms, and current trends in the dance world.

title: Improvisational Structures
description: Exploration of personal creative sources for spontaneous movement through improvisational structures. Emphasis on solo and group problem-solving in creating a performance work.

title: Principles of Dance Technology
description: Examination and application of computer, audio, and video technology to dance. Includes instruction and use in performance, choreography, education and research.

title: Rhythmic Notation for Dance
description: Introduction to basic elements of meter, tempo, rhythm, and notation. Application and practice to dance pedagogy, performance, and choreography.

title: West African Styles of Social Dance and Music
description: Explores several styles of dancing and drumming from West Africa. Instruction includes technique in the traditional social dances, drumming/musical skills, and songs for the social dances. History, traditions, and social context of the dances.

title: Performance Styles
description: Study and practice of technique and performance of specific period/historical, character, or ethnic/specialty dance styles.

title: Pointe Techniques
description: Covers classical ballet pointe technique. Focuses on the development of both technique and artistic expression.

title: Musical Theatre Dance
description: History, technique and performance of musical theatre dance. Rehearsal and performance of selected examples of Broadway choreography.

title: Jazz Dance II
description: Intermediate course in jazz technique and style focusing on development of isolations, rhythmic articulation, and the control and release of energy.

title: Jazz Dance III
description: Advanced course in jazz technique and style focusing on development of isolations, rhythmic articulation, and the control and release of energy.

title: Tap ll
description: Designed as an intermediate dance class that targets the development of skills, knowledge, and appreciation of tap dance through the presentation of fundamental techniques and other movement experiences. Topics include: concert, rhythm, and fundamental tap dance.

title: Tap lll
description: Designed as an advanced dance class that targets the development of skills, knowledge, and appreciation of tap dance through the presentation of techniques and other movement experiences. Topics include: Broadway and concert dance, rhythm, and Funk Tap dance.

title: Dance Composition I
description: Introduction to the principles of the choreographic craft. Practical experience in development of movement phrases. Culminating presentation and critique of work.

title: Honors Seminar
description: Open only to qualified students in the arts and sciences honors program.

title: Dance History I
description: Covers dance, particularly ballet and modern dance, from beginnings until the turn of 20th century.

title: Dance Composition II
description: Advanced training and directed experiences in dance composition. Development of theme, phrasing, and style with particular emphasis on group forms.

title: Honors Tutorial in Dance
description: Individually directed research/creative endeavor in dance, normally as a preliminary to writing a senior honors thesis.

title: Honors Project
description: Open only to Arts and Sciences students who are active members of the University Honors Program.

title: Performance Production
description: Studies in the techniques of dance production and performance. Emphasis is on practical application.

title: K-State Tap Dance Ensemble
description: A performing ensemble that dances for the K-State and Manhattan communities.

title: Contemporary Dance Ensemble
description: A contemporary dance repertory and performing ensemble.

title: Methods and Materials of Teaching Dance
description: An in-depth survey of the development of dance education and a practical examination of dance for its educative, artistic, disciplinary, and therapeutic values.  Emphasis on role of dance education, pedagogy, and advocacy.

title: Dance Education Fieldwork
description: A semester of supervised fieldwork incorporating dance as an educative tool in the classroom, in a therapeutic setting, or in an advocacy position. Application of dance education theory under faculty supervision and conference.

title: K-State African Dance and Music Ensemble
description: A performing group that is open to all students and does not require an audition.

title: CAPSTONE SEMINAR
description: Introduction to the dance capstone project fields: choreographic, pedagogical, or research.  The course orients students to research methodologies, IRB, and dance production requirements, while providing structured support during the proposal writing and submission process.

title: CAPSTONE PROJECT
description: Student develops and presents culminating interdisciplinary research, choreographic, or pedagogical project.

title: Movement: Fundamentals, Analysis, and Observation
description: Study, application, observation, and analysis of somatic movement fundamentals and their use in performance, education, and therapeutic practices.

title: Introduction To The Honors Program In Arts And Sciences
Honors Program
description: All students participating in the University Honors Program and who are enrolled in the College of Arts & Sciences must enroll each semester.

title: Intro to English Language Program
description: The purpose for DAS 030 is to provide a monitoring mechanism for English Language Program students.  It also allows the ELP, International Programs and International Pre-admissions to track new international students.

title: Training in Organization, Wellness, Academics, Responsibilities and Discipline in the ELP
description: This course is designed to engage English Language Program (ELP) students who are at risk due to repeated non-performance in ELP classes. Explores good study skills, healthy living habits, community involvement, self-discipline, and guided study sessions.

title: National Student Exchange
description: Enrollment in this course would be required for students participating in the National Student Exchange program.  Enrollment in this course would serve primarily as a placeholder and enable the student to maintain full-time enrollment status at K-State. A grade of I (incomplete) would be posted at the end of each term (semester) of enrollment. Upon receipt of the transfer transcript from the chosen exchange institution, the DAS 050 course and would be removed from the student's record and would be replaced by a text entry detailing participation in the National Student Exchange program. The actual hours earned would be reflected as transfer credit from the institution attended.

title: Ambassador Service and Leadership in the College of Arts and Sciences
description: For students who have been chosen as ambassadors for the College of Arts and Sciences. Students are required to enroll to be ambassadors.

title: First Year Seminar in K-State Culture
description: An overview of the intellectual and cultural life of the university. Students will attend a variety of events as a community and learn to reflect on and evaluate these events through writing, class discussion, and public speaking.

title: Discover, Create, and Thrive: Arts and Sciences Orientation
description: Orients learners to studies in the College of Arts and Sciences, helps learners to develop skills and strategies indicative of success, map their college plan, and compare interdisciplinary ways of knowing and learning across disciplines.

title: Introduction To The Honors Program In Arts And Sciences
Orientation to Health Careers
description: Acquaints students whose career goals are in the health professions with the variety of options available and with the corresponding academic requirements. Discussion covers an introduction to the personal responsibilities that health-care workers assume and the impact of social and economic problems on our health-care delivery system. Includes an orientation to general requirements for success as a student at K-State and in professional health related programs.

title: College Of Arts And Sciences Ambassador Course
Careers in Science and Engineering
description: Introduction to career opportunities with emphasis on gaining perspective from practicing professionals from multiple science and engineering disciplines through weekly seminars held throughout the semester.

title: Special Studies in English
description: Introduction to basic English syntax, writing, reading, speaking, and listening for native speakers of other languages.

title: Beginning English I
description: Introduction to basic English syntax, writing, reading, speaking, and listening for native speakers of other languages.

title: Beginning English II
description: Introduction to basic English syntax, writing, reading, speaking, and listening for native speakers of other languages.

title: Intermediate English I
description: Intermediate study of basic English sentence structure, writing, reading, speaking and listening for native speakers of other languages.

title: Basic English Language Orientation
description: Emphasizes the basic cultural and academic understanding necessary for new international students to be successful in ELP and university classes at K-State. Introduction to cultural differences, campus resources, health and safety, study skills, housing issues, plagiarism, basic immigration rules, classroom expectations.

title: Intermediate English II
description: Intermediate study of basic English sentence structure, writing, reading, speaking and listening for native speakers of other languages.

title: Advanced English Speaking I for Non-native Speakers of English
description: Comprehension and appropriate response to non-academic discourse; development of accurate pronunciation, oral grammar, and vocabulary usage. Development of fluency.

title: Advanced English Writing I
description: Review of advanced grammatical structures; practice in writing using academic style, organization and rhetorical conventions; transition to an academic vocabulary in writing; introduction to conventions of research writing and citation.

title: Advanced English Reading I for Non-native Speakers of English
description: Introduction to advanced critical reading skills in at level reading passages; introduction to unsimplified academic readings; development of an academic vocabulary; increase of reading speed to approach that of U.S. undergraduates.

title: Advanced English Listening I for Non-native Speakers of English
description: Comprehension and note-taking of academic lectures at level; introduction to unsimplified academic lectures; comprehension of non-academic discourse at level; use of notes from lecture for test taking.

title: Introduction to American Academic Research Writing for International Students
description: Introduction to American conventions of research paper writing with emphasis on American academic standards.

title: Introduction to American Academic Presentation for Inter St
description: Introduction to American rhetorical patterns of academic speaking, focusing specifically on deductive and inductive organization patterns and expectations of American audiences.

title: Orientation to American Culture and Education
description: Designed to support English Language Program students as they transition to K-State, the English Language Program (ELP), and the Manhattan community. Introduction to the campus resources available.

title: Advanced English Writing II for Non-native Speakers of English
description: Use of advanced grammatical structures and academic vocabulary in writing; practice in editing and revision with an eye to academic to academic style, organization and rhetorical conventions; use of conventions of research writing and citation.

title: Advanced English Reading II for Non-native Speakers of English
description: Development of advanced critical reading skills with unsimplified academic passages; active usage of an academic vocabulary; increase of reading speed equivalent to that of U.S. undergraduates.

title: Advanced English Listening II for Non-native Speakers of English
description: Comprehension and note-taking of non-simplified academic lectures; comprehension of non-lecture academic discourse; use of notes from lectures and other sources for test taking.

title: Advanced English Speaking II for Non-native Speakers of English
description: Comprehension and appropriate response to non-academic and academic discourse; development of accurate pronunciation, oral grammar, and vocabulary usage. Development of fluency.

title: Advanced English Writing III for Non-native Speakers of English
description: Mastery of independent editing and revision with an eye to academic style, organization, rhetorical conventions and register; mastery of conventions of research writing and citation.

title: Basic Grammar in Writing
description: Review of basic English grammar; practice of basic grammar in writing; review of grammar usage in academic writing; practice in proofreading and editing of work.

title: Technical Communication for International Students
description: Addresses the needs of non-native English speakers studying in the STEM fields. Introduces technical vocabulary, conventions, forms of documents, and practices of written, oral and online technical communication in the United States. Grammar and pronunciation support provided as needed. Student portfolio will include a resume, project proposal, memos, technical report, poster presentation, and personal website.

title: Digital English for International Students
description: Instruction in online language and communication for international students in order to develop strategies and skills for working effectively in academic digital interaction and collaboration. Includes study of function and use of different web 2.0 tools. Communication skills for intercultural collaboration, and the English language styles and rhetoric appropriate in specific online settings.

title: Business Communications for Non-Native Speakers
description: This course will introduce non-native English speakers to key U.S. business terminology, norms for written and oral communication, and business culture in the U.S. Students will practice business communication and teamwork skills in a hands-on setting.

title: Reading Skills For Non-Native Speakers of English
description: An advanced course designed to improve reading skills and increase academic vocabulary of non-native speakers of English engaged in academic classes.

title: Written Communication Skills For Non-Native Speakers/Engl
description: Instruction in academic reading and writing skills in order to develop reading skills, increase reading speed, and improve writing of reports, analyses, and summaries. Includes grammar review, the study of style, and development of an academic vocabulary.

title: Listening Skills
description: Instruction to improve listening, comprehension, and note-taking skills. Instruction in conversational speaking, with an emphasis on developing oral fluency and the effective use of vocabulary and idioms. Includes pronunciation practice, group work, and short academic presentations.

title: Pronunciation & Speaking Skills For Non-Native Speaker/Engl
description: Introduction in speaking for non-native speakers of English to improve comprehension of non-simplified conversational English. Practice in asking for clarification, responding appropriately to requests, and encouraging interaction, as well as the pronunciation of troublesome English sounds and intonation patterns. Also brief introduction to American culture, especially the American educational system.

title: Advanced Integrated Skills
description: An advanced, combined-skills course to improve the reading, writing, and speaking skills of non-native speakers of English.

title: Introduction To American Academic Research Writing Inter St
Introduction To American Academic Presentation For Inter St
Practical Training in Esl
description: Introduction to practical classroom skills for the non-education major planning to teach ESL abroad as part of a study abroad or internship abroad experience.

title: Teaching & Cultural Orientation For International Tch Asst
description: Introduction to American undergraduate education, American culture, and university policies. Discussion of effective teaching techniques, nonverbal language, and coping techniques. Practice focusing on pronunciation, vocabulary, grammar, and interactive speaking skills. Individual tutorials based on video-taped teaching presentations.

title: Orientation To The American Higher Education System
Introduction To The University Honors Program
CAT Community Connections
description: Interdisciplinary course that explores the connections among two different fields, courses, or disciplines.

title: Introduction to Integrated Health Studies
description: Acquaints students with the Integrated Health Studies secondary major program themes: social and humanistic dimensions of health, ethics in health sciences, multicultural awareness in health, and scientific and statistical reasoning. Prepares students for integrated learning in the coursework associated with the program to set the tone for the capstone experience. Thematic learning and exposure to digital literacy are key components.

title: Introduction to Arts & Sciences Education Theory and Practice
description: Introduces learning theory and teaching practices for arts and sciences learning assistants. Presents theoretical issues such as conceptual development, questioning techniques, and cooperative learning. Students will read research literature on teaching and learning in arts and sciences and engage in classroom discussions. Students are expected to reflect on their own teaching practices throughout the semester as well.

title: Fundamentals of Global Food Systems Leadership
description: An interdisciplinary approach to the fundamental roles people, policies, and cultures play in the global food system enterprise as it relates to sustainable food production, processing, distribution, and availability. Students will explore complexities within self, others, and community related to leadership in the context of a rapidly increasing global population.

title: Practicum in Pre-Health
description: Forty hours spent observing the practice of dentistry, medicine, or optometry. Students are under the supervision and direction of individual dentists, physicians, or optometrists.

title: Uncertainty in Global Food Systems Leadership
description: Explore technical and adaptive elements of grand challenges across disciplines. This course addresses the impact of cultural identity, life experience, and world views on leadership relationships as it relates to privilege and inclusion in the context of global food systems. Students will consider elements of community-engaged work.

title: Origins
description: An interdisciplinary general education course examining how scholars in the sciences and humanities approach knowledge about the universe, the planet earth, and life itself.  How did the universe begin?  How did life begin and how does it evolve?  How has the planet changed throughout its history?  How were answers to these questions discovered?  How do we construct narratives about these events, and how do those texts reveal our assumptions and values?

title: Topics in the Humanities (Study Abroad)
description: This is an umbrella course for numerous individual courses offered to students in the humanities in Italy program and other study abroad experiences.

title: Introduction to Nonviolence Studies
description: The theory and practice of nonviolence as a method of social change and as a personal way of life. This class will engage students in the consideration of violence and nonviolence, from dynamics that occur within ourselves to those that affect the future of the Earth and its many life forms. Students will share with each other from their own lived experience and will learn from the writings of people who have practiced nonviolence in their lives and work.

title: Honors Internship
description: A scholarly investigation related to activities in a place of employment or in a volunteer situation.

title: Undergraduate Research in Arts and Sciences
Independent Undergraduate Research in Arts and Sciences
Topics in Clinical Laboratory Science (Medical Technology)
description: Basic principles and practices of the medical laboratory, techniques and special projects.

title: Primary Texts Seminar
description: Emphasizes intellectual history through the lens of social and political development. Students will learn about the impacts of cultural developments such as the awakening of ancient idealism, Rennaissance philosophy and literature, the Scientific Revolution, the Enlightenment, and Postmodern philosophy and art.

title: Global Food Systems Leadership in Action
Honors Colloquium
description: An interdisciplinary colloquium in which topics vary by semester. Consistently incorporates perspectives from more than one discipline and area among the arts, humanities, social sciences, and sciences.

title: Violence, Nonviolence and Social Change
description: This course reviews historical and contemporary responses to violence around the world by studying ideas, strategies, organizational approaches, and societal impacts on nonviolence and social change. Emphasis is placed on historical antecedents to recent violence and nonviolence, and on the analysis of up-to-date resources as they relate to social change.

title: Nonviolence Studies: Women and Peace Movements
description: Analyzes women and girls as leaders of diverse peace movements in the present and recent past.

title: Humanities Capstone
description: Individual exploration of the humanities major to synthesize knowledge and skills acquired in humanities courses.

title: Integrated Computer Science Capstone
description: Capstone project for the Integrated Computer Science Major.

title: Integrated Health Studies Capstone
description: Capstone experience for the Integrated Health Studies Secondary Major.  Follows the structure of the major, fostering integration of learning through the application of knowledge and skills from different areas.  Concludes with individual exploration and guided research in an area of specific interest to the student that integrates or synthesizes different aspects of health studies.

title: Social Sciences Capstone
description: Individual exploration of the social science major to synthesize knowledge and skills acquired in social science courses.

title: Physical Sciences Senior Report
description: Individual exploration of an area of physical sciences culminating in a final formal written report. Capstone course required of physical sciences interdisciplinary major.

title: The Americas: An Introduction To Latin America
Recitation Leadership In American Ethnic Studies
Undergraduate Cancer Research
description: Independent mentored research on a cancer-related research project. Includes three seminars covering an introduction to cancer and cancer research, responsible conduct in research, and keeping laboratory notebooks. Students will present brief oral and/or poster presentations on their research project and its relevance to cancer.

title: Senior Research In Latin American Studies
description: Independent research product under supervision by director of Latin American Studies.

title: Senior Research in International Studies
description: A research paper or project on an international topic.

title: Advanced Topics in the Humanities (Study Abroad)
description: This is an umbrella course for numerous individual courses offered to students in the Humanities in Italy program and other study abroad experiences.

title: Themes in Nonviolence Studies
description: Explores nonviolence from a national and global perspective, with particular emphasis on emergent techniques and themes in nonviolence studies with particular emphasis on conflict resolution, peacekeeping and peacebuilding, and intersections of gender, race, class, ethnicity, and religion.

title: Peacebuilding
description: Explores how social problems, violence and war are generated and how people are working to build conditions that produce peace. Students will analyze peacebuilding efforts at home, in communities, in countries, in regions, and in the global social system.

title: Natural Resources/Environmental Sciences Project
description: A comprehensive project in NRES. Requires integration of information and understanding acquired in NRES secondary major courses. Students must prepare and present written and oral reports.

title: Applied Nonviolence
description: The capstone experience for the Undergraduate Certificate in Nonviolence Studies, includes field placement in a campus or community organization in which student will have a skill-and-research-based experience in nonviolence-related social change project. Projects can be local, regional, national, or international in scope.  Concurrently, students will engage in academic readings and class sessions that address feminist and nonviolent approaches to social change, program design, and participatory action research. This course will provide students with professional and academic skills that relate to employment and graduate work in diverse fields.

title: Honors Program
description: All students accepted into the College of Education honors program must enroll each semester.

title: Study Skills Laboratory
description: Helps the student to learn effective study methods, analyze difficulties in reading and studying, and prepare for and improve performance in examinations.

title: Pre-Professional Laboratory Experiences
description: Supervised experiences in education designed to facilitate orientation and investigation of teaching through the teacher aide program.

title: Topics in Education
description: A study of topics in the field of education.

title: Introduction to the University Honors Program
description: Overview of the University Honors Program including directions, goals, and student requirements for completion of the program.

title: Problems in Education
description: A study of topics in the field of education.

title: Honors Project
description: Individual projects under the supervision of a professor.

title: Senior Research Project In American Ethnic Studies
Preparing for the Professoriate in Education
description: This course targets graduate students who plan to enter the professoriate in education.  Examines topics such as the job search, integration into the professoriate, expectations for promotion and tenure, and achievement in academia.

title: Engineering Student Success Seminar
description: Supports engineering students in identifying potential barriers to student success and gaining access to related campus resources. Students will participate in interactive small groups and discussions. This course will also assist students in developing effective academic skills including, but not limited to: time management, organization, learning styles, effective communication, academic planning and goal setting.

title: Conditional Admittance Support for Transfer Students
Reinstatement Support
description: Students who have been reinstated into the College of Engineering must enroll in this course. This course will provide frequent communication and support in the areas of study skills and time management.

title: Multicultural Engineering Enrichment Seminar
description: Introduction to the academic and intellectual demands of an engineering curriculum from a multicultural perspective. Develop group cohesiveness and an attitude of mutual support by engaging in collaborative learning. Help students acquire effective study methods, analyze/compare learning/teaching styles, prepare for and improve examination performance, promote optimum utilization of campus resources, develop leadership and communication skills and enhance self-esteem.

title: Careers in Science and Engineering
description: Introduction to career opportunities with emphasis on gaining perspective from practicing professionals from multiple science and engineering disciplines through weekly seminars held throughout the semester.

title: College of Engineering Orientation
description: Help incoming K-State students’ transition to the college of engineering. Students will be introduced to the requirements and expectations of the college of engineering.  They will be exposed to tools to promote student success, professional development, and professionalism. Additionally, students will be introduced to all majors offered at K-State in the college of engineering and will be assisted in evaluating their options in order to select the most compatible major.

title: Engineering Problem Solving
description: Introduction to application-oriented engineering problem solving skills using fundamental engineering problems specific to each discipline, teamwork, and communication skills.

title: Introduction to the University Honors Program
description: Overview of the University Honors Program, including directions, goals and student requirements for completion of the program.

title: Multicultural Engineering Colloquium
description: Continuation of DEN 120. Emphasis on career exploration and development, introduction to graduate school options, preparation and responsibility for advising process, tips on breaking the failure cycle, behavior modification strategies, and developing and utilizing leadership skills.

title: Impact Of Engineering Technology On Society
Introduction To Personal And Professional Development
Introduction to Total Quality Management and Six Sigma
Creative Problem Solving in Engineering
description: Overview of major topics related to creative thinking, including idea development, overcoming personal and professional obstacles, managing change, effective problem solving, and teamwork productivity.

title: Leading Individuals and Engineering Project Teams
Market Research, Technology Updates & Environmental Scanng
Project Management for Engineers and Technical Professionals
Engineering Professionalism and Decision Making
description: An introduction to the complexities of making decisions as an engineer.  Students will explore the relationship between ethics and engineering by applying classical moral theory and decision making concepts to evaluate engineering issues and decisions encountered in academic and professional careers.

title: Problems in Engineering and Technology
description: A study of problems or topics in a specialized area of engineering or technology.

title: Introduction To Energy And Environmental Technology
Impact of Technology on Society
description: A study of social, economic, and environmental problems as a function of technology. Study of effect of various significant technological developments on present society and parallels with present developments. Study of current problems, detection of causes, and analysis of solutions. Implications for the future; governmental, industrial, and individual responsibility in detection of potential problems and methods of control or solution.

title: Honors Research in Engineering
description: Individual research problem selected with approval of faculty advisor.  Written report is presented at end of second semester.

title: Interdisciplinary Engineering Capstone Design Experience I
description: Provides culminating capstone engineering hands-on experiences for students from different degree programs in the Carl R. Ice College of Engineering, working collectively as interdisciplinary project teams. This class focuses on solving real-world complex engineering problems through the application of engineering design principles, engineering science/analyses, and interdisciplinary collaboration and innovation. Students will work in teams with the facilitation of instructors to design, build, and test engineering devices, prototypes, simulation models, or data/computing-based solutions using a design process with design reviews and presentations.

title: Interdisciplinary Engineering Capstone Design Experience II
description: Continuation of the capstone engineering hands-on experiences for students from different degree programs in the Carl R. Ice College of Engineering, working collectively as interdisciplinary project teams. This class focuses on solving real-world complex engineering problems through the application of engineering design principles, engineering science/analyses, and interdisciplinary collaboration and innovation. Students will work in teams with the facilitation of instructors to design, build, and test engineering devices, prototypes, simulation models, or data/computing-based solutions using a design process with design reviews and presentations.

title: Topics in Engineering and Technology
description: A study of problems or topics in a specialized area of engineering or technology.

title: Service-Learning In Research, Teach, Admin Or Extension
University Honors Program
description: All students participating in the University Honors Program and who are enrolled in the College of Health and Human Sciences must enroll each semester.

title: Honors Practicum in Research, Teaching, Admin or Extension
description: Practicum completed in collaboration with a faculty member that enhances skills in research, teaching, administration, or extension programming.

title: Introduction To The University Honors Program
CAT Community Connections
description: Interdisciplinary course that explores the connections among two different fields, courses, or disciplines. For first-year students enrolled in a specific CAT Community linked to the College of Health and Human Sciences programs.

title: Conducting Honors Research
description: This one credit seminar provides an overview of the process of conducting an honors project, which could be undergraduate research or other scholarly project. The seminar is designed for a sophomore and junior participants in the University Honors Program.

title: Honors Seminar in Human Ecology
description: Selected topics in health and human services.

title: Senior Research Project in American Ethnic Studies
Topics in Gender & Ethnicity in Jewish American Literature
Fundamentals of Visual Communication
description: An examination of the elements of visual design essential to communication with digital technology. Topics include design elements, color theory, graphics creation and optimization, and multimedia. Students receive hands-on experience with the elements and principles of visual literacy and working with 2-D organization.

title: Digital Literacy
description: Effective digital communications using industry standard technologies to create and edit various media including photographs, videos, sound recordings, websites and other forms of print and online media. Students analyze, evaluate and participate in digital culture. Digital publishing topics include media access, censorship, copyright, fair use, mashups and remixes.

title: Visual Communication Studio
description: Students complete portfolio projects that tie together topics related to visual communications design. Content topics include creating graphics, animations, photographs, videos and other relevant visual elements to be viewed in print and digital formats.

title: Digital Rhetoric
description: Provides students with the theoretical and applied understanding of how rhetoric works in online environments. Rhetorical strategies employed in media are explored with a close analysis of genre, digital literacy and the basics of design. Topics include new media, genre, digital literacy and rhetoric.

title: Digital Media Production Studio
description: Students complete portfolio projects that tie together topics related digital media production. Content topics include digital manipulation of media with audio editing, video editing, and photo editing tools and techniques.

title: Social Media
description: An exploration of the social media aspects of producing and consuming digital media content; including blogging, podcasting, and vodcasting. Diversity of the human condition is emphasized. Students will develop and publish an online portfolio of their best digital media work.

title: Digital Media Senior Capstone Project
description: Provides seniors with the capstone experience of developing a professional quality project in digital media. Students work individually or in teams to develop a significant project in their area of interest. Students are expected to apply the production techniques covered in previous digital media courses, write project documentation, and make verbal presentations. Whenever feasible, real-world projects are solicited from local businesses.

title: Introduction to Epidemiology
description: This introductory course explores the concepts, methods, and tools of epidemiology; the science of disease occurrence and distribution.  Students will learn how epidemiologists or ""disease detectives"" gather, analyze, and interpret information on the occurrence of infectious, non-infectious, and chronic diseases.  Through examples of current and/or controversial health topics, students will develop skills to read, interpret and evaluate health information from published epidemiologic studies.

title: Environmental and Public Health
description: This introductory course explores environmental health, the branch of public health that focuses on relationships between the environment and human health and well-being including the promotion of safe communities at the local, regional, national, and global level. Students will explore physical, chemical, biological, social, and psychosocial factors in the environment that impact human health and the ways in which societies create environmental health hazards. Environmental health issues in globalization, industrialization, and environmental justice, in both developed and developing countries will be discussed. Students will understand the roles of public health professionals, government, and non-governmental agencies in communicating, managing, and protecting environmental and public health.

title: Feedlot Health Systems
description: Health of cattle in a commercial feedyard. Includes health risk assessment, cattle handling, processing, vaccination protocols, identification/treatment of sick cattle, necropsy techniques, using computer data to make management decisions for feeder cattle, other management issues. Discussion of disease syndromes and foreign diseases.

title: Cow Calf Health Systems
description: Management of animal well-being and efficient production in a cow-calf system. Includes the areas of health, growth, nutrition, pharmaceutical management, and reproduction.

title: Fundamentals of Public Health and Food Safety
description: Organization and function of food inspection services; principles of disease transmission; diseases transmitted to humans through the food chain.

title: Essential Practices for BSL-3 Research Settings
description: This hands-on laboratory centered course will offer students functional skills training and an in-depth understanding of standard microbiological practices, principles and techniques necessary to safely conduct research in a Biosafety Level-3 setting. Trainees will be exposed to basic concepts such as: research project design, risk assessment, compliance issues, selection of appropriate methods, and introduction to new technologies based on their specific research project. Concurrent enrollment of DMP 691 Introduction to High Containment Research Topics and Techniques is required.

title: Introduction to High Containment Laboratory Topics and Techniques
description: This course examines the rationale behind the skills and techniques taught in DMP 690 Essential Practices for High Containment Research Settings.  Students will gain familiarity with the regulatory and operational requirements of high containment facilities and discuss best practices in the laboratory. Students review and discuss scientific readings, case studies, topics, and laboratory techniques appropriate for laboratory work conducted in BSL-3, Animal BSL-3 (ABSL-3), and Large Animal BSL-3 (BSL-3Ag) facilities. This course is designed to complement DMP 690 Essential Practices for High Containment Research Settings.

title: Introduction to One Health
description: One Health encompasses the complex interrelationships among humans, animals, and the environment. This online course provides a broad introduction to One Health, incorporating original videos of leading experts, case studies, and scientific readings. It addresses zoonotic diseases and environmental issues that impact human, animal, and ecosystem health.

title: GIS Applications in Animal and Public Health
description: Explores the relevance/benefits of applying GIS (Geographic Information System) and the concept of spatial thinking in animal/public health research and practice. Health relevant GIS concepts and terminologies, nature and sources of geospatial data, their manipulation methods in a GIS environment, and spatial analysis techniques will be presented.

title: GIS Applications in Animal and Public Health Lab
description: Through hands-on laboratory exercises different technical methods of applying GIS for health data analysis, spatial analytical methods, model building, cartographic principles and geographic visualization are explored.

title: Introduction to Epidemiology
description: The purpose of this course is to introduce students to the basic principles and methods of epidemiology in order to recognize and understand how disease affects populations (and the associated implications for individuals). This course will prepare students to use epidemiologic methods to solve current and future challenges to diagnose, treat, prevent, and control disease during their professional training and throughout their career.

title: Emerging Diseases
description: An investigation into recently identified emerging diseases, the conditions that enable their emergence, and the human health implications of each disease.

title: Introduction To Research In Laboratory Medicine
Environmental Health
description: This is a three-credit graduate-level course. Students will be exposed to professional practice of environmental sciences, epidemiology, toxicology, occupational health and industrial hygiene, and consumer health and safety. Topics include the methods for defining environmental contamination; identifying contaminants, pathogens and toxins; assessing risks and causality; determining health impact; ameliorating hazards; and protecting the population through waste management, regulatory programs, environmental inspections, food and product safety, and environmental policy. Participation is required.

title: Select Agent Studies
description: This course provides an in-depth study of select agent regulations and guidance. Specific topics include a historical overview of select agent regulations, program rationales, tier one regulations, personnel suitability assessments, inventory systems and oversight. Additional content will look outside the select agent program and address other regulations and guidelines which impact high-containment research such as Dual-Use regulations and the NIH Guidelines for Recombinant DNA. The course will also introduce students to specific agents covered under the select agent program, and review characteristics and research considerations when working with these agents.

title: Veterinary Bacteriology & Mycology Lecture
description: Veterinary Bacteriology and Mycology, is designed to provide graduate students in pathobiology or related field with basic knowledge of bacteria, fungi and the diseases that they cause primarily in animals. The course is also designed to provide latest information of the pathogenic mechanisms, virulence factors, antigenic nature, zoonosis and disease transmission, treatment and prevention.

title: Trade and Agricultural Health
description: This course considers the multilateral trading system as it relates to food safety, food security, animal health, plant health, and international cooperation. The course content will be of value to students interested in food safety and security, epidemiology, public health, agriculture, food science, security studies, political science, agricultural economics, veterinary medicine, and international relations.

title: Principles of Veterinary Immunology
description: Innate and adaptive defense mechanisms in domestic animals. Topics include vaccinology, immunopathology, autoimmunity, immunodeficiency, and immunomodulation.

title: Veterinary Epidemiology
description: Introduction to the principles and methods of veterinary epidemiology: emphasizing how diseases affect populations (and associated implications for individuals), and application to disease diagnosis, treatment, prevention, and control.

title: Rumen Metabolism
description: Metabolism, absorption, digestion, and passage of nutrients in the rumen; factors affecting the environment of the rumen; certain aspects of rumen function and dysfunction; techniques used in rumen research.

title: Advanced Clinical Pathology Laboratory
description: Practical training in advanced techniques of clinical chemistry and hematology used in a large clinical pathology laboratory.

title: Quantitative Analysis in Veterinary Medicine
description: Practical experience managing, describing, and interpreting data from animal health and management systems so that the data can be informative for decision-makers evaluating and implementing health programs, diagnostic strategies, and animal management processes.

title: Veterinary Virology
description: Morphology, biology, and classification of viruses and their relation to the causes of disease. Veterinary Virology is a 3 credit lecture course that is designed for veterinary students in the second year of the professional curriculum. The course provides basic and fundamental knowledge on the classification, pathogenesis, and diagnosis of viral pathogens affecting common veterinary species, including ovine, caprine, feline, canine, equine, bovine, avian, and porcine species. In addition, the course is designed to provide adequate knowledge and training for veterinarians in practice on the prevention and management of these viral diseases.

title: General Pathology
description: Etiology, pathogenesis, lesions, and termination of processes of disease, including inflammation, necrosis, regeneration, oncology, and disturbances of metabolism, circulation, and growth.

title: Veterinary Parasitology
description: Study of the helminth, arthropod, and protozoan parasites of companion and food animals. Emphases are on diagnosis, clinical signs, lesions, treatment, control epidemiology, and public heath aspects of parasitic disease.

title: Public Health Practice
description: Supervised field experience in an international, state, local, or district health agency or other appropriate health agency.

title: Veterinary Public Health
description: The linkages between human health and animal health and production. Topics include zoonotic disease, emerging and exotic animal diseases, disaster preparedness, regulatory and community health issues focusing on the role of the veterinarian in all.

title: Global Health Issues
description: A review of global health problems and various strategies to manage international health concerns. The class is open to graduate students, including veterinary students, with an interest in public health that have at least 12 hours in biology or related courses.

title: Food Safety Risk Analysis
description: Three hours of lectures on interwoven roles of risk assessment, management and communication   defined as risk analysis   will be applied to problems and policy development in food safety. This course will aid students in developing the ability to critically examine food safety risk issues from various stakeholder perspectives, leading to risk management and communication activities to reduce the impact of foodborne disease. A significant portion of the course will focus on the importance of thorough research and good communication skills, as well as the suitability of communication efforts. The course will be presented through lectures, case study presentations, and Internet-based support material including text, audio and video through the extensive database maintained by Dr. Douglas Powell of Kansas State University and colleagues (foodsafety.ksu.edu; barfblog.com).

title: Foundations of Biosecurity
description: The course examines the biosecurity policies and procedures required in high and maximum-containment research facilities. It provides a historical perspective of biosecurity, establishes definitions and explores concepts related to personnel, governmental and contemporary biosecurity topics. Agro-security topics are highlighted providing a perspective on risk and threat assessment to public health and institutional, local, regional, national and global threats.

title: Immunology of Domestic Animals
description: This course is designed to introduce graduate students to immune responses of domestic animals to pathogens and parasites.

title: Intermediate Epidemiology
description: Epidemiologic principles of disease with a focus on measures of disease occurrence, association and impact, determinants of disease diagnostic test evaluation, study design and critical literature evaluation.

title: Disease Detection, Surveillance and Risk Assessment
description: The course is focused on understanding the principles underlying quantitative risk assessments and disease detection/surveillance systems suited to a variety of animal health and food safety applications. These will then be used to advance the practical application of risk assessment and disease detection in the development of valid and useful herd, regional and national disease surveillance programs.

title: Advanced Veterinary Parasitology
description: Structure, life cycle, pathology, immunology, public health significance, diagnosis, and treatment of protozoan and metazoan parasites of veterinary significance.

title: Systemic Pathology
description: Pathology of the organ systems of domestic animals including gross and microscopic study of lesions.

title: Introduction to Infectious Disease Modeling for Animal Health
description: This is a graduate-level course focused on understanding and implementing infectious disease models using Excel and Monte-Carlo Statistical Methods as well as Spatially Explicit Stochastic models. The course is focused on food animal diseases using a mixture of lecture, scientific literature evaluation, discussion and hands on computer lab exercises.

title: Surgical Pathology
description: Practical experience in examining and processing surgical biopsy specimens and writing histopathological reports.

title: Bacterial Pathogenesis and Host Response
description: Virulence factors of infectious microorganisms and the host response to infection. Topics include pathogenesis of human and animal diseases and mechanisms of immunity.

title: Advanced Diagnostic Pathology
description: Pathologic alterations of disease with emphasis on diagnostic characteristics.

title: Applied Domestic Animal Immunology
description: Real-life immune problems of veterinary and zoonotic relevance and their application to domestic animal immune systems.

title: Advanced Principles of Pathology
description: Disease and its effects with emphasis on etiology and pathogenesis; morphologic change will be correlated with changes in chemical composition and function.

title: Pathology of Diseases of Laboratory Animals, Fish, Wildlife
Advanced Topics in Comparative Pathology
description: Selected topics to assist pathology majors in their area of specialization.

title: Pathobiology Seminar
description: Oral presentations on topics in epidemiology, food safety, immunology, microbiology, molecular biology, parasitology, pathology, and toxicology. Reports will include critical review of the relevant literature; experimental design and methodology; and presentation and critical evaluation of data. The course is for MS students.

title: Molecular Diagnostics of Infectious Diseases
description: This graduate course is aimed at reviewing, and evaluating new and improved molecular diagnostic methods for infectious diseases. Theory, development, and applications of molecular diagnostic tests will be discussed in the context of current literature. This course will provide an opportunity for students to learn and apply recent advances in the development of molecular diagnostic test.

title: Applications of Flow Cytometry
description: Theory and practical experience in the use of flow cytometry in diagnosis and research.

title: Problems in Pathobiology
description: A special problems course for graduate students working toward the MS degree in Pathobiology. The course is generally problems- or techniques-based in any of the disciplines in the Pathobiology program, conducted under the supervision of a graduate faculty in the Pathobiology Graduate Program.

title: Globalization, Cooperation, and the Food Trade
description: This course will focus on issues and topics including, but not limited to, disputes in the international agricultural and food trade, new concepts such as Food and Agriculture Security, and the food-related challenges faced by wealthy as well as economically poor societies. The course will also address such important topics as the roles played by innovation and investment in the agricultural and food system, the importance of cross-border cooperation in regulation and trade, and the accomplishments of public health and trade policy thought leaders across history.

title: Principles of Biosafety and Biocontainment
description: This course will introduce participants to the advanced and administrative principles of biosafety and biocontainment. The course targets future P.I.'s, lab managers, and individuals with previous high-containment research experience. Specific topics include laboratory biosafety levels, special considerations for agriculture and animal labs, facility and building design, regulations, biosafety practices and procedures, and risk assessment processes required in high and maximum containment research facilities.

title: Topics in Pathobiology (Ms)
description: A special course for graduate students working toward the MS degree. Lectures, readings, and discussion of topics of current interest in any of the disciplines of Pathobiology.

title: MS Report in Pathobiology
description: A written report of either laboratory-based research, field-based research, or a review paper on a topic in the major field.

title: Ms Research in Pathobiology
description: For graduate students working towards the MS degree. Individual research in the fields of epidemiology, food safety, immunology, microbiology, molecular biology, parasitology, pathology, and toxicology.

title: Pathogenic Mechanisms of Viruses
description: The goals of the course are to learn various pathogenic mechanisms (virus-host interactions) of selected virus (RNA and DNA) and Prion diseases. The course will cover the molecular, cellular and immunological bases of pathogenesis both in vitro and in animal models.

title: Rumen Microbiology
description: Lecture dealing with the microorganisms of the rumen, their habitat, diversity, structure, interactions, and biochemical activities. Techniques for enumeration, isolation and identification of ruminal microorganisms.

title: Advanced Epidemiology
description: Advanced theory and methods for designing, analyzing and interpreting epidemiologic research. Emphasis on observational study design and analysis issues including design identification and optimization, bias recognition and control, and appropriate analytical approaches for epidemiologic data.

title: Advanced Molecular Biology of Foodborne Pathogens
description: This lecture will meet once a week for 2 hours per period. The first hour of each meeting will consist of brief student presentations of research papers illustrating key concepts presented in the previous lecture. The second hour of each meeting will consist of a lecture on the indicated topic.

title: Cellular And Molecular Pathology
description: Biochemistry of the injured cell, relationship of intracellular parasitism to cellular metabolism, metabolic and genetic basis of inherited disease.

title: Pathobiology Seminar
description: Oral presentations on topics in epidemiology, food safety, immunology, microbiology, molecular biology, parasitology, pathology, and toxicology. Reports will include critical review of the relevant literature; experimental design and methodology; and presentation and critical evaluation of data. The course is for PhD students.

title: Seminal Papers in Molecular Biology
description: This discussion course will improve graduate student communication, scientific reasoning, and data analysis skills. Students will gain familiarity with the seminal papers of molecular biology that resolved key issues of the central dogma. Key breakthroughs in molecular evolution, microbial genetics, and biotechnology will be discussed.

title: Problem in Pathobiology
description: A special problems course for graduate students working toward a PhD degree in Pathobiology. The course is generally problems- or techniques-based in any of the disciplines in the program, conducted under the supervision of a graduate faculty in the Pathobiology Graduate Program.

title: Topics in Pathobiology (Phd)
description: A special course for graduate students working toward the PhD degree. Lectures, readings, and discussion of topics of current interest in any of the disciplines of Pathobiology

title: Phd Research in Pathobiology
description: For graduate students working towards the PhD degree in pathobiology. Individual research in the fields of epidemiology, food safety, immunology, microbiology, molecular biology, parasitology, pathology, and toxicology.

title: Field Experience In Dietetics And Institutional Management
Problems In Dietetics Restaurant & Institutional Management
Current Issues In Dietetics, Restaurant, And Institutionalmt
Resource Procurement And Foodservice System Planning
Genetics of Poultry and Dairy Cattle I
Genetics of Poultry and Dairy Cattle II
New Student Assembly
description: Assemblies are held once a month for new electrical engineering and computer engineering students to provide them information on the department. The students get an opportunity to meet student leaders, department faculty, and learn about department activities.

title: New Student Design Project
description: Students work in teams to design, develop, test, and present a solution to an appropriate engineering problem.

title: Introduction to Electrical Engineering
description: Basic circuit theory including voltage, current, Kirchoff's laws, and Ohm's law. Introduction to circuit elements, application circuits, complex impedance and power, and additional theory fundamental and the EE curriculum. Includes laboratory work (e.g., with multimeters and oscilloscopes) and circuit construction techniques.

title: Introduction to Electrical and Computer Engineering
