Reading Skills For Non-Native Speakers Of English
Written Communication Skills For Non Native Speakers/Engl
Oral Communication Skills For Non Native Speakers Of Engl
Pronunciation & Speaking Skills For Non-Native Speaker/Engl
Developmental English
description: Basics of standard edited (written) English with emphasis on grammar, usage, and sentence structure. This course does not fulfill requirements for the associate degree.

title: Teaching & Cultural Orientation For International Tch Asst
Expository Writing I
description: Introduction to expressive and informative writing. Frequent discussions, workshops, and conferences. Offers extensive practice in the process of writing: getting ideas, drafting, analyzing drafts, revising, and editing.

title: Expository Writing I Support
description:
Note: Expository Writing I Support credits cannot count towards the 120 credits of the degree program. This course was designed through the Kansas Board of Regents English Pathways initiative. Students who do not meet one of the multiple placement measures must enroll concurrently in ENGL 101.
Expository Writing I Support is the corequisite support course for ENGL 100 - Expository Writing I. The support course provides additional review of the background knowledge and foundational skills which promote student success in ENGL 100. The content of ENGL 100 is directly aligned with the course objectives of ENGL 100.

title: Race, Film, and American Stories
description: Introduces students to how race functions in movies, books, and more. It will further examine how movies and books activate and reflect change. Students will analyze artistic expression from one or more American Ethnic traditions, including film and literature, and possibly also music, comics, TV, and other texts.

title: Book Club
description: Cultivates a reading habit that enhances life skills such as critical reading, thinking, discussion, and writing strategies. Readings will include a range of books selected by the instructor and the students.

title: Expository Writing II
description: Introduction to writing persuasively. As with ENGL 100, uses discussion, workshops, and conferences, and emphasizes the writing process.

title: Writing Arguments for Architecture Students
description: Introduction to writing persuasively for students in Architecture. As with ENGL 100, uses discussion, workshops, and conferences, and emphasizes the writing process.

title: Fiction Into Film
description: Critical analysis of literary texts and their film adaptations.

title: Television as Literature
description: Critical analysis of television as a literary form.

title: Video Games as Literature
description: Critical analysis of video games as a literary form.

title: Classical Cultures
description: Courses examine Western culture through literature, philosophy, religion, art, and music. Introduction to Western Humanities courses: ENGL 230, ENGL 231, ENGL 233, ENGL 234 may be taken individually and in any order. This course examines Ancient Greek and Roman cultures.

title: Medieval and Renaissance
description: Courses examine Western culture through literature, philosophy, religion, art, and music. Introduction to Western Humanities courses: ENGL 230, ENGL 231, ENGL 233, ENGL 234 may be taken individually and in any order. This course examines Middle Ages to mid 1600s.

title: Reformation to Enlightenment
description: Courses examine Western culture through literature, philosophy, religion, art, and music. Introduction to Western Humanities courses: ENGL 230, ENGL 231, ENGL 233, ENGL 234 may be taken individually and in any order. This course examines the Beginnings of Protestantism through the 18th century.

title: Modern
description: Courses examine Western culture through literature, philosophy, religion, art, and music. Introduction to Western Humanities courses: ENGL 230, ENGL 231, ENGL 233, ENGL 234 may be taken individually and in any order. This course examines the 19th century to the present.

title: Introduction to Literature
description: Study of fiction, poetry, drama, and nonfiction.

title: The Short Story
description: Study of short stories from world literature with emphasis on American, British, and Continental.

title: British Literature
description: Selected writers from various periods of British literary history.

title: Introduction to Creative Writing
description: An introduction to creative writing with practice in short fiction, poetry, and creative nonfiction.

title: American Literature
description: Selected writers from various periods in American literary history.

title: Wild Lit
description: An introductory survey of environmental literature.

title: Studies in World Literature and Culture
description: Investigation of a particular theme or genre in World literature or culture.

title: Introduction to American Ethnic Literatures
description: Study of ethnic and multicultural literatures of the United States, such as African American, Asian American, Latina/o, Jewish, and Native American.  May offer cross-cultural comparisons of different ethnic traditions or may focus on a single ethnic tradition.

title: Great Books
description: Introduction to world classics from past to present.

title: Selected Studies in English
description: Selected studies in literature, language, rhetoric, and cultural studies.

title: Honors Introduction to the  Humanities I
description: Study of selected major works of history, literature, and philosophy of central importance in the Western cultural tradition. Emphasis on classroom discussion and writing interpretive essays.

title: Honors Topics in English
description: Readings and colloquia in selected topics in literature or language.

title: Expository Writing III
description: Advanced practice in writing a variety of expository forms: personal essays and informative and persuasive reports. Additional work on style and the demands of various rhetorical situations.

title: Technical Writing
description: This writing course will provide students from a number of business, technology and aviation disciplines with intensive practice writing the kinds of documents that are common in their future professional lives.

title: Introduction to Classical Literature in Translation
description: Introduction to canonical works of Greek and Roman Literature in translation. Discussion of classical texts with attention to material culture, visual media, and classical culture.

title: Introduction To Literary Studies
description: Elements of literary form and style: an introduction to criticism for English majors. Intended as a first course in the analysis of form and technique, an introduction to literary terms commonly used in later courses, and practice in critical writing. Readings from a broad range: poems, plays, essays, and novels.

title: Writing with AI: Fundamentals of Generative AI Literacy
description: Introduction to the fundamentals of generative AI literacy, including writing, research, and workflow terminology and applications and the benefits, risks, and responsible use of these tools.

title: Introduction to Popular Culture
description: Theories and methods for studying popular culture including literature, film, comics, television, social media, games, and other forms of popular culture.

title: Television
description: Study of television as a genre from historical beginnings to contemporary moment. Emphasis on form and critical analysis.

title: Literature and Technology
description: Students will read in a variety of literary, civic, and professional genres about technology and its effect on society; through writing, understand technology in terms of humanistic themes.

title: Introduction to Digital Humanities
description: Explore digital mediums for humanities scholarship, focusing primarily on literary texts.

title: Fiction
description: Study of fiction as genre from historical and contemporary contexts and cultures. Emphasis on form and critical analysis.

title: Film
description: Study of film as genre from historical beginnings through classic Hollywood to contemporary cinema. Emphasis on form and critical analysis.

title: Poetry
description: Study of poetry as genre from historical and contemporary contexts and cultures. Emphasis on form and critical analysis.

title: Drama
description: Study of drama as genre from historical and contemporary contexts and cultures. Emphasis on form and critical analysis.

title: Introduction to Shakespeare
description: Study of representative comedies, histories, and tragedies.

title: Literature For Children
description: Survey emphasizing the reading and evaluating of books for children. Required for licensure in elementary education.

title: British Survey I
description: British literature from c. 400 to c. 1700.

title: British Survey II
description: British literature from c.1700 to the present.

title: American Survey I
description: American literature from pre-colonial times to the Civil War.

title: American Survey II
description: American literature from the Civil War to the present.

title: Multicultural Children's Literature
description: Theories of differences and underrepresented experiences as related to, for example, ability, gender, ethnicity, and sexuality, through children's literature.

title: Selected American Ethnic Literature
description: Comparative study of ethnic and multicultural literatures of the United States, such as African American, Asian American, Latina/o, Jewish, and Native American. May focus on a single ethnic tradition outside of the four federally recognized minority groups.

title: Fable and Fantasy
description: Study of modern works in the fabulous or fantastic modes in relation to the traditions underlying them.

title: Literature and the Body
description: Study of literature of the body in relation to health, psychology, life cycle, the environment, and cultural patterns and influences.

title: Topics in English
description: Selected studies in literature and language.

title: Honors Seminar in English
description: Readings and colloquia in selected masterpieces.

title: Advanced Expository Writing For Prospective Teachers
description: Expository writing and a brief introduction to the history and theory of teaching writing, primarily for candidates for Secondary licensure in English.

title: Writing with AI: Intermediate Generative AI Skills
description: Intermediate-level prompt engineering, informed editing, and writing process skills for using generative AI tools for professional and personal writing tasks, with attention to audience and ethical considerations.

title: Written Communication For Engineers
description: Study and intensive use of writing forms characteristic of professional practice.

title: Written Communication For the Workplace
description: Theory and intensive writing practice for students entering the workplace.

title: The Structure of English
description: Systematic study of the structure of the English language utilizing linguistic approaches to the analysis of sounds, lexicon, grammatical structure, and social variation.

title: Linguistics For Teachers of English
description: Pedagogical aspects of the structure, history, and use of the English language.

title: Themes in Literature
description: Explores the literary treatment of important and recurring themes.

title: Literary Kinds
description: Examines the characteristics, the growth and development, or the uses of specified literary genres.

title: Literature and Society
description: Literature in relation to social and cultural patterns and influences.

title: Introduction to Fiction Writing
description: A practical introduction to short fiction writing.

title: Introduction to Poetry Writing
description: A practical introduction to poetry writing.

title: Introduction to Creative Nonfiction
description: A practical introduction to creative nonfiction or what can be called ''the literature of fact.'' Writers of creative nonfiction use many of the stylistic and literary tools that fiction writers and poets use, but in the service of rendering factual, literally accurate prose.

title: The Bible
description: Literature, history, and cultural backgrounds of the Hebrew Bible (Old Testament) and/or the New Testament and early Christianity.

title: American English
description: Systematic study of the English language as it has been and is spoken in the continental United States. Topics include regional, social, and ethnic variation, language ideologies, intersections of language and power, and analysis of language variation.

title: Introduction to History and Theory of Composition/Rhetoric
description: Content may emphasize visual rhetoric and literacy, literacy theory, and persuasion, and how such topics are understood in particular contexts and cultures.

title: Development of the English Language
description: Depicts the English language in its place among other world languages, and introduces students to the major ways in which English has changed through time. Considers both internal and external influences as causes of language change.

title: Hum Seminar
description: Study in depth of selected major figures and movements in Western arts, ideas, and literature.

title: English Internship
description: Choice between research and service learning internships. A research internship works with English professor on semester-long research project. A service learning intern works with a community organization or other external office to develop written and other materials on behalf of that entity.

title: Special Investigations in English
description: Individual investigation in authors, genres, periods of literature or language.

title: Honors Tutorial in English
description: Individually guided study in which the student will formulate and explore a narrowly defined topic in literature or language.

title: Honors Project
description: Open only to Arts & Sciences students who are active members of the University Honors Program.

title: Writing Center Theory/Practice
description: Readings in the field of writing center studies, with attention to topics such as ESL, literacy, and multiculturalism. Literacy studied as it intersects with race, gender, class, ethnicity, and social and institutional structures. Application of theory through a research project and peer tutoring apprenticeship.

title: Topics in Classical Literature in Translation
description: A detailed study of Greek and/or Roman literature in translation in a small discussion setting. Either an open survey of classical literature or the consideration of a specific question.

title: Introduction to Professional Writing
description: Intensive practice in applying rhetorical principles to a number of genres common in non-academic professions and workplaces; an introduction to allied topics such as document design and editing.

title: Writing Creatively and Collaboratively with AI
description: Advanced practice utilizing generative AI tools to hone writing practice and invention for specific audiences, and the resonsibilities associated with entering into a human/machine collaboration.

title: Written Communication For the Sciences
description: Theory and intensive writing practice for students in the basic and applied sciences.

title: Women in Literature
description: Study of literary works by or about women. Emphasis on historical and cultural contexts.

title: Literature For Adolescents
description: Selecting, reading, and evaluating books for adolescents.

title: Holocaust Literature
description: Examines texts that represent and react to the experience of Jews during the Holocaust. Emphasis on historical and cultural contexts.

title: Selected World Literature
description: Addresses writing by authors whose native origins lie outside Europe or the United States. Content may vary with instructor. May examine literature from several countries or regions, concentrate on literature from one country or region, or focus on a topic which transcends national or regional boundaries. Works are written in or translated into English.

title: Literary Studies Abroad
description: Travel abroad, with selected readings, lectures, and discussions which explore the relationships between literary texts and their physical and cultural environments.

title: Career Paths in English and the Humanities
description: Professional skills development alongside guided research on career options and paths to success for students majoring in English and other humanities disciplines.

title: Special Research In English
description: Individual investigation in authors, genres, periods of literature, or language. Background of preparation needed for investigation undertaken.

title: General Phonetics
description: Description and classification of speech sounds according to point and manner of articulation. Transcription in the International Phonetic Association Alphabet. Includes sounds of English, French, Spanish, German, and others.

title: Historical Linguistics
description: Internal and comparative reconstruction of earlier forms of languages. Genetic relationships in language families, and various typological considerations. Includes French, Spanish, and others.

title: Topics In Linguistics
description: Seminar on a special-topic in linguistics: decipherment of ancient writing systems, linguistics applied to the teaching of English or other languages, discourse analysis (especially of spoken texts), etc. Topic to be announced for semester in which offered.

title: Expository Writing Workshop
description: Course emphasizes stylistic analysis of modern non-fiction prose in the sciences, social sciences, and humanities. Extensive writing required.

title: Readings in Medieval Literature
description: This course is one within a sequence of period studies covering the chronological range of English and American literature. Within these historical periods, the specific course contents will vary by semester and instructor. They may emphasize literary figures and movements, historical and cultural contexts, or different genres and forms within the periods.

Within these historical periods, the specific course contents will vary by semester and instructor. They may emphasize literary figures and movements, historical and cultural contexts, or different genres and forms within the periods.

Readings in Major Authors

They may emphasize literary figures and movements, historical and cultural contexts, or different genres and forms within the periods.

title: Playwriting
description: Theoretical study and practical application of techniques of playwriting with regard to plot, characters, and production; emphasis on the one-act form.

title: Advanced Creative Writing:  Nonfiction
description: Advanced writing of prose creative nonfiction.

title: Topics in British Literature
description: This course addresses topics not confined to a single period in a national literature. Specific course content will vary by semester and instructor. It may emphasize cross-national subjects, literary criticism, the development of a theme or genre over time, new perspectives from social, intellectual, or cultural studies, or non-traditional texts and topics.

title: Methods and Techniques of Learning a Second Language
Topics in American Literature
description: This course addresses topics not confined to a single period in a national literature. Specific course content will vary by semester and instructor. It may emphasize cross-national subjects, literary criticism, the development of a theme or genre over time, new perspectives from social, intellectual, or cultural studies, or non-traditional texts and topics.

title: Topics in Literature For the Young
description: This course addresses topics not confined to a single period in a national literature. Specific course content will vary by semester and instructor. It may emphasize cross-national subjects, literary criticism, the development of a theme or genre over time, new perspectives from social, intellectual, or cultural studies, or non-traditional texts and topics.

title: Topics in Literature
description: This course addresses topics not confined to a single period in a national literature. Specific course content will vary by semester and instructor. It may emphasize cross-national subjects, literary criticism, the development of a theme or genre over time, new perspectives from social, intellectual, or cultural studies, or non-traditional texts and topics.

title: Capstone Seminar
description: A writing- and discussion-intensive course that provides a culminating experience to the English major.

title: Old English
description: The elements of Old English grammar, with readings in prose and poetry.

title: Critical Approaches to Children's Literature
description: Introduction to advanced study of children's and young adult literature.

title: Theories of Cultural Studies
description: An overview of theoretical approaches to the study of culture and their current application in English studies.

title: Studies in a Literary Genre
description: The specific contents will vary by semester and instructor, but the studies courses will reflect concerns with literary and rhetorical forms and genres; with specific authors, periods, or literary movements; with perspectives from social, intellectual, and cultural studies; with literary themes; or with language or linguistics.

title: Topics in Film / Screen Studies
description: A topic course in film, television, and/or media analysis that applies a theoretical approach or approaches.

title: Studies in a Major Author
description: The specific contents will vary by semester and instructor, but the studies courses will reflect concerns with literary and rhetorical forms and genres; with specific authors, periods, or literary movements; with perspectives from social, intellectual, and cultural studies; with literary themes; or with language or linguistics.

title: Studies in Children's/Young Adult Literature
description: Addresses topics not confined to a single period in a national literature. May emphasize cross-national subjects, literary criticism, the development of a theme or genre over time, new perspectives from social, intellectual, or cultural studies, or non-traditional texts and topics.

title: Studies in a Literary Period
description: The specific contents will vary by semester and instructor, but the studies courses will reflect concerns with literary and rhetorical forms and genres; with specific authors, periods, or literary movements; with perspectives from social, intellectual, and cultural studies; with literary themes; or with language or linguistics.

description: The specific contents will vary by semester and instructor, but the studies courses will reflect concerns with literary and rhetorical forms and genres; with specific authors, periods, or literary movements; with perspectives from social, intellectual, and cultural studies; with literary themes; or with language or linguistics.

title: Cultural Studies
description: A topic course in Cultural Studies that applies a theoretical approach or approaches.

title: Theories of Composition and Rhetoric
description: An introduction to major theories of composition and/or rhetoric.

title: Studies in Composition and Rhetoric
description: The specific contents will vary by semester and instructor, but the studies courses will reflect concerns with literary and rhetorical forms and genres; with specific authors, periods, or literary movements; with perspectives from social, intellectual, and cultural studies; with literary themes; or with language or linguistics.

title: Business Communication
description: A writing-intensive course intended for advanced undergraduate students, graduate students, and working professionals in business.

title: Studies in Language and Linguistics
description: The specific contents will vary by semester and instructor, but the studies courses will reflect concerns with literary and rhetorical forms and genres; with specific authors, periods, or literary movements; with perspectives from social, intellectual, and cultural studies; with literary themes; or with language or linguistics.

title: Scientific Communication
description: A writing-intensive course intended for advanced undergraduate students, graduate students, and working professionals in science.

title: Studies in Technical Communications
description: The specific contents will vary by semester and instructor, but the studies courses will reflect concerns with literary and rhetorical forms and genres; with specific authors, periods, or literary movements; with perspectives from social, intellectual, and cultural studies; with literary themes; or with language or linguistics.

title: Creative Writing Workshop/Short Fiction
description: Advanced writing of short prose fiction.

title: Advanced Playwriting
description: Further study in the writing of drama; emphasis on problems of writing the full-length play.

title: Creative Writing Workshop: The YA Novel
description: Advanced novel writing focused on young adult readers. Students will read young adult (YA) novels and analyze their structure and form. Students will workshop sections of their own young adult novel.

title: History of the English Language
description: The development of British and American English from Indo-European origins to the present.

title: Methods And Techniques Of Learning A Second Language
Literary Theory
description: A historical survey of literary theory and criticism.

title: Professional Writing Internship
description: Faculty-supervised professional experience, emphasizing application of writing skills in professional contexts. Student projects must be approved by on-site supervisor and faculty supervisor. Report must be submitted at the end of the semester.

title: Problems In English
description: Independent study in major authors, genres, and periods of English and American literature and language.

title: Graduate Studies In English
description: A survey of the methods and aims of advanced level research and scholarship in language and literature. Taken in the first year of study toward the M. A. in English as an orientation to the profession.

title: Practicum In Teaching University Expository Writing
description: Required of GTAs teaching Expository Writing in the English Department. Instruction in the theory and practice of teaching in a university expository writing program. Cannot count in the student's program of study.

title: Selected Topics in the Study of Language
description: Intensive research concerned with one or more topics in the structure and history of the English language.

title: Seminar in Literature
description: Intensive research concerned with one or more literary genres, periods, authors, or issues/problems.

title: Seminar in Cultural Studies
description: Intensive research concerned with one or more topics central to the theory and practice of Cultural Studies.

title: Seminar in Composition and Rhetoric
description: Intensive research on materials germane to the history, structure, and processes central to the concerns of Composition and Rhetoric.

title: Selected Topics In The Study Of Literature
Introduction Environmental Sciences
description: Explores environmental science and environmental issues at global and local scales, points out the diverse, interdisciplinary backgrounds and skills needed to solve environmental issues, and reinforces key concepts through field visits.

title: Environmental Sciences Internship
description: Students will gain experience in the work force, improve communication and interpersonal relation skills, build professional network, and apply classroom knowledge to solve problems in a professional setting. Students will learn what they may enjoy or dislike about potential careers and employers and share that information with their peers.

title: Insects and People
description: Intended for undergraduate non-majors. The focus will be on the global impact of insects and their relatives on human concerns, from acting as disease vectors, agricultural pests, and pollinators to their roles in art, history, and religion.

title: Introduction to Entomology
description: An introduction to the foundational areas of Entomology, including classification, identification, insect biology, and applied Entomology.

title: Insect Collecting Techniques and Collections
description: An introduction to the techniques used to collect insects in different habitats and times of day, as well as the preparation of insect specimens and their maintanence in collections.

title: Introduction to Animal Health Entomology
description: An introduction to Animal Health Entomology, including the wide range of insects that vector diseases, the types of organisms that are spread by insects, the diseases those organisms cause, and management techniques for the insects.

title: Introduction to Plant Health Entomology
description: Introduction to the insects that influence plant health, including their identification, their influence, the mechanisms they use to benefit or harm plants, and their management or management abilities.

title: Insect Sampling and Surveillance
description: Introduction and hands-on experience with insect sampling and surveillance techniques.

title: Art and Insects
description: Science and art are not different ends of an academic continuum, but rather simultaneously found in all disciplines within academia. The goal of this course is to provide a framework and appreciation that students can utilize to consider all aspects of novel experiences and problems, as global problems require global thinking. The model system for this course will be insects, where students will learn about evolution, genetics, behavior, and biodiversity, while also learning art history, interpretation, analysis of color and shape, perception, and the role of insect representations in the humanities. While there are specific lectures that cover Insects and Art separately, the majority of lectures combine both science and art.

title: Introduction to Insect Data Analysis
description: Introduction and hands-on experience with basic experimental design and common data analysis techniques used in insect sampling, surveillance, and experiments.

title: Economic Entomology
description: Classification, biology, ecology, and principles of management of economically important insects.

title: Animal Health Entomology
description: Introduction to biology of arthropods that affect animal health. The main focus is on strategies to manage arthropods that negatively affect the health and productivity of animals, including beef and dairy cattle, swine, poultry, horses, sheep, goats, pets, and urban wildlife. This course is particularly recommended for students interested in animal science, pre-veterinary medicine, and other agricultural curricula.

title: Animal Health Entomology Laboratory
description: This laboratory course complements ENTOM 305 and offers hands-on experience in identification and management of arthropods of veterinary importance. Includes field trips to food animal operations.

title: General Entomology
description: A basic study of insects and related arthropods, their structure, physiology, behavior, and relations to plants and animals, including man.

title: Horticultural Entomology
description: Biological principles and management considerations for insects and related arthropods affecting horticulture. Practical application of classification and life history information for accurate recognition, monitoring, and pest management decisions. Control tactics and conservation of beneficial species.

title: Insects Of Home, Lawn, And Garden Laboratory
Basic Beekeeping
description: The basics of Beekeeping, including all components of hive development and maintenance, pre- and post-harvest, and business operations are covered.

title: Crops, Insects, and Agroecosystems
description: Insects play an important role in crop production and impact many agroecosystems that produce our food. This course will utilize inquiry-based learning to explore the impact of insects on crops and agroecosystems, focusing model crops such as corn.

title: Undergraduate Research Experience
description: Open to students pursuing undergraduate research projects.

title: Insect and Arthropod Biology
description: The advanced study of insect and arthropod identification and biology.

title: Seminar in Entomology
description: Explore current research, teaching, and extension ideas and projects in Entomology through the Departmental seminar series.

title: Entomological Problems
description: Study or work in a specific area of entomology that is specialized around student interests or career goals.

title: Research Experience in Entomology
description: A research experience associated with Entomology.

title: Teaching Experience in Entomology
description: Teaching experience in an Entomology classroom or online environment.

title: Extension Experience in Entomology
description: Extension experience in an area associated with Entomology

title: Entomological Topics
description: Study of a select topic within Entomology.

title: Internship in Entomology
description: An internship experience in an area associated with entomology at an organization outside of the Department of Entomology.

title: Survey of Horticultural Ornamental and Food Crop Pests
Capstone Experience
description: A research, extension, teaching, or industry-based experience that builds upon an individual student's academic training in preparation for a successful career. A final report is to be submitted at the end of the semester along with a presentation over the capstone experience.

title: Forensic Entomology
description: The study of insects, and other arthropods, their physiology and life cycle in association with criminal events is the realm of Forensic entomology. The purpose is uncovering and revealing information useful to an investigation. This is an introductory course designed to provide a basic understanding of the interaction between the discipline of entomology and the legal system. We will discuss the importance of insects in forensics related to aspects in criminal investigations (case studies). An emphasis will be placed on collection, preservation, identification, analysis, and presentation of information derived from arthropod samples collected as evidence.

title: Introduction to Insects of Stored Products
description: Biology, ecology, and behavior of stored-product insects and current practices involved in their control.

title: Insect Pest Diagnosis
description: Diagnosis of plant damage by insects and mites, recognition of harmful insects and mites and beneficial insects. Emphasis on field crop pests but pests of other crops will be considered if there is sufficient interest.

title: Pesticides, People, and the Environment
description: Introduction to insecticides and related pesticides, including their classification, formulation, properties, mode of action, metabolism, resistance, benefits and environmental impact, and federal and state laws that regulate their development, sale, use and storage.

title: Introduction to Biological Control
description: The theory and practice of biological control with an emphasis on natural enemies of insect pests. Relationship and importance of insect ecology and integrated pest management to biological control. Experimental approaches, evaluation, recognition and life histories of beneficial species will be covered.

title: Integrative Behavioral Ecology
description: This course is designed to provide an overview of the genetics, ecology, and evolution of insect behavior, emphasizing research done in insect systems. The topics covered in this course will include: (1) Conceptual and empirical framework for studying behavior, (2) the genetics of behavior, (3) ""kinds"" of behaviors, and (4) detailed examples of each ""kind"" of behavior.

title: Introduction to Molecular Entomology
description: Introduction to use of molecular techniques to address fundamental and applies entomological problems, including systematics, marker-assisted selection of resistant genotypes of plants, mechanisms of insecticide resistance, insect transgenesis, risk assessment of genetically modified organisms, and other topics.

title: Insect Evolution
description: This course covers the evolution and biodiversity of insects. Topics covered include  insects in the fossil record, insect phylogenies, the traits responsible for insect diversity, and the evolution of  traits like wings, color, mimicry, learning, behavior, farming, parasitic lifestyles and social living.

title: Introduction to Arthropod Vectors of Human Pathogens
description: The course will focus on vectors of medical and veterinary importance. Students will learn several underlying aspects involved in pathogen-vector-vertebrate host interactions, including those associated with cellular, molecular and innate immune response of insects to pathogens they transmit. Students will be introduced to current research programs and topics of interest in the field of medical entomology.

title: Introduction to Insect Molecular Techniques
description: A hands-on introduction to basic molecular biology techniques used in understanding insect biology and physiology, including nucleic acid isolation, polymerase chain reaction, RNA interference, and analysis of experimental results.

title: Insect Pests of Field Crops Stored Grain and Livestock
description: Insect Pests of Field Crops Stored Grain and Livestock

title: Introduction to Insect Physiology
description: Functions of insect systems for development, metamorphosis, and reproduction. Physiological and biochemical mechanisms underlying insect activities, behavior, and ecological adaptations.

title: Insect Ecology
description: Abiotic and biotic factors underlying the distribution, abundance, and dynamics of insects. How to measure these factors, how they affect insect population processes, interactions, and community structure, especially in agricultural systems. Emphasis on basic concepts and their application, experimental methods and field techniques.

title: Insect Taxonomy
description: Laboratory study of insect Order and family-group identification. Proper preparation and maintenance of adult insect collections. Lecture stresses the principles of systematics, legal principles of nomenclature, and the phylogeny of insects and their near relatives. For beginning graduate and advanced undergraduate students.

title: Introduction to Plant Resistance of Pests
description: Basic concepts of the biology, ecology, genetics and breeding for pest resistance in plants.

title: Problems in Entomology
description: For nonthesis or nondissertation studies. Work in various fields of entomology.

title: Insects of Stored Products
description: Biology, ecology, and behavior of stored-product insects and current practices involved in their control.

title: Insect Pest Management
description: The theory and practice of Integrated Pest Management (IPM) with an emphasis on pests in agricultural systems. Integrated concepts include but are not limited to economic thresholds, sampling plans, plant resistance to insects, biological control, pesticides and resistance management. Case studies from several row-crop systems are used to explore key concepts.

title: Experience/Ext Entom
description: Major emphasis is to give students a realistic view of the history, structure, philosophy, and position responsibilities assumed by entomology state and area specialists within the Cooperative Extension Service through hands-on experience.

title: Biological Control
description: The theory and practice of biological control with an emphasis on natural enemies of insect pests. Relationship and importance of insect ecology and integrated pest management to biological control. Experimental approaches, evaluation, recognition and life histories of beneficial species will be covered.

title: Advanced Integrative Behavioral Ecology
description: This course is designed to provide an advanced overview of the genetics, ecology, and evolution of insect behavior, emphasizing research done in insect systems. The topics covered in this course will include: (1) Conceptual and empirical framework for studying behavior, (2) the genetics of behavior, (3) ""kinds"" of behaviors, and (4) detailed examples of each ""kind"" of behavior.

title: Molecular Entomology
description: Introduction to use of molecular techniques to address fundamental and applies entomological problems, including systematics, marker-assisted selection of resistant genotypes of plants, mechanisms of insecticide resistance, insect transgenesis, risk assessment of genetically modified organisms, and other topics.

title: Advanced Insect Evolution
description: This course covers the evolution and biodiversity of insects.  Topics covered include  insects in the fossil record, insect phylogenies, the traits responsible for insect diversity, and the evolution of traits like wings, color, mimicry, learning, behavior, farming, parasitic lifestyles and social living.

title: Plant Virus-Vector Interactions
description: A study of modes of virus transmission, important arthropod vectors, plant responses to viruses and insects, and current literature and techniques.

title: Immature Insects
description: Identification of immature insects to the family level, with emphasis on terrestrial Holometabola and aquatic insects. Techniques for collecting and preserving immatures, with an emphasis on field collecting and identification in laboratory.

title: Insect Control By Host Plant Resistance
Arthropod Vectors of Human Pathogens
description: The course will focus on vectors of medical and veterinary importance. Students will learn several underlying aspects involved in pathogen-vector-vertebrate host interactions, including those associated with cellular, molecular and innate immune response of insects to pathogens they transmit. Students will be introduced to current research programs and topics of interest in the field of medical entomology.

title: Scanning Electron Microscopy
description: Use of scanning electron miscroscopy for studying organic, inorganic or synthetic materials. Includes theory of SEM operation, techniques for specimen preparation, one-on-one sessions with a technician on a Hitachi 3500N variable pressure SEM, and post-image processing.

title: Insect Molecular Techniques
description: Hands-on experience with basic molecular biology techniques used in understanding insect biology and physiology, including nucleic acid isolation, polymerase chain reaction, RNA interference, and analysis of experimental results. Students will conduct an individual research project and presentation.

title: Advanced Plant Resistance to Insects
description: Plant resistance in crop plants including transgenic plants. Insect behavior, physiology, and ecology as affected by resistance. Discussion of methods of assessing and quantifying plant resistance.

title: Toxicology and Properties of Insecticides
description: Advanced study of synthetic and naturally occurring insecticides with emphasis on distribution and elimination, metabolism, mechanism of action, resistance mechanisms, and current advances in new insecticide discovery and new approaches to insect control. The lab focuses on providing hands-on experience with techniques frequently used by toxicologists.

title: Insect Physiology
description: Functions of insect systems for development, metamorphosis, and reproduction. Physiological and biochemical mechanisms underlying insect activities, behavior, and ecological adaptations.

title: Conventional/Molecular Methods Eval Crop Plnt Resist Pests
description: A series of laboratories illustrating different modes of plant resistance to pests, quantification of resistance effects, resistance gene flow, plant DNA isolation & quantification, and molecular marker-assisted selection of resistance genes of interest. Students develop hands-on experience using both conventional and molecular techniques to identify and quantify genetic plant resistance to pests.

title: Ecology Of Insects In Natural And Agronomic Environments
Advanced Insect Ecology
description: This course addresses population and community ecology, plant-insect interactions, insect biodiversity and biogeography, and applied ecology.   The course will weave basic ecological theory and principles with their application to entomological problems such as conservation, biological control, agriculture, and insect-vectored diseases of plants and humans.

title: Advanced Insect Taxonomy
description: Advanced laboratory study of insect Order and Family-group identification. Proper preparation and maintenance of adult insect collections. Lecture stresses the principles of systematics, legal principles of nomenclature, and the phylogeny of insects and their near relatives.

title: Report in Entomology
description: Work in various fields of entomology.

title: Topics in Entomology
description: Selected topics for advanced study in entomology.

title: Teaching Experience in Entomology
description: Teaching experiences for graduate students, including classroom instruction, development of curriculum materials, and other pedagogical methods.

title: Research In Entomology
description: Dissertation credit for students majoring in entomology.

title: Business Innovation and the Entrepreneurial Mindset
description: An examination of business innovation and provides students with an understanding of how creativity and the entrepreneurial mindset impact the success of organizations.

title: Intrapreneurship
description: The role of the organizational member is examined as to how ideas and innovations are created, nurtured, assessed, and implemented within existing organizations. Challenges to be studied include how entrepreneurial insight can be channeled toward product creation, how product championing can lead to legitimization within the organization, and how product and organizational strategies can be implemented, assessed and renewed.

title: Funding Your Business
description: This course is designed to provide potential entrepreneurs and small business owners with sufficient knowledge of applied finance to start, fund, and run an entrepreneurial firm. We will focus on financial funding, forecasting, and decision making, as well as understanding growth projections, venture capital financing, exit strategies, and the application of organizational theories to better understand the entrepreneurial firm.

title: Entrepreneurial Experience
description: Explores the creative and innovative careers of successful entrepreneurs. Students will learn what it takes to be a successful entrepreneur from a multitude of business owners; learning from their extensive professional backgrounds and personal experiences. Will promote a deeper understanding of common entrepreneurial pitfalls and explores the skills and tools necessary to start and grow a successful business.

title: Entrepreneurship
description: The role of the entrepreneur is examined in the conception, start-up, organization, and development of new independent businesses.  New venture problems to be studied include identification of possible new products and services, evaluation of practical commercial potential, and development of a business plan, with attention to financing, operating, and marketing.

title: Online Presence Management and E-Commerce
description: A practical examination of concepts, processes, and technologies necessary to facilitate e-commerce and manage your business’s online visibility and reputation.

title: International Entrepreneurship Experience
description: This course is designed to provide students with an opportunity to observe international business and entrepreneurship perspectives by participating in a field trip outside the United States. This experiential course may involve a combination of business visits, cultural experiences, internship activities, applied research projects, personal reflection exercises, or other learning activities while abroad.

title: Entrepreneurship Internship
description: Applied entrepreneurial business experience designed to coordinate the interests of students and firms.

title: Topics in Entrepreneurship
description: Discussion and analysis of current issues in entrepreneurship, including topics related to new theory and the practice of entrepreneurship.

title: Social Entrepreneurship
description: Social entrepreneurship is one of the fastest growing disciplines to emerge in management and is increasingly getting recognition as an important policy strategy at the national level. It is concerned with how to engage the talents and tools of professional entrepreneurship to address critical social needs both within and beyond the market. Social entrepreneurship is situated within a framework of social institutions, organizations, and entrepreneurial change in response to human and citizen rights and needs. The relevant social institutions include government, the market, education, philanthropy, and the family. Organizations may be for profit or non-profit, but all organizations, regardless of their legal structure, are seen to have a social purpose. Several non-market venues for sustainable social change will be examined in addition to the traditional delivery of goods and services through the market. The course provides a number of crucial tools and resources that may better orient and equip the prospective social entrepreneur to identify and act on opportunities.

title: Entrepreneurial Consulting
description: This course is designed to provide students and faculty in the Entrepreneurship program the opportunity to give business assistance to small firms in the University community.

title: Technology and Innovation Management
description: The course focuses on four themes: (1) the source, discovery, and evaluation of innovation and opportunities; (2) the provisions of innovation (products and services) that satisfy the needs of customers; (3) the development of mechanisms to appropriate the returns from the exploitation of the innovation; and (4) the organization of efforts to innovate.

title: New Venture Creation
description: An in-depth examination of the nature of entrepreneurship including success factors, the requirements of successful new venture planning and implementation, and researching the current literature in the field.

title: Technology and Innovation Management
description: The course focuses on four themes: (1) the source, discovery, and evaluation of innovation and opportunities; (2) the provisions of innovation (products and services) that satisfy the needs of customers; (3) the development of mechanisms to appropriate the returns from the exploitation of the innovation; and (4) the organization of efforts to innovate

title: New Venture Creation
description: An in-depth examination of the nature of entrepreneurship including success factors, the requirements of successful new venture planning and implementation, and researching the current literature in the field.

title: Strategic Innovation
description: Today's top companies succeed by making innovation a fundamental part of their strategy. As the pace of change continues to increase and the threat of disruption becomes the new normal, company leaders need to have a focused and effective approach to innovation. Students will be introduced to some of the core tools and strategies companies use to harness the innovative abilities, talents, and passion of their employees.

title: Introduction to the University Honors Program
description: Directions and goals for the honors program.

title: CAT Community Connections
description: Interdisciplinary course that explores the connections among two different fields, courses, or disciplines.

title: Student Success Seminar
description: Provides students with strategies for creating success in college and life. Topics to be covered include accepting personal responsibility, motivation, academic self-management, self-awareness, and life balance.

title: Environmental Design Studio I
description: Foundation studies introducing principles, processes, and vocabularies of environmental design. Instruction in two and three dimensional visualization of objects and spaces. Instruction in the use of instrument-aided drawing, freehand drawing, and model building to represent and communicate design ideas at different scales of observation.

title: Environmental Design Studio II
description: Foundation studies introducing principles, processes, and vocabularies of environmental design. Instruction in two and three dimensional visualization of objects and spaces. Instruction in the use of instrument-aided drawing, freehand drawing, and model building to represent and communicate design ideas at different scales of observation.

title: Survey of the Design, Planning and Community Development Professions
description: Overview of the environmental design professions. Comparative study of the working methods, and societal and occupational roles of the architect, interior architect, industrial designer, landscape architect, planner, and community developer.

title: Studio Seminar
description: Seminar for students enrolled in ENVD 201. Topics related to studio and student success will be covered.

title: Indian Art, Architecture & Culture
description: This course is designed to assist students in understanding India and its cultural, artistic, and architectural heritage so that students can develop a deeper understanding and appreciation for Indian culture, people and their way of life.

title: Studio For Environmental Design and Graphics
Theory of Environmental Design Honors II
Theory of Environmental Design Honors III
Design Drawing
description: This course offers an introduction to and sustained freehand drawing practice in fundamental drawing systems, methods and media for the environmental design professions.

title: History of the Designed Environment I
description: An introduction to key ideas, practices, and works that have shaped global histories of the built environment through the mid-18th century.

title: History of the Designed Environment II
description: An introduction to key ideas, practices, and works that have shaped global histories of the built environment from the mid-18th century to the present.

title: Techology of the Designed Environment Laboratory
Problems in Basic Design
description: A study of specified problems in elementary environmental design under the guidance of a member of the staff.

title: Undergraduate Research
description: Mentored undergraduate research in architecture, interior architecture and product design, landscape architecture or planning.

title: K-State APDesign:  APDPro
description: This course indicates and tracks which College of Architecture, Planning & Design students complete the APDPro  requirements for each year.

title: Italian, Art, History and Culture
description: The course provides a synthesis of art, history, design and culture in the context of the Italian Peninsula and the Mediterranean region over time.  Integration of material covered in lectures with field trips in Italy is expected.

title: Education Abroad Orientation
description: Preparation for participation in study abroad with a focus on specific information pertaining to the design professions and the analysis of the built and natural environment.

title: International Field Study
description: Group observation of design precedents (ancient to present day) of a selected region, conducted in situ , to study significant aspects of environment, culture, and technology as expressed in vernacular and designed environments.

title: History Of American Architecture & Allied Design 1
History Of American Architecture And Allied Design 2
Problems In Environmental Design
description: A study of specific environmental design problems under the direction of a member(s) of the departmental staff.

title: Problems in Environmental Design & Planning
description: This course is intended to provide students an opportunity to focus on specific problems in environmental design and planning under the guidance of a member of the college's graduate faculty.

title: Conceptual Approaches to Design and Planning
description: Overview of contributions and limitations of various theoretical perspectives that inform the field of design and planning inquiry. Knowledge and theory construction. Nature, scope, and merits of scientific approach. Criticism of the scientific approach and examination of alternative approaches utilized in design and planning research and practice. Linking philosophy, research, and action/practice/application.

title: Research Methods in Design and Planning
description: Survey of research methods in the fields of design and planning, including overall systems of inquiry, criteria for assessing quality, strategies and tactics. Strengths and weaknesses of various research designs. Methods covered include experimental and quasi-experimental research, correlational research, qualitative research strategies, simulation and modeling research, action research and design intervention, case study, and combined strategies.

title: Dissertation Research
description: For students who have completed all credit hour, full-time enrollment, preliminary examination, and residency requirements for the doctoral degree, and are writing and defending their dissertations.

title: Introduction to Environmental Engineering
description: Introduction to discipline, department, university, and profession. Exploration of career options. Gain skills through application-oriented problem solving.

title: Professional Practice for Environmental Engineering Design
description: A preparatory course for ENVE Senior Design class. Topics will include professionalism, project management, intellectual property, organizational behavior and ethics. Students will explore potential design project ideas, build design teams, and chose a project to complete for senior design in the following semester.

title: Environ. Eng. Fund.
description: Basic physical, chemical, and biological concepts and the applications to the protection of the environment with emphasis on techniques used in water and wastewater treatment.

title: Environmental Engineering Senior Design
description: Team-oriented capstone design laboratory with projects selected to address the design of equipment or systems to solve environmental engineering problems.

title: Introduction To Food Engineering Technology Lab
Engineering Technology Seminar
description: An introduction to university life and the skills necesary for personal and professional success after college. Activities include discussions, hands-on projects and opportunities to meet and learn from professionals. Topics explore the responsibilities of a college student and the benefits of a university education.

title: Problems in Engineering Technology
description: Independent study in specific topics in engineering technology.

title: Electronics Seminar
description: An introductory seminar for students needing to further their education in the electronics area designed to provide basic hardware, software, and analytical skills necessary to be successful in their future course work related to electronics. Students will also be exposed to technology trends and standards of the electronic industry.

title: Applied Data Analysis and Tools
description: An introduction to numerical computing, data analysis, and visualization with emphasis on the use of software tools and programming to model, analyze, and solve a variety of science and engineering problems.

title: Microcontroller applications in Unmanned Systems
description: This course examines the hardware and software necessary to control Unmanned Systems.  The students will learn how to interface microcontrollers to various sensors, actuators and communication devices.  They will also learn the programming associated with these devices.

title: Unmanned Communication Circuits
description: Communication electronics with applications in unmanned systems. Students will be exposed to mixed signal design circuits; RF circuits; and software defined radio (SDR) concepts that are key to the design modern radio systems.

title: UAS Senior Design I
description: Application of UAS principles and design methodology to solving a significant design problem in a team context. Includes determining customer requirements, exploring and choosing design alternatives, scheduling, and project management. Significant milestones are the project's conceptual, preliminary, and critical design reviews, which require written and oral presentations.

title: UAS Senior Design II
description: A continuation of ETB 480. Includes the implementation, testing, and delivery of the project initiated in ETB 480 UAS Senior Design I. Significant milestones are the project prototype, design report, and final presentation.

title: Senior Design Project I
description: Application of engineering principles and design methodology to solve a significant design problem in a team context. Includes determining customer requirements, exploring and choosing design alternatives, scheduling, and project management. Significant milestones are the project's conceptual, preliminary, and critical design reviews, which require written and oral presentations.

title: Senior Design Project II
description: A continuation of ETB 482. Includes the implementation, testing, and delivery of the project initiated in ETB 482 Senior Design I. Significant milestones are the project prototype, design report, and final presentation.

title: Advanced Problems in Engineering Technology
description: Advanced independent study in specific topics in engineering technology.

title: Introduction to Product Development
description: Fundamental knowledge and skills in fashion product development and garment construction. Focus on foundational techniques, terminology, methods and fashion industry standards.

title: Fashion Industry
description: Survey of the organization and operation of the global fashion industry; examination of the principles and processes required to meet the needs of the apparel/textile consumer; particular emphasis on sustainability challenges and opportunities pertaining to the industry.

title: Textiles
description: Fundamentals of textiles as related to the production, sale, and use of soft goods. Understanding of the environmental impacts of fiber, yarn, and fabric production. Identification of fibers, yarns, and fabrics. Care and performance of textile products.

title: Fashion Production I
description: Fundamentals of fashion production using industrial equipment; fit and tech packs.

title: Fashion Retailing
description: Introduction to the strategic role of retailing in the value chain; analyzing components, processes, and controls involved in managing a soft goods retailing organization while exploring the impetus for triple bottom line thinking in an industry transitioning toward sustainability.

title: Dress and Human Behavior
description: Cultural, social, psychological, and economic aspects related to dress and human behavior of individuals and groups; focus on market segmentation.

title: Fashion Aesthetics
description: Understanding and application of aesthetics as it relates to the roles of the fashion industry professional and the development, selection, and promotion of fashion products.

title: Our Sustainable World
description: Sustainability has become one of the most pressing scientific and social challenges of the modern era. This course introduces students to the theory, principles, and practices of sustainability - by focusing on environmental, social, and economic sustainability perspectives, behaviors, policies, and customs on a global level. It includes discussions on creating ecological health, economic welfare, and social justice.

title: Digital Fashion Communications
description: Studies in computer-based advertising and promotional strategies for the fashion industry. Students will apply skills to graphic presentation and current industry technologies.

title: Fashion Illustration and Flats
description: Fashion rendering by hand; technical flats and digital presentation using Adobe Illustrator.

title: Fashion Production II
description: Intermediate level fashion production using industrial equipment; fit and use of challenging fabrics.

title: History of Fashion
description: Interrelationship of fashion and social, cultural, political, and economic environments from the 1800s to present day.

title: Professional Development
description: Preparation for internship and professional development in the fashion industry.

title: Showcase of Excellence Event Planning
description: Planning, promotion, and production of a fashion runway event and static exhibit.

title: Soft Goods Evaluation
description: Identification and evaluation of soft goods product quality and serviceability from the standpoint of fiber, fabric, and construction performance, material and product standards and specifications, and cost.

title: Product Development for Circular Fashion
description: Sustainable soft goods product development. Focus on development of textile related products and services (clothing, footwear, accessories, home goods, etc.) for the circular economy, from concept to consumer.

title: Merchandise Math and Buying Concepts
description: Introduction to the processes and methods used by fashion and retail buyers to plan and purchase merchandise. Application of fashion theory, principles, and sources of information in conducting market and consumer research. Forecasting economic trends in retailing and consumer buying behavior. Emphasis placed on merchandising mathematics, creating spreadsheets in Excel, and key industry terminology used by retail buyers.

title: Topics in Fashion Studies
description: New content, technology or theory related to fashion studies.

title: Computer-aided Design of Fashion
description: Development and presentation of fashion and textile design using visual graphic software.

title: Fashion Internship
description: Pre-planned and supervised off-campus work experience in the fashion industry.

title: Fashion Forecasting
description: Introduction of the processes and methods used by the fashion industry to conduct short-term and long-term forecasts. Application of fashion theory and principles, sources of information, knowledge, techniques and skills for planning and implementing fashion projection.

title: Retail Finance and Merchandise Control
description: Introduction and application of fashion and soft goods merchandise assortment planning, forecasting operational expenses, vendor negotiations in merchandise purchases, retail profit planning, financial strategy, and economic sustainability. Emphasis on creating detailed spreadsheets in Excel.

title: Soft Goods Supply Chain Management
description:
Analysis of global fiber, textile, and apparel production and distribution; structure of fashion industry and distribution channels; impact of culture, economics, and government regulations on production and distribution. 
Recommend prerequisite: ECON 110 or ECON 120.

title: Sustainable Fashion Business
description: Application of marketing principles to the fashion industry. Focus on development of marketing, promotional, and merchandising plans.

title: Fashion Studies Study Tour
description: Supervised off-campus tour of facilities or equivalent experience where softgoods products are designed, manufactured, tested, marketed, exhibited, and/or conserved.

title: Flat Pattern
description: Fashion design through flat pattern methods.

title: Pre-Production Technologies
description: Computer applications in 2D for pattern drafting, grading, marker, and tech packs; 3D visualization and evaluation.

title: Sustainable Fashion and Color
description: Sustainable fashion design and coloration strategies, systems, and standards; coloration of textiles with natural dye and sustainable methods.

title: Foundations of Sustainability
description: Introduction to principles and practices of sustainability. Examination of the challenges to environmental, social, and economic sustainability on both a global and for consumer goods and services industries. Exploration of innovative practices and social change strategies for the advancement of sustainability.

title: Professional Advancement in Merchandising
description: Analysis of leadership in a merchandising firm and how it affects organizational culture and change. Various leadership styles examined, and a personal leadership philosophy developed.

title: Theory and Practice of Soft Goods Marketing and Distribution
description: Theoretical and applied analysis of soft goods marketing and distribution strategies, with an emphasis on the effectiveness of a market-oriented approach for decision-making; assessment of environmental forces affecting strategic decisions by firms in the distribution channel; synthesis of past and present trends in order to forecast probably future patterns.

title: Promotional Strategies in Merchandising
description: Examination of promotional strategies and techniques used by merchandising firms, emphasizing cultural and global awareness, social responsibility, and ethical decision making.

title: Merchandising Analytics
description: Using quantitative data from the merchandising industry to support managerial decision making. Specifically, how to format and analyze Point of Sale (POS) and Enterprise Resource Planning (ERP) merchandising data with the goal of maximizing profitability.  Applying analytical approaches to problem solving using Microsoft Excel. Strengthening decision making and analytical skills while providing new perspectives and approaches to apply quantitative techniques and methods to solve real-world merchandising problems.

title: Problem Solving for a Sustainable World
description: Study of problem-solving approaches with application to sustainability challenges in the fashion industry or discipline. Systems thinking, design thinking, critical thinking, and/or creative thinking skills will be employed to explore solutions for enhancing sustainability and human wellbeing.

title: Practice-Based Research
description: Examination and application of approaches for undertaking practice-based research in fashion studies.

title: International Merchandise Management
description: Comprehensive understanding of theory, practices, and trends in international merchandise management. Analysis of the global merchandising system and way goods are distributed to consumers in various countries.

title: Financial Merchandising Implications
description: The advanced study of financial trends in the merchandising industries. Focus will be on the financial implications of recent advances in the field.

title: Strategic Planning for Sustainability
description: Examination of the executive-level planning process utilized to develop successful business strategies; emphasis on the importance of a market orientation for building customer value and sustaining a competitive advantage.

title: Fashion Behavior & Sustainability
description: Evaluation of historical, sociological, psychological, economic, and theoretical underpinnings of fashion process, and examination of approaches fostering greater sustainable apparel consumption.

title: Product Development
description: Advanced study of issues and management strategies necessary to design and produce a competitively priced product. Examination of the role of globalization and rapidly changing technology on the development of a successful product.

title: Consumers in the Soft Goods Market
description: Analysis of consumers and their decision-making processes; examination of internal and external factors influencing evaluation and purchasing behavior in order to predict future consumption and purchasing patterns.

title: Research Methods in Fashion Studies
description: Research methods that address practical and theoretical issues involved in designing, conducting, and evaluating research.

title: Readings in Fashion Studies
description: Directed reading and study of selected topics.

title: Contemporary Topics in Fashion Studies
description: Selected contemporary topics related to fashion studies.

title: Problems in Fashion Studies
description: Independent study related to fashion studies.

title: Practicum in Fashion Studies
description: Preplanned and supervised off-campus experience in business, industry, museums, government agencies, or the cooperative extension service.

title: Project Report
description: Research and written documentation for the M.S. Project Report option.

title: Project Exhibit
description: Project development for creative-oriented M.S. Project Report option resulting in a body of original works to be exhibited on campus during the final semester of the student's program.

title: Master's Thesis Research
description: Research and written documentation for the M.S. Thesis option.

title: Dissertation Proposal Seminar
description: Presentation and discussion of proposals for dissertation research.

title: Grantsmanship and Publication
description: Grant writing, identifying external funding, managing grants, preparing manuscripts for peer-reviewed publication, and preparing papers and poster for presentation at professional meetings.

title: Dissertation Research in Fashion Studies
description: Research in fashion studies for the doctoral dissertation.

Special Topics in FDSCI
description: Seminars of special interest. Will be offered upon sufficient demand in selected areas relating to food science.

title: Foundations in Food Science & Industry
description: Introduction to the food science & industry programs, activities, resources, faculty and career opportunities.

title: Introduction to Food Science
description: This course is the beginning course in food science designed to acquaint the student with the breadth and scope of the food industry and the role of science in the preservation, processing, and utilization of foods.

title: Food Science Problems
description: Independent guided studies in any of the many fields associated with food science.

title: Transfer Foundations in FDSCI
description: An overview of the Food Science and Industry undergraduate program, including its undergraduate degree requirements, activities, and resources.

title: Fundamentals of Food Processing
description: The study of basic unit operations such as thermal processing, separation, chilling and freezing, dehydration, and fermentation as applied to the processing of food products.

title: Applied Microbiology For Meat and Poultry Processors
description: An introduction to basic food microbiology and food safety concepts with application and integration of principles to the meat and poultry processing industry, microbiological techniques for products and environmental samples, antimicrobial intervention strategies, employee hygiene, Good Manufacturing Practices (GMPs), food plant sanitation, and introduction to Hazard Analysis Critical Control Points (HACCP) programs.

title: Food Science Professional Preparation
description: This course prepares students to pursue and be successful in food science careers. Students will develop professional resumes, identify internship and career opportunities, hone interviewing skills, examine standards of professionalism with respect to personal integrity and business ethics, and practice professional communication in oral and written formats.

title: Food Science Problems
description: Independent guided studies in any of the many fields associated with food science.

title: Special Topics in Food Science
description: Topics of special interest. Will be offered upon sufficient demand in selected areas relating to food science.

title: Food Products Evaluation
description: Introduction to the sensory evaluation of food products, including basic sensory testing methods, with special emphasis on proper judging techniques for evaluating dairy products.

title: Food Science Seminar
description: Review of recent developments in the food science industry and in food science research. Food science literature and intradepartmental research will provide source material.

title: Food Chemistry
description: An in-depth coverage of the chemical structures of major food components and the chemical reactions occurring during storage and processing.

title: Fruit and Vegetable Processing
description: Fundamental fruit and vegetable processes and related unit operations including blanching, cooling, freezing, canning, irradiation, fermentation, pickling, drying, packaging in relation to quality, safety, and nutritional aspects of related products. Importance of raw material characteristics and storage conditions will be discussed in terms of post-harvest physiology of fruits and vegetables and different product types. Functionality of ingredients common in fruit and vegetable processing will also be introduced.

title: Fruit and Vegetable Processing Lab
description: Lab component of FDSCI 510 Fruit and Vegetable Processing. The labs will demonstrate both industrial and lab scale applications of technologies, unit operations, quality measurements, and related processing calculations commonly used in fruit and vegetable processing.

title: Undergraduate Research in Food Science and Industry
description: Mentored undergraduate research experience in food sciences.

title: Food Microbiology
description: This course deals with the isolation, identification, enumeration, and characterization of bacteria, yeasts, molds and other microbes associated with foods and food processing. Effects of physical and chemical agents on micro-organisms will be studied. Microbiological problems in food spoilage, food preservation, food fermentation, and food-borne diseases will be discussed.

title: Food Microbiology Lab
description: Laboratory procedures involving isolation, identification, enumeration, and characterization of bacteria, yeasts, molds and other microbes associated with foods and food processing.

title: Food Science Internship
description: Supervised professional field experience in food science.

title: Food Analysis
description: Principles, methods, and techniques necessary for quantitative, instrumental, physical, and chemical analyses of food products. Analytical techniques covered include proximate analysis, spectroscopy, chromatography (GC, HPLC), mass spectrometry, water activity, color, texture, rheology and interfacial phenomena.

title: Chemical Methods of Food Analysis
description: Methods for quantitative chemical analyses of foods products. Analytical techniques covered include proximate analysis, spectroscopy, chromatography, mass spectrometry and immunochemistry. The analyses will be related to standards and regulations for food processing.

title: Physical Methods of Food Analysis
description: Principles of physical methods of food analysis including techniques related to water activity, water content, color, soft materials, gel networks, interfacial phenomena and colloidal structure, texture, rheology, and vibrational spectroscopy.

title: Food Science Problems
description: Research or related work with others, or a literature search. Written reports are required. Any field of food science for which the student has adequate background.
