title: Research and Development of Food Products
description: Research and development of new food products, including idea generation; market screening; focus groups; prototype development; ingredient functionality and interactions; statistical designs; processing; packaging; scale-up; regulatory issues; labeling; physical, chemical, microbiological and sensory evaluations; quality control procedures; and food safety plans.

title: International Study Experience in Food Science
description: Seminar and travel course designed to prepare students before the experience and for students to analyze, critique, and report their experiences of an international experience associated with study tours or short courses. Course will focus on appropriate oral and written documentation of the student learning objectives for course experience. The academic topics will vary with each international activity.

title: Principles of HACCP and Preventive Controls for Human Food
description: A comprehensive study of the Hazard Analysis and Critical Control Point (HACCP) System and Preventive Controls for Human Food and their application in the meat and food industries. Students will meet the training requirements under USDA HACCP and the FDA Food Safety Modernization Act (FSMA) for food safety for human foods. The class is offered in person in the fall and online fall/spring.

title: Quality Assurance of Food Products
description: A comprehensive course covering all aspects of quality assurance practices in the food industry. Emphasis is placed on interrelations of food chemistry, microbiology, sanitation, processing, and laws and regulations.

title: Kosher and Halal Food Regulations
description: This course is an introduction to the origin and application of Jewish and Muslim dietary laws and their application in commercial food production and processing with some coverage of home practices. Hebrew, Yiddish, and Arabic words and phrases will be presented and explained as they relate to kosher and halal food production, processing, and consumption. Religious festivals and customs, and the associated food products, will also be presented. Current food-related topics and issues in both the Jewish and Islam communities will be discussed. Food scientists should be aware of what constitutes a kosher or halal product and the importance of regulations dealing with the production and processing of these products.

title: Flavor Chemistry
description: Evaluation of flavor reactions and flavor chemicals used in food systems. The impact of fat, carbohydrates, proteins, and texture on food flavors, as well as other key topics such as ingredient sources for flavorings, regulating agencies, analytical tools and instruments used by flavor chemists are presented.

title: Food Packaging
description: Covers the functionality of different food packaging types, as well as the materials and technology used in new package development. Packaging concept types as well as the functionality of different materials are presented.

title: Rapid Methods and Automation in Microbiology
description: Rapid methods and automation is a dynamic area in applied microbiology dealing with the study of improved methods in the isolation, detection, characterization, and enumeration of microorganisms and their products in clinical, food, industrial, and environmental samples. The knowledge and techniques of this course are useful for students interested in medical, food, industrial, and environmental microbiology for early detection of beneficial as well as harmful microorganisms in their work.

title: Reaction Kinetics in Foods
description: A comprehensive course covering the principles of reaction kinetics in food systems to make quantitative predictions about food quality and food safety, and optimize food processing conditions.

title: Reaction Kinetics in Foods Recitation
description: Recitation course covering the theories, case-studies, and exercise problems for the lecture course FDSCI 720, which needs to be taken concurrently.

title: Food Analysis
description: Principles, methods, and techniques necessary for quantitative, instrumental, physical, and chemical analyses of food products. Analytical techniques covered include proximate analysis, spectroscopy, chromatography (GC, HPLC), mass spectrometry, water activity, color, texture, rheology and interfacial phenomena.

title: Chemical Methods of Food Analysis
description: Methods for quantitative chemical analyses of foods products. Analytical techniques covered include proximate analysis, spectroscopy, chromatography, mass spectrometry and immunochemistry. The analyses will be related to standards and regulations for food processing.

title: Physical Methods of Food Analysis
description: Principles of physical methods of food analysis including techniques related to water activity, water content, color, soft materials, gel networks, interfacial phenomena and colloidal structure, texture, rheology, and vibrational spectroscopy.

title: A Multidisciplinary Overview of Food Safety and Security
description: Multidisciplinary food safety and security perspectives provided by numerous subject matter experts. Topics include food safety policy, ag bioterrorism, border security, animal ID, food defense and site security, risk analysis, crisis communication, epidemiology, HACCP, and more.

title: Food Protection and Defense--Essential Concepts
description: This course presents foundational concepts relevant to protecting the food supply from intentional contamination, including the nature of the food and agriculture system as a critical infrastructure, threats to food and agricultural systems, as well as concepts and strategies related to response and mitigation of food protection incidents.

title: Research and Development of Food Products
description: Research and development of new food products, including idea generation; market screening; focus groups; prototype development; ingredient functionality and interactions; statistical designs; processing; packaging; scale-up; regulatory issues; labeling; physical, chemical, microbiological and sensory evaluations; quality control procedures; and food safety plans.

title: Food Toxicants
description: Quantitation metabolism and mechanisms of action of major foodborne toxicants. Toxicant defense and control systems, risk analysis and assessment and application of food laws.

title: Food Laws and the Regulatory Process
description: History of the development of the current federal and state food regulations. Guidelines that govern the practice of regulating the wholesomeness of red meats, poultry, and eggs. Presentations by state and federal food regulators.

title: Risk Assessment for Food, Ag, & Vet Med
description: Risk assessment principles as applied to biological systems. Exposure and effects characterization in human and animal health and ecological risk assessment. Risk analysis frameworks and regulatory decision-making. Introduction to quantitative methods for risk assessment using epidemiological and distributional analyses. Uncertainty analysis.

title: Advanced Application of HACCP Principles
description: Evaluation of control parameters and methodology at critical control points, validating and auditing the effectiveness of critical control points, critical limits, monitoring tools, corrective action procedures, recordkeeping and verification procedures in addressing biological, chemical, and physical hazards that may be present in food products.

title: Fermented Foods
description: Application of the principles of microbiology to the understanding of the fermentation of various categories of foods. Chemical, biochemical and microbiological changes under controlled and uncontrolled conditions.

title: Food Fermentation
description: Application of the principles of microbiology to the understanding of the fermentation of various categories of foods. Chemical, biochemical and microbiological changes under controlled and uncontrolled conditions.

title: Advanced Food Chemistry
description: Relationship of chemical composition to properties and to physical and chemical stability of foods. Chemical reactions will be covered extensively. Special attention will be given to dairy and poultry products, red meats, vegetables, and cereal grains.

title: Advanced Food Microbiology and Biotechnology
description: Principles of food biotechnology, including introduction of molecular biology and enzyme immobilization. Theory and concepts of current biotechnology trends as it relates to food safety and security issues.

title: Public Health Practice
description: Supervised field experience in an international, state, local, or district health agency or other appropriate health agency.

title: Food Science Graduate Seminar
description: This course is designed to provide students with experience in developing and delivering a scholarly presentation on a specific topic. The course will include instruction on how to properly design technical presentations for effective communication. Students will learn how to use visual aids such as transparencies, slides and multimedia projectors. At least one technical presentation will be required. One credit required for an M.S. degree and 2 credits for a Ph.D. Degree.

title: Master's Report in Food Science
description: A written report of either research or problem work on a topic in the major field.

title: Master's Research in Food Science
description: Research leading to the completion of a master's thesis.

title: Food Dispersions
description: Laws and theorems applied to the formation, mechanization and stabilization of food dispersions: properties of food dispersions; food solids, gels, batters, emulsions and foams.

title: Food Toxicology
description: This course deals with the study of occurrence, detection, and control of microbial toxins and chemical toxins in fresh and processed foods. The genetics, physiology, and mechanisms of toxin production by microbial cells and the chemistry, formation, and interactions of chemical toxins with food systems during food processing will be addressed.

title: Graduate Problem in Food Science
description: In-depth study of a topic supervised by a member of the graduate faculty.

title: Doctoral Research in Food Science
description: Research leading to the completion of a Ph.D. Degree.

title: Consumer And Energy Issues In Housing
Family Resource Management Theory And Application
Social Effects Of The Housing Environment
Consumer And Energy Issues In Housing
Advances In Consumer And Family Economics
Social Effects Of The Housing Environment
Family Resource Management Theory And Application
Advances In Consumer And Family Economics
Foundations in Food and Feed Manufacturing
description: This online orientation course provides an introduction to the key instructors, facilities, online platform and functionalities needed to be successful in completion of the food and feed manufacturing AAS degree. Students will explore the fundamentals of how to use available systems and to successfully communicate with their professors. In addition, this course is designed to increase the students’ academic and personal success in college by providing them with the scholarly, personal, and life management tools needed to function effectively and complete their AAS degree.

title: Introduction to Grain and Food Manufacturing Industries
description: This course provides an in-depth introduction to the structure and function of the grain and food manufacturing industries, focusing on their organization, key players, and market dynamics. Students will learn an overview of the operational processes, from raw material procurement to distribution, while also exploring the roles of major industry sectors such as grain elevators, grain milling, baking, and supply chain logistics. Emphasis will be placed on how different departments and functions collaborate to ensure efficient production and delivery. The course also highlights career paths, from entry-level roles to specialized positions, within these industries.

title: Introduction to Feed and Pet Food Manufacturing Industries
description: This course provides an in-depth introduction to the structure and function of the feed and pet food manufacturing industries, focusing on their organization, key players, and market dynamics. Students will learn an overview of the operational processes, from raw material procurement to distribution, while also exploring the roles of major industry sectors such as commercial feed mills, integrated feed mills, dry pet food, wet pet food, pet treats, and others. Emphasis will be placed on how different departments and functions collaborate to ensure efficient production and delivery of feed and pet food. The course also highlights career paths, from entry-level roles to specialized positions, within these industries.

title: Ingredient Identification and Quality: Cereal Grains
description: This course teaches students the skills necessary to identify various types of cereal grains and assess their quality. Students will learn about key physical and chemical characteristics, as well as the factors that influence grain quality such as growing conditions, handling, and storage. The course includes practical instruction on grading, testing, and evaluating grains for food and feed production. By the end of the course, students will be able to recognize common cereal grains and determine their suitability for different applications based on quality standards.

title: Ingredient Identification and Quality: Oil Seeds and Legumes
description: This course provides students with the knowledge and skills to identify various types of oilseeds and legumes and assess their quality. Students will explore the physical, chemical, and nutritional properties of these crops, along with the factors affecting their quality, such as cultivation, harvesting, and storage practices. The course offers practical instruction on grading, testing, and evaluating oilseeds and legumes for use in food and feed production. By the end of the course, students will be equipped to determine the quality and market value of oilseeds and legumes based on industry standards.

title: Ingredient Identification and Quality: Co-products and Additives
description: This course introduces students to the identification and quality assessment of ingredient co-products and additives used in food and feed production. Students will learn about common co-products, such as bran and meal, and various additives, including preservatives, emulsifiers, and vitamins. The course covers the factors that impact the quality and functionality of these ingredients, with practical instruction on evaluating their suitability for specific applications. By the end of the course, students will be equipped to assess ingredient quality and understand their roles in enhancing product performance.

title: Preparing Grains
description: This course provides an in-depth overview of the preparation of grains for the milling process, covering cleaning, conditioning, and handling techniques. Students will learn about grain characteristics, impurity removal, moisture adjustment, and the equipment used to prepare grains for efficient milling. The course emphasizes the importance of proper grain preparation to ensure optimal milling performance, product quality, and safety. By the end of the course, students will understand the critical steps involved in preparing grains for milling and their impact on final product outcomes.

title: Milling Process
description: This course provides an overview of the key processing steps involved in the milling process, including grinding, sifting, purifying, and packaging. Students will learn about the equipment used at each stage, how grain is transformed into flour or other products, and the importance of process control for product quality and efficiency. The course emphasizes the integration of each step to optimize production and maintain consistency in the final product. By the end of the course, students will have a clear understanding of the entire milling workflow and the factors that influence process performance.

title: Milling Specialty Grains
description: This course offers an overview of the unique processing steps involved in milling specialty grains, such as quinoa, spelt, and buckwheat. Students will explore how these grains differ from conventional wheat in terms of cleaning, grinding, sifting, and handling, with an emphasis on the specialized equipment and techniques required. The course highlights the challenges and opportunities in maintaining quality, nutrition, and functionality during the milling of specialty grains. By the end of the course, students will understand the distinct processing methods needed to optimize production and ensure quality in specialty grain milling.

title: Intro to Milling Practicum
description: This hands-on course offers an applied learning experience in grain processing and flour milling, providing students with a practical overview of industry operations. Students will engage in activities such as grain cleaning, grinding, sifting, and flour quality testing, while learning about the equipment and processes involved in milling. The course emphasizes real-world problem-solving and process optimization within grain and flour milling facilities. By the end of the course, students will have gained valuable hands-on experience, preparing them for operational roles in grain processing and milling.

title: Bakery Ingredients
description: This course provides an overview of the role and function of both micro and macro ingredients in the baking process, including flours, sugars, fats, leavening agents, emulsifiers, and flavorings. Students will learn how these ingredients interact to influence dough development, texture, flavor, and shelf life in baked products. The course emphasizes the importance of ingredient selection, measurement accuracy, and the impact of ingredient quality on the final product. By the end of the course, students will understand how to optimize ingredient use to produce high-quality baked goods consistently.

title: Bakery Processes
description: This course provides a comprehensive overview of the production steps involved in the baking process, including mixing, proofing, baking, cooling, and packaging. Students will examine each stage of production, from ingredient preparation to final product assembly, with an emphasis on how these steps affect product quality and consistency. The course covers best practices for optimizing each step to enhance efficiency and ensure product excellence. By the end of the course, students will have a thorough understanding of baking unit operations and the skills needed to manage and improve production processes.

title: Bakery Products
description: This course provides an overview of the diverse range of products produced in the baking industry, including bread, pastries, cakes, cookies, and specialized baked goods. Students will explore the characteristics, ingredients, and production techniques specific to each product category. The course emphasizes product differentiation, market trends, and the impact of formulation and processing on product quality. By the end of the course, students will have a broad understanding of the variety of baked goods and the factors influencing their production and success in the market.

title: Intro to Baking Practicum
description: This hands-on course provides an applied learning experience offering a comprehensive overview of the baking industry. Students will engage in practical activities such as mixing, baking, and quality testing, while learning about the equipment and processes used in commercial bakeries. The course emphasizes understanding ingredient functionality, production techniques, and troubleshooting common issues in bakery operations. By the end of the course, students will have gained real-world experience, preparing them for technical and management roles in the baking industry.

title: Feed Processing
description: This course provides a comprehensive overview of the feed manufacturing process, focusing on process flow, equipment identification, and operational techniques. Students will learn about the key stages of feed production, from raw material intake to mixing, pelleting, and packaging, as well as the roles of various equipment used in each stage. The course includes practical insights into equipment operation, maintenance, and troubleshooting to ensure efficient and safe feed production. By the end of the course, students will understand the complete feed manufacturing process and be able to identify and operate essential machinery.

title: Pet Food Processing
description: This course provides an in-depth overview of the pet food manufacturing process, covering the production of dry food, wet food, canned food, and pet treats. Students will explore the process flow for each type of product, learning how raw materials are processed, formulated, and packaged, along with the identification and operation of key equipment used in each stage. The course emphasizes the technical aspects of machinery operation, maintenance, and quality control specific to pet food manufacturing. By the end of the course, students will have a comprehensive understanding of how different pet foods are produced and the equipment involved in their creation.

title: Finished Feed and Pet Food Quality Assurance
description: This course offers an overview of quality assurance practices in the feed and pet food manufacturing processes, focusing on maintaining product safety, consistency, and regulatory compliance. Students will learn about key quality control measures, testing protocols, and industry standards for ingredient sourcing, production, and packaging. The course also covers the tools and technologies used to monitor and ensure product quality throughout the manufacturing process. By the end of the course, students will understand how quality assurance impacts the overall success and safety of feed and pet food products.

title: Intro to Feed and Pet Food Practicum
description: This hands-on course offers an applied learning experience that provides an overview of feed manufacturing and pet food processing. Students will engage in practical activities such as ingredient handling, formulation, mixing, pelleting, extrusion, canning, and quality testing, while learning about the equipment and processes involved in both industries. The course emphasizes real-world problem-solving, production optimization, and maintaining product quality and safety. By the end of the course, students will have gained valuable experience in the operational aspects of feed and pet food manufacturing, preparing them for technical and managerial roles.

title: Food and Feed Manufacturing Independent Study
description: Student-specific projects or independent guided studies in any of the many fields associated food and feed manufacturing.

title: Topics in Food and Feed Manufacturing
description: Topics of special interest. Will be offered upon sufficient demand in selected areas relating to food and feed manufacturing.

title: Food and Feed Manufacturing Equipment Maintenance
description: This course introduces students to the fundamentals of equipment maintenance in food and feed manufacturing, focusing on routine upkeep, troubleshooting, and repair techniques. Students will learn about the types of equipment commonly used in the industry, as well as best practices for maintaining operational efficiency and minimizing downtime. The course includes practical insights into preventive maintenance strategies and common issues that arise in equipment operations. By the end of the course, students will be equipped to implement effective maintenance programs and ensure the reliability of manufacturing equipment.

title: Preventative and Predictive Maintenance: Milling Specific
description: This course focuses on developing equipment maintenance programs specifically for the grain and flour milling industries. Students will learn to design and implement maintenance schedules tailored to the unique demands of milling equipment, including preventive and predictive maintenance strategies. The course covers techniques for diagnosing common equipment issues and best practices for minimizing downtime and extending equipment life. By the end of the course, students will be prepared to create and manage effective maintenance programs that optimize performance and reliability in grain and flour milling operations.

title: Maintenance Programs: Bakery Specific
description: This course provides an in-depth exploration of developing equipment maintenance programs tailored to the baking industry. Students will learn how to design and implement maintenance schedules for bakery equipment, including ovens, mixers, and packaging machines, with a focus on preventive and corrective measures. The course covers techniques for troubleshooting common issues, ensuring compliance with industry standards, and optimizing equipment performance. By the end of the course, students will be equipped to create and manage maintenance programs that enhance the efficiency and longevity of bakery operations.

title: Maintenance Programs: Feed and Pet Food Specific
description: This course focuses on developing equipment maintenance programs tailored specifically to feed and pet food manufacturing facilities. Students will learn to design and implement maintenance schedules for machinery used in processing, mixing, and packaging feed and pet food, emphasizing preventive and corrective maintenance strategies. The course includes practical training on diagnosing equipment issues, ensuring compliance with industry standards, and enhancing operational efficiency. By the end of the course, students will be adept at creating and managing maintenance programs that optimize the performance and reliability of equipment in feed and pet food manufacturing.

title: Milling Equipment Maintenance Practicum
description: This hands-on course offers students practical experience in equipment maintenance programs specific to grain processing and flour milling facilities. Students will learn to perform routine inspections, preventive maintenance, and basic repairs on critical milling equipment, such as conveyors, sifters, and grinders. The course emphasizes real-world problem-solving and the importance of maintaining equipment to minimize downtime and improve operational efficiency. By the end of the course, students will have gained valuable skills in managing maintenance programs, ensuring the reliability and longevity of equipment in grain and flour milling operations.

title: Baking Equipment Maintenance Practicum
description: This hands-on course provides students with practical experience in developing and implementing equipment maintenance programs specific to baking facilities. Students will work with common bakery machinery, such as ovens and mixers, learning preventive maintenance techniques, troubleshooting methods, and basic repairs. The course focuses on maintaining operational efficiency, minimizing downtime, and ensuring equipment safety in a bakery setting. By the end of the course, students will have the skills needed to manage maintenance programs that enhance the performance and longevity of bakery equipment.

title: Feed Equipment Maintenance Practicum
description: This hands-on course provides practical experience in developing and implementing equipment maintenance programs for feed manufacturing and pet food processing facilities. Students will work with machinery such as mixers, extruders, conveyors, and packaging equipment, learning preventive maintenance, troubleshooting, and basic repair techniques. The course emphasizes maintaining equipment efficiency, minimizing downtime, and ensuring product safety and quality. By the end of the course, students will have gained the skills necessary to manage maintenance programs that optimize equipment performance in feed and pet food production.

title: Advanced Food and Feed Manufacturing Management
description: This course offers a comprehensive overview of managing food and feed manufacturing facilities, with a focus on integrating statistical process controls and Lean Six Sigma methodologies. Students will learn how to apply statistical tools to monitor and improve production processes, as well as implement Lean Six Sigma principles to enhance efficiency and reduce waste. The course covers key management strategies for optimizing facility operations, ensuring product quality, and complying with industry regulations. By the end of the course, students will be equipped to manage manufacturing operations effectively, utilizing data-driven approaches and continuous improvement techniques.

title: Advanced Milling Management
description: This course provides an advanced exploration of management techniques specific to grain and flour milling facilities. Students will delve into sophisticated strategies for optimizing milling processes, improving operational efficiency, and ensuring product quality. The course includes in-depth coverage of advanced technologies, process control systems, and management practices tailored to the unique challenges of the milling industry. By the end of the course, students will have the expertise to lead and manage grain and flour milling operations effectively, leveraging advanced management techniques to enhance productivity and sustainability.

title: Advanced Milling Manufacturing Management Practicum
description: This hands-on course offers students practical experience in managing the day-to-day operations of grain milling and flour milling facilities. Students will engage in activities such as overseeing production processes, managing equipment, coordinating staff, and ensuring product quality and safety. The course emphasizes problem-solving, workflow optimization, and adherence to industry regulations to ensure efficient facility management. By the end of the course, students will be equipped with the skills to lead and manage a milling facility, balancing operational efficiency and product quality.

title: Advanced Bakery Management
description: This course explores advanced management practices specific to bakery facilities, focusing on optimizing production processes, enhancing product quality, and integrating innovative technologies. Students will study advanced techniques for managing bakery operations, including process control, and supply chain management. The course also covers methods and tools to improve production efficiency, performance, capability, profitability, and quality in a bakery operation.  By the end of the course, students will be equipped with the skills to lead bakery facilities effectively, driving efficiency and maintaining high standards of product excellence.

title: Advanced Baking Manufacturing Management Practicum
description: This hands-on course provides students with practical experience in managing the operations of a commercial baking facility. Students will engage in overseeing production processes, managing staff, maintaining equipment, and ensuring product quality and food safety. The course emphasizes process optimization, inventory control, and adherence to regulatory standards specific to the baking industry. By the end of the course, students will have the skills and knowledge to efficiently manage a baking facility, balancing operational demands with quality control.

title: Advanced Feed and Pet Food Management
description: This course offers an advanced examination of management practices tailored for feed and pet food facilities, emphasizing strategies for optimizing production, ensuring quality control, and integrating cutting-edge technologies. Students will explore advanced operational techniques, including automation, process optimization, and regulatory compliance specific to feed and pet food manufacturing. The course also covers strategic decision-making, resource management, and staff leadership to address the unique challenges of these industries. By the end of the course, students will possess the expertise to effectively manage feed and pet food facilities, driving operational excellence and innovation.

title: Advanced Feed and Pet Food Manufacturing Management Practicum
description: This hands-on course offers students practical experience in the daily operations of feed manufacturing and pet food processing facilities. Students will engage in activities such as overseeing production processes, managing ingredient handling, operating machinery, and ensuring product quality and safety. The course emphasizes workflow optimization, regulatory compliance, and the integration of food safety standards within the industry. By the end of the course, students will be prepared to manage and optimize operations in feed and pet food production facilities.

title: Introduction to Food and Feed Safety
description: This course introduces students in technical education to foundational concepts in food and feed safety, focusing on the identification and control of various hazards. Students will learn about the different types of hazards—physical, chemical, and biological—and explore key biological and viral pathogens that can impact food and feed production. The course covers essential safety practices, regulatory standards, and methods for preventing contamination. By the end of the course, students will understand the importance of hazard identification and control in maintaining food and feed safety.

title: Food Safety Principles in Milling and Baking
description: This course provides a comprehensive overview of Hazard Analysis and Critical Control Points (HACCP), a systematic approach to food safety management for the milling and baking industries. Students will learn to identify potential hazards, establish critical control points, and implement monitoring procedures to ensure the safety of products. The course covers the seven principles of HACCP, risk assessment techniques, and compliance with industry regulations. By the end of the course, students will be equipped to develop, manage, and evaluate HACCP plans in food production environments.

title: Advanced Feed and Pet Food Safety
description: This course prepares students to become Preventive Control Qualified Individuals (PCQI), equipping them with the skills to develop and implement food safety plans in accordance with FDA regulations. Students will learn how to identify hazards, establish preventive controls, monitor processes, and ensure compliance with the Food Safety Modernization Act (FSMA). The course covers risk-based preventive controls, corrective actions, and verification procedures. By the end of the course, students will be qualified to oversee and manage food safety systems in a variety of food production environments.

title: Personal Investing and Risk Management
description: Provides a framework for identifying, analyzing, and managing the lifetime financial risks faced by the average person. An overview of the types and mechanics of investment instruments, development of personal risk profiles and investment plans, asset allocation methods, diversifiable and non-diversifiable risk, and risk avoidance and hedging methods.

title: Long-Term Value Creation
description: Provides an introductory, non-quantitative look at how long-term value is created in businesses.  The course will define how a business works, how businesses make a profit, how long-term value is created and sustained, and how these concepts are applied to understand how businesses add value to society.  Case examples are used throughout the course to illustrate the concepts covered in the course.

title: Sports Finance
description: This course explores the unique financial issues in collegiate athletics including scholarships, NIL, and professional contracts, and how financial markets and institutions interact with athletics.  Topics include budgeting for scholarships, NIL, and professional contracts; capital markets, banking, risk management in college athletics; and finance in athletic careers.

title: Principles of Finance
description: Study of the basic principles of finance, including discounted cash flow analysis, risk-return tradeoff, asset pricing models, and financial and real asset valuation. Applications of these concepts to the firm's investment and financing decisions and performance analysis will be discussed.

title: Introduction to Integrated Investment Management
description: This course provides a survey of the investment management industry. The course examines various aspects of the investment management industry including discussions of investment markets, products, companies, and strategies. The course also examines the business of investment management by surveying each of the functional areas of investment management industry: accounting, finance, management, and marketing. The course introduces students to the basics of investment management and the course provides a survey of the legal and ethical environments in the investment management industry.

title: Risk Management
description: A study of life, property, casualty, and health insurance from the purchaser's point of view with additional emphasis on the operation and contribution of the insurance industry.

title: Undergraduate Research Experience
description: Open to students pursuing undergraduate research projects.

title: Problems in Finance
description: Internship program and selected projects appropriate to the student's program of study.

title: Investment Management Concepts
description: An introduction and study of the issues and skills involved in successful investment management. This course will survey the role of capital markets, the basics of fundamental and technical analysis, behavioral finance, the basic of financial statements, client development, and management skills necessary for investment management success.

title: Financial Institutions and Markets
description: An analysis of financial institutions and markets from a risk management point of view. The role of  financial institutions in the marketplace, the determinants of interest rates, and the impact of inflation on asset returns will be examined. Risks in financial institutions will be analyzed and management of these risks will be emphasized.

title: Investments
description: An analysis of investment securities from the investor's point of view. Topics covered include the mechanics of investing in securities and market basics, the risk-return tradeoff, asset pricing models, market efficiency, the valuation of securities, portfolio performance measurement, and an introduction to derivatives.

title: Commercial Banking
description: An application of financial management concepts to the liquidity management, investment portfolio analysis, capital budgeting, and capital structure decision-making process required by a commercial bank to perform effectively its financial intermediation role within the financial system's institutional, regulatory, and competitive environment.

title: Real Estate
description: Principles and practices including legal, economic, and social implications from the viewpoint of the real estate practitioner, investor, and society.

title: Finance Scholars Seminar
description: Weekly seminar for the students in the Kansas State University Finance Scholars Program

title: Concepts in Financial Technology
description: Introduces the most recent technological innovations which affect how capital market participants perform functions such as borrowing, lending, contracting, and transacting. These include blockchain for business, decision support systems, and innovations in marketplace lending

title: Finance for Entrepreneurs
description: A comprehensive set of knowledge and tools to start, run, and exit an entrepreneurial or small business. These tools include financial forecasting and business planning, financing operations and capital investments, evaluation of asset acquisitions, tax planning, short-term capital management, mergers/acquisitions, and evaluation of exit strategies.

title: Intermediate Finance
description: An in-depth study of a firm's investment and financing decisions, firm performance measurement and financial planning for a business enterprise. Topics include financial statement analysis and forecasting, capital budgeting, risk considerations in capital budgeting, cost of capital, capital structure theory and practice, distribution policy, leasing and mergers and acquisitions.

title: Fixed Income Securities and Markets
description: Provides an overview of fixed income markets and conventions; the analytical underpinning of fixed income markets and securities; and fixed income derivatives.

title: Financial Modeling
description: This course is designed to provide an in-depth knowledge of the use of Excel for use in the corporate finance and equity valuation environment.  Topics include:   Use of Excel  Developing Models in Excel   Developing functional understanding of how to use Excel.

title: International Financial Management
description: The international (cross-currency) aspects of financial management. Topics include currency markets and exchange rate determination, parity conditions, foreign exchange exposure and management, and valuation of international projects.

title: Security and Portfolio Analysis
description: The analysis and valuation of securities and the management of investment portfolios. Students analyze the composition of, make buy/sell recommendations for, and evaluate the performance of an actual portfolio.

title: Derivative Securities and Markets
description: Structure and operation of markets for futures, swaps, options, synthetic options, and futures on options. Valuation of futures contracts and options. Applications of derivatives to hedging and speculating strategies.

title: Professional Financial Planning
description: A study of the principles and practices of professional financial planning using an integrated planning model. Topics include the planning environment, concepts, tax management, asset acquisition and management, credit management, risk management, investments, retirement planning, and estate planning. Contemporary applications, professional opportunities, and legal/ethical standards are emphasized.

title: Mergers and Acquisition
description: Presents the theories and empirical evidence on mergers, acquisitions, and restructuring, and analyzes the effects of various policy options on stock values of acquirer and target companies.

title: Cases in Finance
description: Capstone course in finance utilizing real world cases to integrate and apply financial concepts and theories, with emphasis on analytical and written and oral communication skills.

title: Foundations of Finance
description: Advanced financial concepts and tools with emphasis on corporate valuation. Topics include risk and return, valuation techniques, capital budgeting, capital structure, options and futures, real options, and financial risk management.

title: Special Topics in Finance
description: Selected projects appropriate to the student's program of study.

title: Advanced International Financial Management
description: A study of the international dimensions of corporate financial management with an applied orientation.

title: Financial Market Theory
description: Development and analysis of conceptual framework for understanding (1) the functions performed by financial markets and their associated institutional arrangements, and (2) the contractual claims in transferring savings among business, household, and government participants in the economic system.

title: Entrepreneurial Finance
description: This course will provide potential entrepreneurs a sufficient knowledge of applied finance needed to start and run an entrepreneurial business. Concepts covered will include the basic paradigms of finance such as risk and portfolio diversification. Discussions of the accounting environment of a small business and how the financial conditions can be analyzed will also be included. Finally, the course will focus on the financial skills a beginning entrepreneur will need to know to operate a small business, such as valuation, short-term capital management, financing decisions, and the exit strategies available to such enterprises.

title: Seminar in Finance
description: In-depth study of selected contemporary issues in finance.

title: Advanced Problems in Finance
description: Independent study of selected advanced topic(s) in finance.

title: Nutrition For Elementary And Middle Level Teachers
Nutrtion And Health For Elementry/Middle Level Teachers
Principles Of Food Product Development And Control
Nutritional Aspects Of Food Processing And Preparation
Advanced Application Of Haccp Principles In The Food Indust
Fundamentals Of Meat Processing And Preparation
Graduate Seminar In Foods And Nutrition
Introduction to Public Health
description: This is an introductory survey course designed to provide a history of public health, an overview of the five core disciplines (epidemiology, biostatistics, environmental health, social and behavioral health, and health policy and management), and information about career options in public health. Using these introductory materials as a foundation, students will investigate and analyze contemporary issues in public health such as bioterrorism, threats to the nation's food supply, the importance of health promoting behaviors in the prevention of disease, burden and distribution of infectious diseases on a global population, and the role of public health disaster preparedness and management.

title: Introduction to Athletic Training
description: Introduction to athletic training for first year athletic training majors. Emphasis will be on sports medicine team, legal concerns, pre-participation physicals, basic principles of injury prevention, policies and procedures.

title: Introduction to Athletic Training Lab
description: Introduction to athletic training for first year athletic training majors. Students will be required to complete a competencies skill list with a passing grade. Clinical hours in the athletic training room will give the student the opportunity to use the knowledge, skills, and techniques learned in this course.

title: Careers in Nutrition and Dietetics
description: An introduction to career opportunities in the field of nutrition and dietetics with emphasis on academic preparation, acquisition of professional credentials, and career laddering. Guest speakers from various areas of professional practice will supplement lectures and student assignments.

title: Basic Nutrition
description: Concepts of human nutrition applied to personal food choices and health.

title: Care and Prevention of Athletic Injuries
description: Principles and practices of treatment, taping, and care of minor athletic injuries.

title: Personal Wellness
description: Impact of the effect of personal actions on lifelong wellness. Practical methods of assessing, maintaining, and improving behaviors to reduce the risk of illness and disability. Emphasis on developing skills to make informed, responsible health decisions.

title: Dietetics ServSafe Manager Certification
description: Supports student completion of ServSafe Manager online certification exam (National Restaurant Association) which is required for enrollment in FNDH 342.  Course is credit/no credit. Credit is earned by uploading ServSafe Manager certification card. Students will register and pay for the ServSafe online exam through the National Restaurant Association https://www.servsafe.com/

title: Food Trends, Legislation, and Regulation
description: Current trends in market forms, packaging, and utilization of various foods. Food laws, regulation, labeling, additives, and residues.

title: Science of Food
description: This course explores the chemical, physical, sensory, and nutritional properties of food as they relate to principles and techniques used in food preparation. Students gain foundational knowledge of food science through a combination of theoretical instruction and hands-on laboratory experiences. Emphasis is placed on understanding how scientific principles influence food quality, safety, and functionality in both culinary and health-related contexts.

title: Medical Documentation
description: This course is to prepare students with the knowledge of medical nomenclature related to human systems, diseases, and scientific concepts for applications of electronic medical documentation (Electronic Medical Records; EMR) and scientific communication.

title: Food and Health: Safety, Allergies and Intolerances
description: An overview of the food system as it relates to the field of dietetics, focusing on issues that impact the quality, safety, accessibility, and sustainability of food. The course includes the systems approach, food safety principles, food allergy/intolerances principles, food contamination, religious issues, and related government regulations. This course will cover the following topics: food safety, food allergies and intolerances, sustainability, religious and cultural issues that affect food safety and health. Specific applications of these topics will be made for the dietetics profession (institutions, health care settings, grocery stores, etc.)

title: Rehabilitation and Exercise Modification Techniques I
description: This course will cover the fundamental aspects of tactical strength and conditioning, and injury prevention techniques that occur during physical activity.

title: Human Nutrition
description: Nutrients, their function, metabolism, and relation to health and disease: the digestion, absorption, transport, utilization, and storage of nutrients in humans.

title: Food Production Management
description: Application and principles of food production systems that include planning, procurement, production controls, work simplification, commercial equipment use, safety, communication, and decision making.

title: Nutritional Assessment
description: This course introduces methods for assessing nutritional status in individuals and populations. Students will explore dietary, anthropometric, biochemical, and clinical approaches to evaluate nutrition-related health risks and outcomes. Emphasis is placed on understanding how these methods work together to inform nutrition care. Through lectures and hands-on laboratory experiences, students will gain practical skills using tools and techniques for collecting and interpreting nutritional data.

title: Rehabilitation and Exercise Modification Techniques II
description: This course is designed to provide an academic platform to apply and enhance knowledge and skills in rehabilitation techniques for humans. Focus is on applying progressive resistive exercise programs for athletes who are engaged in reconditioning and rehabilitation programs.

title: Practicum in Clinical Dietetics
description: Through a series of case studies and projects, this course introduces the student dietitian to the basics of the practice of clinical dietetics including review of the medical record, nutritional assessment and documentation of medical nutrition therapy in the medical record.  Students gain practical experience by completing shadowing experiences with a dietitian in clinical practice.

title: Problems in Human Nutrition
description: Supervised individual project to study current topics or participation in research.

title: Life Span Nutrition
description: Physiological and environmental influences on nutritional requirements; nutritional problems and eating patterns of age groups throughout the life cycle.

title: Counseling Strategies in Nutrition and Dietetics
description: This course introduces evidence-based nutrition counseling, emphasizing empathetic, client-centered communication. Students will develop foundational skills in motivational interviewing, behavior change theory, and collaborative relationship-building. Through lectures, discussions, and simulations, they will practice counseling techniques, identify communication barriers, and explore documentation and billing for medical nutrition therapy.

title: Communication Competencies in Dietetics Practice
description: Assessment of audiences and development of effective food/nutrition messages in community settings.

title: Communication Competencies in Dietetics Practicum
description: Application of principles learned in FNDH 516 Communication Competencies in Dietetics Practice to the development, implementation, and evaluation of a community project under the guidance of a community mentor.

title: Introduction to Clinical Dietetics
description: Preparation for entrance into the clinical supervised practice experience. Review of medical record content; current practice protocols in clinical dietetics; practice case studies.

title: Applied Clinical Dietetics
description: Application of clinical nutrition principles through case studies, independent research, discussion, groups, and oral presentations. Case studies will focus on medical nutrition therapy and education of persons throughout the life cycle and nutrition intervention for individuals with multiple disease states in various healthcare settings.

title: Clinical Dietetics Practicum
description: Supervised clinical/community experience in the nutritional care of patients/clients. Practicum experiences are arranged with participating healthcare facilities.

title: Nutrition Education In Extension
description: The process of and strategies for providing nutrition education outreach to diverse community audiences, including those with limited resources.

title: Energy Balance
description: This course will cover metabolic principles as they relate to energy intake (diet) and expenditure (exercise). Specific topics will include: obesity, anorexia/bulimia, bioenergetics, substrate selection/regulation (especially exercise and diabetes), body composition; methodology to assess each; and metabolism related disease.

title: Nutrition Education and Program Planning
description: Education strategies for communicating evidence-based health information and planning strategies for developing community programs.

title: Management in Dietetics II
description: ¿¿Discussion of principles used to manage resources in dietetics practice.  Topics include financial management, human resources, technology, benchmarking, and quality assurance.¿

title: Management in Dietetics III
description: Administration and leadership in dietetic practice. Discussion of current topics and their application to the various practice areas of dietetics.

title: Evaluation of Athletic Injuries of the Extremities
description: This course is designed to familiarize the athletic training student with the principles of orthopedic assessment and to apply these principles to specific regions of the body. Knowledge gained in this course may be applicable to other individuals interested in health related professions which require systematic examination of the body.

title: Emergency Procedures & Evaluation of Core Athletic Injuries
description: Familiarize the athletic training student with the procedures of emergency management of athletic injuries and to apply these procedures both on the field and off the field. Familiarized with the principles of orthopedic and emergency medical assessment and to apply these principles to the core of the body. Knowledge gained may be applicable to other individuals interested in health related professions which require systematic examination of the body in emergency settings.

title: Pharmacology In Athletic Training
description: Study of medications commonly encountered in the practice of athletic training. Includes categories of drugs, generic and trade names of common drugs, the use, effects and precautions for common drugs and drug-interactions. Focuses on how various drugs affect the patient response to activity, exercise and other therapeutic interventions.

title: General Medical Conditions in the Athletic
description: Recognition, evaluation, management, and prevention of the most common medical conditions that affect athletic participation. Emphasis placed on the appropriate history, physical exam, indications for referral, and treatment for each condition.

title: Therapeutic Modalities in Athletic Training
description: The theory and application of the various energy systems used in the treatment of athletic injuries. Practical experiences will be emphasized.

title: Rehabilitation and Conditioning for Athletic Injuries
description: A study of applied rehabilitation and conditioning techniques used by athletic trainers.

title: Administration of Athletic Training Programs
description: A study of issues affecting athletic trainers in their roles as administrators in the areas of role delineation, budget designs, legal aspects of sport, facility design, and drug testing and drug education.

title: Management in Dietetics Practicum
description: Supervised practice experience in the application of management principles in foodservice operations or other dietetics practice settings.

title: Management in Dietetics Practicum Recitation
description: Focused discussion of significant occurrences during the supervised practice experience and the application of management principles in foodservice operations or other dietetic practice settings. Behavior-based assessment and progression is utilized to encourage self-development and entry-level competence in dietetics practice.

title: Introduction to Research in Dietetics Practice
description: Introduction to basic research design in dietetics with application of the research to evidenced-based practice using the Evidence Analysis Library of the Academy of Nutrition and Dietetics.

title: Research in Health Sciences
description: Provide initial instruction on how to locate, understand, and evaluate the research literature in the student's area of interest in health science.  A range of health research methods will be covered, and studies about treatment effectiveness.  Student learning outcomes will include various forms of communication.

title: Practicum I in Athletic Training
description: A supervised, clinical experience at approved clinical settings. The athletic training student will apply principles taught in FNDH 320.

title: Practicum II In Athletic Training
description: A supervised, clinical experience at approved clinical settings. The athletic training student will apply principles taught in the classroom and practiced in the lab throughout the curriculum program.

title: Practicum III in Athletic Training
description: A supervised clinical experience at approved clinical settings. The athletic training student will apply principles taught in the classroom and practiced in the lab throughout the curriculum program.

title: Practicum IV In Athletic Training
description: A supervised, clinical experience at approved clinical settings. The athletic training student will apply principles taught in the classroom and practiced in the lab throughout the curriculum program.

title: Practicum V In Athletic Training
description: A supervised, clinical experience at approved clinical settings. The athletic training student will apply principles taught in the classroom and practiced in the lab throughout the curriculum program.

title: Practicum VI In Athletic Training
description: A supervised, clinical experience at approved clinical settings. The athletic training student will apply principles taught in the classroom and practiced in the lab throughout the curriculum program.

title: Research Experience in Food, Nutrition, Dietetics and Health
description: Exposure to and assisting with current research projects in Food, Nutrition, Dietetics and Health.

title: Public Health Nutrition
description: Public health nutrition issues for various segments of the population; nutritional components of community assessment; program planning and evaluation; and policy issues pertaining to the nutritional status of the population.

title: Nutrient Metabolism
description: Basic concepts of the mechanisms of actions, interactions, and the processes of cellular assimilation and utilization of nutrients in humans. Emphasis on the coordinated control of nutrient utilization among the major organs.

title: Clinical Nutrition l
description: First course of a two semester study of the application of nutrition principles in disease; includes physiological basis of nutritional care, effects of disease on nutrient metabolism, medical nutrition therapy, in-class team diagnoses presentations, and nutrition counseling.

title: Clinical Nutrition II
description: Continuation of the application of nutrition principles in disease; includes physiological and biochemical basis of nutritional care, effects of disease on nutrient metabolism, medical nutrition therapy for critical care needs, standardized language in advanced professional nutrition diagnoses and care of selected diseases.

title: Sports Nutrition
description: The interrelationships among diet, nutrition, and performance. Topics covered include nutrient metabolism during exercise training and performance, nutrition and training periodization, demands of sport training, supplements and ergogenic aids, and nutrients of concern for athletes. There is a focus on athletes/sport, but topics also include normal exercising individuals who have goals related to weight loss, weight gain, or performance and competition.

title: Food Writing
description: Understanding and appreciating how to communicate effectively in writing about food and food-related topics.  Hands-on experience in research and writing for various audiences and types of publications.

title: Professional Practice and Leadership in Dietetics
description: This course explores leadership, professionalism, and current issues in dietetic practice. Students will critically examine the governance of the dietetics profession, engage in civil discourse around emerging topics, and begin to cultivate their professional identity. Emphasis is placed on advocacy, self-representation, and interprofessional collaboration within diverse practice settings. Through discussion, case studies, and applied frameworks, students will develop skills to navigate the evolving landscape of nutrition and dietetics.

title: Practicum in Food, Nutrition, Dietetics and Health
Pharmacology
description: This course is designed to bring together the body of knowledge regarding the study of medications commonly encountered in the profession of athletic training. Includes categories of drugs, generic and trade names of common drugs, the use, effects and precautions for common drugs and drug-interactions. Focuses on how various drugs affect the patient response to activity, exercise and other therapeutic interventions.

title: Pathophysiology and Clinical Evaluation
description: Designed to bring together the body of knowledge regarding the manifestation, diagnosis, and treatment of human illness, disease, and injury and the resulting physiological responses that occur during the lifespan. Content will build on anatomy, physiology and chemistry content from previous courses.

title: Nutrition and Food Behavior
description: Focus on the physiological, environmental, cultural, and economic factors that influence the use of food. Identification of appropriate methodology to study these factors as well as programs to modify food behavior.

title: Global Health and Nutrition
description: This course is designed to examine major global nutrition and health challenges, programs and policies. Students will become acquainted with the epidemiology of nutritional disease in developed and developing countries.  Programs and resources available to combat malnutrition will be evaluated. It will help students analyze current and emerging global health priorities, including emerging infectious diseases, newer nutritional problems, health inequity, health systems reforms, and major global initiatives for disease prevention and health promotion. Designed for advanced undergraduate and graduate students in health related field, including biological or social sciences.

title: Nutrition in Developing Countries
description: Nutritional problems in developing countries, including an analysis of factors which contribute to malnutrition, effects of undernutrition, methods for assessing nutritional status, and interventions to combat nutrition problems.

title: Food Across Cultures and Countries
description: Exploration of food across cultures in the U.S. and the world with emphasis on the affects of geographic, social, economic, religious, and other issues.

title: Processed Foods and Human Health
description: This course explains the relationship between processed foods and human health with a focus on ultra-processed foods (UFPs), ingredients, novel foods, epidemiological relevance, and their metabolic and physiological effects. Students will gain knowledge about the impact food structure, processing technologies, and formulation have on nutritional quality and sensory appeal. The course will also address reformulation strategies and alternative processing methods for designing healthier food systems.

title: Nutritional Immunology
description: Principles and issues related to nutrition and immunology. Impact of nutrients and nutritional status on immune responses. Impact of disease states on nutritional status.

title: Clinical Applications of Human Anatomy
description: To bring together the body of knowledge from human anatomy and biomechanics to the clinical application, palpations, and testing of human anatomy. Provides the opportunity to apply knowledge of anatomy to a structural level allowing for palpating, locating, and incorporating the findings into their diagnosis.

title: Foundations of Patient Management
description: Introduction to the role of the athletic trainer in the healthcare system and contemporary healthcare competencies that support expected standards of care.

title: Physical Health and Aging
description: Focus is on the physiological theories of aging, the relationship between normal aging processes, and the major chronic and acute diseases of the elderly, and community health promotion/maintenance programs for older adults.

title: Nutrition and Wellness
description: Epidemiology and pathophysiology of chronic disease related to nutrition (e.g., obesity, cardiovascular disease, osteoporosis, hypertension, diabetes, cancer). Nutritional risk and protective factors will be examined as they relate to public health and individual nutrition.

title: Nutritional Oncology
description: Students will gain understanding of basic cancer biology and methodology used to study nutrition and cancer relationships. Using current research as a basis, the role of nutrition in specific cancers will be explored. Students will learn about sources of information for cancer prevention programs, and how to apply this information to clinical patient management.

title: Advanced Diabetes Management
description: The course is designed for students to gain a comprehensive understanding of the classification, presentation, and diagnosis of diabetes and pre-diabetes. The course will explore etiology, pathophysiology, diagnostic tests, clinical management, chronic complications and co-morbidities, and emerging technologies.

title: Advanced Cardiovascular Medical Nutrition Therapy
description: This course provides in-depth exploration of nutrition in prevention, management, and treatment of cardiovascular diseases (CVD). Students will evaluate evidence for dietary patterns, nutrients, and supplements in relation to CVD risk factors. Medical nutrition therapy for cardiovascular disease, including hypertension, hyperlipidemia, heart failure, and metabolic syndrome, will be explored.

title: Advanced Energy Balance
description: Lifestyle, metabolic, and endocrine factors affecting energy balance and the development of obesity. Research methods to assess and apply energy balance data will also be discussed.

title: Dietary Supplements and Human Performance
description: Examines and applies information about diet supplements (efficacy, doses, mechanisms of action) and the diet supplement industry (history, policies, regulation). Will cover common and novel diet supplement compounds commercially available. Time will be set aside for specific supplements of interest to enrolled students.

title: Fundamentals of Public Health and Food Safety
description: Organization and function of food inspection services; principles of disease transmission; diseases transmitted to humans through the food chain.

title: Emergency Management in Athletic Training
description: This course is designed to be a fast paced review of introduction to athletic training and care and prevention of athletic training injuries. This course will also bring together the body of knowledge regarding emergency management while applying it to the profession of athletic training.

title: Clinical Applications of Neurological Function
description: Scientific approach to advance knowledge related to the assessment of structured movement of the human body in relationship to neurological function and pathologies. Cognitive (knowledge), psychomotor (hands-on) learning, affective, and clinical/quantitative skills with a heavy emphasis on neuroanatomy to stress clinical application.

title: Advanced Athletic Training Techniques
description: This course will explore the application of advanced athletic training techniques in specific orthopedic-related areas.

title: Applied Dietetics
description: Application of dietetics/nutrition principles through workshops, case studies, independent research, discussion, and simulation. Behavior-based assessment and progression is utilized to encourage self-development and entry-level competence in dietetics practice. A variety of practice areas will be covered.

title: Applied Management in Dietetics
description: Application of management principles in foodservice operations or other dietetic practice settings through case studies, independent research, discussion, and projects.  Behavior-based assessment and progression is utilized to encourage self-development and entry-level competence in dietetics practice.  Includes focused discussion of significant occurrences during the Dietetic Internship experience.

title: Applied Clinical Dietetics
description: Application will focus on medical nutrition therapy and education of persons throughout the life cycle and nutrition intervention for individuals with multiple disease states in various healthcare settings.  Behavior-based assessment and progression is utilized to encourage self-development and entry-level competence in dietetics practice.  Includes focused discussion of significant occurrences during the Dietetic Internship experience.

title: Research in Health Sciences
description: Students in this graduate course will review the role and importance of the scientific method in health professions, especially those with clinical application. The rationale and role of evidence-based practice and research will be reviewed, and research language and core concepts will be discussed. Students will demonstrate their ability to ask research questions and translate questions into search strategies for finding evidence.  The purpose of this course is to provide students the opportunity to demonstrate their capacity to locate, understand, and evaluate the research literature in the student's area of interest. The expectation is to demonstrate understanding of research methodology the ability to critically evaluate research literature. Students will demonstrate knowledge of quantitative and qualitative research methods, types of data, how data are described and how biostatistics are used to provide meaning to research data. A range of health research methods will be covered, and studies about treatment effectiveness (clinical trials and systematic reviews), as well as qualitative approaches will be discussed.

title: Problems in Food, Nutrition, Dietetics and Health
description: Supervised individual project to study current issues.

title: Topics in Food Nutrition, and Health
Professional Preparation for Athletic Training
description: â€‹Provide a comprehensive review of athletic training knowledge in preparation for the national athletic training board examination and cover topics important to transition to practice for entry-level athletic trainers.

title: Injury Prevention and Performance
description: Theory and application of the various strategies commonly used for strength and conditioning techniques and prescription, movement strategy and preventative care, ergonomic assessment, and use of biometrics and physiological data for preventative and performance intervention prescription. This course is a study of applied injury prevention and performance enhancement techniques used in the profession of athletic training.

title: Foundations of Therapeutic Interventions
description: Provide students with the foundational knowledge of how to integrate the basic sciences, exercise physiology, biomechanical, and injury prevention strategies into a well-planned and evidence based patient care plan. Focused theory and application of therapeutic modalities, applied rehabilitation, and conditioning techniques used in the profession of athletic training.

title: Nutrition Education and Communication
description: Apply principles of communication and learning technologies to nutrition education research and practice.

title: Clinical Therapeutic Interventions I - Functional Therapeutic Interventions
description: Application of treatment strategies for return to activity, strength and conditioning techniques and prescription, movement strategy and preventative care and ergonomic assessment. Use of biometrics, and physiological data for intervention prescription.  This course is a study of applied rehabilitation and conditioning techniques used in the profession of athletic training.

title: Clinical Therapeutic Interventions II- Extremity Interventions
description: Theory and application of the various treatment strategies used in the treatment of the upper extremity and lower extremity.

title: Clinical Therapeutic Interventions III- Pharmacology, Nutrition, and Behavior Modification
description: Inform students of the basic principles pertaining to and the reasoning behind the inclusion of pharmacological agents and nutrition as part of a patient's holistic plan of care. Emphasis placed on the athletic trainer's role as educator and part of an interdisciplinary team to ensure behavior modification and compliance.

title: Advanced Macronutrient Metabolism
description: Dietary, metabolic, and endocrine factors regulating food intake and selection, and the absorption, function, metabolism, and interrelationships of macronutrients under varying nutritional conditions, with emphasis on recent advances in macronutrient nutrition.

title: Advanced Micronutrient Metabolism
description: Nutritional roles and metabolism of vitamins and minerals. Functions, biological availability, hormonal regulation, requirements, deficiency and toxicity signs, and interrelations with other nutrients.

title: Molecular Basis of Nutrient Function
description: The course is designed to explore the role that certain nutrients have at the gene and molecular levels in terms of their mechanism of action. The physiological consequences of nutrient gene-interaction from a tissue, organ and organism level, as well as the subcellular levels, are to be considered.

title: Functional Foods for Chronic Disease Prevention
description: Integrate and evaluate the regulatory principles, food science, nutrient science and nutritional metabolism for the development of functional foods, nutraceuticals, and dietary supplements for chronic disease prevention.

title: Clinical Evaluation I-Core Evaluation
description: Application of clinical and functional injury evaluation procedures and techniques for the trunk, torso, abdomen, spine, and kinetic chain.

title: Clinical Evaluation II-Extremities
description: Application of contemporary principles and practices of patient examination for formulation of a diagnosis and plan of care for patients with health conditions of the extremities that are commonly seen in athletic training practice.

title: Evaluation and Management of General Medical Conditions in Athletic Training I
description: This is the first in a series of three courses to meet outcomes and guidelines related to the prevention, evaluation, and management of medical conditions/disabilities as they relate to athletic training practice. Students will obtain knowledge, skill, and clinical decision-making to accurately assess and recognize general medical conditions, make appropriate referrals, and work as part of a healthcare team to implement plans for safe participation in physical activity and sport. Instruction will consist of lectures, labs, problem/case-based learning, and small group collaboration.

title: Evaluation and Management of General Medical Conditions in Athletic Training II
description: This is the second in a series of three courses designed to meet outcomes and guidelines related to the prevention, evaluation, and management of medical conditions/disabilities as they relate to athletic training practice. Students will obtain knowledge, skill, and clinical decision-making to accurately assess and recognize general medical conditions, make appropriate referrals, and work as part of a healthcare team to implement plans for safe participation in physical activity and sport. Instruction will consist of lectures, labs, problem/case-based learning, and small group collaboration.

title: Evaluation and Management of General Medical Conditions in Athletic Training III
description: This is the third in a series of three courses designed to meet outcomes and guidelines related to prevention, evaluation, and management of medical conditions/disabilities as they relate to athletic training practice. Students will obtain knowledge, skill, and clinical decision-making to accurately assess and recognize general medical conditions, make appropriate referrals, and work as part of a healthcare team to implement plans for safe participation in physical activity and sport. Instruction will consist of lectures, labs, problem/case-based learning, and small group collaboration.

title: Advanced Clinical Dietetics
description: An in-depth study of the pathophysiology of nutritional disease. Those functional disorders which result in nutritional disease or those nutritional diseases which affect physiological function will be explored. The emphasis will be in the following areas: nutritional assessment, endocrinology, metabolism, gastroenterology, and organ failure.

title: Clinical Nutrition Support
description: Specialized nutrition assessment and support. Review of energy expenditure and substrate utilization in specific disease states. Current methods for the initiation and management of enteral and parenteral nutrition therapy including access, metabolic and mechanical complications. Evaluation of nutrition support methodology in selected disease states.

title: Public Health Practice
description: Supervised field experience in an international, state, local, or district health agency or other appropriate health agency.
