title: Advanced Nutrition:  Nutrigenomics, Nutrigenetics, and Advanced Lipid Metabolism
description: Fundamentals as how the macronutrients; fat, carbohydrates and protein act to regulate gene expression(nutrigenetics) and how an individuals genotype may alter an individuals macronutrient requirements (nutrigenetics) and influence phenotype with respect to chronic disease and human health.

title: Nutritional Epidemiology
description: Methods and issues involved in understanding and conducting studies on the relationship between human diet and disease.

title: Administration in Athletic Training
description: This course is designed to bring together the body of knowledge regarding organization and administration while applying it to the profession of athletic training.

title: Durable Medical Equipment and Casting
description: Application of contemporary principles and practices of how to select, fabricate, and/or customize prophylactic, assistive, and restrictive devices, materials, and techniques for incorporation into the plan of care, including the Durable medical equipment, Orthotic devices, Taping, splinting, protective padding, and casting.

title: Nutrition: A Focus on Life Stages
description: Nutritional needs throughout the life span including pregnancy, lactation, growth and aging. Approaches to nutrition education for different ages.

title: Dietetics Practicum
description: Application of dietetics/nutrition principles in a supervised practice experience within an approved setting.  Practicum experiences are arranged with healthcare facilities, foodservice operations, private practice, community organizations, and other related partners.

title: Maternal and Child Nutrition
description: A critical examination of behavioral, physiological, and public health issues impacting dietary and nutritional factors that support normal growth and development. The course content focuses on the early stages of the life cycle: gestation, lactation, infancy, preschool, school age and adolescence. Topics include the fetal programming hypothesis, growth and nutritional requirements, breast and formula feeding of infants, infant weaning, and eating behaviors that lead to normal growth, growth faltering, and pediatric obesity.

title: Pediatric Clinical Nutrition
description: The physiological, biochemical and nutritional aspects of disease processes relevant to infants and children up to 18 years of age. Medical nutrition therapy for a variety of medical conditions found in this Population will be discussed including inborn errors of metabolism, food hypersensitivity, obesity, and diseases of the major organ systems.

title: Professional Communication in FNDH
description: Develop and deliver oral presentations, write for the profession, and communicate in a professional manner.

title: Athletic Training Practicum I
description: Application of classroom principles in a supervised, clinical experience within an approved clinical setting.

title: Athletic Training Practicum II
description: Application of classroom principles in a supervised, clinical experience within an approved clinical setting.

title: Athletic Training Practicum III
description: Application of classroom principles in a supervised, clinical experience within an approved clinical setting.

title: Athletic Training Practicum IV
description: Application of classroom principles in a supervised, clinical experience within an approved clinical setting.

title: Athletic Training Practicum V
description: Application of classroom principles in a supervised, clinical experience within an approved clinical setting.

title: Athletic Training Practicum VI
description: Application of classroom principles in a supervised, clinical experience within an approved clinical setting.

title: Research Experience in Athletic Training
description: Evidence based research application for Athletic Training. To inform contemporary principles and clinical athletic training practices in a manner that uses evidence to inform clinical decisions.

title: Environmental Scanning and Analysis of Current Issues in Dietetics
description: Discussion of changes in the economic, social, ethical, political, legal, technological, and ecological environments in which dietitians practice. Implications of these changes for education, practice and research within the field with particular emphasis on the healthcare industry.

title: Population Health in Sports Medicine
description: An introduction for athletic trainers and other healthcare providers, to the benefits of using a population-based approach to injury and illness prevention as it relates to a physically active/athletic population and to explore opportunities for partnering with public health professionals on these initiatives.

title: Financial Management and Cost Control in Dietetics Practice
description: This course provides an overview of the fundamental knowledge of financial management, managerial, accounting, and operational cost controls for dietetics professionals who work predominately in not-for-profit organizations.  Topics include a review of managerial accounting concepts for not-for-profit organizations, value and risk analyses, budgeting, asset management franchising and management contractgs, cost-volume-profit analyses, and operational applications for financial performance.

title: Master's Report
description: Survey in depth of the literature.

title: Advanced Nutrition Topics: Contemporary Issues
description: Contemporary concerns in health and disease from an advanced nutrition perspective.

title: Eating Behavior
description: Study of the theoretical and practical aspects of food choice.  Impacts of social, economic, and political policies on food choice and impacts to global issues by individual food choices.

title: Grantsmanship and Publications
description: Grant writing, identifying external funding, managing grants, preparing manuscripts for peer-reviewed publication, and preparing papers and poster for presentation at professional meetings.

title: Park And Recreation Areas Field Studies
Microcomputer Applications In Natural Resource Management
Use Of Natural Resources For Leisure
Urban Forest Administration
description: This course is a study of urban and community forest administration. It considers the urban forest ecosystem involving an in-depth look at ownerships. composition, distribution, benefits, values, and administrative operation. The policies and politics of successful administration will be emphasized.

title: Forestry Problems
description: Work is offered in various fields of forestry.

title: Orientation For Summer School Program in Paris
French I (Elementary French I)
description: Develops basic language skills and cultural awareness to function in everyday situations in French-speaking communities, for students with little to no prior French experience. Conducted in French with practice in speaking, listening, reading, and writing.

title: French II
description: Continuation of French I, completion of basic presentation of the structure of French. Emphasis on spoken language.

title: Beginning Accelerated French
description: Course covering material from French I and II in one semester. Listening, speaking, reading, and writing. Includes one hour lab per week in language laboratory or other language opportunities outside of class time.

title: French III
description: Continuation of French II, presentation of more advanced elements of the French language. Emphasis on spoken language.

title: French IV
description: Continuation of French III, presentation of more advanced elements of the French language. Emphasis on spoken language.

title: Intermediate Studies In French
description: Offered only to participants in study abroad programs.

title: Readings in French
description: Practice in reading a variety of literary, journalistic, and specialized texts.

title: Black African Francophone Literature In Translation
French Phonetics
description: The fundamentals of French phonetics. Intensive practice in diction.

title: French/Francophone Cinema
description: The course consists of readings, written assignments, viewing, and discussion of French or Francophone films.

title: Contemporary France
description: Introduction to French culture with special emphasis on social and historical developments since World War II.

title: History of French Culture
description: A survey of French culture from the Middle Ages to the present, including but not limited to, developments in art and architecture, music, and literature.

title: French for the Professions
description: Development of French language skills useful in diverse professional situations including specialized terminology, conversation and discussion, and translation. May include agriculture, tourism, fashion, international relations, and business.

title: French Conversation
description: Practice in spoken French, with emphasis on idiomatic expression. Course not open to students whose primary language is French and whose competence has been demonstrated in the language at this level.

title: Introduction to French Literature (19th Century to the Present
description: The reading and discussion of major works of French literature from the early nineteenth century to the present.

title: Introduction to French Literature (Medieval to 18th Century)
description: The reading and discussion of major works of French literature from the Middle Ages to the end of the eighteenth century.

title: Topics in French/Francophone Literature and Culture
description: Provides the students the opportunity to investigate in detail a particular theme or genre in French literature or culture.

title: Discovering French Linguistics
description: Introduction to the fundamentals of linguistic analysis. Topics may include sound system, word and sentence formation, semantic and pragmatic meaning, application of linguistics to language acquisition theory and to pedagogy.

title: Medieval French Literature
description: An introduction to literary forms, style, and the thought from the eleventh century to the fifteenth century in France. Readings in modern French in Chanson de Roland, Chretien de Troyes, Roman de la Rose , etc.

title: Sixteenth-Century French Literature
description: Reading and discussion of selected prose and poetry of the French Renaissance.

title: Seventeenth Century French Literature
description: Various literary forms of the French Baroque and Classical period. Reading of representative texts by Corneille, Pascal, Moliere, Racine, La Fontaine, and others.

title: Eighteenth-Century French Literature
description: Critical study of the literature of the Enlightenment.

title: Romantic French Literature
description: A study of preromanticism and romanticism.

title: 19th Century French Literature
description: Topics in French literature of 1800-1899 including developments in poetry, prose, and theater.

title: Topics in Contemporary French Literature
description: The study of major themes and trends in the novel, drama, and poetry since 1900 as reflected by major authors as Proust, Camus, Sarraute, and others. This is a topics course repeatable with a different topic.

title: Advanced Spoken and Written French
description: An advanced, intensive study of French prose style. Introduction to the techniques of translation from English to French. Intensive practice in oral style and diction.

title: Seminar in French Literature and Culture
description: A seminar with various topics centered on literature and culture.

title: Francophone Literature and Culture
description: The development of French colonial and postcolonial literature and culture with an emphasis on the twentieth and twenty-first centuries. Regional focus may vary from Quebecois, Caribbean, Sub-Saharan or North African literature and culture.

title: Advanced French for Professions
description: Advanced language necessary for adequate oral and written expression in international business and diplomatic situations, including specialized terminology, conversation and discussion, and translation.

title: French and Francophone Literature for Second Language Acquisition
description: Analysis and interpretation of cultural and literary texts from French-speaking countries, with emphasis on the development of interpretive skills and materials, and their application to the French curriculum at all levels.

title: French Second Language Acquisition Culture and Literature
description: Analysis and interpretation of cultural resources and texts from French-speaking countries, with emphasis on the development of interpretive skills and materials, and their application to the French curriculum at all levels.

title: Topics in French Translation
description: Analysis and practice in bi-directional translation [French-English-French] of a variety of texts from a number of genres, including literary texts.  Discussion of professional opportunities for translators and interpreters.  Development of personal portfolio.

title: Topics in French Linguistics
description: Advanced topics in French linguistics.  These may include introduction to the social history and internal structure of the French language, advanced phonetics, advanced grammar (word and sentence formation, semantic and pragmatic meaning), application of linguistics to language acquisition theory and to pedagogy.

title: Master's Report
description: A written report of either research or problem work on a topic in the major field.

title: International Experience in Family Studies and Human Services
Families, Employment Benefits And Retirement Planning
Seminar in Family Studies and Human Services
Clinical Approaches to Family Health and Illness
Practica in Family Studies and Human Services
Application of Research and Statistics in Family Science
Principles and Techniques of Family Measurement
Undergraduate Education Abroad
description: ""Education abroad"" is a chance to pursue your college studies in a foreign country . Students attend lectures or carry out research at a foreign university or through K-State's education-abroad program.

title: Agriculture Scholars Program
description: For all undergraduate students in the College of Agriculture Scholars Program. Students should enroll every semester while active in the program.

title: University Honors Program
description: All students participating in the University Honors Program and who are enrolled in the College of Agriculture must enroll each semester.

title: Ag Orientation
description: Objectives, organization, and procedures of the College of Agriculture and the university are studied. Historical developments and projected trends in agriculture and the application of basic sciences to agriculture are presented.

title: Agriculture Advocacy and Leadership Training
description: The Agricultural Leadership Program (ALP) equips students with knowledge of the College of Agriculture and provides basic advocacy and leadership training, including skills development in active listening and communication. Facilitated by College of Agriculture Ambassadors, the course features guest speakers, hands-on activities, and field trips. While required for newly selected Ag Ambassadors, it is open to all K-State students.

title: Ag Pathway: Animal Science, Health and Related Industries
description: For completers of the Kansas Career Cluster Pathway in Agriculture, Food, and Natural Resources Career Cluster, Animal Science, Health and Related Industries

title: Ag Pathway: Diversified Agricultural Science
description: For completers of the Kansas Career Cluster Pathway in Agriculture, Food, and Natural Resources Career Cluster, Diversified Agricultural Science

title: Ag Pathway: Food Products and Processing Systems
description: For completers of the Kansas Career Cluster Pathway in Agriculture, Food, and Natural Resources Career Cluster, Food Products and Processing Systems

title: Ag Pathway: Natural Resources and Environmental Sustainability
description: For completers of the Kansas Career Cluster Pathway in Agriculture, Food, and Natural Resources Career Cluster, Natural Resources and Environmental Sustainability

title: Ag Pathway: Plant Science and Industry Operations
description: For completers of the Kansas Career Cluster Pathway in Agriculture, Food, and Natural Resources Career Cluster, Plant Science and Industry Operations

title: Introduction to the University Honors Program
description: Overview of the University Honors Program including directions, goals, and student requirements for completion of the program.

title: Independent Study in Agriculture
description: Student-specific projects or independent guided studies in agriculture.

title: Leadership for Agriculture Advocacy
description: This CAT Community ""connector class"" will help develop your advocacy skills for the agricultural industry by examining assumptions of advocacy and leadership.  Explore and engage agricultural issues from the perspective of multiple stakeholders and develop your skills in critical thinking, communication, and productive conflict. This class will be highly interactive and discussion-based. We will focus on current events and engage with industry leaders.

title: Animal Agriculture in 2050
description: This CAT Community ""connector class"" will focus on issues and opportunities related to livestock production in the United States. Potential impacts of new technologies, new international competitors and markets, evolving consumer preferences and expectations, and societal concerns with animal agriculture will be examined. Paradigms that result in successful academic performance and meeting academic and career goals will also be discussed.

title: The Need for Feed: Grain Science
description: This CAT Community ""connector class"" will address the fundamentals of grain science, while at the same time gaining hands-on experience in K-State's modern laboratories, which include industrial scale flour mills, a feed production facility, and a bakery. It will also serve as an introduction to grains and their processing/utilization in food and non-food products, as well as exposure to career opportunities in grain and related industries.

title: Human Dynamics in the Food and Agriculture Sciences
description: This course introduces agriculture students to the human and social dimensions of food and agricultural sciences. Through a variety of activities, students will explore topics such as cultural awareness, current issues, and the wide-ranging contributions of individuals and communities within the agricultural field.

title: Fundamentals of Global Food Systems Leadership
description: An interdisciplinary approach to the fundamental roles people, policies, and cultures play in the global food system enterprise as it relates to sustainable food production, processing, distribution, and availability. Students will explore complexities within self, others, and community related to leadership in the context of a rapidly increasing global population.

title: Uncertainty in Global Food Systems Leadership
description: Explore technical and adaptive elements of grand challenges across disciplines. This course addresses the impact of cultural identity, life experience, and world views on leadership relationships as it relates to privilege and inclusion in the context of global food systems. Students will consider elements of community-engaged work.

title: Research Topic and Proposal Development
description: Undergraduate honors and scholars students learn the research process and identify and work with a faculty mentor to prepare an individual undergraduate research project proposal. Class presentations focus on strategies to identify and narrow a research topic, seek a research mentor, and develop a research proposal. Additional content will address common research methods, safety and compliance issues, and tips for persuasively presenting research results.

title: Agricultural Cooperative Education Experience
description: Integration of academic principles into supervised industry experience in agricultural related field.

title: Global Agricultural Solutions and Leadership
description: This interdisciplinary, experiential-learning course prepares undergraduate students to collaboratively address real-world agricultural challenges in global contexts. Students work in teams on a project provided by an international organization focused on issues such as food insecurity, clean water, crop or animal production, or sustainable development. The course culminates in international travel, where students collaborate with global scientists and present their final projects on-site.

title: Global Agricultural Solutions and Leadership Travel
description: This interdisciplinary, experiential-learning course prepares undergraduate students to collaboratively address real-world agricultural challenges in global contexts. Students work in teams on a project provided by an international organization focused on issues such as food insecurity, clean water, crop or animal production, or sustainable development. The course culminates in international travel, where students collaborate with global scientists and present their final projects on-site.

title: Global Food Systems Leadership in Action
description: In this capstone course, students will conduct a community-engaged global food systems leadership research/service project. Emphasis will be placed on data collection, analysis and dissemination to appropriate audiences. Additional emphases will be on ethical dimensions of leadership and individual exploration of careers and roles within global food systems.

title: Citizenship and Ethics in Agriculture
description: The study of agriculture's relationship with society while encompassing ethics and personal development. Current controversial issues and multidimensional policy topics facing the agricultural industry will be explored with an emphasis on moral and philosophical debates. Issues regarding professional ethics and decision making will also be an emphasis.

title: Pre-Travel Seminar
description: Prepares students for short-term international study through academic, cultural, and logistical orientation. Content includes destination-specific history and culture, discipline-relevant topics, learning objectives, and reflective communication connected to the international experience.

title: Agriculture Global Learning Experience
description: An immersive, faculty-guided international course in which students apply disciplinary concepts through site visits, field activities, and cultural engagement. Emphasizes experiential learning and structured reflection within the context of the host country.

title: Agriculture Honors Seminar
description: Seminars treat topics relevant to students participating in the University Honors Program.

title: Honors/Scholars Project
description: Completion of an independent undergraduate research project or scholarly activity required for the University Honors Program or the College of Agriculture Scholars Program. Oral presentation and written report required. Taken during the semester when the project will be completed.

title: Natural Resources/Environmental Sciences Project
description: A comprehensive project in NRES. Requires integration of information and understanding acquired in NRES secondary major courses. Students must prepare and present written and oral reports.

title: Introduction to Agricultural Resources and Environmental Management
description: Interdisciplinary, international study of agricultural and natural resource management sustainability.  Discussion of agro-ecologic, economic, and social science perspectives; sustainability criteria; issues of scale and culture; disciplinary methods; and systems-level approaches. Applications to important natural resources and environmental management issues in Kansas, U.S., and in other countries.

title: Seminar in International Agriculture
description: An in-depth study of international agriculture topics.

title: Current Topics in Agriculture
description: Selected topics studied to provide an in-depth understanding of current agricultural issues.

title: Managing Occupational and Agricultural Safety and Health
description: Examines program management and leadership issues in occupational and agricultural safety and health. Topics include business aspects of safety and health, management functions, workforce involvement, program development, motivation and leadership, program evaluation, ethics of safety and health, responding to the needs of a diverse workforce, and innovative program management for changing economic and societal contexts.

title: Capstone for Agricultural Resources and Environmental Management
description: Interdisciplinary, international application of agro-ecologic, economic, social science, and sustainability concepts and methods to important agricultural, natural resources and environmental management issues in Kansas, U.S., and other countries.

title: University Honors Program
description: All students participating in the University Honors Program and who are enrolled in the College of Business Administration must enroll each semester.

title: Business Foundations
description: In a highly interactive learning environment, topics in accounting, business creation, finance, information systems, management, marketing, and operations are covered, while integrating ethics and responsible business citizenship. The course demonstrates how core business areas are interrelated and the importance of creativity and innovation to business. In this applied class, students well engage in the process of making business decisions.

title: Business and Society
description: An overview of the role of business in society. Students will engage in a variety of activities including group discussions, practical learning exercises, and case examples to examine the historical, current, and future role of business in society. Students will explore the role that critical thinking plays in forming business decisions and learn about the impact of differing economic environments on businesses. Students will examine the importance of the entrepreneurial mindset in value creation and how businesses generate value for society.

title: Introductions to the University Honors Program
description: Overview of the University Honors Program including directions, goals, and student requirements for completion of the program.

title: CAT Community Connections
description: Interdisciplinary course that explores the connections among two different fields, courses, or disciplines. Topics and corequisites vary by section and semester.

title: Career Accelerator
description: Essential course on career preparation for all business students with topics focused on career exploration, personal brand, networking, resumes, interview skills, and workplace expectations.  Students completing this course will be well-equipped to engage with employers while leveraging extensive campus and College of Business resources to enhance internship and future employment opportunities.

title: Honors Colloquium In Business
description: Discussions and lectures on topics of interest to business students.

title: International Business Experience
description: An international business experience that requires travel to a foreign country. The length of the tour varies from one week to 16 weeks. This course enables students to complete an international experience that would include study, research, or work in a foreign country. The course provides enriched experience in the financial and business practices in a foreign country and enhances students'' global and cultural awareness.

title: Administrative Communications
description: Preparation of business communications, reports, and correspondence, and analysis of communication systems within an enterprise structure.

title: Honors Seminar
description: The Honors Seminar is designed as a follow-up course to the Honors colloquium and it prepares students for the honors capstone project that culminates into the honors project. It also provides a forum for all honors students who are working on their capstone honors project to share and discuss their project ideas.

title: Problems In Business Administration
description: In-depth analysis of special problems in general business including study of current literature.

title: Business Honors Project
description: A business honors project is an independent study in an intellectual endeavor (research or creative activity) under the supervision of a faculty member.  The project must be approved by the College of Business Honors Director in consultation with the faculty supervisor and the University Honors Program Director.  Deliverables of an Honors project may include a paper presented at a student or professional conference, a paper presented at a faculty/student seminar, a paper appearing in a poster presentation or a paper submitted for a national competition.

title: International Business Experience
description: An international business experience that requires travel to a foreign country. The length of the tour varies from one week to 16 weeks. This course enables students to complete an international experience that would include study, research or work in a foreign country. The course provides enriched experience in the financial and business practices in a foreign country and enhances students' global and cultural awareness.

title: MBA Professional Development
description: This course will require the student to work with the director of the MBA program to outline a professional development program of 45 hours per hour of credit to enhance their MBA studies and to enhance their professional career.

title: International Field Study
description: Graduate level course designed to provide students with an opportunity to observe international business by participating in a field trip outside the United States.  This experiential course may involve a combination of business visits, interactions with business executives, cultural experiences, internship activities, or other learning activities while abroad.

title: MBA International Business Experience
description: This course will consist of a faculty-led international experience designed to expose students to international business practices and culture.

title: Business Capstone
description: The analysis of business problems, using knowledge and tools from previous courses. Students, under the supervision of a team of faculty, will analyze actual business case problems.

title: Data Analytics Capstone
description: This course is a hands-on, project based course completed in cross-disciplinary groups. The capstone course will require students to work together on projects that will demonstrate their ability to collaboratively analyze large datasets, provide insight for practical problems and effectively communicate the resulting insights.

title: Topics in Business
description: Discussion and analysis of current issues in business such as accounting, management, finance, marketing, etc.

title: World Geography & Globalization
description: Introduction to geography structured on a framework of major world regions and countries. Within the regional approach is an explicit discussion of the essential concepts rooted in historical, cultural, social, political, economic, religious, gender, urban geography, and globalization.

title: Protection Or Perish: Critical Environmental Choices
Introductory Physical Geography: Earth Systems Science
description: A systemic introduction to the processes and spatial patterns of the atmosphere, biosphere, geosphere, and hydrosphere and their interactions at the Earth's surface. Prepares science and engineering majors for more advanced coursework and promotes improved environmental literacy of non-science majors.

title: Introduction to Physical Geography: Earth Systems Science Laboratory
description: Laboratory investigation of the processes and spatial pasterns of the atmosphere, biosphere, geosphere, and hydrosphere and their interactions at the Earth's surface.

title: Human Geography
description: A geographical assessment of the way human activities shape landscapes throughout the world. The course is especially appropriate for students interested in the social and behavioral sciences.

title: Human Geography (Honors)
description: Spatial aspects of human organization and behavior are examined through selected concepts in modern geography. The course is especially appropriate for students interested in the social and behavioral sciences.

title: TESTING Geography is Fun
description: The description of geography is fun & exciting.

title: Atmospheric Science
description: A comprehensive scientific study of the physics, chemistry, and dynamics of the earth's atmosphere, from the earth's surface to several hundred kilometers.  Fundamental topics covered include atmospheric composition and variability, subdivisions of the atmosphere, energy and mass transfers within the Earth-atmosphere system, atmospheric circulation, and accurate prediction of atmospheric phenomena.

title: Geography of Tourism
description: The geography of tourism is concerned with the structure, form, use, and conservation of the landscape as well as with such spatial conditions as the location of tourist areas and the movements of people from place to place. This course addresses such concepts as the economic, environmental, social, and cultural impacts of tourism as well as examining the tourist geography of each of the world's regions, focusing on the major tourist areas.

title: Cartography and Thematic Mapping
description: Introduction to cartographic history, theory and principles, thematic map design, symbolization, map perception, color theory, typography, and digital cartographic research. Laboratory work will familiarize students with the latest cartographic software that will be used to produce a series of thematic maps.

title: Geography of Kansas
description: A regional and historical analysis of Kansas including discussion of the physical environment, historical development, and current geographic patterns.

title: Introduction to Japan
description: Geographical survey of Japan from prehistoric times to the present day, which may include material on society, politics, and culture.

title: Introduction to China
description: Survey of the geography, history, politics, and culture of China from prehistoric times to the present day.

title: European Landscapes
description: Explores how humans have interacted with their environment and modified the natural and cultural landscape within Europe.

title: Natural Resources
description: The significance, environmental consequences, and locational characteristics of natural resources and resource-based economic activities, including agriculture, fishing, forestry, and mining.  Sustainability and conservation considerations related to natural resource extraction, management, and use.

title: Sustainability Concepts & Issues
description: Exploration of sustainability concepts, approaches, and decision-making.  Relation of sustainability to environmental, economic, and social considerations at global, regional, and local scales.

title: Environmental Justice
description: Introduction to the history of the environmental justice movement with a focus on resource exploitation, degradation of natural resources, (in)justice, race, class, and governance.

title: World Population Issues
description: Discussion of world population distribution, composition, growth, and migration patterns and processes.  Emphasis is on recent demographic changes at global, regional and local scales and their socioeconomic and environmental implications.

title: Introduction to the Civilization of South Asia II
description: Introduction to the Civilization of South Asia II

title: Spatial Analysis of Surface Water Hydrology
description: Introduces components and measurement techniques of surface water hydrology including precipitation, evapotranspiration, overland flow, and stream flow, as well as their spatial distribution on the Earth's surface and relationships between changes in surface water and the environment.

title: Biogeography
description: Covers: 1) the spatial and temporal patterns of the distribution of living organisms on Earth (including plants, animals, and microbes), and 2) the biological and physical processes responsible for these patterns. Biogeography has crucial insights to offer regarding conservation of rare species, global changes such as climate change, and human domination of the Earth surface.

title: Geography of Economic Behavior
description: The location of manufacturing industries and patterns of commercial activity. Case studies and simulations are used with emphasis on modern concepts of site selection and community development.

title: Human Dimensions of Global Change
description: An examination of the complexity of forces driving global change, with an emphasis on change related to population growth, technological development, and sociocultural and socioeconomic institutions.

title: Capstone Seminar in Geography
description: An integrative capstone seminar requiring geography majors to synthesize knowledge and skills acquired in prior geography courses. Students pursue independent projects in consultation with a faculty member and present their findings in written and oral reports. The course exposes students to and helps prepare them for a variety of professional and scholarly opportunities available after graduation.

title: Undergraduate Research in Geography
description: Independent research project conducted with the guidance of a faculty mentor.

title: Honors Tutorial in Geography
description: Individual directed research and study of a topic in geography, normally as a preliminary to writing a senior honors thesis.

title: Honors Project
description: Open only to Arts & Sciences students who are active members of the University Honors Program.

title: Geography of the United States
description: A regional analysis of the United States with special attention to the historical, political, economic, and social factors which contribute to areal differentiation within the area.

title: South Asian Civilizations
description: Interdisciplinary survey on the development of civilization in India, Pakistan, Sri Lanka, Bangladesh, Nepal, Bhutan, and Afghanistan, including geography, philosophy, social, economic, political institutions, and historical movements.

title: Introduction To The Civilization Of South Asia 2
Geographic Information Systems I
description: Examination of the major theories, concepts, and operations in geographic information systems (GIS). Topics include: the structure of geographic data models, geographic data acquisition, spatial database management, data processing methods. Vector and raster GIS operations, and general approaches to GIS-based spatial modeling.

title: Geography of the American West
description: A broad survey of the geography of the American West with a focus on the distinctive human and environmental characteristics of the region. Historical, cultural, ethnic, resource, land use, and physical landscape patterns are examined through lectures, readings, videos, and discussions.

title: Global Climatology and Climate Change
description: An examination of the physical processes that control the dynamics and spatial distribution of climates at the global scale, and how these processes can result in variation and change in the climate system. Topics include  radiation and energy balance, global circulation, and the hydrologic cycle as well as the biosphere and humans as climate regulators and causes of change.

title: Political Geography and Geopolitics
description: Critically analyzes relations between space and power in historical and contemporary geopolitical contexts, including topics such as sovereignty, border making, territorial disputes, and the formation (and dissolution) of nation-states.

title: Mountain Geography
description: A broad survey of the human and physical geography of mountains. The course utilizes lectures, discussion, videos, and photographs to examine the human-environment interactions, cultural symbolism and sacredness, recreation and tourism, and sustainable development of mountain landscapes. The regional focus is primarily on the American West, but other mountains throughout the world will also be studied.

title: Computer Mapping and Geographic Visualization
description: Basic cartographic principles, advanced methods for representing spatial data, approaches for exploratory spatial data analysis, and practical applications of thematic maps, animated and Internet-based maps, and geographic visualization techniques. Students will prepare a series of maps and visualization products using modern geographic information system and illustration software.

title: Remote Sensing of the Environment
description: Remote sensing and its application to earth study, especially environmental problems and land use. Course employs both readings and the use of imagery.

title: Geographic Information Systems II
description: Advanced principles of and applications for geographic information systems (GIS). Examines the nature and accuracy of geo-referenced data and methods of data capture, storage, retrieval, modeling, and digital map display. Students will use modern GIS software packages and digital geographic data from physical and/or cultural sources to explore software procedures and techniques of spatial analysis, decision support, and geographic visualization.

title: Geography Internship
description: Faculty-supervised field experience, emphasizing the application of geographical topics and/or techniques. Student projects must be approved by both the on-site director and the faculty supervisor, and a report must be submitted at the end of the semester.

title: Mexico, Central America and Caribbean
description: A broad survey of the physical and human geographic patterns of Middle America (Mexico, Central America, and the Caribbean), past and present, with emphasis on the changing landscape features in the successive patterns of human occupancy and globalization.

title: Geography of South America
description: A survey of the physical and human patterns of South America, past and present, with emphasis on the changing landscape features in the successive patterns of human occupancy and globalization.

title: Geography of East Asia/China
description: An introduction to the human and physical geography of East Asia, with emphasis on China. Examines this region's physical, cultural, and socioeconomic patterns and changes, as well as interactions with other parts of the world.

title: Geography of Landscape and Place
description: This seminar explores cultural landscapes (built environments) and the character of place from a geographic perspective, offering junior, senior, and graduate students insights into geographical patterns and opportunities to develop research interests. Grounded in human and cultural geography, the course fosters interdisciplinary discussions, benefiting students from diverse backgrounds who are encouraged to contribute to the topics presented.

title: Historical Geography of the United States
description: Interpretation and analysis of the American landscape and its regions from c. 1500 to c. 1950, with particular emphasis on landscape as both place and history. Also introduces and examines such current research topics as identity, contested places, landscape-as-text, and cultural politics.

title: Quantitative Analysis in Geography
description: Quantitative methods employed in modern geographical research. Applications of both statistical and mathematical approaches will be treated. Emphasis will be placed on interpretation and evaluation of techniques employed in spatial analysis.

title: Thematic Remote Sensing
description: Introduction to digital image analysis in remote sensing, with an emphasis on extraction of thematic information from imagery. Emphasizes mastery of both practical classification techniques and the theory behind those techniques. Topics covered include preparing imagery for classification; per-pixel and object-based classification techniques; use of parametric, non-parametric, and machine learning\A1-based decision rules; and techniques for validating classification accuracy. Classification techniques for hyperspectral imagery will also be covered.

title: Biophysical Remote Sensing
description: An advanced seminar covering methods and models for retrieving quantitative information about the Earth's surface from remotely sensed data. Topics covered will include inversion techniques for determining surface radiance and reflectance, correction of atmospheric effects, theory of vegetation indices and their use in empirical determination of canopy properties, canopy models and their inversion, and the use of hyperspectral data for retrieval of surface biophysical information.

title: Remote Sensing of Water
description: Reviews and expands the fundamentals of remote sensing theory, basic surface hydrology and hydraulics, remote sensing methods and instruments, measurements of various hydrologic parameters, and integrated approaches in water resource studies.

title: Geographic Field Research Techniques
description: Explores methods and techniques employed in modern field research. Stresses research design, field data acquisition techniques, and data analysis.

title: Topics in Remote Sensing
description: Examination of a selected remote sensing topic in an area of faculty specialization.

title: Internet GIS and Distributed Geographic Information Services
description: Introduces the development and deployment of Internet maps and GIS-related web services. Students will use virtual globes and Internet map servers to learn the techniques of Internet mapping and development/delivery of geodata and geoprocessing services via the Web.  Studio-style classes will focus on building the necessary skills for creating, as well as the practical applications of, customized GIS map applications and ""mashups"", web-based GIS services, and mobile GIS applications.

title: World Population Patterns
description: Geographical processes that govern population distributions, growth rates, and migrations. Emphasis on international comparisons and implications for world society of continued differential growth rates.

title: Geography of Public Lands
description: Overview of public lands systems, including distribution and uses of public lands, with an emphasis on United States federal lands. Historic and recent controversies regarding the public lands will be addressed. Seminar course with discussion and independent research components.

title: Geography of Land Use
description: Critical factors affecting land use, scarcity, and management examined in a regional, national, and global context; land use classification system and variation of land use patterns.

title: Geographies of International Conservation
description: Examines geographic perspectives of global biodiversity loss and conservation landscapes with an emphasis on the political, economic, and cultural dimensions of conservation around the world.

title: Geography of Water Resources
description: Interpretation and analysis of the physical geography of water and water as a resource. Evaluation of water, emphasizing quality, hazards, institutions, and selected domestic and global issues.

title: Programming for Geographic Analysis
description: Introduces programming languages, scripting methods, and/or free and open-source software (FOSS) and their use with geospatial technologies and spatial data. Explanations of programming concepts, demonstrations of techniques, and practical exercises that will enable students to develop their own programs and workflows for geoprocessing, spatial analysis, and map production.

title: Topics in Climatology
description: Examination of a selected climatology topic in an area of faculty specialization.

title: Fluvial Geomorphology
description: Exploration of the forms and processes of streams and rivers with a focus on fluid flow mechanics, sediment transport in moving water, and predicting how rivers can change through time.  Utilizes a combination of lectures, data analysis, and field work.

title: Digital Geomorphology
description: An exploration of geomorphic concepts and 'laws' describing how landscapes and rivers can be used to understand historic and contemporary issues through raster-GIS and the ArcGIS toolbox.

title: Soil Mapping
description: Presents various approaches to soil mapping including the traditional soil-landscape paradigm, digital soil mapping, and a novel computer modeling. Local field sites will serve to reinforce techniques discussed.

title: Urban Geography
description: A study of geographic principles relating to the distribution, function, and structure of cities: a geographic analysis and classification of urban settlements.

title: Human Impact on the Environment
description: Assessment of human impacts on the natural environment. Surveys changing human impacts on and attitudes towards the environment, and details alteration of water systems, the atmosphere, landforms, plants, and animals.

title: Geography of Natural Hazards
description: Examines important emergency management issues related to hazard mitigation, preparedness, disaster response, and recovery, including sociocultural and physical components of disaster process. Assesses human vulnerability and risk to environmental calamities, such as droughts, floods, tornadoes, hurricanes, and earthquakes.

title: Perception of the Environment
description: An examination of the way people perceive their geographic environment and the role of perception in spatial behavior. Perceptions of neighborhoods, cities, states, nations, frontier regions, and environmental processes are explored.

title: Cultural Geography
description: A study of the forms of human occupancy of landscapes, with consideration of innovations in the use of landscape, the origins and the dispersals of these innovations, and human attitudes toward the natural environment.

title: Topics in Geographic Information Science
description: Selected geographic information science topic in science topic in an area of faculty specialization.

title: Graduate Seminar in Geography
description: Attendance of presentations and discussion of research procedures, results and philosophies.

title: Seminar in Qualitative and Mixed Methods Research
description: Qualitative research techniques and integration of qualitative with basic statistical research techniques (mixed methods). Consideration of ethics, data collection, and data analysis.

title: Geocomputation
description: Explores the application of computationally-intensive approaches to the study of complex spatio-temporal problems. Students will learn to simulate, model, analyze, and visualize geographic phenomena that do not lend themselves to other mathematical and statistical methods.

title: Seminar in Rural Population Geography
description: An examination of the population dynamics responsible for regional change in rural areas of the United States. Emphasis is placed on migration systems and changing population composition in Appalachia, Great Plains, rural South, and the Midwest.

title: Geographic Thought
description: History of geographic thought from ancient to modern times, emphasizing major themes and significant individual contributions.

title: Research & Professional Development in Geography
description: Geographic research approaches, design, implementation and reporting, with applications to research proposals and thesis conceptualization and structuring. Programmatic expectations of graduate students and professional development.

title: Advanced Geographies of International Conservation
description: Examines geographic perspectives of global biodiversity loss and conservation landscapes with an emphasis on the political, economic, and cultural dimensions of conservation around the world.

title: Seminar in Rural Geography
description: Exploration of rural geography concepts and research. Examines social, economic, cultural and environmental issues for rural areas and small towns, including natural resources and stresses and concerns related to sustainability of rural livelihoods and communities.

title: Seminar in Sustainability Science, Thought and Approaches
description: Exploration of scientific, social, and philosophical aspects of sustainability, including concepts, approaches, and decision-making. Environmental, economic, and social considerations will be included.

title: Seminar in Rural Economic Development
description: Applications of regional and community development models in rural areas of North America. Emphasis is placed on case studies of locational relationships between regional economic profiles, including changes in structure, and indices of betterment.

title: Political Ecology of Land Cover Change
description: An introduction to political ecology, concentrating on land use and land cover change.

title: Seminar in Advanced Environmental Geography
description: An examination of post-European settlement land use and climate changes and their impact on rural landform stability. Emphasis is on the response of uplands and river systems to land use and climate change and the techniques for documenting historical landform change.

title: Advanced Fluvial Geomorphology
description: Studies the forms and processes in streams and rivers. Topics include mechanics of open channel flow, sediment entrainment and transport, river form in determining bank material, and sediment supply.

title: Advanced Digital Geomorphology
description: A thorough exploration of geomorphic concepts and 'laws', by leveraging the power of raster-GIS and ArcGIS models in combination with modern digital elevation datasets.

title: Seminar in Cultural Geography
description: An examination of the development and distribution of the rural cultural landscapes, focusing particularly on elements of the material landscape. Emphasis placed upon research conducted in the Great Plains.

title: Human Impact on the Environment
description: Assessment of human impacts on and attitudes towards the environment, and details alteration of water systems, the atmosphere, landforms,plants, and animals.

title: Seminar in Medical Geography
description: Emphasizing the pattern of social and environmental conditions on health and disease, this course examines medical care systems and health issues in rural areas of both developed and developing countries.

title: Spatial Data Analysis and Modeling
description: An examination of statistical techniques and models for analyzing spatial data, including global and local measures of spatial association, spatial cluster analysis, spatial autoregressive and geostatistical models, and geographically weighted regression (GWR).

title: Seminar in Advanced Spatial Analysis Techniques
description: Integration of spatial analysis techniques and models with tools such as Geographical Information Systems and Remote Sensing as applied to rural resource systems. Explores strategies for adaptation of various types of spatial models into the GIS framework. Sources of data for analysis will be considered with special emphasis placed on use of remotely sensed data.

title: Topics in Spatial Analysis
description: Independent advanced study of a selected topic. An example is the use of remote sensing and GIS in environmental modeling.

title: Masters Report
description: For students enrolled in geography option B.

title: Masters Thesis
description: For student enrolled in geography option A.

title: Methods, Theory, and Models in Geography
description: Comparative analysis of contemporary methodology of geographic explanation showing their development, current status and future trends. Examination of problems and techniques of design, data collection, analysis, and interpretation which accompany particular research themes.

title: Research Problems in Rural Geography
description: Individual study and research for students admitted to doctoral standing in the graduate school.

title: Earth in Action
description: The earth's physical, structural, and dynamic features; the most common minerals and rocks; processes affecting the earth.

title: Geology Colloquium
description: Topics in earth science chosen to illustrate current research of scientists and methods chosen to study the physical universe. At each offering of this course a syllabus will be available giving the topics to be studied and the details of administration of the course.

title: Geology Laboratory
description: Laboratory investigation of rocks and minerals; use of geologic and topographic maps; understanding of stream and groundwater processes and landforms.

title: Dynamic Earth Lab
description: Covers basic principles and tools of the geosciences and their applications.

title: Oceanography
description: The oceans: their boundaries, contents, and processes.

title: Introductory Geology Honors
description: Survey of earth materials, features, and processes. Higher level of sophistication and challenge than GEOL 100.

title: Environmental Geology
description: Discussion of Earth system materials including minerals, soil, sediment, rocks, water, and atmospheric gases and how they are impacted by human-environmental interactions, including contamination of surface water and groundwater; energy resources and the green energy agenda; plate tectonics, volcanism and accumulation of greenhouse gases as a driver of climate change.

title: Age of Dinosaurs
description: Reconstructs the world of the dinosaurs, and places it in the context of Earth's dynamic physical and biological history. The inferred biology and ecology of dinosaur species will be discussed, and the theories concerning their origin, evolution, and extinction will be presented.

title: Natural Disasters
description: Discussion of geological phenomena such as earthquakes, volcanic eruptions, landslides, and floods, with particular emphasis on their causes, effects, and significance as hazards.

title: Earth Through Time
description: Earth Through Time provides a comprehensive survey of Earth’s 4.5 billion year history. It explores the major events that have shaped the physical and biological development of Earth and how processes like plate tectonics and climate change interact with life, forming an integrated system.

title: Sustainable Earth Resources
description: Formation and development of Earth’s energy, mineral (metallic and non-metallic), and water resources. Additional emphasis will be placed on public and environmental impacts (e.g., climate change, water quality, food resources, etc.) of responsible resource exploitation and whether resource extraction can truly be sustainable.

title: Topics in Geology
description: Seminar discussion of subjects of current interest in geology.

title: Geology of the National Parks
description: Geological survey of the land forms, structure, rocks and hazards  of America’s National Parks. Each park’s geological development through time is explained with special attention placed on how the geologic processes (igneous activity, mountain building and uplift, glaciation, weathering and erosion, wave action, and groundwater) shaped and created the exceptional landscapes in our national parks and the impact of human activities on these unique places that we as a nation have chosen to preserve.

title: Quantitative Methods in Geoscience
description: Design geological data sampling, investigate whether or not two geological datasets are significantly different, develop regression-based equations among various quantities, analyze data in Excel and MATLAB, and handle large geological datasets.

title: Senior Honors Thesis
description: Directed research and preparation of an honors thesis.

title: Independent Study in Geology
description: Independent reading; field or laboratory investigations, or both, of geologic problems.

title: Mineralogy
description: Physical and chemical properties of minerals; crystallography; mineral formation; applications to economic geology and petrology; emphasis on mineral identification through laboratory analysis.

title: Petrology
description: Investigation of the physical and chemical processes forming igneous, metamorphic and sedimentary rocks and their links to tectonic processes that form the Earth's crust and mantle. Develop petrogenetic understanding through macroscopic and mircroscopic examination of rock mineralogy and textures.

title: Oil And Gas Exploration And Evaluation Methods
Geology and Environment
description: Physical and chemical qualities of natural environments and health from a geologic perspective-detection and prediction of environmental changes, identification of sources of pollutants and their movements in soils, rocks, and waters.

title: Geology of Planets
description: Explores recent findings concerning the origin and evolution of planetary bodies in our Solar System, and of exoplanets in orbit around stars other than our Sun.

title: Earth Science
description: A critical study of the atmosphere, weather, climate, composition, and processes of the earth; also, the interaction of these in producing the pattern of landforms and human activity.

title: Geomorphology
description: Classroom, laboratory, and field studies of landforms and surficial deposits, with an emphasis on earth-surface processes. Classroom lectures and discussions; laboratory exercises; and field investigations.

title: Structural Geology
description: Mechanics of the earth’s crust; origin and interrelation of structures of the Earth. Students learn to identify the main structures, to interpret and integrate them in a regional geodynamic context. In addition, students learn to collect field data and to do quantitative analyses using stereographic projection.

title: Geologic Record of Climate Change
description: Introduction to the major forcing and feedback mechanisms responsible for climate change, and climate proxies from sedimentological, biological and geochemical archives. Investigation of key events on the Earth's climate history.

title: Field Methods
description: Introduction to methods used to collect geologic data in the field. Emphasis is placed on map-reading, rock description, use of aerial photographs, and construction of geologic maps and cross sections. Learn how to write a field mapping report and safety protocols for field work.

title: Principles of Paleontology
description: An overview of paleontological principles relating to the application of the fossil record to questions of evolution, paleoecology, paleoenvironment, depositional dynamics, facies analysis, and stratigraphy. Identification of main fossil groups.

title: Senior Thesis
description: Directed research and preparation of a senior thesis.

title: Mineral Exploration for a Sustainable Future
description: Covers basic mineral exploration techniques required for a sustainable future for society. Students will gain experience in different geological, geophysical (induced polarization, magnetic and radiometry surveys), and geochemical techniques employed by the industry, with specific emphasis on critical materials used for the energy transition. Multidisciplinary approaches used to interpret large datasets to generate exploration targets which can be sustainably obtained are taught as well as guest lectures from industry professionals.

title: Introduction to Aqueous Geochemistry
description: Application of chemical principles to geological environments. Emphasis is placed on aqueous systems. Topics include acid-base and redox reactions in natural environments, geochemical kinetics, surface water and groundwater sampling, analytical geochemistry, and data analysis.

title: Hydrogeology
description: Physical and chemical principles of the water cycle (evaporation, condensation, precipitation, runoff, stream flow, percolation, and groundwater flow), as well as environmentally relevant applications based on case studies. Extensive coverage of groundwater hydrology, surface water hydrology, soil moisture, contemporary global issues related to water resources, including pollution control, and also hydrogeochemistry will be discussed.

title: Volcanology
description: Examines processes associated with active volcanoes as revealed by volcanic deposits. Emphasis on influence of volcanoes on human civilizations including economic deposits, climate change, or volcano monitoring.

title: Introduction to Engineering Geology and Petrophysics
description: Students learn basic fundamentals to solve real-world problems in the area of geology and petrophysics. A field trip to an onshore oil rig in Kansas helps consolidating course materials with demands.

title: Sedimentology and Stratigraphy
description: Identification of sedimentary rocks and interpretation of sedimentary properties and depositional environments; analysis of spatial and temporal relationships to interpret depositional history and basin evolution.

title: Geoenergy Exploration
description: Examines geoenergy resources, including conventional and unconventional hydrocarbons, geothermal energy, natural hydrogen, subsurface energy storage, and carbon sequestration. Students apply integrative basin analysis to assess resource potential and examine societal and environmental impacts.

title: Introduction to Geophysics
description: Introduction to geophysics, its uses in studies of the earth's interior, its utility of illuminating subsurface geological features, and its applications in fields such as groundwater studies and the development of energy resources.

title: Field Geophysics
description: Acquisition and computer processing of geophysical data such as those from seismic reflection, seismic refraction, gravity, magnetic and electrical methods.

title: Geomicrobiology
description: An introduction to microbiology and biogeochemical cycling and examination of interactions between microbes and geological environments. Topics include biomineralization, microbial weathering of minerals, and microbial impacts to greenhouse gas levels and water quality.

title: Field Geology
description: Field projects in the Rocky Mountains designed to give students practical experience in apply geologic knowledge and skills.

title: Economic Geology
description: Geology and origin of mineral deposits, with an emphasis on critical minerals, suistainibility, economic significance, and resources necessary for society.

title: Economic Geology Laboratory
description: Laboratory activities related to metallic and nonmetallic mineral deposits, including detailed studies of selected deposits.

title: Carbonate Paleoenvironments
description: Examination of the biological, physical, and chemical factors in reefs and shallow carbonate platform environments through Earth history, including their modern analogs. Attention will be given to the geometry of carbonate facies, their geographic and temporal distribution, and their sedimentary fabric and porosity.

title: Biomineralization
description: Discussion and critique of current and classic research in biomineralization.

title: Sedimentary Geochemistry
description: Geochemical principles and processes in deposition and diagenesis of sediments; different chemical pathways in the exogenic cycle.

title: Water Resources Geochemistry
description: Geochemistry of ground and surface waters; emphasis on mineralogical, biochemical, and hydrologic controls on inorganic and organic interactions in natural waters.

title: Advanced Geochemistry
description: Application of chemical principles to high temperature conditions relevant to igneous and metamorphic systems; emphasis is on integrating and utilizing a variety of scientific techniques (e.g., microscopy, geochemical analysis, modelling, theory) to solve problems and communicate ideas effectively.

title: Hi Tem Geoch
description: Examination of mineral shapes, orientations and assemblages (rock fabric) as a record of geologic processes.

title: Dating Earth Materials
description: Discusses how ages are measured for rocks, minerals, waters, and sediments by analysis of isotopes produced by radioactive decay or cosmic rays.

title: Quaternary Geology
description: Quaternary stratigraphy as the framework for studying the geomorphic, climactic, archaeological, and biological changes of the last two million years, with emphasis on the North American record.

title: Geoenergy Exploration
description: Examines geoenergy resources, including conventional and unconventional hydrocarbons, geothermal energy, natural hydrogen, subsurface energy storage, and carbon sequestration. Students apply integrative basin analysis to assess resource potential and examine societal and environmental impacts.

title: Fossil Fuel Sedimentology
description: Introduces geology and geochemistry of fossil fuels.  Provides basic background information on carbon chemistry and its relationship to fossil fuels such as oil, natural gas and coal, as well as geochemical and petrologic (microscopic) characteristics of sedimentary organic matter and the distribution of fossil fuels through geologic time. Case studies of particular fossil fuel reserves are included.

title: Formation Evaluation
description: Investigates projects on well log interpretation, petro-physical calculations, log corrections, and post-drilling decision workflows through lectures, discussions, laboratory exercises, or field trips.

title: Regional Geology
description: Interpretations of geomorphological, structural, stratigraphic, petrologic (igneous, metamorphic, and  sedimentary), and tectonic history of selected geological regions on Earth. Classroom and field investigation.

title: Seismic Data Processing
description: Principles of 3D seismic data processing techniques and the application of industrial software package(s) to process raw field seismic reflection data to render it in a form suitable for the seismic interpretation stage. This form of seismic data is utilized in subsurface reservoir characterization.

title: Seismic Data Interpretation
description: Fundamental interpretation of 3D seismic reflection data as applied to hydrocarbon exploration and development problems. Students will conduct a project interpreting surface 3D seismic reflection data using industrial software package(s) for subsurface reservoir characterization.

title: Exploration Geophysics
description: Principles and applications of seismic, gravity, magnetic, and other geophysical methods used in energy, mining, and other industries to locate subsurface targets of interest.

title: Intro to Matlab
description: Practical introduction to Matlab designed for students who may have limited experience with data processing. Includes handling data (import/export from other softwares/format), data structure, data processing, data visualization (2D, 3D graphs), designing functions, selection statements, loops, programming, descriptive statistics, plots for exploratory data analysis, fit probability distributions to data, regression analysis.

title: Geologic Evolution of Planet Earth
description: Integration of the various geologic disciplines into a broad-based understanding of the history and dynamics of the earth.

title: Subsurface Methods
description: Principles and applications of subsurface geology.

title: Applied Field Geology
description:
Field projects designed to give students practical experience in applying geologic knowledge and skills. Collecting qualitative and quantitative data in the field, geological mapping (e.g., the Anaconda technique), cross-section construction, data interpretation (using GIS), writing final reports in the field of geology, hydrogeology, mineral exploration, mining, geological hazard, geotechnical engineering.
This field project is designed to give students an applied learning experience and interpretative skills for career training in Mineral Exploration.

title: Problems in Geology
description: Work is offered in mineralogy, paleobiology, paleoecology, stratigraphy, structural geology, igneous, metamorphic, and sedimentary petrology, geomorphology, planetary geology, hydrogeology, geochemistry, and isotope geology.

title: Graduate Seminar in Geology
description: Topics in geology, geochemistry, and geophysics.

title: Advanced Paleobiology
description: Detailed study of the functional morphology, ecology, biogeography, evolution, and classification of selected groups.

title: Mineral Exploration for a Sustainable Future
description: Investigates basic mineral exploration techniques required for a sustainable future for society. Students gain experience in different geological, geophysical (induced polarization, magnetic and radiometry surveys), and geochemical techniques employed by the industry, with specific emphasis on critical materials used for the energy transition. Multidisciplinary approach used to interpret large datasets to generate exploration targets which can be sustainably obtained are taught as well as guest lectures from industry professionals.

title: Igneous and Metamorphic Petrology
description: Selected problems in the petrogenesis of igneous and metamorphic rocks.

title: Advanced Igneous and Metamorphic Petrology
description: Explores the geochemical, petrographic, and petrologic characteristics of the common igneous and metamorphic rocks, building on concepts learned in introductory-level petrology courses.

title: Sedimentary Petrology
description: Petrography, classification, and origin of terrigenous and chemical sedimentary rocks.

title: Advanced Sedimentary Petrology
description: Field and laboratory study of selected problems in the origin of sedimentary rocks.

title: Isotope Geology
description: The principal goal of the course is to understand how variations in radiogenic and stable isotopic abundances can be used to understand Earth processes, including evolution of the solid Earth, the hydrological cycle, the carbon cycle, and climate change.

title: Groundwater Modeling
description: Introduction to conceptual, analytical, and numerical groundwater models. Guides students in developing and applying these approaches to real-world problems. Students will learn to formulate mathematical representations of flow and transport, understand best practices in groundwater modeling, and use industry-standard software such as MODFLOW and MT3D. Students will gain the skills to select appropriate modeling tools, construct models to analyze groundwater processes, and critically evaluate existing studies.

title: Advanced Engineering Geology and Petrophysics
description: Students learn advanced concepts with broad applications in the oil/gas industry, analyze actual experimental data, and use state-of-the-art software in the areas of unconventional reservoirs.

title: Geotectonics
description: Origin and history of major tectonic elements of the earth, especially their interaction through time.

title: Advanced Petroleum Exploration, Imperial Barrel Award Competition
description: Evaluation of exploration prospects in frontier and underdeveloped petroleum provinces using borehole-derived and geophysical data. Team taught course that uses industry provided datasets and current data management and interpretation software to reach drill or no-drill decisions based on science, risk analysis, and economics.

title: Groundwater Contaminant Remediation
description: Identification and mensuration of groundwater contaminants and consideration of means for their remediation strategies.

title: Biogeochemistry of Soils and Sediments
description: Review and discussion of current understanding of the chemical, biological, and mineralogical interactions of groundwater, soils, shallow and deep sediments. This course will deal with biotic controls on chemistry of the environment [and] with the geochemical control of the structure and function of ecosystems.

title: Clay Mineralogy
description: Geologic occurrences, physical properties, atomic structures, and the identification of clay minerals, including thermal analytical methods and the study of X-ray diffraction patterns.

title: Advanced Seismology
description: Mechanics of faulting and earthquakes; seismotectonics; advanced data analysis; seismic wave propagation in three-dimensionally inhomogeneous media; earthquake prediction and hazard analysis.

title: Master's Report Research
description: Research or practice of geology summarized in a scholarly report.

title: Master's Theses Research
description: Research in geology culminating in a master's thesis.

title: Introduction to Gerontology
description: Introductory exploration of diverse biological, psychological, and sociological factors that impact the aging process across the life course.

title: Introduction to Physical Health & Well-Being of Older Adults
description: Introductory examination of processes involved in physiological aging that considers the effects of normal aging, chronic and acute disease, and environmental factors on systems of the body. Focus on health promotion and practical application with diverse populations to prepare students for careers in the field of aging.

title: Introduction to Mental Health & Aging
description: Introductory exploration of mental health and aging utilizing a variety of theoretical perspectives within an overall systems framework. Major emotional, mental, and psychiatric functions and problems encountered in old age will be examined along with evidence-based approaches to assessment, intervention, and treatment.

title: Culture Change in Long-Term Care
description: This course will cover the basic characteristics of traditional and culture change nursing home models. Student will learn about the various processes of organizational change, the steps toward change, and potential regulatory, environmental and social barriers to major change. The course will include discussions about the necessity to change the way care is provided in such areas as dining, bathing, family/relationships, and environment.

title: Measuring Change in Long-Term Care
description: Topics covered will include process and outcome measures in nursing home change as well as the importance of and the utility of measurement for the advancement of quality of life issues in long-term care. Students will gain an understanding of how regulatory support will be more likely to occur if culture change features can show a cost savings as well as improvements in the quality of life of residents.

title: Creating Home in Long-Term Care
description: Students will gain a better understanding of the interdependent networks within nursing homes, environmental factors in the development of community and culture in a nursing home, learn how fostering resident, family and staff interactions can improve quality of life for all those groups, understand the role of structural redesign and innovation, and consider how environmental change can improve relationships within the larger community.

title: Strengthening Staff in Long-Term Care
