description: Topics covered will include the development of deeper relational bonds within the nursing home environment, benefits of permanent staffing as a key component of building a relationship centered culture, how these types of changes can benefit and empower staff, and models for bridging cultural divides between long-term care administrators and staff to benefit the residents. A review of the impact of traditional models of managed care that focus on providing food, shelter, and medical attention and factors that hinder purposeful, proactive interactions between residents, families and staff.

title: Dining in Long-Term Care
description: Students will develop an appreciation of the impact dining has on the quality of social, physical and emotional health and quality of life of nursing home residents, become aware of the meanings/customs that residents may associate with the dining experience, recognize the importance of the dining experience in the overall culture of the home and develop an understanding of various institutional dining styles.

title: Activities in Long-Term Care
description: A review of common activities in long-term care, their importance in resident care, the importance of meaningful activity and relationships, how the long-term care culture impacts resident individuality/choice, and individualized care plans.

title: Introduction to Aging Veterans
description: An introductory overview to the unique issues involved with aging military veterans and their families. Topics include military structure, major United States conflicts, personnel issues resulting from conflicts since WWII, Veteran benefits, cultural issues, veteran health issues and application to working with aging veterans.

title: Aging Well: Lessons from the Blue Zones
description: Cultural and psychological foundations of health, well-being, and longevity. Examination of lifestyle, social organization, and meaning-making practices associated with healthy aging in designated international “Blue Zones.” Emphasis on purpose, social connection, diet, physical activity, and spirituality, and their implications for individual and community well-being. Includes experiential and observational learning through international field study.

title: Introductory Topics in Gerontology
description: Introductory study of selected topics in gerontology.

title: Introduction to Sexuality & Aging
description: Introductory exploration of sexual development and expression across the lifespan with an emphasis on older adults.  Topics include misconceptions about older adult sexuality, physiological changes, social barriers and challenges, sexual diversity, policy and access considerations, and impacts of disease and cognitive decline.

title: Introduction to Creativity & Aging
description: Introductory overview of the transformation of the aging brain that can lead to changes in creative output over time. Students will grow an appreciation for creativity produced and inspired by older people while simultaneously exploring their own creative potential.

title: Introduction to Environments & Aging
description: Introductory interdisciplinary exploration of preferences and needs of older adults' physical environments. Emphasis placed on successful environmental adaptations, design and management of housing, institutional facilities, neighborhoods, and communities.

title: Introduction to Diversity in the Aging Experience
description: Introductory exploration of aging in the contexts of the social determinants of health and diversity, equity, inclusion, and belonging.

title: Introduction to Program Design, Implementation, & Evaluation in Gerontology
description: Introductory study of selecting, adapting, designing, implementing, and evaluating effective prevention and intervention programs for older adults.

title: Introduction to Policy & Advocacy in Gerontology
description: Introductory examination of complex and interrelated policies and programs that impact the well-being of older adults.

title: Introduction to Aging Family Dynamics & Caregiving
description: Introductory exploration of inter-generational, interpersonal, and care-giving relationships during the middle and later years of the family life cycle.

title: Practicum in Gerontology
description: Supervised field experience in an aging-related setting as a practical application of gerontological knowledge and skills.

title: Healthcare Administration
description:
Explore healthcare leadership and management across diverse organizations including hospitals, clinics, insurance companies, government agencies, and specialty facilities. Develop essential skills in strategic planning, organizational management, regulatory compliance, and change implementation. Examine care delivery models and administrative challenges unique to different healthcare sectors.
Students with a secondary major in gerontology (LTC administration emphasis) complete specialized coursework focused on senior living organizations, long-term care regulations, and aging services leadership.

title: Long-Term Care Administration Internship
description: Supervised field experience in an approved long-term care setting designed to meet the requirements for the National Association of Long-Term Care Administrator Board exam. Most students who successfully complete this experience will be eligible to sit for the necessary national and/or state administrator exams.

title: Independent Study in Gerontology
description: Independent study on topics in gerontology.

title: Advanced Physical Health & Well-Being of Older Adults
description: Advanced examination of the processes involved in physiological aging that considers the effects of normal aging, chronic and acute disease, and environmental factors on systems of the body. Focuses on health promotion and practical application with diverse populations to prepare students for careers in the field of aging.

title: Advanced Mental Health & Aging
description: Advanced exploration of mental health and aging utilizing a variety of theoretical perspectives within an overall systems framework. Major emotional, mental, and psychiatric functions and problems encountered in old age are examined along with evidence-based approaches to assessment, intervention, and treatment.

title: Gerontechnology
description: An interdisciplinary approach to the understanding of the biological, environmental, and social spheres where technology and gerontology meet.  Topics include the interrelationship between population dynamics and technological change, technological research and devices that may improve elders' lives, particular issues for rural communities, and the social and cultural meanings, challenges, and benefits of gerontechnologies.  Particular emphasis will be given to placing both population aging and technological change in a broader social perspective.

title: Advanced Death and Dying
description: An in-depth evaluation of the experience of death, dying, loss and bereavement through a multi and cross-cultural lens. Emphasis will be given to strategies for working with older adults and their support systems. These strategies include coping and preparation for end of life-process and advanced care planning. Students will also be encouraged to share their own experiences with death, dying and coping.

title: Aging Veterans
description: Introduction to the unique issues involved with aging military veterans. Topics include basic military structure, media portrayal of war, personnel issues resulting from American conflicts since WWII, veteran benefits, cultural issues, veteran health issues and navigating a career within the U.S. federal system.

title: Design for Aging in the Modern World
description: This course will explore the dynamic process of aging and the role of the built environment in the, multidimensional as well as multi-spheral spaces of life-course occurring in the family, work, education, leisure as well as other domains and its relationship to the environment from a life-course perspective. The emphasis on aging and the environment will focus on how individuals and groups manage interactions associated with the social and physical settings. The course will develop students' foundational knowledge in the theories of aging, environment and behavior, and the ecology of the aging process. These theories will be used as the basis to discuss and debate contemporary issues that confront an aging society. Three levels of the environment will be explored: the microenvironment including those products that people of all ages interact with on a daily basis; the meso (or middle) environment that includes residential and work settings that shape our relationships with family, friends, and associates, and lastly at the macro environment of our communities where people are connected to the larger social settings, services and supports that make up our cities and rural settings, both from a national and international perspective. Each of the sections covered will acknowledge how behavior is influenced by the environment and spans the life-cycle.

title: Advanced Topics in Gerontology
description: Advanced study of selected topics in gerontology.

title: Adv Sex & Aging
description: Advanced exploration of sexual development and expression across the lifespan with an emphasis on older adults.  Topics include misconceptions about older adult sexuality, physiological changes, social barriers and challenges, sexual diversity, policy and access considerations, and the impacts of disease and cognitive decline.

title: Advanced Creativity & Aging
description: Advanced overview of transformation of the aging brain that can lead to changes in creative output over time. Students will grow an appreciation for creativity produced and inspired by older people while simultaneously exploring their own creative potential.

title: Adult Development & Aging
description: Exploration of the biological, psychological, and sociological aspects of individual and interpersonal development in aging from a life course perspective.

title: Physical Health and Nutrition in Aging
description: This course identifies the basic physiologic changes during aging and their impacts in health and disease.  The focus will be on successful aging with special emphasis on physical activity and nutrition.  Practical application to community settings is addressed.

title: Advanced Environments & Aging
description: Advanced interdisciplinary exploration of preferences and needs of older adults' physical environments. Emphasis placed on successful environmental adaptations, design and management of housing, institutional facilities, neighborhoods, and communities.

title: Advanced Diversity in the Aging Experience
description: An advanced exploration of aging in the contexts of the social determinants of health and diversity, equity, inclusion, and belonging.

title: Advanced Program Design, Implementation, & Evaluation in Gerontology
description: Advanced study of selecting, adapting, designing, implementing, and evaluating effective prevention and intervention programs for older adults.

title: Advanced Policy & Advocacy in Gerontology
description: Advanced examination of complex and interrelated policies and programs that directly and indirectly impact the well-being of older adults.

title: Advanced Aging Family Dynamics & Caregiving
description: Advanced exploration of intergenerational, interpersonal, and caregiving relationships during the middle and later years of the family life cycle.

title: Professional Seminar In Gerontology
description: Students apply and integrate knowledge gained in earlier courses and strengthen skills in ethical decisions-making through applications in gerontology-related areas such as advocacy, professionalism, family and workplace issues. Students from a variety of professions bring their unique perspective to bear on topics of common interest.

title: Professional Application in Gerontology
description: This course is designed for students near the end of the master’s program in Gerontology. Students will integrate interdisciplinary knowledge from their core gerontology studies with expertise from their primary discipline or interest areas to develop professional competencies for real-world application. Through flexible assignments, scholarly research, and reflective practice, students will demonstrate their ability to apply gerontological principles to address contemporary challenges in aging and prepare for their chosen career paths

title: Methods of Research in Home Economics
Intro to Pharmacology of Farm Animals
Graduate Education Abroad
description: ""Education abroad"" is a chance to pursue your college studies in a foreign country . Students attend lectures or carry out research at a foreign university or through K-State's education-abroad program.

title: Water and Society:  Interdisciplinary Foundation
description: Interdisciplinary course focused on the natural and human elements of water resource systems; introduction to traditional disciplinary procedures, theory, and models; cross-disciplinary approaches to understand and quantify dynamic system relationships; case study for integrated problem solving, management and policy.

title: Leadership Practicum
description: Develops the connections between leadership theory and practice. By conducting a practicum project, students demonstrate the ability to apply concepts and ideas from the study of leadership to a practical leadership problem within an organization.

title: Foundations of Homeland Security
description: This introductory course provides the foundational framework for the Homeland Security program and surveys the major policies, practices, concepts, and challenges confronting practitioners in Homeland Security. Topics include an overview of threats to homeland security and an introduction to the roles, functions, and policies of organizations and government structures at the federal, tribal, state, and local levels. The National Response Framework (NRF) and National Incident Management System (NIMS) are studied.

title: Homeland Security Threats
description: Students will understand the various types of disasters, methodologies of disaster recovery, and roles and responsibilities of Federal, State, and local government. It will address the impact of disasters, including acts of terrorism, to include economic, physical, emotional, and psychological effects. Students will understand how disasters affect society, risk mitigation strategies, and how the levels of government coordinate to address disaster impacts.

title: Organizations Amid Crisis
description: The fundamental concepts and subject areas necessary for an organization to address in preparing for catastrophic emergency events and other events encountered by organizations as a result of homeland security are examined. It outlines the organization and practical steps required to develop an effective crisis response plan. The relationships between private and public service organizations, including governmental, nonprofit, and hybrid types are studied.

title: Homeland Security Process and Management
description: Students will understand strategic, political, legal, and organizational challenges associated with the defense of the U.S. homeland, efforts that are under way to meet these challenges, and possible policy options. Intergovernmental responsibilities and relationships of local, state, and federal agencies in an ""all-hazards"" approach to disasters are emphasized. Major policy and regulatory issues of emergency management, including the role of the military in response to disaster operations, are examined.

title: Classical Greek I
description: An introduction to the grammar, vocabulary, and structure of Attic Greek.

title: Classical Greek II
description: Develops reading and comprehension skills in the language of classical Athens, with a focus on further developing knowledge of grammar, vocabulary, and reading. Critical reading of texts in the original Greek.

title: Intermediate Greek-Prose
description: The reading of classical Greek prose at an intermediate level.

title: Intermediate Greek-Poetry
description: Intermediate readings in classical Greek poetry. Renewed focus on Greek grammar, style, and vocabulary. Introduces a new dialect of Greek. Critical reading of texts.

title: Orientation For Summer School Program in Germany
German I
description: Develops basic language skills and cultural awareness to function in everyday situations in German-speaking communities, for students with little to no prior German experience. Conducted in German with practice in speaking, listening, reading, and writing.

title: German II
description: Continuation to the introduction of modern German. Practice of the spoken language, additional experience in reading and with a variety of additional media.

title: German III
description: Conclusion of the introduction to modern German. Continued practice of the spoken language, reading, and additional experience with a range of audio and visual media.

title: Elementary German Conversation 3a
description: Practice in beginning conversational German and review of grammar.

title: German IV
description: Review of select points of German language structure accompanied by practice in conversation, speaking, reading, and writing, as well as additional experience with audio and visual media.

title: Intermediate Studies in German
description: Offered only to participants in study abroad programs.

title: K-State in Berlin
description: Ten-day, faculty-led study abroad program in Berlin and Potsdam. Focuses on German cultural memory through Berlin's architectural topography. Visits to, and discussions of, historical sites and buildings. Prior to departure, students attend meetings on the K-State campus and complete assignments to prepare for travel.

title: German Literature in Translation
description: Selected readings in English from such major German authors as Mann, Brecht, Hesse, Grass, and Kafka.

title: German Film
description: In English. Addresses various topics within a German historical-cultural context. Students will consider what makes cinema a distinct form of artistic expression.

title: Topics in German Cultural Studies
description: In English. Specific course content will vary by semester and instructor.

title: Readings in German
description: Practice in reading a variety of literary, journalistic, and specialized texts.

title: Intro Topics In German Literature & Culture 18th/19th Cent
description: Topics courses constitute a sequence of introductory period studies covering the chronological range of German literature from about 1750 to the present. Within these historical periods, the specific course contents will vary by semester and instructor.

title: Introductory Topic in Grmn Literature & Cult 20th/21th Cent
description: Topics courses constitute a sequence of introductory period studies covering the chronological range of German literature from about 1750 to the present. Within these historical periods, the specific course contents will vary by semester and instructor.

title: German Composition
description: Course emphasizes writing and includes select grammar review.

title: German For Reading Knowledge I
description: The grammar and syntax of German and the reading of basic material selected from modern German texts. (This course is designed for students who need to be able to read German.)

title: German For Reading Knowledge II
description: Continued reading of material from modern German texts. (This course is designed for students who need to be able to read German.).

title: Business German
description: Advanced grammar necessary for adequate oral and written expression in international business and diplomatic situations, including specialized terminology, conversation and discussion and translation. Cultural aspects of business are also considered.

title: Advanced German Conversation
description: Course emphasizes proficiency in oral expression and includes select grammar review. Course not open to students whose competency has been demonstrated in the language at this level.

title: Introductory Topics in German Language and Linguistics
description: Introduction to topics in German Linguistics, with special emphasis on the connection between language structure and use or language structures and learning.

title: German Cultural Studies and History
description: This course addresses cultural and historical topics not confined to a single period. Specific course content will vary by semester and instructor. It may emphasize cross-national subjects, the development of a theme or discourse over time, new perspectives from social, intellectual, or cultural studies, or non-traditional texts and topics.

title: Topics in Eighteenth Century German Literature and Culture
description: Topics courses are designed for undergraduates and graduate students. These courses constitute a sequence of period studies covering the chronological ranges of German speaking literature and culture. Within these historical periods, the specific course contents will vary by semester and instructor. They may emphasize literary figures and movements, historical and cultural contexts, or different genres and forms within the period.

title: Topics in Nineteenth Century German Literature and Culture
description: Topics courses are designed for undergraduates and graduate students. These courses constitute a sequence of period studies covering the chronological ranges of German speaking literature and culture. Within these historical periods, the specific course contents will vary by semester and instructor. They may emphasize literary figures and movements, historical and cultural contexts, or different genres and forms within the period.

title: Topics in Early Twentieth Century German Literature & Cult
description: Topics courses are designed for undergraduates and graduate students. These courses constitute a sequence of period studies covering the chronological ranges of German speaking literature and culture. Within these historical periods, the specific course contents will vary by semester and instructor. They may emphasize literary figures and movements, historical and cultural contexts, or different genres and forms within the period.

title: Top/German Literature and Culture since 1945
description: Topics courses are designed for undergraduates and graduate students. These courses constitute a sequence of period studies covering the chronological ranges of German speaking literature and culture. Within these historical periods, the specific course contents will vary by semester and instructor. They may emphasize literary figures and movements, historical and cultural contexts, or different genres and forms within the period.

title: Topics in 20th & 21th Century German Lit & Culture
description: Topics courses are designed for undergraduates and graduate students. These courses constitute a sequence of period studies covering the chronological ranges of German speaking literature and culture. Within these historical periods, the specific course contents will vary by semester and instructor. They may emphasize literary figures and movements, historical and cultural contexts, or different genres and forms within the period.

title: Open Topics Seminar in German Language and Linguistics
description: Addresses topics in German Linguistics. It may emphasize a specific area of linguistics, a specific research approach, or the connection between language and society.

title: German Prose And Drama Of The Nineteenth Century
History of the German Language
description: A study of the development of the sounds, forms and syntax of standard German.

title: Open Topics Seminar in German Literature and Culture
description: Open Topics courses are designed for undergraduates and graduate students. Course addresses topics not confined to a single period in a national literature. Specific course content will vary by semester and instructor. It my emphasize cross-national subjects, literary or cultural criticism or theory, works of one or a pair of related authors, the development of a theme or genre over time, new perspectives from social, intellectual, or cultural studies, or non-traditional texts and topics.

title: Advanced Spoken and Written German
description: Course combines practice in speaking, writing, and reading, and select advanced grammar review.

title: The Enlightenment And Storm And Stress
Literature Of The German Democratic Republic
German Culture and Literature in Second-Language Learning
description: Analysis and interpretation of cultural and literary text from German-speaking countries, with emphasis on the development of interpretive skills and materials, and their application to the German curriculum at all levels.

title: German Culture In Second Language Learning
Master's Report
description: A written report of either research or problem work on a topic in the major field.

title: Fundamentals of Grain Processing
description: This is an introductory course to grains and their processing/utilization in food and non-food products.

title: Feed and Pet Food Experience
description: Designed to provide a hands-on experience in the feed and pet food processes. The student will learn about the safety programs, quality and standard operating procedures used in the animal feed and pet food industries.

title: Principles of Milling
description: Introduction to grain and feed milling processes.

title: Principles of Milling Laboratory
description: A laboratory course to accompany Principles of Milling (GRSC 150). Laboratory exercises in grain and feed milling processes.

title: Topics in Grain Science
description: Special topics, studies, and activities in grain science not completely taught in other courses.

title: Fundamental Baking Calculations
description: The course is designed to provide students with an understanding of the type of mathematical calculations used in the baking industry. It will cover ratio and proportion, true percent, baker's percent, how to convert a straight dough to a sponge and dough, proper water temperature calculations, specific gravity as it applies to cake making, density, specific volume as it applies to bread making, flour blending calculations in order to achieve a specific flour protein content, flour calculations for proper baking performance and purchasing, major ingredient substitutions, pie filling calculations, parts per million calculations, and baking powder neutralization values.

title: CAD Flow Sheets for Grain Processes
description: The course is designed to teach students the basic concepts of professional 2D drawing designs and drafting using AutoCAD. By the end of the semester, the students will be able to use AutoCAD for preparing process flow drawings and interpret a grain processing flow.

title: Baking Fundamentals: The Art and Science of Baking
description: This introductory course explores the art and science of baking. Students will discover the basic scientific principles that explain how both yeast and chemically leavened bakery products are made. They will learn the products, tools, techniques and aesthetics of making breads, cakes, sweet goods, doughnuts, and cookies.

title: Materials Handling
description: The course is designed to introduce students to how materials including grains, ingredients, mixes and finished products are physically moved and handled in the receiving, storage, formulation, production and shipping of grain related products.

title: Introduction to Feed and Pet Food Processing
description: Introduction to animal feed and pet food manufacturing, including principles of conveying, grinding, mixing, pelleting, and other processing techniques, the formulation of concentrates, premixes, and rations.

title: Intro to Feed and Pet Food Processing Lab
description: This course provides students with hands-on experience in the fundamental processes used in feed and pet food manufacturing. Through laboratory exercises, students will explore key operations such as ingredient preparation, grinding, mixing, pelleting, canning, baking, freeze drying, extrusion, and packaging. Emphasis is placed on understanding equipment operation, safety protocols, and troubleshooting in a production setting. This applied learning approach reinforces theoretical knowledge from lecture-based courses and prepares students with the technical skills necessary for careers in the feed and pet food industry.

title: Grain Analysis Techniques
description: Principles and instrumentation available for testing cereal and other grains and their food and feed products.

title: Faculty-Led Study Abroad
description: Seminar and travel course designed to prepare students before the experience and for students to analyze, critique, and report their experiences of an international experience associated with study tours or short courses.

title: Undergraduate Research in Grain Science
description: This course is an opportunity to earn college credit while conducting an independent research project. The overall objective of the course is to introduce students to scientific research methods and procedures in grain science and related areas through hands-on experience and individual mentoring.  Students will learn how to design, conduct, analyze, and present scientific research.

title: Milling Science I
description: Principles and practices of wheat flour milling with full-scale equipment including grain storage, blending, cleaning, conditioning plant, and a modern pneumatic flour mill, with instrumentation and automation, etc.

title: Milling Science I Laboratory
description: A laboratory course to accompany Milling Science I (GRSC 500). Laboratory exercises in wheat flour milling with full-scale equipment including grain storage, blending, cleaning, conditioning plant, and a modern pneumatic flour mill, with instrumentation and automation.

title: Milling Science II
description: Advanced study of the entire gradual reduction system of wheat flour milling and the many unit process systems that constitute the milling system. The theory and practice of mill control are studied in detail. Processing of other cereal grains and oil seeds are covered as well as general mill management.

title: Milling Science II Laboratory
description: The processes for milling other grains such as corn, oats, sorghum, different classes of wheat, and rye are studied in the laboratory and by practice on small scale milling units. Concepts of material handling properties of grain products and material handling equipment in dry milling operations will be presented. Concepts of statistical process control and spread sheet modeling in the decision making process will be introduced.

title: Extrusion Processing In The Food And Feed Industries
Process Calculations in Food Systems
description: Application of grain/food industry analytical processing concepts including mass and energy balances, fluid flow, heat transfer, dehydration, evaporation.

title: Process Calculations in Food Systems Laboratory
description: Virtual laboratory experiments supplementing GRSC 540-Process Calculations in Food Systems. Simulations will be used to conduct wide range of virtual experiments representing major food processes.

title: Grain Drying, Storage, Aeration and Pest Management
description: Introduction to the scientific principles and engineering practices required for the successful management of post-harvest quality grains.

title: Cereal Food Plant Design
description: This course deals with principles of modern flour mill design, feasibility and equipment selection for plant improvements and new plant construction. It covers the steps of developing adequate process flow diagrams based on the specific production needs, constraints and preferences. Emphasis is placed on the effects of design on plant operating efficiency, finished product quality, and construction costs.

title: Pneumatic Conveying of Dry Solids
description: The course is designed to introduce students to a comprehensive knowledge of the design, operation and capabilities of pneumatic conveying systems for transportation of dry, powdered and granular materials. The student will study the components of pneumatic conveying systems, the requirements for designing efficient and reliable pneumatic conveying systems, and troubleshooting existing systems.

title: Electricity and Industrial Power Distribution
description: Major emphasis will be given to application of electricity to machinery for grain processing and electrical code.

title: Milling of Specialty Grains
description: This is a problem based learning course which aims to increase students' knowledge of flour milling and specialty grain milling. During the semester students develop critical thinking and problem-solving skills needed to troubleshoot real-life issues related to processing, quality assurance, HSE (Health, Safety, and Environment), and finished product quality. Students are expected to analyze their problems and develop the skills needed to effectively communicate their findings during group presentations.

title: Grain Science Problems
description: Study in an area pertinent to grain processing and utilization.

title: Internship in Grain Science
description: A practical application of feed and food manufacturing technology during an eight-week summer internship with an active commercial feed and food manufacturing company. The course will stress applied aspects of commercial feed and food manufacturing, which can include, but not be limited to, plant operations, maintenance, personnel and labor relations, business management, warehousing, ingredient procurement, quality assurance, and fleet management.

title: Extended Internship in Grain Science
description: In-depth work-study experiences for up to 6 months on commercial feed and food manufacturing, which can include, but not limited to, plant operations, maintenance, personnel and labor relations, business management, warehousing, ingredient procurement, quality assurance, and fleet management.

title: Practicum in Bakery Technology I
description: Lectures and hands-on laboratory experience with commercial production scale baking equipment for breads and rolls, cookies and crackers, and cakes and sweet doughs.

title: Practicum in Bakery Technology II
description: Lectures and hands-on laboratory experience with commercial production scale baking equipment for breads and rolls, cookies and crackers, and cakes and sweet doughs.

title: Cereal Science
description: The characteristics of cereals, legumes, their components and their processing to foods.

title: Advanced Feed Technology
description: Advanced study of engineering principles applicable to flour and feed plant operations, equipment selection, and processing systems.

title: Animal Food Safety
description: An introduction to the animal food safety regulations in the Food Safety Modernization Act.

title: Flour and Dough Testing
description: The focus of Flour and Dough testing is the analytical tests used to determine attributes of different flours. This course will provide a broad understanding of standard analytical methods and procedures commonly used in flour quality testing. This course will discuss how analyses are done as well as how to evaluate, interpret, and use the results.

title: Baking Science I
description: Introduction to chemical and physical properties of flour and other principal ingredients used in production of yeast-leavened bakery foods. Study of major processing methods for making yeasted doughs such as breads, sweet goods, frozen dough, and partially baked goods. Examination of ingredient specifications role of quality control, keeping properties of bread products, and nutritional attributes of various types of breads.

title: Baking Science I Laboratory
description: A laboratory course to accompany Baking Science I (GRSC 635).  Laboratory exercises in theory and production of yeast-leavened baked products.

title: Baking Science II
description: The science involved in baking processes of chemically-leavened products. The course will discuss the function of ingredients used in chemically-leavened products, in contrast to functionality in yeast-leavened products. Students also will be introduced to the formulation, processing and equipment used in a variety of products including batter cakes, foam cakes, quick breads, muffins, cake doughnuts, and cookies. Processing is discussed in light of the physical-chemical transformations for each product category.

title: Pet Food Processing
description: The course is designed to provide students with an understanding of pet foods, including food products meant for dogs, cats, pet rodents, pet snakes, ornamental fish, exotic and captive wild animals, and the various processing technologies that are deployed for their production. Extrusion, baking, canning, and injection molding technologies will be covered. Other important aspects of pet food production, such as ingredient handling and compounding, quality control, HACCP and sanitation, and packaging will also be discussed.

title: Concepts of Modern Feed Mill Design
Food and Feed Product Protection
description: Sanitation in relation to processing, handling, and storage of human and animal foods. Emphasis on contaminants, control of causative agents, equipment and plant design, applicable laws and regulations.

title: Feed Mill Plant Design
description: This course deals with principles of modern design, feasibility, and equipment selection for new feed mills. Emphasis is placed on the effects of logistics, ingredient availability, operating efficiency, and finished product quality.

title: Qualities of Feed Ingredients
description: The course provides an integrated overview of the physical, biological, chemical characteristics of common feed ingredients. Quality control and evaluation of ingredients and complete feed is emphasized to ensure the productions of safe feed/safe food.

title: Microscopy Of Feed And Food Ingredients
Bakery Layout and Management
description: Provides the practical knowledge and skills necessary to successfully lead and manage a bakery operation. Current problems in the baking industry are discussed to improve production efficiency, performance, capability, profitability, and quality in the context of a bakery operation. This course utilizes team projects to plan and design a bakery with consideration of market demands, product selection, and feasibility.

title: Grains And Legumes In Human Nutrition
Vibrational Spectroscopic Analysis and Chemometrics
description: Infrared and particularly modern near-infrared spectroscopic analysis of foods, natural products, and synthetic substances is accomplished with direct sampling and the use of multivariate statistics. This course is intended to enable the student to understand the principles and successfully apply this technology to practical analytical problems with emphasis upon food. Method development will be taught using specific analytes in selected products. Theoretical background, working of modern instrumentation and associated software is presented in support of achieving practical competence.

title: Contemporary Chromatographic Analysis Oo Food
description: High performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) is the primary focus of this course. This will be supported by including treatment of topics in contemporary gas chromatography and supercritical fluid chromatography and extraction. Optimizing chromatographic conditions through knowledge of the column chemistry will be covered in addition to detector options, instrumentation, and sample preparation.

title: Fundamentals Of Processing Grains For Food
Biomaterials Processing
description: This course is designed for students who are interested in plant biomaterials processing. This course will teach technologies of biobased materials processing including starch extraction (wet milling), plant oil extraction and refining, plant protein extraction and processing, cellulose processing, biofuel production, chemicals bioconversion, and drying technologies of biomaterials.

title: Fundamentals of Bioprocessing
description: This course is designed for students who desire a clear understanding of bioprocessing principles as applied to the emerging bio-based industry. This course covers the fundamentals of mass and energy balances, fluid dynamics, heat and mass transfer, as applied to bioprocessing. The microbial growth, kinetics and fermenter operation will be covered in detail. Fundamentals of downstream operation as applicable to bioprocessing will be covered in this course. Industrial bioprocessing case studies that involve the integration of the course contents will be discussed.

title: Pet Food Formulation for Commercial Production
description: Students completing this course will become proficient at formulating pet foods and have a working knowledge of ingredients, processes, and the software necessary to create new products, revise existing formulas, and perform business and production analyses.

title: Grain Science Problems
description: Graduate study in an area pertinent to grain processing and utilization.

title: Grains And Legumes In Human Nutrition
Fundamentals of Processing Grains For Food
description: Unit processes in the receiving and storing of grains; grinding, sifting, mixing, separation, conveying, cooling, drying, etc. This course is not open to undergraduate majors in the department.

title: Advanced Extrusion Processing
description: Advanced study of the engineering principles of extrusion forming and cooking with a detailed focus on scale-up. A variety of extrusion systems will be studied in lab with associated group projects.

title: Novel Uses of Biopolymers
description: Teach existing value-added technology of cereal polymers, and explore new processes and utilization in non-traditional food/feed and in non-food/feed.

title: Physical Properties of Cereal Polymers
description: Physical properties of cereal polymers include physical attributes, rheological, mechanical, thermal, electrical, and optical properties. Thermal analysis methods will be taught. Thermal analyses include glass transition, gelatinization, denaturation, and thermal softening and settings of cereal polymers. Rheological properties associated with these thermal transitions will be discussed. Applications of thermal analysis in cereal chemistry, processing, and product quality control will be discussed.

title: Advanced Grain Processing Technology
description: An overview of unit operations used in the grain/food processing industry will be given. Specific applications of the unit operations will be covered in detail for dry and wet milling (wheat/corn/sorghum), masa manufacturing, oilseed processing, rice milling, and packaging systems. A term project, which will include an applied research component will be developed by each student.

title: Grain Protein Chemistry & Technology
description: This graduate course will address the structure, chemistry, properties, and applications of proteins. The primary focus will be on plant food proteins (cereals, pulses, and oilseeds). Animal (milk, egg, meat) and microorganism-derived (mycoproteins, algae) proteins will also be moderately covered to provide students with a comprehensive understanding of the subject.

title: Advanced Processing Series:  Breakfast Cereal Technology
Advanced Processing Series: Pasta And Noodle Technology
Study Abroad Experience in Grain Science
description: Travel seminar coruse designed to prepare graduate students before their international study abroad experience and analyze, critique, and report about their faculty-led study tours or short courses. The academic topics will vary with each international activity.

title: Professional Development in Grain Science
description: A team-taught course that provides students with skills and practices needed to succeed in graduate school and future academic or professional careers.

title: Starch Chemistry and Technology
description: Chemical and physical properties of cereal and legume starches. Isolation, structure, assay methods, and properties. Methods of modifying starches for industrial use, including chemical, physical, and enzymic modification.

title: Carbohydrates in Food
description: Structure and properties of food carbohydrates, including sugars, oligosaccharides, and polysaccharides, and methods of their modification and analysis.

title: Enzyme and Bioprocessing Applications
description: The course explores the applications of enzymes and microbial cultures in the bioprocessing industry, including food, feed, industrial chemicals and biofuels; and major microbial pathways for product synthesis including a clear understanding of enzyme and microbial kinetics at an advanced level.

title: Topics in Grain Science
description: Discussions and lectures on important areas and contributions in the field of grain science not currently covered in present courses.

title: Advanced Cereal Chemistry
description: The chemistry of cereal components at the molecular level. The role and interactions of the various constituents, their functionality in producing an end product, and their influence on nutritional properties.

title: Grain Science Graduate Seminar
description: Weekly evaluation of the scientific literature and the reasoning underlying the selection of research problems, the formulation and testing of hypotheses, and the evaluation and presentation of results. Discussion of research and technical problems in the discipline.

title: Introduction to Gender, Women, and Sexuality Studies
description: An interdisciplinary analysis of personal experiences and social and political institutions as they shape and are shaped by the intersections of gender, race, class, sex, sexuality, ethnicity, nation, citizenship, and ability on local, national, and global levels. Topics include the roles of biology and social construction in shaping gender and sexuality, sources of structural inequities; and feminist, queer, trans, anti-racist, and anti-colonial activisms.

title: Climate Justice Movements
description: Explores climate justice movements and concepts in the US and globally. Particular attention is placed on the ways that gender, race, class, and global location intersect and how different social actors (individuals, activist groups, governments, nations) define climate change and how climate justice activist movements fight for change. Through discussion and critical analysis, will explore a variety of topics and concepts including global climate change, environmental and climate justice, growth and degrowth, colonization and decolonization, and human and nature rights among others. By examining these, we can come to understand how climate justice is a political position, series of movements, and hope for the future.

title: Intersectionality and Health Justice
description: Examines intersections of gender, race, class, Indigeneity, nation, and sexuality in critiques, activisms, and histories of medicine, public health, and health sciences. Joins thinking in racial, environmental, disability, and reproductive justices with queer and trans studies in exploring questions and ethics of healthcare.

title: Activism and Social Justice
description: Examines ethical dilemmas surrounding activism, what constitutes social justice, and civil rights justice processes. Centers Indigenous Studies, Queer Studies, Feminist Studies, American Studies, and Social Transformation Studies perspectives on ethics, activism, and justice.

title: Gender, Race, and Sex in Science Fiction
description: Explores the ways that gender, sex, sexuality, race, and class intersect in science fiction, including Afrofuturism, Asian futurism, Chicanafuturism, and Indigenous futurism. Examines how the ways that we imagine the future and the alien offer insight into contemporary concerns about gender, sexuality, race and racism, colonialism, imperialism, and justice.

title: Selected Studies of Gender, Women, and Sexuality Studies
description: Specific course content will vary by semester and instructor.

title: Engaging Gender, Women, and Sexuality Studies
description: An examination of the origins and current status of the Gender, Women, and Sexuality Studies field, provides core concepts and research methodologies.

title: Queer Studies/Concepts, History, and Politics
description: Examines the origins of Queer Studies and introduces core concepts, foundational histories, and critical frameworks that will prepare students for further study.

title: Transgender Studies
description: Explores the history of and current debates in the field of transgender studies. Examines the roles of race, class, nation, colonialism, and (dis)ability in understanding transgender identities and politics.

title: Women & Aging: Looking at Multicultural Female Aging
Gender in American Film
description: This course examines depictions of gender, race/ethnicity, class, and sexuality in American film. It focuses on the way in which images in mainstream Hollywood films reinforce, normalize and/or question power, power relations, and stereotypes.

title: Reproductive Rights and Wrongs
description: Explores contemporary reproductive politics within the historical context of struggles for reproductive justice in the U.S.

title: Disability Justice and Gender
description: Introduces a disability justice framework and explores how ableism, as a social system, intersects with other systems of inequality. Examines disability from intersectional perspectives that center feminist, queer, and trans analyses of disability, particularly those of U.S. people of color. Explores how disability has been constructed, regulated, and disciplined by structures and institutions as well as met with resistance by individuals and social movements.

title: Global Struggles for Feminist, Queer, and Trans Justice
description: Explores how justice struggles are connected. Examines how colonialism and transnational globalization intersect with gender, sexuality, class, and nation, as well as the effects of colonization, migration, democratization, and capitalist and socialist development strategies on women and queer people in ""Third

title: Resistance and Movements for Social Change
description: Examines Women's Resistance and movements against gender violence and discrimination in the context of colonialism, globalization, war, militarism, and occupation.

title: Feminist Thought
description: Survey of a variety of feminist analyses of society, culture, and work, as well as visions for social change. The historical development of key feminist theories, contemporary debates, and multicultural and global feminism will be analyzed.

title: Race/Sex/Science
description: Examines the ways that race, gender, sex, sexuality, nation, ability, colonialism, and class shape and are shaped by science and technology.  Focus is on U.S,

title: The Stories of a Young Girl
description: An interdisciplinary examination of female adolescence, focusing in particular on the way it is depicted in literature.

title: Coming Out and Sexual Identity
description: Examines how narratives of coming out and the organization of National Coming Out Day impact personal freedom, policies and attitudes toward sexual orientation.

title: Seminar in Gender, Environment & Justice
description: Examines the philosophical, historical, and material aspects of the human/nature relationships and the intersection of gender, race/ethnicity, class and global location with environment and environmental justice.

title: Topics in Gender, Women, and Sexuality Studies
Independent Study in Gender, Women, and Sexuality Studies
description: Independent, supervised study of a topic related to Queer Studies or Gender, Women, and Sexuality Studies.

title: Research Methods in Gender, Women, and Sexuality Studies
description: An advanced course in practices of research in Gender, Women, and Sexuality Studies, with attention to what distinguishes interdisciplinary, feminist research from traditional, disciplinary practices. Methods explored may include survey, interview, oral history, ethnography, hermeneutics, content analysis, case study, experimental, and action research.

title: Gender and Popular Culture
description: Using an intersectional lens, this course explores gender, race, class, global location, and sexuality in the production, portrayals, and consumption of popular culture. The popular culture forms explored in this course may include fiction, film, television, music, magazines, advertising, and/or material culture.

title: The History and Politics of Family Violence
Intersections of Violence and Resistance
description: An examination of violence, and anti-violence justice organizing as the product of race, capitalism, gender, sexuality, nation, empire, and settler colonialism. Centers violence against women, gender violence, and the violence of the normative.

title: Gender and Food Justice
description: Uses the feminist concept of intersectionality to explore the ways that gender intersects with race, class, and global location in food justice and food sovereignty movements. Focusing on works by and about U.S. people of color, students will explore a variety of topics and concepts including systemic bias in food deserts and local food, decolonizing food, hunger and malnutrition, and human rights. Examination of these narratives enables students to understand how food and food systems are both a means of dominant control and powerful sites of gender resistance.

title: Social Change Field-Experience
description: Applied, and interdisciplinary exploration of social change in community, national, and international groups and organizations. Includes field experience, professional-related training.

title: Capstone Seminar in Gender, Women, and Sexuality Studies
description: An advanced seminar for in-depth investigation of a specific topic. Students will conduct independent research and produce a substantial project or paper.

title: Advanced Topics in Gender, Women and Sexuality Studies
description: Provides an in-depth theoretical and empirical analysis of scholarly works relating to an interdisciplinary topic in gender, women, and sexuality studies.

title: Internship in Gender, Women and Sexuality Studies
description: Supervised field work at the local, state, national, or international level in a community, volunteer, activist, or political organization.

title: Independent Study for Graduate Students or Advanced Undergraduate Students
description: Independent study in problems, issues, topics in Gender, Women, and Sexuality Studies. Topics vary.

title: Key Concepts in Gender, Women, and Sexuality Studies
description: Introduction to core theoretical concepts, research methods, and methodologies in Gender, Women, and Sexuality Studies. Provides feminist, queer, trans, women’s, and race and ethnic studies foundations and frameworks for graduate level inquiry.

title: Careers in Human Development & Family Science
description: Focuses on career development and related activities for Human Development & Family Science majors.

title: Introduction To Personal And Family Finance
Introduction to Human Development
description: A study of physical, social, and psychological human development and growth across the life course with an emphasis on relationships with family, peers, and others.

title: Introduction to Philanthropy
description: Examines the history, principles, and practice of philanthropy, with a particular focus on relationship building and the desire to promote the well-being of others by working to prevent or resolve social issues.

title: Interpersonal and Family Relationships
description: Study of diverse family and close interpersonal relationships including romantic, parent-child, sibling, and other close relationships, with an emphasis on how individuals, families, and society influence relationships.

title: Problems In Human Development And Family Studies
Developing Intimate Relationships
description: This course will explore topics related to intimate relationships, which include attraction, communication, casual encounters, cohabitation and conflict. Students will critically evaluate current theory and research concerning the elements of healthy relationships. This course will include a didactic and experiential opportunities for the development of a body of knowledge concerning committed relationships, the development of interpersonal skills, increasing personal awareness, and establishing appropriate expectations.

title: Family Violence Across the Life Course
description: Study of violence within families (e.g., child abuse, dating violence, intimate partner violence, elder abuse), prevalence rates, risk and protective factors, and outcomes with an emphasis on prevention and intervention.

title: Introduction to Marriage and Family Therapy
description: This course is designed as an introduction to the theory and practice of MFT. It includes a study of the philosophical, conceptual, and theoretical background of family therapy. The course will review theoretical formulations, common family problems, and effective treatment strategies. Readings and class discussions will provide students with practical examples of the concepts and offer opportunities for students to examine their own family dynamics.

title: Microcomputers In Human Services And The Home
Transition to Parenthood
description: This course is an empirically based evaluation of the transition to parenthood  from the onset of pregnancy to three years after the child's birth.

title: Introduction to Research Methods in Human Development and Family Science
description: Study of research methods in the field of human development and family science with an emphasis on the intersection of research methods, theory, and practice. Students will develop an understanding of the research process and skills necessary to be a critical consumer of research.

title: Family Resource Management Theory And Application
Introduction to Child Life
description: Introduction to the field of child life practices in direct and non-direct services in pediatric healthcare. Focus on the impact of illness, injury and healthcare on children and their families through the context of development theory and psychosocial treatment issues that affect the healthcare experience.

title: Special Problems in Human Development & Family Services
description: Independent or small group study on special topics in human development and family services.

title: Philanthropy in Context
description: Examines philanthropy in the context of diverse individuals and organizations, and considers the unique contributions of diversity in the areas of donor motivation, identification of funding sources, and approaches to philanthropic efforts that might be culturally or contextually influenced. Ethical considerations relevant to the field will also be explored.

title: Families, Employment Benefits And Retirement Planning
Adolescent and Emerging Adult Development
description: Study of development during adolescence and emerging adulthood with an emphasis on individual, family, societal, and contextual factors.

title: Developmental Program Planning For Young Children
Developmental Program Planning For Young Children Lab
Methods And Resources In Early Childhood Education
Methods And Resources In Early Childhood Education Lab
Curriculum For Cognitive And Language Development/Young Kids
Curriculum For Emotional, Social, And Physical Devel Yng Kid
Families in Context
description: Study of how individuals and families experience inequality, power, and privilege related to race, ethnicity, national origin, gender, social class, religion, sexual orientation, socioeconomic status, geographic location, ability, and other social positions.

title: Program Development and Implementation
description: Study of translating research-based knowledge into educational materials and programs for individuals and families, with an emphasis on prevention programs.

title: Research Practicum
description: Independent or small group study. Provides students with a high impact experiential research opportunity with faculty.

title: Orientation to Human Development and Family Science Internship
description: Consideration and application of professional knowledge and skills necessary for selection and placement in a social agency for a supervised experience in direct service to clients.

title: Human Development Family Science Internship
description: An internship experience at a student-selected agency or organization.

title: Professional Seminar In Family Life Education
Professional Seminar In Family Financial Planning
Directed Experiences In Early Childhood Education
Directed Experiences In Early Childhood Education
Death and Family
description: Exploration of contemporary attitudes toward death and dying; related influences on individual development and family life.

title: Marriage Preparation and Enrichment
description: History, processes, components, goals, and research support base for contemporary marriage preparation and enrichment programs.

title: Problems In Family And Child Development
Seminar In Family And Child Development
Topics in Human Development and Family Science
description: Review of recent research and theory related to exploration of methods and family and interpersonal processes.

title: Foundations of Youth Development
description: This course examines the fundamentals of youth development and the youth development profession. Through this introduction to the field, students will explore the ethical, professional and historical elements of youth development as it has evolved toward professionalization.

title: Community Youth Development
description: Community Youth Development focuses upon the national emphasis of a strength-based or asset approach to community youth development. Emphasis is placed upon research, theory, and practice applied in communities throughout the United States. Students will explore existing models, theoretical and applied literature and current community efforts as a basis for understanding community youth development.

title: Adolescents & Their Families: Implications For Youth Professionals
description: This course covers adolescent development as it is related to and intertwined with family development. The reciprocal influences between adolescents and their families will be examined. Working with youth in relation to the family system will be highlighted.

title: Program Design and Evaluation
description: This course overviews the program development process and outcome evaluation of community, children, and family programs. Modes of outcome scholarship and their implications for community-based programs are discussed. Students will develop knowledge through participating in a community-based project involving the practical application of program design and evaluation methods.

title: Topics in Contemporary Youth Issues
description: This course will present issues faced by youth today and associated risk and resiliency factors.

title: Youth Policy
description: Examines various federal and state policies designed specifically for youth including (a) defining and identifying current youth policies; (b) examining policies designed to ""protect the well-being"" of youth (e.g., zero tolerance, restorative justice, juvenile justice and reform); and (c) examining targeted youth policies (e.g., foster care, policies for youth with disabilities, homeless youth).This course examines various federal and state policies designed specifically for youth. This course will be divided into three sections: (a) what is policy and what youth policies exist? (b) policies specifically designed for to ""protect the well-being"" of youth (e.g., zero tolerance, restorative justice, juvenile justice and reform), and (c) targeted youth policies (e.g., foster care, policies for youth with disabilities, homeless youth).

title: Youth Professionals as Consumers of Research
description: Designed to help youth development professionals understand and apply research results and theories to practice. Emphasis placed on research and theory reports related to the youth development with particular attention to research procedures and outcomes.

title: Program Administration and Management
description: Designed to introduce students to the development, administration, and management of youth, family, and community service organizations.

title: Youth Development
description: Designed to introduce students to the developmental period of adolescence including theory and research on positive youth development. Through a critical examination of the professional literature, students will recognize and become familiar with the major issues and transitions adolescents face as they successfully navigate this developmental stage.

title: Youth and Cultural Contexts
description: Examine the cultural heritage of differing family structures, types, and social and educational processes experienced by youth in these families. Students will critically examine how ethnic groups fit historically into our society, and the results of how history has shaped our present day situation.

title: Practicum in Youth Development
description: Supervised experience in youth development practice and service provision in organizational setting.

title: Working with Military Families
description: Initial information necessary to prepare a service-provider for work with military families. Introduction to the culture, context, and complexities of the contemporary military family. This course equips learners to work in capacities which support military families through times of challenge, helping them foster resilience as they cope with military life.

title: Community Mental and Relationship Health Interventions
description: Graduate students will explore theoretical models and research on how social scientists, educators, and practitioners can collaborate with communities to improve mental health and relationship well-being. Students will have the opportunity to lead teams that will apply strategies by partnering with community organizations to develop a brief mental health and relationship well-being community program and develop materials applicable to community change in diverse future professions.

title: Application of Theories in HDFS
description: Examines theories of human development and families, with emphasis on the application of human development and family theory in prevention science.

title: Families in Context
description: A critical examination and overview of the variety of family types, forms, and experiences, and how individuals and families experience social, economic, and geographic differences. Students will be asked to engage with, identify, and explore their own experiences and will gain insight into the experiences of others within the communities they may work within, as helping professionals.

title: Foundations and Principles of Family and Community Services
description: An introduction to the field of family studies and related professions that involve working with individuals and families in communities.

title: Family Dynamics and Interpersonal Relationships
description: This course will examine family functioning, interpersonal relationships, assessment, and evidence-based prevention and intervention. The course explores theoretical perspectives, research methods, relationship forms and processes, and the impact of context and systemic factors on relationship dynamics.

title: Lifespan Development
description: Human development from both lifespan and bio-ecological perspectives focusing on major theories of development and current research on the micro-macro relationship.

title: Resilience in Families
description: Exploration of resilience approaches to the study of families and human development across the life cycle.

title: Families in Poverty
description: Examining the complex nature of poverty and its impact on individuals and families throughout the lifespan; implications for the professional practice of family and community services in relation to families in poverty will be addressed.

title: Nonprofits in a Global Society
description: An introduction to the role(s) of nonprofit and nongovernmental organizations (NGOs) working with diverse families in U.S. and global settings.

title: Collaborating with Parents and Families
description: An examination of theories, models, methods, research, and skills related to parent education and collaborating with parents.

title: Navigating Family Crisis, Trauma, and Resilience
description: This course explores stress management, coping strategies, and resilience across the lifespan within a developmental and family contextual framework. Students will examine the bio-psychosocial nature of stress, effective family functioning in crises, and resilience across populations. The course integrates current research and resilience models to support personal well-being and professional practice by introducing evidence-based interventions.

title: Introduction to Human Development and Family Science Graduate Program
description: Orientation for graduate students in HDFS. Assists with acclimating students to HDFS graduate study, emphasizing the importance of setting professional goals, identifying and utilizing resources for successful completion of graduate degree.

title: Grant Development and Management
description: Introduces students to the grant development process and provides an overview of what happens after a grant is awarded. Topics include: identifying funding sources, generating fundable ideas, assessing needs, project planning, budget design, evaluation and project management.

title: Statistical Methods in Human Development and Family Science I
description: Introductory course in the use of statistics in family and human services research. Examines the use of statistical methods from basic descriptive and univariate measures to multivariate measures including ANOVA and Multiple Regression Analyses as they are used in family and human services research. Emphasis is placed on the analytical and applied aspects of the statistical procedures.

title: Topics in Applied Family Science
description: Recent research focusing on selected relevant topics.

title: Qualitative Research Methods in Human Development and Family Science
description: Expands students' skills in theories and methods associated with qualitative research. Emphasis on understanding foundations of qualitative methods, comparing and using various qualitative traditions and applying this knowledge to a research project.

title: Family Resource Management
description: Survey course of personal finance and family resource management literature to provide an overview of how individuals and family members develop and exercise their capacity to obtain and manage resources to meet life needs. Resources include the self, other people, time, money, energy, material assets, space, and environment.

title: Family Science
description: Survey of family research literature to illustrate various approaches to the study of the family and to understand family changes within the life cycle.

title: Principles Of Marriage And Family Therapy
Capstone in Human Development and Family Science
description: Supervised experience in providing help and/or instruction in the several areas of human development and family studies presented in terms of the special interests of the students. Consent of practicum supervisor is required for each.

title: Practicum in Study of Student Development
description: Supervised experience in providing help and/or instruction in the several areas of human development and family studies presented in terms of the special interests of the students. Consent of practicum supervisor is required for each.

title: Practicum In Marriage And Family Therapy
Research Methods in Human Development and Family Science
description: Focuses on the study and application of research methodology in the field of Human Development and Family Science.

title: Application of Research & Statistics in Human Development and Family Science
description: Development of students' skills as quantitative methodologists and scholars. Students will refine their thinking about their research program and explore data sets that complement their research interests. Application, for purposes of this course, involves the utilization of statistical knowledge and techniques in practice, and the ability to convey this information to others, both through academic writing and through hands-on instruction.

title: Readings in Human Development and Family Science
description: Implications of research findings in preparation for professional work in counseling, teaching, and research in human development and family science.

title: Professional and Ethical Issues in Applied Family Science
description: Emphasizes the research-theory-practice connection. Includes cutting-edge issues in the field, ethics and professional development.

title: MS Research in Human Development and Family Science
description: Individual research problems which may form the basis for the master's thesis or report.

title: Statistical Methods in Family Studies and Human Sciences II
description: Advanced applied statistics course designed for graduate students with a foundation and understanding of basic and multivariate statistics. Examines multiple variable analyses including log-linear, discriminate analyses, OLS and logistic regression and the issues involved using these techniques; principal component and factor analyses; path analyses; structural equation modeling; hierarchical linear and nonlinear modeling; and growth curve modeling.

title: Topics In Family Life Education And Consultation
Advanced Qualitative Methods in Family Science
description: Emphasizes the diversity and variety within qualitative methodology. Focuses on hands-on experiences of completing a qualitative research project after the basic research design has been determined.

title: Topics In Marriage And Family Therapy
Advanced Family Life Education And Consultation
Advanced Practicum In Family And Community Services
College Teaching Practicum in HDFS
description: This course is designed for students in the Prevention Science in HDFS doctoral program to gain practical skills in teaching through a structured teaching experience. This will involve observing; developing activities, lectures, assignments, and exams; teaching part of the assigned course; evaluating student performance; as well as other relevant teaching experiences.

title: Research Practicum in HDFS
description: This course is designed for students in the Prevention Science in HDFS doctoral program to gain practical, advanced skills in human development and family science research through a structured research experience. This may consist of data collection, data analysis, literature reviews, dissemination of findings, as well as other relevant research experiences.

title: Supervision Of Marriage And Family Therapy
Ph.D. Practicum In Marriage And Family
Conjoint And Group Techniques In Family Counseling
Research In Family And Child Development
Well-being 1: You and Community
description: Explore the meaning of well-being and how we foster it in the context of community. Learn how a better understanding of ourselves leads to greater well-being and sense of community.

title: Introduction to Well-being
description: Explore history, mission, and theoretical perspective of the College of Health and Human Sciences and their relationships to well-being. Introduction to the five essential elements of well-being, CliftonStrengths, Wellbeing Finder, and transformational undergraduate experience.

title: Well-being 2: Mind and Body
description: Reflect on and assess current physical, psychological, and social well-being. Utilize evidence-based recommendations, reliable sources, and personal strengths to set and track progress towards mind, body, and relational goals.

title: Community Well-being
description: Explore the differentiating factors between a good and great life with community well-being. Learn the interrelationship of career, social, financial, physical, and community well-being across the lifespan.

title: Social Well-being
description: Explore how relationships shape identity and well-being. Learn the interrelationship of career, social, financial, physical, and community well-being across the lifespan.

title: Financial Well-being
description: Explores components of financial security and how to access advice when major life issues arise with financial implications. Learn the interrelationship of career, social, financial, physical, and community well-being across the lifespan.

title: Physical Well-being
description: Explores how every choice has a net positive or negative effect on well-being. Discover how mood is affected by diet, physical activity, and sleep. Learn the interrelationship of career, social, financial, physical and community well-being across the lifespan.

title: Foundations of Well-Being
description: Introduces key dimensions of well-being (e.g. physical, psychological, social, financial, and community). Students will explore personal strengths, assess behaviors and habits, set goals, and build practical skills to plan for lifelong well-being. Emphasis is placed on understanding influences on well-being and accessing resources for informed decision-making.

title: Well-being 3: Money and Meaning
description: Explore components of financial well-being and how to access advice when major life issues arise with financial implications. Examine career well-being as a broad concept and how it relates to our sense of personal well-being, purpose, and meaning. Reflect on the interrelationship of career, social, financial, physical, and community well-being across the lifespan.

title: Career Well-being
description: Explores career well-being as a broad concept that discusses purpose in life. Learn the interrelationship of career, social, financial, physical, and community well-being across the lifespan. It will also include the capstone, integrative well-being activity/reflection.

title: Undergraduate Research Experience
description: Open to students pursuing undergraduate research projects.

title: Foundations in Research Methodology
description: Graduate level course provides a foundation in research methodology in health and human sciences.

title: Grant Writing and Management
description: Focus on identifying extramural funding sources, grant writing, and managing grants.

title: Hindi I
description: Introduction to the structure of modern Hindi. Listening, speaking, reading, and writing.

title: Hindi II
description: Continuation of Hindi I, completion of the basic structural and linguistic principles of Hindi.

title: Hindi III
description: Continuation of Hindi II. Listening, speaking, reading, and writing.

title: Hindi IV
description: Continuation of Hindi III. Listening, speaking, reading and writing.

title: Introduction to History
description: What history is, how it is produced, and what its functions are. Designed for freshmen who want an introductory course which explains the methodology, purposes, and career options of the discipline.

title: The Premodern West, 500 BC-1600 AD
