description: Major trends in Western Civilization from its beginnings to the seventeenth century. The scope of this course includes ancient Greece and Rome, the Middle Ages, the Renaissance, and the Reformation, but chronological and topical emphases vary with individual sections.

title: Western Civilization II: Europe in the Modern World
description: Principal developments since the beginning of the eighteenth century. Scope includes the Enlightenment, the French Revolution, the Industrial Revolution, nationalism, imperialism, communism, fascism, the two world wars, the Holocause, and Europe during and after the Cold War. Chronological and topical emphases vary with individual sections.

title: Overseas European Studies
description: Selected aspects of European history and culture with readings, lectures, and discussions which will relate historical events to places visited.

title: Western Civilization/the Rise of Europe(Honors)
description: Major trends in Western history from the beginnings of European civilization to the end of the seventeenth century. Scope includes classical antiquity, the Middle Ages, the Renaissance, the Reformation, and early modern Europe, but chronological and topical emphases vary with individual sections.

title: Western Civilization/the Modern Era (Honors)
description: Principal developments in Western civilization from the beginning of the eighteenth century to the present. Scope includes the Enlightenment, the French Revolution, the Industrial Revolution, nationalism, imperialism, communism, fascism, and the two world wars, but chronological and topical emphases vary with individual sections.

title: World History to 1450
description: Major developments in the history of the world from the earliest civilizations to the eve of European expansion in the fifteenth century. Comparison of key trends and conditions in different parts of the world. Topics include the agricultural revolution, the birth of the city, the formation of states and empires, and the emergence of world religions.

title: World Hist From 1450
description: Major trends in the history of the world from the 15th century to the present. Global patterns of contact, mutual influence, and interdependence.

title: History of the United States Since 1877
description: Ethnic, social, political, economic, and diplomatic history. The goal of the course is to achieve a broad understanding of American civilization since 1877.

title: Introduction to US Military History
description: Studies the history of the military and warfare in the United States from 1775 to the present. Includes strategic, operational, political, and social developments to explain how warfare has shaped US society and culture, and how they, in turn, shaped the US military. Topics include the history and development of the armed forces, the operational history of US wars, the place of the military in US society and culture, the influence of technological changes on warfare, and the role of women and people of color in the armed forces over time.

title: Topics in History For Freshmen and Sophomores
description: Exploration of the historical dimensions of a particular topic or theme.

title: America In The First World War
Military History Of Twentieth Century China
Russian Culture and Civilization
description: Russia's past and present in the light of principle ideologies with emphasis upon fine arts, literature, music, religion, politics, and education. Equal time will be given to the Tsarist and the Soviet periods.

title: History of Christianity to 1500AD
description: A basic introduction to how most academic historians approach the history of pre-modern Christianity. An explantion of how a secular approach, grounded in an epistemology of rational empiricism, differs from the approach, grounded in an epistemology of divine revelation, that most Christians have used – an approach known as “church history” or faith history. Ultimately, students will develop the tools to recognize and think critically about both types of history, so that when they encounter histories of Christianity in the future, they are prepared to understand each type.

title: History of the US to 1877 H
description: History of the US to 1877 Honors

title: United States Since 1877 Honors
description: History of the United States since 1877 Honors

title: Honors Introduction to the Humanities I
description: Study of selected major works of history, literature, and philosophy which have been of central importance in the Western cultural tradition. Considerable emphasis is placed on classroom discussion and writing interpretive essays.

title: Honors Introduction to the Humanities II
Introduction to Historical Thinking
description: Introduces the history major to the major principles of historical thinking as well as providing an introduction to the methodologies of historical research.

title: Latin American History and Civilization
description: Introduces the history of Latin America from the earliest times to the present. Argentina, Brazil, Cuba, Mexico, and Peru will receive special attention. Themes treated will include race and ethnicity, cultural survival, revolution and authoritarianism, women and family, and the role of economic development in Latin American history.

title: Introduction to Modern East Asia
description: The history of China, Japan, and surrounding countries including the arrival of Europeans in the sixteenth century, reactions to Western imperialism, the rise of nationalism, and revolution. The impact of the two world wars, the era of post war developments, communism in China, democracy in Japan, and the end of Western colonialism are also examined.

title: History of Technology
description: This course focuses on the development of technology from ancient times to modern day, with emphasis on technology and its impact on American society from colonial times to present. Students will prepare a portfolio project that will feature a research or service learning component.

title: History of East Asian Civilizations
description: East Asian history from earliest times to the present. Social structures, beliefs, and values during key periods of Chinese and Japanese history. Major developments in religion, philosophy, literature, and the arts.

title: Introduction to Japan
description: Historical survey of Japan from prehistoric times to the present day, which may include material on society, politics, and culture.

title: Introduction to China
description: Survey of the history, geography, politics, and culture of China from prehistoric times to the present day.

title: War and Conquest in Hispanic America
description: Explores the conquests of Spanish America: how did just a few hundred Spaniards topple the expansive Aztec empire? What are the legacies and myths of conquest that persist today? Examines relations between indigenous inhabitants of the Americas and European conquistadors through historical texts, artwork, and literature.

title: The Middle Ages, 500-1500 AD
description: Surveys the consensus schemas that drive academic approaches to the culture of western Europe between roughly 500 and 1500 AD including the ""Tripartite Chronological (Antiquity, Middle Ages, Modern), ""Western Civilization

title: Middle East History 1500-1914
description: A comprehensive introduction to the history of the Modern Middle East and North Africa region, encompassing the period from the creation of the Safavid Empire during the 1500s until World War I. The Exploration of the three major regional powers that emerged during this era: the Ottoman Empire, the Safavid Empire, and the Arabic-speaking world.

title: Middle East History 1914-Present
description: An introduction to the history of the modern Middle East and North Africa region, encompassing the pivotal period from the aftermath of World War I to the transformative events of the Arab Spring.

title: Hollywood and the Military
description: Considers the impact of changes in the political, social, cultural, and geopolitical context on Hollywood's representation of the military.

title: The Vikings
description: A basic treatment of the pirates that modern people call vikings. Prejudices derived from old scholarly constructions of these people as savage warriors have produced distorted popular understandings of them. Discussion of what most historians now argue about the vikings in the Viking Age (c. 800-1100 AD). Careful attention to how the majority of historians have recently begun to recognize that the vikings operated in a global, rather than a simply western European, context.

title: Cold War Cultures
description: Examines how the Cold War (1945-1991) conflict was not only a diplomatic and military conflict. It also influenced US culture and other societies' cultures in profound ways. This course examines Cold War life for citizens of all corners of the globe on both sides of the Iron Curtain and in the Global South.

title: Chemical Warfare, 1914-Present
description: Interdisciplinary look at chemical warfare. Encourages students to engage with the nature of chemical weapons as a way to help them better understand the implications of their use over the last century. Students are asked to think about the ways in which chemical compounds created for benign purposes can be subverted and used as weapons regardless of their creator’s original intent.

title: Blood and Steel: War in Europe since 1618
description: A survey of European warfare that focuses on military operations and grand strategy. It engenders a deeper understanding of the development of European military practices and institutions and how war has shaped Europe’s history.

title: A World in Flames: Global World War II
description: A global exploration of the origins, conduct, and consequence of World War II.

title: The Pacific War: World War II in Asia and the Pacific
description: Explores the origins, conduct, and consequences of World War II in Asia and the Pacific. It places particular emphasis on this war's roots in the intensifying Sino-Japanese animosities by the early twentieth century, the war's interconnections to the concurrent conflicts in Europe, and the US significance to course and outcomes of the war.

title: Bloody Battlefields: WWII in Europe
description: From Hitler’s rise to power, to siege of Stalingrad, to the Normandy landings, this class will follow the unfolding of WWII on both eastern and western fronts until the meeting of Soviet and American troops on the Elbe River in 1945. In addition to the belligerents’ grand strategies and the unfolding of specific battles, this class will look at the ways the war affected people and societies.

title: Pirates and Piracy in Global History
description: A global history of pirates and piracy, from Greece and Rome to the Caribbean, the South China Sea, and the Dark Web.

title: The Soviet Union
description: Survey of the political, economic, cultural, and social history of the Soviet Union, including coverage of the Russian Revolution, WWII, the Cold War, and the Soviet Union’s collapse.

title: Nazi Germany and the Holocaust
description: Examines the political, social, economic, and intellectual developments in Germany from World War I to the end of World War II, including the Nazi ideology and the Holocaust.

title: Grand Strategy Simulation
description: The annual grand strategy simulation provides students with a high-impact, applied learning experience that pushes them to move from theory to practice and implement their strategic-thinking skills they have gained in their classes by playing out a realistic crisis scenario.

title: History Workshop
description: Provides students the opportunity to engage in historical research and writing in innovative formats beyond the traditional term paper, with an emphasis on primary source analysis and/or digital humanities.

title: History of K-State Workshop
description: Investigation of the history of Kansas State University and its students, carried out in the University Archives. Students pursue guided individual research projects and present their results in a public-facing way.

title: Introduction To The History Of Aviation
History Of Dance In Its Cultural Setting
Honors Project
description: Open only to Arts & Sciences students who are active members of the University Honors Program.

title: Japan's Samurai Age
description: The history of Japan from earliest times to the fall of the Tokugawa shogunate in 1868, including major developments in political institutions, social structures, and religious beliefs. Examines the rise and development of the warrior class that dominated society and government for much of this period.

title: Historical Study in International Setting
description: Course work that enhances the experience of international travel and study. Requires an historical engagement with the travel experience through readings, lectures, discussion, and written work.

title: Intellectual History of Early China
description: Introduction to the development of Chinese thought during the period from about 1200 BC to 100 BC, with emphasis on the foundational ideas of Confucianism, Daoism, and Legalism and their incorporation within the emerging imperial order.

title: South Asian Civilizations
description: Interdisciplinary survey of the development of civilization in India, Pakistan, Sri Lanka, Bangladesh, Nepal, Bhutan, and Afghanistan, including geography, philosophy, social, economic, political institutions, and historical movements.

title: Modern India and South Asia
description: The history of India, Pakistan, and Bangladesh since 1500, beginning with Mogul rule and continuing through European colonialism, the anti-imperial struggle, and the troubled transition to independence.

title: China Since 1644
description: China from the founding of the Manchu Qing dynasty to the present. Includes the western imperialist challenge in the nineteenth century, the Revolution of 1949, and the post-Mao Reforms. Emphasis on social, political and intellectual changes in the context of increasing contact with western nations and Japan.

title: Japan Since 1550
description: Japan from reunification in the sixteenth century through the Tokugawa and Imperial eras to the postwar recovery. Emphasis on understanding modern Japan as the product of traditional culture, the Meiji Restoration, and World War II.

title: World War I
description: Examines the origins, events and consequences of the ""war to end all wars."" The impact and influence of the war on colonialism, imperialism, and popular culture will be discussed.

title: Environmental History
description: An introduction to environmental history as an academic specialization through selected reading and topical lectures. The course emphasizes the study of people in nature through time; it stresses people's response to environmental change through three broadly defined periods: pre-industrial, modern industrial, and contemporary.

title: Women in European History
description: A study of women in primitive European societies, in preindustrial times, and in the industrial era. Emphasis will be upon the position and role of women within the society.

title: Battles and Leaders
description: The course will emphasize military organization, tactics and strategy, generalship and grand strategy, manpower and logistics, and the wartime ramifications of war on land, at sea, and in the air.

title: History of Sport
description: The historical development of sport (especially in Europe and North America) including the growth of competition, the rise of mass spectator sports, elitism, and the changing function of sport. History of sport as business and history of the relationship between sport and other institutions.

title: The Modern Middle East
description: History of Islam and the broader Middle East including North Africa since 1500. Topics covered include the rise of early modern empires, closer contact with Western Europe and East Asia, the impact of Western imperialism, state-sponsored defensive reforms, the development of religious modernism, social movements and constitutionalism, the emergence of nationalism, oil-dependent economies and rentier states, the origins of religious activism, and the contemporary struggle for civil rights.

title: Iranian People Since Antiquity
description: Critical introduction to the history, geography, society, politics, economy and culture of Iran (historical Persia) from prehistoric times to the present day.

title: Science & Technology in the United States
description: A survey of United States science and technology from the colonial era to the present, with special attention to how the wider social context, including the role of institutions and government, affected processes of scientific and technological development. Coverage also of the challenges of addressing the social problems that arose in connection with scientific and technological development throughout United States history.

title: Death and Dying In History
description: Examines European and American attitudes toward death and dying in various historical periods. Topics include: death and dying in the European Middle Ages and in nineteenth and twentieth century America, the impact of the Nazi Holocaust on modern opinions about death, suicide as a historical problem, the fear of cancer in modern times, and others.

title: History of Christianity
description: A history of the Christian religion from the era of Jesus Christ to the present with special emphasis on people and ideas.

title: Religion in American History
description: A study of the impact of religion on American culture and of American culture on religion, the Social Gospel and related issues, and the interrelationship of Christianity and politics.

title: A History of the Occult And Witchcraft
description: A study of the history of the occult and witchcraft in Western civilization with special attention to religious, intellectual, and social issues and influences.

title: The History of Baseball in American Culture
description: The history of baseball from its origins in the early nineteenth century to the present, with emphasis on the major leagues and their collateral organizations but also with attention to semi-pro and amateur baseball and to the old Negro Leagues. The history of the game will be examined in the context of American history with special reference to social issues, politics, religion, literature, music, and the medial.

title: Colonial America
description: About 1450 to 1763. Includes the European background of North American colonization, the rivalry for new world empire, seventeenth century English colonial foundations, and development of the various colonial societies.

title: The American Revolution
description: Eighteenth century colonial background of the Revolution and the revolutionary era itself, 1763-1789. Stresses ideological and other causes of the Revolution, the course of the war, its social results, the Confederation and its demise.

title: The Early National Period
description: Foundations of the new nation from the adoption of the Constitution to the conclusion of the War of 1812, approximately 1789-1815. Stresses the contest between Hamiltonians and Jeffersonians for philosophical dominance of institutions; other topics include diplomacy, westward expansion, military developments, the social and intellectual life of the era.

title: America In The Age Of Jackson
Civil War and Reconstruction
description: 1848-1877. Examination of the sectional controversy, the failure of the political system to resolve peacefully the conflict between North and South, the resort to arms, the nature of the post-war settlement. Emphasis is on the attempt of mid-nineteenth-century American leaders to deal with the complex problems of slavery and race.

title: United States in the 19th Century
description: Explores the history of the United States during the 19th century and traces the transition of the country from a fragile, uncertain republic to a robust imperial power standing in the first rank of the world's nations.

title: The United States in the Twentieth Century
description: Examines the creation of modern America, 1890 to the present. Emphasis on the social and cultural roots, and political consequences, of Progressivism, World War I, the Great Depression, World War II, the Sixties, and Post-Vietnam America.

title: History Of American Criminology And Penology
Topics in the History of the Americas
description: Provides instructor and students the opportunity to investigate in detail a particular theme, event, or problem in the history of North, Central, or South America. Topics vary.

title: Social History of Medicine
description: An exploration of the development of American social thought and practices regarding health care from colonial times to the present. The course stresses changing cultural attitudes toward disease as well as alterations in social practices and institutions related to healing. Special emphasis is given to the institutional development and professionalization of modern medicine.

title: The American West
description: Primary emphasis on the nineteenth century when Americans were rapidly spreading across the continent. Also examines the earlier developments of the frontier and considers the twentieth century role of the trans-Mississippi region.

title: History of Indigenous Peoples of North America
description: A survey of the Indigenous peoples of the North American continent, with a focus on the United States, from 30,000 BP to the present. Drawing on the work of anthropologists, ethnologists, historians, and the voices of Native peoples, this course provides students a Native-based perspective that challenges common stereotypes and provides a pathway for understanding contemporary sovereignty movements.

title: Women in Sport
description: A survey of the history of the female athlete, with special emphasis on the significance of women in sport in the United States. Themes covered include the evolution of the social roles of sport, the relationship between sport and gender, and athletes' construction of identity.

title: African American History
description: An overview of the African-American experience from the seventeenth century through the civil rights movement. Emphasizes social, legal, economic, political, and intellectual aspects of black history as well African-American contributions to American life and culture.

title: Women In America, 1600 to the Civil War
description: An overview of the history of American women from the beginning of the European colonization to the Civil War. Women's changing social role and its relation to the major transformations in American culture and society during this period is stressed. Special emphasis is given to ethnic, racial, and class variations among women.

title: Women in America, Civil War to the Present
description: An overview of the history of American women from the end of the Civil War to the present. Examines women's changing role in modern industrial society with special emphasis upon the women's rights movement of both the nineteenth and twentieth centuries.

title: Forging an American Empire: US Foreign Relations to 1920
description: History of U.S. foreign relations from its origins in the colonial era through World War I.

title: From World Power to Superpower: US Foreign Relations since 1920
description: History of United States foreign relations from the end of World War I through the present day.  Explores how the United States engaged the world as a global power.

title: War in the Twentieth Century
description: Considers the military theory and practice, the technology, and the political and ideological constraints of World Wars I and II, the Spanish Civil War, the Korean War, and the Indochinese wars. Students are to gain an understanding of the varieties of military experience in the twentieth century, including civil wars, ""total war,''and guerrilla warfare.

title: History of American Military Affairs
description: Deals with the development of military institutions in colonial America and the United States, civil-military relations and conflicts between political constraints and strategic demands, popular attitudes toward the military, and the rise of the military-industrial complex.

title: Economic History of the United States
description: Major topics in American economic history from the colonial period to the present.  Focus is on nineteenth- and twentieth-century issues, including the rise of big business; the expanding role of the government in the economy; the Great Depression; the postwar boom and its end; issues of class, race, and gender; America's shifting roles in the global economy.

title: Modern Africa from 1850
description: Introduction to modern African history since 1850. Key topics include the establishment and impact of the colonial empires that dominated Africa between the 1880s and 1960s; the process of decolonization; African responses to colonial rule; and the development of post-Independence Africa with a special emphasis on the challenges faced by African countries.

title: History of Family Violence
description: Explores the history of family violence in America as a social, cultural, legal, and public policy issue from colonial times to the present. Stress is placed upon the cultural roots and evolution of domestic law. The development of state-controlled social welfare agencies as well as the emergence of the ""battered women's movement"" is particularly emphasized.

title: History of American Culture
description: Main emphasis is on political, religious, and social thought and ideology, 1620 to present.

title: History of the South
description: Topical analysis of important issues in Southern history. Compares the plantation myth of popular films with interpretations by important historians. Emphasis on plantation agriculture, slavery, race relations, class, and gender in the Old South. Post-Civil War topics include federal Reconstruction efforts, segregation, economic reform, and the modern Civil Rights movement.

title: American Consitutional History
description: Survey of constitutional and legal development from colonial times to the present. English constitutional ideas and the common law in the American colonies, formation of the Constitution, the role of the Supreme Court, development of the modern American legal system, growth of the legal profession, the problem of civil liberties. The course offers insight into the relationship of constitutional-legal institutions to American society.

title: Bill of Rights in American History
description: This course provides a topical survey of the American Bill of Rights from the colonial era to the present. It begins with the origins of American rights in England and colonial America. An analysis of the need for a Bill of Rights at the founding and Supreme Court interpretations in 1835 and during the Reconstruction era follow. The bulk of the course is concerned with the nationalization and expansion of the Bill of Rights in the twentieth century and its meaning in the everyday lives of American citizens.

title: History of American Agriculture
description: Concentrates on the period since 1850 in an attempt to acquaint the student with the political and economic history of American agriculture. No attempt will be made to present the scientific or technological side of agriculture in detail, but agriculture will be shown in relation to the life of the entire United States. The life of the farmer and his family, the relationship between agricultural changes and other parts of the economy will be part of this course. Special attention will be paid to agriculture in Kansas and the Great Plains.

title: History of Kansas
description: Land, people, and cultural developments in Kansas, from the earliest written records to the present. Provides the student with an intimate understanding of the state of Kansas.

title: History of Aztecs, Inca, and Maya
description: Introduction to the history of Ancient Mesoamerican and Andean civilizations through a multi-disciplinary approach combining materials from the fields of history, art history, anthropology, and archaeology.

title: Latin American Nations
description: Survey of economic, social, and political developments of the Latin American nations from independence to the present decade with emphasis on Argentina, Brazil, Peru, Chile, and Mexico. Stresses reform and revolution of the last 50 years.

title: Colonial Hispanic America
description: Iberian and indigenous American background, exploration, conquest, settlement, and development of Latin America. Stresses growth of mestizo culture, colonial styles of living, and wars of independence.

title: Modern Mexico
description: Brief survey of lines of national development, 1821-1910, and major emphasis on the twentieth-century revolution and its reforms (1910-1940) as well as its subsequent implications.

title: History of Mass Communications in America
description: Growth and development of media and media technology in the United States and their economic, political, and social significance.

title: History and Culture of Greece
description: The rise of civilization in the ancient Near East, the migrations of the Greeks and the Heroic Age, the Greek city-states, commerce and colonization, the Persian invasion, Athens' leadership of Greece, the war between Athens and Sparta, Alexander the Great, and the total Hellenic achievement.

title: History and Culture of Rome
description: Examines the various theories of Rome's origin, the causes, problems, and influences upon the republican government, political and economic problems of Roman expansion, and the Roman world. Various reforms including those of the Gracchi, Caesar, and Augustus. Contact with Greece and the older areas of civilization. The Roman imperial system, the many causes of Rome's fall, and Rome's role as a synthesizer of the ancient classical culture.

title: Europe in the Middle Ages
description: Europe from the fall of the Roman Empire to the thirteenth century. Investigates the conflict and interaction of Roman, Christian, and Germanic ideals and attitudes in the early Middle Ages, and the increasing complexity and sophistication of society, culture, religion, and government of the high Middle Ages.

title: The Renaissance
description: The Italian Renaissance as a major phase in the history of Western civilization and its spread to northern Europe.

title: The Reformation
description: A study of the Protestant, Catholic, and Radical Reformations with special attention to Luther, Calvin, the origins of the Church of England and the Presbyterian Church, the Anabaptists, the Puritans, and Roman Catholic Reform, and the impact of religious developments on the political, economic, social, and intellectual history of the Western world. Covers the period from approximately 1500 to 1660.

title: Europe in the Seventeenth Century
description: Surveys the economic, social, political and intellectual history of western Europe in the seventeenth century, a period marked by economic depression, international conflict, and domestic revolutions as well as by cultural achievement. Emphasizes the complex interaction among social groups; the rise of a European state system; the development of constitutional monarchy in England and absolute monarchy in France; and the change in values generated by the scientific revolution.

title: Eighteenth-Century Europe
description: Europe from the death of Louis XIV in 1715 to the fall of Napoleon in 1815. The origins and development of the French Revolution and the Napoleonic legacy, also examines reform and counter-revolutionary movements in England, Italy, Russia, Poland, and the Germanies.

title: Nineteenth-Century Europe
description: The history of Europe from the French Revolution to the end of the first World War. Major topics covered will include the rise of conservatism as an ideology and its application in practice, the nature of liberalism and socialism, the impact of science and technology, the origins and course of World War I.

title: Twentieth-Century Europe
description: Examines the political, social, and intellectual developments of Europe in the period of the two world wars. Emphasis on the failure of democracy and the rise of competing antidemocratic and nondemocratic mass movements and ideologies. The course will also deal with the attempted system of collective security, its failure, and the origins and course of World War II.

title: Europe Since World WarII
description: Postwar European society, politics, economy, and culture. The effects of total war on the population; restoration and reconstruction. The influence of the U.S. and U.S.S.R. on Europe. Capitalism, socialism, and communism in technological society. European unity movements and their conflicts with traditional values.

title: European International Relations to 1815
description: The nature, evolution, and function of the diplomatic system for the Ancient World to 1815. Analyzes the Greek and Roman diplomatic tradition, international relations during the Medieval, Renaissance, and Early Modern periods, and the works of various theorists.

title: European International Relations Since 1815
description: The nature, evolution, and functions of the European diplomatic system from 1815 to the present. Focuses on the Vienna settlement, the Eastern Question, the Crimean War, Italian and German unification, origins of World War I, international developments between the two world wars, the cold war, and the post-cold war era. Includes analysis of major theorists.

title: Central Europe, 1500-1914
description: The diplomatic, military, political, cultural, and social aspects of the Hapsburg empire in Central Europe from its foundation to its dissolution in the twentieth century.

title: The British Isles to 1603
description: English, Scottish, and Irish culture in the medieval and pre-modern periods. Early folk societies, feudalism, the church in society and politics, the origins of representative institutions and the religious reformations are studied topically.

title: The British Isles Since 1603
description: English society and politics in modern times with reference also to Scotland and Ireland. Emphasis on topics such as the three orders of society (king, lords, and commons), the churches and religion, the appearance of parliamentary sovereignty, the industrial revolution, and the extension of democratic institutions.

title: Gender and Sexuality in Greece and Rome
description: Provides an introduction to constructions of gender and sexuality with special focus on their intersections with culture, ethnicity, and class.  Course will combine close readings of primary texts and discussions of material culture with secondary literature. Broader discussions will emphasize change over time, noting not only how gender and sexuality develop and evolve across centuries of Greek and Roman culture but also how they influenced subsequent Western cultures.  By means of close readings of these texts students will practice analyzing, interpreting, and contextualizing literature and material culture, and also come to a greater appreciation of how antiquity and modernity construct gender and sexuality. Course format will involve lecture combined with discursive analysis of texts, material culture, and especially visual arts.

title: History of France,1400-1715
description: France from the conclusion of the Hundred Years War to the death of Louis XIV. French economy, society, and royal administration, and the changes generated in these areas by significant events: the Reformation and the Wars of Religion; the rise of France to world power; peasant uprisings and constitutional crisis; and the reforms of Richelieu, Colbert, and Louis XIV. Trends in art, architecture, and philosophy.

title: Medieval Christianity
description: A survey of the development of Christianity in western Europe during the Middle Ages (c. 500-1500). Topics include the development of the papacy and the ecclesiastical hierarchy, the role of kings and other rulers, the Crusades, scholasticism, purgatory, penance, monasticism, specific religious orders like the Franciscans, baptism, marriage, asceticism, how medieval Christians read the Bible, saints, the roles of women, and heresy.

title: Advanced Seminar In History
description: An undergraduate seminar that focuses on the intellectual principles of the historical discipline as well as the fundamental research techniques and writing skills used by historians. Each section of the seminar will center on a particular topic or historical problem. The students will prepare a research paper on a relevant subject of their choice.

title: Modern Germany,1789-1914
description: Central Europe in the French Revolutionary era, the revolutions of 1848, German unification, imperial Germany, emphasizing social changes, especially the transition from agrarian to industrial society.

title: Lost Kansas Communities
description: Combined lecture-field research course in the history of vanished Kansas towns. Overview of settlement, persistence, and decline. Students also research a lost community of their choosing.

title: The Russian Empire
description: Imperial Russia from the earliest Slavic tribes through 1881, with emphasis on Russia's heritage as a multi-ethnic state and the phenomenon of Russia's revolutional intellectuals.

title: Twentietn Century Russia
description: The turbulent history of modern Russia, including the upheaval of the Russian Revolution and Civil War, Stalin's transformation of Soviet society, World War II, failed attempts to transform the Soviet system, and the fall of the Soviet Union.

title: The Vietnam War
description: Examines the origins, conduct, and long-term ramificationsâ€¯of the Vietnam War from 1945 to the present, emphasizing military, cultural, and environmental perspectives.

title: The American Peace Movement In The Inter-War Years
French Revolution 1789-1815
description: The political, economic, cultural, and social development of France from the origins of the Revolution to the Congress of Vienna.

title: Holocaust/The Destruction Of The European Jews
Topics in European History
description: Provides instructor and students the opportunity to investigate in detail a particular theme, event, or problem in European history.

title: Topics in Non-Western History
description: Provides instructor and students the opportunity to investigate in detail a particular theme, event, or problem in non-Western history.

title: Science and Society from Newton to the Industrial Revolution
Introduction To The History Of Aviation
Internship in History
description: Practical professional experience involving at least three weeks in an archive, museum, historical library, or business. Student projects must be approved in advance and a report submitted at the end of the work period.

title: Overseas European Studies
description: Short-term, intensive, and in-depth study of various aspects of European history and culture with readings, lectures, discussions, and on-the-spot experiences which will relate historical events to the places visited.

title: Clinical Observations And Inferences For Psychohistorians
European thought in the Nineteenth Century
description: European thought in the Nineteenth Century

title: European and American Political and Social Movement
The Russian Revolutions And The Soviet System
Readings in History
description: Students will read on a central theme, attend weekly discussions, and write a final report.

title: Problems in History
description: Intensive study of a particular phase of history. Students will attend weekly discussions and write a major research paper on their findings.

title: Historiography
description: Main currents in historical research, the writing of history, and the influence of the great historians from Herodotus to the present.

title: Security Studies Methodology
description: An exploration of commonly used research methods in history and political science. Students will investigate quantitative and qualitative humanities and social sciences methodology through selected readings and limited research exercises.

title: Research Design in Security Studies
description: An exploration of research methods in history and political science culminating in a piece of original research in Security Studies.

title: History and Security: East Asia
description: An examination of the major historical problems and literature relating to national and international security in East Asia.

title: History and Security: Latin America
description: An examination of the major historical problems and literature relating to national and international security in Latin America.

title: History and Security: the Middle East
description: An examination of the major historical problems and literature relating to national and international security in the Middle East.

title: History and Security: Russia/Central Asia
description: An examination of the major historical problems and literature relating to national and international security in Russian and Central Asia.

title: History and Security:  South Asia
description: An examination of the major historical problems and literature relating to national and international security in South Asia.

title: History and Security: Modern Africa since 1850
description: Focuses on major political events and their repercussions. Key topics include war in African history, the creation and administration of colonial empires, decolonization, and the political and security struggles of the post-independence era.

title: Topics in International History
description: Introduce the most important themes and issues in historical topics and subjects of transnational and global significance. Contextualizes historical literature to help students prepare for teaching and for their general field exams.

title: Topics in the History of Science
description: Topics in the History of Science

title: Advanced Historiography
description: Advanced work offered on demand and by arrangement, in main currents in historical research, the writing of history, and the influence of great historians.

title: Renaissance and Reformation Europe
description: An examination of the major historical problems and literature.

title: Early-Modern Europe
description: An examination of the major historical problems and literature.

title: 19th Century Europe
description: An examination of the major historical problems and literature.

title: Twentieth-Century Europe
description: An examination of the major historical problems and literature.

title: Colonial/Revolutionary America
description: An examination of the major historical problems and literature.

title: Nineteenth-Century America
description: An examination of the major historical problems and literature.

title: Twentieth-Century America
description: An examination of the major historical problems and literature.

title: Historical Approaches to Security
description: Examination of theories and approaches to military history and the history of international relations.

title: Historical Methods in Security Studies
description: Training in primary-source research in military history, foreign policy, and international relations.

title: Seminar in History of Christianity
description: This seminar is a course in which students are expected to study the primary source material for particular topics within a general area of study and to write a paper based on research using such sources. These courses are intended to develop graduate students' skills in the use of research tools and methods, ability to conceptualize and organize an argument, and facility in presenting research findings. Within the general areas listed below, these courses may focus inter alia upon political, diplomatic, social, religious, intellectual, or environmental history.

Within the general areas listed below, these courses may focus inter alia upon political, diplomatic, social, religious, intellectual, or environmental history.

title: Seminar in Writing History for Publication
description: Students will take an example of their past or current written work (a seminar paper, a section of a thesis or dissertation) and refine it for publication as a scholarly article. Emphasis will be on writing in a clear, correct, and well-organized way; on sharpening arguments and interpretations; on undertaking additional research in primary and secondary sources; and on critiquing the work of fellow students in a professional manner.

title: Military History Seminar
description: Discusses such problems as command, the military and diplomacy, the officer corps, training, and women in the military.

title: Seminar in American Military Affairs
description: Studies the great writers of American military history and emphasizes student research into such topics as the War for American Independence, limited wars, the Civil War, the role of the President as commander in chief, and the militia.

title: History of Military Thought From Machiavelli to Present
description: The History of Military Thought from Machiavelli to the Present.

title: Introduction to Health Sciences Professions
description: This course provides an overview of careers and professional roles within the health sciences. Students will explore key concepts related to healthcare systems, professional practice, and career pathways. Emphasis is placed on communication, ethics, collaboration, and critical thinking across disciplines. Professionals and faculty from various health fields contribute perspectives to help students understand the diverse opportunities and educational requirements in health-related professions.

title: First Aid/CPR/AED
description: This course is designed to give individuals the knowledge and skills necessary to recognize and provide basic care for breathing and cardiac emergencies, injuries and sudden illnesses, including how to use an automated external defibrillator (AED) for victims of sudden cardiac arrest until advanced medical personnel arrive and take over.

title: Global Healthcare Systems and Public Health
description: Comprehensive review of global healthcare systems and their role within public health, economic, social/ethical, political/legal, technological, and ecological environments.

title: Health Sciences Capstone
description: Capstone experience for coursework only, non-culminating experience MS students. Typically involves a literature review, an application project based on it, and a presentation about both.

title: Freshman Seminar in Hospitality Management
description: A general orientation to the university, college, and department. Students will develop an understanding of Strengths Quest, academic career planning, the enrollment process, basic interviewing skills, resume development, and other issues as they apply to students in the hospitality management program.

title: Introduction to the Hospitality Industry
description: Overview of the hospitality industry. Survey of the history, scope, trends, and career opportunities that comprise the segments of the industry.

title: Food Safety and Protection in Hospitality Operations
description: Overview of environmental issues that impact the hospitality industry. Includes: food safety principles and applications, Hazard Analysis Critical Control Point (HACCP) systems, and governmental regulations in the hospitality industry. Successful completion of the ServSafe Food Protection Manager Exam is required of all students in the course.

title: Environmental Issues In Hospitality
description: Overview of environmental issues that impact the hospitality industry. Includes: food safety principles and applications, Hazard Analysis Critical Control Point (HACCP) systems, conservation of natural resources, solid waste management, air quality, and governmental regulations in the hospitality industry.

title: Issues in Tourism
description: An overview of the history and implications of travel and tourism as social and cultural phenomena. Applying social science perspectives, students explore tourism from the perspectives of travelers and communities, while identifying tourism's economic, socio-cultural, and environmental impacts.

title: Hospitality Work Experience I
description: Planned experience in a hospitality operation; minimum of 200 hours exclusive of course work.

title: Junior Seminar in Hospitality Management
description: This seminar course will emphasize and reinforce important job searching skills including resume writing, interviewing techniques, job searches, communicating with employers before and after interviews, and social media management. This course will also introduce and emphasize critical thinking, research, and presentation skills that are needed for special projects in hospitality firms. After successfully completing the course, student will be able to search and secure an internship/professional position and to design, execute, and present a job related project in a hospitality firm.

title: Operational Research in the Hospitality Industry
description: An applied research course for hospitality operations examining researchable ideas, data collection methods, data analysis, and interpretation and presentation of data, based on activities that occur in food and beverage, lodging, and event operations.

title: Hospitality Service Systems
description: Examination of the complexities of leading quality and service within all segments of the hospitality industry. A focus on developing leadership, problem solving and process management skills, work methods, team development, and evaluation of service systems.

title: Resort and Club Management
description: Exploration of the topics involved in resort and private club management, including operations, ownership and management structures, history and current trends, food and beverage service, marketing, and financial management.Exploration of topics involved in club management. Topics discussed are history and current trends of public and private clubs, food and beverage service, marketing, and event and financial management.

title: Principles of Food Production Management
description: Basic principles and theories of foodservice systems; menu planning; development, standardization, adjustment, and costing of quantity recipes; procurement and production of quality food; foodservice computer applications.

title: Commercial Food Production Management
description: Applications of principles of commercial food production and hospitality service management systems, including procurement, production, and service in a commercial foodservice system; quality and cost controls, employee performance evaluation, process improvement (i.e., work simplification, motion economy), and commercial foodservice equipment operation in the program's commercial foodservice facility.

title: Hotel Operations
description: The development of the lodging industry and current trends. Organization and administration of hotel operations including front desk, housekeeping, laundry, sales/marketing, management, and other positions common to hotel operations.

title: Lodging Practicum
description: Supervised experiences in the operational areas of a lodging property.

title: Event Management
description: This course introduces students to the key players and potential careers in the meeting and event industry. Students will explore various perspectives and responsibilities of event planners. Additionally, basic core concepts essential for planning a meeting will be discussed.

title: The Business of Wedding Consulting
description: This course will introduce the student to the competencies required to become a professional wedding consultant. Students will analyze essential services that provide the necessary infrastructure for a wedding event and will examine the business, creative, and practical aspects of coordinating the wedding environment.

title: Hospitality Work Experience II
description: A 300-hour planned experience that combines observation, and employment within a hospitality operation. Must be completed in the student's declared track.

title: Convention, Meeting, and Event Management Work Experience
Cost Controls In Hospitality Operations
description: Application of accounting principles to analyze control measures used in lodging meeting/conventions/events, and commercial and noncommercial foodservice operations. Includes managerial, financial, and ethical considerations associated with procurement and human resources.

title: Facility Management
description: Evaluation and selection of equipment, maintenance contracts, layout, design, sustainability of hospitality operations, facility renovation, and selection of consultants. Assessment of safety and security measures and development of risk management programs to increase guest/customer safety.

title: Hospitality Sales and Promotion
description: This course guides the student to explore and understand successful sales and sales management behaviors in the hospitality industry. This course will focus on relational selling and include both asking for sales and being a hotel sales manager. Students will develop a comprehension in selling approaches, conversations and presentations, and sales management techniques. Course topics will include hotel sales structures, duties and responsibilities, sales forms, relationships, prospecting, sales call planning, communicating the message, closing the sale, as well as how to motivate, compensate and train sales people specific to the hospitality industry.

title: Fundraising and Nonprofit Events
description: This course introduces students to the basics of fundraising and nonprofit events.  Students will gain an understanding of the similarities and differences across nonprofit events and the key elements that make them successful. Students will build and develop a plan for all facets of fundraising or nonprofit events.

title: Travel and Dining Auction Event Management
description: Application of event planning principles by analyzing previous travel and dining auctions, managing and executing the current year's travel and dining auction, and evaluating the results.

title: Study Abroad Experience in Hospitality Management
description: A Faculty-led Study Abroad Experience in Hospitality Management.

title: Beverage Management
description: The study of historic, social, ethical, physiological, and legal issues relating to alcoholic beverage service and use in contemporary America with emphasis on responsible and knowledgeable service of beer, wine, and spirits in hospitality operations. Course will include beverage tasting and sensory analysis of products commonly served in the beverage industry.

title: Introduction to Wines
description: Overview of wine varietals and major wine regions of the world, tasting and identifying wine varietals based on their specific characteristics, proper purchasing and service of wines, and using wines to complement foods.

title: Advanced Wines
description: An in-depth look at specific wine producing regions and sub-regions, a critical view of wines that define these regions, and preparation for service of wine in the hospitality industry.

title: Business Event Coordination
description: In this course, students will plan a theoretical event from start to completion and learn details pertinent to the organization and execution of a business meeting. Topics include objectives, timelines, finance and contracts, site selection, program development, marketing, and evaluation.

title: Revenue Management
description: Application of revenue management, capacity management, duration control, pricing, and discounting as utilized in the hotel, restaurant, theme park, and car rental industries. Comprehensive examination of the complex factors that influence revenue management strategies and tactics in order to improve decision making and maximize revenue.

title: Catering and Banquet Management
description: Application and principles of catering management, including off-site and on-site functions. Organizational, logistical, aesthetic, and marketing aspects of the catering industry will be utilized and applied.

title: Internship in Hospitality Management
description: Planned and supervised experience in a foodservice or hospitality operation; minimum of 400 hours.

title: Human Resource Management in the Hospitality
description: A focus on the role of the hospitality manager in facilitating, training, and motivating employees. Discussion and analysis of methods to attract, select, develop, motivate, and retain quality employees to promote competitive organizational advantage in the hospitality industry.

title: Golf Course Internship In Hospitality Management
Problems in Hospitality Management
description: Independent study under the supervision of a faculty member.

title: Fair and Festival Management
description: Examination of fairs, festivals, and special events. Understanding of the similarities and differences across different event types and the key elements that make them successful.

title: Hospitality Law
description: Legal aspects of managing hospitality operations and responsibilities for the operations, patron civil rights, governmental regulations, franchising, and commercial transactions.

title: Procurement in the Hospitality Industry
description: Principles and theories of procurement of food and supplies for hospitality operations. Includes management, financial, safety, and ethical considerations in the procurement process.

title: Entrepreneurship in Hospitality Management
description: Development and management of small businesses or private practice within the hospitality industry. Business plan development, marketing, cost considerations. Overview of consulting to healthcare and hospitality operations and examination of skills required for success.

title: Senior Seminar in Hospitality Management
description: The application of critical thinking skills to explore hospitality management problems and perspectives. Decision-making tools will integrate marketing, human resources, diversity, ethics, leadership, team work, strategic management, and financial performance for food service, lodging, and event management.

title: Foodservice Systems Management
description: A capstone course in foodservice management that focuses on the management, analysis, and evaluation of food and beverage systems. Emphasis on planning, coordinating, and improving commercial and noncommercial operations. Development of analytical and decision-making skills for solving managerial and operational problems.

title: Business Event Management
description: A capstone course in meeting and event management. This course will provide students with an opportunity to integrate knowledge, concepts and capacities from different parts of their learning experiences into real-world event planning experiences. This course will enable students to plan an event from initial conception to final evaluation.

title: Advanced Hotel Operations
description: This senior-level course provides students the opportunity to utilize a strategic management process as a decision-making tool that integrates marketing, human resources, operations, and financial performance measures. This is a computer simulation class, where teams will compete with each other in real-time to understand and evaluate the market dynamics of a competitive set of hotels.

title: Casino Management
description: Investigation of the histories and overall operations of casino properties and their impacts on the foodservice and hospitality industry from the social, economical, political and environmental perspectives.

title: Information Technology Systems in Hospitality Operations
description: Review of computer development in foodservice and hospitality operations; development of criteria for implementation of a computer system; analysis of foodservice and hospitality hardware and software.

title: Readings in Hospitality Administration
description: Directed study of current literature in hospitality management and related areas.

title: Advanced Service Management
description: Explores advanced service management strategies involved in optimizing the guest experience in service-intensive industries, with a focus on optimizing operations and driving operational efficiency.

title: Practicum in Hospitality Management
description: Professional experiences in approved foodservice organization as a member of the management team under faculty supervision.

title: Topics in Hospitality Management
description: Presentation of timely, cutting-edge material pertinent to professional practice in hospitality management and dietetics.

title: Advanced Foodservice Management
description: Production planning and controls in foodservice systems analysis in foodservice systems. Decision optimization and systems analysis in foodservice organizations. Consideration of various types of foodservice systems.

title: Research Techniques in Hospitality Management
description: Survey and application of research methodology in foodservice and hospitality management.

title: Advanced Lodging Management
description: This course provides an introduction to conceptual and empirical research in the lodging industry and academic discipline. The course will familiarize students with the historical streams of lodging-focused research and introduce them to the current methods, trends, theory, and application of research in the lodging industry.

title: Problems in Hospitality Management
description: Individual investigation of problems in foodservice and hospitality management. Conferences and reports at appointed hours.

title: Advanced Meetings and Business Event Management
description: This three-hour seminar course will expose students to a variety of higher level event management concepts. By the end of the course, students will be able to complete executive tasks, conduct effective event management research, incorporate findings into applicable strategic plans for the future, and disseminate knowledge to educators, industry, and executives.

title: Survey of Research in Hospitality Management
description: Explores classic and contemporary research to introduce research topics and trends in the hospitality discipline. Underlying theories, research designs, and industry implications of both theoretical and empirical research will be analyzed and critiqued.

title: Seminar in Hospitality Administration
description: Discussions of research related to foodservice and hospitality management.

title: Administration of Hospitality Organizations
description: Advanced study of management applied to foodservice and hospitality organizations.

title: Financial Management and Cost Controls for the Hospitality Industry
description: Overview of fundamental knowledge of financial management, managerial accounting and operational cost controls for the hospitality industry. Important topics include review of managerial accounting concepts based on the Uniform System of Accounts for the Lodging Industry, value and risk analyses, budgeting, asset management, expansion via franchising and management contracts, cost-volume-profit analyses, and operational applications for financial performance.

title: Research in Hospitality Administration
description: Individual research which may form the basis for master's report or thesis.

title: Advanced Hospitality Strategic Management
description: Through lecture, case and literature review and analysis, and projects; Advanced Hospitality Strategic Management introduces students to empirical research in hospitality strategic management and is designed to improve knowledge and abilities to analyze and apply different perspectives of strategic management in the hospitality industry. Topics include but are not limited to environmental scanning techniques, value chain analyses, strategies of choice in variety of business levels, implementation strategies, and maximizing effectiveness of strategic management.

title: Research and Applied Theories in Hospitality Management
description: Critique of theoretical constructs and research applications in the study in foodservice and hospitality management. Emphasis on preliminary stages of research development including preparation of literature reviews and preliminary proposals for research.

title: Higher Education in Hospitality Management
description: An in-depth study of the higher education system in hospitality administration and professional development that prepares for a successful scholar/educator in hospitality education.

title: Advances in Hospitality Management
description: Analysis of selected topics and research in foodservice and hospitality management.

title: Dissertation Proposal Seminar
description: Presentation and discussion of proposals for dissertation research.

title: Teaching Practicum in Hospitality Management
description: This course is designed to serve as a culminating experience in which students apply the teaching and learning strategies learned in coursework through practice in the higher education classroom.  In addition to teaching a subsection of a current course, weekly meetings will discuss relevant topics that affect instructors in the classroom environment.

title: Grantsmanship and Publication
description: Grant writing, identifying external funding, managing grants, preparing manuscripts for peer-reviewed publication, and preparing papers and poster for presentation at professional meetings.

title: Research in Hospitality Administration
description: Research in hospitality management for the doctoral dissertation.

title: Current Issues in Hospitality and Dietetics
Organization And Management Of Foodservice Operations
Seminar In Hotel And Restaurant Management
Management In The Hotel And Restaurant Industry
Leadership in Hospitality and Dietetics Administration
description: Explores classic and contemporary leadership theories and their applications to dietetics, healthcare, and hospitality organizations.

title: Introduction to Horticulture and Natural Resources
description: This course is an introduction to the Horticulture and Natural Resource department.  It is designed to assist students in developing skills and an awareness of resources to promote a successful college experience and prepare them for future career opportunities within the HNR fields.

title: Special Topics in Horticulture and Natural Resources
description: Discussion and lectures on selected topic areas related to Horticulture and Natural Resources

title: Horticulture and Natural Resources Problems
description: Problems and reports in horticulture, park management and conservation, or wildlife and outdoor enterprise management.

title: Horticulture and Natural Resources Topics
description: Lectures and discussion of topics of importance to undergraduate majors in horticulture, park management and conservation, or wildlife and outdoor enterprise management.

title: Advanced Horticulture and Natural Resources Problems
description: Advanced problems and reports in horticulture, park management and conservation, or wildlife and outdoor enterprise management.

title: Pre-Travel Seminar
description: Prepares students for short-term international study through academic, cultural, and logistical orientation. Content includes destination-specific history and culture, discipline-relevant topics, learning objectives, and reflective communication connected to the international experience.

title: Horticulture and Natural Resources Global Learning Experience
description: An immersive, faculty-guided international course in which students apply disciplinary concepts through site visits, field activities, and cultural engagement. Emphasizes experiential learning and structured reflection within the context of the host country.

title: Masters Research
description: Investigations in horticulture, park management and conservation, or wildlife and outdoor enterprise management for preparation of master’s thesis.

title: PhD Research
description: Investigations in horticulture, park management and conservation, or wildlife and outdoor enterprise management. Data collected may form basis for a thesis or dissertation.

title: Preparing for Your Horticulture Career
description: This course is designed to assist students in their personal and professional development. Specifically, students will gain an appreciation for the significance of the horticultural industry while acquiring various skills necessary for a rewarding internship experience and their future professional career. Students will also develop an understanding, awareness and empathy of human diversity as it relates to each individual and the world around them.

title: Principles of Horticultural Science
description: The basic principles of plant science and the environment that apply to horticulture; survey of the industry; plant taxonomy, anatomy, morphology, and physiology; environment and plant growth; plant propagation, pest management, plant breeding.

title: Principles of Horticultural Science Lab
description: The lab for basic principles of plant science and the environment that apply to horticulture; survey of the industry; plant taxonomy, anatomy, morphology, and physiology; environment and plant growth; plant propagation, pest management, plant breeding.

title: Concepts of Floral Design
description: An introduction to the use of flowers and related products with emphasis on fundamentals of design.

title: Human Dimensions of Horticulture
description: Introduction to horticulture applied in schools, psychiatric and medical hospitals, corrections, vocational rehabilitation centers, elderly programs, and consumer horticulture settings. Networking the art and science of horticulture with architecture, business, social sciences, health care, horticulture, and education.

title: Landscape Plants I
description: An introduction to landscape plant identification of trees, shrubs, and cultivated herbaceous cultivated plants, including perennials, annuals, biennials, and bulbs. Topics covered in the course include classification and nomenclature, plant growth characteristics and their landscape use. Weekly labs consist of lengthy walking campus tours to observe and identify plant specimens. A field trip may be required.

title: Horticultural Design I
description: Introduction to the landscape design process including historical perspectives and contemporary issues; development of planning, graphical presentation, and communication skills; and application of design principles to horticultural garden design.

title: Foundations of Horticultural Pest Management
description: Pesticide use and handling, horticultural pesticides and their characteristics, integrated pest management approaches, and pesticide applicator certification.

title: Plants and Society
description: Provides a perspective of horticulture plants and their importance in society and how they are intertwined with everyday activities. Fosters student well-being, highlighting and emphasizing the art and science of growing and using plants to enhance and improve one's environment, through hands-on learning opportunities, while engaging in the plant world.

title: Horticulture Practicum
description: Experiential approach to learning horticulture through teaching. Students will assist faculty with specific horticulture courses.

title: Plant Propagation
description: Designed to develop proficiency in various skills and techniques necessary for propagation of horticultural plants. Basic fundamentals of seed structure and vegetative makeup of plants are emphasized.

title: Public Horticulture
description: In-depth presentation and discussion of techniques and requirements for the management and administration of a public horticultural facility.  Topics include oral and written presentation skills, proposal development, public relations, budgeting, fundraising, dealing with governing boards, interpersonal relationships and managing horticultural operations.

title: Landscape Plants II
description: Continued identification of cultivated landscape tree, shrubs, and herbaceous plants, including perennials, annuals, biennials, and bulbs. Topics covered in the course include classification and botanical nomenclature, plant growth characteristics and their landscape use.

title: Interior Plantscaping
description: A study of the interior plantscaping industry to include identification of plants used in interior environments; designing, installing and maintaining interiorscapes and green walls; and managing interior plantscaping businesses.

title: Horticulture Topics
description: Lectures and discussion of topics of importance to undergraduate majors.

title: Independent Study in Floral Design
description: Independent, supervised study of a topic related to floral design.

title: Water Issues in the Lawn and Landscape
description: This course will examine critical water issues related to irrigation in urbanizing watersheds, with an emphasis on water quality and quantity. Factors impacting water scarcity and quality will be discussed. Successful completion of this course will help students understand the interrelatedness of incorrect irrigation practices and water quality/quantity, and will equip them to help protect water resources through application of science based irrigation practices.

title: Floral Planning for Special Events
description: Operation of a successful event planning floral business for weddings and other special occasions with a focus on the consultation and proposal plan.

title: Introduction to Controlled Environment Agriculture
description: Controlled Environment Agriculture (CEA), including greenhouses, vertical farms, hydroponic and other systems, offers benefits such as year-round production, reduced water and pesticide use, and the ability to grow crops in urban areas. This course introduces and examines current commercial methods of controlled environmental agricultural production. Basic plant physiology, nutrient management, and pest identification and control methods will be investigated. Weekly laboratory sessions provide students valuable hands-on learning opportunities, supporting and enhancing the scientific concepts presented during lectures.

title: Mushroom Production
description: An introduction to the principles and practices of mushroom cultivation within controlled environment agriculture (CEA) systems. Students will explore the biological and environmental factors that influence mushroom growth, including substrate preparation, spawn inoculation, climate regulation, and harvesting techniques. The course emphasizes hands-on learning by providing students with practical experience in growing gourmet and medicinal mushrooms. Sustainability and commercial scalability are central themes, preparing students for small-scale and industrial production settings.

title: CEA Engineering and Technology Systems
description: This course offers a foundational overview of engineering and technological systems used in Controlled Environment Agriculture (CEA). Emphasis is placed on the principles and applications of indoor and greenhouse crop production. Students will examine core components of environmental control, including fertigation, lighting, heating, ventilation, air conditioning (HVAC), and dehumidification. The course highlights the integration of these systems to create optimized growing conditions for sustainable and efficient plant production.

title: Horticulture Internship
description: Principles of commercial or public horticulture activity including exposure to multiple phases of the working horticulture operation.

title: Undergraduate Research in Horticulture
description: Undergraduate students work with faculty mentors on projects involving various elements of research in horticultural science including identifying a problem, designing and conducting experiments, interpreting and communicating the research results. The completed project must be presented at a professional forum, and students are required to write a final project report on completion of their project.

title: The Horticultural Professional
description: Completion of horticulture program assessment tools. Completion of modules associated with professional development during careers in horticultural disciplines, including diversity and ethics in the workplace, job search skills, and providing leadership in the profession.

title: Introduction to Therapeutic Horticulture
description: Examines the principles of therapeutic horticulture and its influence on well-being and targeted applications for special populations.

title: Therapeutic Horticulture for Youth
description: Identifies common diagnoses among youth and strategies for integrating therapeutic horticulture practices into settings where these youth are involved to support positive well-being.

title: Therapeutic Horticulture for Adults
description: Identifies common diagnoses among adults and strategies for integrating therapeutic horticulture practices into settings where these adults are involved. Explains best practices for completing gardening tasks to keep participants safe and improve overall well-being.

title: Therapeutic Gardens and Gardening
description: This course explores the principles, practices and benefits of therapeutic gardens and gardening as tools for promoting physical, emotional and psychological well-being. Students will explore the design elements that make outdoor spaces therapeutic. Through lectures, hands-on activities, case studies and design projects students will learn how to create and use gardens and gardening to support diverse populations with varying needs, skills and experiences.

title: Business of Sustainable Landscape Management
description: Understanding and implementing maintenance requirements for the care of young trees, shrubs and herbaceous plants that focus on practices that address environmental issues related to the management of residential and commercial landscapes. Students will learn how to analyze and apply landscape management practices while creating an attractive landscape in balance with the local climate that requires minimal resource inputs, such as human energy, fertilizers, pesticides and water.

title: Horticultural Design II
description: Reinforcement of the horticultural design process as applied to the use of native and introduced plant materials. Emphasis on functional and aesthetic arrangement of plants in small-scale design to meet site design objectives and adaptation to microclimates.  Students expected to develop site adapted plant palettes and graphic ability to present their design.

title: Basic Turfgrass Culture
description: Turfgrass identification and adaptation; establishment and maintenance of turf for golf courses, sports fields, home lawns, parks, and commercial/institutional grounds.

title: Intensive Culture of Golf and Sports Turf
Golf Course & Sports Turf Operations
description: Strategies involved in golf course and sports turf operation, including development of cultural practices, effective communication, personnel management, and budgeting.

title: Fruit Production
description: Principles and practices of cultivating fruit and nut crops commercially.  Laboratory offers experiences in pomological practices.

title: Horticulture For Special Populations
