description: An intensive study of the concepts and methods of using plants and gardening as therapeutic activities with developmentally disabled, geriatric, economically and socially disadvantaged, emotionally disturbed, or educationally deprived clients.

title: Horticultural Therapy Case Management
description: Guest lecturer and student presentations of topics relating to professionalism, current issues, or goals of horticultural therapy. The course is intended to help students focus expectations and assumptions about a professional career in horticultural therapy and to give them practice in articulating their understanding of the field. Client case management is used as part of career practice.

title: Horticultural Therapy Field Techniques
description: Students under supervision will plan, conduct, and evaluate horticultural therapy activities at Manhattan institutional sites selected according to student's interest. A weekly discussion session addresses evaluation and issues of professionalism.

title: Horticulture Therapy Field Experiences
description: Supervised training at institutions with horticultural therapy programs to gain experience in the application and use of horticultural activities for special populations. Six months (1,000) hours continuous internships required in psychiatric and correctional programs.

title: Hydroponic Food Production
description: This course explores hydroponic crop production in the United States, covering production of tomatoes, cucumbers, peppers, leafy greens, strawberries, herbs, cut flowers, and microgreens in a variety of production systems, including aquaponics, used in controlled environment agriculture. Topics include system design and construction considerations, nutrient management, pollination and pest management in hydroponics, sustainability of the systems and safe food handling practices.

title: Hydroponic Food Production Lab
description: This laboratory course consists of hands-on hydroponic crop production of tomatoes, cucumbers, peppers, leafy greens, strawberries, herbs, cut flowers, and microgreens in a variety of production systems, including aquaponics, used in controlled environment agriculture. Topics include system design and construction considerations, nutrient management, pollination and pest management in hydroponics, sustainability of the systems and safe food handling practices.

title: Computer Applications In Horticultural Design
description: Introduction to a variety of computer software packages that students may encounter in the nursery/garden center environment. These include planning, routing, and estimating packages.

title: Horticultural Irrigation Systems
description: Application of the principles and practices of horticultural irrigation which involve the various aspects of irrigation system installation, maintenance and scheduling, electrical troubleshooting, basic hydraulics, and drip irrigation as these topics pertain to residential and commercial landscapes, recreational turf areas, and greenhouse and field production systems.

title: Landscape Contracting and Construction
description: The study of contracts and specifications, and the bidding and estimating processes as applied to landscape contracting.  The interpretation and implementation of small-scale landscape construction (hardscape) projects.

title: Horticultural Landscape Construction
description: The interpretation and implementation of small-scale landscape construction (hardscape) projects.

title: Vegetable Crop Production
description: Study of production principles and cultural practices involved in the growing of vegetable crops.

title: Indoor Vertical Farming Systems
description: Indoor Vertical Farming Systems is a lecture-based course focused on all aspects of indoor food crop production in environments with no natural sunlight such as in plant factories. Topics include lighting and HVAC design; managing humidity, temperature, and carbon dioxide; energy efficiency; product logistics and food safety; and operation profitability. The application of indoor farming systems is beneficial where land and water resources are limited, such as in urban areas.

title: Advanced Culture of Golf and Sports Turf
description: Advanced topics in golf and sports turf maintenance focusing on construction methods and management of abiotic and biotic stresses.

title: Indoor Vertical Farming Systems Lab
description: Indoor Vertical Farming Systems Lab is a lab-based course that provides experiential learning in tandem with HORT 460 Indoor Vertical Farming Systems lecture course. The lab is focused on experiencing the daily tasks of growing food crops in smart tents by managing all environmental parameters. Field trips to commercial vertical farms will also be taken.

title: Greenhouse Operations Management
description: Greenhouse systems operations and management including greenhouse layout; structures; glazing materials; heating, ventilation, irrigation, lighting, benching, growing medium handling, and fertilization systems; traffic flow; crop handling, processing and shipping.

title: Nursery Management
description: A study of the various practices and methods of operating a commercial nursery for the production of woody ornamental plants for the landscaping industry. Nursery layout, planting systems, nutrition management, plant maintenance, and harvesting technologies will be discussed.

title: Advanced Horticultural Design
description: Emphasis is on horticultural design projects with clients, working with the design process, design articulation and communication with the clients.

title: Survey of Horticultural Ornamental and Food Crop Pests
Arboriculture
description: Principles and practices of maintaining shade and ornamental trees under urban environments.

title: Turfgrass Diseases and Their Management
description: Biology, diagnosis and integrated management of turfgrass diseases.  Emphasis is on diseases of intensively maintained turfgrass.

title: Turfgrass Weeds and Their Management
description: Biology, diagnosis and integrated management of turfgrass weeds.

title: Turfgrass Insects and Their Management
description: Biology, diagnosis and integrated management of turfgrass insect pests.

title: Small Farm Practicum
description: Project-based practical experience in small-scale, diversified, agricultural production.

title: Horticulture Study Abroad
description: Seminar and travel course designed to prepare students before an international study abroad experience focused on horticulture. Upon completion of travel, students will analyze, critique, and report their experiences. In addition, students will be introduced and immersed in historical components, both disciplinary and culturally.

title: Herbaceous Plant Production
description: The principles and commercial practices for producing annual and herbaceous perennial plants from seed and cuttings, including vegetable and herb crops in controlled environments. Analysis of crop production costs and plant growth regulation will be emphasized.

title: Floral Crops Production and Handling
description: The principles and commercial practices for producing floral potted crops and fresh flowers crops, emphasizing the physical responses of plants to their environment. Aspects of postharvest physiology of fresh flowers will be covered.

title: General Viticulture
description: Focus is on aspects of grapes, from vine anatomy to final products produced from them.  Includes cultivars, propagation, canopy management, diseases, weed control, physiology, anatomy, irrigation, wine production, climates and soils.

title: Horticultural Problems
description: Problems and reports in floriculture, olericulture, ornamental horticulture, pomology, turfgrass and horticultural therapy.

title: Agrarian Traditions: Literature Shaping Modern Food Movements
description: Examines the Agrarian literary and philosophical traditions to better understand the drivers of food and agriculture movements in the United States and globally. Studies Virgil, Sir Albert Howard, Aldo Leopold, and Wendell Berry, among others. Analyzes texts both within their historical contexts and as they apply to modern food movements.

title: Introduction to Permaculture
description: Exploration of a thinking/design methodology that seeks to provide for the physical needs of humans, including food, water, shelter, energy, etc. while doing so in an environmentally-friendly, sustainable manner.

title: Introduction to Therapeutic Horticulture
description: Examines the principles of therapeutic horticulture and its influence on well-being and targeted applications for special populations.

title: Therapeutic Horticulture for Youth
description: Identifies common diagnoses among youth and strategies for integrating therapeutic horticulture practices into settings where these youth are involved to support positive well-being.

title: Therapeutic Horticulture for Adults
description: Identifies common diagnoses among adults and strategies for integrating therapeutic horticulture practices into settings where these adults are involved. Explains best practices for completing gardening tasks to keep participants safe and improve overall well-being.

title: Therapeutic Gardens and Gardening
description: This course explores the principles, practices and benefits of therapeutic gardens and gardening as tools for promoting physical, emotional and psychological well-being. Students will explore the design elements that make outdoor spaces therapeutic. Through lectures, hands-on activities, case studies and design projects students will learn how to create and use gardens and gardening to support diverse populations with varying needs, skills and experiences.

title: Plant Cell, Tissue and Organ Culture
description: Course will cover the principles and laboratory exercises that demonstrate major concepts and practical techniques in plant cell, tissue and organ culture. The history and use of plant cell-, tissue-, and organ-culture for crop improvement will be explained. The variety of tissue-culture techniques will be highlighted. Selected readings and practical tissue culture projects will be required.

title: Advanced Interiorscaping
description: Focus is the physiological principles and industry practices in the production, moving, care, and maintenance of interior plants.  This course will provide students the career tools to design, install and maintain interior plantscapes through knowledge of interior plant physiology, care and maintenance.

title: Environmental Nursery Production
description: Cultural practices used with nursery production will be presented with focus on the adoption of best management practices, conservation of resources, scientific research-based investigations related to nursery cultural practices, potential risks to nursery personnel, and off-site movement of air-borne materials and effluents to surrounding areas and public watersheds.

title: Postharvest Technology and Physiology of Horticultural Crops
description: A study of the principles and practices involved in the harvesting, handling and storage of horticultural products. The relationship of plant structure and physiology will be emphasized in discussing effects of postharvest handling and storage to maximize quality and shelf life of products.

title: Hydroponic Food Production
description: This course explores hydroponic crop production in the United States, covering production of tomatoes, cucumbers, peppers, leafy greens, strawberries, herbs, cut flowers, and microgreens in a variety of production systems, including aquaponics, used in controlled environment agriculture. Topics include system design and construction considerations, nutrient management, pollination and pest management in hydroponics, sustainability of the systems and safe food handling practices.

title: Hydroponic Food Production Lab
description: This laboratory course consists of hands-on hydroponic crop production of tomatoes, cucumbers, peppers, leafy greens, strawberries, herbs, cut flowers, and microgreens in a variety of production systems, including aquaponics, used in controlled environment agriculture. Topics include system design and construction considerations, nutrient management, pollination and pest management in hydroponics, sustainability of the systems and safe food handling practices.

title: Human Issues in Horticultural Therapy
description: This course will explore human issues in horticulture generally, and human issues in horticultural therapy specifically. Consideration of selected topics and issues such as people-plant relationship theories, horticultural therapy as a specialized allied health field, health-care gardens and their use, societal impacts on horticultural therapy, and research issues. Provides students with a knowledge and understanding of the literature in horticultural therapy and develops an appreciation of the interrelationships involved in this multidisciplinary area.

title: Horticulture in Horticultural Therapy
description: Application of horticulture principles and practices in therapeutic settings; considerations for greenhouse systems operations and management in therapeutic settings; landscape maintenance, fruit and vegetable gardening, and production horticulture as part of a therapeutic program; and plant propagation for production and therapy.

title: Clinical Skills in Horticultural Therapy
description: Development of horticultural therapy skills and methodology is facilitated through real-life case studies. Includes instruction, practice, and evaluation of clinical skills with population groups typically served through horticultural therapy.

title: Practicum in Horticultural Therapy
description: The practicum is a means of demonstrating the interconnections between 'theory' and 'practice'. By conducting a practicum project, students demonstrate the ability to apply concepts and ideas from their study of horticultural therapy to a specific population or setting.

title: Business Management for Horticultural Enterprises
description: Focus is on developing a detailed business plan for the service, design and production businesses in horticulture that incorporates considerations of start-up capitalization, insurance, investments, legal accounting and employee compensation.  Strategic decision-making and aspects of a horticulture firms that are unique to its industries, such as product seasonality and perishability, will be discussed.

title: Indoor Vertical Farming Systems
description: Indoor Vertical Farming Systems is a lecture-based course focused on all aspects of indoor food crop production in environments with no natural sunlight such as in plant factories. Topics include lighting and HVAC design; managing humidity, temperature, and carbon dioxide; energy efficiency; product logistics and food safety; and operation profitability. The application of indoor farming systems is beneficial where land and water resources are limited, such as in urban areas.

title: Indoor Vertical Farming Systems Lab
description: Indoor Vertical Farming Systems Lab is a lab-based course that provides experiential learning in tandem with HORT 760 Indoor Vertical Farming Systems lecture course. The lab is focused on experiencing the daily tasks of growing food crops in smart tents by managing all environmental parameters. Field trips to commercial vertical farms will also be taken.

title: Plant Breeding Methods in Horticulture
description: Focus is on introductory plant breeding principles with emphasis on traditional methods of developing improved cultivars of crosspollinated, self-pollinated, and asexually-propagated horticultural crops, and the genetic principles on which breeding methods are based.  The course provides a general background in all areas of plant breeding as a foundation for mastering more complex breeding principles.

title: Health-Promoting Phytochemicals: Fruits and Vegetables
description: The course deals with various aspects of phytochemicals in plant-based foods including fruits and vegetables and their impact on human health and well-being. It includes potential effects of phytochemicals in promoting human health, preventing various diseases and fostering wellness. It also includes biosynthesis and metabolism of phytochemicals in plants. Emphasis is placed on developing strategies to improve the phytochemical content of food crops through approaches involving crop management, environmental and biotechnology tools. Two hours lecture per week.

title: Urban Agriculture
description: Students will become familiar with a wide variety of urban agriculture types and production systems utilized in urban settings. The course will include background readings, case studies, guest speakers, student-facilitated class discussions, and lectures.

title: Handling And Processing Fruit And Vegetables
Farm to Fork Produce Safety
description: This course will cover all aspects of food safety for fresh produce grown in urban and rural environments, including pathogen ecology and production aspects as well as pre-harvest and postharvest factors that influence the risk of microbial contamination. More specifically, we will discuss ways to minimize the risk of human pathogens on fresh produce using strategies such as the implementation of Good Agricultural Practices (GAPs) and Good Handling Practices (GHPs). The course will cover postharvest interventions that are currently used (chemical sanitizers) as well as innovative technology applications like physical treatments, irradiation, and biological control techniques. Additionally, students will explore the impact of food borne outbreaks on public health and the fresh produce industry in terms of economics, consumer acceptance, and legal aspects.

title: Urban Food Systems
description: This course will cover all components of urban food systems through the lens of food security, food justice, access, policy, and community planning.  Students will gain skills in grant-writing, non-profit planning and management, and working with urban policy and planning boards.

title: Professional Development in Urban Food Systems
description: A team instructed seminar course that provides urban food systems students with a suite of skills complimentary to their research experience but still necessary for professional career success.

title: Horticultural Physiology
description: Discussions of recent advances in horticultural crop plant physiology, including improvements in horticultural crops resulting from applications of molecular biology and biotechnology.

title: Plant Nutrition and Nutrient Management
description: Focuses on the macro and micronutrient elements and their function in the growth and development of plants. Emphasis will be placed on the roles of single elements, interactions/balances between elements, and nutrient deficiency/toxicity symptoms as they affect the physiology of the whole plant and management of nutrient applications. The relationships between crop nutrition and production and environmental considerations (yield, drought, temperature, pests) will be explored.

title: Plant Research Methods
description: Review of history and forms of plant science literature. Discussion on selecting experimental procedures, interpreting data, and reporting results.

title: Topics in Horticulture
description: Discussion and lectures of important papers and contributions in this field.

title: Master's Report
description: Investigations in pomology, olericulture, floriculture, ornamental horticulture, turfgrass, or horticultural therapy for preparation of master's report.

title: Masters Research
description: Investigations in pomology, olericulture, floriculture, ornamental horticulture, turfgrass, or horticultural therapy for preparation of master's thesis.

title: Advances in Plant Cell Culture
description: Course will cover current topics in plant cell culture, applications and commercial practices, with emphasis on plant genetic improvement. Research-level skills in this area will be taught.

title: Plant Regulators In Horticulture
description: A study of synthetic plant regulators used to initiate, induce, promote, inhibit, or alter characteristics of horticultural plants and crops. Included are kinds and types of exogenous plant regulators used on crops, their activity, plant responses, benefits and problems, and application technology.

title: Graduate Seminar
description: Student presentations and discussion of investigational works in the various disciplines of Horticulture and Natural Resources.

title: Environmental Plant Stress
description: Physiological, biochemical and morphological factors involved in stress development and resistance will be discussed.

title: Topics in Horticultural Therapy
description: Discussion and lectures on important papers and contributions in horticultural therapy.

title: Research in Horticulture,Phd
description: Investigations in pomology, olericulture, floriculture, ornamental horticulture, and turfgrass. Data collected may form basis for a thesis or dissertation.

title: Teaching Health in Elementary and Secondary Schools
Admin Health Phys Ed in Elem and Sec Schools
Writing Behavioral Objectives for Phys Ed
Administration of School Health Education Programs
Field Experience In Dietetics And Institutional Management
Cost Controls In Hotel And Restaurant Operations
Fundamentals Of Public Health And Food Safety
Seminar In Hotel, Restaurant Management And Dietetics
Procurement For Foodservice And Hospitality Operations
Design and Material Culture
description: This course examines contemporary topics within material culture and design across the three domains of practice and scales: Interior Architecture, Furniture Design, and Industrial Design. It serves as an introduction to the disciplinary topics that are shaping current and future design practice. The course begins with a chronological survey of design movements and their grounding theories which serves to frame a deeper thematic discussion around the three major course topics: environmental sustainability, health, and equity.

title: History of Furniture
description: Exploration of furniture history from prehistory to the present. Focus on socio-cultural, architectural, technological, material culture, manufacturing, spiritual, aesthetic, sustainable factors over the human timeline.

title: Design Workshop I
description: An introduction to the principles of furniture design, with special emphasis on material properties, fabrication methods, tectonics, and structural considerations of furniture design. The course also serves as an introduction to best-practice techniques for safely using the tools and equipment within the Fabrication Lab. Through a series of initial exercises students will develop a versatile foundation in woodworking and metalworking skills that will be applied to the design and fabrication of small-scale furniture projects.

title: Visual Communication I
description: Seeks to provide a basic understanding of design visualization and presentation in both traditional and digital applications. A two and three-dimensional sensitivity is developed through a series of projects and lectures utilizing the principles of proportion, scale, hierarchy, balance, movement, line, plane, volume, texture, color and an introduction of vector and raster graphics software.

title: Visual Communication II
description: Provides an advanced understanding of design visualization and presentation. Introduces a variety of 3D modeling and rendering software commonly used in the Industrial and Product Design field. Explores digital form development, materials representation, perceptual education and visual communication.

title: Visual Communication III
description: Explores advanced techniques in design visualization and presentation. Advanced techniques in image generation and editing using raster and vector tools. Advanced topics in layout and typesetting. Introduction to time-based media, including audio and video editing and 2D animation. Introduction to web and interaction design.

title: Introduction to Industrial Design
description: Introduction to the Profession of Product/Industrial Design including history; major movements; key practitioners; socio-cultural, architectural and technological drivers; unique design/make processes and methodologies; materials and manufacturing techniques; and, contemporary issues.

title: Furniture Design II
description: This course continues to enhance and increase the understanding of a human-centered approach to the design and prototyping of furniture, while focusing more deeply on function and context. Students will develop and practice intermediate woodworking and metalworking techniques and skills. The topics of the course may also include contextual and functional analysis for furniture design, sustainable practices, intermediate structural considerations,and an introduction to human factors as they relate to the design of furniture and accessories.

title: Problems in IAID
description: Study of specific problems related to Interior Architecture, Industrial Design, or Furniture Design.

title: Topics in Furniture Design
description: Study of specific furniture design topics.

title: Production Furniture for the Contract Market Research
description: Introduction and implementation of market research; client end-user research; manufacturing implications; BIFMA and other testing requirements; and, competitive market analysis and implications to develop a complete ""design brief"" for a line of contract furniture within a major manufacturer's existing line.

title: Production Furniture for the Contract Market Design
description: This course builds upon the knowledge gained and the design brief developed in IARC 680, Production Furniture for the Contract Market Research and develops the designers' understanding of intersecting forces affecting the design of a line of furniture, from conception to final production. Students work in teams with a manufacturer in the design and development of a line/family of products-expectations of design, prototyping, royalties and licensing-as well as the manufacturing processes and knowledge of effectively specifying furnishings.

title: Professional Practice: Professional Responsibility
description: Interdisciplinary course addresses the connections between allied design professions and the larger community.  It helps students to understand and appreciate their professional legal and ethical responsibilities.

title: Professional Practice: Office Practices
description: Interdisciplinary course addresses practice-based issues concerning professional relationships in the design and delivery process, management of projects, communication practices, and fiscal concerns.

title: Furniture Des III
description: Advanced exploration of contemporary topics in furniture design. Students explore innovative design solutions through an iterative, inclusive, and human-centered design process. There is a special emphasis on the generation of multiple mock-ups and prototypes to explore and test their design solutions.

title: Advanced Furniture Design Studio
description: Advanced design, construction, and finishing of contemporary furniture. Students explore projects programmed under the previous semester's Advanced Studio Programming. Projects must be relevant in a contemporary socio-cultural, architectural and technological context; employ rigorous design research processes and methodologies; identify user and usability needs/ desires; accommodate market opportunities and constraints; explore multiple conceptual solutions in a variety of hand and digital media; and demonstrate experimentation in the design of spatial components utilizing advanced construction techniques and materials through the development and documentation of models and prototypes. A book, oral and visual presentation and display materials for exhibition of the student's work will be required at the completion of the course.

title: Contemporary Design Seminar
description: Seminar on current interior architecture, product design and furniture design that illustrate principles and practical concerns that motivate design with readings, class discussion, student presentations, research papers or projects.

title: Interior Arch Design Studio I Lec
Advanced Interior Architectural Design
description: Advanced study of interior space planning, product design, and interior design.

title: Fundamentals of Architectural Technology
description: Instruction in the fundamentals of architectural technology with emphasis on the concepts and principles necessary for developing an integrated approach to building design.

title: Design & Material Culture
description: People's personal characteristics or social position can be expressed by the products they purchase, the possessions they own, and the physical environments they create. Design has become a major factor influencing one's final decision for purchasing these products. This course examines material culture which is created from the influences of human behavior, design, history, marketing, economics, politics, geography, the arts, and sociology.

title: IARC Design Studio I
description: Introduction to the Interior Architecture design process. Generation of design concept and design alternatives for small-scale projects. Introduction to basic design theory, beginning design research, human scale, behavior, ergonomics and anthropometrics as they affect design decisions. Introduction of structure and environmental analysis as informants and determinants of design. Focus on multiple graphic representations and compositions, including diagramming, sketching, freehand drawing, and enhancing through digital means.

title: IARC Digital Applications
description: This course addresses current digital media as it is used by the profession to draft and visualize design projects. Survey a wide variety of contemporary design and visualization software. The intention is not mastery of any single software but instead to form an understanding of the interface and use of the tools and when they are appropriate. Enable the thoughtful discussion and understanding of design representation and process in both drafting and visualization. Enable and empower the student to comfortably engage design software as they continue to evolve.

title: IARC Design Studio II
description: This studio introduces the principles of design, design terminology, and the values of a human centered design philosophy for the interior environment. Significant emphasis will be on the theoretical underpinnings of project ideation and development; and formal design processes and decision-making. Oral, graphic and visual communication skills and model making techniques will also be stressed to assist students in representing their design ideas.

title: Topics in Contemporary Design Seminar
description: Seminar on current interior architecture, industrial design, and furniture design that illustrate principles and practical concerns that motivate design with readings, class discussion, student presentations, research papers or projects.

title: Problems in IARC
description: Study of specific interior architectural problems under direct supervision of a member of the department.

title: Materials and Finishes
description: Introduction to materials, textiles, and finishes specific to interior applications. Criteria for evaluation, selection, and application of interior materials and finishes with the building fabric and their impact on building design. Preparation of written and graphic communications to illustrate and direct the construction process.

title: Design Workshop I Studio
description: Exploration of design alternatives; process, presentation and construction graphics communicating design.

title: IARC Design Studio III
description: This studio builds on the knowledge and skills developed in IARC 320. It provides an in-depth understanding of the design methodologies and principles of interior architecture enhanced with an introduction to evidence-based design through the principles of environment-behavior, review and analysis of historic precedents, user needs, and the impact of shapes and forms, materials, lighting and color.

title: IARC Design Studio IV
description: This course will build upon and extend the knowledge and skill base gained by students in previous studios, and will include the introduction of programming methodology and its relationship to the design and organization of interior space. Emphasis will be placed on an understanding of how human behavior affects the designed environment, including the elements and objects that shape it such as the appropriate selection of furniture, finishes, fixtures, and equipment within the context of their relationships to form, function, task and users' needs.

title: Design Workshop II Studio
description: Studio component of Design Workshop II. Exploration of design alternatives; process, presentation and construction graphics, students increase their proficiency at communicating ideas through working and presentation drawings for projects to be created in IARC 802: Design Workshop II.

title: Problems in IARC
description: Study of specific problems related to Interior Architecture.

title: IARC Design Studio VI
description: This semester of study provides an option for an interdisciplinary collaborative studio course oriented towards replicating the learning experience and interactive activities that takes place in the modern multidisciplinary professional office or a validated equivalent studio completed in an approved study program.

title: Lighting
description: An examination of design and technical issues related to luminous environments in building interiors. Topics include color and light, vision, light sources, luminaire selection and design, and the evaluation of lighting solutions.

title: Construction Documentation
description: A technical capstone course that explores the development of construction documentation for interior architecture projects includes demolition, partition, and reflected ceiling plans, power and communications plans, finish and furnishings plans, interior elevations, sections, details, and schedules. Understanding of the mechanical, electrical, plumbing and structural systems, as well as building and life-safety codes, and the Americans with Disabilities Act will be demonstrated through completion of the set of drawings.

title: Internship
description: Fifteen week internship in a professional environment with a focus on interior architecture, product/industrial design and/or furniture design: office and field experience.

title: Internship Report
description: Development of the student's analysis; observation, and communication skills in relationship to the professional office and field environment. Detailed documentation, online and on-campus, of the experiences encountered during internship, allowing classmates to learn from their peer's varied experiences as well as provide a detailed documentation of the student's work during internship.

title: Summer Internship
description: Ten week internship in a professional environment with a focus on interior architecture, industrial/product design and/or furniture design: office and field experience.

title: Summer Internship Report
description: Development of the student's analysis, observation, and communication skills in relationship to the professional office and field environment. Detailed documentation, online and on-campus, of the experiences encountered during internship, allowing classmates to learn from their peer's varied experiences as well as provide a detailed documentation of the student's work during internship.

title: Advanced 3-D Computer Modeling
description: Lighting, materials, animation, advanced modeling techniques, sound, and storyboarding are methodologies required to successfully build a digital presentation for projection in a two dimensional, or 3-dimensional environment using the vision domes. Projects that utilize these topics will include an introduction to 3D modeling, animation, sound, titling, lighting, and a material editor.

title: Professional Practice: Discipline-Specific Topics
description: Covers discipline-specific concerns, focusing on issues specific to architecture, interior architecture and product design, landscape architecture or regional and community planning.

title: IARC Seminar
description: Readings and discussion of contemporary thought and movements within the field of interior architecture design with special emphasis on the societal factors that produce and effect change.

title: IARC Design Studio V
description: This semester of study provides an option for an interdisciplinary collaborative studio course oriented towards replicating the learning experience and interactive activities that take place in the modern multi-disciplinary professional office.

title: Design Workshop II
description: Design Workshop II is intended to further develop the student's understanding of the three dimensional design process through research, design, prototype construction, evaluation and redesign. Enhance and increase the student's understanding of the structural characteristics of materials.

title: Design Workshop II Studio
description: Studio component of Design Workshop II. Exploration of design alternatives, process, presentation and construction graphics, students increase their proficiency at communicating ideas through working and presentation drawings for projects to be created in IARC 802: Design Workshop II.

title: IARC Capstone Studio
description: Students are to pursue design projects that encompass their previous educational experience validating their knowledge of the IARC program studies. The project will be based on extensive research and review. The project presentation will suggest unique and applicable solutions with clearly identified methodology of implementation.

title: Design Research
description: Identify evaluation criteria appropriate for design research and problem solving, analysis of literature, construction of problem statements, development and documentation of design problem proposals and reports.

title: Furniture Des Workshop Studio
description: Studio component of IARC 814 Furniture Design Workshop. Exploration of contemporary design alternatives, students explore innovation and demonstrate knowledge through design processes and including process, presentation and construction graphics for projects to be created in IARC 814: Furniture Design Workshop.

title: Advanced Studio Programming
description: Research of programming methodologies culminating in a programmatic document used as the basis for the design project in IARC 822, IARC 823 or IARC 824.

title: Advanced Product Design Studio
description: Students explore projects programmed under the previous semester IARC 815 Advanced Studio Programming. Projects must be relevant in a contemporary socio-cultural, architectural and technological context; employ rigorous design research processes and methodologies; identify user and usability needs/desires; accommodate market opportunities and constraints; explore multiple conceptual solutions in a variety of hand and digital media; build and document models and prototypes; and estimate material and manufacturing costs. A book, oral and visual presentation, and display materials for exhibition of the student's work will be required at the completion of the course.

title: Advanced Interior Architecture Design Studio
description: Students explore projects programmed under the previous semester IAPD 815 Advanced Studio Programming. Projects focus on interior architecture represent a suitable design relevancy and proper academic rigor on the level of graduate design study. Design solutions must be relevant in a contemporary socio-cultural and architectural context; employ rigorous design research processes and methodologies; respond to user needs and desires; explore multiple innovative conceptual solutions in a variety of hand and digital media; and illustrate advanced knowledge and application of materiality, understanding of structural and building environmental systems' implication on interior architecture, and the selection or design of furnishings that enhance the goals for the spaces.

title: Problems in IARC
description: Study of specific interior architectural problems under direct supervision of the departmental staff.

title: Our Sustainable World: Current Challenges and Future Opportunities
Resilient Systems for a Sustainable World
description: Study of resilience in environmental, economic, and social systems to foster human wellbeing from a human ecology perspective. Examines theories and approaches that create resilient systems resulting in ecological health, economic welfare, and social justice.

title: Leading Change for a Sustainable World
description: Study of skills, techniques, and methods of leading groups to overcome challenges to human wellbeing associated with the climate crisis from a human ecology perspective. Students in the course will learn and apply effective leadership approaches and processes and develop individual skills of effective change leadership.

title: Design And Behavior In The Interior Environment
Construction Methods And Materials For Interior Design
Computer-Aided Design And Drafting For Interior Design
Problems In Interior Design And Housing
Advanced Design And Behavior In The Interior Environment
Historic Preservation And Restoration Of Interiors
Problems In Interior Design And Housing
Readings In Interior Design And Housing
Problems In Interior Design And Housing
Practicum In Interior Design And Housing
Research In Interior Design And Housing
First Year ID Student Seminar
description: First Year Seminar is structured to engage and support students during their first semester of study. The course is focused on learning strategies and resources that build toward successful outcomes for the remainder of their college studies.

title: Design and Behavior in the Interior Environment
description: Developing awareness of aesthetic and behavioral relationships fundamental to interior design.

title: Interior Design Studio 1
description: Introduction to two-dimensional and three-dimensional design fundamentals including design elements and principles, the design process,  interior design theory and its application, and fundamental design communication methods including drawing and model building.

title: Interior Design Studio 2
description: Exploration of two-dimensional and three-dimensional design fundamentals. Introduction to color theory in spatial design. Introduction to light, shade, and shadow. Introduction to rendering, constructed perspective, and paraline drawing.

title: Global Experiences in Interior Design
description: An experiential learning course that prepares students for and immerses them in an international study tour designed to enhance their global and cultural awareness. Lectures and tours target important interior/architectural design, furniture, and objects of material culture.

title: Construction Methods and Materials for Interior Design
description: Introduction to theories, concepts, selection, and application of construction processes, materials, and finishes.  Introduction to codes, regulations, representation of construction methods, and sustainability.

title: Digital Visualization I
description: Introduction to and application of digital technologies used by interior design professionals for 2D drafting and presentation layout.

title: History of Interior Design I
description: A historic survey of all aspects of the built environment, including architecture, furniture, textiles, and the minor arts from antiquity to 1850.

title: Interior Design Studio 3
description: Advanced spatial design fundamentals.  Application of human factors and theories of human behavior in the built environment.  Selection of materials and colors.  Emphasis on design conceptualization and creativity.  Advanced visual communication methods including perspective drawing and rendering and other rapid visualization skills.

title: Textiles for Interior Design
description: Introduction to textiles for interior environments, with a focus on the fabrication and application of materials designed specifically for use as upholstery, window coverings, soft floor coverings, wall coverings, panel systems, and other miscellaneous interior applications.  Also includes the regulatory, environmental, and human health impacts of textile production, application, and maintenance.

title: Interior Design Studio 4
description: Application of interior design theory and evidence-based design in small scale projects. Introduction of project programming for interior design. Application of codes and regulations. Continued emphasis on design communication skills.

title: History of Interior Design II
description: A survey of design evolution of architecture, interiors, furniture, textiles, and the minor arts from 1850 to the present.

title: Interior Design Practicum
description: Off-campus professional experience in an appropriate design-related setting.

title: Housing and Its Environment
description: Socioeconomic, political-legal, and consumer overview of housing. Includes individual, family, and public decisions related to residential alternatives, their acquisition, and housing and environmental standards.

title: Digital Visualization II
description: Introduction to and application of digital technologies used by interior design professionals for 3D representation and rendering of design solutions.

title: Interior Design Studio 5
description: Application of theory and research in medium scale projects. Focus on construction systems, materials, and methods. Continued emphasis on communication skills, programming, spatial design, and application of codes and regulations.

title: Systems and Technology for Interior Design I
description: Introduction to the principles of lighting, daylighting, acoustics, water supply, sanitation, thermal comfort, and indoor air quality to enhance human wellbeing.

title: Systems and Technology for Interior Design II
description: Integration and application of all building systems with a focus on new technology and support of human wellbeing.

title: Interior Design Studio 6
description: Development of working drawings and specifications for projects integrating building systems and interior construction.

title: Interior Design Practices and Procedures
description: Ethics, business procedures, and professional development; contract services and administration; and preparation for job market entry as applied to the practice of interior design.

title: Interior Design Studio 7
description: Application of design theory to create solutions for complex, large-scale interior spaces. Projects require implementation of all phases of the design process. Students will gain greater understanding of the interior designer's responsibilities within the context of the design professions.

title: Interior Design Internship
description: Supervised off-campus professional experience in appropriate design-related firms, government agencies, or the housing industry.

title: Interior Design Study Tour
description: Supervised off-campus tour to acquaint the student with rich artistic and cultural locations around the world and expand student's global perspectives of the design profession. Lectures and tours target important interior/architectural design and furniture collections.

title: Topics in Advanced Interior Design Theory
description: An examination of contemporary social and cultural influences on design theory and applications within the interior design profession.

title: Interior Design Studio 8
description: Application of design theory to create solutions for complex, large-scale interior spaces. Projects will conform to professional standards regarding design process, protection of health, safety, and welfare, and construction methods.

title: Advanced Design and Behavior in the Interior Environment
description: The design of interior environments explored in an ecological, behavioral, and cultural context.

title: Design for Supportive Environments
description: Analysis of the age and ability related needs and challenges faced by children, older adults, and persons with disabilities. Team approaches to providing living and work environments that accommodate both universal and special human needs.

title: Advanced Kitchen and Bath Design
description: Functional and research basis for planning and arranging based on activity analysis, equipment, materials, lighting, and ventilation.

title: Historic Preservation and Restoration of Interiors
description: Principles, guidelines, and qualities of preservation and restoration of interiors. Research and application.

title: Advanced Independent Study In Industrial Engineering
Simulation Of Industrial And Management Systems
Advanced Topics In Computer Integrated Manufacturing
Introduction to Integrative Human Sciences
description: This course will provide the foundation for students in the IHS major. The course will introduce students to the framework that guides the discipline and the curriculum.

title: Topics in Integrative Human Sciences I
description: Exploration of topics in human science/family and consumer sciences.

title: Foundations of Integrative Human Sciences
description: Examination of the Family and Consumer Sciences Body of Knowledge Framework and application to professional practice and ethical decision-making.

title: Integrative Human Sciences Applied Learning Orientation
description: Provides support and guidance for students as they select an applied learning experience specific to their personal and professional goals.

title: IHS Applied Learning Experience
description: Learners will participate in applied learning experiences specific to their personal and professional goals. These experiences will be designed to meet the needs of the individual learner and provide an opportunity for application of skills and knowledge; reflection on professional practice and preparation for roles as future leaders and change agents.

title: Integrative Human Sciences Seminar
description: Application of career and professionals skills relevant to students majoring in Integrated Human Sciences. Specific focus on the application of the FCS Body of Knowledge to professional practice.

title: Community Health Practicum
description: This practicum is for students who seek a community health field experience. Study, experience with, and reflection on the factors that contribute to health issues and what communities and small groups can do to overcome these issues is required. Exploration of lifestyle behaviors related to health risks, and how where we live, learn, work, and play affects overall health is emphasized through field experiences determined by the student and the practicum advisor.

title: Family and Consumer Science Methods for Secondary and Middle Schools
description: Principles of teaching applied to family and consumer sciences instruction in the secondary and middle schools including: motivation, organization of subject matter, lesson/unit planning, assessment and reporting, organization and management of the classroom, and methodology and materials of the secondary and middle schools.

title: Family and Consumer Science Education Practicum
description: This field-based experience provides the opportunity for students to plan, teach, and evaluate family and consumer science education lessons within a placement at the middle or secondary level. This practicum also allows students to incorporate instructional strategies and application of materials from the methods course.

title: Topics in Integrative Human Sciences II
description: Advanced exploration of topics in human science/family and consumer sciences.

title: Engineering Assembly
description: Assemblies are held once a month for practicing industrial engineers to make presentations to the students. Students are given an opportunity to interact with the visitors. The purpose is to provide an opportunity to learn about various companies and their products and operations.

title: Industrial Plant Studies
description: Trip to industrial centers for study of facilities of special interest to industrial engineering students.

title: Introduction to Industrial Engineering
description: Introduction to the core areas of industrial engineering including ergonomics, operations, research, quality control, management, engineering economics, resource management, production systems and inventory control. Both engineering ethics and careers in industrial engineering will be discussed.

title: Introduction to Manufacturing Processes & Systems
description: This course provides an introduction to manufacturing processes and systems. The history and impact of manufacturing on society will be explored. A review of manufacturing processes and the products to which they are best suited will be emphasized. The impact of product design on manufacturability will be introduced. The role of engineers in designing good manufacturing processes and systems will be discussed.

title: Manufacturing Processes Laboratory
description: General introduction to foundry, welding, and machining. Includes safe manufacturing practices, metrology, and hands-on experience in foundry, welding, and machining operations.

title: Welding Laboratory
description: Introduction to welding. Includes safe welding practices and lab experiments in gas, spot, and arc welding.

title: Computer Numerical Control Laboratory
description: Introduction to computer numerical control. Part programming for CNC lathes and mills will be accomplished.

title: Introduction to Analytics
description: This course covers an introduction to analytics, which includes computer programming, statistics and mathematical decision making. Topics include: arrays, loops, data structures, data visualization, hypothesis testing, confidence intervals, multidimensional linear regression, distribution fitting, and identifying objectives and constraints to recommend data driven solutions.

title: Undergraduate Research Experience
description: Open to students pursuing undergraduate research projects.

title: Honors Research in Industrial Engineering
description: Individual research problem selected with approval of faculty advisor. Open to students in the College of Engineering honors program. A report is presented orally and in writing during the last semester.

title: Industrial Management
description: Basic functions in an industrial organization and their interrelationships; management considerations involving product, process, plant, and personnel.

title: Engineering Economic Analysis
description: The analysis of the economic aspects of engineering/industrial projects. Focus on decision making among competing alternatives, including replacement decisions. It includes: cost estimating, time-value of money and effects of depreciation and taxation. Methods of comparing alternatives are developed, including: equivalent worth, rate of return, break even analysis, and benefit-cost ratio.

title: Industrial Project Evaluation
description: The analysis and selection of engineering/industrial projects. This course is a continuation of IMSE 530. Topics in addition to IMSE 530 includes: risk/uncertainty, impact of inflation, capital rationing, multiple criteria decision making, and determining the MARR.

title: Statistical Quality Control
description: Normal, binomial, and frequency distributions. Seven process improvement tools. Control charts on means and variances for variables and attributes. Design of experiments for process and product design. Acceptance sampling plans.

title: Industrial Facilities Layout and Design
description: Design of industrial facilities with emphasis on manufacturing engineering and material handling.

title: Operations Research I
description: A study of the methods of operations research including model formulation and optimization. Topics include: linear programming, sensitivity analysis, network flows, and graph optimization.

title: Materials and the Impact of Manufacturing Processes
description: The categorization and analysis of materials used in manufacturing is introduced.  Emphasis is placed on the electronic and mechanical properties of materials along with the impact that manufacturing processes have on these properties.  Through laboratory work, the students will learn how to design and conduct manufacturing experiments to measure material properties and explore the interplay between processes and properties. 3 credit hours, 2 hours lecture, 1 hour lab  per week [2 lab sections]. Offered spring semesters every year.

title: Manufacturing Processes Engineering
description: The effects of operating variables on manufacturing processes such as machining, metal forming, casting, welding, plastics, etc. Emphases are on manufacturing process theory, process variables measurement, and the technical inferences of collected data. Strength of materials, manufacturing process theory, instrumentation, computer data acquisition, and data analysis concepts are included. Laboratory testing of manufacturing processes and the engineering design of experiments for process variable measurements are used to develop efficient manufacturing processes.

title: Product and Process Engineering
description: A study of the interrelationships between product design and production process selection. Emphasis is on the development of economic production systems for discrete products in a competitive manufacturing environment. Concepts of design for manufacture and assembly, tool engineering, and manufacturing systems design are included.

title: Manufacturing Systems Design and Analysis
description: Comprehensive design and analysis of a manufacturing system: integration of the undergraduate industrial engineering and manufacturing engineering courses.

title: Senior Design Project I
description: Students organize themselves in teams, not exceeding five students in each team. Each team is responsible to establish a client. The teams select a general subject agreeable to the client, formulate a specific design project, and gather data and resources needed to support the project.

title: Senior Design Project II
description: Continuation of IMSE 591 in which student teams complete engineering design projects formulated and approved in IMSE 591.

title: Topics in Industrial Engineering
description: Lectures on recent topics in industrial engineering.

title: Independent Study in Industrial Engineering
description: This course involves independent study at the introductory graduate level.

title: Advanced Industrial Management
description: Contemporary management philosophies and their impact on engineers and engineering managers. Topics include: planning, organizing, leading, and controlling.

title: Occupational Safety Engineering
description: An overview of factors affecting safety in organizations, emphasizing analysis techniques and design strategies. Topics include occupational safety, accidents, fire protection, industrial hygiene, hazardous waste, toxicology, radiation safety, product liability and federal standards. A project involving a hazard analysis and the design of a solution for a field location is required.

title: Industrial Ergonomics
description: Process analysis and charting; principles of motion economy and ergonomics; work stations and environments; micromotion analysis and an introduction to standard data systems.

title: Work Environments
description: Basic structure and performance of the human, viewed as a component in information processing and control systems. Effect of visual, auditory, toxic, and thermal environments.

title: Production Planning and Inventory Control
description: Principles, techniques, and applications of production planning and inventory control. The course covers strategic planning tools such as forecasting and aggregate planning as well as tactical tools such as inventory management and scheduling.

title: Quality Engineering
description: An introduction to the modern practice of quality engineering concepts, systems, strategies, and tools to both manufacturing and service industries. Topics include advanced techniques related to statistical process control, international quality standards, quality data management, and automatic inspection.

title: Industrial Simulation
description: Basic concepts of computer simulation modeling of manufacturing, production, service and other stochastic systems. Use of a commercial simulation software environment to build, analyze, verify and validate models. Use of models as a system design tool through statistical and optimization techniques.

title: Operations Research II
description: Continuation of IMSE 560. Topics include: integer programming, nonlinear programming, Markov chains, and queuing theory.

title: Computer Aided Manufacturing
description: Concepts in CAM, integrated control of machine tools and transport devices with production control. Concepts of CAM and automated assembly in small lot production environment.

title: Additive Manufacturing
description: A study of current 3D printing or additive manufacturing technologies that convert a digital file into a physical object. Emphasis is on rapid prototyping involving materials, processes, techniques, and 3D printing machines. Capabilities and limitations of 3D printing technologies are included.

title: Operations Research III
description: A continuation of IMSE 660.  Covers more advanced aspects of LP and NLP.  Introduces three new topics: normative theory of decision and games, dynamic programming and Markov decision systems.

title: Quantitative Problem Solving Techniques
description: Survey of decision making techniques. Topics covered include: linear, integer and nonlinear programming; network flows and stochastic processes. A focus of the course will include modeling and the use of software to solve these problems.

title: Principles of Manufacturing Information Systems
description: Introduction to the theory and concepts of information for manufacturing. Design of manufacturing systems such as MRP, SFRS, CAD/CAM, etc. Concerns of integration and man-machine interface in manufacturing systems.

title: Transportation Logistics
description: This course provides comprehensive coverage of logistics system design, analysis, and management, with a particular emphasis on freight transportation and supply chain systems. It introduces key challenges arising in supply chain systems, quantitative models for addressing these challenges, and methods for solving the models. Emphasis is placed on analyzing model results and communicating their implications for decision-making.

title: Data Engineering I – Time Series & Topological Data Analysis
description: Introduction to theories and concepts of data analytic and machine learning on time series and topological data.  Data models and analysis systems include forecasting, predictive models, process change point detections, classification, clustering, stage-dependent decisions applying to high-dimensional time series data and topological data. Applications include data integration and data-to-decisions in real-world systems, such as supply chain, logistics, financial engineering, and advanced manufacturing processes.

title: Normative Theory of Decisions and Games
description: Bayes theorem, Bayesian estimators, utility, loss function and risk, minimax strategies, elementary game theory.

title: Multiple Criteria Decision Analysis
description: This course covers decision processes for problems involving multiple conflicting criteria, including individual and group decision making. Multiple approaches, including value function methods, goal/aspiration methods, and outranking methods, are considered.

title: Stochastic Calculus Financial Engineering
description: This course will serve as an introduction to the basic concepts and computing techniques of financial engineering and its real-life applications. These basics can be applied in many other aspects of Industrial Engineering used throughout stochastic processes, probability theory, system simulation, portfolio/risk management, and supply chain optimization.

title: Methods of Operations Research
description: This course is intended to give an overview of OR at the graduate level. After this course, the student will have the general basic knowledge in OR and a better idea about the usefulness and interrelationships of the various subjects in O.R. Topics to be covered include the various optimization techniques, stochastic processes and optimization, and the various approaches in the treatment of uncertainty.

title: Big Data Analytics
description: Topics include big data management, data architecture of hosting big data, big data retrieval languages, parallel computing methods, big data analytical methods, and data visualization.

title: Advanced Topics in Industrial Engineering
description: Lectures on recent advanced topics in industrial engineering.

title: Advanced Independent Study in Industrial Engineering
description: This course involves independent study of recent advanced topics in industrial engineering.

title: Management of Research and Engineering
description: Engineering administration; organization factors in decision-making.

title: Engineering Project Management
description: Planning, scheduling, and controlling engineering projects. Includes determination of appropriate project team, cost/benefit analysis, PERT and CPM scheduling techniques, reporting, and use of computerized project management tools.

title: Systems Engineering Fundamentals
description: Systems engineering is an interdisciplinary engineering management process used to develop an integrated, life-cycle balanced set of system solutions that satisfy customer needs. This course provides the fundamentals of a systems engineering approach to solving complex engineering systems problems. This course will address systems engineering processes and tools as they relate to the development and life cycle management of complex systems. Topics included in the course are analyzing customer's needs, requirements development, systems design, development and integration, developing technical performance measures, system verification and managing cost, schedule, and risk in engineering tasks. This course will also introduce reliability, availability and maintainability concepts and their applicability with complex systems. This course is applicable to all engineering disciplines involved with complex engineering systems.

title: Industrial Logistics Engineering
description: The course provides comprehensive coverage of supply chain logistics components and system issues, including control of the movement of goods and information, coordination of supply and demand in creation and maximization of time and place utility. Emphasis is given to integrating workflow modeling, control, and design concepts and performance evaluation of logistic activities such as transportation, inventory, warehousing, and information technology. The systems viewpoint is explored at the individual firm level and from the perspective of inter-firm relationships among participants in logistics supply chains. Recognition is also given to important interrelationships between logistics and production, marketing, and financial management.

title: Advanced Production and Inventory Control
description: Analytical and mathematical methods of making decisions on production, inventories, human resources, and shipping in modern industrial plants.

title: Advanced Engineering Economy
description: This course expands on the principles of the fundamental engineering economics analysis. Emphasis is placed on quantification and evaluation of risk and uncertainty factors, effects of inflation and taxes, economic consequences of estimating models, utility theory, engineering capital equipment replacement analysis and decision-tree and multi-attribute decision models.

title: Tribology in Manufacturing
description: An introduction to system approach to Tribology; Surface Topography; Physical, Chemical, and Geometric Nature of Surfaces; and the Mechanics of contact between surfaces. This course also investigates various theories of friction and wear; hydrodynamic; elastohydrodynamic, and boundary lubrication; frictional instabilities; rolling contact problems; and application of system methodology to tribological problems in engineering design and manufacturing.

title: Applied Fuzzy Set Theory
description: The emphasis will be on applicational aspects. Topics covered are elementary fuzzy set theory, fuzzy measure, possibility theory, fuzzy linear programming and other fuzzy optimization techniques, fuzzy linguistics and expert systems, fuzzy production and inventory control, and fuzzy operations research models.

title: Advanced Topics in Quality Engineering
description: A survey of current advances in quality engineering. Includes both off-line and on-line quality engineering.

title: Reliability Theory
description: The mathematics of reliability theory. The hazard function. Calculation of failure density and mean life for series, parallel systems, and various types of standby systems. Hypotheses tests on mean life. Life testing with censoring.

title: Ergonomics (Human Factors) Engineering
description: The design and analysis of applied experimental research on human behavior as applied to engineering systems. An experimental project.

title: Simulation of Industrial Management Systems
description: Simulating industrial management systems on computers utilizing Monte Carlo techniques and simulation languages. Numerical methods related to simulation.

title: Applied Stochastic Processes
description: Survey of stochastic processes. Topics covered include: discrete and continuous Markov chains, random walks, queuing theory and renewal theory.

title: Stochastic Programming
description: Stochastic programming (SP) aims to determine optimal solutions in the presence of data uncertainty. The course covers two-stage stochastic linear programming, recourse, chance constraint optimization, multi-stage SP, stochastic integer programming, Benders decomposition, L-shaped method, heuristics, simulation-based (Monte Carlo simulation) methods, and bounds on optimal solutions. Numerous applications exist, and examples may be taken from agriculture planning, manufacturing, supply chain, reliability, cyber security, power grids or other systems.

title: Manufacturing Processes for Semiconductor Materials and Devices
description: Research topics on manufacturing processes for semiconductor materials and devices. Processes covered include; crystal growing, slicing, edge profiling, lapping, wafer grinding, wet etching, polishing, cleaning, oxidation, lithography, dry etching, diffusion, metallization, CMP (Chemical-Mechanical Planarization), backside thinning, dicing, bonding, packaging, final testing.

title: Advanced Topics in Computer Integrated Manufacturing
description: Modern issues of computerized manufacturing considering both hardware and software approaches and methods. Advanced concepts in intelligent machine programming and applications, group technology, computer aided process planning, and scheduling will be discussed. Research issues will be presented.

title: Industrial Forecasting Techniques and Applications
description: The problems of model construction for industrial forecasting. The application of least squares, regression, exponential smoothing, and adaptive fitting will be studied in solving industrial engineering problems.

title: Industrial Systems Analysis
description: Analysis and synthesis of automatic control systems with application to machines and processes and industrial management systems. A study of optimal control, stability, and sensibility of industrial management systems.

title: Linear Programming
description: Development of the theory of linear programming and related topics including simplex methods, duality theory, integer programming, transportation methods, and stochastic linear programming. Application to industrial problems and the use of computer solutions are emphasized.

title: Network Flows and Graph Theory
description: Survey of topics from Network Flows and Graph Theory with an emphasis on algorithmic analysis. The topics covered include: Complexity Analysis, Shortest Paths, Maximum Flows, Network Simplex, Minimum Spanning Trees, Matching, Planar Graphs, Colorings, Perfect Graphs and Heuristics.

title: Integer Programming and Combinatorial Optimization
description: Solution techniques to optimization problems invloving integer variables. The topics covered include: single-and multiple-branching, implicit enumeration, polyhedral theory and cutting planes, mixed integer programs, unimodular matrices and matroids.

title: Advanced Manufacturing Information Systems
description: Survey of topics in Computer Integrated Manufacturing. Issues such as the Manufacturing Automation Protocol (MAP), representation of solids in CAD, storage and retrieval of such information are considered.

title: Research Methods in Industrial Engineering
description: This course will provide students with effective methods to perform literature reviews, survey current solution methodologies, develop and evaluate research and present research results.

title: Applied Methods in Industrial Engineering I
description: This class requires students to have at least 6 weeks of full-time work experience in a job related to Industrial Engineering.

title: Applied Methods in Industrial Engineering ll
description: This class requires students to have at least 12 weeks of full-time work experience in a job related to Industrial Engineering.

title: Graduate Seminar in Industrial Engineering
description: Presentation and discussion of topics of contemporary interest in industrial or manufacturing engineering. M.S. and Ph.D. candidates make one presentation.

title: Master's Report
description: Topics selected with approval of major professor and department head.

title: Master's Thesis
description: Topics selected with approval of major professor and department head.

title: Industrial Queuing Processes
description: Introduction to the queuing process and theory of queues; analysis of single and multistation queues; application to production, materials handling, inventory and maintenance systems.

title: Scheduling Theory
description: Project scheduling, assembly line balancing, shop scheduling, basic structure, measures of performance, combinatorial and statistical aspects. Various approaches to the analysis of shop scheduling.

title: Advanced Topics in Manufacturing Research
description: Study of topics related to recent advances in manufacturing processes and manufacturing engineering. Selected topics vary by semester according to the interests and needs of students and faculty.

title: Nonlinear Programming
description: Study of nonlinear models and their solution. Topics covered are nonlinear programming including Kuhn-Tucker theory, quadratic programming, separable programming, geometric programming, gradient and search methods, quasilinearization, and invariant imbedding.

title: Advanced Topics in Operations Research
description: Study of topics related to operations research not covered in other courses. Selected according to the interests and needs of graduate students.

title: Advanced Topics in Decision Theory
description: Decision processes for problems involving multiple conflicting criteria; multiple attribute decision making; multiple objective decision making, and group decision making under multiple criteria. Various methods/approaches for different problems are discussed.

title: Dissertation Research
description: Topics selected with approval of major professor and department head.

title: Design Thinking
description: Design Thinking is the process of analyzing the problem areas from a given project statement and generating a solution through observation, research, conceptualization, and iteration. Through this course, students will learn how to apply user-centered design approaches to a given design challenge. This course will cover selected design thinking tools and methods in four phases: 1) Explore, 2) Discover, 3) Develop, and 4) Deliver. Several case studies will demonstrate how Design Thinking can be applied to other fields, and students will share their reflections and experiences about how they can make use of the process, methods, and tools they have learned in their respective fields.

title: INDD Design Studio I
description: Introduction to design research, user behavior, ergonomics, and human factors in the context of product design. Projects focus on iterative ideation and development through ink and marker sketching and physical model making. Students will learn to document and present designs including physical models, detailed renderings, and documentation of their design development process.

title: INDD Digital Applications
description: Provides an advanced understanding of digital lighting, materials, animation, advanced modeling techniques, and renders. Explores parametric design uses and applications. Investigates strategies and techniques to create products using digital tools.

title: INDD Design Studio II
description: Basic principles of Product Design process through research, ideation, sketching, modeling, prototyping and presentation. Explores and analyzes complex social, cultural, and economical aspects of society for product design development.

title: Human Factors in Industrial Design
description: Deals with an interdisciplinary science focusing on the interaction of human performance, capabilities, limitations and behavior with design factors in performance environments like workplaces and products with the emphasis on optimal safety, efficiency, productivity and comfort for diverse human populations.

title: Product Semantics
description: Explores visual and symbolic qualities of the man-made forms in the context of their use and application of this knowledge in order to understand the aspects of communication in Industrial Design. Course also focuses on the cognitive and social context of the products.

title: User Interface and User Experience Design
description: In-depth study of the development, strategy, and execution of interactive products and services to create meaningful experiences that enhance user engagement and satisfaction within the scope of industrial design. Applies user-centered design by conducting user research including surveys, interviews, and usability testing. Students will create prototypes with high fidelity mockups using industry standard tools.

title: INDD Design III
description: Advanced principles of the Industrial Design process. Explores different theories and techniques to develop products utilizing Human Centered Design, Design Thinking, and Universal Design. Holistic thinking in regard to ethical responsibility throughout the product's Life Cycle from decisions made in manufacturing processes, material choices and human factor design.

title: INDD Design Studio IV
