description: Exposes students to a deeper understanding about user experience, user interface and form development in the Industrial Design process. Provides an advance research and analysis of design problems and opportunities, using Human Centered Design, Universal Design and Design Thinking.

title: Materials and Manufacturing Processes
description: Seeks to provide an advanced understanding of materials and processes commonly used by Industrial Designers and manufacturers. The objective is to develop a more precise, professional and accurate approach to design while broadening the student's technical base.

title: Materials and Manufacturing Processes II
description: Building upon the knowledge established in INDD 500, this course deepens comprehension of materials and processes. Emphasizing practical applications to a design development and expand student's technical proficiency.

title: Professional Practice
description: Studies of conventional and newly developing modes of professional design practice. Presented are the relationships of Industrial Designers to users, clients, manufacturing industry, society, government, and one another. Provides an overview of design services and principal structures for industrial designers including consultancy, in-house, freelance, and self-production.

title: Problems in INDD
description: Study of specific Industrial Design problems under the direct supervision of a member of the department.

title: INDD Design Studio V
description: This semester of study provides an option for an interdisciplinary collaborative studio course oriented towards replicating the learning experience and interactive activities that takes place in the modern multidisciplinary professional office or a validated equivalent studio completed in an approved study program.

title: Internship
description: Fifteen-week internship in a professional environment with a focus on Industrial Design and/or Furniture Design: office and field experience.

title: Internship Report
description: Development of the student's analysis; observation, and communication skills in relationship to the professional office and field environment. Detailed documentation, online and on-campus, of the experiences encountered during internship, allowing classmates to learn from their peer's varied experiences as well as provide detailed documentation of the student's work during internship.

title: Summer Internship
description: Ten-week internship in a professional environment with a focus on Industrial Design and/or Furniture Design: office and field experience.

title: Summer Internship Report
description: Development of the student's analysis; observation, and communication skills in relationship to the professional office and field environment. Detailed documentation, online and on-campus, of the experiences encountered during internship, allowing classmates to learn from their peer's varied experiences as well as provide a detailed documentation of the student's work during internship.

title: Professional Practice: Discipline-Specific Topics
description: Covers discipline-specific concerns, focusing on issues specific to architecture, interior architecture and product design, landscape architecture or regional and community planning.

title: Professional Practice
description: Studies of conventional and newly developing modes of professional design practice. Presented are the relationships of Industrial Designers to users, clients, manufacturing industry, society, government, and one another. Provides an overview of design services and principal structures for industrial designers including consultancy, in-house, freelance, and self-production.

title: INDD Design Studio VI
description: Examines the interdisciplinary and collaborative design process with the aim of different formations in accordance with entrepreneurship studies, marketing, business, and engineering, as well as research and development. Focus areas vary from interdisciplinary practices to innovative technologies.

title: INDD Capstone Studio
description: Students are to pursue design projects that encompass their previous educational experience validating their knowledge of the INDD program studies. The project will be based on extensive research and review. The project presentation will suggest unique and applicable solutions with clearly identified methodology of implementation.

title: Design Research
description: Identify evaluation criteria appropriate for design research and problem-solving; analysis of literature; construction of problem statements, development and documentation of design problem proposals and reports.

title: Advanced Studio Programming
description: Research of programming methodologies culminating in a programmatic document used as the basis for the design project in INDD 822 or IAID 824.

title: Advanced Industrial Design Studio
description: Students explore projects programmed under the previous semester INDD 815: Advanced Studio Programming. Projects must be relevant in a contemporary socio-cultural, architectural and technological context; employ rigorous design research processes and methodologies; identify user and usability needs/desires; accommodate market opportunities and constraints; explore multiple conceptual solutions in a variety of hand and digital media; build and document models and prototypes; and estimate material and manufacturing costs.  Note  A book, oral and visual presentation and display materials for an exhibition of the student's work will be required at the completion of the course.

title: Italian I
description: Introduction to the structure of modern Italian.

title: Italian II
description: Continuation and completion of the study of modern Italian grammar, using the facilities of the language laboratory for audiolingual practice.

title: Italian for Travelers
description: Prepares students for everyday situations on a visit to Italy.

title: Italian III
description: Grammar review and reading selections from Italian literature.

title: Italian IV
description: Selective review of grammar and reading of examples of modern Italian literature.

title: Japanese I
description: Introduction to the fundamental linguistics and cultural characteristics of the Japanese language and its writing systems (Hiragana, Katakana, and Kanji).

title: Japanese II
description: Continuation of Japanese I. Development of functional skills for familiar situations.

title: Japanese III
description: Continuation of Japanese II with emphasis on building and applying basic language knowledge in communicative tasks; exercises in oral interactions and interactions involving text; weekly language lab; approx. 1-- new kanji in text.

title: Japanese IV
description: Continuation of Japanese III. Enhancement of speaking and writing skills, and reading and listening comprehension.

title: Culture of Japanese Communication
description: Introduction to verbal and nonverbal cultural aspects of Japanese discourse. Includes comparative study, followed by discussion on application of knowledge in cross-cultural context.

title: Japanese V
description: Development of communication skills through application activities such as problem-solving tasks and role plays. Enhancement of vocabulary, structures, and their usage. Emphasis on extended discourse.

title: Japanese VI
description: Continuation of Japanese V.  Development of functional skills for general situations.

title: Basic Computer Applications In Journalism And Mass Comm
The Mass Communicator/ Ethics And Issues
Seminar In The Future Of The Media
Fundaments of Radio and Television Production
description: Fundaments of Radio and Television Production

title: Adaptive Physical Activites
description: Exercise programs adapted to the needs of the special student.

title: Topics in Strength and Conditioning
description: Discussion of topics and participation in activities of importance in strength and conditioning.

title: Topics in Aerobic Exercise
description: Discussion of topics and participation in activities of importance in aerobic exercise.

title: Swimming I
description: Beginning instruction for students who have no previous experience with swimming.

title: Swimming II
description: For the beginning swimmer who has had some previous swimming experience.

title: Strength and Conditioning Specialist
description: Course prepares students for completion of exam required for Certified Strength and Conditioning Specialist (CSCS) credential from the National Strength and Conditioning Association.

title: Professional Development for Coaches
description: Principles of improving relationships, enhancing engagement and gaining trust of athletes through targeted communication.

title: Adaptive Training
description: Course covers techniques related to functional fitness classes for Adaptive Athletes including veterans, active duty personnel, and civilians alike.

title: Electrocardiography Technician Certification Preparation
description: Course presents an introductory overview related to the anatomy and physiology of the heart and explores normal electrical conduction as well as common variations as evidenced by changes in the waveform on the cardiac monitoring device. Focus on the student's ability to perform cardiac monitoring via 3, 5 and 12 lead monitoring devices and prepare students certification as an electrocardiography technician through the National Healthcareer Association.

title: Phlebotomy Certification Preparation
description: This course is designed to present students with an introduction to phlebotomy & infection control, legal issues, human anatomy & physiology, phlebotomy equipment & supplies, phlebotomy procedures, and phlebotomy fundamental essentials. Allows for preparation for the Certified Phlebotomy Technician certification exam offered by the National Healthcareer Association.

title: Weight Training
description: For students in the College of Health and Human Sciences, no more than 4 credit hours in lifetime sports and exercise activity classes may be applied toward a degree.

title: Biobehavioral Bases of Physical Activity
description: Current perspectives from the biological and behavioral domains of kinesiology will be used to explore the significance of physical activity for optimal health.  Topics include physiology of physical activity, social and behavioral epidemiology of physical activity and health, control and biomechanics of human movement.

title: Individual And Dual Sports For Secondary Schools
Measurement and Research Techniques in Kinesiology
Measurement and Research Techniques in Kinesiology
description: Theory and techniques of measurement and research in the biological and behavioral aspects of kinesiology.

title: Prevention And Recognition Of Athletic Injuries
Rural Community Health
description: Study of social, economic, political, and cultural influences that impact the health of individuals and families in rural communities with special focus on Kansas and the Midwest. Focuses on theories and concepts relevant to rural community health and their utility in designing rural public health interventions.

title: Motor Learning and Development
description: Issues of motor learning and development as they relate to the application of instructional techniques.

title: Introduction To Physical Culture In The Western World
Biomechanics
description: Mechanical and anatomical aspects of human movement.  Kinematic and kinetic principles applied to the analysis of human movement.

title: Physiology of Exercise
description: The responses of the human body to exercise. Emphasis will be placed on understanding the structure-function relationships of the respiratory, cardiovascular, and muscular systems and how their function is integrated to support the dynamics of muscular contraction. Limitations to exercise performance will be examined in health and disease and the adaptability of the human body to physiological (i.e., exercise training) and environmental (e.g., hypoxia) stressors will be examined.

title: Physiology of Exercise Lab
description: A laboratory course to supplement the material of KIN 335.

title: Social And Psychological Dimensions Of Physical Activity
Exercise Behavioral Science
description: Examination of the social and behavioral determinants of physical activity using a social ecological framework. Social ecological levels include individual, interpersonal, community, institutional and societal/policy factors.

title: Laboratory Experience for Social and Behavioral Epidemiology of Public Health Phy
Anatomy and Physiology
description: Anatomy and Physiology of the organ systems of the human body. Laboratory includes physiology experiments, study of anatomy from computer simulation, dissection demonstrations, X-rays, and slide work.

title: Anatomy and Physiology I
description: Rigorous, comprehensive study of human anatomy and physiology at the cellular, tissue, and organ systems level with an emphasis on the structure/function relationships of the integumentary, skeletal, muscular, and nervous systems.

title: Anatomy and Physiology II
description: Rigorous, comprehensive study of human anatomy and physiology at the cellular, tissue, and organ systems level with an emphasis on the structure/function relationships of the endocrine, cardiovascular, lymphatic, respiratory, digestive, urinary, and reproductive systems.

title: Foundations of Exercise Training
description: This course introduces the foundational principles of exercise training and their scientific underpinnings. Topics include anatomy, biomechanics, physiology, nutrition, and psychology as they relate to training adaptations. Students will learn movement assessment, exercise techniques, and evaluation methods with an emphasis on safe and effective execution. Major training areas covered include resistance training adaptations, aerobic and anaerobic endurance, and muscular endurance. Practical labs require active participation and demonstration of proper training techniques.

title: Exercise and Chronic Disease
description: Focus on the epidemic of chronic disease impacting society and the benefits/use of exercise as a therapy for selected diseases.

title: Strength and Conditioning for Performance
description: This course emphasizes advanced concepts and methods in strength and conditioning to enhance athletic performance. Students will examine the relationship between imposed training stress and physiological adaptation, with application to strength, power, speed, and endurance. Emphasis is placed on speed mechanics, agility development, plyometrics, and explosive training, as well as advanced resistance training strategies including Olympic lifts and their variations. Practical experience will integrate coaching and analysis of skilled performance movements.

title: Exercise and Older Adults
description: Examines the relationship between exercise and the aging process. Specific focus will be on the impacts of aging on the organ systems of the human body, the extent to which exercise may beneficially impact this process, and methodology for assessment of fitness and prescription of exercise in the older adult population

title: Certifications in Health and Fitness
description: Examine careers and certifications available within the field of Kinesiology/Applied Fitness.

title: Fundamentals of Health and Fitness
description: Examine human behavior and exercise as the forefront of discussions about health. Examine relationship between health behavior, exercise, inactivity, and fitness adaptations.

title: Pediatric Physical Activity, Exercise & Sport
description: Introduction to physiological and behavioral exercise considerations for a holistic approach to increasing activity levels effectively and safely in the pediatric population. Prepares students planning to work with healthy to obese 3-17 year olds in physical activity, exercise, and sport to encourage lifelong physical activity adherence and obesity reduction.

title: Introduction to Integrative Human Pathophysiology
description: Focus on the main mechanisms that lead to organ and system dysfunction. The basic processes covered will highlight some common diseases, rather than covering an exhaustive list of known diseases.

title: Topics in Kinesiology
description: Study of a selected topic in an area not covered in the curriculum or involving application of theory presented in a related subject core course.

title: Practicum in Lifetime Sports
description: Supervised students assist in lifetime sports classes.

title: Principles and Practice of Exercise Program Design
description: This course develops applied skills in designing, progressing, and evaluating exercise programs for diverse populations. Students will integrate scientific foundations and training methods into structured, evidence-based plans that address strength, power, endurance, and performance goals. Emphasis is placed on periodization models, progression strategies, and balancing training variables (intensity, volume, frequency). Students will practice writing, critiquing, and adapting programs to optimize performance outcomes and client success.

title: Honors Tutorial in Kinesiology
description: Individually directed research in kinesiology, normally as a preliminary to writing a senior honors thesis.

title: Honors Project
description: Open only to Health and Human Sciences students who are active members of the University Honors Program.

title: Measurement And Research Techniques In Kinesiology
Practicum in Fitness Settings
description: Practical experiences in the fitness setting such as observation and participation in exercise testing and prescription, exercise leadership, and record keeping and program management.

title: Prac/Public Health Physical Activity
description: Practical experiences in the public health setting, such as observation and participation in public health physical activity program planning and evaluation.

title: Evaluation And Emergency Management Of Athletic Injuries
Rehabilitation And Conditioning For Athletic Injuries
Seminar In Issues And Administration Of Athletic Train Prog
Internship in Kinesiology
description: Supervised field experience in approved fitness, strength and conditioning, clinical, public health, or healthcare setting 3-8 credit hours, 50 hours required at the internship site per credit.

title: Psychology of Exercise and Sport Injury
description: Underlying effects, treatment of psychological consequences, and physical trauma of exercise and sport injury. Topics include the impact of injury on exercise behavior, the athlete at risk, behavioral risk factors, injury assessment, treatment of injury, and psychological factors addressed by the sports medicine team.

title: Sport and Exercise Personality
description: Intersession only The role of personality in sport, sport performance and exercise behavior. Theories relevant to sport and exercise peresonality will be examined to provide a theoretical framerwork for understanding and applying research. Assessment instruments and relevant research will be examined and critiqued.

title: Sport and Exercise Psychology
description: This course is designed to introduce the student to the basic psychological principles related to sport and exercise behavior and performance. Factors that influence sport performance will be examined as will the principles related to exercise and exercise behavior.

title: Teaching Experience in Kinesiology
description: This course will be a learning experience for students supporting a faculty course. Objectives include identifying and discussing learning and teaching, leading discussions, and providing assistance and direction to students in the course.

title: Research Experience in Kinesiology
description: Exposure to and assisting with current research projects in a laboratory setting in Kinesiology.

title: Topics in Kinesiology
description: Study of a selected topic in Kinesiology involving either an in-depth study or application of theory presented in a related core course.

title: Independent Studies in Kinesiology
description: Selected topics in kinesiology. Maximum of 3 hours applicable towards a degree.

title: Interpersonal Aspects of Physical Activity
description: Examination of theory and research related to interpersonal aspects of physical activity. Topics include:  social networks, social support, social influence, cohesion and intervention strategies that target interpersonal factors to increase and maintain physical activity participation.

title: Cardiorespiratory Physiology
description: An examination of the structure and function of the respiratory system and the manner in which oxygen passes from the atmosphere to its site of utilization in the mitochondria. Exercise, disease, and environmental stresses will form the basis for examining the capacity, plasticity, and limitations to respiratory function.

title: Social Structural Determinants of Physical Activity
description: An examination of how social structural determinants impact participation in physical activity. Topics include:  social class, race and ethnicity, gender, sexual orientation, family, education and work. Promising physical activity intervention strategies are also discussed.

title: Cardiovascular Physiology
description: Study of the structure and function of the cardiovascular system as it pertains to health and disease. Topics include the control of blood pressure, vascular volume, and blood flow during orthostasis, aging and disease.

title: Topics in the Biological Basis of Kinesiology
description: Study of a selected topic in the biological basis of kinesiology involving either an in-depth study or application of theory presented in a related course area.

title: Topics in Behavioral Basis of Kinesiology
description: Study of a selected topic in the behavioral basis of kinesiology involving either an in-depth study or application of theory presented in a related course area.

title: Muscle Physiology
description: Subcellular, cellular, and tissue structure of skeletal muscle and the relationship of these structural characteristics to the functioning of the muscle. Examines energy pathways available to the muscle to support the various functions, and mechanisms underlying changes in exercise tolerance that accompany exercise training and detraining, aging, and diseases that affect skeletal muscle.

title: Body Image, Eating Disorders And Obesity
Environmental Physiology
description: Study of the physiological adaptations to acute and chronic challenges imposed by the environment. Topics will focus on how different physiological systems respond to different environmental stressors (heat, cold, deep sea diving, high altitude).

title: Program Planning and Evaluation
description: Theories and models and the stages and activities of planning, implementing, and evaluating health promotion programs.

title: Autonomic Neurophysiology
description: Study of the structure and function of the autonomic nervous system primarily as it pertains to physical stress. Particular emphasis is placed on the mechanisms evoking changes in the autonomic nervous system during physical stress including feed-forward and feedback autonomic control signals. The effects of disease and exercise training on those neural control signals are discussed

title: Policy, Built Environment and Physical Activity
description: Examination of the characteristics of active living policies and neighborhood/community design that contribute to improved health.

title: Physical Activity Behavior Settings: Youth Sport to Senior Centers
description: The course is a study of key physical activity behavior settings across life-span development. Topics include observation of social and physical environmental variables, surveillance of physical activity in behavior settings, motivational theory and responses, setting interventions and developmental outcomes, and intervention evaluation methods.

title: Cardiorespiratory/Comparative Physiology in Health and Disease
description: This course seeks to fulfill the following objectives: (1) To provide the student with an awareness and mechanistic understanding of the effects of physical activity on humans and animals and the concept of homeostasis. (2) Foster an understanding of the integrative nature of kinesiology/physiology by considering the relationships among different systems and their response to stress in humans and animal species in health and disease. Focus is brought to bear in particular on the pulmonary, cardiovascular and muscle systems.  Chronic and acute adaptations to special environments and physical stress are considered and the students are asked to consider how the different systems are controlled in health and disease.  This course is recommended for pre-med students, biology and physiology majors, aspiring scientists in physiology and/or medicine and those interested in a career in health and human services.

title: Obesity and Physical Activity: Behavioral, Environment, and Policy Influences
description: Examines the contributing factors to obesity, especially through the lens of physical activity/inactivity.  Attention is paid to the environment and policy influences on behaviors that contribute to weight gain.

title: Signaling Pathways in Physiology
description:
Current concepts of biological signaling transduction and its role in controlling genes expression and protein synthesis involved in exercise adaptation, including resistance and endurance exercise, and disease states, including cardiovascular disease and cancer. This course seeks to fulfill the following
objectives: (1) To train students to understand the molecular and cellular signaling mechanisms associated with changes in skeletal and cardiac muscle mass, cardiac function, and endothelial function change in response to exercise and disease. (2) Foster an understanding of the correlation of
'macro-level' concepts like muscle hypertrophy to 'micro-level' concepts such as mTOR signaling with the goal of understanding the role cell signaling plays in mediating chronic adaptation. This course is recommended for pre-med students, pre-physical therapy students, biology and physiology majors, 80 and those interested in a career in health and human services. This course will cover concepts that are of emphasis of the MCAT exam.

title: Exercise Testing and Prescription
description: Benefits and risks of exercise testing and prescription with healthy populations, individuals at risk, and patients with cardiovascular and metabolic diseases. Includes experiences with exercise test technology and methods of exercise prescription.

title: Design And Analysis Of Exercise And Sport Equipment
Nutrition and Exercise
description: The interrelationships between diet, nutrition, and exercise. Topics covered include physical fitness, weight control, nutrient metabolism during exercise, and athletic performance.

title: Development of Motor Control
description: A multi-level analysis of the neurophysiological activation of muscle, reflexes, sensory integration during movement, and theories of voluntary movement.

title: Individual Physical Activity Promotion
description: An in-depth study of individual-level influences on physical activity and strategies to promote physical activity among individuals.

title: Integrative Human Pathophysiology
description: This course is designed to introduce the student to the mechanistic pathways of prominent chronic diseases (e.g., cardiovascular, respiratory, renal, cancer) and the mechanistic basis for current recommended treatments. This course will focus on the vertical integration of cardiovascular, renal, respiratory, and neural physiological pathways as they relate to pathogenesis, diagnosis, and treatment of these chronic diseases. A large emphasis will be placed on the cellular and molecular response, how they manifest in histological analysis and subsequent organ system dysfunction with the role of exercise therapy (example: cardiac/respiratory therapy) and pharmacology in mitigating these physiological changes.

title: Physical Culture In The Western World
Cardiorespiratory Physiology
description: An examination of the structure and function of the respiratory system and the manner in which oxygen passes from the atmosphere to its site of utilization in the mitochondria. Exercise, disease, and environmental stresses will form the basis for examining the capacity, plasticity, and limitations to respiratory function.

Examines energy pathways available to the muscle to support the various functions, and mechanisms underlying changes in exercise tolerance that accompany exercise training and detraining, aging, and diseases that affect skeletal muscle.

title: Environmental Physiology
description: Study of the physiological adaptations to acute and chronic challenges imposed by the environment. Topics will focus on how different physiological systems respond to different environmental stressors (heat, cold, deep sea diving, high altitude).

title: Program Planning and Evaluation
description: Theories and models and the stages and activities of planning, implementing, and evaluating health promotion programs.

description: Study of the structure and function of the autonomic nervous system primarily as it pertains to physical stress. Particular emphasis is placed on the mechanisms evoking changes in the autonomic nervous system during physical stress including feed-forward and feedback autonomic control signals. The effects of disease and exercise training on those neural control signals are discussed.

title: Policy, Built Environment and Physical Activity
description: Examination of the characteristics of active living policies and neighborhood/community design that contribute to improved health.

(2) Foster an understanding of the integrative nature of kinesiology/physiology by considering the relationships among different systems and their response to stress in humans and animal species in health and disease. Focus is brought to bear in particular on the pulmonary, cardiovascular and muscle systems. Chronic and acute adaptations to special environments and physical stress are considered and the students are asked to consider the the different systems are controlled in health and disease. This course is recommended for pre-med students, biology and physiology majors, aspiring scientists in physiology and/or medicine and those interested in a career in health and human services.

title: Obesity and Physical Activity: Behavioral, Environment, and Policy Influences
description: Examines the contributing factors to obesity, especially through the lens of physical activity/inactivity. Attention is paid to the environment and policy influences on behaviors that contribute to weight gain.

title: Cinematographic And Videographic Analysis Of Human Movement
Individual Physical Activity Promotion
description: An in-depth study of individual-level influences on physical activity and strategies to promote physical activity among individuals.

title: Graduate Internship in Kinesiology¿
description: Supervised field experience in approved fitness, strength and conditioning, clinical, public health, or healthcare setting 3-8 credit hours, 50 hours required at the internship site per credit.

title: Internship/Public Health Physical Activity
description: Supervised field experience in settings such as public health departments, foundations, government offices, or research programs.

title: Topics in Exercise Physiology
description: Selected topics in excercise physiology involving either greater in-depth study or application of theory presented in a related course.

title: Topics in Public Health Physical Activity Behavior
description: Selected topics in public health physical activity behavior involving either greater in-depth study or application of theory presented in a related course.

title: Advanced Physiology of Exercise
description: An in-depth study of the physiological responses of the human body during exercise, the adaptations that occur with exercise training, and the laboratory techniques to assess these responses and adaptations.

title: Physical Activity: Physiology to Public Health
description: This graduate seminar covers the study of physical activity and its impact on public health across levels of analysis from basic exercise physiology to social ecology.

title: Physical Activity and Human Behavior
description: An examination of the theory and research related to the psychological antecedents and outcomes of participation in physical activity and exercise. Topics include models of exercise motivation, social cognitive theory of exercise and social ecological models of physical activity.

title: Mtr Learn & Control
description: Application of learning principles to skill acquisition in sport and human domain; and practical applications.

title: Social Epidemiology of Physical Activity
description: An examination of the social factors that contribute to an understanding of why people participate in physical activity. Topics include the consideration of individual social and demographic factors such as gender, race, socioeconomic status, education, and social support as well as social structural factors such as the role of the social institutions (e.g. family, work and school environments).

title: Research Methods in Kinesiology
description: A study of techniques of the research process including the identification of a research problem, the design of experimental and non-experimental strategies, and the presentation of written research.

title: Social and Behavioral Bases of Public Health
description: The role of behavioral, social, psychological, economic, environmental, and social structural factors in both the occurrence of health problems in groups and populations, and in the development of the risk factors that contribute to these problems. Principles of health behavior change and the application of these principles to a variety of health issues as well as an emphasis on how social structural factors impact health are examined to better understand health behavior and health inequities in contemporary society.

title: Advanced Muscle Physiology
description: Course will examine current topics in muscle physiology.  Format to include discussion of scientific papers, oral presentations, and debate of controversial topics.

title: Physiology of Oxygen Transport
description: This course is designed to promote critical reading of the literature, particularly with respect to the understanding of exercise physiology and cardiorespiratory, vascular and muscle energetics responses.  Activities will include presenting papers, debate and discussion regarding all aspects of science and scientific philosophy.

title: Mechanical Analysis of Human Movement
description: Mechanical principles and analysis procedures for the study of overt human movement. Applications to movements in exercise, sport, occupational, and daily living activities will be emphasized.

title: Advanced Cardiovascular Physiology
description: Discussion and critical examination of cardiovascular control mechanisms, with specific emphasis on regulation of blood pressure during severe challenges such as exercise, heat stress, and upright posture.

title: Advanced Public Health Physical Activity
description: Exploration of the social and behavioral epidemiology of physical activity in public health: outcomes, influences, and promotion in individuals, settings, and communities.

title: Group Dynamics and Physical Activity
description: Examines the principles of group development and maintenance in physical activity environments. Topics include the nature of groups, group environment, important member attributes, group structure, group cohesion, group processes and tactics of group development.

title: Public Health Practice
description: Supervised field experience in an international, state, local, or district health agency or other appropriate health agency.

title: Exercise Adherence
description: Study and discussion of empirical research and theory as it relates to exercise adherence from psychological, behavioral, and public health perspectives. Topics addressed are epidemiological trends, conceptual issues of measurement, research design, statistical analysis, and interventions focusing on settings and populations.

title: Topics in the Physiological Basis of Kinesiology
description: Study of a selected topic in the physiological basis of kinesiology involving either an in-depth study or application of theory presented in a related course area.

title: Topics in the Behavioral Basis of Kinesiology
description: Study of a selected topic in the behavioral basis of kinesiology involving either an in-depth study or application of theory presented in a related course area.

title: Exercise Psychology in Special Populations
description: A study of the psychological determinants and outcomes of exercise participation in special populations (e.g. arthritic, obese, cardiovascular disease). Emphasis in understanding the motive underlying involvement in exercise and physical activity and the situational and personal determinants associated with failure to initiate and/or adhere to exercise and physical activity programs.

title: Graduate Seminar in Kinesiology
description: Designed to provide students opportunities to improve oral and written communication skills and to expose them to the breadth of research topics within the Kinesiology department.

title: Independent Study in Kinesiology
description: Intensive independent study in a topic or problem involving the integration, application, and synthesis of theory presented in a related course. The course culminates in the preparation of an original paper.

title: Research in Kinesiology
description: Independent study in one or more of the research processes involved in the scientific method.

title: History and Theory of Landscape Design
Design of Parks and Recreational Areas
Travel: Regional I
description: Multi-day regional field trip experience. Students will study existing natural and/or built environments to develop landscape architecture knowledge base and apply findings to other courses.

title: Travel: Regional ll
description: Multi-day field trip experience in an urban environment. Students will study notable works of landscape architecture, and visit professional offices to develop landscape architecture knowledge base and apply findings to other courses.

title: Travel: Narratives and Placemaking
description: Immersive field trip experience that supports the Narrative and Placemaking Studio.

title: Travel: Major City l
description: Multi-day field trip experience in a major city. Students will study notable works of landscape architecture, and visit professional offices to develop landscape architecture knowledge base and apply findings to other courses.

title: Travel: Major City 2
description: Multi-day field trip experience in a major city. Students will study notable works of landscape architecture, and visit professional offices to develop landscape architecture knowledge base and apply findings to other courses.

title: Introduction to Landscape Architecture
description: Exploratory lecture course open to students in all majors providing an overview of landscape architecture which focuses on innovations in natural and built environments. Students will learn about a wide variety of global issues and perspectives, types of planning and design projects, and professional aspects of the discipline.

title: Unlocking Creativity
description: Do you want to become creative? Good news, you already are! For students in all majors, this course is designed to help you access your creative potential and become more imaginative, innovative, and courageous. Learning outcomes include overcoming blocks, refining your creative process, practicing activities to enhance creativity, and effectively communicating ideas.

title: Drawing Landscapes Studio
description: Foundational aspects of 2D analog and digital medium. Students will learn how to view, analyze, represent and design through the lens of landscape architecture.

title: Modeling Landscapes Studio
description: Foundational aspects of 3D modeling using both analog and digital medium. Students will learn how to view, analyze, represent and design through creation and fabrication of 3D models.

title: Landscape Architecture Materials and Methods
description: To provide the student a basic understanding of materials and methods commonly utilized in the practice of landscape architecture as a basis to pursue subsequent land design and land construction course work.

title: Ethics and Environmental Dilemmas
description: Introduction to environmental ethics and significant local and global dilemmas. Students will learn about how humans have impacted the environment through time and the implications of those actions, and environmental/social movements and justice.

title: Design Graphics & Visual Thinking
description: A study of graphic communication techniques for the exploration and presentation of landscape architecture design ideas.

title: Site Design Studio II
description: Small- to medium-scale site design studies with emphasis placed on design process, response to cultural and natural conditions, detailed articulation of space including definition and use of materials, and design communication.

title: Plants and Design
description: Introduction to plant morphology, forms of classification, and a basic understanding of plant habitat. Students will learn to identify a palette of plants in the field and understand their application for design.

title: Plant Patterns and Use
description: Introduction to the patterns and use of plants in cultural and natural contexts. Students will identify plant palettes and communities in historical and contemporary landscapes.

title: Landscape Performance Lab
description: Introduction to the scientific process as well as methods for assessing landscape performance. Students will learn how to acquire, organize and analyze information using qualitative and quantitative methods.

title: Plant Selection
description: Selection of plants for specific site requirements. Students learn how to anticipate plant performance and identify plants to achieve design goals in associated courses.

title: Landscape Restoration
description: Development of landscape restoration plans. Students will learn the principles, methods, and plants of ecological restoration at local and regional scales.

title: Planting Design Studio
description: Design studies emphasizing functional, aesthetic, and ecological uses of plants. projects emphasize design process and the relationship between plants and the built environment; elements and principles of planting design; plant selection; preparation of planting plans; specification writing; and design implementation.

title: Socio-Ecological Systems Studio
description: Introduction to key principles of ecology as related to ecological planning and design. Students will learn how to connect theoretical knowledge of socio-ecological systems and processes to address multi-scale problems in urban and rural contexts.

title: Narrative/Placmkng Sem
description: Immersive exploration into fundamentals of landscape narratives, place theory and placemaking. Students will examine and engage in a variety of locations, create expressions of narrative using a variety of representational techniques, and apply knowledge gained in site design.

title: Narratives and Placemaking Studio
description: Immersive exploration into fundamentals of landscape narratives, place theory and placemaking. Students will examine and engage in a variety of locations, create expressions of narrative using a variety of representational techniques, and apply knowledge gained in site design.

title: Landscape History and Design Studio
description: Introduction to the history and theory of landscape architecture materials and design from the Middle Ages to the present with a focus on Europe and North America. Students will gain competency in landscape architecture vocabulary, the ability to recognize notable works and practitioners, and apply historical precedents to site design projects.

title: Site Design Implementation
description: Introduction to construction materials, methods, and documentation appropriate for design implementation at the site scale. Focus on producing a set of construction documents including grading, stormwater management, plants and materials.

title: Master Plan Design and Implementation
description: Design implementation at the master plan and regional scales. Focus on suitability analyses and systems supporting community services such as pedestrian and vehicular transportation, land use, hydrology and other natural systems.

title: Site Research and Design Studio
description: Iterative development of site plans and designs. Students will research and share precedent studies to expand knowledge base, and will apply findings to multiple design projects.

title: Career and Academic Planning
description: Exploration and identification of career paths and development of academic plans for reaching personal and professional goals including specific coursework, internships, study abroad and extracurricular activities.

title: Career Development Communications
description: Development of visual, written and oral communication skills for career development. Students will prepare materials for internship application, job search, or career advancement.

title: Computer Applications in Landscape Architecture I
Site Planning and Design
description: Theory, principles, and elements of site planning and design. Lectures, readings, short problems, and site visits dealing with site analysis, ecological consideration, grading drainage, circulation and parking, lighting, planting design, materials and details, management and maintenance, and cost factors.

title: Landscape Architecture Seminar I
description: Discussion of current trends in landscape architecture and related fields by students, faculty, and invited speakers.

title: Landscape Architecture Tech Module I
description: Introductory theory, methods, and application of technological skills and support of concurrent landscape architecture studios and courses. Emphasis is on data collection and management and mapping and analysis.

title: Landscape Architecture Tech Module II
description: Introductory theory, methods, and application of technological skills and support of concurrent landscape architecture studios and courses. Emphasis is on CAD and 3-D modeling.

title: Landscape Architecture Tech Module III
description: Intermediate theory, methods, and application of technological skills and support of concurrent landscape architecture studios and courses. Emphasis is on CAD and 3-D modeling and surveying.

title: Landscape Architecture Tech Module IV
description: Intermediate theory, methods, and application of technological skills and support of concurrent landscape architecture studios and courses. Emphasis is on CAD and 3-D modeling and presentation graphics.

title: Landscape Architecture Tech Module V
description: Advanced application of technological skills and support of concurrent landscape architecture studios and courses. Emphasis is on CAD and 3-D modeling, thematic mapping, and geoprocessing.

title: Landscape Architecture Tech Module VI
description: Advanced project specific applications in data collection and management, CAD modeling, thematic mapping and GIS, and presentation graphics.

title: Planning & Design Intro to GIS
description: This course will provide students with the essential background in geospatial analysis and information systems for the design and planning fields. Students will learn basics about data models and management, vector and raster analysis and cartography. Students will learn how to use these skills to develop suitability analyses to evaluate the performance of plans and designs or to identify which location would be best for a particular purpose.

title: History And Theory Of Landscape Architecture 2
Golf Course Planning and Design
description: Fundamentals of golf course planning and design, including: history, management, design, facilities, aesthetics, and technical development.

title: Special Studies in Landscape Architecture
description: Special studies in the area of landscape architecture

title: Professional Internship Report
description: Oral and graphic presentation of work completed in LAR 702 and LAR 703.

title: Community Engagement Studio
description: Engagement with communities and stakeholders through service learning projects. Focus on engagement methods and visualization, representation and communication to general audiences. Utilizes planning and design techniques and knowledge gained in previous semesters.

title: Design Implementation III
description: Continuation of LAR 439 to include large scale site design, road alignment, large area grading, soils and excavation methods, storm drainage, and utilities routing.

title: Landscape Architecture Seminar II
description: Literature review and discussion of current issues and trends in community planning and design. Readings and discussion in preparation for field trip.

title: Landscape Architecture Professional Internship
description: Confirmed internship with a professional sponsor subject to departmental approval.

title: Project Programming
description: Research and the development of a program directed toward the advancement of a capstone project for LAR 705 Master's Project and Report.

title: Landscape Architecture Off-Campus Studio
description: Off campus design studies fulfilled in an approved professional design office or in an approved international studies program.

title: Environmental Landscape Planning & Design
description: Introduction to and understanding of environmental planning, design and management of natural and social landscape systems beyond site scale. The course focuses on theories, techniques and processes for designing sustainable landscapes and urban environments. Students will develop a final project starting with the conceptualization, through inventory, analysis and simulation, in order to develop a final design proposal.

title: Master's Project Studio 1
description: In-depth exploration of a specific landscape architecture topic through research and design supervised by a major professor in accordance with Graduate School guidelines.

title: Master's Project Studio 2
description: In-depth exploration of a specific landscape architecture topic through research and design supervised by a major professor in accordance with Graduate School guidelines.

title: Public Lands and Natural Resources Law
description: Legal aspects of land use and natural resource management on the federal public lands. A brief history of the acquisition and disposition of the public domain and a review of legal authority on the public lands are followed by an examination of key legal issues concerning the resources of water, minerals, timber, range, wildlife, recreation and wilderness.

title: Research Methods
description: Development of the research design process. Students will learn to employ appropriate methods and strategies to answer research questions, and to effectively and clearly communicate research-based information to peers and the public.

title: Rivers: Processes & Form
description: This course addresses key aspects of hydrology such as precipitation and movement of water from uplands to channels. This movement, generally referred to as ""runoff processes

title: Advanced Golf Course Planning and Design
description: Advanced methods and strategies of golf course and resort planning and design.

title: Advanced Studio
description: In-depth exploration of a specific landscape architecture topic.

title: Problems in Landscape Architecture
description: Specific problems and/or reports in the area of landscape architecture.

title: Topics in Italian Landscape
description: Investigation of Italian culture, landscape and communities through analytical readings and/or drawings.

title: Community Planning and Design
description: Study of the relationships between planning and design in the growth and development of cities and towns.

title: Professional Practice
description: Studies of conventional and newly developing methods of professional design practice. Instruction in the relationships of architects, landscape architects, interior architects, and other professionals to users, clients, construction industry, society, government, and one another.

title: Urban Design Studio I
description: An interdisciplinary design studio involving large-scale design; projects with extensive time implementation sequence; responses to socioeconomic, cultural, environmental, and technical needs; and implementation strategies. Design methods are applied to selected urban areas of the Midwest.

title: Advanced Seminar
description: Studies and discussion of topics related to specialization studio.

title: Comprehensive Studio 1
description: Culminating experience in landscape architecture design studio.

title: Comprehensive Studio 2
description: Culminating experience in landscape architecture studio.

title: Professional Practice: Professional Responsibility
description: Interdisciplinary course addresses the connections between allied design professions and the larger community. It helps students to understand and appreciate their professional legal and ethical responsibilities.

title: Professional Practice: Office Practices
description: Interdisciplinary course addresses practice-based issues concerning professional relationships in the design and delivery process, management of projects, communication practices, and fiscal concerns.

title: Professional Practice: Discipline-Specific Topics
description: Covers discipline-specific concerns, focusing on issues specific to architecture, interior architecture and product design, landscape architecture or regional and community planning.

title: Design of Parks and Recreation Areas
description: Site planning of national, state, municipal, and private parks, and specialized recreation areas.

title: Landscape Resource Evaluation
description: The determination of the impact of physical project design upon the natural and man-made environment. Studies of existing site conditions and projections of the effect of such projects upon the site and vicinity.

title: Portfolio Design
description: The Portfolio Design course guides students in development of a professional portfolio of work, with the goal of producing a product that can be used for internship application, job search, or career advancement. Students explore visual thinking and visual communication with a culminating goal of producing a final portfolio.

title: Unlocking Creativity
description: Do you want to become creative? Good news, you already are! For students in all majors, this course is designed to help you access your creative potential and become more imaginative, innovative, and courageous. Learning outcomes include overcoming blocks, refining your creative process, practicing activities to enhance creativity, and effectively communicating ideas.

title: Advanced Planting Design
description: Special studies in advanced planting design.

title: Advanced Landscape Architecture
description: Special studies and design in advanced landscape architecture.

title: Advanced Landscape Architecture Construction
description: Specialized study of large-scale landscape planning involving landscape construction and grading.

title: Proposal Writing
description: Development of a master's thesis proposal.

title: Master's Report
description: Comprehensive application of research and design study through individual studies approved by the department faculty.

title: Research in Landscape Architecture
description: Investigations in landscape architecture and related areas, of such caliber as to form the basis for a graduate thesis.

title: Latin I
description: An introductory study of the structure of Latin.

title: Latin II
description: Continuation and completion of the study of the structure of Latin.

title: Intermediate Latin-Prose.
description: Continuation of the study of Latin syntax and grammar, based upon reading of Roman prose. Topics may include Caesar, Cicero, or Sallust.

title: Intermediate Latin-Poetry
description: The reading of Latin poetry in the original at an intermediate level. Topics may include Virgil, Ovid, and Lucretius.

title: Agriculture Advocacy and Leadership Training
description: The Agricultural Leadership Program (ALP) equips students with knowledge of the College of Agricullture and provides advocacy and leadership training, including skills development in active listening and communication. Facilitated by College of Agriculture Ambassadors, the course features guest speakers, hands-on activities, and field trips. While required for newly selected Ag Ambassadors, it is open to all K-State students.

title: CAT Community Connections
description: Interdisciplinary course that explores the connections among two different fields, courses, or disciplines. For first-year students enrolled in a specific CAT (Connections Across Topics) Community linked to the Staley School of Leadership Studies.

title: Foundations of Leadership in Society
description: Expose students to a variety of concepts, theories and skills relevant to contemporary leadership thought. Consider their personal conceptions and philosophy of leadership. Examine leadership within particular contexts such as creating change, ethical leadership, leadership and management, and historical leadership thought and leaders.

title: Introduction to Leadership Concepts
description: This course is organized to provide students with a broad overview of leadership theories, an introduction to ethical decision making, identification of personal leadership styles, and current societal issues for leaders.

title: Applied Leadership Skills
description: This course provides opportunities for all interested students to be introduced to leadership skills application, with emphasis on practice in supervised clinical settings.

title: Fundamentals of Global Food Systems Leadership
description: An interdisciplinary approach to the fundamental roles people, policies, and cultures play in the global food system enterprise as it relates to sustainable food production, processing, distribution, and availability. Students will explore complexities within self, others, and community related to leadership in the context of a rapidly increasing global population.

title: Honors Leadership I
description: This is the first of two courses in a year-long curriculum that is designed to explore four tenets of leadership: citizenship, stewardship, humanitarianism, and purposeful passion. Students will begin to clarify and express their core values as they explore questions around effective leadership and followership concepts and strategies.

title: Honors Leadership II
description: This is the second of two courses in a year-long curriculum that is designed to explore four tenets of leadership: citizenship, stewardship, humanitarianism, and purposeful passion. Students will continue to explore their values and develop leadership and teamwork skills through service, reflection, and designing a purposeful passion project.

title: Strengths Peer Coach Practicum
description: This course is designed to help students develop the skill of coaching within the context of strengths-based leadership while serving as a strengths peer coach. Additional frameworks will include research on employee engagement, well-being, and positive psychology. Students will participate in training, peer observations, weekly seminars, reflections, and debriefs around the peer coaching experience. Students will also apply their coaching skills by holding weekly peer coaching sessions.

title: Peer Leader Practicum
description: This course uses experiential learning to prepare students with the personal, relational, planning, and facilitation skills needed to effectively lead a team or organization. Weekly LEAD 212 facilitation, class preparation seminars, and peer feedback will allow for experience, reflection, meaning-making, and experimentation.

title: Theories of Leadership
description: This course provides an overview of the major theories of leadership and the empirical and theoretical evidence supporting those theories. Students will demonstrate an understanding of the theories and the empirical evidence supporting them as well as develop their ability to critically assess the theoretical and empirical work surrounding leadership theories and models.

title: Exploring Uncertainty in Global Food Systems Leadership
description: Explore technical and adaptive elements of grand challenges across disciplines. This course addresses the impact of cultural identity, life experience, and world views on leadership relationships as it relates to privilege and inclusion in the context of global food systems. Students will consider elements of community-engaged work.

title: Culture and Context in Leadership
description: Opportunity to integrate the course and leadership experiences in light of contemporary issues in the study of leadership behavior across cultures and contexts. Based on the current research and writing that introduce and discuss the impact of culture and context on the concept of leadership and development of individuals as interculturally competent leaders.

title: Internships for Career Development
description: This course helps you apply your academic knowledge, skills and understanding to experiences as a developing leader within the workplace. The course is structured to incorporate your internship experience with self-directed analysis to develop your personal, academic and professional competencies. You will practice and reflect on your leadership strengths.

title: Global Agricultural Solutions and Leadership
description: This interdisciplinary, experiential-learning course prepares undergraduate students to collaboratively address real-world agricultural challenges in global contexts. Students work in teams on a project provided by an international organization focused on issues such as food insecurity, clean water, crop or animal production, or sustainable development. The course culminates in international travel, where students collaborate with global scientists and present their final projects on-site.

title: Global Agricultural Solutions and Leadership Travel
description: This interdisciplinary, experiential-learning course prepares undergraduate students to collaboratively address real-world agricultural challenges in global contexts. Students work in teams on a project provided by an international organization focused on issues such as food insecurity, clean water, crop or animal production, or sustainable development. The course culminates in international travel, where students collaborate with global scientists and present their final projects on-site.

title: Leadership in Practice
description: This course provides an opportunity to practice leadership and followership in an experiential setting followed by an in-depth classroom discussion.

title: Theories of Nonprofit Leadership
description: This course provides an overview of nonprofit organization leadership by integrating concepts and theories with applied practice.  Students will demonstrate an understanding and application of competencies (knowledge and skills) necessary for professional success as a nonprofit organization leader.

title: Global Food Systems Leadership in Action
description: In this capstone course, students will conduct a community-engaged global food systems leadership research/service project. Emphasis will be placed on data collection, analysis and dissemination to appropriate audiences. Additional emphases will be on ethical dimensions of leadership and individual exploration of careers and roles within global food systems.

title: Women and Leadership
description: This course addresses issues related to leadership as it intersects with women's studies scholarship, such as: women's styles of leadership, women and competition, sexism in the workplace, gender differences in communication, and feminist models of leadership. This course will explore issues related to women's leadership development in the theoretical contexts of leadership studies and women's studies.

title: Capstone in Leadership
description: In this capstone course students will integrate and apply learning from the minor. Emphasis will be on the clarification of the students' personal values, their evaluation of codes of ethics for organizations in their proposed professions, and their analysis of current events through the lens of ethical dimensions of leadership.

title: Seminar in International Service-Learning
description: This course is the first phase of the International Service Teams service-learning program. Learning in the course facilitates preparation for the summer service experience. This course is designed around the PARE service-learning model; both the curriculum and class expectations will be reflective of the service-learning process.

title: Nonprofit Organization Internship
description: This course provides a capstone opportunity for students in the nonprofit leadership focus of the leadership studies minor to document and present their achievement of the foundation and professional development competencies required by American Humanics national certification in nonprofit leadership through coursework, co-curricular and work experiences, and their 300-hour internship experience.  Current texts focusing on the leadership of high impact nonprofit organizations will be incorporated into this process.

title: Foundations of Community-Engaged Leadership
description: This course integrates leadership theory and practice exploring principles, purposes, and processes of community engagement and democratic practice. Utilizing service-learning methods, students will partner with community to consider and examine foundational forms of civic and public life. Community paradigms will be explored and deconstructed in relation to course themes.

title: Teaching Leadership
description: Preparing people for leadership in a diverse and changing world requires new thinking and approaches to leadership learning and development. Learners will explore philosophies, frameworks, and strategies for designing, delivering, and assessing leadership courses and programs in educational (curricular and co-curricular), workplace, and civic contexts. Learners will develop a philosophy of leadership teaching statement and create a contextually and culturally relevant curricular design document (e.g., class syllabus or program outline). We will serve and attract learners who are working in the field of leadership studies and programs, or aspiring leadership educators.

title: Approaches to Community-Engaged Leadership
description: Through theory and practice, students consider the ethical dimensions of leadership in relation to community systems, organizations, and institutions. Students will deconstruct concepts of community-engaged leadership including race, class, gender, justice, and power. Through service-learning, students will work with partners to understand community systems, organizations, and institutions.

title: Experiences in Community-Engaged Leadership
description: In this capstone experience students design, plan, execute, and evaluate a community-engaged research project. The project will be consistent with the principles of community engagement and the purposes and process of democratic practice. Students will exercise leadership with community partners to build capacity for systemic change.

title: Nonprofit Leadership Concepts
description: Explores the unique facets of nonprofits and how to establish vision, mission, and practices that best position organizations to build leadership capacity for community change. Leadership within the nonprofit sector has unique considerations separate from those in governmental or business organizations including democratic governance, championing a cause, volunteer leadership, and mission-driven organizational design.

title: Foundations of Leadership
description: Study of the key issues in the theory, research, and application of leadership in organizations. This includes defining leadership, understanding situational characteristics that facilitate / hinder effective leadership, understanding effective / dysfunctional leadership, and gaining greater insight into one's own leadership style and functioning.

title: Facilitating Leadership in Complex Adaptive Systems
description: This course surveys the theory and practice of facilitating leadership activity in Complex Adaptive Systems through various process models and approaches to practice.

title: Teaching Leadership
description:
Preparing people for leadership in a diverse and changing world requires new thinking and approaches to leadership learning and development. Learners will explore philosophies and frameworks of leadership and leadership development, as well as strategies for designing, delivering, and assessing leadership courses and programs in educational (curricular and co-curricular), workplace, and civic contexts. Learners will develop a leadership teaching philosophy statement and create a contextually and culturally relevant leadership curriculum design document (e.g., class syllabus or program outline). 
Updated 3.25.24 after feedback from Dr. Collins in EdLea

title: Leadership Coaching
description: This course develops practices of coaching, listening, and empathy for leadership and examines current theory and practice around coaching as a leadership practice. Coaching frameworks from the field of leadership will be examined through the lens of contemporary theories and frameworks.

title: Advanced Coaching
description: This course develops coaching skills following the International Coaching Federation (ICF) coaching principles, ethics, and competencies. This course focuses on coaching practice, peer mentoring, and competency development. Students will create research proposals based on their coaching practices.

title: Leading Change
description: This?course?introduces theoretical?and practical?leadership frameworks for understanding and responding to local and global challenges of sustainable development. Students in the course will learn and apply effective leadership processes and develop individual capacity for leading change.

title: Systems Leadership
description: This course examines theories and approaches to leadership through a systems lens, a critical approach to leading complex organizations and addressing global challenges. Learners will develop an in-depth understanding of systems dynamics and gain skills necessary to navigate and influence systems effectively. The course will equip learners with capacities to catalyze, enable, and support processes of systems-level change in their own organizations and society.

title: Applied Coaching Practicum
description: The Applied Coaching Practicum course will walk students through building a coaching practice, writing agreements, building a client base, and other aspects of engaging in real world coaching. Students will identify community partners to work with, engage in peer mentoring, and continue to practice and build coaching skills.

title: Practicum in Leadership for Conflict Transformation
description: In this capstone seminar, participants will explore culminating perspectives and reflect on learning related to leadership, conflict transformation, and change. Participants will complete a final portfolio representing a trajectory of learning. They will synthesize key learning from the program, describe practical implications from prior learning, and design future application goals to support the practice of leadership, conflict transformation, and change in their personal lives and professional careers.

title: Graduate Studies in Leadership and Communication
description: This course examines the intersections of community-engaged scholarship and leadership through practices of relationship, story, strategy, and action. Students are introduced to resources and opportunities for pursuing public engagement, community-engaged scholarship, and successful program completion.

title: Seminar in Leadership and Communication
description: This seminar provides doctoral students opportunities for collaboration and mentorship with peers, faculty, and visiting scholars, and exploration of career pathways in public engagement.

title: Qualitative Approaches to Studying Leadership and Engagement
description: This course is designed to provide students a survey of community-engaged and qualitative approaches to studying leadership and engagement process and practice in community and organizations.

title: Contemporary Perspectives on Leadership Theory and Practice
description: This course is designed to build students' capacity to engage with leadership theory as critical learners. Students will explore ideas, philosophical assumptions, and practices associated with the term ""leadership"" as a concept through theories that have developed over time. We will also examine how the practice of leading in contemporary contexts can be understood through theories.

title: Approaches to Public/Community Engagement
description: The course will provide students a survey of key concepts and theoretical frameworks associated with deliberative democracy and public engagement. A specific focus will be given to how forms of community-engaged scholarship intersect course content in both theory and practice.

title: Soc Sc Res Pub Prob Sol
description: The study of how social science research and social sector evaluation can be applied to address public problems, including the identification of public problems and the critique of current evaluation methods, and applying concepts by developing an evaluation including quantitative and qualitative analysis in partnership with social sector partners.

title: Research in Leadership and Communication
description: Doctoral research demonstrates the candidate's ability to conduct significant original research related to leadership and communication, to analyze the information obtained from the research, and to present the results in a dissertation format appropriate to the field.

title: Introduction to the Study of Language
Introduction To Research In Laboratory Medicine
Principles & Techniques Of Research In Medical Investigation
Therapeutic Recreation Processes In Rehabilitation Agencies
Problems in Life Span Human Development
description: Relevant topics in life span human development will be explored in a small group setting.

title: Adult Development and Aging
description: Developmental aging research as related to individual, social, and family functioning throughout adulthood.

title: Practicum in Human Development Research
description: Observation, modification, and reporting of behavior.

title: Readings in Life Span Human Development
description: Implications of research findings in preparation for professional work and research in life span human development.

title: MS Research in Life Span Human Development
description: Individual research problems which may form the basis for the master's thesis or report.

title: Human Development Seminar
description: Analysis of the continuous and systematic changes in the development of individuals as they interact with their physical and social environments.

title: MACH DES 3
description: More advanced consideration of the design of machine elements and of simple machines. Three hours of recitation a week. Prerequisite: MACH 422

title: Principles of Management
description: Provides an introduction to the four basic managerial functions of planning, organizing, leading and controlling and their application to today's complex work environment. An emphasis is placed on the roles and responsibilities of managers that help them to successfully meet organizational objectives by effectively leading employees and optimizing processes.

title: Business Law I
description: A study of law as it relates to business, including court procedures and systems, contracts, sales, business crimes, torts, business organizations, and government regulation.

title: Workplace Dynamics and Organizational Culture
description: This course explores the complex factors that influence workplace dynamics and organizational culture. Students will examine how managing individual differences can improve conflict resolution, drive cultural change, and improve the effectiveness of teams and organizations. Students will gain practical insights into fostering positive work environments that promote belonging, collaboration, satisfaction, motivation, and productivity.

title: Introduction to Total Quality Management
description: Overview of major topics related to Total Quality Management (TQM), including managerial and engineering aspects.

title: Management Consulting Skills
description: This course provides an introduction to foundational consulting skills and methodologies. Students will learn how to craft coherent messaging and design and deliver effective presentations through applied learning experiences. This content is relevant to anyone seeking to refine their critical thinking, analytical, and professional communication skills and is particularly beneficial to students participating in business case competitions or who are considering a career in consulting.

title: Leading in Business Organizations
description: Examines how executives and managers guide organizational success through strategic decision-making and organizational influence. Students explore business-focused theories, with emphasis on how executive-level decisions impact organizational performance. The course integrates established business theories with contemporary management practices.

title: Information Systems and Business Analytics
description: A comprehensive view of organizational information systems required for obtaining and transforming data into business analytics and intelligence. Cases and hands-on exercises emphasize information systems usage in data acquisition, analysis, and presentation leading to effective organizational decision-making.

title: Introduction to Operations and Supply Chain Management
description: To gain knowledge of Operations and Supply Chain Management (OSCM) principles and techniques, such as process management, project management, quality management, continuous improvement, forecasting, supply chain management, inventory management, planning and scheduling, and lean systems. The primary goals are to develop students' analytical skills in solving OSCM-related problems in a variety of business settings and to help students understand how OSCM contributes to a firm's competitive position in a global marketplace.

title: Non-Profit Management
description: This course introduces students to strategic management issues in nonprofit organizations. Nonprofits represent an important sector of the economy that ifluence the lives of many individuals. In this course, students will learn about the trends and issues affecting the sector as the lines between private, public, and nonprofit organizations continue to evolve.
