Major topics include: strategic planning, board governance, nonprofit financial management, risk management, resource development, public relations, and impact and accountability measures. The emphasis is on nonprofit organizations in the United States.

title: Undergraduate Research Experience
description: Open to students pursuing undergraduate research projects.

title: International Management Experience
description: This course is designed to provide students with an opportunity to observe international business and management perspectives by participating in a field trip outside the United States. This experiential course may involve a combination of business visits, cultural experiences, internship activities, applied research projects, personal reflection exercises, or other learning activities while abroad.

title: Management Internship
description: Applied management business experience designed to coordinate the interests of students and firms.

title: Topics In Management
description: Discussion and analysis of current issues in management, including topics related to new theory, the practice of management, and management development.

title: Independent Studies in Management
description: In-depth analysis of special problems in management including study of current literature.

title: Organizational Behavior
description: An examination of human behavior in organizations to prepare individuals for their managerial careers. The course provides a framework for students to diagnose and manage issues associated with personality and individual differences, perceptions, motivation, diversity and inclusion, fairness and equity, decision making, group functioning, conflict management, culture, and leadership in organizations.

title: Managerial Decision Analytics
description: Managerial decision analytics uses descriptive, predictive, and prescriptive data analytics to improve organizational decision-making processes.

title: Operations Planning and Control
description: Development of concepts and understanding of planning and control systems for allocating resources and scheduling activities in business firms. To guide and coordinate the flow of materials, labor inputs, and goods and services through physical productive systems. Topics include: aggregate planning, master production scheduling, production activity planning and control, operations information systems, inventory control, material requirements planning, and total quality control.

title: Human Resources Analytics
description: Explores using data analytics to examine Human Resource Management issues in organizations. A diverse set of research methods and analytic techniques will be used to collect, organize, analyze, and visualize data to examine problems and formulate solutions that can help organization better manage their HR processes.

title: Strategic Management Analytics
description: Provides students with new knowledge and capability in the use of a diverse set of research methods and data analytic techniques to collect, organize, analyze, and visualize data to examine organizational problems and present solutions that can help create a sustainable competitive advantage.

title: Sports Management Analytics
description: Provides knowledge and capabilities in analytics and artificial intelligence (AI) for the quantitative management of professional sports organizations. Explores topics in measurement and managerial assessment of athletic and team performance, strategic and tactical analyses in professional sports, and leveraging AI and data for optimized decision making in sports management.

title: Labor Relations and Negotiations
description: Provides broad coverage of the goals and operations of labor unions as well as the interactions of labor and management. In addition, this course introduces students to the bargaining and negotiation process within organizations. Effective negotiation strategies are taught and individual negotiation skills are enhanced through opportunities to participate in negotiation simulations.

title: Human Resources Management
description: This course provides an overview of the human resource systems and processes needed to achieve organizational effectiveness and strategic success. All key functional areas including human resource planning, staffing, performance management, employee development, and compensation are addressed.

title: Employment Law
description: A survey course designed to acquaint students with the broad and controlling aspects of prominent laws which affect human resource management. Topics include ADA, ADEA, OSHA, Title VII, etc.

title: Management of Quality
description: Coverage of quality as a management philosophy through the study of ideas from contemporary quality philosophies on continuous improvement, employee empowerment, statistical process control charting, and other applicable organizational topics.

title: Organizational Training and Development
description: The process of training and developing the human resources in organizations, which includes organizational diagnosis, needs assessment, program design, program implementation, and the evaluation of program effectiveness. Current trends in the content and process of training and development activities are also examined.

title: Business Project Management
description: Provides an in-depth coverage of project management concepts and methodologies required for service and manufacturing operations. Topics to be covered include, but are not limited to: project selection and evaluation, project organization, management or project team members organization, project scheduling, project budgeting, project monitoring and control and project closure.

title: Transportation and Warehouse Management
description: Covers concepts and techniques in transportation and warehouse management that create competitive advantage and enhanced customer service. Develops understanding of the role of transportation and warehousing in the supply chain, various transportation modes and their intermodal connectivity, principles of transportation network design, and analysis of warehouse strategic and operational management.

title: Computer Systems for Finance and Investment Management
description: An integrated course examining the interrelationship between finance/investment management and information technology taught using a mixture of theory/concepts and hands-on activities. Topics include spreadsheet design and use in finance and investments, decision support systems and artificial intelligence used in risk analysis, financial database design, and information technologies used to transmit financial and investment information.

title: K-State MIS: Health Info Sys Portfolio I
description: This course serves a mechanism to indicate students have completed a series of Health Information Systems courses at Johnson County Community College in the process of completing their Associate's Degree. It is used solely for transfer credit tracking and fulfills the thematic sequence requirement in the College of Business. Students cannot enroll in this class nor can this course be transferred into programs other than Management Information Systems in the College of Business Administration.

title: K-State MIS: Health Info Sys Portfolio II
description: This course serves a mechanism to indicate students have completed a series of Health Information Systems courses at Johnson County Community College in the process of completing their Associate's Degree. It is used solely for transfer credit tracking and fulfills the unrestricted electives requirement in the College of Business. Students cannot enroll in this class nor can this course be transferred into programs other than Management Information Systems in the College of Business Administration.

title: Strategic Management
description: Undergraduate capstone course that requires integration of knowledge gained in prior functional coursework and application of strategic management concepts to develop value-creating solutions to business challenges. Students develop the knowledge and skills necessary to identify and analyze strategic issues, formulate strategic plans, and implement strategic initiatives within organizations.

title: Business Ethics and Corporate Citizenship
description: Delivers a managerial perspective on how corporations can fulfill their economic, legal, ethical, and philanthropic responsibilities to a broad array of business stakeholders.

title: Managing for Sustainability: Strategy, Investing, and Impact
description: Examines how organizations create long-term value for stakeholders and society through environmental, social, and governance (ESG) strategy, investment, and impact. Students explore the concept of business sustainability from an applied perspective, to understand better how economic, human, and natural environmental systems are interlinked. Specific topics include the formulation and implementation of ESG strategies within organizations, managing relationships with stakeholders, and the measurement and disclosure of ESG impact.

title: Compensation and Performance Management
description: An in-depth analysis of compensation system design and performance management strategies needed to attract, retain, develop and motivate the human capital required for organizational effectiveness and strategic success.

title: Business Law II
description: A study of law as it relates to business, including securities regulation and business crimes, secured transactions, bankruptcy, and principles of agency and employment law.

title: Advanced Human Resource Management
description: An in-depth analysis of selected topics in human resource management including study of current research and literature.

title: Organizational Staffing
description: Focuses on recruitment, selection, and retention issues, including an in-depth consideration of legal and practical issues involved in the acquisition and retention of human resources. Detailed coverage of job analysis, criterion development, recruitment strategies, and techniques and procedures for ensuring valid and effective selection of employees.

title: Constraint Management and Continuous Improvement
description: The intent of this course is to provide an overview of the application of Theory of Constraints (TOC). TOC suggests that every process or system has at least one constraint that prevents the operation from being more efficient. TOC offers methodologies that are specifically developed to identify and manage constraints to enable the operation to achieve its goals. Students will be taught the skills required for the identification and management of constraints within an operation system.

title: Supply Chain Planning and Control
description: Provides a framework of how the demand side of the supply chain should interface with supply side in the context of global manufacturing and service. Topics include: layout/process design and control, capacity planning, master scheduling, materials requirement, coordination, and revenue management.

title: Procurement, Logistics and Supply Chain Design
description: Addresses the interrelationship between operations and other functions required to deliver value to the end customer of a supply chain. Topics include: strategic procurement and purchasing operations and process, sourcing decisions, location strategies, distribution network design, global supply chain network design, and supply chain risk management.

title: Supply Chain Analytics
description: The objective of this course is to introduce models and tools to effectively manage a supply chain that purchase, produce, and distribute products. Topics include: forecasting, production and logistics, and inventory management. We illustrate the effectiveness of these models and tools using real-life cases and examples drawn from diverse industries.

title: Operations and Supply Chain Management
description: The study of the role of operations systems in the provision of value for the customer. Operations systems design, capacity determination, resource requirements planning and control, theory of constraints, supply chain management, quality management and control and project management are discussed and analyzed.

title: Managing Organizational Behavior
description: An in-depth analysis of the development of the behavioral bases of individual and group behavior in business, governmental, educational, and other organizations with emphasis on current research literature and applications.

title: Strategic Management Analytics
description: This course exposes students to a diverse set of research methods and data analytic techniques used to address organizational problems. Students engage in experiential learning to develop their capabilities in collecting, organizing, analyzing, and visualizing data, making inferences from data analytic processes, and communicating solutions to organizational stakeholders through storytelling.

title: Legal And Social Environment Of Business
Advanced Management Consulting
description: This course provides an overview of the management consulting process. Students will learn how to apply consulting tools and frameworks to engage clients, analyze organizational problems, formulate and present recommendations, and implement change initiatives. The content is relevant to anyone interested in analyzing internal business opportunities, tasked with managing organizational change, or those pursuing traditional consulting careers.

title: Advanced Business Law
description: A study of civil law as it affects commercial transactions, including corporations, partnerships, property, commercial paper, and secured transactions.

title: Business Project Management
description: Provides an in-depth coverage of project management concepts and methodolgogies required for service and manufacturing operations.  Topics to be covered include, but are not limited to:  project selection and evaluation, project dynamics, motivation and evaluation of project team members, project scheduling, project budgeting, and project closure.

title: Managing the Triple Bottom Line Business
description: Examines how organizations can deliver sustainable social, environmental, and economic benefits to a plurality of stakeholders. Topics include best practices for business ethics, corporate social responsibility, and triple bottom line management.

title: Enterprise Information Systems Management
description: Concepts and information resource management techniques are integrated into a conceptual framework of enterprise information systems. The impact of current issues and new technologies on business is emphasized through discussion and application.

title: Managing Animal Health Organizations
description: Relevant theoretical concepts will be drawn from strategic management, management of change, human resource management, and the management of innovation to provide and intellectual grounding for understanding industry dynamics and the analysis of contemporary managerial challenges and issues for organizations in the animal health industry. Special emphasis will be given to proposing strategic solutions to solve major problems that companies face in the animal health corridor.

title: Strategic Management
description: Graduate capstone course that requires integration of knowledge gained in prior functional coursework and application of strategic management concepts to develop value-creating solutions to business challenges. Students develop the knowledge and skills necessary to identify and analyze strategic issues, formulate strategic plans, and implement strategic initiatives within organizations.

title: International Management
description: Analysis of the political, legal, economic, and sociocultural environments and how they influence international management decisions such as timing and place of foreign market entry, mode of entry, and formulation and implementation of international strategies. Students develop the knowledge and skills necessary to more effectively manage the international operations of organizations.

title: Legal And Social Environment Of Business
International Operations Management
description: Explores the global market context and the environment for multinational operations. Examines important tools, concepts, theories, models, and structures as defined and applied to manufacturing and service operations in other countries. Emphasis is placed on Japanese technology and practices. It draws upon those aspects of manufacturing and service operations that can help U.S. firms become increasingly competitive worldwide.

title: Management Science
description: The application of management science methods to business problems to provide a basis for rational decision making. Includes mathematical programming, inventory theory, simulation, model building, and heuristics.

title: Topics in Management Contemporary Issues in Management
description: Discussion and analysis of contemporary issues in management, including applications, development and study of relevant literature and research findings.

title: Applied Case Analysis
description: Students may apply for acceptance into this course to receive credit for participating on a graduate-level case competition team. Students will develop advanced analytical and presentation skills through lectures, discussions, practice cases, and participation in either in-person or virtual case competition events. This course provides unique opportunities for specialized training, individual feedback, faculty mentorship, and networking with students from other universities.

title: Intermediate Algebra
description: Preparatory course for MATH 100. Includes arithmetic (signed numbers, polynomials, algebraic fractions, exponents, and roots), solutions to equations (linear, quadratic, polynomial, root, and fractional), graphs (linear and quadratic), and geometry (area, perimeter, and the Pythagorean Theorem).

title: Intermediate Algebra Review
description: Supplemental algebra lab that is required to be taken in conjunction with MATH 010.

title: College Algebra Review
description: Supplemental algebra lab to be taken in conjunction with MATH 100 for students who need additional instruction in algebra.

title: College Algebra
description: Fundamental concepts of algebra; algebraic equations and inequalities; functions and graphs; zeros of polynomial functions; exponential and logarithmic functions; systems of equations and inequalities.

title: College Algebra Support
description:
Note: College Algebra Support credits cannot count towards the 120 credits of the degree program. This course was designed through the Kansas Board of Regents Math Pathways initiative. Students who do not meet one of the multiple placement measures must enroll concurrently in MATH 101 and MATH 100. 

College Algebra Support is the corequisite support course for MATH 100 - College Algebra. The support course provides additional review of the background knowledge and foundational skills which promote student success in MATH 100. The content of MATH 101 is directly aligned with the course objectives of MATH 100.

title: Plane Trigonometry
description: Trigonometric and inverse trigonometric functions; trigonometric identities and equations; applications involving right triangles and applications illustrating the laws of sines and cosines.

title: Applied Plane Trigonometry
description: Trigonometry and inverse trigonometric functions, trigonometric identities and equations; applications involving right triangles and applications illustrating the laws of sines and cosines. Emphasis is placed on applications to engineering technology, tool and machine design.

title: Introduction to Contemporary Mathematics
description: Mathematics as used in the contemporary world. Combinatorics and probability; descriptions of data; graph theory; and various additional topics selected by the individual instructors.

title: Contemporary Mathematics Support
description:
Note: Contemporary Mathematics Support credits cannot count towards the 120 credits of the degree program. This course was designed through the Kansas Board of Regents Math Pathways initiative. Students who do not meet one of the multiple placement measures must enroll concurrently in MATH 161 and MATH 160.

Contemporary Mathematics Support is the corequisite support course for MATH 160 - Contemporary Mathematics. The support course provides additional review of the background knowledge and foundational skills which promote student success in MATH 160. The content of MATH 161 is directly aligned with the course objectives of MATH 160.

title: Undergraduate Mathematics Seminar
description: Topics of special interest to undergraduates in mathematics, including orientation to the mathematics curriculum, possible careers in mathematics, and cultural and professional aspects of mathematics.

title: General Calculus
description: Introduction to calculus concepts that are particularly useful to the study of economics and business administration with special emphasis on working problems.

title: Technical Calculus I
description: A condensed course in analytic geometry and differential calculus with an emphasis on applications.

title: Tech Calc II
description: A continuation of MATH 210 to include integral calculus with an emphasis on application.

title: Calculus I
description: Course content includes a brief review of pre-calculus materials of algebra and trigonometry, functions, limits, differentiation, applications of differentiation, integration, and applications of the definite integral. Theory is presented in a style tailored for first-semester students of mathematics.

title: Calculus II
description: An extension of MATH 215, Calculus I, to include integration, differentiation, and applications of transcendent functions.

title: Analytic Geometry and Calculus I
description: Analytic geometry, differential and integral calculus of algebraic and trigonometric functions.

title: Analytic Geometry and Calculus II
description: Continuation of MATH 220 to include transcendental functions, techniques of integration, and infinite series.

title: Analytic Geometry and Calculus III
description: Continuation of MATH 221 to include functions of more than one variable.

title: Algebra and Calculus in Euclidean Spaces 1
Algebra and Calculus in Euclidean Spaces 2
Mathematical Scholars Calculus l
description: A rigorous introduction to the central ideas and techniques of calculus where students will be exposed to both the computational and conceptual underpinnings of calculus including functions, graphs, limits, continuity, derivatives and integrals. Enrollment is not restricted to students in the Mathematical Scholars Program.

title: Mathematical Scholars Calculus ll
description: A continuation of MATH 250 where students will be trained in both the computational and conceptual underpinnings of calculus including techniques of integration, transcendental functions, infinite sequences and infinite series.

title: Topics In Mathematics For Elementary School Teachers
Finite Applications of Mathematics
description: Systems of equations, vector operations, linear algebra, and linear programming. Practice in setting up, solving, and interpreting mathematical models which arise in social sciences and business.

title: Computational Number Theory
description: Topics in number theory selected from: divisibility, primes, modular arithmetic and special types of numbers. Emphasis is on computations. Primarily for prospective elementary school teachers of mathematics.

title: Mathematics For Elementary School Teachers I
description: Mathematical problem solving and reasoning, development of whole number concepts and the whole number system, computation and estimation with whole numbers, number patterns and number theory, integers, fractions and rational numbers, decimals and real numbers, geometry and measurement.

title: Alg Calc Euc
description: Geometric figures and patterns, properties of geometric figures, transformation and coordinate geometry, measurement.

title: Mathematical Scholars Calculus lV
description: A continuation in the sequence of courses focused on Calculus. Provides the second semester of an integrated approach to linear algebra, multivariable calculus, and ordinary differential equations. Students will apply knowledge from one variable calculus and linear algebra to the higher dimensional setting, but many computations will take place in the low 2 and 3 variable cases. Topics include linear ordinary differential equations, Picard's Theorem, limits and continuity in many variables, partial derivatives, optimization problems, integration of functions of several variables, Fubini's Theorem, integration of differential forms and Generalized Stokes' Theorem.

title: Academic Excellence Workshop
description: This course provides enriched supplementary instruction to selected students enrolled in selected lower-division courses.

title: Applications Of Mathematics To Agricultural Economics
Introduction to Set Theory & Metric Spaces I
description: Introduction to Set Theory & Metric Spaces I

title: Introduction to Set Theory & Metric Spaces I I
description: Introduction to Set Theory & Metric Spaces I I

title: Honors Project
description: Open only to Arts and Sciences students who are active members of the University Honors Program.

title: Undergraduate Topics In Mathematics
description: Reading courses in advanced undergraduate mathematics.

title: Mathematical Theory of Interest
description: Extensive introduction to the mathematical theory of interest with applications to the field of actuarial science. Prepares students to take the second professional examination administered by the Society of Actuaries and the Casualty Actuarial Society.

title: Mathematical Foundations of Actuarial Science
description: Introduction to calculus-based applications and models with an emphasis on applications to insurance. Prepares students to take the first professional examination administered by the Society of Actuaries and Casualty Actuarial Society.

title: Introduction to Number Theory
description: Divisibility properties of integers, prime numbers, congruences, multiplicative functions.

title: Topics In Mathematics For Elementary School Teachers
description: Topics In Mathematics For Elementary School Teachers

title: Discrete Mathematics
description: Combinatorics and graph theory. Topics selected from counting principles, permutations and combinations, the inclusion-exclusion principle, recurrence relations, trees, graph coloring, Eulerian and Hamiltonian circuits, block designs, and Ramsey Theory.

title: Introduction to Algebraic Systems
description: Properties of groups, rings, domains and fields. Examples selected from subsystems of the complex numbers, elementary number theory, and solving equations.

title: Introduction to Modern Algebra
description: Introduction to the basic algebraic systems, viz., groups, rings, integral domains, and fields, often drawing from elementary number theory. Special emphasis will be given to methods of theorem proving.

title: Introduction to Linear Algebra
description: Finite-dimensional vector spaces, linear transformations and their matrix representations, dual spaces, invariant subspaces, Euclidean and unitary spaces, solution spaces for systems of linear equations.

title: Foundations of Analysis
description: A study of sets and sequences, neighborhoods, limit points, convergence, open and closed sets in the real line and in the plane, the concept of a continuous function.

title: The Real Number System
description: An extensive development of number systems, with emphasis upon structure. Includes systems of natural numbers, integers, rational numbers, and real numbers.

title: Advanced Ordinary Differential Equations
description: First-order scalar equations: geometry of integral curves, symmetries and exactly soluble equations; existence, uniqueness and dependence on parameters with examples. Systems of first-order equations, Hamilton's equations and classical mechanics, completely integrable systems. Higher-order equations. Initial value problems for second order linear equations, series solutions and special functions. Boundary value problems with applications. Introduction to perturbation theory and stability.

title: Applied Matrix Theory
description: Matrix algebra, solutions to systems of linear equations, determinants, vector spaces, linear transformations, eigenvalues, linear programming, approximation techniques.

title: Introduction to Topology
description: An introduction to the basic topological concepts. Topological spaces, metric spaces, closure, interior, and frontier operators, subspaces, separation and countability properties, bases, sub bases, convergence, continuity, homeomorphisms, compactness, connectedness, quotients and products. The course will include a brief introduction to proof techniques and set theory. Other topics in topology also may be included.

title: History of Mathematics
description: A survey of the development of mathematics from ancient to modern times.

title: Foundations of Geometry
description: Euclidean, non-Euclidean, and finite geometries; role of axioms; practice proving theorems in a formal system; synthetic, metric, and transformation approaches to Euclidean geometry.

title: Topics in Mathematics For Teachers
description: Topics of importance for teachers of mathematics.

title: Introduction to Digital Image Processing
description: The basic ideas and techniques in digital image processing stem from mathematics, engineering, and computer science. This course focuses on ideas and techniques such as spatial filtering, frequency filtering (Fast Fourier Transform), scale filtering (Fast Wavelet Transform), and their applications to image compression standards and image recognition. The course will place equal emphasis on the mathematical ideas and their applications.

title: Convex Optimization for Data Science
description: Convex optimization is an essential tool in data science and underlies many machine learning algorithms. This course provides students with the necessary tools, skills, vocabulary, and techniques, that will allow them to become life-long users of convex optimization in the context of data analysis.

title: Mathematical Principles of Artificial Intelligence
description: Examines the mathematical principles of artificial intelligence. Provides a survey of core topics, including optimization and automatic differentiation. Explores how neural network architectures can be generalized to learn from structured data, such as graphs and manifolds, by leveraging principles of symmetry and geometric invariance.

title: Introduction to Complex Analysis
description: Complex analytic functions and power series, complex integrals. Taylor and Laurent expansions, residues, Laplace transformations, and the inversion integral.

title: Elementary Partial Differential Equations
description: Orthogonal functions, Fourier series, boundary value problems in partial differential equations.

title: Advanced Calculus I
description: Functions of one variable; limits, continuity, differentiability, Riemann-Stieltjes integral, sequences, series, power series, improper integrals.

title: Advanced Calculus II
description: Functions of several variables; partial differentiation and implicit function theorems, curvilinear coordinates, differential geometry of curves and surfaces, vectors and vector fields, line and surface integrals, double and triple integrals, Green's Theorem, Stokes' Theorem, and Divergence Theorem.

title: Elementary Numerical Analysis I
description: Error analysis, root finding, interpolation, approximation of functions, numerical integration and differentiation, systems of linear equations.

title: Elementary Numerical Analysis II
description: A continuation of MATH 655. Linear programming, numerical solutions of differential equations, and the use of standard packages for the solutions of applied problems.

title: Mathematical Modeling
description: Introduction of modeling procedures. Case studies in mathematical modeling projects from physical, biological, and social sciences.

title: Set Theory and Logic
description: An introduction to logic, mathematical proof, and elementary set theory. Topics include elementary logic, relations, partitions, functions, cartesian products, disjoint unions, orders, construction of the natural numbers, ordinal and cardinal numbers, the Axiom of Choice, and transfinite induction. Special emphasis will be given to proving theorems.

title: Introduction to the Theory of Groups
description: Introduction to abstract group theory, permutation groups, homomorphisms, direct products, Abelian groups. Jordan-Holder and Sylow theorems.

title: Computational Mathematics
description: Topics from complex analysis, vector calculus, higher-dimensional calculus, ordinary differential equations, liner algebra, and geometry of curves.

title: Theory of Numbers
description: Divisibility, congruences, multiplicative functions, number theory from an algebraic viewpoint, quadratic reciprocity, Diophantine equations, prime numbers.

title: Theory of Numbers II
description: Continuation of MATH 706, including p-adic numbers, basic analytic number theory, geometry of numbers, and diophantine approximation.

title: Introduction to Category Theory
description: Categories, duality, special morphisms, functors, natural transformations, limits and colimits, adjoint situations, and applications.

title: Category Theory
description: Set-valued functors and concrete categories, factorization structures, algebraic and topological functors, categorical completions, Abelian categories.

title: Advanced Applied Matrix Theory
description: A development of the concepts of eigenvalues by considering applications in differential equations, quadratic forms and estimation problems. Supporting topics include the Jordan canonical form, functions of matrices, vector and matrix norms, convex sets, with selected topics from the theory and application of the simplex algorithm, Markov chains, Leslie population models, Leontieff input-output model.

title: Applied Mathematics I
description: Analysis of numerical methods for linear algebra.  Perturbation theory and error analysis, matrix factorizations, solutions to linear systems, least-squares, problems, techniques for special matrix structures, symmetric and nonsymmetric eigenvalue problems, iterative and direct methods.

title: Applied Mathematics II
description: Linear operator theory applied to matrix, integral and differential equations. Spectral theory, the Fredholm Alternative, least-squares and pseudo-inverses. Banach and Hilbert space techniques, Fourier series and wavelets, theory of distributions, Green's functions.

title: Introduction to Real Analysis
description: Limits, continuity, uniform convergence, completeness, differentiation, Riemann integration.

title: Introduction to Functions of Several Variables
description: Analysis of functions of several variables, including differentiability, partial differentiability, maxima and minima, inverse function theorem, implicit function theorem, integration, Fubini's theorem.

title: Complex Functions
description: Introduction to the theory of analytic functions, designed to prepare students for the qualifying exams. Holomorphic functions, contour integrals, residue theory, conformal mapping and other topics.

title: The Mathematics of Data and Networks II
description: Continuation of MATH 725. Develops the mathematical tools necessary for studying and handling large data-sets and networks. Includes matrix, graph, and probability theory, dimensionality reduction, clustering, maximum likelihood, Bayesian networks, sparsification, modularity, information theory, hidden Markov chains, branching processes, electrical networks, internet mathematics, random walks, random graphs, random matrices, and search algorithms.

title: Abstract Algebra I
description: Groups, rings, fields, vector spaces and their homomorphisms. Elementary Galois theory and decomposition theorems for linear transformations on a finite dimensional vector space.

title: Calculus of Variations
description: Necessary conditions and the Euler-Lagrange equations. Hamilton-Jacobi theory, Noether's theorems, direct methods, applications to geometry and physics.

title: Ordinary Differential Equations
description: First-order equations and applications, second-order equations and oscillation theorems, series solutions and special functions, Sturm Liouville problems, linear systems, autonomous systems and phase plane analysis, stability, Liapunov's method, periodic solutions, perturbation and asymptotic methods, existence and uniqueness theorems.

title: Professional Development Seminar for Intelligence and Learning Practitioners
description: Develop skills for building their professional identity, including creating profiles on professional social networking platforms, writing effective resumes, developing interviewing skills, and building professional portfolios.  Will include panels of alumni and practitioners in the fields of Intelligence and Learning.

title: Linear Algebra for Data Science
description: Linear algebra with applications to core machine learning algorithms. Topics include vector spaces, subspaces, basis, and dimension, matrix operations, eigenvalue and eigenvector analysis, conditioning of matrix operations, singular value decomposition, generalized inverses.

title: Dynamic Modeling Process
description: Topics to include equilibrium and stability, limit circles, reaction-diffusion, and shock phenomena, Hopf bifurcation and cusp catastrophes, chaos and strange attractors, bang-bang principle. Applications from physical and biological sciences and engineering.

title: Introduction to Probability Theory
description: An introduction to the mathematical theory of probability. Material covered includes combinatorial probability, random variables, independence, expectations, limit theorems, Markov chains, random walks, and martingales.

title: Introduction to Topology/Geometry I
description: This is the first course of a two course sequence including definitions and examples of topological spaces, simplicial complexes, topological and smooth manifolds, quotient spaces, CW complexes, projective spaces and knots. Topological properties including elementary separation, compactness, connectedness. Homotopy, the fundamental group, Covering spaces, Euler characteristics, classification of closed surfaces, differential forms, integration, Stokes' theorem, de Rham cohomology.

title: Introduction to Topology/Geometry II
description: The second course in a two class introduction to topology at a graduate level. Second term topics include: covering spaces, Euler characteristic, classification of closed surfaces, differential forms, integration, Stokes' theorem, and de Rham cohomology.

title: Elementary Differential Geometry
description: Curves and surfaces in Euclidean spaces, differential forms and exterior differentiation, differential invariants and frame fields, uniqueness theorems for curves and surfaces, geodesics, introduction to Riemannian geometry, some global theorems, minimal surfaces.

title: Numerical Solution Of Ordinary Differential Equations
Numerical Solution Of Partial Differential Equations
Combinatorial Analysis
description: Permutations, combinations, inversion formulas, generating functions, partitions, finite geometries, difference sets, and other topics.

title: Topics In Mathematics For Secondary School Teachers
description: Topics of importance in the preparation of secondary school teachers to teach modern mathematics.

title: Statistical and Mathematical Intelligence and Learning Capstone Experience
description: A research, extension, or industry-based experience that builds upon an individual student’s academic training and personal experience in preparation for a successful career. This course is a hands-on project on solving a practical problem from delimitating the project goals, establishing a data gathering/usage strategy, identifying the analysis strategy, conducting the analysis, providing insights, and effectively communicating the results.

title: Topics in Mathematics
description: Courses on special topics to be taught on sufficient demand.

title: Numerical Solution of Ordinary Differential Equations I
description: Single and multistep methods for initial-value problems for ordinary differential equations; discretization and round-off error; consistency, convergence, and stability of these methods; stiff equations and implicit methods; two-point boundary value problems; initial and boundary-value problems for partial differential equations; finite difference methods; marching schemes for parabolic and hyperbolic problems; consistency, stability, convergence, and the Lax equivalence theorem; treatment of boundary conditions; boundary-value problems for elliptic equations; relaxation, alternating direction, and strongly-implicit iterative methods; nonlinear problems; finite element method.

title: Numerical Solution of Partial Differential Equations
Higher Algebra I
description: Theory of groups, theory of rings and ideals, polynomial domains, theory of fields and their extensions.

title: Homological Algebra I
description: Introduction to the basics of homological and cohomological delta functors with applications to specific categories. Topics include Abelian categories, functors, exactness, chain complexes and maps, cochain complexes and maps, projective and injective resolutions, left and right derived functors, homological dimension, double complexes, spectral sequences, Tor and Ext functors, cohomology of groups, Lie algebras, and Hochschild homology.

title: Homological Algebra ll
description: Introduction to special homology and cohomology theories. Topics include Galois cohomology, cyclic homology, local cohomology theories, localization, derived categories, and the calculus of fractions in Abelian categories.

title: Lie Algebras and Representations I
description: Introduction to Lie algebras. Topics include ideals, homomorphisms, nilpotent and solvable algebras, radicals, killing forms, Cartan subalgebras, semisimple Lie algebras and root systems, classification of semisimple Lie algebras, conjugacy theorems, enveloping algebras and PBW theorems, Serre relations, and constructions of semisimple Lie algebras and their enveloping algebras.

title: Lie Algebras and Representations II
description: Introduction to Kac-Moody algebras and their representations, Verma modules and BGG categories, and the Kac-Weyl character formula. In addition, special topics include quantum groups and their representations.

title: Algebraic Geometry I
description: Introduction to affine algebraic varieties over algebraically closed fields. Topics include Hilbert Nullstellensatz, Zarski topology, morphisms, differentials, smoothness, separability, and normality, algebraic and projective varieties, sheaf theory, sheaf cohomology, and vector bundles.

title: Algebraic Geometry ll
description: Introduction to schemes. Topics include categories, representable functors, prime spectra, algebraic schemes, separable schemes, normal schemes, proper schemes, schemes with bases, completions, formal schemes, Zariski's Theorem, Frobenius morphisms, and etale morphisms.

title: Introduction to Algebraic Groups I
description: Introduction to algebraic groups. Topics include a review of algebraic geometry on varieties and morphisms, Lie algebras of algebraic groups, actions of algebraic groups over algebraic varieties, Jordan-Chevalley decompositions, solvable and unipotent algebraic groups, radicals and unipotent radicals, Borel subgroups, and parabolic subgroups.

title: Introduction to Algebraic Groups ll
description: Introduction to reductive and semisimple algebraic groups, Bruhat decompositions, flag varieties, cohomology groups of line bundles over flag manifolds, Chevalley groups and their representations, Weyl modules and classification of irreducible modules, group functors and schemes, Hopf algebras and their representations.

title: Real Analysis
description: Measure theory and integration, Lebesgue integration, Lp-spaces, Hilbert spaces, integration on product spaces, Fubini's theorem.

title: Real Analysis II
description: Measure and integration on locally compact spaces: Radon measures, the Riesz Representation Theorem. Signed measures and differentiation: Lebesgue decomposition, Radon-Nikodym theorem, Differentiation of measures. Some topics on Probability (e.g., probability measures, the Central Limit Theorem, the Law of Large Numbers) or on Harmonic analysis groups, depending on the interests of students in class

title: Geometric Function and Measure Theory I
description: Topics include general measure theory, covering theorems, Hausdorff measure, area and coarea formulas, distributions, Sobolev spaces, Poincare' inequalities, embeddings theorem, changes of variables, extentions, and capacity. Applications include quasiconformal and quasiregular maps in IRIN  and analysis on fractals.

title: Geometric Function and Measure Theory II
description: Continuation of Geometric Function and Measure Theory I.

title: Complex Analysis I
description: Holomorphic functions, harmonic functions, the Cauchy integral theorem, normal families and the Riemann mapping theorem, and the Mittag-Leffler theorem.

title: Complex Analysis II
description: Analytic continuation, the Picard theorem, Hp-spaces, elementary theory of Banach algebra, the theory of Fourier transforms, and the Paley-Wiener theorems.

title: Classical and Modern Fourier Analysis I
description: Topics include Fourier analysis on the circle, singular integrals of convolution type, Littlewood-Paley theory and multipliers, BMO and Carleson Measures, and boundedness and convergence of Fourier integrals (or singular integrals of nonconvolution type).

title: Classical and Modern Fourier Analysis II
description: Continuation of Classical and Modern Fourier Analysis I.

title: Algebraic Number Theory
description: Topics include lattice theory, geometry of numbers, algebraic number fields, Dedekind domains, discrete valuation rings, ideal class group, group units, cyclotomic fields, Galois extensions.

title: Analytic Number Theory
description: Potential topics include; prime number theorem characters, L-functions, Diophantine problems, exponential sums, p-adic analysis, Diophantine approximations, Sieve Methods, Circle method.

title: Differential Equations I
description: Basic ordinary and partial differential equations. First-order ordinary differential equations: symmetries and solutions in quadratures; existence, uniqueness and dependence on parameters, systems of first order equations, analysis of equilibria. Second order equations: series solutions and special functions, initial- and boundary-value problems for second-order equations. Elements of integral equations. First-order partial differential equations. Basic second-order partial differential equations: wave equations, heat equations, Poisson equation, Schrodinger equation.

title: Differential Equations II
description: Where PDEs come from. Initial and boundary-value problems. A crash course in distribution theory: different spaces of distributions, Fourier and Laplace transformations of distributions, Sobolev spaces. The Poisson equation in bounded and in exterior domains; properties of solutions of elliptic equations. The classical evolution equations revisited. Energy estimates, existence and uniqueness theorems, regularity and other properties of solutions. Simple examples of nonlinear PDEs.

title: Advanced Probability I
description: Review of measure theory notions specific to probability, including classical limit theorems, constructions of Brownian motion, Stochastic integration, the martingale representation theorem and martingale-based function spaces.

title: Advanced Probability II
description: Topics may include stochastic processes, random matrix theory, free probability, random fractals and random analytic maps.

title: Functional Analysis I
description: Topological vector spaces; locally convex spaces (Hahn-Banach Theorem, weak topology, dual pairs, Krein-Milman Theorem, theory of distributions); Banach spaces (Uniform Boundedness Principle, Open Mapping Theorem and applications, Alaoglu's Theorem, analytic vector-valued functions, Krein-Smulian Theorem); C(X) as a Banach space (Stone-Weierstrass Theorem, Riesz Theorem); Lp spaces.

title: Functional Analysis II
description: Banach Algebras (spectrum, Gelfand Fourier transform, holomorphic functional calculus); Hilbert spaces (geometric properties, Riesz's Theorem, projections, the adjoint); functional calculus for normal operators; compact operators (spectral properties, Min-Max Theorem, Schatten-vol Neumann classes); Fredholm operators; other operator topologies on B(H); unbounded self-adjoint operators. Other topics.

title: Methods of Applied Mathematics I
description: An introduction to the mathematical techniques of problem solving in the sciences and engineering. Construction of mathematical models; problem formulation, dimensional analysis and scaling; solution methods for differential equations and difference equations; methods for obtaining approximate solutions; regular and singular perturbations methods, asymptotic series, applications to specific equations and scientific problems.

title: Methods of Applied Mathematics II
description: A continuation of MATH 855. Asymptotic expansion of integrals; the methods of stationary phase and steepest descent; summations of series, the Shanks transformation and the Pade fractions; boundary layer theory; the WKB and Langer approximations; the method of averaging and the method of multiple scales.

title: Nonlinear Analysis I
description: The course deals with studies of nonlinear operator equations, existence of their solutions, uniqueness of the solutions, numerical methods for finding solutions. Fixed point theorems, topological principles, nonlinear elliptic equations and evolution equations, ill-posed and inverse problems are discussed.

title: Numerical Analysis I
description: Topics covered may include elementary functional analysis relevant to numerical analysis; numerical solution of differential or integral equations; analysis of stability and convergence; numerical linear algebra including large-scale systems; approximation theory.

title: Theory of Ordinary Differential Equations I
description: The modern theory of ordinary differential equations including general theory and the theory of linear differential equations.

title: Theory of Ordinary Differential Equations II
description: Continuation of MATH 864 to include nonlinear equations and differential equations in Banach spaces.

title: Algebraic Topology I
description: Homology and cohomology; simplicial, singular, cellular, and axiomatic theories; cup products; universal coefficient and Kunneth theorems; Hurewicz theorem in dimension one; orientation of manifolds; degree of maps spectral sequences, Whitehead's theorem.

title: Algebraic Topology II
description: Continuation of MATH 875. Fibrations, cofibrations, higher homotopy groups, characteristic classes, sheaves, rational homotopy theory, simplicial objects, spectra, K-theory.

title: Classical and Quantum General Relativity
description: A self contained introduction to general Relativity, followed by a mathematical introduction to quantum mechanics and quantum field theory. Course culminates in review of current approaches to quantizing general Relativity.

title: Differential Topology
description: Tangent spaces and tensor bundles; derivatives; inverse function theorem, Sard's theorem and applications; vector fields and flows; Morse functions; handle decompositions; embedding theorems; orientations and orientability via tangent bundles; intersection theory; cobordism theory.

title: Differential Geometry
description: Riemannian metrics, covariant derivatives, Gauss curvature, Riemann curvature; Gauss-Bonnet theorem; Exponential map and normal coordinates; The Hopf-Rinow Theorem; Jacobi fields; The Cartan-Hadamard Theorem; Ricci curvature and Myers' and Bonnet's Theorems; selected topics and K hler geometry; symplectic topology; gauge theory.

title: Riemann Surfaces
description: Introduction to Riemann Surfaces. Topics will include complex charts, complex structures, holomorphic and meromorphic maps (and functions), order of poles and zeros, covering spaces, monodromy of holomorphic maps, differential forms, DeRham cohomology groups, integration on Riemann surfaces, Cech cohomology of sheaves, compact Riemann surfaces, finite theorems, divisors and sheaves of meromorphic forms, and the Riemann Roch Theorem.

title: Theory of Groups
description: Group representations and group characters, transfer, signalizer functors, theory of pushing-up, groups of Lie type, (B, N)-pairs, chamber systems and buildings, sporadic simple groups, amalgam methods, Bass-Serre theory.

title: Several Complex Variables
description: An introduction to the theory and analytic functions of several variables, domains of homomorphy and pseudoconvexity, the Levi problem, delta bar equations, Cousin problems, zeros of analytic functions, integral formulas, holomorphic mappings.

title: Group Representations and Character Theory I
description: The basic topics in representation theory are covered: Schur's Lemma, irreducibility, class functions, characters, orthogonality relations, Frobenius-Schur theorem, induced characters and Frobenius reciprocity, Mackey's theorem, Clifford's theorem, exceptional characters and applications to group orders, generalized characters and Brauer's characterizations of characters.

title: Group Representations and Character Theory II
description: Depending on the interests of the students, topics may be chosen from the following: modular representations, Brauer's theory of blocks, characters of the linear groups, homologically induced representations, representations of complex Lie algebras.

title: SMC Orientation
description: An overview of the curriculum electives, extracurricular activities in mass communications, the advising process, career options, and ways to improve student success.

title: Mass Communication in Society
description: A historical, social, legal, economic, and technological study of mass communication and its role and impact in society.

title: Principles of Advertising
description: An examination of the advertising field and its relationship to marketing and journalism.

title: Writing Conventions and Mechanics
description: Reorientation to basic rules of grammar, spelling and punctuation, and assessment of student writing skills prior to admission to the A.Q. Miller School. Students must receive a ""C"" or better to advance in JMC curriculum.

title: Elements of Media Writing
description: The basics of Associated Press writing style and writing across media platforms. Students must receive a ""C"" or better to advance in JMC curriculum.

title: Writing Styles and Audiences
description: Basic exposure to writing across platforms for target audiences in journalism and strategic communications. Students must receive a ""C"" or better to advance in JMC curriculum.

title: Principles of Journalism
description: An overview of the current news industry, including legacy media and newer platforms. Introduction to news gathering and reporting, including hands-on work on the multi-platform JMC NewsDesk.

title: KSDB-FM Participation
description: Students participate in various music, news, sports, program production and other operational roles on student radio station KSDB-FM.

title: KKSU-TV Participation
description: Exposure to basic video production roles and participation in producing a television program for KKSU-TV.

title: Principles of Public Relations
description: Contemporary persuasive social science principles, processes, and issues involved in the management of communications between an organization and its publics.

title: Topics in Journalism and Mass Communications
description: Selected topics in the study of mass communication practices and principles.

title: Audio Essentials
description: Basic audio production concepts and techniques.

title: Photography Essentials
description: Builds a working knowledge of photographic theory and practical application of photo composition, editing and production using DSLR cameras and professional photo editing software. Part of the Content Creator Studio.

title: Video Production Essentials
description: Basic video production practices with a focus on editing concepts.

title: Social Media Essentials
description: Introduction to basic concepts and uses of social media for business and industry. Provides foundational knowledge of today's dynamic social media environment so that students may analyze successful social media programs within their future professions.

title: Creative Design
description: Introduction to essential concepts and skills needed to create effective online visual content. Examines creative strategies, identifies content design techniques that attract attention, and builds skills in developing engaging copy and visuals. Part of the Content Creator Studio.

title: Content Management for Distribution
description: Introduction to basic knowledge and skills needed to manage, structure, and distribute online content and measure content impact. Explores various content types to be used across multiple digital platforms and content strategies such as SEO and paid promotion to maximize visibility. Part of the Content Creator Studio.

title: Podcasting Essentials
description: Introduces students to podcasting and uses hands-on instruction to teach foundational skills and principles in audio, content creation, audience understanding and podcast management.

title: News Reporting and Writing Across Platforms
description: Instruction in news reporting and writing for publication across various media platforms.

title: Innovations in Media and Communication
description: Examines new technological innovations and explores how technology in historical, legal, economic and social contexts combine to enable the changes thought of as innovations in media and communication.

title: Writing for Advertising & Public Relations
description: Examines various forms of advertising and public relations writing techniques, with special emphasis on creation of tactical messages for different media and audiences utilizing persuasion, engagement and professionalism.

title: Topics in Mass Communications
description: Selected topics in the study of mass communication principles and practices.

title: Photo & Video Storytelling
description: Explores the power of journalistic storytelling through professional still and moving images. Develops skills in shooting and retouching photographs for a wide range of genres, creating videos for commercial and journalistic applications, and developing a workflow while building your storytelling portfolio.

title: What's the Score? Sports Media in Society
description: Considers the role of sports and how the way they are covered and portrayed in the media plays a role in society. Examines how gender, race and other identities are reflected in sports coverage and fandom.

title: Interactive Journalism Design
description: Fosters AI literacy and the understanding of its potential and implications for the media industry and news production. Builds on the foundations of news storytelling, data analytics and adds interactivity techniques to help students tell dynamic journalistic stories.

title: Photography and Commercial Imaging
description: Introduces advanced photography and commercial imaging techniques. Builds skill in producing professional images in commercial settings. Examines principles of lighting, photo/video editing, workflow, and monetization of work and portfolio.

title: Photography and Digital Storytelling Abroad
description: Participants in this study abroad program will learn basic digital photography and profile writing techniques through the experience of meeting and telling the stories of those who live and work in Orvieto, Italy. Students will create an online platform to share their stories with the rest of the world.  They will come away with a professional style portfolio piece, as well as a deeper understanding of the stories and customs of a different country.

title: Mass Communications And The World Wide Web
Editing for Print and Online Media
description: Fundamentals of micro and macro journalistic editing for print and digital media including industry standards, practices, and ethics.

title: Immersive Digital Storytelling
description: Builds on theories and concepts of visual communication. Uses special equipment and software programs to introduce concepts and build skills in brainstorming, sketching, filming, modeling, editing, producing visual effects and immersive experiences, and distributing extended reality (XR) stories for both traditional and emerging media platforms.

title: Application of Layout and Printing Techniques in Agriculture and Natural Resources
description: Advances students' understanding of how industry-adopted software and hardware are used to develop print media, from individual use to integrating a variety of software and hardware in production.

title: Theories in Strategic Communication
description: An examination of persuasion and behavior change theories as applied to advertising and public relations messages.

title: Social Media Strategy & Management
description: Strategic methods and approaches for messaging via social media platforms.

title: Social Media Content Development
description: Builds skills in translating ideas into authentic, interactive and meaningful social media content through compelling imagery, video, design, and writing. Covers tools and techniques needed to develop a social media content roadmap for creating engaging content across multiple platforms.

title: Social Media Influencing & Personal Branding
description: Develops skills in working with social media influencers and building personal influence. Examines concepts related to building and analyzing personal brands, creatively designing influencer campaigns, engaging and persuading online audiences, and understanding the legalities of influencing.

title: Topics in Journalism and Mass Communications
description: Selected topics in the study of mass communications practices and principles.

title: Research for Advertising and Public Relations
description: Examines how research data and insights inform successful advertising and public relations strategy. Explores concepts and skills in research methods, database analysis, results communication and visualization, and research-strategy connection.

title: Advanced News Reporting and Writing Across Platforms
description: Intensive course emphasizing advanced reportorial principles and practices for multiple media platforms.

title: Perfect Podcasting
description: Focuses on the history, form, and functions of podcasting as a mass communication tool that is increasingly replacing broadcast radio as America's audio delivery system of choice.

title: Multiplatform News Reporting
description: Advanced techniques of reporting, writing, and producing news for audio, video, and Web-related media.

title: Advanced Strategic Communications Writing
description: Strategic aspects of public relations writing and messaging through advanced types of client communications projects.

title: Sports Reporting
description: The study of today's sports media environment with an emphasis on reporting, writing, and producing sports stories for audio, video, print, Internet, and other platforms.

title: Photojournalism
description: The materials, principles, and processes of photography directed toward visual reporting in newspapers, magazines and other media.

title: Magazine Writing, Editing and Design
description: Preparation of feature stories and articles for Update magazines and other publications; techniques of market analysis, and marketing of articles written in course.

title: Magazine Production
description: The practical application of theory to writing, editing, graphic reproduction, layout and management of magazines.

title: Digital Brand Strategy
description: Exploration of modern digital brand strategy, focusing on the latest approaches to digital advertising, the influence of AI, implementation, and evaluation.

title: Media Planning
description: Builds skills in creating and executing a media plan according to advertising/public relations business goals. Develops skills in multi-media strategy - digital, mobile, social media, tv, audio streaming, radio and print media - based on research and industry trends. Uses simulation software and covers up-to-date digital tools in media planning.

title: Health Communication
description: Developing strategy and messages in health communication campaigns.

title: Media Law & Ethics
description: A study of the legal and ethical issues relating to media, strategic communications and personal media use. Emphasis on defamation, privacy, copyright, administrative controls and other legal areas. Includes a study of ethics in the media, perfect and imperfect ethical duties, and situational ethical decision making.

title: Drone Photography and Video
description: Techniques and procedures to use a Small Unmanned Ariel System (Drone) to acquire and produce video and photographic data that can be used for storytelling, advertising, short film, public relation and other media related analyses.

title: Account Management
description: Examines the role of account managers in modern advertising agencies.

title: Advanced Production
description: Emphasis on aesthetics of audio and video writing, producing, directing, sound recording, lighting, camera work, editing, mixing, and distribution.

title: Multiplatform News Production
description: An advanced production course that emphasizes producing and directing techniques for news and other information-oriented programming.

title: Social Media Management Simulation Lab
description: Uses simulation software to build experience in running a social media campaign for a business. Develops skills in multiple areas of campaign management, including content creation, budget management, impact analysis, and decision-making.

title: Military Strategic Communications
description: An examination of the U.S. military in terms of operational and communication issues and its overall relationship with the media.

title: Travel and Tourism Strategic Communications
description: The role and function of advertising and public relations in travel and tourism, focusing on the development of communication strategies and tactics.

title: Entertainment and Strategic Communications
description: Strategic communications in the entertainment industry and the role that practitioners play in the development of strategies, message development, and management

title: Strategic Communications and Sports
description: Understanding the role public relations and strategic communication play in sports. Defining strategic communication, developing communication strategy, evaluating communication channels and messages. Provides students with public relations management tools.

title: Advertising and Public Relations Design and Production
Ideation, Strategy and Planning for Advertising and Public Relations
description: Develops advertising and public relations skills in long-term planning, strategy, creativity, research, and meaningful evaluation and measurement. Examines concepts such as strategic planning, effective message creation, and project management.

title: Mass Communication Internship
description: Supervised professional experience; reports from student and supervisor required.

title: Media Portfolio
description: Emphasis on enhancement of creative concepts, personal branding and portfolio development.

title: Honors Project
description: Open only to Arts & Sciences students who are active members on the University Honors Program.

title: Social Media News Strategies
description: Social media is an ever-changing and important part of journalism today.  Learn strategies, tips and techniques to be job-ready for to maximize social media strategies, from both sides of the communications pathway, journalism and strategic communications.

title: Concepts And Procedures In Creating Multimedia
Communication Center Of The South:Atlanta Study Tour
Media Audiences
description: Explores the meaning of the term ""audience"" and how it has undergone significant historical shifts, evolving from simply viewers to now consumers, producers, participants and performers. Topics covered include audience history and theory, audience measurement, media fandom and audience subcultures.

title: History of Mass Communication
description: Growth and development of media and media technology in the United States and their economic, political and social significance.

title: Ethics in Mass Communication
description: Making ethical decisions in mass media fields and the principles on which those decisions are based.

title: Mass Communications and Political Campaigns
description: The roles of journalism, advertising and public relations in political campaigns. Examines campaign communication strategies, staffing, and the role of political consultants. Analyzes how voter decision making is affected by mass communications and how voters develop attitudes about candidates and issues.

title: Storytelling Across Platforms
description: Reporting, writing, and production across multiple platforms. Critical analysis of media and engagement strategies for reporting in a digital world.

title: Campaigns in Advertising and Public Relations
description: Implements skills learned in coursework, internships, job experiences, and elsewhere to experience all aspects of strategic communications planning in an applied environment with a client. Capstone course for the Advertising and Public Relations degree.

title: Media Management
description: Issues, ethics and practices in managing media companies, with special emphasis on problems and practices, in converged media operations.

title: Issues in Mass Communication
description: Advanced topics in mass communication practices and principles.

title: Supervision of School Publications
description: A methods course for those planning to teach secondary or community college journalism courses and advise high school or community college publications.

title: Diversity in Media
description: Explores diversity issues in the media, including the nature of mass media's portrayal of gender, race, ethnicity, sexuality, sexual orientation and class in entertainment, news, advertising, public relations, and other media.

title: Communicating in Global Markets
description: Issues associated with advertising in a global environment, including cultural impact, international media, regulation, ethics and digital media technologies.

title: Readings in Mass Communications
description: Investigation of the literature of mass communications.  Three significant books per credit hour with written analysis and oral presentation.

title: Seminar in Public Relations
description: Contemporary topics in public relations practice, theory, and research.

title: Reputation Management
description: Managing the public perception of organizations from the standpoint of preparing for issues and unforeseen emergencies that can potentially disrupt the relationship between organizations and the publics they serve.

title: Orientation to Graduate Studies in Communication
description: Introduction to graduate-level work and practices in the communication disciplines, areas of study and research from current graduate faculty, and graduate life within the A.Q. Miller School of Media and Communication.

title: History of Mass Communications
description: Growth and development of the news media and mass communication media and systems in the United States and their economic, political, and social significance.

title: Environmental Communications
description: Combines theoretical discussions with practical experience regarding communications about environmental issues and provides introduction to natural and applied science topics related to the communications plan.

title: Ethics of Mass Communications
description: Moral analysis, argument, and decision-making by the mass communicator.

title: International Communications
description: Comparative study of world media systems and the role of mass communications in national development.

title: Digital Advocacy and Strategy
description: Explores how to craft compelling narratives and develop impactful digital campaigns, with a particular focus on the role of digital innovations and AI in enhancing advocacy efforts.

title: Seminar in Issues of the Media
description: A study of philosophical and technological advances in mass communications with emphasis on projected patterns of future growth and development.

title: Algorithms, Analytics, and AI in Media
description: An exploration of the foundational concepts of algorithms, data analytics, and artificial intelligence (AI) in a code-free environment. Students will gain an understanding of algorithm design, analytical techniques for data interpretation, and the principles underlying AI technologies. Emphasis will be placed on practical applications and ethical considerations in the deployment of these technologies in media industries.

title: Colloquium in Mass Communications
description: Discussion of selected topics in mass communications research and practice.

title: Communication and Leadership
description: Analysis of mass communication freedoms and limitations in such areas as defamation, privacy, copyright, censorship, obscenity, and advertising and electronic media regulation.

title: Strategic Health Communication
description: The role of effective communication through mass media and other communication strategies in health promotion and behavior change.  Includes the theories and strategies used to promote public health messages, services, or products for multicultural audiences, with a focus on human, environment and animal health.  Legal, ethical issues and gender issues in health communication will be given special attention.

title: Issues, Risk, and Reputation
description: Promotes understanding of strategic communication and media as tools to prepare for, respond to, and recover from threats to organizational and brand reputation.

title: Communication Theory
description: An examination of major communication theories as they relate to mass communications.

title: Mass Communication Practicum
description: Supervised practical work in professional  mass communications settings, including laboratory investigation, field work, internships, and applied workplace research.

title: Research Methods in Mass Communications
description: Survey of research methods used in the study of the mass media.

title: Strategic Communications Management
description: Study of various forms of communication management and strategies that further the objectives of an organization, including organizational communication, marketing communication, and public relations.

title: Independent Study in Mass Communication
description: Supervised independent study in mass communication. Requires contractual agreement between student and professor, specifying content, meeting times, and credits to be awarded.

title: Advanced Research Methods: Qualitative
description: Introduction to qualitative methods, analysis, interpretation, and writing in media-related fields, and how qualitative inquiry contributes to theory construction in mass communication.

title: Digital Media Audiences and Analytics
description: Theories and concepts related to digital audiences in mass communication, content strategies, and how media audiences are formed, analyzed, and measured in the digital age.

title: Media and Community Engagement
description: Explores the role of media in increasing civic engagement and public participation, enabling the exchange of useful information, and fostering social connectivity to strengthen citizen agency and the overall democratic process.

title: Social Media Data Visualization
description: Familiarizes students with social-scientific methods for large-scale social-media data analysis and visualization, including the application of relevant user and concept networks, content categorizations, and sentiment analysis. In addition, the use of germane software in emerging and digital media research is developed.

title: Applied Research in Mass Media
description: Study and application of mass media research, its literature, and methodology.

title: Introduction to Mechanical Engineering
description: Introduction to the mechanical engineering curriculum and profession. Orientation for successful transition of new students to college life. Engineering approach to problem solving and computer use in all areas of mechanical engineering. Exchange of information regarding academic, technical, social, ethical, and professional matters between students, faculty, and practicing professionals.

title: Engineering Graphics
description: Technical sketching, study of basic principles of projective geometry, multiview drawings, pictorials, reading and interpreting drawings, CAD, sectioning, dimensioning.

title: Elements of Thermodynamics
description: Properties of pure substances.  The first law of thermodynamics for open and closed systems.  A brief introduction to the second law of thermodynamics.

title: The Second Law of Thermodynamics and Psychrometrics
description: This course builds on Elements of Thermodynamics; taken together, the two courses cover the full content of Thermodynamics I. Topics include the Second Law of Thermodynamics, nonreacting ideal gas mixtures, and psychrometrics.

title: Advanced Engineering Graphics
description: Advanced 3D CAD features and solid modeling techniques including an introduction to sheet metals, multi-body parts, weldments, etc.  Advanced SolidWorks features such as creating configurations from other configurations, deformed features, surfacing and Macros will be introduced.

title: Topics in Mechanical Engineering
description: Introductory Topics in Mechanical Engineering offers students an opportunity to explore foundational or emerging areas of the field. Content varies by offering and may introduce core mechanical engineering concepts, problem-solving approaches, and basic analytical or hands-on skills. The course provides a broad overview that prepares students for future coursework.  Course may be repeated for credit with change in topic.

title: Computer Applications in Mechanical Engineering
description: The development and application of computer techniques to the problems of design and analysis in mechanical engineering, including computer programming (abstraction and problem solving; algorithms; control structures; input/output; functions; arrays and array processing).

title: Undergraduate Research Experience
description: Open to students pursuing undergraduate research projects.

title: Honors Research in Mechanical Engineering
