description: Individual research problem selected with approval of faculty advisor. Open to students in the College of Engineering honors program.  A report is presented orally and in writing during the last semester.

title: Robotics Programming and Design
description: Application of mechanical engineering design and embedded programming techniques to design, build, and control a robotic system.  Focus on robotic kinematics, differential motion, and trajectory planning to support the programming and design of a 3-axis pick and place machine.  Robotic theory will be implemented using C++ deployed on an embedded system with the goal of implementing a complete and functional pick and place system.

title: Dynamics
description: Vector treatment of kinematics, Newton's Laws, work and energy, impulse and momentum, with applications to problems of particle and rigid body motion.

title: Thermodynamics I
description: Properties of the pure substance. The first and second laws of thermodynamics. Gas mixtures and psychrometry.

title: Electrical Circuits for Mechanical and Nuclear Engineering
description: Analysis, design, construction, and application of electric and electronic circuits using analytic tools, fundamentals of common components, instrumentation, and computer-based tools.

title: Thermodynamics II
description: Continuation of Thermodynamics I. Energy analysis, thermodynamic cycles, generalized thermodynamic relations, and reactive systems.

title: Experimental Strength of Materials
description: The experimental investigation of engineering materials used in the fabrication of products including metals, polymers, ceramics, composites, and elastomers. Provides basic knowledge in material science and inherit material properties such as elastic modulus, yield strength, toughness, etc., so students can solve real engineering problems and design engineering structures/systems.

title: Machine Design I
description: Introduction to the design and analysis of machine elements. Emphasis on materials, loads, stress, strain, deflection, failure theories, and finite element analysis. Applications include design and analysis of shafts, gears, and fasteners, weldments, springs, bearings.

title: Measurement and Instrumentation Laboratory
description: Theory and application of mechanical engineering measurements, instrumentation, and computer-based data acquisition.

title: Intermediate Mechanics of Materials
description: Fundamental principles of mechanics and its application to design and analysis of engineering structures. Topics covered include stress and strain, elastic and inelastic properties of materials, composite structures, unsymmetric bending, thin-walled structures and energy methods in structural analysis.

title: Machine Design II
description: Design and analysis of machine elements. Applications include design and analysis of bearings, gears, shafts, clutches, brakes, belt and chain drives, and hydraulic fluid power.

title: Control of Mechanical Systems I
description: Introduction to modeling and control of dynamic systems encountered by Mechanical Engineers. Topics include basic linear systems modeling and analysis; feedback control; time response and stability of dynamic systems; introduction to root locus and frequency response design.

title: Fluid Mechanics
description: Properties of fluids; fluid statics and dynamics; applications of the conservation principles (mass, momentum, and energy) to fluid systems; dimensional analysis and similitude; internal and external flow.

title: Heat Transfer
description: Fundamentals of conduction, convection, and radiation; principles of heat exchanger design and dimensional analysis.

title: Applied Engineering Design
description: Introduction to design theory, project management, team dynamics and socio-economic context of design, etc.; Application of design principles, engineering analysis and experimental methods to a project involving design, analysis, fabrication and testing. Discussion of ethics, technical/professional societies, and engineering licensing.

title: Mechanical Engineering Capstone Design
description: In-depth application of design principles, engineering analyses and experimental methods to an industrial project addressing design, analysis, constraints, standards, fabrication, and testing.

title: Topics in Mechanical Engineering
description: Topics in Mechanical Engineering allows students to study focused subjects of current interest within the discipline. Course content varies by semester and may include specialized concepts, applied problem-solving, or project-based learning. These offerings are intended for students with foundational coursework completed and provide an opportunity to engage with evolving areas of mechanical engineering practice. Course may be repeated for credit with change in topic.

title: Finite Element Applications in Mechanical Engineering
description: The application of the finite element method to the solution of engineering problems. Topics include introductions to the methods, linear elastic stress analysis, thermal analysis, and modeling limitations and errors. Commercial computer codes are used in the applications.

title: Applications in Mechatronics
description: Application of Mechanical and Electronic engineering to design. Microcontrollers; sensors; analog-to-digital signal conversion; DC motor operation and pulse with modulation; drive train configuration; embedded C programming; competition at Engineering Open House.

title: Internal Combustion Engines
description: Application of principles of thermodynamics, fluid mechanics, and chemistry, kinematics, and heat transfer to internal combustion engine processes. Performance and design characteristics of internal combustion engines.

title: Indoor Environmental Engineering  I
description: Ventilation, heating and cooling system design for buildings. Application of thermodynamic, heat transfer, and fluid mechanics principles for determination of building heating and cooling loads. Determination of ventilation requirements. Sizing, design and integration of environmental control systems.

title: Aerodynamics I
description: A general introduction to aerodynamics including the analysis of lift, drag, thrust, and performance for subsonic aircraft, and the application of aerodynamic principles to design.

title: Aircraft Propulsion
description: Mechanics and thermodynamics of aircraft propulsion systems; gas turbine aircraft engines; applied compressible flow; performance and design of propulsion systems.

title: Thermodynamics of Modern Power Production
description: The first and second law analysis of modern stationary power systems using fossil, nuclear, and renewable energy sources. Component and system design, such as cycle design, load factor, and auxiliaries are considered in conjuction with their costs.

title: Control of Mechanical Systems II
description: Design and analysis of control systems. Topics include linear and nonlinear systems modeling; parameter estimation/system identification; steady state errors; advanced root locus and frequency response design; controller implementation.

title: Introduction to Composites
description: Design, fabrication and testing of various composite materials. Analysis of mechanical properties of laminated composites.

title: Mechanical Vibrations
description: A general consideration for the formation and analysis of free and forced vibration of mechanical systems of single and mulitple degrees of freedom.

title: Introduction to Nanoscience and Nanotechnology
description: This interdisciplinary course will cover basic areas of nanotechnology, which includes synthesis of various nanomaterials (0D, 1D and 2D nanostructure), mechanical property assessment (hardness, modulus, yield strength measurements), energy generation (solar energy, thermoelectrics and energy generation from vibrations), 3-D printing of nanocomposites and energy storage.

title: Advanced Topics in Mechanical Engineering
description: Advanced Topics in Mechanical Engineering provides the opportunity to engage with specialized or emerging subjects requiring significant technical rigor. Course content varies by offering and typically includes analytical depth, advanced problem-solving, or project components. These courses are designed to serve as a platform for independent study, advanced design, or exploration of cutting-edge developments in mechanical engineering. Course may be repeated for credit with change in topic.

title: Development of Computer Applications in Mechanical Engg
description: Nature of design, graphical user interface development to support computer-aided design, algorithms and computer graphics in computer applications, feature-based design, applications to design problems.

title: Intermediate Topics in Interdisciplinary Systems Analysis
description: Analyze complex, interdisciplinary systems using the systems thinking framework. Model the systems using narrative and visual tools for systems thinking. Systems analyzed include engineering systems and may include economic, and/or social elements.

title: Interdisciplinary Seminar
description: The interdisciplinary seminar creates an opportunity to learn from other interdisciplinary researchers, form interdisciplinary research teams, and engage in interdisciplinary discussions. Topics include interdisciplinary research and collaboration, and professional development.

title: Intermediate Dynamics
description: General vector principles of the dynamics of particles and rigid bodies; applications to orbital calculations, gyrodynamics, and rocket performance; introduction to the energy methods of advanced dynamics.

title: Introduction To The Theory Of Continuous Media
Intermediate Fluid Mechanics
description: A continuation of ME 571 in the study of general topics in fluid mechanics. Conservation of mass and momentum principles with particular emphasis on analysis of inviscid (potential) flows, compressible flows, and more advanced viscous flows including boundary layers. Numerous applications utilizing numerical methods.

title: Thermal Systems Design
description: Thermal systems design including economics, simulation, and optimization. Includes heating, ventilating and air conditioning design and control.

title: Human Thermal Engineering
description: Application of thermodynamic, heat transfer, and fluid mechanics principles of the thermal analysis of the human body. Mathematical analysis and computer modeling of human response to the thermal environment. Evaluation of heat stress and cold stress. Protection from heat and cold. Requirements for thermal comfort and impact on human performance.

title: Computer Control of Electromechanical Systems
description: Discrete modeling and analysis of dynamic physical systems in mechanical engineering. Sampling and data conversion and reconstruction. Real time implementation of control on a computer. Digital controller design and implementation. Laboratory exercises in control applications and design.

title: Control Systems Analysis and Design
description: Use of classical analysis techniques for control system compensation. State space-control theory fundamentals are presented in addition to an introductory treatment of several major systems areas.

title: Experimental Stress Analysis
description: Experimental methods of investigating stress distributions. Photoelastic models, photoelastic coatings, brittle coatings, and resistance strain gauges applied to static and dynamic problems.

title: Engineering Analysis I
description: Methods of analysis and solution of problems selected from various branches of engineering.  Topics typically include vector algebra, vector spaces, linear algebra and matrices, vector calculus, line, surface and volume integrals, and ordinary differential equations for engineering applications.

title: Intermediate Heat Transfer
description: Conduction, convection and radiation, mass transfer, phase change, heat exchangers, introductory numerical methods.

title: Monte Carlo Methods
description: The objective of this course is to explore various methods of Monte Carlo for solving direct and inverse problems in engineering. The course covers probability distributions; laws of large numbers and Central Limit Theorem; pseudo random number generation; sampling, scoring, and precision; variance reduction procedures; Markov chain Monte Carlo; inverse Monte Carlo; solution of linear operator equations; particle transport simulation.

title: Mne Graduate Seminar/Research Paper
description: Presentation(oral and written) and discussion of progress in research. Credit hours can be earned by preparing and/or presenting publication quality papers. Topics may be drawn from any current research area in mechanical and nuclear engineering.

title: Advanced Mechanics of Materials and Applied Elasticity
description: Two- and three-dimensional deformation analysis, equilibrium, and elastic constitutive laws. Stress-strain transformations between coordinate systems. Governing equations of elasticity. Advanced topics in bending, shearing, torsion and combined loads, with applications to engineering problems.

title: Thermodynamic Analysis
description: Basic considerations of the three laws of equilibrium thermodynamics. Availability analysis with applications including multicomponent systems.

title: L Inv TD HT FF CB
Introduction To The Theory Of Continuous Media
Intermediate Topics in Thermal and Fluid Mechanics
description: Topics may include combustion, direct energy conversion, modeling and design of internal combustion engines, nonequilibrium multiphase and multicomponent systems, refrigeration, cryogenics, stability and turbulence.

title: Computational Fluid Dynamics
description: The course introduces basic theories and algorithms for numerical simulations, and uses them to solve both model problems and classical fluid mechanics problems computationally.

title: Intermediate Micro-Electromechanical Systems
description: MEMS (micro-electromechanical systems) adapt the fabrication techniques and materials of semiconductor IC (integrated circuits) chips to make mechanical components, like cantilever beams, hinges, micromotors, micromirrors and microfluidic chips. In this course, the fabrication, design, and application of MEMS will be covered. Students will gain the knowledge of MEMS that combines interdisciplinary subjects of mechanical, electrical, and chemical engineering and material sciences. Advanced applications of MEMS are covered.

title: Intermediate Biomedical Micro-Electromechanical Systems
description: BioMEMS and microfluidics handle minute amount of liquids (microliter to picoliter) and bio-objects (DNA, protein, and cell). The course will cover (1) unique sciences of microfluidics, (2) liquid and bio-object manipulating principles, (3) design and manufacturing of microfluidic and biosensing devices, and (4) application in in vitro (out of body) diagnosis, 3D bioprinting, origami, and tissue engineering. Advanced applications of BioMEMS are covered.

title: Intermediate Topics in Automatic Controls
description: Topics may include analysis and design of nonlinear, adaptive, optimal, digitals or stochastic control systems and the applications of intermediate control and stability theory.

title: Boundary Layer Theory I
description: The development and solution of various laminar boundary layer problems involving momentum, heat, and mass transfer for a compressible viscous fluid.

title: Introduction to Fracture Mechanics
description: This course provides an introduction to fracture mechanics concepts and applications. Topics include the asymptotic solution for stress at a crack tip, energy balance and crack propagation, computing stress intensity factors, fatigue crack growth, fracture of concrete, applications and current topics.

title: Intermediate Topics in Solid Mechanics and Machine Design
description: Topics may include intermediate elasticity, plasticity, tribology, probabilistic machine design, robotics, computational dynamics and nonlinear mechanics.

title: Vibrations of Contunuous Media
description: Basic mathematical and physical descriptions for wave phenomena in continuous media, with emphasis on propagations of mechanical disturbance in linearly elastic solids. Other selected topics in wave dynamics, including acoustics, water waves, nonlinear phenomena, will be discussed depending on students' interest.

title: Engineering Analysis II
description: Continuation of Engineering Analysis I to cover more methods of analysis and solution important for engineering applications. Topics typically include complex variable theory, differential operators and orthogonal functions, Sturm-Liouville theory, Fourier series and Fourier transforms, and partial differential equations for engineering applications.

title: Finite Elements
description: The foundations of the finite element method using weighted residuals and variational methods. Element formulation, assembly and solution are covered in detail. Formulation for dynamic and nonlinear problems. Discussion of advanced topics. The student will develop a complete finite element program.

title: Mechanics of Composite Materials
description: Topics include classification of composite materials, elasticity theory for anisotropic and in homogeneous materials, basic model for characterization of composite properties, laminated plate theory, textile composites, strength and criteria for composite failure, and fracture modes in composites.

title: Master's Report
description: Topics selected with approval of major professor and department head.

title: Master's Thesis
description: Topics selected with approval of major professor and department head.

title: Theory of Plasticity
description: Foundations of plasticity. Vectorial and sensorial analysis. Coverage of pressure-dependent and pressure-independent materials. Hyper elasticity-Green elasticity. Deformation theory of plasticity. Flow theory of plasticity. Plastic work rate-equivalent strain. Classical yield criteria. Closed form solution of simple cases. Numerical solutions of more complex cases.

title: Computational Methods in Design
description: Selected topics from optimal design, geometric modeling, and multi-physics finite element methods.

title: Advanced Topics in Thermal and Fluid Mechanics
description: Non-Fourier heat transfer models used for microscale transport phenomena. Boltzmann integral methods for determining ion/neutron effects on metal material temperature.

title: Thermal Systems Analysis
description: Advanced study of steady-state and dynamic simulation of thermal systems; thermal systems optimization. Thermodynamic availability and probabilistics in thermal system design.

title: Advanced Topics in Automatic Controls
description: Topics may include analysis and design of nonlinear, adaptive, optimal, digital, or stochastic control systems and the application of advanced control and stability theory.

title: Heat Conduction in Solids
description: General differential equation of heat conduction and methods of solution for steady-state and transient heat conduction, periodic heat flow, and internal heat sources.

title: Advanced Topics in Solid Mechanics and Machine Design
description: Topics may include advanced elasticity, plasticity, tribology, probabilistic machine design, robotics, advanced and computational dynamics and nonlinear mechanics.

title: Convection Heat Transfer
description: Energy and momentum equations in convective heat transfer, laminar and turbulent thermal boundary layers, steady and nonsteady convection problems.

title: Radiation Heat Transfer
description: Basic theories of thermal radiation, shape factors; exact and approximate solutions of integral equations of radiation heat transfer between solid surfaces with absorbing or nonabsorbing medium.

title: Boiling Heat Transfer
description: Principles of boiling heat transfer and thermal hydraulics of two-phase flow; computational methods; design and analysis applications.

title: Dissertation Research in Mechanical Engineering
description: Dissertation Research in Mechanical Engineering PhD Level

title: Technical Graphics
description: Introduction to applications in sketching and 2D computer-aided design applied to geometric development and engineering communication. Techniques are applied to produce finished drawings and schematics to National and international standards. Theory and applications of orthographic and auxiliary projection and pictorial drawing. Standards for symbols, section views, and dimensioning included.

title: Mechanical Modeling and Detailing
description: Professional practices in the development and application of 3D part and assembly modeling for design and manufacturing. Preparation of 3D model for manufacturing data or production drawing. Development of tolerances versus cost, fit tolerances, and introductory geometric dimensioning and tolerancing (GD&T) specifications. Application of handbook data and spreadsheet computation to design detail development.

title: Manufacturing Methods
description: Introduction to contemporary manufacturing processes and practices, including precision measurement and inspection, machining, forming, casting, and welding processes. Recitation and laboratory experience in manufacturing practices, including metrology and basic setup and operation of manufacturing equipment. Observation of applications in local industry.

title: Computer-Numerical-Controlled Machine Processes
description: Study and practice of basic CNC programming and machining operations.

title: Statics
description: A study of forces and their effects on the bodies upon which they act.

title: Automated Manufacturing Systems I
description: A general survey of the various components and systems in automated manufacturing, including material handling, electropneumatic control, programmable logic control, robotics, tooling, inspection and quality control, CNC, and other production processes.

title: Physical Materials and Metallurgy
description: A broad view of materials used in industry, including structures of materials, how they react to stress and temperature, how the polyphase structures form, and how they are controlled to produce optimum properties. Students will examine through study and laboratory experimentation ferrous and nonferrous metals, polymers, composites, and ceramics.

title: Industrial Robotics Laboratory
description: An introduction to industrial robots emphasizing robot operation, programming, and integration with programmable logic controllers and other automation equipment.

title: Material Strength and Testing
description: Calculations of material strength and deformation are complemented with principles and practice of mechanical testing including instrumentation and measurement in the areas of loads, stresses, deformations, thermal stresses, and other quantities.

title: Fluid Power Technology
description: Study, design, analysis, operation, maintenance, and applications of hydraulic and pneumatic power systems and components.

title: Machine Design Technology I
description: Introduction to application and selection of machine elements to design mechanical systems.  Covers primary machine elements such as shafts and shaft components, screws and fasteners, welded joints, springs, bearings, gears, clutches and brakes.

title: Topics in Mechanical Engineering Technology
description: Provides an opportunity for faculty to present mechanical engineering technology topics.

title: Finite Element Analysis and Design Modeling
description: Introduction to finite element analysis applied to mechanical design.  Study and application of advanced computer-aided modeling techniques.

title: Additive Manufacturing
description: The course develops an understanding of additive manufacturing (AM) principles and applications combined with a problem-based learning project which develops design, manufacturing and maintenance skill sets for AM practitioners.

title: Advanced Materials Science
description: A continuation of MET 231 Physical Materials and Metallurgy. Emphasizes the understanding of material properties used to give various materials their function. Theory and laboratory work focus on controlling and testing material properties. Ferrous and non-ferrous metals, polymers and adhesives, composites, smart materials, effects of corrosion, failure analysis, and selection techniques for design.

title: Dynamics of Machines
description: Velocities, accelerations, and forces in existing mechanisms to produce motions. Work, energy, impulse and momentum concepts in kinetics. Vibrations in machine parts.

title: Elements of Mechanisms
description: Fundamental motion concepts of displacement, velocity, and acceleration are studied, as well as analytical and graphical analysis and synthesis of linkages, gear trains, cams, pulleys, and combinations of these elements.

title: Fluid Mechanics
description: Fundamental concepts of fluid mechanics. Study of buoyancy, energy equation, viscosity, and flow measurement. Selected applications of fluid mechanics in civil and mechanical technologies. Computer-aided solution of problems in fluid mechanics.

title: Machine Design Technology II
description: Continued study of design of mechanical elements and systems including engineering mechanics and materials, investigation of failure theories under static and variable loading, stress analysis, stress concentration, and deflections.

title: Quality Control
description: An introductory course in quality concepts and techniques used in industry. Topics include fundamentals of statistics and probability, statistical process control charts, and quality improvement tools.

title: Industrial Instrumentation and Controls
description: An introduction to process control systems for industrial applications. Course topics include concepts and terminology, first- and second-order systems, measurement of motion, gauges and transducers, signal processing, and measurement of properties.

title: Managerial and Project Economics
description: Economic analysis of problems as applied to managerial decision making. Students consider the economic viability of solutions in engineering and a variety of other kinds of projects common in technology-oriented businesses.

title: Tool Design For Manufacturing
description: Principles and practices involved in tool drawing and design concepts necessary for the manufacture of products. Emphasis on design of jigs and fixtures, gauging devices, dies, ease of operation, and methods of assembly. Production cost related to selection of parts and methods of production is stressed. Applied laboratory exercises illustrated through specific case studies.

title: Senior Design Project I
description: Selection, definition, and analysis of a project supervised by faculty. Includes consideration of project parameters, trade-off studies, alternative solutions, and justification of selected solution. Completion and presentation of a written project proposal included.

title: Senior Design Project II
description: Development and implementation of project proposal submitted in MET 462. Construction, packaging, and testing of project culminating in a senior design project report which may include full documentation and performance specifications, functional description, theoretical analysis, schematics, cost analysis, parts list, drawings, etc. Project results will be presented orally to a select committee at the end of the course.

title: Thermodynamics and Heat Transfer
description: This course emphasizes thermodynamic laws and equations and the use of tables and charts for properties of important fluids. Applications to systems used for producing, transforming, and applying heat and mechanical energy are also studied. Conduction, convection, and radiation heat transfer processes are investigated.

title: Automated Manufacturing Systems II
description: Covers systems for manufacturing operations including facilities, supplies, materials, procedures, and control. Topics include design, programming, feedback for manufacturing, production set-up, automated work cells, and decision issues.

title: Industrial Work Internship
description: The student will work as an intern with business and industry in mechanical engineering technology field. A report detailing duties performed and tasks accomplished is required at the end of the internship period.

title: Advanced Topics in Mechanical Engineering Technology
description: Provides an opportunity for faculty to present advanced mechanical engineering technology topics.

title: Introduction to Business Analytics and Information Systems
description: A comprehensive view of organizational information systems required for obtaining and transforming data into business analytics and intelligence. Cases and hands-on exercises emphasize information systems usage in data acquisition, analysis, and presentation leading to effective organizational decision-making.

title: Business Programming Applications
description: Business-related object-oriented programming fundamentals used to enhance skills for problem solving and logical thinking. Focuses on class diagrams, and the utilization of programming languages for understanding and applying software development concepts.

title: Business Database and SQL
description: Examination of database theory, design, implementation and the value of data to an organization. Study of structured query languages (SQL) including the definition and creation of database objects, manipulation of data stores including entry, modification, removal and extraction, and database programming fundamentals relevant to business and data analytics.

title: ERP Project Planning and Implementation
description: Applications of enterprise systems with emphasis on ERP systems configuration, business process integration and hands-on project management.

title: Business Networks and Security
description: Study of digital networks and security considering impact and value to business organizations. Coverage includes communications protocols and standards, hardware, software, network implementation and configuration; and security. Emphasis on hands-on network setup, configuration and testing.

title: Undergraduate Research Experience
description: Open to students pursuing undergraduate research projects.

title: MIS Internship
description: Eight weeks of applied Management Information Systems (MIS) experience designed to coordinate the interests of students and firms.

title: Special Topics in MIS and Data Analytics
description: Discussion and analysis of current and emerging issues in management information systems or data analytics, including topics related to new theory, new applications, and technology or data analytics practice.

title: Cloud Computing with Business Programming Applications
description: Advanced application of business-related programming fundamentals in cloud environments. Focuses on utilization of programming languages and mobile software development for creating solutions to data-intensive business problems in cloud environments.

title: Systems Analysis and Design
description: An in-depth study of systems analysis and design techniques viewing information systems as an integral component of organizational strategic planning. Emphasis on systems planning, project management, use of CASE tools, process and data modeling, quality and systems reengineering.

title: Business Analytics and Data Mining
description: In-depth study of a broad range of topics and techniques in business intelligence (BI), data mining, and database marketing (DM). Emphasis on fundamentals of relational database management, data warehousing as a business practice, customer relationship management (CRM), customer segmentation, various data mining techniques, data visualization, business performance management, use of scorecard/dashboard, and advanced BI and data mining software tools.

title: Applications of Data Models in Business
description: Implementation and management of extract, transform, load (ETL) processing and automation from a business perspective. Topics include design of data migration approaches, data cleaning techniques, and technologies used to move and distribute data between various database types including relational, non-relational, on-site and cloud. SQL, NoSQL and other technologies provide context and hands-on practice in data pipelines implementation and management.

title: Social Media Analytics and Web Mining
description: This course is an in-depth study of a broad range of topics and techniques in the areas of social media analytics, web mining and social network analysis. Emphasis is on fundamentals of data acquisition from the web and social media platforms, transformation of these unstructured data into structured format, advanced data processing techniques for analysis, business intelligence through web crawling, analysis of web and social media data using classification, clustering, and association techniques, sentiment analysis (or opinion mining) for business decisions, visualization of unstructured data, and social network analysis.

title: Senior MIS Project
description: Senior project. Students must demonstrate proficiency in the analysis, design and implementation of a business or data analytics project by creating a working prototype of a business application.

title: Business Data Pipelines
description: Implementation and management of extract, transform, load (ETL) processing and automation from a business perspective. Topics include design of data migration approaches, data cleaning techniques, and technologies used to move and distribute data between various database types including relational, non-relational, on-site and cloud. SQL, NoSQL and other technologies provide context and hands-on practice in data pipelines implementation and management.

title: Topics: Information Technology and Data Analytics
description: Discussion and analysis of contemporary issues in information technology, business analytics, trends, and emerging technology. Can include applications, relevant literature and research.

title: Business Intelligence and Storytelling in Cloud Environments
description: A practical exposure to information technology as it applies to business. Emphasis will be placed on problem-solving using spreadsheets, databases, and HTML based information systems.

title: Introduction to Marketing
description: A general study of marketing principles which lead to the development of marketing strategy. A review of environmental influences and key analytical tools used in formulating marketing plans. Product or service design, distribution, pricing, and promotional programs.

title: Introduction to Digital Marketing
description: Designed to teach students the basic fundamentals of digital marketing. Topics include digital marketing terminology, search engine marketing, search engine optimization, mobile digital marketing, social media marketing, technology integration, and marketing analytics.

title: Brand Marketing
description: Focuses on building and measuring brands as business strategy, as well as personal branding.

title: Socially Responsible Marketing
description: Ethical marketing practices in the U.S. and global economy. There is a brief introduction to business ethics, ethics within the marketing mix, ethics and the promotional mix and special topics in marketing ethics. It will include case studies to help build experience with real world domestic and global situations and increase critical thinking and problem-solving skills.

title: Business to Business Marketing
description: A study of the nature of the industrial marketplace, concentrating on those aspects that differentiate it from the consumer markets. The major topics include analysis of market needs, market segments, estimating market demand, organizational buying behavior, marketing channels, marketing strategy and mix for institutional customers, global marketing strategies, managing products and new product development, buyer/seller relationships, pricing strategies, and business marketing communications.

title: Services Marketing
description: An analysis of the unique marketing challenges faced by service organizations. Major topics include the unique characteristics of services, creating and positioning a service in the marketplace, distributing, promoting and pricing services. An evaluation of marketing strategies from the perspective of a service firm.

title: International Marketing
description: This course deals with the problems and perspectives of marketing across national boundaries. It also focuses on the tools and practices for structuring and controlling marketing programs related to overseas business. Emphasis is on the management of marketing functions in a global context. Topics include international trade organizations, international economic factors, foreign business customs, and the international marketing mix.

title: International Business
description: This course provides students with an appreciation of the opportunities and unique challenges in international business, an understanding of the strategic and operational options available to an international firm, and managerial decision making abilities required to be successful abroad. The course examines strategic and operational issues involved in international business activity and how to best manage these issues.

title: AI for Marketing
description: Explores the use of Artificial Intelligence (AI) in marketing. Students will evaluate AI tools and learn how these tools can be used to complement and leverage marketing efforts.  The current and future uses of AI in marketing, ethical and legal implications, skillsets needed, and best practices of using AI tools will be discussed. The use of AI in other fields, such as sales, will also be discussed.

title: Relationship Marketing
description: This course will explore the principles, strategies, and practices of relationship marketing, with a focus on building and sustaining long-term, mutually beneficial relationships between businesses and both their customers and partners. Indeed, developing and maintaining strong relationships is critical to companies’ long-term financial performance and overall success. As such, this course will examine building and maintaining strong relationships for businesses, the various types of consumer-brand relationships that can be developed, targeting and adapting relationships, and improving firms’ long-term success.

title: Entertainment Marketing
description: Examines the entertainment industry using strategic marketing approaches to assess market opportunities, develop marketing strategies, and design implementation tactics.

title: Consumer Behavior
description: A study of the concepts, principles, and theories to understand, evaluate and predict consumer behavior. Explaining the behavior of consumers provides insight into ambiguous marketing situations. The course focuses on applying scientific methods to aid in marketing decision making. In addition, this course integrates current consumer research and theory, as well as current topics.

title: Marketing Analytics Fundamentals
description: Students will develop an understanding of data collection, data analysis and data visualization in order to make strategic business decisions.

title: Undergraduate Research Experience
description: Open to students pursuing undergraduate research projects.

title: Marketing Internship
description: Eight weeks of applied marketing business experience between junior and senior years designed to coordinate the interests of students and firms.

title: Special Topics in Marketing
description: Discussion and analysis of current and emerging issues in marketing, including topics related to new theory, new applications, and the practice of marketing.

title: Corporate Marketing Practicum
description: For high GPA marketing students to gain additional marketing insight by working with a company in applying marketing concepts.

title: Digital Content Strategy and Development
description: Development of strategic communications plans for digital marketing, including content structure and development. Students will have the opportunity to incorporate industry certifications as the course will instruct students on the skills needed for the certification exams.

title: Retailing
description: Designed introduce the student to the role retailing performs in the distribution of consumer goods and services. The course will survey the retailing environment and incorporate strategic planning from the management point of view; study retail policies and organizations; merchandise control; personnel management; retail accounting and expense control.

title: Marketing Channels
description: Study of the quantitative and qualitative factors involved in selecting, developing, managing, and controlling marketing channels of distribution. Includes decision models from industrial marketers through purchasing units.

title: Marketing Field Study
description: This course is designed to provide students with an opportunity to observe international business and marketing perspectives by participating in a field trip outside the United States.  This experiential course may involve a combination of business visits, cultural experiences, internship activities, or other learning activities while abroad.

title: Data Narratives and Visualization
description: Students learn to interpret complex datasets and effectively communicate the analysis through visualization and storytelling. They learn these techniques with and without the use of dashboards and/or presentation software.

title: Sports Marketing
description: Covers the concepts, metrics, and techniques used to address the opportunities and challenges faced by marketers in the context of sports.

title: Digital Marketing Analysis and Strategy
description: Examines aspects of tracking, analyzing, and optimizing marketing efforts to achieve strategic objectives. It provides learners with an understanding of search engine marketing (SEM) and search engine optimization (SED), web analysis and tools, and social media platforms and analytics.  Learners have an opportunity to earn industry certifications.

title: Applications of Marketing Analytics
description:
Marketing analytics focuses on applications of quantitative analysis to understand dynamic marketing phenomena. This course is a mixture of lectures on essential marketing concepts/econometrics/text mining, instructions on software program (Stata and R), and discussions of marketing cases to facilitate students' critical thinking and decision making leveraged by analytical ability. To facilitate learning statistical software programs, students will practice hands-on projects in the computer lab.Learning outcomes are that students will be able to do the following:Choose correct quantitative analysis methodologies
Learn data analysis software programs (Stata and R)
Make strategic decisions based on data analysis and communicate marketing strategies

title: Marketing Research
description: Learn the essential techniques and tools for data collection, analysis, and interpretation, including survey and experimental design (A/B testing). Students will explore qualitative and quantitative methods, learn to utilize statistical software, and engage in hands-on projects to apply concepts in marketing contexts to make strategic data-driven decisions.

title: Marketing Strategy
description: Analysis of marketing situations which lead to appropriate management of the marketing program's objectives. Capstone course integrates knowledge of marketing and other business management principles into marketing strategy, development, implementation, and control.

title: Marketing Concepts and Research
description: Presents marketing concepts and marketing research concepts at the graduate level. Emphasis is directed toward managerial strategy development using marketing theories and applied marketing research techniques.

title: Digital Marketing Essentials: From Strategy to Execution
description: Students will gain the skills necessary to design and execute data-driven digital marketing campaigns integrating technology, strategy, and communication. They will explore strategies for optimizing content across digital platforms, while learning to measure campaign effectiveness, engage with target audiences, and adjust marketing tactics based on performance data to maximize impact.

title: Mastering Digital Marketing Tactics
description: Students will develop expertise in advanced digital marketing strategies to enhance campaign effectiveness, foster customer relationships, and leverage new technologies for impactful brand engagement across diverse platforms.

title: Strategic Marketing Planning for Long-Term Success
description: Students will master advanced digital marketing strategies to create customer-centric, data-driven campaigns across multiple channels, optimizing ROI and effectively communicating plans to stakeholders.

title: Market Research Fundamentals: Qualitative and Quantitative Approaches
description: Students will learn to design and execute research studies, apply survey techniques, understand customer needs, and effectively communicate research findings to support problem-solving and decision-making in marketing.

title: Mastering Impactful User-Centered Design
description: Students will develop comprehensive UX design skills to research, create, visualize, and evaluate user-centered interfaces and experiences.

title: Optimizing Content for Superior Customer Experiences
description: Students will develop skills to create tailored, optimized content, enhance customer experiences, and analyze customer journeys to improve satisfaction and engagement across various channels.

title: Marketing Technology: Automation and CRM
description: Students will develop comprehensive skills to implement and optimize marketing automation and CRM systems, integrating digital strategies to enhance customer relationships and maximize marketing effectiveness.

title: Data-Driven Marketing: Analytics for Strategic Decisions
description: Students will develop skills to collect, analyze, and interpret business data using statistical and predictive modeling techniques, applying insights to optimize marketing strategies and effectively communicate results to diverse audiences.

title: Customer Insights: Analytics, Segmentation, and Targeting
description: Students will gain expertise in analyzing customer behavior using analytics, segmentation, and forecasting, and will develop the skills to align marketing strategies and communicate insights effectively.

title: Marketing Performance: ROI Measurement and Attribution Modeling
description: Students will master the application of marketing attribution models to evaluate, optimize, and communicate marketing effectiveness across channels and market types, driving data-informed strategies to maximize ROI.

title: Special Topics in Digital Marketing
description: Investigation and discussion of a selected topic in digital marketing.

title: Maximizing ROI Through Data-Driven Marketing Strategies
description: Students will master the integration of advanced marketing concepts, technologies, and analytical techniques to solve real-world business challenges, developing and presenting comprehensive, data-driven marketing strategies and implementation plans.

title: Advanced Marketing Research
description: Application of advanced research methods to help solve marketing problems. Intensive examination of experimental and survey designs, data collection methods, and selected multivariate analysis techniques are covered.

title: Advanced International Marketing
description: This course is designed to develop the students' managerial decision making ability in international marketing. The course will focus on the internationalization process of firms, the unique issues in international marketing, and international marketing strategy. The course will integrate short lectures, class discussions, and case analysis to achieve its objectives.

title: Advanced International Business
description: This course is designed to introduce the student to the nature and scope of fundamental business skills requisite for a career in international business. A unified survey of the international aspects of accounting, finance, management, marketing and related subjects will be made that is useful to a manager in a global business enterprise. Emphasis will be placed on the identification, articulation and implementation of successful global strategies.

title: Applied Marketing Analy
description: Business Intelligence is a systematic approach to harnessing customer data and competitive information to drive strategic business decision making. This course deals with how to collect and analyze business data to enhance quality of decision making in modern enterprises. Unlike courses based on data mining (inductive approach), this course will be largely based on regression techniques (deductive approach). The course will be based on lectures, case analysis, and hands on exercises to make students comfortable with powerful computing tools used for data analysis. The cases and exercises will be bundled with data which will be used to apply concepts learned in class to real business situations.

title: Advanced Marketing Analytics
description: Students will gain an understanding of econometric and statistical methods of analysis and their application to business problems.  Through lectures and exercises, student will use software tools often used in business for data analysis.  Students will also learn how to interpret and apply the results from the software in data analyses.  Emphasis will be placed on the application of econometric and statistical methods of data analyses. Topics will include customer choice analysis, customer segmentation and targeting, customer lifetime value calculation, and forecasting.

title: Special Topics in Marketing
description: Investigation and discussion of a selected topic in marketing. One of the following five topics will be chosen for intensive study: (1) industrial marketing management, (2) advanced consumer behavior, (3) product policy, (4) financial aspects of marketing management, (5) marketing in the service sector.

title: Artificial Intelligence Systems Architecture
description: This course examines the architectural foundations of artificial intelligence systems, focusing on the design, integration, and deployment of scalable, reliable, and secure AI solutions. Topics include data pipelines, model lifecycle management, distributed and cloud-based AI infrastructures, edge and embedded AI systems, and system performance optimization. Students will explore architectural trade-offs and best practices through case studies and applied design exercises.

title: Code and Command: AI Statecraft and Algorithmic Power
description: This course explores the strategic, political, and societal implications of artificial intelligence as a tool of statecraft and global influence. Students examine how algorithms, data, and AI systems shape governance, security, economic power, and information ecosystems. Topics include algorithmic governance, AI in defense and intelligence, cyber and information operations, ethical frameworks, and international competition in AI development. Emphasis is placed on critical analysis, policy implications, and the intersection of technology and power.

title: Generative Artificial Intelligence
description: This course provides an in-depth study of generative artificial intelligence methods and systems. Students explore the theoretical foundations and practical implementations of generative models, including autoregressive models, variational autoencoders, diffusion models, and large-scale foundation models. Emphasis is placed on representation learning, model training at scale, prompt and conditioning strategies, evaluation of generative outputs, and deployment considerations.

title: Special Topics in Artificial Intelligence and Autonomous Systems
description: This course offers an in-depth examination of emerging and specialized topics in artificial intelligence and autonomous systems. Subject matters vary by offering and may include machine learning advances, advanced artificial intelligence topologies (generative, agentic, and synthetic), robotics, human-machine teaming, autonomous decision-making, AI safety, or domain-specific applications. The course emphasizes current research, experimental methods, and applied problem solving, allowing students to engage with cutting-edge developments in the field.

title: Artificial Intelligence Senior Capstone I
description: First course in a two-semester capstone sequence. Students identify a real-world or research-based problem in artificial intelligence and develop a project proposal. Emphasis is placed on problem definition, background research, project planning, and system design, including the selection of appropriate AI techniques, tools, and implementation pathways.

title: Artificial Intelligence Senior Capstone II
description: Second course in a two-semester capstone sequence. Students implement, evaluate, and refine the proposed AI solution developed in MLAS 503. Students carry out system development, conduct rigorous testing and performance analysis, prepare professional documentation, and present their final results. Emphasis is placed on technical execution, validation of outcomes, adherence to professional and ethical standards, and effective dissemination of project findings.

title: Orientation For Summer School Abroad Program In Mexico City
Beginner Studies in Foreign Language
description: Course used to transfer credit for first and second semester language courses not offered at K-State. Consultation for approval is expected.

title: Legal Terminology: Latin and French for Law Professions
description: Designed to familiarize students with vocabulary at use in the American legal system. Teaches basic French and Latin components used in legal terminology.

title: Hebrew (Ivrit) For Beginners
description: An introduction to the Hebrew language and the culture of the people who speak the language. This general introduction includes skill development in reading, writing, and speaking basic Hebrew.

title: Multilingualism in the U.S.
description: Examines the sociolinguistic and sociocultural coexistence among English and varied minority languages (e.g., French, Chinese, Spanish, and German) through different blocks or content units. Students will gain an appreciation for multiculturalism and multilingualism in the U.S. while expanding their awareness concerning global issues. To this end, the students will learn and engage in discussions about race, identity, migration, acculturation, syncretical views and practices, language variation, and linguistic adaptability as experienced by multilingual communities with diverse ethnic backgrounds.

title: Intermediate Studies in Foreign Language I
description: Course used to transfer credit for third semester language courses not offered at K-State.

title: Studies in World Literature and Culture
description: Provides the students the opportunity to investigate in detail a particular theme or genre in World literature or culture. Taught in English.

title: Honors Introduction to the Humanities I
description: Study of selected major works of history, literature, and philosophy which have been of central importance in the Western cultural tradition. Considerable emphasis is placed on classroom discussion and writing interpretive essays.

title: Honors Introduction to the Humanities II
Intermediate Studies in Foreign Language II
description: Course used to transfer credit for fourth semester language course not offered at K-State.

title: Languages and Your Future
description: Investigation and application of resources for developing language and culture skills, as well as a knowledge base about related fields and careers. Taught in English.

title: Studies in Foreign Language
description: Offered only to participants in study abroad programs. Prior consultation for approval is expected.

title: Honors Seminar in Modern Languages
description: Reading and discussion of selected masterpieces of European literature in English translation.

title: Cross Cultural Experiences In Literature:Native Americans
Honors Project
description: Open only to Arts and Sciences students who are active members of the University Honors Program.

title: European Literature in Translation
description: Selected readings in English from the major authors of Europe and the Spanish-speaking world.

title: Principles of Linguistics: Language Structure and Language Use
description: Introduction to the fundamentals of linguistic analysis, including the sound system (phonetics and phonology), word formation (morphology), sentence formation (syntax), meaning (semantics and pragmatics). Formal and functional (usage-based) perspectives. Application of linguistics to language acquisition theory and to pedagogy.

title: Foreign Language Pedagogy
description: Second and foreign language pedagogical theory is combined with the examination of practical concerns in the teaching of languages, in order to provide future and current instructors with the necessary background for making informed decisions concerning classroom practices. Taught in English.

title: Introduction to Literary Theory and Research Methodology
description: An overview of theoretical approaches and classical texts that inform current trends in literary research in the modern languages.

title: German Prose And Drama Of The Nineteenth Century
The Enlightenment And Storm And Stress
Theories of 2nd Language Acquisition
description: Introduction to the major theoretical frameworks of second language acquisition (SLA). Reading, discussion, and analysis of SLA research on a variety of linguistic and learning issues. Of interest to students of both language acquisition and literature. Taught in English.

title: Spanish Drama Of The Golden Age
Colloquium in Modern Languages
description: A graduate colloquium for M. A. candidates in French, German, and Spanish. Variable topics in literary and cultural fields appropriate to study in common by students in these languages.

title: Practicum in Adult TESL/TEFL: Written and Oral Communication
description: Overview of current issues and methodology in TES/FL and foundation for further exploration of teaching techniques used in skill specific areas of oral and written communication (including listening, speaking, reading, and writing). Provides students with training in TES/FL for adult learners. Students will acquire specific content and practice in defined skill areas.

title: Seminar/Topics in TESL/TEFL
description: Variable topics on different aspects /areas of TEFL, e.g., pragmatics, assessment, and writing, etc. Overview of key concepts for each topic, issues, and methodology, discussion of its applications in TEFL. Equip students with the necessary knowledge and skills to competently teach different areas in TEFL.

title: Second Language Assessment
description: Explores the basic concepts, principles, and methodology of second language assessment.

title: Survey of Machine Learning and Autonomous Systems
description: Rapid progress has been made in the development of autonomous systems. Terrestrial robotics, interplanetary systems, planetary robotics, uncrewed aerial systems, remote sensing platforms, and autonomous automobiles have all made great advances. These technologies have been enhanced by advances in artificial intelligence (AI). A non-programming perspective, this course will survey the current state?of?the?art perspective on artificial intelligence technologies used in autonomous systems. We start by presenting both ML-based and AI?based architectures that form the basis of perception, path planning, cognitive arbitration, and motion control.

title: Introduction to Automata and Cybernetic Systems Theory
description: This introductory course is a survey of the most common machine learning approaches used in creating descriptive and predictive analytical solutions. Students will be introduced to both the theoretical concepts and practical applications of machine learning and autonomous system. The course material introduces the probabilistic programming paradigm. Case studies will be used to illustrate the application of probabilistic programming frameworks in an applied industrial perspective.

title: Data Structures for Machine Learning
description: This course will cover the design, development, and employment of data structures and algorithms with an emphasis on time-space complexity. Data structure processing and computational processing problems related to the following time-space problems will be explored: constant, logarithmic, cubic, quadratic, polynomial, and exponential. Additionally, students will leverage Bachmann-Landau notation to describe time-space complexity.

title: Unsupervised Learning in Autonomous Systems
description: Unsupervised learning is a type of machine learning that looks for previously undetected patterns in a data set with no pre-existing labels and with a minimum of human supervision. Using probabilistic programming languages, students will learn how to develop, interpret and deploy unsupervised machine learning models within autonomous systems. Common machine learning algorithms for unsupervised learning will be leveraged: k-means clustering, principal component analysis, non-negative matrix factorization, singular decomposition, and density-based spatial clustering of application with noise.

title: Supervised learning in Autonomous Systems
description: Supervised learning is the machine learning task of learning a function that maps an input to an output based on example input-output pairs. Using probabilistic programming languages, this course will illustrate supervised machine learning concepts via data preparation, training data, model evaluation, and operationalization. Common machine learning algorithms for supervised learning such as K-nearest neighbors, logistic regression, decision trees and random forests will be explored and operationalized within an autonomous system.

title: Natural Language Processing
description: This course gives an overview of modern data-driven techniques for natural language processing. We will discuss all the steps that are required for NLP such as text classification, neural nets for text classification, language modeling, word embedding, sequence labeling, context free grammar, constituency parsing, dependency parsing syntax, computational ethics, information extraction, conversational agents, machine translation and computational social science.

title: Deep Learning
description: This advanced deep learning course is designed to provide students with a comprehensive understanding of deep learning techniques, their applications, and the practical skills needed to work with deep neural networks. By the end of this course, students will have the knowledge and skills to design, implement, and evaluate advanced deep learning models for a wide range of applications.

title: Reinforcement Learning in Autonomous Systems
description: This course will guide students through the development and operationalization of reinforcement learning frameworks.  Reinforcement learning architectures such as Markov decision processes, Q-Learning, artificial neural networks, recurrent neural networks, and generative adversarial networks will be explored. This course is a guide to the complexities of reinforcement learning, their respective architectures and implementations within an autonomous system.

title: Artificial Intelligence Studio
description: Students are expected to develop and operationalize an artificially intelligent framework using applied statistical methods, techniques in probabilistic programming, and principles of artificial intelligence. Students will be required to document underlying AI theories and demonstrate an operationalized AI framework.

title: Autonomous Systems Senior Capstone
description: Students are expected to develop and operationalize an autonomous system using cybernetic principles of learning, computational frameworks, quantitative methods, and principles of engineering. Students will develop project documentation as needed to describe their autonomous systems project. Lastly, students will be required to successfully defend their project via a final oral presentation to associated faculty members. Whenever feasible, real-world projects are solicited from businesses.

title: Autonomous Systems Architecture Studio
description: This course introduces the conceptualization and architectural development of cognitive processing engines within an autonomous system that also includes the architecture of autonomous systems, design of agents, models and knowledge representations, control of robotic manipulators, machine vision, the dynamics of wheeled, air, space and underwater robots, navigation and localization.

title: Advanced Cybernetic Systems Development
description: This course is focused on the design and development of cognitive processing engines within the context of the larger autonomous system.  General cybernetic theories will be used in specific example where students will gain experience designing and implementing computerized feedback control systems. The principles of cybernetics are universal so that students will be able to transfer their understanding and skills to other industrial domains.

title: Autonomous Networking within Cyber-Physical Systems
description: Students will design and develop communication frameworks for cognitive processing engines within autonomous systems connected to CPS frameworks.  The development of communication infrastructures that connect bodied (and unbodied) autonomous entities will also be explored.  Current literature, techniques, theories, and methodologies will be reviewed and discussed. Industry-base projects will help students creatively apply cutting-edge CPS research to their autonomous systems research problems.

title: Integrated Machine Learning and Artificial Intelligence
description: Students will study and develop an intelligent autonomous system, integrating perception, manipulation, learning, planning, and interaction. The autonomous system will be capable of learning and performing a variety of tasks in real-world environments while collaborating with humans. Additional artificial intelligence topics include: contextual reasoning, knowledge representation, perception and control, deep learning, and transfer learning.

title: Orientation for Summer School Program in Germany
description: Orientation for Summer School Program in Germany

title: Orientation for Summer School Abroad Program in Mexico City
The Works of Federico Garcia Lorca
Methods and Techniques of Learning a Second Language
Fundamental Methods of Biostatistics
description: A course emphasizing concepts and practice of statistical data analysis for the health sciences. Basic techniques of descriptive and inferential statistical methods applied to health related surveys and designed experiments. Populations and samples, parameters and statistics; sampling distributions for hypothesis testing and confidence intervals for means and proportions involving one sample, paired samples and multiple independent samples; odds ratios, risk ratios, simple linear regression. Use of statistical software to facilitate the collection, manipulation, analysis and interpretation of health related data.

title: Veterinary Epidemiology
description: Introduction to the principles and methods of veterinary epidemiology: emphasizing how diseases affect populations (and associated implications for individuals), and application to disease diagnosis, treatment, prevention, and control.

title: Administration of Health Care Organizations
description: Comprehensive review of current health care institutions and their response to the economic, social/ethical, political/legal, technological, and ecological environments.

title: Introduction to Epidemiology
description: The purpose of this course is to introduce students to the basic principles and methods of epidemiology in order to recognize and understand how disease affects populations (and the associated implications for individuals). This course will prepare students to use epidemiologic methods to solve current and future challenges to diagnose, treat, prevent, and control disease during their professional training and throughout their career.

title: Environmental Health
description: This is a three-credit graduate-level course consisting of a 3-hour meeting per week. Students will be exposed to professional practice of environmental sciences, epidemiology, toxicology, occupational health and industrial hygiene, and consumer health and safety. Topics include the methods for defining environmental contamination; identifying contaminants, pathogens and toxins; assessing risks and causality; determining health impact; ameliorating hazards; and protecting the population through waste management, regulatory programs, environmental inspections, food and product safety, and environmental policy. Includes interaction with professionals in public health practice.  There will be 3 one hour lectures each week. Participation is required.

title: Environmental Toxicology
description: An advanced toxicology course concerned with the occurrence, biological effect, detection, and control of foreign chemicals in the environment.

title: Social and Behavioral Bases of Public Health
description: The role of behavioral, social, psychological, economic, environmental, and social structural factors in both the occurrence of health problems in groups and populations, and in the development of the risk factors that contribute to these problems. Principles of health behavior change and the application of these principles to a variety of health issues as well as an emphasis on how social structural factors impact health are examined to better understand health behavior and health inequities in contemporary society.

title: Public Health Practice
description: Supervised field experience in an international, state, local, or district health agency or other appropriate health agency.

title: Intermediate Epidemiology
description: Epidemiologic principles of disease with a focus on measures of disease occurrence, association and impact, determinants of disease diagnostic test evaluation, study design and critical literature evaluation.

title: Introduction to Military Science and ROTC
description: Basic drill, physical fitness, rappelling, army values, military presentations and basic marksmanship.

title: Introduction to Military Leadership
description: Principles of effective leading. Communication skills to improve individual performance and group interaction. Relation of military organizational ethical values to the effectiveness of a leader, incorporating perspectives of the evolution of the U.S. Military from colonial times through the modern era.

title: Orienteering And Introduction To Military Science 1c
Introduction To Military Leadership And Confidence
Introduction To Military Leadership And Confidence
Self/Team Development
description: Ethics-based military leadership skills that develop individual abilities and contribute to building effective teams. Oral presentations, leadership, land navigation, and basic military tactics.

title: Individual/Team Military Tactics
description: Introduction to individual and team aspects of military tactics in small unit operations. Safety assessments, movement techniques, military orders process, rifle marksmanship, rappelling.

title: Care Of Combat Casualties And Intro To The Us Army
Map Reading And Common Military Concepts
Leaders Training Course
description: A five-week summer camp conducted at Fort Knox, Kentucky. The United States Army provides pay, travel, lodging and meal costs. No military obligation incurred.

title: Care Of Combat Casualties And Intro To The Us Army
Map Reading And Common Military Concepts
Leading Small Military Organizations I
description: Series of practical opportunities to lead small groups in situations of increasing complexity. Uses small unit defensive tactics and opportunities to plan and conduct training.

title: Leading Small Military Organizations II
description: Continues methodology of MSCI 301. Military missions and task analysis. Ethical decision making and lessons from leadership case studies.

title: Leaders Development and Assessment Course
description: A five-week camp conducted at Fort Lewis, Washington, by members of Kansas State University and other university Army ROTC faculty. The U.S. Army provides pay, travel, lodging and most meal costs. Highly structured and demanding, stressing leadership at small unit levels under varying, challenging conditions.

title: Military Leadership Studies and Practical Applications
description: Independent research, analysis and monthly discussion on related military topics. Small unit tactics and practical application of leadership skills and individual research projects.

title: Leadership Challenges and Objective-Setting
description: Plan, conduct, and evaluate activities of the ROTC cadet organization. Articulate goals, put plans into action to attain them. Assess organizational cohesion and develop strategies to improve it. Develop confidence in skills to lead people and manage resources. Learn and apply various Army policies and programs.

title: Transition to Lieutenant
description: Continues the methodology from MSCI 401. Identify and resolve dilemmas. Refine counseling and motivating techniques. Examine aspects of tradition and law as relate to leading as an officer in the United States Army. Prepare for a future as a successful United States Army lieutenant.

title: Honors Project
description: Open only to Arts & Sciences students who are active members of the University Honors Program and Advanced Military Science Curriculum (must have completed the Basic Military Science Curriculum or received United States Army Cadet Command credit through prior military service or completion of the Leaders Training Course).

title: Advanced Transition to Lieutenant I
description: Independent research, analysis and monthly discussion on related military topics. Personal, academic, and professional goals and objectives, development and maintenance of an officer evaluation report support form.

title: Advanced Transition to Lieutenant II
description: Independent research, analysis, and monthly discussion on related military topics. Personal, academic, and professional goals and objectives, development, and maintenance of an officer evaluation report support form.

title: Piano Proficiency
description: Required for graduation of all music majors.

title: Introduction to Music Theory
description: Introduction to the theory of music for non-music majors, including the skills needed to read and write Western music notation and to understand the rhythmic, melodic, harmonic, and structural elements of music.

title: Wind Ensemble
description: Using a player-pool concept, the ensemble will explore outstanding and varied wind literature. Repertoire may range from a classical octet to a one-on-a-part performance of a major large band work. Admission by audition.

title: Voice Class I
description: A beginning course in the basics of singing for non-music majors.

title: Voice Class II
description: Singing technique skills beyond the basics to include performance skills for non-music majors.

title: University Chorus
description: Open to all K-State students without audition.

title: University Band
description: Open to all interested wind and percussion performers without audition.

title: Concert Band
description: Open to all interested wind and percussion performers without audition.

title: Madrigal and Motet Ensemble
description: Vocal chamber ensemble open to any KSU student by audition. Ensemble performs literature from the Middle Ages through the 18th century. Rehearses once per week and perform one or two concerts per semester.

title: Recorder Ensemble
description: Recorder Ensemble is open to all KSU students. The ensemble performs literature from the Middle Ages through contemporary compositions. Rehearses once per week and performs one or two concerts per semester.

title: Collegiate Chorale
description: Open to all interested singers. Audition determines membership in this and other choral organizations.

title: University Orchestra
description: A non-audition ensemble open to all  KSU string musicians who perform literature ranging from grade levels 1-3. Conducted by KSU string education majors, students rehearse once per week and perform one or two concerts throughout the spring semester.

title: History of Musical Instruments
description: The development of musical instruments in each period of Western music.

title: Introduction to Film Music
description: Explores the evolution of the cinematic soundtrack. Introduces students to notable film composers, film-scoring techniques, and significant works in this genre. Designed for non-music majors and does not require previous musical training, also open to music majors.

title: Salsa: Afro-Cuban Music of the Past and Present
description: Appreciation, historical knowledge, mechanics, aesthetics, and cultural contexts of salsa.

title: Jazz in Kansas City and the Southwest
description: The history and development of jazz styles in Kansas City and the southwestern United States, emphasizing the influence on styles of other geographic areas.

title: Introduction to the Broadway Musical
description: The history of operetta, broadway musicals, and musical comedy.

title: Introduction to American Music
description: An introduction to the functions of music in American society and the elements of music, including a survey of the development of various types and styles of music in America.

title: Introduction to Music of the World
description: Develop listening skills and an understanding of the meaning of music from around the world. Students will discover how music can be used as a form of human expression, and also reflect the culture in which it was created. Due to the non-Western focus, cultural background and perspective will be provided for each population discussed including the influence of music within and upon societies and its members highlighting issues of human diversity outside the U.S.

title: Experiencing Live Music
description: Through attendance at professional music performances, guest speakers, panel discussions, and course readings, students will develop the vocabulary and critical listening skills to understand and discuss music and the socio-cultural factors that shape the experience of music performances. Students will critically examine how music experiences change or remain the same across formats, cultures, and styles, including popular music, jazz, Western art music, and world music traditions. Students will have the option to take this course for three (3) credits which will add a action research project as part of this experience.

title: History of Rock and Roll
description: A study of Rock and Roll from its origins through today, focusing on the development of rock styles from its roots in blues, folk, country, and pop.
