Expansion of listening experience through study of song forms, musical instruments of rock, and the socio-political significance of song lyrics.  Examinations, research paper required.

title: Inventing the Future/Underground Rock/1968-1993
description: An introduction to the underground rock music, musicians, and scenes from the 1960's to the 1990's that have proven most influential on modern popular music and culture.

title: Music as Protest and Propaganda in the United States
description: Explores music's transformative role in protest movements and as propaganda in the United States from 1775-present. Curated playlists from Jazz, Country, Soul, Hip Hop, Rock, Spirituals, and more encourage students to analyze sociopolitical issues through historical and contemporary music. Emphasis on musical responses to racial and ethnic inequities.

title: Styles 3, Musical Styles Of The Middle Ages And Renaissance
Vocal Techniques l
description: A beginning course in the basics of singing and teaching skills.

title: Vocal Techniques II
description: Singing and teaching skills beyond the basics and presentation of materials suitable for private and public school instruction at the secondary level.

title: Music for Elementary Teachers
description: The contribution of music to child development in elementary schools. A study of music literature suited to children through the development of purposive listening and the expressive phases of music including rhythmic response, singing, playing, reading, and writing.

title: Guitar Class I
description: Beginning-level group instruction in guitar performance.

title: Music Theory l
description: An introduction to the basic language of music; scales, triads and seventh chords, and all simple intervals. Written and aural skills reinforce concepts of melody, rhythm, and harmony.

title: Piano Class I
description: For students with no piano background.  Topics covered will include notation, intervals, accompaniment patterns, major scales, triads, basic progressions, and musicianship.

title: Styles 4, Musical Styles Of The Baroque Period
Styles 5, Musical Styles Of The Classical Period
Topics in Music
description: Exploration of the musical dimensions of a particular topic or theme. Topics vary.

title: Freshman Survey
description: An introduction to the elements of music; the major historical periods of western classical music; music of non-western cultures; popular styles.

title: Music Theory II
description: Continued experience with the language of music. Part-writing, analysis, composition.

title: Aural Skills l
description: Development of aural skills through sight singing, rhythmic training, and ear-training; melodic, rhythmic, and harmonic dictation to reinforce concepts in Music Theory II.

title: String Techniques and Materials
description: A beginning course in the fundamentals of playing and methods for teaching stringed instruments.

title: Percussion Techniques and Materials
description: The fundamentals of playing and methods of teaching percussion instruments.

title: Clarinet and Saxophone Woodwind Techniques and Materials
description: A course in the fundamentals of playing and methods for teaching single reed woodwind instruments.

title: Double Reed and Flute Woodwind Techniques and Materials
description: A course in the fundamentals of playing and methods for teaching double reed and flute woodwind instruments.

title: High Brass Techniques and Materials
description: A course in the fundamentals of playing and methods for teaching high brass instruments.

title: Low Brass Techniques and Materials
description: A course in the fundmentals of playing and methods for teaching low brass instruments.

title: French Diction
description: Rules for pronouncing and translating French vocal texts.

title: West African Styles of Social Dance and Music
description: Explores several styles of drumming and African Xylophone playing from the Ga, Dagara, and Ewe traditions from Ghana and other parts of West Africa. Instruction, includes but it is not limited to playing techniques and musical structure for drumming and Xylophone playing as associated with common recreational dances. No prior experience is necessary.

title: Drumset Techniques
description: Develops and refines skills,vocabulary, and rhetoric necessary to performing as a functional drummer in a variety of settings and styles. Topics of focus will include buidling a groove vocabulary, expanding listening to a wide variety of drummers, developing physical coordination at the kit, and reading drum charts for maximum musicality.

title: Songwriting
description: Basic music theory pertainng to the creation of a melody and the harmonization of that melody; how to craft creative lyrics, identify song form, and write a song using the most common and relevant forms in popular music.

title: Secondary Performance
description: For students who wish to study an instrument or voice other than the major performance area. Instruction is offered every semester in voice and each of the following instruments: baritone, bassoon, clarinet, double bass, early winds, flute, French horn, guitar, harp, harpsichord, oboe, organ, percussion, piano, saxophone, trombone, trumpet, tuba, viola, viola da gamba, violin, and violoncello.

title: Lower-Division Performance
description: Instruction and experience in solo performance is offered every semester in voice and each of the following instruments: baritone, bassoon, clarinet, double bass, early winds, flute, French horn, guitar, harp, harpsichord, oboe, organ, percussion, piano, saxophone, trombone, trumpet, tuba, viola, viola da gamba, violin, and violoncello.

title: Aural Skills II
description: Sight singing, rhythmic training, and ear-training through drills and dictation to reinforce concepts in Music Theory III.

title: Marching Band Techniques for School Music Educators
description: Practical techniques of marching band in school music programs and the skills necessary to design, organize, instruct, and evaluate a marching band show.

title: Basic Woodwind Repair Methods for the Music Educator
description: Explores the nomenclature, mechanics, diagnostic procedures and repair processes for woodwind instruments. Fundamental skills of padding, key corking, regulation and adjustment of mechanisms, and spring installation methods for the music educator.

title: Basic Brass Repair Methods for the Music Educator
description: Explores the nomenclature, mechanics, diagnostic procedures and repair processes for brass instruments. Fundamental skills of cleaning, soldering, piston valve repair, and basic dent repair methods for the music educator.

title: Lower-Division Ensemble Performance
description: Instruction is offered each semester in the following areas: brass, chamber music, concert jazz, jazz combo, strings, winds, percussion, and vocal ensemble.

title: Music Theory III
description: Theoretical concepts of eighteenth-century music. Modulations, secondary function chords, counterpoint, and some chromatic harmony. Identify and work with elements of musical structure through exercises, analysis and composition.

title: Lower-Division Composition
description: Composition instruction through individual lessons and group seminar instruction.

title: Collaborative Piano-Studio
description: Piano student assigned to studio instructor.

title: Collaborative Piano
description: Piano student assigned to a music major preparing for graduation recital.

title: Music Theory IV
description: Theoretical concepts of late nineteenth-century music and twentieth-century music: chromatic harmony and twentieth-century compositional techniques, analysis, and composition.  Identify and work with elements of musical structure.

title: Aural Skills III
description: Sight singing, rhythmic training, and ear-training through drills and dictation to reinforce concepts in Music Theory IV.

title: Aural Skills Proficiency
description: Required for graduation of all music majors.

title: Conducting
description: Techniques of the baton gestures, signs, and cues as generally used in conducting choral and instrumental organizations.

title: Intermediate Woodwind Repair Methods for the Music Educator
description: Continued exploration of nomenclature, mechanics, diagnostic procedures and repair processes for woodwind instruments. Continued development in skills of padding, key corking, regulation and adjustment of mechanisms, and spring installation methods for the music educator.

title: Intermediate Brass Repair Methods for the Music Educator
description: Continued exploration of nomenclature, mechanics, diagnostic procedures and repair processes for brass instruments. Continued development in skills of cleaning, soldering, piston valve repair, and basic dent repair methods for the music educator.

title: Jazz Guitar Seminar
description: Listening to jazz guitar literature, performing, and offering meaningful critique.

title: History Of The American Popular Song
Keyboard Pedagogy
description: A systematic study of pedagogy which examines effective teaching methods and aids in the development of a philosophy of professional teaching.

title: Musical Styles Of The Baroque Period
Concert Band
description: Open to all interested wind and percussion performers without audition.

title: Collegiate Chorale
description: Open to all interested singers. Audition determines membership in other choral organizations.

title: Collegiate Jazz Ensemble
description: An advanced level college jazz ensemble.  Examines and preforms various jazz ensemble pieces selected from extant literature which may include cool, swing, hard bop, bossa, Latin, samba. funk/rock, and other styles.

title: University Band
description: Open to all interested wind and percussion performers by audition.

title: Wind Symphony
description: Using a player-pool concept, the ensemble will explore outstanding and varied wind literature. Repertoire may range from a classical octet to a one-on-a-part performance of a major large band work. Admission by audition.

title: Topics in Jazz
description: Big bands; jazz pianists and styles; survey of combo jazz styles, etc.

title: Pop Music Icons
description: An exploration of the musical decisions that define the world’s leading pop musicians and songwriters. Using directed listening and curated playlists, students will engage with musical offerings and develop an understanding of influences for each artist studied. Individual artists or collected groups of artists are examined in detail.

title: Upper Division Secondary Performance
description: For students who wish to study an instrument or voice other than the major performance area. Instruction is offered every semester in voice and each of the following instruments: baritone, bassoon, clarinet, double bass, early winds, flute, French horn, guitar, harp, harpsichord, oboe, organ, percussion, piano, saxophone, trombone, trumpet, tuba, viola, viola da gamba, violin, and violoncello. This course allows for upper-division study within their secondary instruments.

title: Upper-Division Performance
description: Instruction and experience in solog performance is offered every semester in voice and each of the following instruments: baritone, bassoon, clarinet, double bass, early winds, flute, French horn, guitar, harp, harpsichord, oboe, organ, percussion, piano, saxophone, trombone, trumpet, tuba, viola, viola da gamba, violin, and violoncello.

title: Instrumental Conducting
description: Continued mastery of the skills in Conducting while emphasizing essential techniques and interpretation for instrumental ensembles.

title: Music Programs in Middle School/High School
description: Organization and administration of the comprehensive music program in junior and senior high schools; including the study of vocal and instrumental ensemble development, as well as techniques and materials for other types of music classes.

title: Teaching Beginning Instruments
description: Students will learn and employ techniques and strategies for teaching beginning band and orchestra instruments.

title: Advanced Studies in Music Education
description: Advanced instructional methods and technologies related to the teaching of music in grades pre-K-12 implemented through investigation of current trends in the field, advanced unit design, and field experience.

title: Instrumental Jazz Instruction
description: Guides future instrumental teachers on techniques to teach jazz including beginning improvisation, combo, big band techniques, and school jazz literature.

title: Vocal Jazz Instruction
description: Guides future choral teachers on techniques to teach jazz including beginning improvisation, jazz choir, and school jazz literature.

title: Music History III: 1850 to the Present
description: An overview of the development of stylistic and cultural trends, major composers, and repertoire of the late Romantic period through the present.

title: Instrumentation and Arranging
description: Study of writing and arranging for the strings, woodwinds, brass and percussion as well as choral ensembles. Functional ranges and practical applications through score study and projects.

title: Diatonic Harmony and the American Song
description: Composition of original small song forms including preparation of lead sheet and vocal score using guitar chord symbols.

title: Advanced Woodwind Repair Methods for the Music Educator
description: Mastery of nomenclature, mechanics, diagnostic procedures and repair processes for woodwind instruments. Progression toward mastery in skills of padding, key corking, regulation and adjustment of mechanisms, and spring installation methods for the music educator. Culminating experience demonstrating mastery of skills.

title: Advanced Brass Repair Methods for the Music Educator
description: Mastery of nomenclature, mechanics, diagnostic procedures and repair processes for brass instruments. Progression toward mastery in skills of cleaning, soldering, piston valve repair, and basic dent repair methods for the music educator. Culminating experience demonstrating mastery of skills.

title: Problems In Musical Style And Music Pedagogy
Opera Workshop
description: Principles and techniques of operatic and musical theatre production, with emphasis on class rehearsal and performance of selected scenes from opera and musical drama; brief survey of the history of opera.

title: Upper-Division Ensemble Performance
description: Instruction is offered each semester in the following areas: brass, chamber music, concert jazz, jazz combo, strings, winds, percussion, and vocal ensemble.

title: Workshop in Music
description: Specialized interest areas for undergraduate students only.

title: Collegium Musicum Early Music Ensemble
description: A vocal and instrumental ensemble devoted to the historically-informed performance of music written primarily during the Medieval, Renaissance, and Baroque periods (c. 1100-1750. Instruments of the historical period used when possible.

title: Mus Hon Tut
description: Individual directed research and study of a topic in music, normally as a preliminary to writing a senior honors thesis.

title: Honors Project
description: Open only to Arts & Sciences students who are active members of the University Honors Program.

title: Applied Recital
description: Public performance; vocal or instrumental with suggested performing time of 25 minutes. Intended for performances in addition to required recital performances (MUSIC 502 and/or 501).

title: Half Recital
description: Public performance; vocal or instrumental with suggested performing time of 25 minutes.

title: Full Recital
description: Public performance; vocal or instrumental with suggested performing time of 50 minutes.

title: A Survey of Music Therapy
description: This course will provide students with an overview of music therapy: its history, theoretical bases, training requirements, common settings for its use, and general techniques employed. Upon completion of this course, students will have a basic understanding of the music therapy profession, and  have knowledge of music therapy as a viable career option.

title: The Choral Program In Secondary Schools
The Instrumental Program In Secondary Schools
Upper-Division Composition
description: Composition instruction through individual lessons and group seminar instruction. Emphasis on meeting professional standards of composition and score/part preparation and creation of a varied portfolio of works.

title: Instrument Repair Industry Foundations
description: Industry-specific topics for Music Repair; addresses the materials, processes, professional relationships, vendor relations, business acumen and climate, technology, and other skills needed in the Music Repair Industry.

title: Instrument Repair Internship
description: Dedicated internship in the Music Repair Industry.

title: Jazz Theory I
description: Basic fundamentals of jazz theory to develop critical thinking and performing skills.

title: Jazz Theory II
description: Intermediate level fundamentals of jazz theory to develop critical thinking and performing skills.

title: Jazz Improvisation I
description: Develop skills in the area of jazz improvisation in a performance lab setting.

title: Jazz Improvisation II
description: Continued development of jazz improvisational skills in a performance lab setting.

title: Literature Analysis
description: Explore, evaluate, and analyze the masterworks of the wind, choral, and orchestral genres.

title: Graduate Theory Review
description: For graduate students in music for whom additional work in the harmonic and formal aspects of tonal and chromatic music is needed.

title: Style Analysis
description: Training in a comprehensive, systematic analytical approach to all style periods, and in verbalizing analytical perceptions.

title: Graduate History Review
description: For graduate students in music for whom additional work in music history is needed based on thier performance on the Graduate Music History Entrance Exam. This course does not fulfill graduate degree requirements. A grade of an A or B is required to enroll in graduate level music history courses.

title: History and Literature of the Orchestra
description: The development of orchestral music from the late Baroque to the present, with emphasis on selected symphonies of the late eighteenth and nineteenth centuries.

title: Chamber Music Literature
description: A selected survey of masterpieces of small ensemble music from 1750 to the present. Special emphasis on the string quartet.

title: Song Literature
description: Survey, by historical period and national styles, of major solo vocal works.

title: History and Literature of the Wind Band
description: Development of the wind band medium from the Renaissance to the present, with focus on the composers and literature.

title: Choral Literature
description: A study of standard choral masterpieces in both large and small forms from 1450 to the present.

title: History and Literature of School Choral Music
description: Development of choral music in schools with focus on composers and literature.

title: Percussion Pedagogy
description: Advanced study of percussion instruments and related performance techniques, teaching methods and responsibilities, organization, and literature.

title: Jazz and Marching Percussion Techniques
description: Advanced study of percussion performance techniques commonly associated with marching percussion and jazz idioms.

title: Survey of Vocal Solos for Adolescents
description: An in-depth study of appropriate solo literature for adolescent singers.

title: Advanced Orchestration
description: The study of orchestra and band scores. Exercises in orchestrating this type of music for different choirs of instruments, as well as scoring for full orchestra and symphonic band.

title: Advanced Woodwind Techniques
description: Advanced techniques in the teaching of all woodwind instruments for music teachers.

title: Advanced Brass Techniques
description: Advanced techniques in the teaching of all brass instruments for music teachers.

title: Lower String Pedagogy
description: Study of low string technique and related teaching methods. Intended for teachers of string, concert/jazz bands and vocal music if they use a bass with accompaniment combos for pit orchestras or swing choirs.

title: Upper String Pedagogy
description: Study of violin technique and related teaching methods, also applicable to other bowed string instruments.

title: Marching Band Techniques
description: Philosophical and practical purposes of the marching band, and the skills necessary to design, organize, instruct, and evaluate a marching band show.

title: Choral Ensemble Techniques
description: Philosophical and practical study of the choral ensemble, and the skills necessary to design, organize, instruct and evaluate the choral program.

title: Jazz Techniques
description: Basic practices found in jazz education, including literature, teaching techniques, and resource materials.

title: Arranging for Bands
description: Assessing, evaluating, and applying the basic issues, techniques, and possibilities inherent in scoring and arranging for various wind band media. Primary attention will be given to concert and marching bands, but students will also spend time learning about various jazz ensembles and other possible combinations of wind and percussion instruments.

title: Arranging Choral Music
description: Examination of basic compositional issues. Application of compositional techniques. Scoring and arranging for various choral ensembles.

title: 18th Century Counterpoint
description: Contrapuntal compositional techniques from the late Baroque to Classical periods, including compositional procedures for the canon and fugue.

title: Theories of Contemporary Music
description: Introduction to analytical and compositional techniques applicable to the predominately non-tonal music of the 20th and 21st centuries.

title: History of the Opera
description: A study of selected masterpieces of musical drama, with emphasis on the relationship of music and drama, and on the unique qualities of opera as a collective artwork.

title: Music Theory in the Junior College 1
Advanced Instrumental Rehearsal Techniques
description: Explore, evaluate, and develop the musical understanding and skills necessary in leading instrumental ensembles toward significant musical expression through effective rehearsal techniques.

title: Advanced Choral Rehearsal Techniques
description: Explore, evaluate, and develop the musical understanding and skills necessary in leading choral ensembles toward significant musical expression through effective rehearsal techniques.

title: Music Business 1
description: Provides students with the entrepreneurial tools for a career in music. Topics covered include: resumes, curriculum vitas, developing music websites, creating an on-line presence, tax protocol for musicians, goal-setting strategies, and meeting short and long-term music business goals

title: Music Business 2
description: Explores elements of the music industry including business frameworks, legal topics, and interpersonal relations. Projects are individualized to the student's specialty area within the music industry.

title: Music Marketing and Management Internship
description: Students engage directly with the music industry though established internships in the area. Opportunites will vary by specialty and availability.

title: Technology of the Electronic Music Studio
description: Instrumentation and systematic procedures as applied to the construction of electronic music. Principles of voltage-controlled systems, synchronous tape machines, and audio mixing. Individual and team projects.

title: Digital Sound Synthesis
description: Exploration of real-time interactive systems. Theory and application pertaining to the creation of instruments and scores using additive and FM techniques. Team projects.

title: Organ Literature
description: A survey of significant compositions from the Renaissance to the present with emphasis on performance practice.

title: Piano Literature
description: Selective survey of music for piano from 1750 to the present.

title: Studies In Music Literature
description: Study of the repertory of a selected musical genre or medium of performance.

title: Wind Ensemble Pedagogy
description: Advanced techniques in the teaching of all woodwind, brass, and percussion instruments for music educators.

title: Techniques of Music Technology
description: Students develop skills of notation, scanning, manipulating, sequencing, and recording necessary to integrate digital technologies into the practice of music making and teaching.

title: Seminar in the Life and Works of an Individual Composer
description: Study of the career and achievements of a selected composer of major stature.

title: Topics in American Music
description: Studies of the various genres of American music.

title: Advanced Studies In Elementary School Music
Advanced Studies In Secondary School General Music
Advanced Studies In Secondary School Choral Music
Advanced Studies In Secondary School Instrumental Music
Advanced Vocal Pedagogy
description: Advanced study of vocal production, exploring the scientific principles of breathing, resonance, timbre, and other vocal features. Discussion of vocal health and teaching styles.

title: Problems In Music
description: Individual guided work in a selected area.

title: Introduction to Graduate Study in Music
description: Library procedures, bibliography, research methods, and practice in preparing scholarly papers.

title: Seminar in Music Theory
description: Comparison of major theoretical treatises and historical compositional practices; practical application for the modern musician.

title: Sem Mus Hist
description: The history of music with emphasis on the correlation of stylistic factors and man's cultural environment.

title: Advanced Analysis
description: An in-depth study of works by later Romantic and modern composers: techniques and styles in relation to form.

title: Theories Music Teaching
description: A survey of the history of music teaching in the United States, with emphasis on the relationship of various theories of music, musical perception, and musical cognition to current practices in teaching music at all levels.

title: Psychology of Music
description: Acquaints students with musical acoustics, perception and cognition of music, music learning, nature and testing of musical aptitude and achievement, affective response to music, musical preference, and the functionality of music.

title: History and Philosophy of Music Education
description: The study of history and philosophy of music education in the U.S.

title: Research in Music Education
description: An introduction to historical, descriptive, and experimental research in music education, including a study of techniques for the evaluation of music teaching and learning.

title: Seminar in Music Education
description: Advanced studies of various topics related to the music.

title: Curriculum Development and Learning Assessment
description: MUSIC 810 focuses on development and implementation of comprehensive music education in K-12 public schools. Attention is given to sequential scaffolding of content within and across developmental levels, measuring student outcomes, and options for dissemination. Among factors studied will be evaluation materials, techniques, and design of reliable and valid measures.

title: Pedagogy of Carl Orff
description: An intensive workshop-style graduate course that provides an in-depth exploration of Carl Orff’s pedagogical philosophy and its application in the contemporary elementary music classroom.

title: Workshop In Service Playing For The Church Organist
Improvisation and Composition in Elementary Curriculum
description: Development of pedagogical practices in the areas of improvisation and composition as appropriate for the elementary music classroom.

title: World Pedagogy, Culturally Relevant Pedagogy, and Special Populations in Music Education
description: Explores current issues facing music education, including culturally relevant pedagogy, world music pedagogy, special learners in music education, and popular music.

title: UG Music Curriculum
description: Provides foundational understanding and developing skills in designing, administering, and assessing learning in an undergraduate music education curriculum.  Focuses on preparing future music teacher educators for success in their future profession.

title: Field Experiences in an Undergraduate Music Curriculum
description: Prepares future music education professors to design, administer, and guide undergraduate music education field experiences.

title: Piano Pedagogy I
description: Piano Pedagogy I focuses on how to teach and perform a broad spectrum of intermediate piano repertoire.

title: Piano Pedagogy II
description: Piano Pedagogy II teaches students how to teach and perform selected advances-level piano repertoire by notable composers for the instrument.

title: Supervised Teaching in Piano
description: Students will be observed by the instructor and classmates teaching lessons and provide feedback and commentary in a masterclass format.

title: Half Recital in Piano
description: Students will present a 30 minute recital demonstrating proficiencies as defined by the instructor.

title: Methods and Materials for the Studio
description: Methods of teaching fundamental techniques; selection of teaching materials outlining courses of study. For graduate students in performance curricula. Taught in divisions according to the major. Practical application through supervised studio teaching.

title: Seminar in Medieval and Renaissance Music
description: In-depth investigation of a selected area or problem in medieval or Renaissance music. Emphasis on individual research.

title: Seminar in Baroque Music
description: In-depth investigation of a selected area or problem in Baroque music. Emphasis on individual research.

title: Seminar in Classical Music
description: In-depth investigation of a selected area or problem in classical music. Emphasis on individual research.

title: Seminar in Romantic Music
description: In-depth investigation of a selected area or problem in Romantic music. Emphasis on individual research.

title: Seminar in 20th-Century Music
description: In-depth investigation of a selected area or problem in twentieth-century music. Emphasis on individual research.

title: Vocal Ensemble
description: Performance and study with established University vocal organization or small ensemble.

title: Instrumental Ensemble
description: Performance and study with an established University instrumental organization or a small ensemble.

title: Collegium Musicum Early Music Ensemble
description: A vocal and instrumental ensemble devoted to the historically-informed performance of music written primarily during the Medieval, Renaissance, and Baroque periods (c. 1100-1750. Instruments of the historical period used when possible.

title: Wind Ensemble-Wind Symphony
description: Performing Ensemble for Graduate Instrumentalists. Audition Required.

title: Marching Band - Graduate Level
description: Athletic Band Performing at Football Events.

title: Cat Band - Graduate Level
description: Athletic Band Performing at Volleyball and Basketball Events.

title: Concert Band
description: Using a player-pool concept, the ensemble will explore outstanding and varied wind literature. Repertoire may range from a classical octet to a one-on-a-part performance of a major large band work. Admission by audition.

title: Graduate Level Performance
description: Instruction is offered every semester in voice and each of the following instruments: baritone, bassoon, clarinet, double bass, early winds, flute, french horn, guitar, harpsichord, oboe, organ, percussion, piano, saxophone, trombone, trumpet, tuba, viola, viola da gamba, violin, and violoncello.

title: Advanced Choral Conducting
description: Exercises and techniques to refine the conducting gesture; techniques of noted choral conductors; and score study.

title: Advanced Diction
description: Concentrated study of Italian, German, and French diction for singing. Materials are related to work in the voice studio, and concurrent registration in MUSIC 855 is required.

title: Master's Report in Music
description: Independent directed research leading to master's report.

title: Research in Music
description: Independent research that may lead to master's thesis.

title: Reactor Operations Laboratory
description: A basic course in reactor operator licensing, nuclear safety, and reactor operations with structured laboratory exercises.

title: Introduction to Engineering Analysis
description: Introduction to analytical, statistical, and numerical analysis, including computer programming, as applied to engineering.

title: Elements of Nuclear Engineering
description: Survey of nuclear engineering concepts and applications. Nuclear reactions, radioactivity, radiation interaction with matter, reactor physics, risk and dose assessment, applications in medicine, industry, agriculture, and research.

title: Undergraduate Research Experience
description: Open to students pursuing undergraduate research projects.

title: Nuclear Engineering Design Projects
description: In-depth application of design principles, engineering analyses, and experimental methods to a nuclear engineering design project addressing design, analysis, constraints, standards, fabrication, and testing.

title: Radiation Transport Simulation and Modeling
description: Become proficient in solving nuclear radiation transport problems using common Monte Carlo simulation software, including an open-source interface package called TOPAS for the Geant4 toolkit.  Obtain capability to simulate shielding problems, dose rate problems, and radiation sensing problems students will encounter in required nuclear engineering courses and in research.

title: Principles of Radiation Detection
description: Operating principles and general properties of devices used in the detection and characterization of ionizing radiation. Fundamental methods of data interpretation and presentation.

title: Problems in Nuclear Engineering
description: Specific studies in current and advanced problems in various phases of nuclear engineering.

title: Nuclear Reactor Theory
description: Theory of neutron diffusion and thermalization with application to steady-state nuclear reactors.

title: Nuclear Reactor Thermal Hydraulics
description: Engineering principles underlying the design and operation of nuclear power plant components and systems. Fundamentals of nuclear energy generation, heat transport, and single- and two-phase flows.

title: Nuclear Reactor Laboratory
description: Theory and measurement of nuclear and reactor parameters of fundamental importance to nuclear reactors and their operation.

title: Radiation Protection and Shielding
description: Basic concepts of radiation protection, doses, associated risks, and exposure limits. Properties of natural and other radiation sources, and evaluation of internal and external doses. Techniques for shield design including ray, point kernel, and transport theories for both neutrons and gamma rays.

title: Applied Reactor Physics
description: Principles of reactor physics as used in practice.  Topics include the fundamentals of nuclear data processing, pin- and lattice-level analysis for generation of multigroup constants, and full-core models of nuclear reactors based on multigroup diffusion theory. Throughout, students will use commercial-grade software, including CASMO 4 and SIMULATE 3, to complete computational laboratory assignments.

title: Advanced Nuclear Reactor Systems and Design
description: In-depth study of advanced nuclear reactor systems, including Generation III/III+, Generation IV technologies, small modular reactors (SMRs), and microreactors. Emphasis is placed on reactor design principles, system integration, safety analysis, and deployment strategies. Topics include advanced core configurations, high-temperature coolants, passive and inherent safety features, fuel cycle innovations, and regulatory pathways.

title: Intermediate Radiation Measurement Applications
description: The course will cover physical principles and mathematical modeling of radiation-based measurement systems used in medical and non-medical applications. Topics include measurement principles and inverse methods, radio-gauging, radio-tracing, quantitative analysis methods, imaging with ionizing radiation, and radiation scanning.

title: Nuclear Systems and Design
description: Principles underlying the design and operation of nuclear systems with emphasis on nuclear power reactors. Energy generation and removal in heat transfer fluids, reactor and component control systems and nuclear fuel reloading patterns. Design philosophy of existing, advanced and passively safe nuclear reactors will be covered.

title: Radiation Measurement Systems
description: Execute methods and techniques to acquire, process, display, and interpret electrical signals from the most common types of radiation sensors with focus on the complexity of gamma-ray spectroscopy and the sensing applications of detection, localization, and isotope identification.

title: Special Topics in Nuclear Engineering
description: Topical material of importance in nuclear engineering, such as controlled thermonuclear reactions, numerical analysis, Monte Carlo methods in radiation transport, effects of nuclear explosions, etc.

title: Radiation Transport Simulation and Modeling
description: Become proficient in solving nuclear radiation transport problems using and developing common Monte Carlo simulation software, including a new interface package called TOPAS for the Geant4 toolkit.  Obtain capability to simulate shielding problems, dose rate problems, and radiation sensing problems to support experiments and analysis in research.

title: Neutronics
description: Particle transport, theories of diffusion, numerical analysis of diffusion, transient core analysis.

title: Graduate Problems in Nuclear Engineering
description: Specific studies in advanced problems in various phases of nuclear engineering.

title: Nuclear Engineering Laboratory
description: Design of experiments for the TRIGA nuclear reactor.

title: Advanced Topics in Nuclear Engineering
description: A presentation of various special topics covering advanced nuclear engineering specialties.

title: Advanced Radiation Measurement Systems
description: Execute methods and techniques to acquire, process, display, and interpret electrical signals from the most common types of radiation sensors with focus on the complexity of gamma-ray spectroscopy and the sensing applications of detection, localization, and isotope identification.

title: Advanced Radiation and Human Health
description: Explores characterization of human health effects due to ionizing and nonionizing radiation exposure. Includes radiation health effects; radiation sources; radiation interactions with biological material; computational methods for exposure assessment; limits and safety guidelines; and risk analysis. Application of state-of-the-art exposure and risk assessment.

title: Masters Report
description: Topics selected with approval of major professor and department head.

title: Master's Thesis
description: Topics selected with approval of major professor and program director.

title: The Interaction of Radiation with Matter
Selected Advanced Topics In Nuclear Engineering
description: Current topics of interest in nuclear engineering at an advanced level, such as controlled thermonuclear reactions, numerical analysis, Monte Carlo methods in radiation transport, etc.

title: Dissertation Research
description: Topics selected with approval of major professor and program director.

title: Pathology Of Diseases Of Laboratory Animals,Fish, Wildlife
Applied Anatomy and Physiology I
description: Intensive study of human anatomy and physiology with clinical application of medical terminology. Designed to develop the PA student's working knowledge and understanding of the general structures and functional principles of the human body. Clinically relevant areas of radiographic imaging, surface anatomy, and clinical case correlations are integrated in course. Students will acquire anatomic knowledge with clinical application through cadaveric laboratory experience to include human and/or virtual dissection.  Develops a strong foundation for the study of pathophysiology and disease states.

title: PA Profession
description: Explores the history of the Physician Assistant (PA) profession, the role of the PA in today's health care system, and the factors affecting the PA. Designed to aid the student's transition into the medical profession, and serve as an introduction to multiple professional practice issues.

title: Applied Anatomy and Physiology II
description: The second of two intensive human anatomy and physiology courses. Designed to develop the PA student's working knowledge and understanding of the human body's general structures and functional principles. Clinically relevant areas of radiographic imaging, surface anatomy, and clinical case correlations are integrated. Students will acquire anatomic knowledge with clinical application through the virtual dissection experience. Develops a strong foundation for the study of pathophysiology and disease states.

title: Evidence Based Medicine
description: Intensive study in evidence based medicine and critical thinking about the information and evidence needed to produce clinical decisions. The student will be prepared to cope with the vast amount of medical literature produced every year.  The course is structured to equip the student to effectively evaluate medical literature, and apply the information acquired to clinical decision making in the provision of individualized patient care.

title: Medical Genetics
description: Will review the basic principles of medical genetics and its application to clinical practice. Students will learn to obtain a detailed family history, and assess possible modes of inheritance. This involves the ability to understand the medical genetics literature, as well as the clinical application of genetic knowledge in the care of individual patients. Ethical principles related to genetic evaluation, testing, and diagnosis will be discussed. The underlying etiology, clinical presentation, evaluation, diagnosis, prognosis, and treatment options of specific genetic disorders will be reviewed.

title: Clinical Geriatrics
description: Designed to provide an overview of the aging population and age-related disorders, and correlates theory and clinical practice in providing care to the geriatric population. The adjustment of older adults to degenerative changes, nursing home placement, and alternatives to placement in long term care facilities are discussed. Concepts relevant to pathophysiology, nutrition, pharmacology, psychosocial development, and ethical/legal responsibilities are emphasized using an evidence-based and functional approach.

title: Clinical Pediatrics
description: Course introduces the PA student to pediatric medicine. Focuses on the physiological and psychological fundamentals of child development, major common pediatric illnesses, various immunizations and medications used in pediatrics, pediatric emergencies, and the management of both healthy and ill infants, children and adolescents.

title: Psychiatry & Behavioral Medicine
description: Course will present an introduction to the neurobiological, psychobiological, emotional, social, and cultural influences on mental health and illness of diverse patient populations with acute and chronic conditions across the lifespan, including end-of-life care, and will integrate human physical and psychological development, human sexuality, and responses to stress, injury, illness and death. Also incorporated is the training in substance use, abuse, addiction, and treatment adherence issues. Principles of violence identification and prevention will be examined, and basic counseling skills and instruments for the evaluation and management of psychiatric disorders will be presented.

title: Clinical Procedures I
description: Designed to allow students to become competent in a variety of patient care skills. Skills are sequenced to the body systems being covered in the physical diagnosis course. Allows students to safely apply the medical-decision making process, and learn the procedural skills in a simulated environment. Course will utilize simulators, task trainers and standardized patient experiences.

title: Clinical Procedures II
description: Designed to allow students to become competent in a variety of patient care skills. Skills are sequenced to the body systems being covered in the physical diagnosis course. Allows students to safely apply the medical-decision making process, and learn the procedural skills in a simulated environment. Course will utilize simulators, task trainers and standardized patient experiences.

title: Clinical Procedures III
description: Designed to allow students to become competent in a variety of patient care skills. Skills are sequenced to the body systems being covered in the physical diagnosis course. Allows students to safely apply the medical-decision making process, and learn the procedural skills in a simulated environment. Course will utilize simulators, task trainers and standardized patient experiences.

title: Physical Diagnosis I
description: Designed to provide training on the skills and competencies necessary for obtaining a patient's medical history, and performing a physical examination. Includes an approach to patient interviewing, acquiring clinical information, organizing and processing this information, and presenting it in a clear and concise manner. This will be achieved by learning examination techniques through lectures, small group settings, faculty mentoring, practicum examinations, and select body system specialty exams.

title: Physical Diagnosis II
description: Designed to provide training on the skills and competencies necessary for obtaining a patient's medical history, and performing a physical examination. Includes an approach to patient interviewing, acquiring clinical information, organizing and processing this information, and  presenting it in a clear and concise manner.  This will be achieved by learning examination techniques through lectures, small group settings, faculty mentoring, practicum examinations, and select body system specialty exams.

title: Physical Diagnosis III
description: Designed to provide training on the skills and competencies necessary for obtaining a patient's medical history, and performing a physical examination. Includes an approach to patient interviewing, acquiring clinical information, organizing and processing this information, and  presenting it in a clear and concise manner. This will be achieved by learning examination techniques through lectures, small group settings, faculty mentoring, practicum examinations, and select body system specialty exams.

title: Diagnostics I - Laboratory
description: First of a three-part series designed to introduce the student to clinical diagnostics in medicine. Presents a foundation for understanding the appropriate use and interpretation of diagnostic testing. Various diagnostic tests will be reviewed to allow the student to differentiate normal from abnormal values, and provide the basis for selection, utilization, and interpretation of the tests used to evaluate principal functions of body systems, and their correlation to clinical conditions.

title: Diagnostics II - EKG
description: Second of a three-part series designed to introduce the student to clinical diagnostics in medicine. Presents a foundation for understanding the appropriate use and interpretation of diagnostic testing. Various diagnostic tests will be reviewed to allow the student to differentiate normal from abnormal values, and provide the basis for selection, utilization, and interpretation of the tests used to evaluate principal functions of body systems, and their correlation to clinical conditions.

title: Diagnostics III- Radiology
description: Third of a three-part series designed to introduce the student to clinical diagnostics in medicine. Presents a foundation for understanding the appropriate use and interpretation of diagnostic testing. Various diagnostic tests will be reviewed to allow the student to differentiate normal from abnormal values, and provide the basis for selection, utilization, and interpretation of the tests used to evaluate principal functions of body systems, and their correlation to clinical conditions.

title: Applied Pathophysiology I
description: Designed to develop the PA student's working knowledge and comprehensive understanding of the pathophysiological principles of the human body.  An intensive study of pathology of the human body, which explores the etiology of organic and systemic disease, providing an insight into the disordered physiological changes resulting from specific disease processes. Provides the necessary linkage between the basic sciences and the clinical presentation of disease states.

title: Applied Pathophysiology II
description: Designed to develop the PA student's working knowledge and comprehensive understanding of the pathophysiological principles of the human body.  An intensive study of pathology of the human body, which explores the etiology of organic and systemic disease, providing an insight into the disordered physiological changes resulting from specific disease processes. Provides the necessary linkage between the basic sciences and the clinical presentation of disease states.

title: Applied Pathophysiology III
description: Designed to develop the PA student's working knowledge and comprehensive understanding of the pathophysiological principles of the human body.  An intensive study of pathology of the human body, which explores the etiology of organic and systemic disease, providing an insight into the disordered physiological changes resulting from specific disease processes. Provides the necessary linkage between the basic sciences and the clinical presentation of disease states.

title: Clinical Medicine I
description: The first in a series of three courses designed to guide the development of problem-solving skills in the medical decision-making process. This course uses a body systems approach to thoroughly study diseases/disorders encountered in primary care practice. Medical knowledge includes the epidemiology, etiology, pathophysiology, clinical manifestations, diagnosis, management and patient education of diseases/disorders. Instruction includes providing care to preventive, emergent, acute, chronic, adolescent, adult, and elderly populations as it applies to disease processes. Instruction will consist of lectures, problem/case-based learning, and small group collaboration.

title: Clinical Medicine II
description: The second in a series of three courses designed to guide the development of problem-solving skills in the medical decision-making process. This course uses a body systems approach to thoroughly study diseases/disorders encountered in primary care practice. Medical knowledge includes the epidemiology, etiology, pathophysiology, clinical manifestations, diagnosis, management and patient education of diseases/disorders. Instruction includes providing care to preventive, emergent, acute, chronic, adolescent, adult, and elderly populations as it applies to disease processes. Instruction will consist of lectures, problem/case-based learning, and small group collaboration.

title: Clinical Medicine III
description: The third in a series of three courses designed to guide the development of problem-solving skills in the medical decision-making process. This course uses a body systems approach to thoroughly study diseases/disorders encountered in primary care practice. Medical knowledge includes the epidemiology, etiology, pathophysiology, clinical manifestations, diagnosis, management and patient education of diseases/disorders. Instruction includes providing care to preventive, emergent, acute, chronic, adolescent, adult, and elderly populations as it applies to disease processes. Instruction will consist of lectures, problem/case-based learning, and small group collaboration.

title: Pharmacology I
description: Designed as a survey of pharmacology, course emphasizes the fundamentals of drug absorption, distribution, metabolism, and elimination. Course focuses on the relationship between drugs and receptors, adverse drug reactions, toxicity, and drug indications and contraindications. Utilizing evidence-based medicine, pharmacotherapeutics with application to patient care is also incorporated.

title: Pharmacology II
description: Designed as a survey of pharmacology, course emphasizes the fundamentals of drug absorption, distribution, metabolism, and elimination. Course focuses on the relationship between drugs and receptors, adverse drug reactions, toxicity, and drug indications and contraindications. Utilizing evidence-based medicine, pharmacotherapeutics with application to patient care is also incorporated.

title: Pharmacology III
description: Designed as a survey of pharmacology, course emphasizes the fundamentals of drug absorption, distribution, metabolism, and elimination. Course focuses on the relationship between drugs and receptors, adverse drug reactions, toxicity, and drug indications and contraindications. Utilizing evidence-based medicine, pharmacotherapeutics with application to patient care is also incorporated.

title: Family Medicine I - SCPE
description: A supervised clinical practice experience designed to give students the opportunity to participate in the medical decision-making process while developing the appropriate knowledge, skills, and abilities to provide care in the ambulatory outpatient family medicine clinic. The course will deliver education on providing comprehensive evidence-based, gender/age specific individualized care, addressing acute and chronic diseases, health promotion and disease prevention in the context of each patient's social, economic, cultural and religious background. Students will engage with the interprofessional team, and behave as a medical professional with patients, families and other members of the medical team.

title: Family Medicine II - SCPE
description: A supervised clinical practice experience designed to give students the opportunity to participate in the medical decision-making process while developing the appropriate knowledge, skills, and abilities to provide care in the ambulatory outpatient family medicine clinic. The course will deliver education on providing comprehensive evidence-based, gender/age specific individualized care, addressing acute and chronic diseases, health promotion and disease prevention in the context of each patient's social, economic, cultural and religious background. Students will engage with the interprofessional team, and behave as a medical professional with patients, families and other members of the medical team.

title: Internal Medicine - SCPE
description: A supervised clinical practice experience designed to give students the opportunity to participate in the medical decision making process while developing the appropriate knowledge, skills and abilities to provide care in the in/outpatient internal medicine practice. Students will encounter a full range of experiences to include, preventative care, acute and chronic illness, and palliative/end of life care in the context of each patient's social, economic, cultural and religious background. Students will have the opportunity to work as part of the primary team while coordinating with subspecialist, and interdisciplinary allied health professionals.

title: Emergency Medicine - SCPE
description: A supervised clinical practice experience designed to give students the opportunity to participate in the medical decision making process while developing the appropriate knowledge, skills and abilities to provide care in the emergency medicine practice. The rotation will take place in emergency departments. This rotation allows students to establish triage skills, learn to recognize and manage conditions that need immediate attention and prioritize care for conditions that are less urgent. Students will engage with the interprofessional team and behave as a medical professional with patients, families and other members of the medical team.

title: Pediatric Medicine - SCPE
description: A supervised clinical practice experience designed to give students the opportunity to participate in the medical decision making process while developing the appropriate knowledge, skills and abilities to provide care in a pediatric medicine practice. The course provides education in comprehensive, evidence-based, gender/age specific individualized care, addressing acute and chronic diseases (sick child), health promotion (well child) and disease prevention in the context of each patient's social, economic, cultural and religious background. Students will engage with the interprofessional team and behave as a medical professional with patients, families and other members of the medical team.

title: Surgery - SCPE
description: A supervised clinical practice experience designed to give students the opportunity to participate in the medical decision making process while developing the appropriate knowledge, skills and abilities to provide care in the surgical practice. The experiences in this rotation will include pre-operative, intra-operative (assisting) and post-operative surgical care. Students will engage with the interprofessional team and behave as a medical professional with patients, families and other members of the medical team.

title: Behavioral Medicine - SCPE
description: A supervised clinical practice experience designed to give students the opportunity to participate in the medical decision making process while developing appropriate knowledge, skills and abilities to recognize and provide care for various behavioral/mental health problems. In settings such as in/outpatient behavioral/mental health facilities and multiservice centers, students will participate in behavioral/mental health evaluations, observe behavioral/mental therapy/interventions, and discuss management plans. Emphasis is on applying skills learned to treatment of behavioral/mental health issues in primary care. Students will engage with the interprofessional team, and behave as a medical professional with patients, families and other members of the medical team.

title: Women's Health - SCPE
description: A supervised clinical practice experience designed to give students the opportunity to participate in the medical decision making process while developing the appropriate knowledge, skills and abilities to provide care in a women's health medicine practice. Experiences include prenatal care, obstetric/gynecologic health histories, OB/GYN exams, evaluation and management planning. Emphasis is on applying skills learned to the treatment of OB/GYN health issues in primary care in the context of each patient's social, economic, cultural and religious background. Students will engage with the interprofessional team and behave as a medical professional with patients, families and other members of the medical team.

title: Geriatric Medicine - SCPE
description: A supervised clinical practice experience designed to give students the opportunity to participate in the medical decision-making process while developing the appropriate knowledge, skills, and abilities to provide care to the geriatric population. Students will be exposed to a variety of experiences including ambulatory practice, nursing facilities, assisted living centers, and hospice. The course will deliver education on providing comprehensive evidence-based, geriatric specific individualized care, addressing acute and chronic conditions, health promotion and disease prevention in the context of the elderly patient's social, economic, cultural and religious background. Students will engage with the interprofessional team and behave as a medical professional with patients, families and other members of the medical team.

title: Orthopedics - SCPE
description: A supervised clinical practice experience designed to give students the opportunity to participate in the medical decision-making process while developing the appropriate knowledge, skills, and abilities to provide care in orthopedics. The course will deliver education on providing comprehensive evidence-based, gender/age specific individualized care, addressing acute and chronic musculoskeletal conditions with and without surgical intervention, health promotion and disease prevention in the context of each patient's social, economic, cultural and religious background. Students will engage with the interprofessional team and behave as a medical professional with patients, families and other members of the medical team.

title: Elective Experience I
description: A supervised clinical practice experience selected by the student designed to give students the opportunity to develop appropriate knowledge, skills, and abilities in patient care, medical knowledge, practice based learning, communication, professionalism, interprofessional collaboration, system based practice and/or professional development. Options for students to achieve this include but are not limited to, additional experience in a required rotation; medical discipline of interest not encountered in the previous rotations; public health service; administration; and health policy.

title: Summative
description: Designed to prepare students for the final transition from student to medical provider. Didactic and clinical year grades, preceptor evaluations, and assignments specific to the Summative assessment will be reviewed to ascertain the student's ability to function as a successful graduate Physician Assistant.  Physician Assistant practice topics will be covered, including certifying examination review, certification process, professional licensure, malpractice, professional career planning, contract negotiations, credentialing process and financial planning. Content will be delivered in lecture, lab and small groups.

title: Additional Flight Instructor Ratings

title: Senior Capstone
description: This is a culminating experience for the Professional Aviation option. Students will integrate the intellectual, creative, and expressive skills/knowledge they have cultivated in their undergraduate curriculum. Additionally, students will combine discipline-specific knowledge with communication and inquiry methods to solve problems and communicate mastery of their respective area of interest.

Family Financial Planning as a Career
Money 101
description: This course provides an introduction to resources and tools available to assist students in developing beginning personal finance skills that will assist them throughout their lifetime. Application exercises will allow students to apply personal finance knowledge in solving real-life problems.

title: Introduction to Personal Financial Planning
description: Fundamental principles for making financial decisions. Analysis and evaluation of personal and family money management strategies.

title: Problems in Personal Financial Planning
description: Relevant topics in personal financial planning will be explored in a small group setting.

title: Advanced Personal Financial Planning
description: In-depth applications of personal and family money management principles with emphasis on credit, savings, insurance, and budgeting.

title: Love and Money
description: Explores the connection between money and relationships. Understand the factors that influence how individuals, couples, and families perceive and manage money. Become familiar with current literature and research in this area. Gain insight into their own relationship with money and how it influences their relationships in order to enhance their ability to help and work with others experiencing financial difficulties.

title: Applied Behavioral Finance
description: Introductory exploration of the field of behavioral finance and its relationship to personal and financial decision-making.

title: Financial Counseling and Communication
description: This course provides an introduction to the interactive process between the client and the practitioner, including communication techniques, motivation and esteem building, the counseling environment, ethics, and methods of data intake, verification, and analysis. Other topics include legal issues, compensation, uses of technology to identify resources, information management, and current or emerging issues.

title: Retirement Planning Concepts
description: This course surveys various types of retirement plans, ethical considerations in providing retirement planning services, assessing and forecasting financial needs in retirement, and integration of retirement plans with government benefits.

title: Personal Investment Concepts I
description: An in-depth study of investment options for clients, this course will include common stocks, fixed income securities, convertible securities, and related choices. Relationships between investment options and employee/employer benefit plan choice will be studies. Current and emerging issues, and ethics will be an integral part of the course.

title: Estate Planning Concepts
description: This course provides an introduction to fundamentals of the estate planning process. Includes property transfer, tax consequences, probate avoidance, powers of appointment, and various tools/techniques used in implementing an effective estate plan.

title: Personal Risk Management and Insurance Planning
description: This course provides an analysis of risk management concepts, tools, and strategies for individuals and families, including: life insurance; property and casualty insurance; liability insurance; accident, disability, health, and long-term care insurance; and government-subsidized programs.

title: Professional Practice Management in PFP
description: Issues and solutions for challenges of managing family financial planning practices.

title: Personal Income Tax Concepts
description: This course provides an introduction to personal income tax practices and procedures including tax regulations, tax return preparation, the tax audit process, the appeals process, preparation for an administrative or judicial forum, and ethical considerations of taxation. New and emerging issues related to taxation will be covered. Family/individual case studies provide practice in applying and analyzing tax information and recommending appropriate tax strategies.

title: Personal Investment Concepts II
description: This course is designed to build off of PFP 462 (Personal Investment Concepts I) and to help students develop a better understanding of the available tools and procedures of investment planning for family and individual goal attainment. Lectures, reading, case studies, and problem sets are designed to help students in preparing for the Investment Planning section of the Certified Financial Planner™examination.

title: Pre-Int Orient in PFP
description: Consideration and application of professional knowledge and skills necessary for selection and placement in a financial planning firm for a supervised experience.

title: Internship in Personal Financial Planning
description: Internship experience in personal financial planning or an allied field.

title: Capstone in Personal Financial Planning
description: Examination of professional issues in personal financial planning, including ethical considerations, regulation and certification requirements, communication skills, and professional responsibility. Development of skills needed for personal financial planners working with families in meeting their financial needs.

title: Fundamentals of Family Financial Planning
description: This course provides an overview of family financial planning by integrating concepts and issues with planning and counseling applications. Students will be introduced to the key concepts of family financial planning, including: insurance, tax, investments, retirement, and estate planning. The family financial planning process is introduced with an emphasis on the integration and application of concepts in meeting individual and family financial goals and objectives. Other topics presented include an ethics overview, compensation trends within the industry, and regulatory frameworks.

title: Field Study in Family Economics
description: Supervised experiences in financial planning, financial counseling, community action, or consumer services.

title: Theories and Research in Family Financial Planning I
Public Policy and Family Economic Well-Being
Financial Counseling
description: Theory and research regarding the interactive process between the client and the practitioner, including communication techniques, motivation and esteem building, the counseling environment, ethics, and methods of data intake, verification, and analysis. Other topics include legal issues, compensation, uses of technology to identify resources, information management, and current or emerging issues.

title: Retirement Planning for Families
description: Study of micro and macro considerations for retirement planning. Survey of various types of retirement plans, ethical considerations in providing retirement planning services, assessing and forecasting financial needs in retirement, and integration of retirement plans with government benefits.

title: Investing for the Family's Future I
description: An in-depth study of investment options for clients, this course will include common stocks, fixed income securities, convertible securities, and related choices. Relationships between investment options and employee/employer benefit plan choice will be studied. Current and emerging issues, and ethics will be an integral part of the course.

title: Estate Planning for Families
description: Introduction to fundamentals of the estate planning process. Includes property transfer, tax consequences, probate avoidance, powers of appointment, and various tools/techniques used in implementing an effective estate plan.

title: Insurance Planning for Families
description: An in-depth study of risk management concepts, tools, and strategies for individuals and families, including: life insurance; property and casualty insurance; liability insurance; accident, disability, health, and long-term care insurance; and government-subsidized programs. Current and emerging issues, as well as ethical considerations, relative to risk management will be discussed. Case studies will provide experience in selecting insurance products suitable for individuals and families.

title: Personal Finance in the Non-Collegiate Classroom
description: This course is an online, synchronous course and the capstone course for the Personal Finance Education Graduate Certificate. Students are expected to utilize skills and knowledge obtained in prior courses in the completion of a comprehensive semester-long syllabus for teaching personal finance in the community or a high school classroom.

title: Introduction to Financial Therapy
description: An applied financial psychology course that examines the intersection of financial planning, coaching, and therapy; the psychology of investing; money disorders frequently seen by financial professionals; advanced techniques to establish rapport; helping clients change problematic/destructive money-related behaviors; dealing with client resistance to change; working with couples and families; and integrating financial psychology tools into personal financial planning.

title: Money and Relationships
description: This course will explore the connection between money and couple and family relationships. The objective of the course is to understand the factors that impact how individuals, couples, and families perceive and manage money. Students will gain in-depth knowledge of current literature, research, and theory in this area. Students will gain personal insight into their own relationship with money and how it impacts their relationships in order to enhance their ability to help and work with others to improve financial well-being.

title: Applied Behavioral Finance
description: This course is applied behavioral finance course that examines the intersection of behavioral finance and financial planning. It reviews the research on behavioral finance, neuroeconomics, and investor psychology; exploring the effects of human emotions and cognitive errors on financial decisions and the financial planning process. This course focuses on the application of behavioral finance theory and research to the practice of personal financial planning to help financial planners improve the financial health of their clients.

title: Financial Therapy Theory & Research
description: Survey of financial therapy and planning literature, conceptual models, and empirical research. Students will be expected to read empirical research related to financial therapy and write a short report on their findings on a weekly basis.

title: Personal Income Taxation
description: This course provides in-depth information of income tax practices and procedures including tax regulations, tax return preparation, the tax audit process, the appeals process, preparation for an administrative or judicial forum, and ethical considerations of taxation. New and emerging issues related to taxation will be covered. Family/individual case studies provide practice in applying and analyzing tax information and recommending appropriate tax strategies.

title: Investing for the Family's Future II
description: The focus of this course is to develop a better understanding of the available tools and procedures of investment planning for family and individual goal attainment. Lectures, readings, case studies, and problem sets are designed to help students in preparing for the Investment Planning section of the Certified Financial Planner examination.

title: Theories and Research in Family Financial Planning II
description: Macroeconomic theory as it relates to family resource allocation decisions. Advanced theories of household economic behavior including the life cycle hypothesis, behavioral economics, behavioral finance, theories of behavioral change, and psychological theories of subjective economic well-being. Focus on empirical research investigating household financial decision-making.

title: Statistical Software Applications in Personal Financial Planning
description: Exposure to and practice in using statistical software to investigate Personal Financial Planning relevant data sets.

title: Statistical Methods in Personal Financial Planning I
description: The course covers applied regression models that are common in personal financial planning research with focus on categorical outcomes. Regression models covered include binomial, multinomial, and ordinal logit and probit models. Particular emphasis is on applying these models in national datasets (e.g., Survey of Consumer Finances) commonly used in financial planning related research and on the interpretation of results.

title: Research Application in Personal Financial Planning
description: In-depth applications of personal and family money management principles with emphasis on credit, savings, insurance, and budgeting.

title: Survey of Personal Financial Planning Research and Theory
description: This course is a survey of current personal finance and family resource management literature designed to provide an overview of current consumer finance research from multiple perspectives. The course also provides an introduction to doctoral studies in Personal Financial Planning at Kansas State University.

title: Professional Practice Management in Personal Financial Planning
description: Challenges of managing personal financial planning practices including, business valuation, personnel, marketing, client services, ethics and technological applications. Relying both on theoretical and applied approach, students analyze case studies that provide relevant, practical exposure to practice management issues, with a strong emphasis on current research findings.

title: Financial Planning Case Studies
description: This course examines professional issues in financial planning, including ethical considerations, regulation and certification requirements, communication skills, and professional responsibility. Students are expected to utilize skills obtained in other courses and work experiences in the completion of personal finance case studies, the development of a targeted investment policy, and other related financial planning assignments.

title: Advanced Retirement Planning Issues
description: Advanced study of micro and macro considerations for retirement planning and its inter?relationship with estate planning objectives, comprehensive assessment of peer?reviewed journal resources and models for forecasting retirement needs; survey of and detailed techniques for implementing various types of retirement plans; integration of retirement plans with marital matters, Social Security benefits, and tax aspects.

title: Advanced Personal Income Tax Planning
description: Explores the complex issues of personal income tax planning and working with individuals with advanced tax situations in business and family income as well as trusts and estates.

title: Advanced Estate Planning Issues
description: Advanced study of the principles and concepts of estate planning. Topics of discussion will include the advantages, disadvantages, consequences, and risks of lifetime transfers versus transfers at death; minimization of gift and estate taxes through legal means; intergenerational wealth transfers and the impact on families; intrafamily and business succession techniques; planning for nontraditional or special needs clients; and recent case law updates on family limited partnerships and various trusts used in estate planning.

title: Research Methods in Personal Financial Planning I
description: Study and application of social science methodology for research in personal financial planning graduate programs and professional careers.

title: Theories and Models in Personal Financial Planning
description: This course is designed to provide a broad exposure to common theoretical frameworks in the field of personal finance.

title: Research Methods in Personal Financial Planning II
description: Intermediate research methods course with a focus on the dissertation proposal process.

title: Professional Issues in Personal Financial Planning
description: Implications of research findings in preparation for professional work in teaching, research, and practice of personal financial planning.

title: Research Seminar
description: In this course, students will attend a research seminar during which a scholar will present current research on topics relevant to Personal Financial Planning. Speakers may include KSU faculty, advanced PFP graduate students, and researchers from other institutions.
