Students and faculty not only listen to learn from the speaker's expertise but also actively engage with the presentation by asking questions. Asking questions is critical for scientific inquiry and advancement. Questions should be asked to (1) allow the speaker to clarify or expand on material, (2) constructively criticize the methods, research design, or findings, and (3) suggest ways the scholar might improve the analysis.

title: Statistical Methods in Personal Financial Planning II
description: Advanced applied statistics course designed for graduate students with a foundation and understanding of basic and multivariable statistics. Examines panel data analyses, such as random-effects and fixed-effects regression, as well as issues involved using these techniques in personal financial planning datasets.

title: Topics in Personal Financial Planning
description: Recent research, theory construction, and program development in personal financial planning, which will focus on selected relevant topics. Designed for doctoral students in personal financial planning.

title: Clinical Research and Applications in Financial Counseling and Planning
description: An overview of client relationship building strategies and the design and application of clinical research methods. Designed for doctoral students in personal financial planning.

title: Global Issues in Personal Financial Planning
description: This course study will give students the opportunity to examine cross cultural differences in defining and practicing personal financial planning. This course will allow students to gain experiences familiarizing them with different cultural views. Students are expected to present research related to the areas of financial planning and counseling to their international counterparts. Students will have the opportunity to learn from guest lecturers and students as well as to experience local culture through sight visits.

title: Dissertation Proposal Seminar
description: Presentation and discussion of proposals for dissertation research.

title: Dissertation Research
description: Presentation and discussion of final dissertation research.

title: CALISTENICS AND FITNESS CONDITIONING FOR WOMEN
description: CALISTENICS AND FITNESS CONDITIONING FOR WOMEN

title: Principles and Philosophy of Physical Education
Introduction to the Philosophy of Art and Literature
description: Introduction to the Philosophy of Art and Literature

title: Introduction to Philosophical Problems
description: An introduction to some of the main problems of philosophy, such as the nature of morality, knowledge, mind and body, political authority, and the existence of God.

title: Introduction to Critical Thinking
description: A basic introduction to both deductive and inductive reasoning. Emphasis is placed on constructing, analyzing, and evaluating arguments.

title: Introduction to Formal Logic
description: Systematic study of deductive reasoning (and possibly inductive reasoning) using the techniques of modern logic. Examines different types of valid inference, the logical structure of English sentences, and the validity of arguments generally. Involves the development and use of a symbolic system which models logical relations among sentences.

title: Causal and Statistical Reasoning
description: This course introduces students to some ideas about causation, and also to some elementary tools for thinking both critically and constructively about causal claims. The treatment is broadly formal, and introduces ideas from statistics, computer science, and philosophy, but requires no mathematical background beyond high school algebra. Some sections are taught using web-based course-ware.

title: Introduction to Philosophy of Religion
description: Arguments pertaining to the existence of God, the nature of religious experience, the problem of evil, the proper relation between reason and faith, and religious diversity.

title: Introduction to Philosophy of Art
description: Philosophical problems concerning the concepts of art and aesthetic value, patterns of reasoning in art appreciation and criticism, and writing histories of art and artistic movements.

title: Introduction to Philosophy of Science
description: Examines the nature of science and how it differs from pseudo-sciences such as astrology, and raises questions about the nature of reality and social value of science.

title: Introduction to Moral Philosophy
description: Philosophical issues arising in and about morality, such as the nature of moral judgments, moral knowledge, moral justification, and the relation of morality to religion. Topics might be approached by a study of contemporary moral Problems, by reading of classical texts, or by both methods.

title: Introduction to the Philosophy of Gender
description: Introduction to philosophy focusing on the concept of gender and its effects in the world, including the metaphysics, language, ethics and the social-political implications of gendered concepts and language.

title: Introduction to the Philosophy of Sex and Love
description: An introduction to philosophy that focuses on philosophical issues pertaining to intimate human relationships, including the concept of love, the ethics of sexuality, and the role of biology and culture in shaping our intimate lives.

title: Introduction to Social and Political Philosophy
description: Examines rival theories of justice and applies them to current debates about economic inequality, gender, race, and sexual orientation. Combines some influential historical texts with contemporary philosophical literature on current political issues.

title: Introduction to Religious Freedom and Democracy
description: Religious freedom and religious toleration. Consider this topic in a variety of contexts, including Western democracies and Muslim majority states.

title: Ethics and Social Media
description: Introduces ethical theories, concepts and methods, e.g. utility, rights and perfect obligations in deontic and consequentialist theories, and applies them to ethical issues in social media, e.g. privacy, bullying, hate speech, doxing, bots and preferences in dating apps.  Students learn to evaluate and apply competing ethical systems, and to defend the resulting analysis in written form.

title: Introduction to Philosophy of Mind
description: Philosophical problems concerning the nature of human beings, including the relation between mind and body, the existence of the soul, the nature of consciousness, the possibility of artificial intelligence, human freedom and personal identity.

title: Historical Introduction to Philosophy
description: Introduction to philosophy through the study of major thinkers in the history of philosophy, such as Plato, Descartes, Hume. Topics may include the immortality of the soul, the existence of God, skepticism, reasons for being moral.

title: Introduction to Philosophy of Feminism
description: Philosophical examination of issues such as femininity and masculinity, the social conditions of gender equality, multiculturalism and gender, affirmative action, sexual harassment, and welfare policy.

title: Introduction to Philosophy of Law
description: Examines fundamental issues concerning the nature and justification of legal institutions. Topics selected from: the nature of law and its relations to morality, criminal justice and punishment, responsibility and liberty, and legal interpretation.

title: Introduction to Medical Ethics
description: An introduction to the elements medical ethics through case studies.

title: Philosophical Composition
description: The purpose of this course is to provide students an introduction to philosophy while assisting them to further develop writing skills in preparation for Expository Writing II. Topics covered vary, but typically are related to understanding ourselves and our moral practices.

title: Introduction to Philosophy of Race
description: Introduction to concepts of race and racial identity; contemporary controversies about the nature of racism and social justice are examined through theories of ethics and political philosophy using various sources, including movies, music, social media.

title: Engineering Ethics
description: An examination of the principles of ethics as applied to cases arising in the practice of the various branches of engineering.

title: Honors Introduction to Philosophy
description: Central problems of philosophy, such as skepticism and knowledge, the nature of human minds, freedom, the nature of morality, justice and the existence of God.

title: Honors Introduction to Moral Philosophy
description: Philosophical issues arising in and about morality. Topics selected from: the nature of moral judgments, moral knowledge, moral justification, and the relation of morality to religion.

title: Introduction to Environmental Ethics
description: Introduces and compares ethical theories applying to issues arising from the use and exploitation of the environment, such as the value of biodiversity, obligations to future generations, obligations to non-humans, and the ethics of environmental risk management.

title: Honors Introduction to the Humanities l
description: Study of selected major works of history, literature, and philosophy which have been of central importance in the Western cultural tradition. Considerable emphasis is placed on classroom discussion and writing interpretive essays.

title: Honors Introduction To The Humanities ll
History of Ancient Philosophy
description: Ancient Greek Philosophy, particularly in the writings of Plato and Aristotle. Pre-Socratic and/or Hellenistic philosophers may be represented as well.

title: History of Philosophy
description: This course introduces students to the development of philosophical ideas from ancient Greece to the modern era, and develops skills in the critical interpretation of original texts.

title: Writing Philosophy
description: Introduction to philosophical composition to develop skills necessary to succinctly explicate single arguments, dialectics relating several arguments, and critical analyses of arguments. Philosophical topics covered vary, but typically are related to understanding ourselves and our moral practices.

title: Introduction to Decision Theory
description: Introduction to decision and social choice theory emphasizing theories of utility, equilibria, and aggregation.

title: Symbolic Logic I
description: First order logic, covering truth tables and truth functions, and derivations in both propositional and predicate logic.

title: Philosophy of Science: Evidence, Evolution and Intelligent Design
description: This course is an introduction to philosophy of science using the controversy over evolutionary theory and creation science/intellegent design to explore ideas about evidence, explanation and tests of scientific theories.

title: Moral Philosophy
description: An introduction to ethics, surveying central issues in normative ethical theory with an emphasis on consequentialism, virtue theory, and deontology.

title: Philosophy of Gender
description: The concept of gender and its effects in the world, including the metaphysical nature of gender, the potentially gendered nature of many areas of ""traditional"" philosophy, the nature of structural injustice, and the way in which gender influences life choices in areas such as career, family life, and sexuality.

title: Philosophy of Sex and Love
description: Engages philosophical issues pertaining to intimate human relationships, including the concept of love, the ethics of sexuality, and the role of biology and culture in shaping our intimate lives.

title: Introduction to Social and Political Philosophy
description: Introduction to modern social and political philosophy, examining questions about central political values, such as equal citizenship, property rights, distributive justice, fairness, and democratic social and political institutions.

title: Global Justice
description: Addresses philosophical and ethical issues of global and international justice, including responsibility to help the poor, human rights, cosmopolitanism and global equality, the moral dimensions of immigration, just war, global health, and responsibility for combatting climate change.

title: Religious Freedom and Democracy
description: Religious freedom and religious toleration. Consider this topic in a variety of contexts, including Western democracies and Muslim majority states.

title: Justification and Reliable Knowledge
description: This course offers an introduction to epistemology, surveying the historical development of important theories of knowledge, alternative conceptions of justification, and differing ideas about evidence and reliable inference.

title: Worlds, Things and Properties
description: Introduction to metaphysics, surveying the historical development of different metaphysical theories, including alternative conceptions of causation and of identity through time, and alternative theories about the relationship between physical and non-physical (e.g. mental) properties.

title: Grasping Reality: The Art of Analysis
description: Introduction to the formal and informal methods of philosophical analysis. Topics include thought experiments, reflective equilibrium, counterfactual analysis, conceptions of 'knowledge' and 'evidence', notions of proof, causation and causal inference, supervenience, natural laws and other dependence relations.

title: Philosophy of Horror
description: Explores philosophical themes in horror films, including the nature of fear and why humans enjoy feeling afraid; the relationship between fear and humor; existentialism and attitudes towards death; the nature of justice; and the horror genre as a medium for social commentary on race, gender, and animal welfare.

title: Topics in Continental Philosophy
description: A study of selected figures (such as Kierkegaard, Fichte, Marx, Nietzsche, Beauvoir, Hegel, Schopenhauer, Sartre, Heideggar, Husserl, Wittgenstein, Gadamer, Ricoeur, Foucault, Lacan), or movements (such as Transcendental Idealism, Existentialism, Marxism, Phenomenology, Post-Modernism), or issues in continental philosophy (such as humanity's relation to God, free will, the state, irrationalism, gender, philosophical methodology).

title: Medical Ethics
description: Selected moral issues which confront the medical professional, including experimentation on human subjects, informed consent, abortion, euthanasia, conflict of interest, and confidentiality.

title: Philosophy and Race
description: The concept of race and racial identity, and contemporary controversies about the nature of racism and social justice, examined through fiction, movies, and readings in biology, anthropology, history, and philosophy.

title: Philosophy of Computer Science and Software Engineering
description: Explores conceptual, ethical, and practical questions about computers and software including the nature of computer science, digital rights, special and general AI, and the ethical responsibilities of software engineers.

title: Business Ethics
description: An examination of the principles of ethics as applied to situations and practices in modern American business.

title: Computers and Society
description: A study of ethical issues raised by the impact of computers and associated technologies on society, including such topics as ethics of computer use, computer fraud, protection of privacy; legal, moral, and public policy-making responsibilities of computer professionals.

title: Honors Project
description: Open only to Arts & Sciences students who are active members of the University Honors Program.

title: Perspectives on Science
description: Provides historical, philosophical, and sociological perspectives on science, its methods and products, the social processes by which its methods are deployed and its products used, and the ways in which its results become imbued with cultural meaning.

title: Symbolic Logic ll
description: An advanced study of logical systems and problems in logical theory.

title: Social/Political Philosophy
description: Examines key developments in contemporary political philosophy in such areas as liberalism, libertarianism, communitarianism, Marxism, and feminism, and on topics of special interest where these theories are applied.

title: Philosophy of Law
description: Philosophical issues arising in the legal context, such as the nature of legal reasoning, the nature and scope of constitutional protections, the justification of punishment, affirmative action, and civil disobedience.

title: Philosophy of the Social Sciences
description: Epistemic methods and metaphysical presuppositions in the social sciences. Topics selected from: models, measurement, reduction, explanation, theories of function, theories of ideal types, and rational choice theory.

title: Aesthetics
description: A study of selected topics in aesthetics and the philosophy of art.

title: Ethics
description: Examines key developments in moral philosophy. May focus on issues in metaethics, ethical theory, or history of ethics.

title: Topics in Philosophy
description: A study of selected topics in applied ethics, applied philosophy, or the continental tradition.

title: Environmental Ethics
description: Ethical issues that arise from the use and exploitation of the environment, such as the value of biodiversity, obligations to future generations, obligations to non-humans, and the ethics of environmental risk management.

title: Advanced Issues in the History of Philosophy
description: Particular sets of issues in the history of philosophy or in-depth examination of the thought of a particular philosopher. Emphasis on issues in metaphysics and epistemology.

title: Topics in Special Topics in Logic
description: Examination of one or more advanced topics in mathematical or philosophical logic, such as: modal, temporal, and epistemic logics; counterfactuals; intuitionistic and other sub-classical logics; higher-order and other super-classical logics; formal semantics of natural language; or theories of truth.

title: Philosophy of Religion
description: Concepts of religion, including truth and faith, God and atheism, reason and revelation, morality and religion, evil, humanity, sin, salvation, eschatology.

title: The Development of Analytical Philosophy
description: The history of analytic philosophy from 1870 to 1960, examining the works of most of the following philosophers: Frege, Russell, Wittgenstein, Moore, the logical positivists, and Quine.

title: The Philosophy of Language
description: Philosophical problems concerning the nature of language and such concepts as meaning and truth.

title: Metaphysics
description: A critical examination of theories about objects and their qualities, causality, space, and time. Both traditional and contemporary sources may be used, but emphasis will be placed on the latter.

title: Epistemology
description: Philosophical issues relating to human knowledge. Issues selected from: the difference between knowledge and belief, whether knowledge is really attainable, whether we have epistemic duties and what they might be, what counts as justification for belief. Special topics may include self-knowledge, a prior knowledge, inductive knowledge, and naturalism.

title: The Philosophy of Science
description: Philosophical problems concerning science, its methods. Topics selected from: qualitative and quantitative confirmation theories and the nature of scientific theories, laws, and explanation in the physical and biological sciences.

title: Philosophy of Physics
description: This course offers an exploration of the conceptual foundations of modern physics, including topics from relativistic space-time theories, quantum mechanics and gauge theories, and related topics in the philosophy of science, including the nature of physical laws, reduction, the measurement problem and non-locality.

title: Philosophy of Biology
description: This course will introduce a range of topics in the philosophy of biology, including: the conceptual foundations of evolutionary theory, interpretations of fitness and selection, group selection, definitions of species, epistemological problems in phylogenetic inference, design arguments, explanation in biological sciences and reliable modeling in ecology and evolutionary biology.

title: Rationality and Action
description: Philosophical issues connected with human action and reasons for action, such as the existence of objective reasons to act one way rather than another, the existence of reasons to act that do not stem from desires, the difference between reasoning about how to act and reasoning about what is true, the nature of intention and desire and their specific roles in action.

title: Philosophy of Mind
description: A philosophical examination of major theories about the nature of the mind, mental causation, consciousness, intentionality, cognition and psychological explanation.

title: Advanced Ethics
description: Selected topics in contemporary ethical theory.

title: Philosophy of Economics
description: Moral and conceptual foundations of modern economic systems. Topics selected from: the relations between ""economics rationality"" and the quality of life, the just distribution of wealth, the nature of property rights, and the value of technology in society.

title: Advanced Social Political Philosophy
description: A study of a single topic in contemporary philosophical literature, with application to current political issues. Topic selected from: multiculturalism, minority rights, nationalism, justification of democracy.

title: Capstone Directed Research in Applied Philosophy
description: Directed research projects in applied philosophy.

title: Current Topics in Metaphysics and Epistemology
description: Selected philosophical issues of current interest in analytic metaphysics and epistemology.

title: Special Topics in Philosophy
description: Selected topics in metaphysics, epistemology, philosophy of science, philosophy of language, or philosophy of mind.

title: Topics in Meta-Logic
description: Selected topics in the analysis of first-order theories and the foundations of mathematics.

title: Topics in the Philosophy and Methodology of Science
description: This course explores philosophical problems concerning sciences and its methods. Topics selected from: conceptual foundations of modern science, qualitative and quantitative confirmation theories, and the nature of theories, laws, and explanation in the sciences.

title: Physiology and Pharmacology of the Hormones
Colloquium in Physics
description: Weekly lectures on topics of current interest in physics by faculty and visiting scientists.

title: The Physical World
description: Presents an overview of physics for students who have little or no physics background. The Physical World is principally classical physics with some discussion of modern physics. The observations and phenomena are simple and basic.

title: Current Topics in the Physical World
description: May include nuclear physics, atomic theory, quantum mechanics, chemistry, geology, molecular biology, astronomy, and other topics of current interest.

title: The Physical World Laboratory
description: This laboratory is designed to introduce students to the scientific method with an emphasis on basic physics experiments in mechanics, thermal physics, electricity and magnetism.

title: Concepts of Physics
description: An introductory course in physics which emphasizes the topics of physics normally presented to elementary school children. A qualitative approach with integrated laboratory, this course is recommended for students preparing for careers as elementary school teachers.

title: Astronomy
description: A qualitative study of the earth, sun, planets, stars, galaxies and the universe; a survey of what is known about the universe and how we know it.

title: Biol In Rel To Man I
description: Biol In Rel To Man I

title: General Physics l
description: A basic development of the principles of mechanics, heat, fluids, oscillations, waves and sound.  Emphasis is on conceptual development and numerical problem solving.

title: General Physics II
description: The continued treatment of the fundamentals of electricity and magnetism, light and optics, atomic and nuclear physics.  These concepts are used to understand D.C. and A.C. circuits, motors, and generators.  Emphasis is placed on conceptual development and problem solving.

title: Descriptive Physics
description: Covers mechanics, electricity, heat, light, sound, and atomic theory. Surveys of the major fields of physics concentrating on how physicists work to understand and describe physical phenomena.

title: Physics Today l
description: The first of a two-semester study of problem solving in physics. Introductory physics problems as well as problems encountered in research will be discussed. Introductory problems will focus on mechanics and thermodynamics. Research discussions will center on topics of current interest at K-State and elsewhere.

title: Physics Today ll
description: Second of a two-semester study of problem solving in physics. Introductory physics problems as well as problems encountered in research will be discussed. Introductory problems will focus on electricity, magnetism, and optics. Research discussions will center on topics of current interest at K-State and elsewhere.

title: Engineering Physics I
description: Mechanics and heat; for students of science and engineering.

title: Engineering Physics II
description: Sound, electricity, magnetism, light, and modern physics; for students of science and engineering.

title: Physics I, Mechanics, and Thermodynamics
description: For physics majors. Lecture and quiz in common with PHYS 213. Special studio.

title: Physics ll, Electromagnetism and Sound
description: For physics majors. Lecture and quiz in common with PHYS 214. Special studio.

title: Physics in Relation to Other Disciplines
description: Variable content, offered only by prearrangement with the physics department and with the instructor. A brief syllabus will be available for each offering of PHYS 300 outlining the objectives and organization of the course for the semester in which offered.

title: Physics III, Relativity, and Quantum Physics
description: An introduction to modern physics as exemplified by atomic, nuclear, condensed matter, and particle phenomena.

title: Physics Honors Seminar
description: Discussions of topics of current interest in physics.

title: Independent Study in Physics
description: Independent theoretical or experimental investigation of a topic for physics majors or for a senior honors thesis.

title: Principles of Contemporary Physics
description: A non mathematical introduction to twentieth century physics: relativity, quantum mechanics, the physics of solids, and fundamental particles.

title: Contemporary Physics: Problems and Principles
description: An introduction to twentieth century physics; relativity, quantum mechanics, the physics of solids, and fundamental particles. The lectures are in common with PHYS 451.

title: Undergraduate Topics in Physics
description: Special topics in physics not completely treated in other courses.

title: Astronomy
description: Topics in modern astronomy. Use of a telescope for observational astronomy will be emphasized.

title: Senior Research in Physics
description: Individually directed research in atomic physics, condensed matter, particle physics or physics education.

title: Honors Tutorial in Physics
description: Individually directed research in physics, normally taken as a preparation for writing an honors thesis.

title: Honors Project
description: Open only to Arts & Sciences students who are active members of the University Honors Program.

title: Advanced Physics Laboratory
description: The completion of experiments of current and/or historical interest in contemporary physics. Students develop skills in and knowledge of measurement techniques using digital and analog instruments. Various data analysis techniques are used.

title: Physics For Science Teachers
description: Study of current topics in physics, with laboratory experience and demonstration of the processes or phenomena under consideration. Topics and activities will be directed toward providing teachers with material for demonstrations and student experiments or projects. Examples of topics are: solar power, laser applications, holography, and subnuclear particles, relativity, or the historical development of some physical concept.

title: Mechanics
description: Principles of statistics and dynamics of systems of particles and rigid bodies. Topics include Newton's laws for one particle, non-inertial reference frames, central forces, system of particles, rigid body statics and motion in a plane and in three dimensions, Lagrangian mechanics and Hamilton's equations, oscillating systems and normal coordinates.

title: Electromagnetic Fields I
description: An introduction to electricity and magnetism. The first of a two semester study of Maxwell's equations in both integral and differential forms. Topics include electrostatics with vector calculus; electrostatic potential solutions in rectangular, cylindrical, and spherical coordinates; dielectrics; electrostatic energy and capacitance; magnetostatics with vector calculus; Biot-Savart law; vector and scalar potentials for magnetisml magnetic permeability; Faraday's law in integral and differential form; magnetic energy and inductance; displacement current; lumped oscillations and LCR systems; impedance.

title: Introduction To The Physics Of Lasers
Teaching University Physics
description: A discussion of techniques which will aid in the development of understanding the concepts in physics. Emphasis is placed on models of learning and teaching techniques which can be applied to the teaching of contemporary physics to university students. These models and techniques are used to analyze a teaching approach of topics, such as quantum mechanics, which is important to today's physicist.

title: Electromagnetic Fields ll
description: Second of a two semester study of Maxwell's equations in both integral and differential forms. Special relativity; Lorentz transformations; relativistic invariants; transformation properties of electric and magnetic fields and potentials; Lorentz force and electrodynamics; electromagnetic fields of a point charge; electromagnetic waves; solutions to the wave equation in rectangular, cylindrical, and spherical geometries; wave propagation in matter; reflection, refraction, and transmission; wave guides and fiber optics; Fresnel equations; polarization; dipole radiation.

title: Physical Measurements Instrumentation
description: A laboratory-oriented course to acquaint students with electrical circuits, their interfacing with measuring instruments, and their use in making physical measurements.

title: Computations in Physics
description: An introduction to applying computational and numerical techniques to solve problems of interest to physicists. Topics include the application of computational analysis and solution to physical problems in both classical, and quantum physics including particle structure and motion, interaction of particles with fields, and model building for simulation of physical phenomena. A practicum is an integral part of the course. Students will use both personal computers and advanced workstations.

title: Nuclear Physics
description: An introduction to the structure of the nucleus, radioactivity, and nuclear energy; the application of quantum mechanics to describe nuclear physics.

title: Introduction to Optics
description: Introduction to modern concepts in optics: electromagnetic waves, propagation of light through media, geometrical optics of lenses, mirrors and simple optical instruments, polarization, interference, coherence, and diffractions.

title: Applied Optics and Optical Measurements
description: Topical approach oriented toward measurements including coherence, Fourier Optics, holography, light scattering, interferometry, laser technology.

title: Physics of Solids
description: An introduction to the physics of solids with an emphasis on energy band structures, electrical and optical properties of solids and solid state devices.

title: Introduction to Quantum Mechanics
description: Concepts and mathematical models of quantum physics. Solutions to the time independent Schr dinger equation, descriptions of one-electron and multi-electron atoms, electron spin and magnetic moments.

title: Thermodynamics and Statistical Physics
description: An introduction to thermodynamics developed from the concepts of statistical physics. Applications include the gas laws, concepts of heat and work, phase transitions, and kinetic theory with applications to statistical physics.

title: Introduction to Astrophysics
description: An introduction to the application of physical principles to understanding astronomical objects. Topics include properties of stars, stellar evolution, galaxies, and cosmology.

title: Introduction to Cosmology
description: An introduction to the physics and astrophysics of the hot big bang model to the Universe.

title: Particle Physics
description: An experimental and phenomenological introduction to high energy physics. The course will emphasize understanding the experimental basis of what is known about the subnuclear domain. Students will be asked to design simple conceptual experiments in addition to solving problems.

title: Cosmology
description: A general-relativity-based discussion of the physics of the hot big bang model of the Universe.

title: Topics in Physics
description: Special topics courses. Topics and credits announced for the semester in which offered. May be given in conjunction with lecture series by visiting scientists.

title: Applied Quantum Mechanics
description: A study of Schr dinger's theory of quantum mechanics and its application to one electron atoms, multielectron atoms, quantum statistics, spectra of molecules and selected topics in quantum excitations of solids, nuclear physics, and elementary particles.

title: Astrodynamics I
description: Uses the laws of physics to perform calculations of orbital properties of space craft and satellites.

title: Astrodynamics II
description: Uses the laws of physics to perform calculations required for the maneuver of spacecraft into different orbits during the course of their flight.

title: Theory Of Atomic Structure And Atomic Interactions
Biological Physics
description: This lecture course introduces basic cellular biology for students lacking a biological background and established connections between molecular and cellular phenomena (such as photosynthesis, cellular foraging, and nerve stimulation) and experimentally verifiable models built from physical (classical, statistical, and quantum mechanical, as well as electromagnetic) considerations.

title: Problems In Physics l
description: Independent study of the solution of advanced problems in physics at a level appropriate to the M.S. Degree.

title: Mathematical Methods of Physics
description: Mathematical techniques for the solution of physical problems. Mathematical topics employed include vector and tensor analysis, matrices, group theory, complex variable theory, differential equations, Sturm-Liouville theory, orthogonal functions, special functions, Fourier series, integral transforms, and the calculus of variations.

title: Computational Methods in Physics
description: Methods of solving physical problems using digital computers including numerical differentiation and integration, error analysis and curve fitting, interpolation, ordinary and partial differential equations, matrix operations, eigenvalues, special functions of mathematical physics. Monte Carlo simulations, and stability of solutions.

title: Journal Club
description: Seminar in current topics in physics.

title: Graduate Physics Seminar
description: Lecture by faculty and graduate students on topics of current research interest.

title: Advanced Problems
description: Independent study in a special problem in physics at the graduate level chosen with the advice of a faculty mentor.

title: Quantum Mechanics l
description: Fundamental concepts and general formalisms of quantum theory and its applications to bound states, scattering or few state systems. Introduction to quantum applications of operators and state vectors.

title: Advanced Dynamics
description: Study of Lagrangian and Hamiltonian mechanics. Includes canonical transformations, the Hamilton-Jacobi equation, and elements of classical chaos theory.

title: Electrodynamics l
description: The interaction of electrical charges with each other and radiation as described by the theory of Maxwell and Lorentz. Topics include Coulomb's law and vector fields, Ampere's law and magnetic fields. Faraday's law and inductive fields, continuity relations and conservation laws.

title: Theory of Atomic Structure and Atomic Interactions
description: The quantum mechanics of atomic structure and spectra: one and two electron atoms, many electron atoms, molecular structure and spectra, atomic collision theory for electron-atom and ion-atom collisions.

title: Electron and Ion Impact Phenomena
description: Atomic collision phenomena; experimental techniques in accelerator-based atomic physics; charged particle and photon spectroscopy; elastic, inelastic, and rearrangement collisions; and applications.

title: Nonlinear and Quantum Optics
description: Theory and applications of nonlinear optics: difference and sum frequency generation; ultrashort pulse characterizations; third order effects; Raman scattering; and higher harmonic generation.  Fundamentals of quantum optics including field quantization; coherent state; nonclassical light; and optical tests of quantum mechanics.

title: Introduction to Solid State Physics
description: Introduction to the physics of condensed matter: crystal lattices; lattice dynamics; electron energy bands; fermi surfaces; optical, magnetic, and transport properties of insulators, semiconductors, and metals.

title: Advanced Topics in Physics
description: Critical studies of selected advanced topics.

title: Problems in Physics ll
description: Independent study of the solution of advanced problems in physics at a level appropriate to the Ph.D. Degree.

title: Quantum Mechanics ll
description: Formalisms and applications of quantum theory, including symmetry in quantum systems, space-time symmetries, the rotation group, many body systems, and an introduction to relativistic quantum mechanics.

title: Advanced Quantum Mechanics
description: Relativistic quantum mechanics; scattering theory; second quantization and the many-body problem. Introduction to quantum electrodymatics.

title: Advanced Topics In Mathematics Physics
description: Critical studies of selected advanced topics.

title: Quantum Field Theory
description: Topics may include second quantization, quantization of the free scalar and Dirac fields, quantum electodynamics, quantization of the electromagnetic fild, propagators and Feynman rules, or other contemporary topics in quantum field theory.

title: Quantum Field Theory II
description: Topics may include: Radiative Corrections of QED; Gauge Theories; Weak Interactions, Gauge Theory of Weak Interactions, Spontaneous Symmetry Breaking, and the Standard Electroweak Theory; Field Theory Methods, and Path Integrals; Quantum Chromodynamics; Regularization; Asymptotic Freedom.

title: Electrodynamics ll
description: The interaction of electrical charges with each other and radiation as described by the theory Maxwell and Lorentz. Topics include the propagation and production of radiation, Lorentz transformations and relativistic dynamics.

title: Advanced Topics in Atomic Interactions
description: Critical studies of advanced topics in atomic interactions.

title: Statistical Mechanics
description: The study of equilibrium states of physical systems involving many particles. Introduces basic concepts of statistical ensembles and presents applications to non-interacting systems for both classical and quantum-mechanical particles. Discusses aspects of interacting classical systems, including a brief introduction to phase transitions and critical phenomena.

title: Solid State Physics
description: Quantized lattice vibrations, methods of band structure calculations, effective mass formulations, applications to optical absorption, excitons, magnetism, and superconductivity.

title: Advanced Topics in Solid Stat Physics
description: Critical studies of selected advanced topics.

title: Planning Field Trip l
description: Multi-day field trip experience in a location of planning significance. Students will learn about professional planning practice in location to develop planning knowledge base and apply findings to other courses.

title: Planning Field Trip
description: Multi-day field trip experience in a location of planning significance. Students will learn about professional planning practice in location to develop planning knowledge base and apply findings to other courses.

title: Planning Principles Seminar
description: Discussion of the principles and process of regional and community planning as practiced by the profession. The tools, skills and knowledge of the planning professions are presented in a focused introduction to the profession.

title: Community Development Field Workshop
description: This course integrates meaningful field experiences with in-class instruction and reflection to introduce students to community assets. Coursework focuses on critical thinking, cultural literacy, and applied classroom knowledge of community assets to encourage shared civic engagement.

title: Urban Data Analytics and Visualization
description: This course will introduce students to different types and sources of urban data and how to use data for planning and decision-making at the community level through data processing and visualization. Spatial data visualization will be a key component of the course focused on mapping and visualization of census, economic, infrastructural, and environmental data.

title: Career Planning Seminar
description: Exploration and identification of career paths and development of academic plans for reaching personal and professional goals including specific coursework internships, study abroad and extracurricular activities.

title: Graphic Composition and Representation for Planning
description: A hands on, introductory course that prepares built environment professionals to meet the varying challenges of the practice world by making them familiar with digital graphic tools, representation and composition rules and skills, and color theory.

title: Computer Applications in Planning and Design
Urban Design and Development
description: Review of recent and historical developments of urban form and space, presented through lecture and discussion. Criteria and methodology for urban design and planning as well as the role of historic preservation.

title: Special Studies in Planning
description: Special Studies in the area of regional and community planning.

title: Professional Internship Report
description: Oral and graphic presentation of work completed in PLAN 703.

title: Housing and Development Programs
description: Review and evaluation of historical and current housing issues, production, and financial systems, including consideration of racial, ethnic, income, and gender issues as they relate to the role of housing developments and programs in community development.

title: Land Development Planning
description: Examination of the process of land development in the United States, and its impacts from the perspective of developers, financial institutions, community planners, and city administrators. Focus is on the understanding of the land development process in meeting community goals and shaping land development to meet community expectations for the improvement of the community. Conflict resolution and negotiation skills represent a communication emphasis.

title: Community Development Planning
description: Examination of past and present approaches to community development planning in the United States. Review and assessment of community development planning policies, programs and practices.

title: Community Development Workshop
description: The organization, planning, design, development, and evaluation of community development projects with real clients and actual locations.

title: Professional Internship
description: Internship with an approved planning sponsor subject to the approval of the departmental faculty.

title: Transportation Planning
description: The background, framing theories, professional activities and policy debates of transportation planning regarding transit, roads, non-motorized travel, energy use and climate change, land use, congestion, finance, economic development, health, safety, politics, asset management, politics, emerging technologies and the sharing economy.

title: Travel Demand Modeling
description: The history, evolving theory, application, and debates surrounding travel demand modeling. Instruction in one of the main travel demand modeling software packages. Practical use of that software.

title: Planning in POP Culture
description: Planning in POP Culture is an exploratory educational adventure of regional and community planning issues through the cultural media of song, literature, visual art, and film.

title: Collective Decisions
description: Provides an introduction to planning as a collective decision-making tool; examines how planners work within a decision-making environment to move from regional and community planning concepts to implementing plans.

title: Regional Econ Development Policy
description: Introduce students to the theory and practice of economic development at the sub-national level. Evaluate the design and implementation of economic development activities.

title: Off-Campus Studies
description: Off campus studies fulfilled in an approved professional office, in an approved international studies program, or at the Kansas City Design Center (KCDC).

title: Report Preparation
description: Comprehensive data collection and analysis based on an approved research proposal.

title: Writing and Thinking the City
description: Hands-on discussion and writing course focused on building an in-depth understanding of the growth, development and social dimensions of cities, and critical thinking and writing skills for built environment scholars and professionals.

title: Principles and Strategies of Community Change
description: This course prepares built environment professionals to address the field of community development through application of community development principles and strategies.

title: Planning Professional Practice
description: Students will learn the functions of public and private sector professional practice including organizational structures, administration and management, and professional ethics.

title: Small Community and Rural Area Planning
description: Synthesis of small community and rural area change, including socio-economic-political determinants as bases for community design and planning.

title: Special Studies in Planning
description: Special Studies in the area of regional and community planning.

title: Urban Design And Preservation Planning Theory
Metropolitan Planning and Design
description: Examination of urban form and the making of livable and sustainable metropolitan regions from an urban design perspective.  Introduction to public and private roles in the creation of urban form at regional, city, neighborhood, and site scales; consideration of regional and global consequences of local decisions; examination of design tools and methods used to shape decision making in urban development.

title: Planning Law
description: Examination of the evolution and current state of land use regulation within constitutional limits. Introduction to zoning, subdivision, and other police power controls within the comprehensive planning process.

title: Professional Practice: Discipline-Specific Topics
description: Covers discipline-specific concerns, focusing on issues specific to architecture, interior architecture and product design, landscape architecture or regional and community planning.

title: Growth Management
description: Study of the process of city growth and change in relation to planning techniques and socio-economic-political determinants. Criteria and methodology for the growth management are reviewed and applied to the contemporary city.

title: Knowledge Methods
description: A course on quantitative analytical methods for planners. The students will learn problem solving and data analysis in the planning profession such as population project, economic analysis and introductory statistics.

title: Planning Methods II
description: The application of computer concepts to problem solving and data analysis in the planning profession for community demographic analysis, economic analysis, and market analysis. Included are elements of professional report preparation and visual graphic presentations of planning projects for use in public meetings and for public viewing. Material developed in PLAN 801 form the subject matter of the presentations.

title: Community Research Methods
description: Focus is on the analytical tools for the selection, collection, analysis, and interpretation of regional and community policy planning activities. Included is the development of survey instruments and interpretation, work flow and work measurement skills, performance standards and program evaluation techniques for policy planning and analysis at the local governmental level.

title: Internship in Planning
description: Assignment to a planning staff for a period of at least 10 weeks; supervision by a professional planner with periodic reports of activities to planning faculty.

title: Planning Theory & Law
description: A course on planning theory and planning law. Students will learn about the procedural theories and legal frameworks that support planning practice.

title: Planning Theory, Ethics and Practice
description: Review of the basic theories of regional and community growth and change, analysis of the process of urbanization in relation to societal determinants and environmental constraints, and the study of a process of planning for professional practice. Exploration of societal and human values in relation to issues of equity, social justice, economic welfare, and efficient use of resources, as well as ethical approaches to these issues.

title: Plan Making
description: This course is about plan-making. Students will learn criteria, standards, and methodology for community and/or regional analysis and planning, and apply them to the practice of planning.

title: Topics in Planning
description: Independent study of selected concepts and trends in regional and community planning and development.

title: Proposal Writing
description: Exploration of procedures of planning, design, scheduling, organization, and management of a regional and community planning research project.

title: Master's Report
description: Comprehensive application of research and planning through individual studies approved by the department faculty.

title: Research in Planning
description: Original research and advanced study in regional and community planning, urban design, and related fields for the master's report/thesis.

title: Microbes, Plants And The Human Perspective
Undergraduate Research in Plant Pathology
description: Research experience is offered in classical and molecular plant pathology and biotechnology.

title: Principles of Plant Pathology
description: An introductory class in the nature of plant pathogens and the cause, effect, and control of plant diseases. Diseases of field and horticultural crops will be addressed.

title: Topics in Plant Pathology
description: Discussions and lectures on important areas and contributions in the field of plant pathology.

title: Special Topics in Plant Pathology
description: Discussions and lectures on important areas and contributions in the field of plant pathology.

title: Survey of Horticultural Ornamental and Food Crop Pests
Crop Diseases
description: An overview of plant diseases associated with Kansas crops, with an emphasis on identification and management strategies.

title: Turfgrass Diseases and Their Management
description: Biology, diagnosis and integrated management of turfgrass diseases. Emphasis is on diseases of intensively maintained turfgrass.

title: Landscape Diseases
description: An overview of plant diseases associated with Kansas greenhouse and nursery production systems as well as landscape settings, with an emphasis on identification and management strategies.

title: Biotechnology
description: The use of biotechnology and molecular genetic approaches in plant and animal sciences. Emphasis is on the use of molecular techniques for plant and animal improvement.

title: Internship for Applied Genomics and Biotechnology
Fusarium Laboratory Workshop
description: A one-week laboratory/lecture course on the identification, systematics, physiology, mycotoxicology, genetics, and molecular biology of fungi in the genus Fusarium. Students should not be enrolled in any other class while attending this workshop.

title: Ecology And Epidemiology Of Plant Pathogens
Plant Nematology
description: An introduction to the morphology, taxonomy, and ecology of phytoparasitic and free-living nematodes found in plants, soil, and fresh water. Emphasis is on the identification and control of plant parasitic nematodes and on lab techniques used in their study.

title: Introduction to Plant Resistance to Pests
description: Basic concepts of the biology, ecology, genetics and breeding for pest resistance in plants.

title: Problems in Plant Pathology
description: Work is offered in general plant pathology, plant virology, plant nematology, disease physiology, epidemiology, and disease diagnosis.

title: Plant Resistance to Diseases
description: Survey of recent literature on conventional and novel strategies for obtaining durable resistance to plant diseases. Several well-characterized host/pathogen systems will be selected for in-depth analysis.

title: Integrated Plant Disease Management
description: Advanced topics in integrated plant disease management will be examined, including: epidemiological concepts, disease loss assessment, fungicides, resistance traits, on farm research trials and utilization of precision agriculture technologies to measure disease impacts.

title: Introduction to Integrative Genome Analysis
description: An introduction to genomics technologies genomics technologies, methods and approaches and their integration to the study of genome biology.

title: Bioinformatics Applications
description: This course will cover the basic principles of regular bioinformatics applications and emphasize the practice of bioinformatics in biological research. The course will introduce the use of computation skills in data analysis focused on the application of bioinformatics in next-generation sequencing (NGS). The goal of this course is to help students be prepared for the next-generation biological research that often generates large data and requires researchers to have the capability in data management and data mining.

title: Population Genetics
description: Theoretical basis for and applications of population genetic principles to populations of humans, domesticated plants and animals, and natural ecosystems.

title: Plant Virology
description: A study of the composition, symptomatology, diagnosis, isolation, replications, molecular biology, genetics, and evolution of plant-infecting viruses.

title: Plant Pathogenic Bacteria
description: Emphasizes the diversity, mechanisms of pathogenicity, host-parasite relations, control measures, and the principles and methods of identifying plant pathogenic bacteria.

title: Plant Pathogenic Fungi
description: The isolation, handling, storage, inoculation, terminology and taxonomy of fungal pathogens of plants. Particular attention will be given to techniques used to study fungi and to the genus and species concepts for important plant pathogenic fungal genera.

title: Introduction to R Programming for Biologists
description: An introduction to the R programming environment for biological applications, including an overview of data management, graphics, introductory statistics, phylogenetic analyses, biomathematical modeling, and bioinformatics.

title: Thesis/Dissertation Proposal Seminar
description: Theory and practice of research proposal development.

title: Professional Communications in Plant Pathology
description: Theory and practice of effective communication of plant pathology research and concepts to scientific and non-scientific audiences.

title: Plant Molecular Biology
description: A study of plant genes and genome organization, plant gene expression and regulation, and functional analysis of plant genes.

title: Genomic Technologies Workshop
description: Lecture and laboratory on the modern techniques to study genes and genomes.

title: Research In Plant Pathology For The Ms Degree
description: Work is offered in each of the major pathogen groups, genetics of plant-microbe interactions, disease physiology, ecology, and epidemiology.

title: Ecology and Epidemiology of Plant Pathogens
description: Experimental and theoretical approaches to the study of plant disease ecology in agricultural and natural systems.

title: Molecular Plant-Microbeinteractions
description: Discussions of molecular and genetic perspectives of resistant and susceptible interactions between plants and bacterial, viral and fungal pathogens. Evaluation of current hypotheses on the nature of disease resistance.

title: Advanced Plant Genetics
description: In-depth treatment of advanced concepts in molecular, quantitative, and cytogenetics of plants.

title: Cytogenetics Laboratory
description: An advanced laboratory course in classical and molecular cytogenetic techniques, C-banding and flourescence in situ hybridization techniques.

title: Topics in Plant Pathology
description: Discussions and lectures on important areas and contributions in the field of plant pathology.

title: Special Topics in Plant Pathology
description: Discussions and lectures on important areas and contributions in the field of plant pathology.

title: Teaching Practicum in Plant Pathology
description: Practical training in teaching courses offered in the Department of Plant Pathology. Most students will serve as Teaching Assistants.

title: Fungal Genetics
description: A study of the classical, molecular, and population aspects of fungal genetics in both model and commercially important systems. Topics to be discussed include genetic analysis via mitosis and meiosis, models of recombination, genetic control of fungal development, basic molecular genetics of fungi, and genetic factors affecting fungal population structure and stability.

title: Research in Pathology for the PhD Degree
description: Work is offered in each of the major pathogen groups, genetics of plant-microbe interactions, disease physiology, ecology, and epidemiology.

title: Nutrition and Management of Farm Animals
Genetics of Poultry and Dairy Cattle I
Genetics of Poultry and Dairy Catlle
Introduction To Research In Laboratory Medicine
Environmental Education and Leadership
description: This course will help students develop and understand the value of outcome based outdoor recreation/environmental education experiences for youth. Students will gain experience in group dynamics and a variety of leadership approaches involving nationally recognized environmental education programs.

title: Topics in Park Management and Conservation
description: Discussion of topics and activities of importance in park management and conservation.

title: Outdoor Recreation Certification
description: This course is designed to provide the PMC major ability to obtain outdoor recreation certifications not directly taught by Kansas State University. Examples are numerous, and include Wilderness First Responder, Archery in Schools Instructor, Aquatic Facility Operator, Playground Inspector Certification, Leave No Trace. Certifications being accepted must involve a minimum of 16 hours of contact, exercise and assessment hours; or additional requirements can be added by PMC faculty to meet 16 hours of content.

title: Intro to Outdoor Recreation
description: Overview of the outdoor recreation industry and profession with emphasis on history of outdoor recreation in the U.S., current trends, public/private delivery systems, and associated individual and societal benefits. Highlights the breadth and diversity of the outdoor recreation industry and emphasizes the contributions and outcomes associated with outdoor recreation participation.

title: Introduction to Natural Resource Management
description: A survey of historic and present-day uses, problems, and basic management approaches associated with our renewable and nonrenewable natural resources. The impact of society, economics, law, politics, and philosophy on the management and use of our natural resources will also be examined.

title: Parks and  Outdoor Recreation Practicum
description: Required professional work experience (paid or volunteer), 120 hours minimum for 1 hour of credit; 240 hours minimum for 2 credit hours of credit. Designed to provide the future park and recreation professional an opportunity to apply acquired professional knowledge, skills and abilities in a supervised setting. A special problem allows the student to explore a topic/issue of professional interest.

title: Natural Resource Based Tourism
description: This course will explore the obstacles and opportunities of using ecotourism and agritourism to contribute to rural economic development. Examples of successful ecotourism projects both domestically and internationally will be presented with an emphasis on the role of National Parks as tourism destinations. Agritourism efforts in Kansas will be highlighted and compared to similar efforts in other states and countries. Principles of attracting and managing tourists and park visitors in a sustainable manner also will be covered.

title: Topics in Park Management and Conservation
description: Discussion of topics and activities of importance in park management and conservation.

title: Natural History for Park Professionals
description: This course will introduce students to the vertebrate fauna of Kansas. Specifically, students will learn to identify the amphibians, reptiles, birds and mammals found regularly in Kansas parks and wildlife areas. The course will prepare students to take the Kansas Conservation Officer wildlife identification test as well as prepare park interpreters and managers to identify species found in their parks and educate the public about them.

title: Outdoor Recreation Program and Event Development
description: A focused study of the design, supply, and marketing of specific outdoor recreation programs and events.  The course will focus on tools and techniques of both the direct supply and facilitation of planned outdoor activities and events.

title: Internship in Parks and Recreation
description: An intensive, paid practical experience with an approved agency, extending over a 10-week, 400-hour span.

title: Undergraduate Research in Park Management and Conservation
description: Undergraduate students work with faculty mentors on projects involving various elements of research in PMC including identifying a problem, designing and conducting experiments, interpreting and communicating the research results. The completed project must be presented at a professional forum, and students are required to write a final project report on completion of their project.

title: Forestry for Park Professionals
description: Principles and practices of managing forest resources in the park setting. Special emphasis will be given to tree management in high use areas such as parks where human visitors present special problems for tree survival and development.

title: Water Management for Natural Resource Managers
description: A study of the management of water resources for public and commercial uses. The course investigates the use of rivers, lakes, reservoirs, and marine resources. Management considerations, including agency policy formation, legal rights, use conflicts, and use valuation are covered.

title: Park Operations and Maintenance Management
description: A focus on the principles and practices of operating and maintaining park and recreation areas and facilities. The focus of the course will be on the daily operation and related issues of various public parks (e.g. federal, state, county and municipal) and specific park features, including: campgrounds, trails and OHV areas, marinas, zoos, shooting ranges, aquatic facilities, and multipurpose sports complexes and limited facilities.

title: Park Planning and Design
description: Analyzes park and protected area planning, its importance, the planning process from beginning to implementation, barriers to successful implementation, and problem-based solutions. Understanding the planning process, related planning policy and the importance of public involvement in the process are the main focus of this course.

title: Methods of Environmental Interpretation
description: This course focuses on principles and techniques necessary to communicate environmental and cultural values to visitors in park areas. The philosophy, theory, design, and application of interpretive media to communicate information about the environment is studied.

title: Parks and Recreation Administration
description: This course will introduce students to the use of specific practices to effectively manage a contemporary leisure service enterprise. Special emphasis is placed on financial management, human resource management, policy development, risk management and general administrative operations.

title: Advanced Environmental Interpretation
description: This course builds on the principles and interpretive techniques which are introduced in PMC 635. Specifically, labs emphasize development of personal interpretive skills and students are introduced to interpretive media not covered in PMC 635 (e.g., video equipment, computers, etc.). The lecture and readings focus on the philosophy of interpretation and the theoretical framework for designing and evaluating interpretive strategies.

title: Quantitative Research Methods in Park Management and Conservation
description: An overview of quantitative research design and methods in park management and conservation settings, focusing on survey design, scale development, and associated statistical analyses.

title: Outdoor Recreation Behavior
description: The practical and theoretical basis of recreational use of public and private lands is examined in the context of ecosystem management. The statutory and regulatory policies and current issues regarding the management and use of lands in wilderness systems, wild and scenic river corridors, parks, and protected areas are discussed in detail. The impacts of recreational uses on the environment and conflicts with other uses of land and resources are discussed. Land use planning policies and decisions, which respond to recreation, wilderness and protected area issues will be examined.

title: Conservation Attitudes and Behaviors
description: Explores the development of the field, psychological constructs that influence behaviors, theories that explain the relationship of these constructs to conservation outcomes, strategies to foster a connection to nature, influencing responses to the natural and built environments, and how to cultivate sustainability through the adoption of conservation behaviors. The main focus is to understand the relationship of humans to nature and how this influences conservation.

title: Political Science First Year Orientation
description: Each meeting of Political Science Freshman Seminar covers a topic related to student success, including student help sources such as tutoring and academic coaching, the Career Center, student involvement opportunities within the Political Science Department (such as research), the university, and community.  In addition, students develop a graduation plan after going over degree requirements and gain competency in navigating KSU online resources.

title: Political Science Colloquium
description: Topics in political science chosen to illustrate current research of political scientists and approaches to the study of politics. Each time the course is offered, a syllabus will outline the topics to be studied and the way the course will be administered.

title: Introduction to Political Science
description: Introduction to politics, public policy, and governmental processes from a political science perspective, studying political power, political thought, public opinion, groups, parties, institutions, public law, careers in politics, and related topics.

title: Introduction To Political Science H
description: Introduction to politics, public policy, and governmental processes. Distribution and use of political power, political thought, public opinion, groups, parties, institutions, public law, careers in politics, and related topics.

title: U. S. Politics
description: The national government with emphasis on constitutional principles, basic structure, functions, and the political process.

title: Introduction To Comparative Politics
description: Comparative analysis of politics in both ''developed'' and ''developing'' countries. Though some attention will be given to abstract and theoretical concepts, the emphasis will be on the actual political process in the countries selected for study.

title: World Politics
description: Introduction to the study of politics among nations-states and other world actors, including a survey of major contemporary problems of world politics and focusing on the pursuit of power, order, wealth, and safe environment.

title: Introduction To Political Thought
description: An introduction to the major themes and leading writers in political philosophy and a discussion of their application to modern politics. This course emphasizes learning how to read and appreciate classic texts.

title: The Origins and Evolution of American Democracy
description: Examines the philosophical foundations, constitutional design, institutional development, and civic practices of American democracy. Students will explore the theoretical ideas that informed the founding of the United States, the debates surrounding constitutional creation and interpretation, the evolution of political institutions and rights, and the opportunities and obligations of citizenship in a democratic republic. Emphasis is placed on close engagement with primary texts, critical analysis of constitutional principles, and application of democratic ideals to contemporary political and social issues.

title: Environmental Political Thought: The Future of Food, Air, Water, and Soil
description: Environmental theories, with a concentration on the world's food production. Discusses important viewpoints past and present, including anarchist and socialist, New Right, liberal, conservative and democratic agrarian ideas concerning agriculture, food production and consumption, and how we should treat the environment in which we live.

title: Introduction To Public Administration
description: The basic concepts of public administration, with emphasis on orientation for citizen understanding; the place of administration and the role of the administrator in the American political process; the organization and activities of government in carrying out public policy; administrative functions, organization, accountability, finance, and personnel.

title: Kansas Politics And Government
description: An introduction to the political institutions of, the political behavior in and surrounding, and the public policies flowing from governmental units in the state of Kansas.

title: United States Politics, Honors
description: The national government with emphasis on constitutional principles, basic structure, functions, and the political process.

title: Global Environmental Politics
description: Examines international politics and policy surrounding efforts to address the human impact on the global environment. Course material identifies initiatives to address global environmental problems via international agreements, their design and efficacy, and their interaction with sustainable economic development.

title: Politics of the World Economy
description: Addresses the reciprocal relationship between politics and economics in the management and functioning of the world economy. Explores the political influences on worldwide trade, financial, and investment flows as well as development policies and financial institutions.

title: American Foreign Policy
description: Examination of American external relations since 1945 and evaluation of processes involved in the formulation and conduct of contemporary foreign policy of the United States.

title: Current Political Issues
description: Each week a different political science faculty member or guest authority explains and analyzes current developments in state, national, and world affairs, using the news media as text material.

title: Contemporary Issues
description: Study and analysis of selected political topics of immediate relevancy and concern.

title: Race and Politics
description: The course will address inequality and political power by examining the role of race and ethnicity in American politics and its contemporary significance to the nation's citizens, politicians, and governmental institutions. Among the specific topics covered are ethno-racial/political identities, political attitudes, partisanship, and voting. In addition, several contemporary public policy areas like policing, labor organizing, voting rights, and housing will be discussed.

title: Practical Politics
description: Strategies and techniques of running for office, organizing a campaign, mobilizing community resources, direct action lobbying, and related practical aspects of local level citizen politics.

title: Ideologies: Their Origins and Impact
description: Explores ideologies, including liberalism, conservatism, socialism, communism, and fascism. Their philosophical origins, transformation into systems of thought with mass appeal, and practical consequences are discussed. The conflict between ideology and philosophy is examined.

title: Introduction To Public Policy
description: The process of public policy formation and analysis with emphasis on the relationship between decisions taken, values maximized, and the social impact of these decisions in over 10 policy areas.

title: Latin American Politics
description: Comparative analysis of selected political systems of Latin America emphasizing political inputs, political organization, and political outputs. special consideration is given to problems of political change.

title: Middle Eastern Politics
description: Comparative analysis of selected political systems in the Middle East including nationalism and the conflict of differing ideologies. Validity and usefulness of various theories of political development are tested.

title: Political Inquiry And Analysis
description: Underlying principles and techniques used in the conduct of political science research.

title: Topics In Politics
description: Different subjects in politics are selected for intensive study.

title: Primary Texts Seminar
description: Emphasizes intellectual history through the lens of social and political development. Students will learn about the impacts of cultural developments such as the awakening of ancient idealism, Rennaissance philosophy and literature, the Scientific Revolution, the Enlightenment, and Postmodern philosophy and art.

title: Honors Project
description: Open only to Arts and Sciences students who are active members of the University Honors Program.

title: South Asian Civilizations
description: Interdisciplinary survey of recent and contemporary civilization in India, Pakistan, Sri Lanka, Bangladesh, Nepal, Bhutan, and Afghanistan, including geography, philosophy, social, economic, political institutions, and historical movements.

title: Introduction To The Civilization Of South Asia 2
The Mass Media And Political Campaigns
Internship in Government, Public Administration, and Politics
description: Supervised field work at the international, national, state, and local levels of government or with political parties or other politically oriented voluntary organizations.

title: Con Chin Pol
description: Principal components of Communist Chinese ideology, conditions determining organizational structure, composition of present leadership, role of social forces, impact of external relations on other Asian nations and on the major world powers.

title: Political Islam
description: Introduction to the main tenets of Muslim politics and a survey of thematic issues across the political systems in the Muslim world. Issues may include Muslim democracy, Islamist movements and political parties, Shia-Sunni conflict, Islamic law (shari'a), Islamophobia, violent organizations, Muslim public opinion, and the Arab Spring.

title: US National Government and Plitics
description: Advanced overview of United States national government. Especially tailored to meet content area needs of prospective K-12 teachers. Topics to be addressed include: the constitution and the American founding; political and electoral processes; institutional structure and function; and, public policy.

title: Comparative Political Corruption
description: Explores the meaning and origins of political corruption. Emphasizes constraints on power and the political economy of corruption-reform efforts from a comparative perspective.

title: Global Security Threats
description: Analysis of global threats to the security of societies and individuals emphasizing issues outside the traditional scope of international relations theory.

title: Pol Of Dev Nations
description: Comparative analysis of politics in emergent states with emphasis on process of modernization and nation building.

title: International Defense Strategies
description: Contemporary international strategies and defense policies with emphasis on nuclear, conventional, and guerrilla war, arms control and disarmament, diplomatic and political roles of the military.

title: Political Geography and Geopolitics
description: Critically analyzes relations between space and power in historical and contemporary geopolitical contexts, including topics such as sovereignty, border making, territorial disputes, and the formation (and dissolution) of nation-states.

title: Mass Communications and Political Campaigns
description: The roles of journalism, advertising and public relations in political campaigns. Examines campaign communication strategies, staffing, and the roles of political consultants.  Analyzes how voter decision making is affected by mass communications and how voters develop attitudes about candidates and issues.

title: Topics in Political Science
