description: Development, structure, and functioning of human groups; societal and cultural patterns; the nature of sociological inquiry. Lecture, discussion, and independent study.

title: Social Problems
description: Analysis of social problems such as drug use, crime, juvenile delinquency, mental illness, unemployment, and family instability.

title: Topics in Sociology
description: Supervised independent and/or interdisciplinary study projects.

title: Global Problems
description: Analysis of globalization and contemporary social problems around the world. Emphasis on non-Western, low-income countries. Examines food and hunger, global warming, debt crisis, democratization, ethnic conflict, and structures of economic and political inequality.

title: Honors Seminar in Sociology
description: Readings and discussion of selected topics.

title: Methods of Social Research l
description: Treatment of the logic and procedures involved in the formulation of a research problem and the difficulties encountered in conducting research. Examines problems of explanation and prediction, the process of inquiry, elements of the scientific method, the design of research, and analysis in the social sciences.

title: Comparative Social Theories
description: A systematic survey of major theoretical approaches in sociology. The works of Marx, Weber, Durkheim, and other classical theorists are examined, along with selections from major contemporary perspectives such as functionalism, symbolic interactionism, structuralism, critical theory, and feminist theory. Current debates over structure versus agency, postmodernism, and other controversies are considered.

title: Sport and Contemporary Society
description: An analysis of sport and its role in contemporary society. Course creates a greater awareness of the social significance of sport in society and fosters the capacity to use critical thinking in the analysis of significant sport issues.

title: Social Organization
description: Principles and processes of the organization and structure of human societies. Examines how people create social institutions and how these organizations and structures shape human relations and experience. Analysis of capitalism and other forms of social organization.

title: Introduction to Social Interaction
description: A survey of theories of social interaction and social psychology with special attention to research on principles of interpersonal relations in social situations, group formation, maintenance, and change.

title: Honors Project
description: Open only to Arts & Sciences students who are active members of the University Honors Program.

title: Sociological Perspectives on Contemporary Issues
description: Analysis of a selected topic of contemporary interest. Topics vary from semester to semester and might include: impact of public policy on rural life; white collar crime; student-athlete education; social change in the Third World.

title: Proficiency Development
description: Integrative review of sociological concepts and skills under faculty supervision.

title: Political Sociology
description: An introduction to the principles of political sociology. Processes of political socialization, participation within and outside established organizational channels, recruitment of elites, communication and influence, power, decision making, and policy outputs. Data are presented from a cross-national perspective.

title: South Asian Civilizations
description: Interdisciplinary survey of the development of civilizations in India, Pakistan, Sri Lanka, Bangladesh, Nepal, Bhutan, and Afghanistan, including geography, philosophy, social, economic, political institutions, and historical movements.

title: International Development and Social Change
description: A survey of the socioeconomic and political dimensions of international development, with attention focused on developing world contexts. Special attention is given to understanding post-war development trends, including the debt crisis, globalization and neoliberalism, and movements for social change, such as the anti-globalization and the fair trade movements.

title: Social Welfare and Policy Analysis
description: Examines policies and programs to address social problems and their impact on individuals. Explores values and beliefs underlying social welfare policy and history of social welfare policy in the United States.  Identifies strengths and weaknesses of current government interventions, with special emphasis on policies to promote social and economic justice in the United States and abroad. Reviews ways to conduct effective social welfare policy analysis and interventions.

title: Community Engaged Research and Evaluation
description: Introduction to partnering with community organizations and applying observation,  surveys, interviews, governmental resources, secondary data identification and analysis, and basic program and policy evaluation.  Students practice notetaking, report writing, and ethical standards that protect the well-being of study participants.

title: Urban Sociology
description: Growth, development, and structure of the city as determined by geographical, ecological, and social factors; relation of rural and urban communities; problems of the city and various approaches to their solution.

title: Society, Food, and Agriculture
description: Examines the relationships between agriculture, food, and society. Topics to be examined include the changing structure of agriculture, food system resilience, the globalization of food and agriculture, issues of distribution, access, and food security, and related social movements.  Examines implications for both rural and urban societies in the US and around the world.

title: Population Dynamics
description: World population trends and their implications for economic development, public policy, and social and cultural change. The interaction of fertility, mortality, and migration with the size, distribution, and structure of populations in nations and world regions.

title: Society and Natural Resources
description: The interrelations among human societies, social institutions, and the biophysical environment. Emphasis on the reciprocal links among technological change, economic structure, and the ecological basis of human societies.

title: Sociology of Emotions
description: Examines different sociological perspectives on the role of emotions in social life, from the level of self and identity, through social interaction, to the macro level of social structure, social order, and social change. Emotions as both social products, and as factors constitutive of social life.

title: Sociology of Culture
description: Introduction to the concept of ""culture"" and the role of culture and collective meaning in social life and social change. How we make sense of the world, and how this affects our responses and actions in the world. Relationship between cultural change and social change.

title: Sexuality and Society
description: Overview of key concepts related to sexuality studies and examination of social and cultural constructions of sexuality. Discussion of issues of power, inequalities, and social control.

title: Wealth, Power and Privilege
description: Analysis of social inequality, particularly within the contemporary United States. Competing explanations for unequal wealth, status, power, etc. Emphasis on explanations related to class, occupational structure, gender, and ethnicity.

title: The Social Organization Of The Future
Social Gerontology/An Introduction to the Sociology of Aging
description: Analysis of the phenomenon of human aging in its individual, social, and cultural aspects with special attention to the problems of aging populations in Western societies.

title: The Sociology of Women
description: Examines patterns of gender in contemporary society and corresponding experiences of women. The course emphasizes both interactional and structural approaches and provides an overview of theoretical work in the field. Particular attention is paid to how gender interacts with ethnicity, class, and sexuality.

title: Bureaucracy in Modern Societies
description: The nature and types of bureaucratic organizations in modern societies. Selected aspects of their internal structure, such as peer group and hierarchical relations in organizations, processes of communication, management, and impersonal mechanisms of control.

title: Program and Policy Formation and Analysis
description: Examination of policies and programs developed to cope with various social problems. Emphasis will be on analysis of existing programs and policies and the formulation of alternative policies. Attention will be given to policy change through legislative action.

title: Pre-Internship Orientation
description: This course prepares students for internship placements. Resumes are written, interview procedures discussed, agency interviews conducted, internships selected, and agency orientation completed.

title: Criminology and Sociology Internship
description: Supervised field experience in various agencies within the criminal justice systems or other public or private organizations in areas involving applied sociological analysis or practice. Criminology majors wishing to pursue careers in the field of criminal justice are strongly encouraged to complete an internship. General sociology students may take this course under the direction of a faculty member who agrees to serve as their internship advisor. Does not fulfill sociology or criminology elective requirements.

title: Criminology and Sociology Professional Seminar
description: Integrates field experience and everyday practices with relevant bodies of sociological and criminological theory and research.  Must be taken concurrent with SOCIO 568.

title: Race and Ethnic Relations in the U.S.A.
description: This survey of racial and ethnic relations focuses on discrimination and conflict now as well as on background factors of the past to enlarge understanding of dominant and minority groups.

title: Senior Thesis in Sociology
description: Independent research in sociology. Students conduct original research and produce a senior thesis in sociology.

title: Religion in Culture
description: The nature of religion and its manifestations in different cultural systems.

title: Gender, Power and Development
description: Examination of various models of development and their impact on roles of women and men in various cultures. Emphasis upon Africa, Asia, and Latin America. Comparisons of public, service, and economic sectors, including agriculture, marketing, and industry. Examination of policy issues.

title: Sociology of Human Trafficking
description: The course explores a range of topics related to human trafficking, anti-trafficking institutions, and legislation in the context of globalization. Analysis of contemporary forms of slavery-like practices (forced labor, child trafficking, domestic servitude, and others), with primary focus on sex trafficking of women.

title: Sociology of the Family
description: Origin and development of marriage customs and systems of family organization; the preparation for family life under present conditions.

title: Sociology of Religion
description: The role of religion as an institution in American society. An assessment of the functions of religion and an exploration of contemporary trends and movements, including information on traditional denominations and emerging sects and cults.

title: Post-Communist Societies
description: Analysis of divergent scenarios of post-communist social transformation, primarily in the countries of Eastern Europe and Eurasia. Discussion of the history of communist societies, changing social institutions, and social stratification in the post-communist contexts.  Examines post-communist space and mobility, class and gender inequalities, political democratization, youth culture and prospects, crime, global connections of the transformation, human rights and  civil society, security and conflict, religion and ethnicity, and the changing family.

title: Sociology of Work
description: Analysis of the world of work, both paid and unpaid. Examines changes that affect the organization of work and the distribution of income, and examines how change alters class, gender, and ethnic relations.

title: The Prison And Other Correctional Institutions
Diversity and Social Interaction in the Workplace
description: Examines changes in the world of work; examines various contexts of work, such as business, the professions, education, and home; analyzes the social organization of work, both in terms of formal arrangements, such as authority and hierarchy, and in terms of informal structure, such as gender, race, class, and other categories of social difference; provides hands-on experience in dealing with interpersonal relations, management styles, communication, diversity issues, and conflict and stress management.

title: International Migration
description: A comprehensive examination of international migration dynamics with a focus on immigration to the United States. Introduces current theoretical debate on migration and examines global migration trends, integration and community formation of immigrants, construction of immigration policies, and impacts on United States society.

title: Society and Change in South Asia
description: Examines recent studies of family and community, population, mobility, urbanization, and modernization in the India-Pakistan region, with focus on social change.

title: Introductory Proseminar
description: Discussions designed to introduce entering graduate students to the discipline of sociology as a profession and to the members of the sociology graduate faculty.

title: Teaching Proseminar
description: Designed to aid new graduate teaching assistants. Includes such topics as planning a sociology course, handling sensitive issues, leading discussions in large and small classes, use of audiovisuals, special projects, classroom deportment, designing and grading examinations.

title: Classical Social Theory
description: Intensive seminar in classical sociological theory. Examines the theoretical frameworks and methodologies of Karl Marx, Max Weber, and Emile Durkheim, along with selections from the works of other major classical theorists. Analysis of primary texts will be emphasized.

title: Contemporary Sociological Theory
description: Comparative analysis of contemporary schools of sociological thought showing their development, current status, and possible future trends. Emphasis on structural functionalism, Marxism and neo-Marxism, symbolic interactionism, phenomenology and ethnomethodology, and exchange theory. A working knowledge of classical sociological theory is assumed.

title: Introduction to Methods of Social Analysis
description: Provides an introduction to the methods and techniques utilized in sociological research. Includes such topics as basic collecting and analyzing sociological data.

title: Intermediate Methods of Social Research
description: Current sociological research techniques, strategies of research design, construction of research instruments, logic of sociological inquiry, conceptualization, problem formation, and preparation of research proposals.

title: Qualitative Methodology
description: Collection, analysis, and presentation of sociological data using such methods as participant observation, ethnomethodology, community analysis, documentary research and historiography, case study, and life history. Emphasis upon formulation of problems and the execution of research.

title: Quantitative Methods
description: Provides instruction for advanced techniques in the quantitative analysis of sociological data. Includes regression analysis and estimation of models with categorical data. A working knowledge of basic statistical concepts is assumed.

title: Social Demography
description: The study of human population, including the social, economic, political, ecological, and cultural determinants and consequences of changes in fertility, mortality, and migration.

title: Sociology of Agriculture
description: Provides a survey of the subfield of sociology of agriculture, including an examination of its roots, growth and current theoretical and substantive directions. Explores the linkages between agriculture, commodity systems, food and the environment.

title: Sociology of Community
description: A survey of theoretical perspectives and current research on the sociology of community in the U.S. and other countries. Examples of issues covered include community growth and decline, social inequality, community power and politics, social implications of community economic change, urbanization, and the global context of local change.

title: Gender Differentiation and Inequality
description: Offers an analysis of the social construction and reproduction of gender identities. Focuses on current feminist theory and research on the links between gender identities and gender inequality.

title: Environment and Society
description: The interrelationships among population, technology, environment, and social organization. An examination of the origins and development of human ecology in sociology, and recent attempts to redefine the area. Special emphasis on current theoretical and research efforts focusing on the history and uses of ecological ideas.

title: Sociology of Culture
description: Intensive reading and discussion of classical and contemporary works relating to the study of culture, all those things that render the world around us comprehensible, communicable, and meaningful. The Focus on collective meaning, the process through which meaning is generated, and the roles collective meanings play in social life.

title: Comparative Social Systems
description: Compares social systems in different regions of the world. Examines models of comparative and historical sociology. Provides students with a background for conducting and evaluating comparative research. Treats such issues as socioeconomic development, group relations, and age and sex roles from a cross-cultural perspective.

title: Social Stratification
description: Survey of major sociological theories and research on inequality in modern societies. Examines the distribution of wealth, status, political power, and other valued resources; the effects of class, race, gender, and other modes of social differentiation; social mobility and the reproduction of inequality.

title: Technology and Society
description: Technology has contributed to significant changes in our social, cultural, and economic landscape. Provides a survey of theoretical perspectives and current research on the role of technology in society.

title: Criminal Justice in Context
description: Examines various institutions of contemporary criminal justice operations, focusing primarily on the ""three C's""-cops, courts, and corrections-and adjacent subjects such as law. Emphasis will be given to foundational works that emphasize history, theory, policy, and empirical research.

title: Sociology of Development
description: Introduction to the sociology of development.  Focus is comparative-historical, involving theoretical and empirical analysis of development dynamics over time and across places, both within the U.S. and abroad.  Significant attention to the interdisciplinary aspects of the sociology of development, including economic, geographic-spatial, and political analyses.

title: International Development
description: Comparative analysis of competing theories and experiences of development in post-WWII Asian, African, and Latin American societies.  Topics include how core theories of development have changed over time and how well they explain related and changing patterns of finance and production, class struggle, state society-relations, labor rights, gender relations, and migration.

title: Sociology of Deviance
description: A critical examination of the nature, types, and societal reactions to deviant behavior. Special emphasis will be given to the process of stigmatization, the social construction of deviance as a social problem, and the effect of inequalities such as race, gender, class, and sexuality on the process of creating and applying deviant labels to individuals and groups.

title: Criminological Theory
description: Focuses on traditional and contemporary criminological theories with secondary emphasis on presentation of empirical studies employing the theories under study. Theoretical integration will be stressed.

title: Sociology of Victimology
description: Focuses on the unique area of Victimology. Examines recent scholarship, methodological issues, and theoretical perspectives. Requires research paper and peer criticism.

title: Social Movements
description: Examines sociological theories about social movements and their organizational structures and strategies, with an analysis of historical and contemporary nationalist, socialist, labor, women's, civil rights, and environmental movements in the United States and around the world.

title: Practicum in Social Analysis
description: Provides professional field experience for students enrolled in the Master's in Social Analysis program in an approved public agency, organization, or private firm, under direction of a faculty member. Requires participation in a quarterly seminar.

title: Research Problems in Sociology
description: Individual study and research for students admitted to doctoral standing in the Graduate School.

title: Seminar in Sociological Theory
description: Selected topics in sociological theory. May be repeated with consent of supervisory committee.

title: Methods of Social Policy Research
description: Examination of principles, techniques, and design of retrospective and prospective social policy research.

title: Specialized Approaches to Socialogical Research
description: Intensive examination of methodological approaches developed for analysis of sociological problems. The approaches to be examined will depend on faculty and student interests. Likely foci include estimation procedures for structural equation models, advanced techniques for quantitative analysis of categorical data, techniques for social network analysis and methods for comparative and historical analysis.

title: Seminar in Demographic Methods
description: Demographic processes such as fertility, mortality, and migration, with emphasis on measurements, methods, and analytical techniques. Includes the construction of life tables and population estimates and projections.

title: Seminar in Comparative Community Organization
description: Recent developments in theory and research on processes affecting community organization. Topics will vary. Possible emphases include world urbanization, community linkages to regional and global systems, technology and local economic development, rural community development, and community-based collective action.

title: Gender and Society
description: Exploration of feminist theorizing of gender relations in constructing and maintaining social institutions. Objective is to acquaint students with the strengths and weaknesses of current theories of gender, and to analyze social structures as both constituting and constitutive of gender relations at the macro/meso level of society.

title: Sociology of Rural Development
description: Consideration of selected topics and issues in the social organization of rural areas, both international and in the U.S.

title: Seminar in Demography
description: Consideration of selected topical areas in demography such as fertility and family planning, migration, population and development, and regional demography.

title: Seminar in Work and Organization
description: Topics and issues in the social structure of industries, firms, and labor processes.

title: Seminar in the Sociology of Aging
description: Consideration of selected topics and issues in the sociology of aging such as retirement and institutional change, societal reactions to aging, population structure and socioeconomic consequences of aging populations, the social organization of leisure, the impact on social organization of services for older people, the structural and organizational consequences of widowhood, age-grading and stratification in aging populations, analysis of the impact on community structure, and organization of special institutions for older people.

title: Seminar in Social Interaction
description: Examination of current theoretical, methodological, and research issues and topics.

title: Political Sociology
description: A survey of core readings in the subfield of political sociology.  Covers classical sociological and political theory followed by current debates and research agendas, including world systems theory, post-colonial theory, neo-marxism, resource mobilization theory, game theory, and cultural theories of political action.

title: States and Civil Society
description: Examines the relationship between the modern nation-state and powerful non-state institutions and social forces.  Introduces prominent theories on the rise of the nation-state and its global transmission as the dominant form of governance.  Addresses the role of social capital, social movements, NGOs, religious organizations, and educational institutions in upholding or challenging nation-state legitimacy.

title: Methods Seminar in Criminology/Diviance
description: Examination of principles, techniques, and design of research in criminology, deviance, and corrections.

title: Topics Seminar in Criminology/Deviance
description: Selected topical areas in criminology/deviance such as gender, race and/or class and crime; social control; domestic violence; lethal violence; victimology; issues in corrections; and issues in law.

title: Orientation To The Social Work Major
Social Work: The Helping Profession
description: An introduction to the profession of social work and the various fields of social service by observing, experiencing, and analyzing social work and its place in society. An opportunity for the student to test social work as a possible career choice.

title: First Year Orientation
description: Orients first year students to the social work major and on-campus resources that will enable them to be successful. Engages students in discussion groups and community engagement activities to meet social work faculty members and students within the major. Develops student relationships and practices that allow them to effectively utilize college resources and become members of a community of learners.

title: Social Work Career Development
description: Develops career goals of social work majors. Exposes students to the breadth of career options in social work. Connects students with practicing social workers and faculty members.

title: Basic Skills for Working with People
description: Course develops basic skill components for the helping professions. Students learn fundamentals of interpersonal communication.

title: Self-care for the Helping Professional
description: Explores personal health and self-care of the helping professional. Discusses importance of self-care and best practices for maintaining personal and professional well-being across the physical, mental, social, and spiritual self.

title: Client Perspectives in Social Work
description: Develops student critical thinking skills and understanding of client experiences through memoirs and first-person accounts of living within different at-risk populations. Explores client experiences and needed policies to address social problems.

title: Diversity, Equity, Inclusion, and Belonging in Social Work Practice
description: Provides foundational knowledge of diversity, equity, inclusion, and belonging within social work practice in the United States. Examines theoretical, conceptual, and policy-based knowledge of systems of oppression, and the ways in which power and privilege are manifested within them.

title: Fundamentals of Communication for the Agriculture and Food Science Community
description: This course develops communication skills among the scientific community to improve discourse with lay persons. Fundamentals of active listening as well as techniques to diffuse tense situations are addressed.

title: Human Behavior in the Social Environment l
description: An introduction to the relationship among biological, social, psychological, and cultural systems as they affect or are affected by human behavior as it relates to social world models of practice. Emphasis on social systems understanding of human development.

title: Dynamics of Working with Older Adults
description: Provide students in all fields a unique foundation upon which successful interaction with older adults is built. Specific communication techniques will be taught along with the knowledge, skills and values necessary to understand working with, or on the behalf of, older consumers, clients, patients and family members.

title: Social Work Research Methods and Analysis
description: First of two research courses. Emphasis on social work research methodology and statistical analysis of small sample data sets. Content examines the ethics and processes of research.

title: Global Social Work
description: Explores the underlying theories, concepts, and practice skills related to international social work.

title: Social Work with Criminal Offenders and Victims
description: This course offers an overview of social work practice and professional helping skills in the criminal justice and legal systems.

title: Substance Abuse Case Management and Holistic Recovery
description: Introduction to the concept of case management and the role case managers play in helping persons with substance use disorders obtain and maintain recovery.

title: Child Welfare
description: Provides students with an understanding of the dynamics of child maltreatment and the role of the child welfare system in responding to maltreatment. Explores maltreatment across generations, the role of societal norms in perpetuating maltreatment, and culturally competent services for ending the intergenerational cycle of abuse. Examines government policies over time to promote child welfare and the effect of these policies on child safety, permanency, and family reunification.

title: Social Work and Intimate Partner Violence
description: This course will provide students an understanding of the knowledge and skills necessary to respond to intimate partner violence in an ethical, victim-centered and trauma informed manner.

title: Mental Health and Wellness: A Toolbox for Helping Professionals
description: Increase understanding of evidence-based treatments for mental illness. Enhance student coping skills and own wellness. Examine impact of different activities on brain and body.

title: Community Activism and Social Change
description: Examines how social workers and citizens, individually and collectively, can achieve social progress through nonviolent social change.

title: Honors Project
description: Open only to Arts & Sciences students who are active members of the University Honors Program.

title: Topics in Social Work
description: Integrative review of social work concepts and skills under faculty supervision.

title: Social Welfare and Policy Analysis
description: Examines policies and programs to address social problems and their impact on individuals. Explores values and beliefs underlying social welfare policy and history of social welfare policy in the United States.  Identifies strengths and weaknesses of current government interventions, with special emphasis on policies to promote social and economic justice in the United States and abroad. Reviews ways to conduct effective social welfare policy analysis and interventions.

title: Human Behavior In The Social Environment
Human Behavior And The Social Environment 2
Social Work Research and Evaluation Methods
description: Focuses on applying research skills to engage in the evidence-based practice (EBP) process, a core component of generalist practice. Students will prepare a program evaluation proposal, including formulating a problem statement, EBP development, designing an evaluation plan, and selecting appropriate outcome measures.

title: Social Work Survey Research
description: Introduction to designing and conducting survey-based research in social work, including formulating research questions, developing survey instruments, navigating the Institutional Review Board (IRB) process, collecting data using Qualtrics, and descriptive data analysis.

title: Field Practicum Preparation
description: Social work majors take this course in the semester before enrollment in SOCWK 562 Field Experience, in preparation for the field practice experience. Students explore various fields of practice and social work settings and work with the instructor to plan their practicum for the following semester.

title: Social Work Practice I
description: Continued development of social work practice skills. The social systems perspective is used to guide the development of a problem-solving methodology with attention to information gathering, assessment, and problem identification. Values clarification and self awareness are emphasized and the skills needed for intervention, termination, and evaluation are introduced.

title: Social Work Practice II
description: Continuation of SOCWK 560 with emphasis on skill development in interventinon techniques, and practice evaluatiuon from a social systems perspective. A variety of intervention strategies and techniques is presented with emphasis on the development of a social work frame of reference.

title: Field Experience
description: Supervised field experience in community agencies and programs as a practical application of social work knowledge and skills gained from major course work. Emphasis on direct work with clients, whether individuals, groups, or communities. Seminars make use of student's experiences to analyze social work theory and practice.

title: The Practice of Social Work in Rural Areas
description: A review of characteristics and social problems of rural areas. The development of practice competency in social work roles and skills necessary for rural practice.

title: Social Work Professional Seminar
description: A review of various theories in the behavioral sciences which influence the practice of social work. Primary focus of the course is on the use of these theories in implementing change in various client systems.

title: Social Policy
description: Examination of policies and programs developed to cope with various social problems. Emphasis will be placed on analysis of existing programs and policies and the formulation of alternative policies. Attention will be given to policy change through organizational and legislative action.

title: Human Behavior In The Social Environment
Macro Practice and Theory
description: Continuation of social work practice sequence with focus on skills development for macro-level social work practice and an understanding of macro-systems using the ecological perspective as a framework.  Community and organization intervention strategies with emphasis on the development of a social work frame of reference.  Focus on institutional racism, institutional discrimination, and the importance of recognizing these functions within society.

title: Social Work with Groups
description: Theoretical framework for effective generalist social work practice with groups, facilitates the development of group leaders who possess an understanding of group dynamics and effective leadership skills through group discussion, experiential exercises and leadership experiences. To be taken concurrently with SOCWK 561.

title: Women's Perspectives On Peace And War
Social Welfare as a Social Institution
description: Examines policies and programs to address social problems and their impact on individuals. Explores values and beliefs underlying social welfare policy and history of social welfare policy in the United States.  Identifies strengths and weaknesses of current government interventions, with special emphasis on policies to promote social and economic justice in the United States and abroad. Reviews ways to conduct effective social welfare policy analysis and interventions.

title: Fundamentals of Communication for the Agric & Food Science Community
description: This course develops communication skills among the scientific community to improve discourse with lay persons. Fundamentals of active listening as well as techniques to diffuse tense situations are addressed. Students will apply the skills learned to their career field through a final research project.

title: Social Work Research and Evaluation Methods
description: This course focuses on applying research skills to engage in the evidence-based practice (EBP) process, a core component of generalist practice. Students will prepare a program evaluation proposal, including formulating a problem statement, EBP development, designing an evaluation plan, and selecting appropriate outcome measures.

title: Assessment and Treatment of Substance Use Disorders
description: Comprehensive examination of substance use treatment and rehabilitation includes a thorough examination of the treatment models of addiction, recovery, and relapse.

title: Social Work Practice I
description: Development of social work practice skills. The social systems perspective is used to guide the development of a problem-solving methodology with attention to information gathering, assessment, and problem identification. Values clarification and self-awareness are emphasized and the skills needed for intervention, termination and evaluation are introducted.

title: Social Work Practice II
description: Advanced development of social work practice skills with emphasis on skill development in intervention techniques, and practice evaluation from a social systems perspective. A variety of intervention strategies and techniques is presented with emphasis on the development of a social work frame of reference.

title: Field Experience
description: Supervised field experience in community agencies and programs as a practical application of social work knowledge and skills gained from social work course work. Emphasis on direct work with clients, whether individuals, groups, or communities. Assignments make use of student's experiences to analyze social work theory and practice.

title: Macro Practice and Theory
description: Social work practice with focus on skills development for macro-level social work skills and an understanding of macro-systems using the ecological perspective as a framework.  Community and organization intervention strategies with emphasis on the development of a social work frame of reference.  Focus on institutional racism, institutional discrimination, and the importance of recognizing these functions within society.

title: Social Work with Groups
description: Provides the theoretical framework for effective generalist social work practice with groups, facilitates the development of group leaders who possess an understanding of group dynamics and effective leadership skills through group discussion, experiential exercises and leadership experiences.

title: Orientation for Summer School Abroad Program in Mexico City
description: Orientation for Summer School Abroad Program in Zacatecas/Cuernavaca, Mexico

title: Spanish I
description: Designed for students with no prior Spanish experience.  Develops basic language skills and cultural awareness to function in everyday situations in Spanish-speaking communities.  Conducted in Spanish with practice in speaking, listening, reading, and writing.

title: Spanish II
description: Continuation of Spanish 1.  Develops basic language skills and cultural awareness to function in everyday situations in Spanish-speaking communities.  Conducted in Spanish with practice in speaking, listening, reading, and writing.

title: Conversational Spanish for the Workplace
description: Introduction to the basic Spanish conversational skills focusing on everyday language used in the workplace with an emphasis on technical terms and concepts, supplemented with grammar and writing.

title: Spanish III
description: Enhances skills in reading and analysis to develop intermediate proficiency in Spanish.  Grammatical structures presented in context through literary and informational texts, media, to promote cultural awareness.  Conducted in Spanish.

title: Spanish IV
description: Bridges to more advanced language, culture, and literature courses.  Grammatical structures studied in context through literary and informational materials.  Conducted in Spanish.  Practice in researching and presenting the history and cultures of Spanish-speaking countries.

title: Spanish IV for Heritage Speakers
description: Designed for individuals who have been exposed to Spanish from an early age at home. Promotes awareness of linguistic variation in Spanish; exposes students to an academic register; develops basic literary skills, such as spelling and lexicon, so that learners can effectively engage in scholarly contexts and endeavors.

title: Intermediate Studies in Spanish
description: Offered only to participants in study abroad programs. Prior consultation for approval is expected.

title: Spanish Communication through Pop Culture
description: Improve spontaneous conversation in Spanish through impromptu discussions, word games, partner conversations and more. Improve our writing skills through creative writing prompts, peer-review and writing workshops. Activate language absorption through authentic input by listening to music, watching documentaries, tv shows and films, and reading short stories and graphic novels in Spanish.

title: Spanish for Heritage Speakers: Composition and Grammar
description: Course for heritage speakers of Spanish to improve reading and writing skills, including grammar, and develop a professional and academic knowledge of the language.

title: Spanish Conversation
description: Intensive practice in conversation to develop listening and speaking skills, expand vocabulary, and review grammatical structures.

title: Listening Skills
description: Develops aural comprehension skills for communicative situations with focus on Spanish phonetics and interpretation of oral texts within the corresponding cultural context.

title: Professional Spanish/Business
description: Develops culturally relevant communication skills in business and professional contexts. Specialized content and vocabulary including professional writing, such as employment materials.

title: Professional Spanish:  Health and Human Services
description: Develops culturally relevant communication skills in health and human services contexts (including social work, kinesiology, dietetics, education).  Specialized content and vocabulary, community-based learning, and experience in professional writing.

title: Land, Air, and Water: Conservation, Sustainability and the Environment in the Spanish-Speaking World
description: The interest in environmental issues has steadily increased in the last two decades. In this course, students will expand their vocabulary in Spanish to discuss environmental issues and examine Latin America and Spain's impressive geography, flora and fauna, and varying climates, climate issues, and sustainability and conservation efforts.

title: Hispanic Readings and Media
description: Communication, critical thinking, and analytical skills applied to Spanish-language literary texts, film, and art. Skill development through conversation, presentations, papers, and creative projects.

title: Hispanic Readings and Media for Heritage Language Learners
description: Spanish literary introductory course for students who have acquired Spanish at home at an early age. A wide variety of literary productions and modalities are explored (e.g., songs, poems, fiction, and films). Special emphasis is placed on 1) identity-related discussions and 2) the sharing of cultural experiences to facilitate the co—construction of knowledge and the creation of a community.

title: Cultures of the Spanish-speaking World: U.S. Latinx
description: Explores the development of literature and other media representing Latinx experiences in the United States since the Mexican-American War.

title: Food in Spanish American History and Culture
description: Traces the sociocultural significance of crops such as chocolate, corn, sugar, and potato in Spanish America, developing themes including race, class, health, commerce, and agriculture. Cultural texts illustrate the role of food across history from the pre-Hispanic era to the present. Develops cultural awareness, critical thinking, communication, and research skills.

title: Cultures of the Spanish-speaking World
description: Develops critical understanding of the historical, political, and social forces that have shaped Spanish literature and culture through interdisciplinary focus on a topic (e.g. cities, music, sport).

title: Cultures of the Spanish-speaking World: Spanish America
description: Develops critical understanding of the historical, political, and social forces that have shaped Spanish-America through interdisciplinary focus on a region or topic (e.g. food identity, journeys, revolutions).

title: Cultures of the Spanish-speaking World: Transatlantic
description: Develops critical understanding of the historical, political, social forces that have connected Spanish and Spanish American literatures and cultures over time through interdisciplinary focus on a topic (e.g., conflict, exploration, social class).

title: Literature of Spanish American
description: Analysis of representative works of Spanish American literature from the pre-Columbian period to the present.  Develops an understanding of historical and cultural context within the framework of Spanish American literary criticism.

title: Introduction to Spanish Translation
description: Introduction to concepts of translation, including aspects of history, theory, and professional ethics.  Practice in bi-directional translation [Spanish-English-Spanish] of a variety of texts in different genres including literature.

title: Introduction to Spanish Linguistics
description: Introduction to the fundamentals of linguistic analysis, including the sound system, word and sentence formation, and semantic and pragmatic meaning.  Formal and usage-based perspectives.  Application of linguistics to language acquisition theory and to pedagogy.

title: Advanced Spanish Grammar
description: Intensive study of the structures of the Spanish Language for communicative and pedagogical purposes.  Comparison/contrast to English structures.

title: Medieval Spanish Literature
description: Texts and contexts in Medieval Spain, from jarchas  to La Celestina .  Focus on the roles of ideologies, schools and genres.  Incorporates manuscripts and oral traditions.  Reading- and writing-intensive class.

title: Early Modern Literature of Spain
description: Contextualized discussion and analysis of literature and cultural life in 15th-17th-century Spain. Course may be focused on a theme, genre, or writer (such as Miguel de Cervantes).

title: Cervantes
description: Reading and discussion of the works of Cervantes and of his literary and cultural background.

title: Environmental Perspectives in Latin America
description: This course explores the representation and protection of climate, nature, and animals in Latin America. Students explore the various perspectives of scientists, authors, and artists as they discuss environmental issues their countries face today. Topics include Agrodiversity, Conservation, Natural and Urban Landscapes, Sustainability and more.

title: 18th and 19th - Century Spain
description: Contextualized discussion and analysis of literature and cultural life in 18th-19th-century Spain. Course may be focused on a theme, genre, or movement (Enlightenment, Romanticism, Realism, Naturalism). Topics may include anticlericalism and Catholicism; revolution and empire; scientific reasoning.

title: 20th- and 21st-Century Spain
description: Contextualized discussion and analysis of contemporary Spanish literature and cultural life. Course may be focused on a theme or genre (e.g., the Generation of '27, the Spanish Civil War, the Franco dictatorship and transition to democracy, feminism, or immigration).

title: Spanish Poetry
description: Texts and contexts of poetry in Spain from the jarchas  of the early Middle Ages through the 21st Century.

title: Spanish American Narrative
description: Contextualized discussion and analysis of literary and cultural narratives over time, focused on a theme or region. Topics may include cultural encounters, illness and disability, ecology, modernity.

title: Spanish/American Narrative:  Early Twentieth Century
description: Contextualized discussion and analysis of early twentieth-century narrative literature.  Includes modernismo , the Avant-Garde, and criollismo/la novela de la tierra.

title: Spanish American Topics
description: Interdisciplinary exploration of a Spanish American political, social, or cultural issue over time. Examples: memory and testimonio, utopia and dystopia, gender and sexuality. Includes literary readings and media.

title: Spanish American Performance Studies
description: Contextualized interdisciplinary approach to performance studies in Spanish America. Discussion and analysis of plays and other performative events (political protests, rituals, installations). May focus on a time period, region, or theme.

title: Spanish-American Poetry
description: Contextualized discussion of Spanish American poetry over time, focused on a theme or region. Examples: musicality and song lyrics, places and journeys, voice and identity, innovation.

title: U.S. Latinx Literature and Culture in Spanish
description: Examines the border as a metaphorical concept in Latinx literature and culture. Analysis of literary texts and media that explore intersecting identities, languages, and counter-traditions that shape the Latinx cultural legacy in the U.S.

title: Spanish Literature Of The Golden Age
Bilingualism and Cognition
description: Examines how bilingualism is defined and measured as well as various acquisition paths to bilingualism, critical periods of development, language processing, maintenance and loss, and linguistic behaviors of bilingual speakers. Throughout the semester, students will examine research conducted in the field of bilingualism and cognition and conduct their own research project, the results of which will be presented in a podcast for the general public and a written report for specialists in the field.

title: Hispanic Cinema
description: Critical analysis of films representing a selected region, period, or theme, within their theoretical, cultural, socio-political and historic contexts.

title: Advanced Spanish Translation
description: Analysis and practice in bi-directional translation [Spanish-English-Spanish] of a variety of texts from a number of genres, including literary texts.  Discussion of professional opportunities for translators and interpreters.  Development of personal portfolio.

title: Spain Today
description: Explores key issues of contemporary Spain including the legacy of the Spanish Civil War. Interdisciplinary focus on a topic, such as regional tensions, economics, politics. Representative literary texts and media serve as a basis for developing a critical view of the region.

title: Spanish American Today
description: Focus on the most significant cultural, social, political, ecological and economic issues affecting Spanish America in recent years.  Representative literary texts and media serve as a basis for developing a critical view of the region.

title: Topics in Spanish Translation/Interpreting
description: Theory, strategies, terminology, and practice in a designated field in SpanishEnglish translation and/or interpreting (fields: healthcare, literary translation, legal, technical and scientific community interpreting).

title: History of the Spanish Language
description: The development of Spanish from Latin to its present-day variation in the Spanish-speaking world.  Influence of and interaction with world cultures and languages across time.

title: Hispanic Cultures and Literatures:  Teaching Strategies
description: An integrated teaching program that develops linguistic, cultural and literary skills through the analysis and interpretation of texts from Spain and Spanish-America.  Emphasis on student-centered cooperative and collaborative learning. Reading- and writing-intensive class.

title: Spanish and Spanish-Amer Cult and Lit in Second Lang/Lrng ll
Selected Topics In Spanish
description: In depth exploration of a special cultural, linguistic and/or literary subject. Reading- and writing-intensive class.

title: Spanish in the US
description: Surveys sociolinguistic features, socio-historical events, and matters pertaining to identity as key elements that shape a growingly popular linguistic variety despite its continuous stigmatization: Spanish in the US.

title: Master's Report
description: A written report of either research or problem work on a topic in the major field.

title: Introduction To The Art Of Film
The Rhetoric Of Black Female Slave Narratives
Analysis Of Experimental Researach Literature In Speech
Tect,Context And Retext: Creating A Performance Event
Methods and Techniques for Learning a Second Language
Communication Problems of the Hearing Impaired
Speech and Hearing Handicapped School Child
Practicum in Public School Speech and Hearing Services
Introduction To Speech Pathology And Audiology
Clinical Procedures In Speech Pathology And Audiology
Fundamentals of Hearing and Acoustic Phonetics
Practicum in Public School Speech and Hearing Services
Undergraduate Topics in Speech-Lang Pathology and Audiology
Clinical Research In Communication Sciences And Disorders
Topics In Communication Sciences And Disorders
Practicum In Public School Speech And Hearing Services
Topics In Speech-Language Pathology Or Audiology
Graduate Seminar In Communication Sciences & Disorders
Electron Microscopy of Aerospace Materials
description: This course introduces students to the theory, fundamental operating principles, specimen preparation techniques, and chemical analysis utilizing scanning electron microscopy (SEM).  Additionally, this course will emphasize these principles and techniques for characterizing the structure of aerospace materials at different length scales.

title: Aerospace Metallurgy and Ceramics
description: The science of metallurgy and ceramics and their manufacture will be the basis of this course and is aimed at providing the necessary fundamentals for understanding aerospace materials.

title: Advanced Composite Materials
description: The fundamentals of polymer-, metal-, and ceramic-matrix composites will be taught in this course and will be applied to solving a variety of aerospace problems.

title: Space Systems Design and Dynamics
description: This course focuses on introducing students to the analysis, architecture, and design of spacecraft including the analysis of orbit and attitude control, navigation systems, physical structures, propulsion systems, communication systems, thermal and electrical power systems. Similarities between spacecraft will be covered in detail and systems aspects of mission profiles will be discussed and analyzed.

title: Space Systems Modeling, Simulation, and Control
description: The course will introduce students to space mission modeling, simulation and control with an emphasis on space missions supporting civil and commercial operations. Topics include orbital mechanics, communications, space environment, spacecraft technology, civil/commercial space systems, model-based systems engineering concepts applied to physical systems, agent-based modeling and discrete event simulation of space systems. Systems dynamics modeling will be applied to physical space systems operating in various microgravity environments.

title: Manufacturing and Materials in Space and on Earth
description: The course will introduce the student to the world of manufacturing on Earth and in space.  The course content includes the history of manufacturing in space, manufacturing spacecraft on Earth (spacecraft, telescopes, satellites, rovers, etc.), manufacturing standards, additive manufacturing, manufacturing construction in space, materials science aboard the International Space Station, challenges of manufacturing in space, circular economics and sustainability and planetary mining, materials and manufacturing.

title: Space Operations and Environment
description: Concentrates on the commercial and military use of space operations. Space operations have two goals: exploration and commercialization of space to benefit humanity as well as space warfare and conflict to defend earth resources and space resources.

title: Statistical Literacy in the Age of Information
description:
Focus will be on the development of an awareness of statistics at the conceptual and interpretative level, in the context of everyday life. Data awareness and quality, sampling, scientific investigation, decision making, and the study of relationships are included. Emphasis will be on the development of critical thinking through in-class experiments and activities, discussions, analysis of real data sets, written reports, and collaborative learning. Computing activities will be included where appropriate; no previous computing experience required.
Intended for majors in non-quantitative fields.

title: Statistics and Data Science: Introductory Seminar for Majors
description: An introduction to the field of statistics and data science. Topics of special interest to undergraduates majoring in statistics and data sciences, including cultural and professional aspects of the field and exploration of careers available to those trained in statistics and data science.

title: Introduction to Statistics
description: A first course in probability and statistics; random sampling; graphical and numeric summaries of qualitative and quantitative data; correlation and least squares regression; probability and random variables; the normal distribution, sampling distributions and the Central Limit Theorem; confidence intervals for one mean and one proportion; sample size estimation; hypothesis tests for one parameter, power and type I and II errors; statistical inference for the difference of two means based on independent samples and matched pairs designs; statistical inference for the difference of two population proportions.

title: Introduction to Statistics Support
description:
Note: Introduction to Statistics Support credits cannot count towards the 120 required credits of a degree program. Students who do not meet any of the KBOR multiple placement measures for elementary statistics must also enroll in STAT 226 as the corequisite course to STAT 225. 

Introduction to Statistics Support is the corequisite course for STAT 225 -Introduction to Statistics. STAT 226 aims to fill in gaps in students’ background knowledge to promote success in STAT 225. The content of STAT 226 is directly aligned with the course objectives and content of STAT 225 but allows for flexibility to fit individual student needs. Topics vary based on student needs but can include simplifying and manipulating numerical and algebraic expressions; and reviewing functions, plots, graphs of functions, exponents, logarithms, and set theory. Students must enroll concurrently in STAT 225 and STAT 226.

This course was designed through a Kansas Board of Regents Math Pathways initiative. STAT 226 is the KBOR math pathways corequisite course for the Introduction to Statistics course.

title: Biometrics I
description: A basic first course in probability and statistics with textbook, examples, and problems aimed toward the biological sciences. Frequency distributions, averages, measures of variation, probability, confidence intervals; tests of significance appropriate to binomial, multinomial, Poisson, and normal sampling; simple regression and correlation.

title: Biometrics I Support
description:
Note: Biometrics I Support credits cannot count towards the 120 required credits of a degree program.  Students that do not meet any of the KBOR multiple placement measures for elementary statistics must also enroll in STAT 241 as the corequisite course to STAT 240.

Biometrics I Support is the corequisite course for STAT 240 – Biometrics I. STAT 241 aims to fill in gaps in students’ background knowledge to promote success in STAT 240. The content of STAT 241 is directly aligned with the course objectives and content of STAT 240 but allows for flexibility to fit individual student needs. Topics vary based on student needs but can include simplifying and manipulating numerical and algebraic expressions; and reviewing functions, plots, graphs of functions, exponents, logarithms, and set theory. Students must enroll concurrently in STAT 240 and STAT 241.

This course was designed through a Kansas Board of Regents Math Pathways initiative. STAT 241 is the KBOR math pathways corequisite course for the Biometrics I course.

title: Business and Economic Statistics I
description: A basic first course in probability and statistics with textbook, examples, and problems pointed toward business administration and economics. Frequency distributions, averages, index numbers, time series, measures of variation, probability, confidence intervals, tests of significance appropriate to binomial, multinomial, Poisson, and normal sampling; simple regression and correlation.

title: Biometrics ll
description: Analysis and interpretation of biological data using analysis of variance, analysis of covariance, and multiple regression. Negative binomial distribution and its applications.

title: Business and Economic Statistics ll
description: Continuation of STAT 350 including study of index numbers, time series, business cycles, seasonal variation, multiple regression and correlation, forecasting; some nonparametric methods applicable in business and economic studies.

title: Probabilistic Systems Modelling
description: Descriptive statistics and graphical methods; basic probability; probability distributions; several random variable; Poisson processes; computer simulation of random phenomena; confidence interval estimation; hypothesis testing.

title: Special Topics for Undergraduates in Statistics and Data Science
description: Study of special topics in statistics and data science methods for undergraduates.

title: Statistics for Engineers l
description: First course in statistics with examples and problems toward engineering. Distributions, means, measures of variation, confidence intervals, graphical display of data, simple regression and correlation, philosophy of experimentation.

title: Statistics for Engineers ll
description: A continuation of STAT 490. Offered second half of the semester following STAT 490. Statistical tests, multiple regression, model fitting, simple comparative and factorial experiments. Emphasis on computer analysis of data.

title: Honors Project
description: Open only to Arts and Science students who are active members of the University Honors Program.

title: Introductory Probability and Statistics l
description: Descriptive statistics, probability concepts and laws, sample spaces; random variables; binomial, uniform, normal, and Poisson; two-dimensional variates; expected values; confidence intervals; binomial parameter, median, mean, and variance; testing simple hypotheses using CIs and X2 ; goodness of fit. Numerous applications.

title: Introductory Probability and Statistics ll
description: Law of Large Numbers, Chebycheff's Inequality; continuation of study of continuous variates; uniform, exponential, gamma, and beta distribution; Central Limit Theorem; distributions from normal sampling; introduction to statistical inference.

title: Introduction to Mathematical Statistics I
description: Development of axiomatic probability; univariate and multivariate random variables and their probability distribution functions; conditional distributions and independent  random variables; methods of transformation for distributions of functions of random variables; convergence in distribution and probability.

title: Fundamental Methods of Biostatistics
description: A course emphasizing concepts and practice of statistical data analysis for the health sciences.  Basic techniques of descriptive and inferential statistical methods applied to health related surveys and designed experiments.  Populations and samples, parameters and statistics; sampling distributions for hypothesis testing and confidence intervals for means and proportions involving one sample, paired samples and multiple independent samples; odds ratios, risk ratios, simple linear regression.  Use of statistical software to facilitate the collection, manipulation, analysis and interpretation of  health related data.

title: Introduction to Statistical Methods for the Sciences
description: Statistical concepts and methods applied to experimental and survey research in the sciences; tests of hypotheses, parametric and rank tests; point estimation and confidence intervals; linear regression; correlation; one-way analysis of variance; contingency tables, chi-square tests.

title: Regression and Analysis of Variance
description: Simple and multiple linear regression, analysis of covariance, correlation analysis, one-, two- and three-way analysis of variance; multiple comparisons; applications including use of computers; blocking and random effects.

title: Basic Elements of Statistical Theory
description: The mathematical representation of frequency distributions, their properties, and the theory of estimation and hypothesis testing. Elementary mathematical functions are used to illustrate theory.

title: Sample Survey Methods
description: Design, conduct, and interpretation of sample surveys.

title: Applied Linear Statistical Models
description: Matrix-based, applied linear regression procedures at a mathematical level appropriate for a first-year graduate statistics major. Topics include basic methodological development for simple and multiple linear regression, model building and diagnostics, analysis of variance/covariance and multiple comparison methods. Other topics from ANOVA modeling may also be covered if time permits.

title: Nonparametric Statistics
description: Hypothesis testing when form of population sampled is unknown: rank, sign, chi-square, and slippage tests; Kolmogorov and Smirnov type tests; confidence intervals and bands.

title: Categorical Data Analysis
description: Analysis of categorical count and proportion data. Topics include tests of association in two-way tables; measures of association; Cochran-Mantel-Haenzel tests for 3-way tables; generalized linear models; logistic regression; loglinear models.

title: Design of Experiments
description: Planning experiments so as to minimize error variance and avoid bias; Latin squares; split-plot designs; switch-back or reversal designs; incomplete block designs; efficiency.

title: Experimental Designs for Product Development and Qual Imprv
description: A study of statistically designed experiments which have proven useful in product development and process improvement. Topics include randomization, blocking, factorial treatment structures, factional factorial designs, screening designs, and response surface methods.

title: Introduction to the SAS Computing
description: Topics may include basic environment and syntax, reading and importing data from files, writing and exporting data to files, data manipulation, basic graphics, and built-in and user-defined functions.

title: Introduction to R Computing
description: Topics may include basic environment and syntax, reading and importing data from files, data manipulation, basic graphics, and built-in and user-defined functions.

title: Statistical Computing/Numerical Methods
description: Topics may include efficient programming techniques, generating data from non-standard distributions, designing simulation studies, resampling methods, creating and using functions and subroutines, parallel computing in statistics, interfacing lower-level languages (e.g., C, C++, Fortran) with data analysis software (e.g., SAS, R), computational complexity, convergence analysis of algorithms.

title: Python for Statistics and Data Science
description: Explores the Python tools for statistical analysis and data science. Topics included but not limited to (1). Python basics (loop, function, object-oriented programming with class, data structure); (2). Getting data with REST APIs (3). Data cleaning and preprocessing, feature engineering, descriptive statistics, and visualization in Python;  (4) Quantiles and sampling from parametric distributions in Python; (5) Hypothesis testing and CI; (6). Regression and classification analysis in Python; (7). Clustering analysis in Python

title: Multivariate Statistical Methods
description: Multivariate analysis of variance and covariance; classification and discrimination; principal components and introductory factor analysis; canonical correlation; digital computing procedures applied to data from natural and social sciences.

title: Bioassay
description: Direct assays; quantitative dose-response models; parallel line assays; slope ratio assays; experimental designs for bioassay; covariance adjustment; weighted estimates; assays based on quantal responses.

title: Statistical Graphics
description: Visual display of quantitative information. Statistical graphics topics to include visual perception, basic graphics construction, quantitative univariate to multivariate statistical graphics, trellis displays, introduction to smoothing and graphics, introduction to density estimation and graphics, and categorical graphics. Modern graphics software will be used.

title: Professional Development Seminar for Intelligence and Learning Practitioners
description: The development of skills for building their professional identity, including creating profiles on professional social networking platforms, writing effective resumes, developing interviewing skills, and building professional portfolios.  Will include panels of alumni and practitioners in the fields of Intelligence and Learning.

title: Studies in Probability and Statistics
description: Studies of topics in probability, statistics, experimental design, stochastic processes, or other topics.

title: Optimization for Data Science
description: The class presents the theory and algorithms for linear and nonlinear optimization problems with continuous variables.  Topics covered include convex analysis, first- and second-order optimality methods, duality, KKT conditions, algorithms for unconstrained optimization, linearly and nonlinearly constrained problems, and convergence rates of algorithms.  The theory and algorithms are applied to data science problems arising in machine learning, statistics, and related fields (e.g., maximizing likelihood and penalized likelihood functions, MM algorithms, EM algorithms, model selection, Sparse PCA). Students are expected to be comfortable with rigorous mathematical arguments.

title: Discrete Optimization and Scalability for Data Science
description: Topics covered include computational complexity, NP-hardness, data as networks, graph theoretic algorithms, exact, approximation, heuristic and online algorithms, and connections between convex and non-convex optimization problems.  The theory and algorithms are applied to data science problems arising in statistical machine learning, statistical clustering, design of experiments, observational studies, sampling, and variable selection.  Applications may be motivated using data from social networks, search engines, the stock market and elections.

title: Spatio-Temporal Statistics
description: Construction and analysis of spatial, time-series, and spatio-temporal data sets.  Topics include data generation using geographic information systems, exploratory data analysis and visualization, and descriptive and dynamic spatio-temporal statistical models.  For context, a focus will be on biological or ecological data.

title: Applied Data Mining/Machine Learning and Predictive Analytics
description: Addresses the complete process of building analytical tools suitable for learning from data, including automatic online data collection, feature extraction, supervised and unsupervised statistical machine learning methods, evaluation, and report writing.  Automatic retrieval of various format online data, including JSON, REST, and Streaming API, http(s), html, xml, and databases.  Statistical text processing/mining, state of the art supervised and unsupervised data mining methods, case studies and applications to business, government,social and news media data.  Methods include regularized linear and logistic regression, classification trees, nearest neighbor methods, support vector machines, naive Bayes, random forests, boosting/bagging/AdaBoost, clustering, latent Dirichlet allocation, network analysis, and topic modeling models.

title: Applied Bayesian Modeling and Prediction
description: Bayes rule, principles of Bayesian inference, Bayesian perspective on statistical models, posterior distribution computations using simulations, Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) (including Gibbs sampling, Metropolis-Hastings algorithm, slice sampler, hybrid forms and alternative algorithms), convergence monitoring and diagnosis, hierarchical models, model checking and model selection, and applications in the sciences using computer software such as R and WinBUGS.

title: Theory of Statistics I
description: Introduction to probability theory, random variables, transformations of random variables, expectations, discrete and continuous distributions, moment generating functions, families of distributions, multivariate random variables, joint and marginal distributions, conditional distributions and independence, sampling distributions of normal populations, order statistics, convergence of random variables, and the central limit theorem.

title: Theory of Statistics II
description: Introduction to the sufficiency principle, the likelihood principle, point estimation, uniformly minimum-variance unbiased estimators, hypothesis testing, uniformly most powerful tests, interval estimation, and asymptotic evaluations of estimators and tests.

title: Statistical and Mathematical Artificial Intelligence and Learning Capstone Experience
description: A research, extension, or industry-based experience that builds upon an individual student’s academic training and personal experience in preparation for a successful career. This course is a hands-on project on solving a practical problem from delimitating the project goals, establishing a data gathering/usage strategy, identifying the analysis strategy, conducting the analysis, providing insights, and effectively communicating the results.

title: Seminar in Probability and Statistics
description: Discussion and lectures on topics in probability and statistics; one seminar talk by each student registered for credit.

title: Theory of Life-Data Analysis
description: A study of models and inferential procedures important to life-data analysis. Comparison of estimators (MLE, BLUE, etc.). Pivotal quantities. Design and regression models for non-normal distributions. Analysis of censored data.

title: Probability for Statistical Inference
description: Probability spaces and random elements, distributions, generating and characteristic functions, conditional expectation, convergence modes and stochastic orders, continuous mapping theorems, central limit theory and accuracy, laws of large numbers, asymptotic expansions for approximating functions of random variables and distributions.

title: Statistical Inference
description: Distributions (commonly used univariate and multivariate distributions, including exponential families of distributions and properties), order statistics and distributional properties, (asymptotic) unbiased estimation and the information inequality, likelihood inference for parametric statistical models (including the multi-parameter case, regular and non-regular cases), confidence sets, functional parameters and statistical functional, density estimation and nonparametric function estimation, permutation methods.

title: Stochastic Processes
description: Normal processes and covariance stationary processes; Poisson processes; renewal counting processes; Markov chains; Brownian motion; applications to science and engineering.

title: Linear Models l
description: Subspaces, projections, and generalized inverses; multivariate normal distribution, distribution of quadratic forms; optimal estimation and hypothesis testing procedures for the general linear model; application to regression models, correlation model.

title: Linear Models ll
description: Continued application of optimal inference procedures for the general linear model to multifactor analysis of variance, experimental design models, analysis of covariance, split-plot models, repeated measures models, mixed models, and variance component models; multiple comparison procedures.

title: Analysis of Messy Data
description: Design structures; treatment structures; equal and unequal variances; multiple comparisons; unequal subclass numbers; missing cells; interpretation of interaction; variance components; mixed models; split-plot and repeated measures; analysis of covariance; cross-over designs.

title: Time Series Analysis l
description: Autocorrelation function; spectral density; autoregressive integrated moving average processes; seasonal time series; transfer function model; intervention analysis; regression model with time series error.

title: Statistical Methods for Spatial Data
description: Theoretical development of modeling and inference techniques for spatially and/or temporally referenced data, including geostatistical data, lattice data and spatial point patterns; methodology for autocorrelation estimation and prediction; nonstationary spatial processes; asymptotics and computational methods for large spatial datasets; computer-aided applications.

title: Resampling Methods
description: Theoretical development of resampling procedures, including parametric and nonparametric bootstrap, jackknife, randomization and permutation methods; expansion theory for bootstrap inference; implementation of resampling procedures via statistical computational tools.

title: High-Dimensional Data and Statistical Learning
description: Theoretical framework of statistical methods for the analysis of large-scale data with the aim of understanding data mining, supervised and unsupervised statistical learning techniques from a statistical decision theoretic framework. Methods for model selection, dimensionality reduction, multiple testing control, and estimation in high-dimension data. Emphasis on the conceptual underpinning of different tools for analysis of large-scale data and how they relate to each other. Applications in various fields, including the sciences and engineering, using computer software.

title: Bayesian Statistical Inference
