description: Principles of Bayesian estimation, testing and prediction; Bayes factors and posterior probabilities of hypotheses; hierarchical modeling; Bayesian model selection and assessment; Bayesian computation and asymptotics; nonparametric Bayesian models.

title: Experimental Design Theory
description: Incomplete block designs; theory of the construction and analysis of experimental designs.

title: Theory of Multivariate Analysis
description: The multivariate normal distribution, the Wishart distribution, Jacobians of vector and matrix transformations, Hotelling's T2statistic, the union-intersection principle, tests on mean vectors and covariance matrices, Box's approximations to critical points, the multivariate general linear model, discriminant analysis, and principal component analysis.

title: Advanced Statistical Methods
description: Generalized linear models and generalized mixed models. Statistical models based on the exponential family of distributions. Applications to non-normal and discrete data, including binary, Poisson and gamma regression, and log-linear models. Topics include likelihood-based estimation and testing, model-fitting, residual analyses, over-dispersed models, quasi-likelihood, large sample properties, and the use of computer packages. Also, methods for longitudinal repeated measures data that will include inference for continuous and discrete data. Inferential objectives include prediction of response and estimation of correlation/covariance structures. Nonparametric and semiparametric methods covered as time permits.

title: Advanced Statistical Inference
description: Foundations and methods of modern statistical inference including asymptotic theory in parametric models (including local asymptotic normality and contiguity), efficiency of estimators and tests, Bayes procedures, rank, sign and permutation statistics, U-,M-, L-, R-estimates, chi-square tests, empirical processes and the functional delta method, quantiles and order statistics, inference for nonparametric and semi-parametric models.

title: Problems in Statistical Consulting
description: Principles and practices of statistical consulting. Supervised experience in consultation and consequent research concerning applied statistics and probability associated with on-campus investigations.

title: Advanced Studies in Probability and Statistics
description: Theoretical studies of advanced topics in probability, decision theory, Markov processes, experimental design, stochastic processes, or advanced topics.

title: Transforming Society
description: Examines a range of social concepts such as race, ethnicity, indigeneity, gender, sex, sexuality, class, disability, citizenship, nation, coloniality, and global location from the perspective of Social Transformation Studies.

title: Practicing Social Justice
description: Students apply their classroom learning?by engaging in a community-based social justice project. Students will work with an organization, program, or creative movement that engages and supports underrepresented and/or historically marginalized communities.

title: Pathways to Social Change
description: An advanced seminar in which students design and launch a research-based social transformation project on a topic and in a form of their choosing.

title: Swahili l
description: Introduction to Swahili. A foundation in listening, speaking, writing, and reading the basic grammatical structures and vocabulary of Swahili. Aspects of Swahili/African culture will be introduced and highlighted as essential components toward achieving communicative competence.

title: Swahili ll
description: A continuation of Swahili 101. A foundation in listening, speaking, writing, and reading the basic grammatical structures and vocabulary of Swahili. Aspects of Swahili/African culture will be introduced and highlighted as essential components toward achieving communicative competence.

title: Swahili III
description: Continuation of Swahili II.  Further development of functional skills.  Intensive practice of spoken and written Swahili.

title: Swahili IV
description: Continuation of Swahili III.  Further development of functional skills.  Intensive practice of spoken and written Swahili.

title: Production Participation
description: Provide students with practical experience when working on a theatre production.  Students must choose and complete one production assignment to earn 1 credit hour. This course is available for both Theatre majors and non-majors.

title: Introduction to Theatre
description: A comprehensive introduction to theatre: basic elements of theatre and theatre production, theatre history, dramatic literature, multicultural theatre traditions and perspectives, and the theatre experience.

title: Introduction to the Art of Film
description: Survey of Cinema, including history, analysis and the process of production.  Will include film screenings outside of regular class meetings.

title: Stage to Screen: Theatre into Film
description: Explores the adaptation of theatrical works into motion pictures.

title: Screams and Scenes: Horror Performance in Film and Theatre
description: Challenges students to actively engage the films, critical readings, and historical materials in an effort to reach a more comprehensive understanding of film and the horror genre.

title: Musical Theatre History
description: Surveys the history of musical theatre.

title: Fundamentals of Acting
description: Theory and practice of fundamental skills and techniques of acting. Major emphasis is on freeing and training the individual's imagination, intellect, body, and voice through designed exercise and performed scenes.

title: Drugs and Alcohol in Theatre and Film
description: Exploration of alcohol and drug use as depicted on stage and in film.

title: Taboo! Transgression and Censorship in Theatre and Film
description: An examination of the cultural controversies surrounding theatrical and cinematic productions.

title: Contemporary American Theatre
description: Investigation of contemporary American work of dramatic literature and Theatre from the 1990s to the present.

title: Run Crew
description: Work on run crew (sets, props, light or sound board) for theatrical production.

title: Wardrobe Crew
description: Work on wardrobe crew for a theatrical production.

title: Stage Movement
description: A study of the technique of stage movement and an investigation of the language of gesture.

title: Oral Interpretation of Literature
description: Techniques of reading from the printed page, selecting portions from various forms of literature, including narrative poetry, essay, lyric, sonnet, nonfictional prose, scenes from plays, and selected short stories.

title: Fundamentals of Improvisation
description: Introduction to the techniques of improvisation with the emphasis upon practical participation.

title: Techniques of Makeup
description: Techniques of makeup for the stage.

title: Intermediate Acting
description: Emphasis upon expanding the actor's capabilities through more advanced scene work and character study.

title: Script Analysis
description: Examination of the play script to develop a common vocabulary and method for analyzing a script.

title: Vocal Expression for Actors
description: Study and application of vocal techniques for stage productions; emphasis on development of the actor's vocal mechanism.

title: Dramatic Comedy And The Theory Of Laughter
Storytelling
description: Techniques of storytelling to people of all ages. History and cultural importance of storytelling. Fictional and personal storytelling will be covered.

title: Seminar On Laughter: Dramatic Comedy & The Psy Of Humor
Intergrating Cultural Diversity with Stories and Style
Scene Painting
description: Practical application of painting techniques for the stage.

title: Fundamentals of Theatrical Design
description: An exploration of the four areas of stage design: sets, lights, costumes, and sound. Includes examination of relevant history and technology in these areas. Emphasis is on the design process and design development.

title: Principles of Directing
description: Principles, processes and techniques of directing for the theatre.

title: Theatre History: Origins to the Renaissance
description: A survey of the development of the theatre from ancient times to the Renaissance.

title: Theatre History: 19th Century to the Present
description: A survey of the development of the theatre from the 19th century to the present.

title: Opera Workshop
description: Principles and techniques of operatic and musical theatre production, with emphasis on class rehearsal and performance of selected scenes from opera and musical drama; brief survey of the history of opera.

title: Techniques of Writing for the Film
Theatre for Young Audiences (TYA) in Production
description: Students will gain insight into the philosophies, practicalities, and processes of developing and producing Theatre for Young Audiences (TYA).

title: Stage Combat
description: Focuses on specific stage combat disciplines and techniques.

title: Sound Design
description: Introduces the student to the fundamental processes involved in Sound Design.

title: Advanced Stage Movement
description: Study in the physical development of character and advanced techniques of stage movement.

title: Stage Management
description: Theory and practice of stage management in the professional and nonprofessional theatre. Emphasis is on the organization of all areas of theatre knowledge needed for the running of theatrical productions.

title: Rehearsal Techniques
description: A laboratory course for students enrolled in performance and production classes.

title: Scene Design
description: Examination of the role of scene design in theatre, principles and techniques of design. Development, presentation, and synthesis of design images with the scripted play.

title: Advanced Technical Production
description: Independent study in advanced technical theatre problems.

title: Dramaturgy
description: An introduction to the theory and practice of dramaturgy, including a survey of contemporary critical theories as they apply to the work of the dramaturg.

title: Lighting Design
description: Theory and practice of theatrical lighting design, control systems, projection equipment, and lighting consulting. Production work with KSU Theatre season required.

title: Music Theatre Workshop
description: Principles and techniques of musical theatre production with emphasis on rehearsal and performance of selected scenes from musical theatre. Dance, music, and theatre are studied as integrated elements within the musical theatre genre. Culminates in a public performance.

title: Internship in Theatre
description: Supervised participation in an internship at a professional theatre.

title: Professional Development
description: Study of audition techniques including supervised preparation of appropriate material. Business aspects of professional theatre, including unions, contracts, and professional ethics.

title: Playwriting
description: Theoretical study and practical application of techniques of playwriting with regard to plot, characters, and production; emphasis on the one-act form.

title: Solo Performance
description: Students create their own solo performance piece through developing and rehearsing a script. Students will create drafts, conduct rehearsals, and culminate with a final performance of their own work.

title: Creative Drama
description: The development of creative imagination and personal well-being through theatre games, improvisation, storytelling, and puppetry for use in educational and recreational settings.

title: Drama Therapy With Special Populations
description: The therapeutic uses of drama in the development of creative imagination, self expression, and social relatedness with special populations such as clients who have developmental disabilities, physical disabilities, health issues, or are aging.

title: Period Styles for the Theatre l
description: Survey of historical styles of architecture, furnishings, and clothing in relation to theatrical design and the history of the theatre from the Greeks to 1800.

title: Period Styles for the Theatre ll
description: Survey of historical styles of architecture, furnishings, and clothing in relation to theatrical design and the history of the theatre from 1800 to present.

title: Playback Theatre
description: Improvisational theatre form in which actors listen to stories told by audience and improvisationally act the story out. Students will learn playback acting forms and how to conduct a session. Playback is often used as a technique by drama therapists in performance and in therapy sessions.

title: American Ethnic Theatre
description: Drama and stagecraft of ethnic groups in the United States, including the theatre of African, Asian, Hispanic, Jewish, and Native Americans.

title: Theatre For Conflict Resolution
description: Drama and theatre techniques used to explore the nature of conflict and how to promote collaboration through action.

title: Drama Therapy With Adolescents
description: The therapeutic uses of drama with adolescents, including normal development, youth-at-risk, ESL, SED, and BD adolescents.

title: Drama Therapy With Older Adults
description: The therapeutic uses of drama with older adults, focusing on the development of life review, social relatedness, and creative self-expression in the here and now.

title: Practicum in Theatre
description: Supervised participation in a position of major responsibility.

title: Topics In Technical Theatre
description: Selected topics in creative techniques and investigation for technical theatre.

title: Theatre Management
description: Theatre management, promotion, finance, organization; emphasis on contract negotiations and use of facilities.

title: Advanced Acting
description: This acting class will examine classic plays (Greek Shakespeare, Moliere, Ibsen etc.) and reinvent scenes to better address contemporary themes towards diversity, inclusion, and equity.

title: Advanced Playwriting
description: Further study in the writing of drama; emphasis on problems of writing the full-length play.

title: Advanced Acting: Consent and Intimacy in Performance
description: An in-depth examination and practice in consent and intimacy in performance. Work will cover the language, process and best practices of Intimacy Direction and Coordination.

title: Practice in Directing
description: A lecture-lab course with emphasis on directing dramatic productions under performance conditions.

title: Theatrical Design Studio
description: Advanced problems in conceptualization and realization of design, including sets, costumes, lights and technical production. Emphasis on advanced techniques in research, analysis, and production problems.

title: Women In Theatre
description: A history of the contributions made by women in theatre as playwrights, managers, directors, and performers; contemporary women in theatre and their experiments in expressing women's consciousness.

title: Practice in Acting
description: Advanced studies in characterization with emphasis on communicating with the director. Taught in conjunction with the Practice in Directing workshop.

title: Psychodrama
description: Theory and practice of psychodrama as a treatment modality for use in drama therapy.

title: Sociodrama
description: Theory and practice of sociodrama as a therapeutic and educational modality for use in drama therapy.

title: Projects in Theatre
description: Individual guided work in selected area.

title: Introduction to Graduate Studies in Theatre: Library, Research and Information Skills
description: Skills for accessing research tools and resources needed in the age of the internet, along with instruction on citations, literature reviews, and other formatting necessary for graduate research papers.

title: Survey of Research in Drama Therapy
description: Basic terminology, concepts, and principles underlying research in drama therapy and the helping fields.

title: Principles of Drama Therapy
description: Theory, history, and practice in the use of drama as therapy group treatment. Survey of major drama therapy methods.

title: Creative Arts Therapies
description: Survey of five creative arts therapy modalities: drama/ psychodrama, art, poetry/bibliotherapy, music, and dance/movement. Instruction in theory and hands-on experience with each modality, as well as reading, discussion and methods for using modalities interdisciplinarily.

title: Play Therapy Practices and Techniques
description: The history and philosophy of, and rationale for, using play media in counseling and therapy with children and adults.  Directive Play Therapy models will be explored and practiced.

title: Ethics and Professionalism in Drama Therapy
description: The application of ethical principles involved in the practice of drama therapy in a variety of educational and therapeutic settings.

title: Greek and Roman Theatre
description: Studies in the drama and stagecraft of the Greek and Roman period.

title: Medieval and Baroque Theatre
description: Studies in the drama and stagecraft of the Medieval and Baroque periods.

title: Contemporary Theatre
description: Studies in drama and stagecraft since 1968.

title: History of the Physical Stage
description: A survey course in the emergence and development of the theatre building as a distinct architectural form, with particular emphasis on the effect of the physical environment on the play.

title: Modern Theatre
description: Studies in the drama and stagecraft of Europe and America in the period from 1870 to the present.

title: Master's Project
description: Major project undertaken to demonstrate competence in Drama Therapy as required for the Master's Degree.

title: Remote Pilot Certification for UAS
description: This course helps prepare students to become an FAA-Certified UAS Pilot. This course covers all knowledge topics important for a commercial UAS pilot. Once complete, the student takes the FAA's Remote Pilot in Command knowledge test.

title: Introduction to Multi-Rotor Flight Lab
description: Basic through advanced flight training on multi-rotor uncrewed aircraft. This course establishes the foundation of manual flight, basic automation and features. Proficiency is built through standarized manuevers to meet practical test standards.

title: Introduction to UAS
description: Introduction to the history of UAS and survey of current UAS platforms, terminology, challenges to airspace integration and operational theory.

title: UAS Safety Fundamentals
description: Introduction to aviation safety, with an emphasis on best practices for safe operation of unmanned aircraft systems. Topics include safety/risk assessments, human factors, crew resource management, aeronautical decision-making, risk management, and safety assurance.

title: Small UAS Maintenance I
description: This course provides students with the knowledge and skill necessary to repair and maintain both fixed- and rotary-wing aircraft during field operations and to ensure continued airworthiness throughout the service life of the aircraft. Instruction emphasizes safe practices, provide an introduction to basic shop tools and machinery used in maintaining sUAS, and develop fundamental skills in platform fabrication and the troubleshooting/repair of the circuits, subsystems and components typically found on sUAS aircraft.

title: Multi-Rotor Construction Lab
description: This course focuses on the construction of a multi-rotor UAS. This includes gaining an understanding of individual components within the system and integrating the components into a fully functional system. Students will also test and tune the UAS once assembled.

title: Small Unmanned Aircraft Maintenance II
description: This course provides students with advanced knowledge and a high level of skill in the maintenance and repair of both fixed- and rotary-wing unmanned aircraft and associated systems. The concept of continued airworthiness is emphasized. Topics include advanced techniques in the fabrication and repair of small unmanned aircraft systems and airframes, maintenance of ground support systems and principles of electronic and integrated circuit maintenance, troubleshooting and repair.

title: UAS Powerplant Fundamentals
description: A study of the principles of operation, design features, and operating characteristics of various powerplants used in UAS. Includes inspection procedures and operational theory of current electric, piston, hybrid, and turbine propulsion systems.

title: UAS Flight Instructor Ground School
description: Intended to prepare the student for the role of UAS flight instructor. Focuses on the fundamentals of flight instruction. Lecture topics cover the techniques and the procedures necessary to generate, organize and present lessons in instructional environment while building necessary skills and emphasizing aspects of instruction necessary to ensure student competencies in the areas of UAS field and flight operations.

title: Multi-rotor Instructor Flight Lab
description: This course refines advanced multi-rotor skills and provides the practical experience necessary to produce competent multi-rotor flight instructors.

title: Command and Control Links and Circuitry
description: This course provides a detailed examination of the theory and design of UAS command and control (2C) systems and applies this knowledge to develop a thorough understanding of the principles used in the design, maintenance and repair of 2C circuits and subsystem components. Topics include design choices in component selection, circuit and overall system design concepts and troubleshooting.

title: Introduction to UAS Fixed-Wing Flight Lab
description: Provides an introduction to the use of an external pilot (EP) console to control fixed-wing UAS. In the event of a lost communications link, avionics or autopilot failure or other emergency, the external pilot is critical to human-in-the-loop operation of the UAS in effecting a successful recovery of the aircraft. This course develops the skills necessary to ensure students are capable of conducting safe EP operation of fixed-wing UAS during adverse flight conditions.

title: Introduction to UAS Automated Fixed-Wing Flight Lab
description: This course focuses on developing the pilot's ability to control the UAS via a computer ground control station in the crew role of air vehicle operator (AVO). Students will become proficient in conducting site surveys, building UAS flight plans, contingency planning, emergency procedures, crew resource management, and developing mission briefings. Students will also learn methods for effectively instructing fixed-wing flight operations by developing lesson plans and conducting instructional flights.

title: Small Unmanned Aircraft Systems Design and Construction
Small Unmanned Aircraft Design and Construction for Non-Aviators
description: Topics include: Unmanned Aircraft System platform, payload and component design and interfacing, system and vehicle maintenance, and systems integration. Intended for non-pilot majors and those without the FAA certifications and ratings required for UAS 370.

title: Processing Remotely Sensed Data
description: Students are introduced to basic theory, history, and practical applications of remote sensing technology, with an emphasis on high spatial resolution multispectral aerial imagery collected using UAS. Other topics include geographic information systems, aerial image interpretation, sensor resolution, orthomosaicing, georegistration, vegetation indices, and image classification.

title: UAS Safety Applications
description: This course provides students with the knowledge needed to best prepare for and conduct advanced UAS operations while staying safe. This course will cover Crew Resource Management, Human Factors, Saftey Risk Mitigation strategies, and case study investigations.

title: Fixed-wing Instructor Flight Lab
description: This is the culminating course intended to prepare students for roles as fixed-wing unmanned aircraft flight instructors. This course furthers develop and refine the flying skills necessary to assume control of the aircraft during adverse circumstances or unusual aircraft attitudes induced by a less experienced pilot. The fixed-wing instructor-in-training is required to demonstrate, in practice, the skills and knowledge obtained in earlier courses covering crew resource management and flight instruction as well as achievement of a high level of piloting skills. Upon successful completion of this course, the student is qualified as a K-State instructor on fixed-wing platforms and as pilot-in-command/operator-in-command during research missions authorized by the unmanned aircraft systems program.

title: Autonomous Flight Simulation Lab
description: This course provides the student with simulation on high-end, sophisticated, proprietary autopilot systems in preparation for field operations flying aircraft equipped with similar autopilot/avionics packages.

title: Introduction to Autopilots and Mission Planning
description: Focus is on mission planning and operations within the context of the simulated environment. Students are introduced to commercial, open source and/or proprietary autopilots and are required to complete simulated training missions with the ultimate goal of demonstrating skills competency and knowledge acquisition through evaluations of proficiency (e.g., through ""check-outs"" or ""check-rides""). Students integrate autopilots into selected fixed-wing platforms. Intended for non-UAS majors and those without the FAA certifications and ratings required for UAS 370, UAS 461 and UAS 465.

title: Fixed-Wing Construction Lab and Autopilot Integration
description: In this course, students will build a fixed-wing UAS. After completing the build and conducting a successful flight, students will integrate an autopilot into their platform. Using open source ground station software and commercial-off-the-shelf (COTS), open source autopilot firmware and hardware, students learn techniques for integrating autopilots into their selected fixed-wing platform and for exploring precision autonomous flight in preparation for conducting field operations to acquire remotely sensed data.

title: Small Unmanned Aircraft Systems Payloads
description: Course content covers various types of sUAS sensor packages and appropriate applications, preparing the student to properly select sensors based on mission profile, data needs and mission objectives. Students develop, design and construct a payload for integration into previously constructed aircraft in preparation for conducting missions to obtain data for post-flight processing.

title: Flight and Field Operations
description: Emphasis is on advanced unmanned aircraft systems operations in the live flight environment with a focus on safety and crew resource management. Emphasis is on experiential learning. Students are required to travel to an area designated for flight operations.

title: Advanced Fixed-Wing Operations Flight Lab
description: This course provides students with training on a high-end sophisticated, proprietary autopilot system. Course delivery will focus on safety and crew resource management. Emphasis is on experiential learning.

title: Data Acquisition and Post-processing
description: Students build upon the basic image processing skills gained in the previous course, expanding their knowledge of common aerial image data processing tasks using industry-standard software packages. Aerial data collection methodologies are introduced, including consideration of aerial mission flight parameters.

title: Low Altitude Remote Sensing Project Development
description: Students gain practical experience collecting and processing their own high resolution aerial datasets using multirotor and/or fixed-wing UAS, and are instructed in proven workflows for successful commercial UAS mapping operations. The topic of payload selection and integration will enable a project to collect data for subsequent processing.

title: Fundamentals of Thermography
description: Students learn the process of acquiring and interpreting radiometrically calibrated thermal imagery, including real-time analysis and ortho mosaicking. Specific topics include history of thermal sensors, best practices for calibration and mission planning, and imagery post-processing methods, analysis, and deliverables. This course will prepare students to enroll in a Level 1 Thermography Certification course.

title: Fundamentals of LIDAR
description: Students learn how Light Detection and Ranging (LiDAR) systems are used in an sUAS setting and demonstrate their ability to collect, process, and analyze LiDAR data in various class, group, and individual projects. Specific topics include sUAS LiDAR integration, Global Positioning Systems (GPS), mission planning, post-processing methods and software, and final product deliverables.

title: Data Analytics for Agricultural Applications
description: Students are introduced to basic theory and use cases of utilizing remotely sensed data in agricultural applications utilizing UAS, fixed-winged aircraft, and satellite-based imagery. Students will utilize UAS-based sensors to collect imagery that will be processed and analyzed in industry-leading software. General topics include RGB and multispectral data collection, processing and analysis, vegetation indices in crop and field health and condition monitoring, yield estimation, terrain analysis, and prescription generation for multiple precision ag platforms.

title: UAS Senior Design Project I
description: Culminating experience is a sustained occasion when students will put into independent practice the intellectual, creative, and expressive skills/knowledge they have cultivated in their undergraduate curriculum. In this course students integrate discipline-specific knowledge in addition to communication and inquiry methods to solve problems and communicate mastery of their respective area of interest.

title: UAS Senior Design Project II
description: A continuation of UAS 480. Includes the implementation, testing, and delivery of the project initiated in UAS 480 UAS Senior Design I. Significant milestones are the completion of checkpoints outlined in the project proposal prepared in UAS 480 along with implementation, final report and final presentation.

title: UAS Aerial Applications and Regulations
description: The UAS Aerial Application and Regulations course equips participants with the knowledge and skills to conduct safe, effective, and compliant agricultural spraying operations using uncrewed aircraft systems (UAS). The course emphasizes the regulatory framework governing aerial application, including federal, state, and local requirements. Core topics include operational planning, airspace integration, safety and risk management, emergency procedures, multi-aircraft coordination, and the use of precision agriculture tools such as multispectral data. By the end of the course, participants will be prepared to navigate both the technical and regulatory environments necessary for professional UAS spraying operations.

title: Topics in Urban Food Systems
description: Selected topics in urban food systems.

title: Urban Agriculture
description: Students will become familiar with a wide variety of urban agriculture types and production systems utilized in urban settings. The course will include background readings, case studies, guest speakers, student-facilitated class discussions, and lectures.

title: Urban Food  Systems
description: This course will cover all components of urban food systems through the lens of food security, food justice, access, policy, and community planning. Students will gain skills in grant-writing, non-profit planning and management, and working with urban policy and planning boards.

title: Professional Development in Urban Food Systems
description: A team instructed seminar course that provides urban food systems students with a suite of skills complimentary to their research experience but still necessary for professional career success.

title: Urban Food Systems Field Experience
description: Students will engage in a work experience at Willow Lake Student Farm, Olathe Horticulture Research and Extension Center or another approved site. Three hours of field work per week are required.

title: Honors First-Year Seminar
description: Selected topics seminar for new members of the University Honors Program. Focal topics will vary by section, but all seminars will overview student requirements and consider potential benefits and opportunities associated with the program.

title: Grand Challenges: Exploring the Problem
description: Interdisciplinary examination of a societal problem through multiple academic disciplines and community-engaged learning. Focus on developing skills and strategies necessary for authentic transdisciplinary interventions at a community level. Pre-requisites: UHP 189

title: Engaging with Grand Challenges
description: Students define, develop, and implement a plan to address a community problem in collaboration with partnering organizations. Emphasizes integration and application of knowledge from multiple disciplines, ethical engagement and skill development.

title: University-Level Research Mentorship
description: URES 200 is a mentored research opportunity for concurrently enrolled students. Once accepted into the course, concurrently enrolled (typically) high school students will be paired with K-State faculty members to engage in a year-long research experience. Students will develop a research question, keep a research log, create a literature review, and present their research at the annual K-State Research Symposium.

title: Onboarding in a VIP Research Team
description: An introduction to a long-term, transdisciplinary, vertically-integrated project (VIP) team led by a faculty member(s). VIP Teams are active research projects guided by faculty expertise. Students are required to commit to at least two semesters on the same VIP team.

title: Intermediate VIP Research Team
description: Continuing team members or students who already have an intermediate-level knowledge on a long-term, transdisciplinary, vertically-integrated project (VIP) team led by a faculty member(s). VIP Teams are active research projects guided by faculty expertise. Students are required to commit to at least two semesters on the same VIP team.

title: Leadership in a VIP Research Team
description: Advanced team members on a long-term, transdisciplinary, vertically-integrated project (VIP) team led by a faculty member(s). VIP Teams are active research projects guided by faculty expertise. Students are required to commit to at least two semesters on the same VIP team.

title: Basic and Applied Pharmacokinetics
description: Provides a conceptual and quantitative understanding of the absorption, distribution, metabolism, and excretion of drugs and factors that influence these processes.

title: Veterinary Physiology I
description: Function of the animal body at the cellular level, including nerve and muscle function. Basic pathophysiological mechanisms and intermediary metabolism will be emphasized and correlated with clinical topics.

title: Cell Physiology
description: Functions of the animal body are described with a primary focus at the cellular and molecular level with correlations to clinical topics. Course content builds on knowledge of chemistry, biochemistry, physics and biology. Basic physiological and pathophysiological mechanisms are examined in the context of the cell environment, intermediary metabolism and cell signaling.

title: Gross Anatomy I
description: Gross dissection of the dog with comparative aspects of the cat.

title: Veterinary Systems 1
description: Microstructure and function of the cardiovascular, endocrine, respiratory, and renal systems of domestic animals with emphasis on physiologic control mechanisms, interrelationships of body systems, and criteria for evaluating animal health.

title: Introduction to Veterinary Medicine and Biomedical Research: Concepts and Values
description: This course is intended to provide first-year students an opportunity to experience the depth of veterinary/biomedical research required to advance the practice of veterinary medicine, and advance the field of medicine in general.  The students will examine the challenges of conducting 1) foundational and translation research (genetic/genomic and mechanistic research), 2) foundational and applications to clinical research 3) population and observational research (epidemiology, prospective clinical trials, retrospective studies), 4) pharmacologic research (the impact of pharmacology on the biomedical sciences).  The students will examine relevant connections, consider conceptual relationships, and critically evaluate integrative issues germane to both the practice of and the innovative progress of veterinary medicine.  Faculty members in the Departments of A&P, DM/P, and CS, will present topical overviews, based on their professional experiences. This will be designed to connect and integrate fundamental principles and relationships between research and clinical application.  These presentations provide examples of the fundamental requirements for clinical therapies and also provide examples of how innovative science leads to new therapies for long standing clinical disease.  Additionally, students will learn the tools required to search relevant topic to their career paths within the medical literature.  Finally, drawing on the multidisciplinary literature presented by faculty, students will explore the challenges in conducting genetic/genomic research, using genomic information, implementing research results to clinical practice, as well as addressing the social, legal, and professional policies that are emerging from new research. Students will be expected to do reading and to complete online worksheets and present their findings/opinions in class. Class time will be devoted to presentations and to discussion of readings and related current events.

title: Microscopic Anatomy I
description: Origin, development, and microscopic structure and appearance of the cells and tissues of the animal body.

title: Gross Anatomy II
description: Gross dissection of the horse and ruminant with comparative aspects of the pig, laboratory animals, and the chicken.

title: Veterinary Systems 2
description: Microstructure and function of the reproductive and digestive systems of domestic animals with emphasis on physiologic control mechanisms, interrelationships of body systems, and criteria for evaluating animal health.

title: Veterinary Physiology II
description: Function of the cardiovascular, endocrine, respiratory, renal, digestive, and reproductive systems of domestic animals with emphasis on physiologic control mechanisms, interrelationships of body systems, and criteria for evaluating animal health.

title: Animal Care and Welfare
description: This course will describe the veterinarian's role in protecting the health and welfare of animals. Students will be introduced to the major philosophical positions of animal use. The evolution of animal welfare science will be discussed and students will learn how to assess the welfare of companion, laboratory, zoo and food animals. Common causes of reduced animal welfare and ethical dilemmas will be covered.

title: Veterinary Pharmacology I
description: The basic principles of pharmacology, the interactions of drugs and living systems including fundamental principles of pharmacokinetics, pharmacodynamics, receptor-coupling, and mechanisms of action. A systems-based approach to drug classes will be emphasized.

title: Veterinary Pharmacology II
description: The basic principles of pharmacology, the relationship of drug actions and interactions on physiological principles and pathophysiological conditions. Pharmacokinetics, pharmacodynamics, and a systems-based approach to drug classes will be emphasized.

title: Toxicology
description: Effects of harmful substances on the animal body. Emphasis placed on toxicologic principles and management of the poisoned patient.

title: AP Veterinary Medicine Electrive
description: Designed primarily for veterinary students in the first, second or third year of the DVM curriculum. The course is intended to teach literature search skills, organization of your own literature library, and critical evaluation of clinical reports and research literature. The format is lecture as well as small group and online discussion. Students are required to contribute to classroom and online discussions and to complete written assignments.

title: A&P Veterinary Medicine Elective Graded
description: Special topics for veterinary students in the disciplines offered by the department.

title: Introduction to Nanomedicine
description: This course is a brief introduction to nanomaterials and nanomedicine with a focus on clinical applications of nanotechnology. The course presents basic principles of nanotechnology; current clinical applications of nanomedicine; future horizons in nanomedicine.

title: Comparative Embryology
description: An overview of development anatomy as it relates to clinically important developmental defects and/or normal structure and function of common domestic animals.

title: Problems in Animal Welfare
description: This course will expand on the role of veterinarians in the field of animal welfare. Students will be introduced to the major philosophical positions of animal use. The evolution of animal welfare science will be discussed and students will learn how to assess the welfare of companion, laboratory, zoo and food animals. Contemporary issues in animal welfare will also be explored.

title: Advanced Clinical Anatomy and Teaching Practicum
description: This 2-week clinical anatomy rotation elective is designed for DVM students seeking advanced knowledge and teaching experience in veterinary anatomy. Participants will enhance their understanding of regional and systemic anatomy through hands-on dissection, journal club presentations, and the development of clinical teaching materials. This course is especially beneficial for those with interests in surgical specialties, radiology, neurology, and emergency medicine.

title: Veterinary Clinical and Professional Skills I
description: The course will be taught during the fall semester as a required introductory level course for first year veterinary students. This is the first in a series of foundational courses designed to introduce students to key clinical skills, including communication and critical thinking, that they will encounter in a clinical setting and to provide students with a problem-based approach to learning.

title: Veterinary Clinical Skills and Professional Skills II
description: The course will be taught during the spring semester as a required introductory level course for first year veterinary students. The primary objective of the course is to provide students with an opportunity to learn normal animal behavior for the most commonly evaluated large animal species in clinical practice (the horse and the cow); to safely handle and restrain them; and to practice basic physical examination skills. A secondary objective of the course is to introduce students to the basic principles of biosecurity, sterile technique, venipuncture, and intravenous catheterization. In addition, students will build onto first-semester instruction in clinical communication, written documentation within the medical record, and introductory surgical skills. The course will include introductory lectures as well as structured and self-directed laboratory sessions that will involve both live animals and non-animal teaching models.

title: Incident Response
description: This introductory course provides students with the training and credentials needed to volunteer as a disaster responder.  In-person lecture classes will introduce students to disaster response as it relates to animals and the role of veterinarians on a local and state level.  Online modules within the Incident Command Systems (ICS) and National Field Response (NFR) will expose veterinary students to the veterinarian's role in ICS and the multiple agencies involved with disaster response on a national level.  Successful completion of the online modules aligns with training required to become certified for disaster response deployment.

title: Veterinary Medicine in a Foreign Context
description: Lecture and discussion-based course preparing students to enroll in CS 821.  Students will investigate agricultural and veterinary medicine in a specific country.

title: Topics in Global Veterinary Medicine
description: An overview of the role of veterinarians around the world.  Topics include international uses and attitudes about animals, problems the world faces pertaining to livestock production, and social tensions.  One hour lecture each week.

title: International Vet Study Tours
description: Faculty-led trip for students to explore veterinary medicine in a foreign country. Prior to travel, students will learn about the country through lectures, research, presentations. During the trip they will interact with nationals in the veterinary profession and experience the culture.

title: Introduction to Sustainable Beef Systems
description: This course provides the fundamentals of assessing sustainability in beef production systems through evaluation of aspects of sustainability pillars, people, planet and profit, in each sector of the beef value chain. The course focuses on principles of sustainable food systems, beef cattle production practices, systems thinking, and impacts of food choices.

title: Antimicrobial Resistance in Bacteria: A One Health Perspective
description: This course addresses the concept of One Health which integrates the knowledge of the biological elements necessary for the evolution of antimicrobial resistance (AMR) in bacteria and other factors (animals, humans, environments, socioeconomic features, and behavioral changes) involved in its emergence and dissemination.

title: Veterinary Clinical and Professional Skills III
description: A continuation of Veterinary Clinical and Professional Skills II. Introduces how to perform common diagnostic tests in clinical practice and interpret them. Builds upon basic palpation skills to practice isolating specific tissues and organs. Applies surgical principles to the practice of advanced suture patterns.

title: Communication with the Agricultural Worker
description: An elective course focused on improving communications between veterinarians and animal caretakers in production agriculture and equine industries. The course will use face to face discussions and online seminars in KSOL to teach communications skills with a focus on common terminology and vocabulary necessary for effective communication. Audio-visual teaching utilizing immersion philosophy is utilized to provide the material course work.

title: Introduction to Veterinary Surgery
description: The purpose of VCS 843 Introduction to Veterinary Surgery is to introduce the second year veterinary student to small animal surgical principles, specific small animal surgical conditions, their clinical signs, appropriate diagnostic evaluation, surgical treatment, pre- and post- operative care, and complications.  Clinical application and reasoning will also be a focus.  This course will establish a foundation of knowledge that students will build on in VCS 863 Veterinary Surgery I in the fall semester of the third year.

title: Medicine I
description: Consideration of medical and pathophysiologic aspects of diseases affecting the urinary, endocrine, integumentary, respiratory, hemic, and neuromuscular systems.

title: Radiology
description: The principles of x-ray production, biologic effects, and safety; formation and interpretation of diagnostic radiographs, emphasizing normal anatomy and small animal and equine clinical radiology.

title: Veterinary Clinical and Professionals Skills IV
description: A continuation of Veterinary Clinical and Professional Skills III. Introduces advanced organ-specific examination techniques including otoscopy and ophthalmoscopy. Expands the concepts of diagnostic testing and diagnostic test interpretation. Prepares students for surgical anesthesia and anesthetic monitoring through the use of non-animal teaching models.

title: Food Animal Medicine
description: A study of the etiology clinical signs, diagnosis, treatment and control of diseases which affect cattle, swine, and sheep.

title: Theriogenology
description: Consideration of prevention, diagnosis, and treatment of disease, and maintenance of health and productivity of the genital tract of domestic animals.  Three hours of lecture a week.

title: Veterinary Surgery I
description: Veterinary Surgery I is a continuation of the second-year course VCS 843, Surgical Principles.  In Veterinary Surgery I, students will be introduced to small animal anesthesia principles which will be the basis for the hands-on anesthesia experiences in the accompanying laboratory course, VCS 864, as well as for the 4th year small animal anesthesia clinical rotation.  Students will also be introduced to small animal orthopedic and surgical neurologic conditions with emphasis on appropriate clinical reasoning through diagnostic evaluation, surgical treatment, pre- and post-operative care, and complications.

title: Applied Clinical Skills and Surgical Laboratories
description: The VCS 864 Applied Clinical Skills and Surgical Laboratories course is a hands-on live animal laboratory course with multiple objectives.  This course teaches basic clinical skills including small animal handling, physical examination, sample collection, diagnostic procedures, patient assessment and management, medical records, and clinical communication.  Additionally, the course will teach basics of small animal veterinary anesthesia, surgical techniques, and live animal patient management.  Skills taught in this course will build on introductory skills learned in 1st and 2nd year clinical skills courses.  The student will progress to performing general anesthesia, surgery (spays and neuters), and perioperative care for humane shelter animals.  The skills taught in this course will be used as a framework for students to continue building on during their senior year as they learn to function as effective veterinarians.

title: Exotic Pet Medicine
description: Entry-level knowledge base for the species likely to be encountered in a private practice setting including snakes/small reptiles, caged birds, and small mammals (ferrets, gerbils, hamsters, chinchillas).

title: 3rd Year Clinical Skills I
description: Demonstration, instruction and experience in physical examination and diagnostic skills necessary for routine veterinary practice.

title: Medicine II
description: Consideration of the medical and pathophysiological aspects of diseases affecting the gastrointestinal, cardiovascular, and ophthalmic systems, oncology, and dentistry.

title: Artificial Intelligence in Veterinary Medicine
description: This elective course explores the impact of artificial intelligence on the field of medicine, with a focus on veterinary applications. Students will learn about the fundamentals of AI, machine learning, and data science, and how these technologies are used to improve diagnostics, treatment planning, patient monitoring, and overall patient care. The course will also address ethical considerations, regulatory frameworks, and the future of AI in healthcare. Through lectures, case studies, and hands-on projects, students will gain a comprehensive understanding of AI's role in advancing medical and veterinary practices.

title: Equine Diagnostic Imaging
description: This course is offered to students who have an interest and career focus in equine medicine, surgery and emergency care. This course will build on the basic knowledge including imaging of the axial and appendicular skeleton, the thorax and abdomen. Whilst not the primary focus of the elective course, there will be some introduction to additional, complementary diagnostic imaging modalities used to work up the equine patient including ultrasound, magnetic resonance imaging and computed tomography.

title: Ethics and Jurisprudence
description: Socratic ethics are discussed along with the American Veterinary Medical Association's Code of Ethics and practical situations with a fundamental ethical basis. The Kansas Practice Act is explored as an example of governance in veterinary medicine. The role of animals in humans' well being is addressed along with the philosophy of animal welfare. The law and the practicing veterinarian are discussed with emphasis upon professional liability.

title: Applied Large Animal Clinical Skills
description: Demonstration, instruction and experience in physical examination and diagnostic skills necessary for routine veterinary practice.

title: Clinical Nutrition
description: A focus on practical aspects of nutrition as it relates to performance, health and animal well-being of food-producing animals and, programs designed for health and well-being of small animals including exotic animals. Nutritional aspects of the mechanisms of health and disease are taught primarily at the individual and population level with some molecular and cellular aspects included. Designed for all students to be able to properly nourish animals in their care during their career.

title: Veterinary Surgery II
description: A continuation of Veterinary Surgery I. Introduction to the basics of veterinary anesthesia, surgical techniques, and patient management by a systems design. Problems common to all species of domestic animals will be presented to provide foundations of surgical knowledge and experience.

title: Veterinary Surgery II Laboratory
description: A continuation of Veterinary Surgery I Lab.  Introduction to the basics of veterinary anesthesia, surgical techniques, and patient management by a systems design. Problems common to all species of domestic animals will be presented to provide foundations of surgical knowledge and experience.

title: Veterinary Business and Professional Development
description: This course provides a foundation in business management and marketing to prepare veterinary students for success in private veterinary practice. Lectures include personal marketing, business organizational structure, gauging and monitoring production, management practice finance, recruitment, personnel management, effective practice marketing (internal and external), achieving better client satisfaction and retention, and the road to practice ownership.

title: Clinical Pharmacology
description: Course focuses on the clinical application of drugs in veterinary species. Clinical pharmacologists and clinicians from multiple clinical specialties will focus on commonly used drugs. Students will be expected to review and build upon their previous pharmacology and medicine courses. Emphasis will be placed on administration (including routes, dosing calculations, and methods), avoiding adverse drug reactions and interactions, evaluating patient response, written and verbal client communication, and utilizing reference resources to select and properly use veterinary therapeutics.

title: Equine Medicine
description: A study of the etiology, clinical signs, diagnosis, treatment, and control of common diseases which affect horses.

title: Foundations in Anesthesia
description: This course is a continuation of the anesthesia-specific lectures from VCS 863 Veterinary Surgery I.  Students will continue to be introduced to small animal anesthesia principles central to day-to-day management of small aniaml patients in the anesthesia clinical rotation during 4th year and beyond, in practice.

title: CS Veterinary Medicine Elective Aux. (Graded)
description: VCS 890 (Variable credit 0.5-3) fall, summer, and spring offering.  Special topics for veterinary students in the disciplines offered by the department.  Lecture or combination lecture with lab.  Continuation of Topics from VCS 891.

title: CS Veterinary Medicine Elective
description: Special topics for veterinary students in the disciplines offered by the department. Topics include: Beef Production Medicine : The role of the veterinarian in livestock production units, including interactions with producers, nutritionists, investors and others in decision analysis. Emphasis is on the professional services that veterinarians provide to beef production systems. Emergency Medicine : Presentations of cases in the ICU or cases that were recently seen on emergency. Current literature that is relevant to the case will be discussed. Advanced Exotic Pet Medicine : Advanced study of the diseases, medicine, and captive management of non-traditional species including companion birds, reptiles, rabbits, ferrets, and rodents. The Basics of Bovine Theriogenology : This class is designed to provide students enrolled in the veterinary curriculum a very practical knowledge base regarding bovine reproduction. Equine Medicine : This course is designed to be complementary to CS 710. The material presented in this class will focus on diagnostic procedures and case presentations that will not be covered in the core curriculum. The Learning Curve of Equine Practice : Covers various aspects of equine practice, not usually covered in traditional didactic lectures, including: equine husbandry, communication issues and insurance, horse colors, genetics, and genetic related diseases, aspects of equine practice and practice management, drug and medication rules for performance horses, and preventative medicine. Pain Management : Discussion of the mechanisms of pain, certain pain syndromes, and the drugs and techniques available to provide analgesia. Surgical Skills : Manual surgical skills will be taught using surgical models and cadaver tissues. Students will be taught the following skills and techniques: instrument handling, speed and efficiency, hand ties, ligature under tension, suture patterns, closure under tension, anastomosis and enterotomy technique and excision of circular lesions. Advanced Concepts in Veterinary Clinical Anesthesia : The lectures will be divided among topics to enhance student preparation for practical clinical application of anesthetic principles in both small and large animal environments. Topics: anesthetic implications for certain disease states, management of intraoperative complications, and special topics in large animal anesthesia. Veterinary Management of Sheep and Goats : Topic content is delivered both as online seminars via KSOL and case-based discussions and problem solving exercises in the classroom on herd management, preventive health care, nutrition, medicine, diseases, reproduction, and surgery of sheep and goats. Veterinary Ultrasonography : Topic content will be delivered both as online seminars via KSOL and case-based discussions. The physics, basic principles of use, and common artifacts of ultrasound will be covered as it relates to veterinary diagnostic imaging. Ultrasound imaging of the thorax (non-cardiac), abdomen, musculoskeletal system, and other areas (eye, thyroid/parathyroid) will be covered, predominantly in small animals. The course will cover both normal and pathologic ultrasound findings. Applied Small Animal Behavior:  Introduction to the commonly encountered canine and feline behavior topics in veterinary medicine.  Students will learn how to address these behavioral concerns through behavior modification, desensitization, counter conditioning, and psychopharmacology.  An additional goal of the course is to discuss practices that reduce fear in these patients during visits to their veterinarian.

title: CSVeterinary Medicine Elective CR/NC
description: Special topics for veterinary students in the disciplines offered by the department. Topic Include: Pre-Clinical Food Animal (Mixed) Practice Mentorship: Structured one week (45 contact hours) mentorship in a large animal (bovine) practice designed to promote clinical skills training in a private practice setting with practitioners who have expressed an interest in training and mentoring veterinary students. Pre-Clinical Small Animal Practice Mentorship: Structured one week (45 contact hours) mentorship in a small animal practice designed to promote clinical skills training in a private practice setting with practitioners who have expressed an interest in training and mentoring veterinary students. Practicing Veterinary Medicine in a Multicultural Society: Diversity in the broadest sense of the word (cultural, racial, sexual, gender, age, religious, etc.) will be explored in the context of the practice of veterinary medicine. Understanding diversity issues can positively affect the bottom line of nearly every veterinary practice. Pre-Clinical Exotics/Zoological Practice Mentorship: Structured one week (45 contact hours) mentorship in an exotics/zoological practice designed to promote clinical skills training in a private practice setting with practitioners who have expressed an interest in training and mentoring veterinary students.

title: CS Veterinary Medicine Elective Aux. (Credit/No Credit)
description: VCS 893 (Variable credit 0.5-3) fall, summer, and spring offering.  Special topics for veterinary students in the disciplines offered by the department.  Lecture or combination lecture with lab.  Continuation of Topics from VCS 892.

title: Veterinary Radiology and Diagnostic Imaging
description: Radiographic, ultrasonographic and nuclear imaging in the clinical setting, with emphasis on making/identifying images of diagnostic quality, interpretation, indications for imaging, and radiation safety.

title: Clinical Externship and/or Programmed Study
description: Provides the professional student with the opportunity to study with specialists not available at Kansas State University or in unique areas of veterinary medical practice or research.

title: Laboratory Animal Medicine
description: The study of laboratory animal medicine or comparative medicine to include colony health monitoring, preventive medicine, surgery, investigator support, and regulatory oversight.

title: Clinical Anesthesia
description: Practical instruction in the skills and techniques used in the practice of clinical veterinary anesthesia of both large and small animals.

title: Zoo, Exotic, Wildlife, and Avian Medicine and Surgery
description: Study of exotic, wildlife, and zoo animal medicine through participation in the clinical service in the Veterinary Health Center. Problem solving, differential diagnosis, diagnostic procedures, and medical and surgical therapy of non-traditional animals will be emphasized. Annual preventive medicine, medical procedures, and surgical procedures are also routinely performed at the City of Manhattan Sunset Zoo.  Occasional medical and surgical procedures are performed in other zoos around the area.

title: Ophthalmology
description: The study of the surgery and medical diagnosis and treatment of ocular disease in animals in the setting of the veterinary medical center. Problem solving, differential diagnosis, diagnostic procedures and medical and surgical therapy will be emphasized using veterinary patients.

title: Supplemental Zoo, Exotic, Wildlife and Avian Medicine and Surgery
description: Additional training in the study of exotic, wildlife, and zoo animal medicine and surgery through participation in the clinical service in the Veterinary Health Center.  Problem solving, differential diagnosis, diagnostic procedures, and medical and surgical therapy of non-traditional animals will be emphasized. Annual preventative medicine, medical procedures, and surgical procedures are also routinely performed at the City of Manhattan Sunset Zoo.  Occasional medical and surgical procedures are performed in other zoos around the area.

title: Urgent Care
description: Additional training and skills in triaging and managing patients in need of urgent or emergent care.  Examples include wounds, fractures, trauma, cardiovascular shock, respiratory distress, and other urgent/emergent conditions.  This will be conducted through lecture/discussion topic rounds and case-based learning.

title: Livestock Local Practice
description: A study of the role of the veterinarian in the practice of clinical medicine in livestock production units. Students will work under faculty supervision in ambulatory and local practice settings.

title: Livestock Medicine and Surgery
description: A study of individual livestock medicine and surgery. Students will work under faculty supervision in an in-house setting.

title: Advanced Feedlot Production Management
description: Students desiring feedlot/stocker production training. Focus on effects of nutrition, preventative medicine, environment, personnel training and other issues on the health of growing/finishing cattle.

title: Advanced Cow-Calf Production Management
description: For students desiring in-depth cow-calf production training including management and assessment of production data, nutrition and ration balancing, reproductive strategies, quality assurance and economic and Standardized Performance Analysis of cow-calf enterprises.

title: Advanced Rural Food Animal Business Management
description: An elective course that incorporates economic and food animal practice management skills. Course will focus on business aspects of agricultural industries and skills to manage a food animal practice.

title: Food Animal Bull Reproduction
description: Students will get hands-on experience and become competent performing a complete Breeding Soundness Exam. Other reproductive topics will be covered.

title: Equine Medicine
description: This course will offer general exposure to equine internal medicine and theriogenology. Students will be responsible for diagnoses, treatment and nursing care of out-patients, in-patients, and emergency duties.

title: Clinical Equine Theriogenology
description: Students will participate in routine procedures that develop skills in handling horses, rectal exams, ultrasonography, semen collection, artificial insemination, uterine therapies and minor surgical procedures.

title: Equine Surgery
description: This course will offer general exposure to equine surgery and theriogenology. Students will be responsible for diagnoses, treatment, and nursing care of out-patients, in-patients, and emergency duties.

title: Advanced Equine Studies
description: Students will have opportunities for routine and advanced clinical skills training and case management. Course discussions and case presentations will be targeted for the equine interested student and will therefore present advanced material; case discussion and rounds materials.

title: Equine Field Service
description: This course will offer general exposure to equine field service and theriogenology. Students will be responsible for diagnoses, treatment, and nursing care of out-patients, in-patients and emergency duties.

title: Supplemental Equine
description: An opportunity for students to pursue additional equine studies (medicine, surgery, etc.) in depth and assume substantial responsibility for care of hospitalized cases. Or students will have option to conduct research and present a seminar on a medicine or surgical subspecialty and pursue a special problem.

title: Small Animal Internal Medicine
description: The study of internal medicine in the setting of the Veterinary Health Center. Students will be engaged in the diagnosis and management of complex cases referred to the internal medicine service.

title: Clinical Oncology
description: The approach to diagnosis and treatment of cancer-bearing patients will be emphasized. Skills to master include fine needle aspirates and basic cytology, biopsy techniques (punch, wedge, pinch and tru-cut), bone marrow aspirate and core biopsy procedures and chemotherapy administration. A greater depth of understanding of the biologic behavior and treatment options of commonly seen tumor types (lymphoma, mast cell tumors, hemangiosarcoma, osteosarcoma, etc.) and chemotherapy safety will be emphasized. Medical management of cancer-related problems as well as treatment of therapy-induced side effects will also be covered.

title: Small Animal General Medicine
description: The study of preventative medicine, general practice medicine, and specialty medicine. Students spend three weeks with the Pet Health Center learning the various aspects of outpatient services.

title: Dermatology/Medicine
description: Designed to emphasize dermatology diagnostic work-up and management of dermatologic cases. May include proper techniques for performing procedures such as skin scraping, bacterial and fungal culturing, performing cytology, obtaining skin biopsies, ear clearing and trichography.

title: Clinical Small Animal Soft Tissue Surgery
description: This course is designed to train senior or elective veterinary students in the diagnosis and treatment of small animal soft tissue and general surgical diseases through participation in the Small Animal Soft Tissue Service of the Veterinary Health Center

title: Comparative Cardiology
description: Designed to expose students to the cardiovascular system of domestic animals, emphasizing clinical management and understanding of common congenital and acquired cardiac diseases.

title: Clinical Small Animal Orthopedic Surgery
description: Course is designed to train senior or elective veterinary students in diagnosis/treatment of small animal orthopedic surgical diseases through the Orthopedic Service of the Veterinary Health Center.

title: Shelter Medicine
description: The rotation will be a combination of in-class and laboratory exercises in Manhattan, and hands-on experiences at regional shelters. Topics include population medicine, infectious disease recognition and control, surgical sterilization, behavioral testing, and legal issues common to shelter settings.

title: General Dentistry
description: Skills and concepts common to general dentistry practice in the feline and canine, pre-operative exams, day-long wet lab to take and interpret digital intraoral radiographs, extract teeth, and perform dental nerve blocks. Also may participate in the evaluation, diagnosis, and treatment of primary care patients.

title: Supplemental Clinical Small Animal Soft Tissue Surgery
description: Additional training in the diagnosis and treatment of small animal soft tissue and general surgical diseases through participation in the Small Animal Soft Tissue Service of the Veterinary Health Center.

title: Small Animal Emergency Rotation
description: Enhanced training in the management of small animal patients presented to the emergency service.  Evaluation, management, and monitoring of small animal patients in the intensive care unit.

title: Supplemental Small Animal Internal Medicine
description: Additional training in the diagnosis, management and treatment of dogs and cats referred to the Internal Medicine service at the Veterinary Health Center.

title: Advanced Surgical Experience
description: Designed to increase exposure to soft tissue surgical theories and techniques by providing both hands on surgical experience on cadavers and literature review skills.

title: Supplemental Clinical Small Animal Orthopedic Surgery
description: Additional training in the diagnosis and treatment of small animal orthopedic surgical diseases through participation in the Small Animal Orthopedic Service of the Veterinary Health Center.

title: Supplemental Small Animal Emergency
description: An opportunity to pursue additional training in the management of small animal patients presented to the emergency services.  Evaluation, management, and monitoring of small animal patients in the intensive care unit.

title: Supplemental Dentistry for Small Animals
description: A one week course where students will be instructed through lecture, cadaver labs, and live animal care.  Topics covered will include dental evaluation techniques, instrumentation, dental radiography, tooth extraction techniques and regional anethesia.  Objectives of the course will be to provide 4th year students with additional training of basic dentistry knowledge and experience performing oral exams, dental probing, scaling, polishing, radiographs and tooth extractions.

title: Shelter Operations Consultation
description: This rotation will expose veterinary students to formal shelter consultation procedures. Students will learn how to take a critical look at the health of animal shelters to identify what is being done well and what needs to be improved. Students will also learn how to formulate recommendations in a way that does not overwhelm the administration of the shelters and can easily be implemented in a step-wise fashion.

title: Advanced Bovine Palpation
description: Provide numerous opportunities for the student to learn how to identify the different structures of the bovine reproductive tract through rectal palpation and transrectal ultrasound, accurately diagnose pregnancy both manually and via ultraonography, and to identify various bovine reproductive abnormalities.

title: Supplemental Small Animal General Medicine
description: Additional training in the study of preventative medicine, general practice medicine, and specialty medicine through participation in the Small Animal General Medicine Service in the Veterinary Health Center.

title: Shelter Medicine - Community Outreach
description: Designed to give students an opportunity to build practice skills in a community outreach setting.  This one week elective course will introduce students to access to care issues and prepare them to deliver veterinary services in socioeconomically diverse settings.  The rotation will consist of in-class lessons and discussion followed by outreach clinics.  Clinics will primarily focus on wellness exams, preventative medicine and treatment of routine medical conditions.

title: Problems in Medicine or Surgery
description: The course provides for the study of medical or surgical problems. The student, in conference with the major professor, outlines the methodology and procedures, conducts the study, and prepares a detailed report.

title: Veterinary Career Development
description: Introduction to career opportunities in veterinary medicine.

title: Clinical Pathology I
description:
Principles, application and interpretation of clinical laboratory procedures in clinical cases.
1 credit lecture / 1 credit lab

title: Veterinary Bacteriology & Mycology Lecture
description: Morphology, biology and classification of pathogenic bacteria and fungi and their relation to the causes of disease.

title: Trade and Agricultural Health
description: This course considers the multilateral trading system as it relates to food safety, food security, animal health, plant health, and international cooperation. The course content will be of value to students interested in food safety and security, epidemiology, public health, agriculture, food science, security studies, political science, agricultural economics, veterinary medicine, and international relations.

title: Principles of Veterinary Immunology
description: Innate and adaptive defense mechanisms in domestic animals. Topics include vaccinology, immunopathology, autoimmunity, immunodeficiency, and immunomodulation.

title: Veterinary Epidemiology
description: Introduction to the principles and methods of veterinary epidemiology: emphasizing how diseases affect populations (and associated implications for individuals), and application to disease diagnosis, treatment, prevention, and control.

title: Veterinary Virology
description: Morphology, biology, and classification of viruses and their relation to the causes of disease. Veterinary Virology is a 3 credit lecture course that is designed for veterinary students in the second year of the professional curriculum. The course provides basic and fundamental knowledge on the classification, pathogenesis, and diagnosis of viral pathogens affecting common veterinary species, including ovine, caprine, feline, canine, equine, bovine, avian, and porcine species. In addition, the course is designed to provide adequate knowledge and training for veterinarians in practice on the prevention and management of these viral diseases.

title: General Pathology
description: Etiology, pathogenesis, lesions, and termination of processes of disease, including inflammation, necrosis, regeneration, oncology, and disturbances of metabolism, circulation, and growth.

title: Veterinary Parasitology
description: Study of the helminth, arthropod, and protozoan parasites of companion and food animals. Emphases are on diagnosis, clinical signs, lesions, treatment, control epidemiology, and public heath aspects of parasitic disease.

title: Clinical Pathology II
description: Principles, application and interpretation of clinical laboratory procedures in clinical cases Two credits lecture, 1 credit lab.

title: Veterinary Public Health
description: The linkages between human health and animal health and production. Topics include zoonotic disease, emerging and exotic animal diseases, disaster preparedness, regulatory and community health issues focusing on the role of the veterinarian in all.

title: Systemic Pathology
description: Pathology of the organ systems of domestic animals including gross and microscopic study of lesions.

title: Globalization, Cooperation, and the Food Trade
description: This course will focus on issues and topics including, but not limited to, disputes in the international agricultural and food trade, new concepts such as Food and Agriculture Security, and the food-related challenges faced by wealthy as well as economically poor societies. The course will also address such important topics as the roles played by innovation and investment in the agricultural and food system, the importance of cross-border cooperation in regulation and trade, and the accomplishments of public health and trade policy thought leaders across history.

title: DMP Veterinary Medicine Elective
description: Special topics for veterinary students in the disciplines offered by the department, including: Immunology, Parasitology, Microbiology, Toxicology, Pathology, Epidemiology, Biosafety and Biosecurity, Herd Disease Outbreak Investigation Techniques, Laboratory Animal Science, Laboratory Diagnosis, and Exotoxicology.

title: Diagnostic Medicine
description: Practical experience in necropsy procedures and laboratory findings.

title: Dairy Production Medicine
description: This course will be conducted using both in-class discussions and field experiences on Kansas dairies.  The discussion portion will incorporate about 60% of class time, and field experiences 40%.

title: Firearms Cartridges and Ballistics
description: This course will cover the types and functions of firearms used in hunting/shooting sports to include rifles, shotguns and handguns. Basic firearms maintenance will also be covered in this course.

title: Rifle and Handgun Range Design, Construction and Operations
description: This course covers the design and construction of rifle/handgun shooting ranges based on national standards. The course will also cover operational issues including events planning and implementation.
